Below, two independent alternatives (A and B) are provided; only ONE is to be attempted in the exam, with each carrying 7 + 7 = 14 marks. --- ### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 1]** **Q:** (a) A saturated soil sample has a water content of 32% and a specific gravity of solids 2.65. Determine the void ratio, porosity, saturated unit weight, and dry unit weight of the soil. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³. **(7 Marks)** **(b) Derive the relationship $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ starting from basic definitions. State two special cases of this relationship and their physical meaning. (7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Given Data:** - Water content, $w = 32\% = 0.32$ - Specific gravity, $G_s = 2.65$ - Soil is **saturated**, so $S = 1$ (and $V_a = 0$) - $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ **Step 1: Find the Void Ratio ($e$) Using the Golden Equation $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$** Since $S = 1$: $$1 \cdot e = 0.32 \times 2.65 = 0.848$$ $$\boxed{e = 0.848}$$ **Step 2: Find the Porosity ($n$) Using $n = e / (1 + e)$** $$n = \dfrac{0.848}{1 + 0.848} = \dfrac{0.848}{1.848} = 0.4589$$ $$\boxed{n = 0.4589 \approx 45.89\%}$$ **Step 3: Find the Saturated Unit Weight ($\gamma_{sat}$)** Using the formula: $$\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(G_s + e) \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$$ $$\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(2.65 + 0.848) \times 9.81}{1 + 0.848}$$ $$\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{3.498 \times 9.81}{1.848} = \dfrac{34.314}{1.848}$$ $$\boxed{\gamma_{sat} = 18.57 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$ **Step 4: Find the Dry Unit Weight ($\gamma_d$)** Using the formula: $$\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$$ $$\gamma_d = \dfrac{2.65 \times 9.81}{1.848} = \dfrac{25.997}{1.848}$$ $$\boxed{\gamma_d = 14.07 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$ **Verification using $\gamma_d = \gamma_{sat} / (1 + w)$:** $$\gamma_d = \dfrac{18.57}{1 + 0.32} = \dfrac{18.57}{1.32} = 14.07 \text{ kN/m}^3 \quad \checkmark$$ **Final Summary Table:** | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Void Ratio, $e$ | 0.848 | | Porosity, $n$ | 45.89% | | Saturated Unit Weight, $\gamma_{sat}$ | 18.57 kN/m³ | | Dry Unit Weight, $\gamma_d$ | 14.07 kN/m³ | --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Derivation of $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$:** Starting with the fundamental definitions: **Step 1:** Water content is the ratio of mass of water to mass of solids: $$w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s} \quad \text{...(1)}$$ **Step 2:** Express $M_w$ in terms of volume of water: $M_w = \rho_w \cdot V_w$ (where $\rho_w$ is density of water). **Step 3:** Express $M_s$ in terms of volume of solids: $M_s = G_s \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_s$. **Step 4:** Substitute into equation (1): $$w = \dfrac{\rho_w \cdot V_w}{G_s \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_s} = \dfrac{V_w}{G_s \cdot V_s}$$ **Step 5:** Use the degree of saturation: $S = \dfrac{V_w}{V_v}$ → $V_w = S \cdot V_v$. **Step 6:** Substitute $V_w = S \cdot V_v$: $$w = \dfrac{S \cdot V_v}{G_s \cdot V_s} = \dfrac{S}{G_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_v}{V_s}$$ **Step 7:** Use void ratio $e = \dfrac{V_v}{V_s}$: $$w = \dfrac{S \cdot e}{G_s}$$ **Step 8:** Cross-multiply: $$\boxed{S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$ **Two Special Cases:** **Special Case 1: Fully Saturated Soil ($S = 1$)** When soil is saturated: $$e = w \cdot G_s$$ This means void ratio is directly proportional to water content for a saturated soil. Doubling the water content doubles the void ratio. Physically, a saturated soil has all voids filled with water, so an increase in water content directly corresponds to an increase in void space (i.e., looser packing). **Special Case 2: Dry Soil ($S = 0$ or $w = 0$)** When soil is dry: $$S \cdot e = 0$$ Since $e \neq 0$ (a soil mass always has voids), we must have $S = 0$. Physically, this means a dry soil has zero water in its voids. (Note: $S = 0$ does NOT mean $e = 0$; a dry sand can still have a void ratio of 0.6.) **Bonus Special Case: Specific Gravity from Saturated State** For a saturated soil ($S = 1$), if both $e$ and $w$ are measured in the lab, the specific gravity can be back-calculated as: $$G_s = \dfrac{e}{w}$$ This is often used in laboratory identification tests. --- ### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 1]** **Q:** (a) An undisturbed soil sample has the following properties: Volume = 0.95 m³, Mass = 1620 kg, Water content = 18%, Specific gravity of solids = 2.70. Determine: (i) Void ratio, (ii) Degree of saturation, (iii) Bulk unit weight, (iv) Dry unit weight, (v) Saturated unit weight, and (vi) Submerged unit weight. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³. **(7 Marks)** **(b) Define relative density. A sand sample in its natural state has a void ratio of 0.62, while the maximum and minimum void ratios (from lab tests) are 0.95 and 0.42 respectively. Compute the relative density and comment on the density state of the soil. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for B(a): **Given Data:** - Total Volume, $V = 0.95$ m³ - Total Mass, $M = 1620$ kg → Total Weight, $W = 1620 \times 9.81 = 15892.2$ N = 15.892 kN - Water content, $w = 18\% = 0.18$ - Specific gravity, $G_s = 2.70$ - $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ **Step 1: Compute Bulk Unit Weight ($\gamma$)** $$\gamma = \dfrac{W}{V} = \dfrac{15.892}{0.95} = 16.728 \text{ kN/m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\gamma = 16.73 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$ **Step 2: Compute Dry Unit Weight ($\gamma_d$)** $$\gamma_d = \dfrac{\gamma}{1 + w} = \dfrac{16.728}{1.18} = 14.176 \text{ kN/m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\gamma_d = 14.18 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$ **Step 3: Compute Mass of Solids ($M_s$) and Volume of Solids ($V_s$)** From the total mass equation: $M = M_s + M_w = M_s + w M_s = M_s(1 + w)$ $$M_s = \dfrac{M}{1 + w} = \dfrac{1620}{1.18} = 1372.88 \text{ kg}$$ Volume of solids: $$V_s = \dfrac{M_s}{G_s \cdot \rho_w} = \dfrac{1372.88}{2.70 \times 1000} = 0.5085 \text{ m}^3$$ (using $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³) **Step 4: Compute Volume of Voids ($V_v$) and Void Ratio ($e$)** $$V_v = V - V_s = 0.95 - 0.5085 = 0.4415 \text{ m}^3$$ $$e = \dfrac{V_v}{V_s} = \dfrac{0.4415}{0.5085} = 0.8683$$ $$\boxed{e = 0.868}$$ **Step 5: Compute Degree of Saturation ($S$) Using $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$** $$S = \dfrac{w \cdot G_s}{e} = \dfrac{0.18 \times 2.70}{0.868} = \dfrac{0.486}{0.868} = 0.5600$$ $$\boxed{S = 0.56 \text{ or } 56\%}$$ **Step 6: Compute Saturated Unit Weight ($\gamma_{sat}$)** $$\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(G_s + e) \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e} = \dfrac{(2.70 + 0.868) \times 9.81}{1.868}$$ $$= \dfrac{3.568 \times 9.81}{1.868} = \dfrac{35.002}{1.868} = 18.74 \text{ kN/m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\gamma_{sat} = 18.74 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$ **Step 7: Compute Submerged Unit Weight ($\gamma'$)** $$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 18.74 - 9.81 = 8.93 \text{ kN/m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\gamma' = 8.93 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$ **Summary Table:** | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Bulk Unit Weight, $\gamma$ | 16.73 kN/m³ | | Dry Unit Weight, $\gamma_d$ | 14.18 kN/m³ | | Void Ratio, $e$ | 0.868 | | Degree of Saturation, $S$ | 56% | | Saturated Unit Weight, $\gamma_{sat}$ | 18.74 kN/m³ | | Submerged Unit Weight, $\gamma'$ | 8.93 kN/m³ | --- #### Model Solution for B(b): **Definition of Relative Density ($D_r$):** Relative density (also called density index) is a measure of the denseness of a coarse-grained (granular) soil, expressed as the ratio of the difference between the void ratio of the soil in its loosest state and the void ratio in its natural state, to the difference between the void ratios in the loosest and densest states. $$D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100\%$$ Where: - $e$ = void ratio in the natural (in-situ) state - $e_{max}$ = void ratio in the loosest state (determined by the *minimum density test* — pouring sand loosely into a mould) - $e_{min}$ = void ratio in the densest state (determined by the *maximum density test* — vibratory or table compaction) **Given Data:** - Natural void ratio, $e = 0.62$ - Maximum void ratio (loosest), $e_{max} = 0.95$ - Minimum void ratio (densest), $e_{min} = 0.42$ **Computation:** $$D_r = \dfrac{0.95 - 0.62}{0.95 - 0.42} \times 100\%$$ $$D_r = \dfrac{0.33}{0.53} \times 100\% = 0.6226 \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{D_r = 62.26\%}$$ **Comment on Density State:** Based on the standard classification (IS 2720 / KTU convention): | Density State | Relative Density ($D_r$) | |---------------|--------------------------| | Very Loose | 0 – 15% | | Loose | 15 – 35% | | Medium Dense | 35 – 65% | | Dense | 65 – 85% | | Very Dense | 85 – 100% | Since $D_r = 62.26\%$ falls in the range **35% – 65%**, the soil is classified as **MEDIUM DENSE**. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake (Loses 1–2 Marks):** > Students often forget to multiply by 100% and write $D_r = 0.62$ instead of $62.26\%$. Also, the **density state classification range** must be quoted. Both omissions attract deductions in the KTU valuation key. Additionally, $e_{max}$ is sometimes confused with the void ratio in the **densest** state — remember: $e_{max}$ corresponds to the LOOSEST packing (largest void volume), and $e_{min}$ to the DENSEST packing. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Soil as a 3-Phase System** ### Core Definitions (Memorize These) - ✅ **Void ratio ($e$)** = $V_v / V_s$ — dimensionless — *always ≥ 0* - ✅ **Porosity ($n$)** = $V_v / V$ — dimensionless — *0 ≤ n ≤ 1* - ✅ **Water content ($w$)** = $M_w / M_s$ — usually expressed as % - ✅ **Degree of saturation ($S$)** = $V_w / V_v$ — 0 (dry) to 1 (saturated) - ✅ **Specific gravity ($G_s$)** = $\gamma_s / \gamma_w$ — typical range 2.65–2.80 - ✅ **Air content ($a_c$)** = $V_a / V_v = 1 - S$ ### Critical Master Formulas (Don't Forget) - ✅ $e = \dfrac{n}{1-n}$ and $n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$ (interconvertible) - ✅ $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$ (the *most-used* formula) - ✅ $\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(G_s + e) \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$ (set $S = 1$) - ✅ $\gamma = \dfrac{(G_s + S \cdot e) \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$ (general bulk unit weight) - ✅ $\gamma_d = \dfrac{\gamma}{1 + w}$ (relates dry and bulk unit weight) - ✅ $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ (buoyant/submerged unit weight) - ✅ **GOLDEN EQUATION: $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$** (the *one* equation to rule them all) ### KTU Examination Pitfalls - ⚠️ **Use $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³** (or 9.81 g/cc for CGS) — wrong value loses 1 mark instantly. - ⚠️ **Draw the block diagram with proper labels** — unlabeled sketch = partial marks. - ⚠️ **Always include units** in the final answer — unitless answers may be marked down. - ⚠️ **Verify physical realism**: $0 \le S \le 1$, $0 \le n \le 1$, $e \ge 0$, $w \ge 0$. - ⚠️ **$D_r$ is for coarse-grained soils only** (sands and gravels) — do not apply to clays. - ⚠️ When $V_a = 0$ (saturated), $S = 1$; when $M_w = 0$ (dry), $w = 0$. ### Quick Memory Aid > **"Sew Ws Gs"** — the Golden Equation $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ — given any 3, find the 4th. ### Typical KTU 2024 Question Weights on This Topic - 📊 2-mark question: Definition of any 1–2 index properties - 📊 3-mark question: Three-phase diagram sketch with labels - 📊 7-mark question: Numerical problem with 3–4 derivable quantities - 📊 14-mark question: Two-part question (derivation + numerical) as shown above **Estimated Weightage in KTU ESE:** 14–18% of the Soil Mechanics paper.

topics.title: Index Properties: Void Ratio - Specific Gravity - Dry Density - Porosity - Water Content - Saturated Unit Weight - Submerged Unit Weight - Degree of Saturation. Determination of Index Properties of Soils: Determination of Water Content by Oven Drying - Specific Gravity Using Pycnometer and Specific Gravity Bottle - Grain Size Analysis by Sieve Analysis

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Three-Phase Soil System — Foundation of All Index Properties

Every engineering problem in geotechnics begins with a **three-phase diagram** representing soil as a mixture of **solids**, **water**, and **air**. All "index properties" you will study in this module are simply mathematical ratios derived from this fundamental model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):** Mastery of the three-phase diagram is NON-NEGOTIABLE. Roughly 60% of Module 1 marks (both theory and numerical) directly test your ability to convert between mass, volume, weight, void ratio, porosity, and degree of saturation using this single diagram.

### Phase Components (Reference Quantities)

| Phase | Volume Symbol | Mass Symbol | Weight Symbol |
|-------|---------------|-------------|---------------|
| Solids | $V_s$ | $M_s$ | $W_s$ |
| Water | $V_w$ | $M_w$ | $W_w$ |
| Air | $V_a$ | $M_a \approx 0$ | $W_a \approx 0$ |
| **Total (Voids + Solids)** | $V = V_s + V_v$ | $M = M_s + M_w$ | $W = W_s + W_w$ |

where the volume of voids $V_v = V_w + V_a$.

---

## 1.2 Core Index Properties — Definitions & Real-World Analogies

### A. Void Ratio (e)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Void ratio is the ratio of the volume of voids to the volume of solids in a soil mass.
> $$e = \frac{V_v}{V_s}$$

- **Range:** $0 \le e < \infty$ (theoretically unbounded; in practice $0.3$ to $1.5$ for most soils).
- **Unit:** Dimensionless.

> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Imagine a jar filled with marbles. The empty space *between* the marbles (which can later be filled with water or air) is the "voids." The volume of those empty spaces divided by the solid marble volume is the void ratio. If you shake the jar, the marbles pack tighter, voids shrink, and $e$ decreases — this is exactly what happens when you compact a soil in the field.

---

### B. Porosity (n)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Porosity is the ratio of the volume of voids to the total volume of the soil mass.
> $$n = \frac{V_v}{V}$$

- **Range:** $0 \le n < 1$ (always less than 1, since voids can never exceed the total volume).
- **Unit:** Dimensionless (often expressed as a percentage).

> **Conceptual Analogy:** Porosity answers the question: *"Out of the entire soil sample, what fraction is just empty space?"* It is expressed as a fraction (or %) of the total. Note that $n$ is *always* less than $e$ for the same soil because the denominator is larger.

---

### C. Water Content (w) / Moisture Content

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Water content is the ratio of the mass of water to the mass of solids in a soil sample, expressed as a percentage.
> $$w = \frac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100\%$$

- **Range:** $0\%$ (oven-dry soil) to $\approx 1500\%$ (some organic peats).
- **Unit:** Percentage (%).

> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a wet sponge. If you weigh the water-logged sponge, then dry it completely in an oven and weigh it again, the difference in mass (water lost) divided by the dry mass tells you how "wet" the sponge was. This is water content — the universal indicator of how much water a soil holds relative to its solid skeleton.

---

### D. Specific Gravity of Solids (G_s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Specific gravity is the ratio of the unit weight (or mass, in CGS) of soil solids to the unit weight of water at $4^\circ C$.
> $$G_s = \frac{\gamma_s}{\gamma_w} = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_w}$$

- **Typical Range:** 
  - Sand: **2.65 – 2.67**
  - Silt: **2.67 – 2.70**
  - Clay: **2.70 – 2.80**
  - Soils with heavy minerals: **> 2.80**
- **Unit:** Dimensionless.
- **Standard Reference:** Water at $4^\circ C$ has $\gamma_w = 9.81\ \text{kN/m}^3$ and $\rho_w = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$.

> **Conceptual Analogy:** Specific gravity is the soil solid's "ID card" of heaviness. If water is the benchmark (1.00), then quartz grains (the dominant mineral in most soils) are about 2.65 times heavier than the same volume of water. It is a fundamental identifier, like density itself.

---

### E. Degree of Saturation (S)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Degree of saturation is the ratio of the volume of water to the volume of voids in a soil mass, expressed as a percentage.
> $$S = \frac{V_w}{V_v} \times 100\%$$

- **Range:** $0\%$ (completely dry soil) to $100\%$ (fully saturated).
- **Special States:**
  - $S = 0\%$ → **Dry soil**
  - $0\% < S < 100\%$ → **Partially saturated (moist) soil**
  - $S = 100\%$ → **Saturated soil**

> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a sponge partly dipped in water. Some pores are filled (water-filled voids) and some still contain air (air-filled voids). Degree of saturation is the percentage of pore space currently holding water. A fully saturated sponge has $S = 100\%$.

---

### F. Unit Weight (γ) and its Variants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unit weight is **weight per unit volume** — it depends on the state of the soil (dry, moist, saturated, or submerged). The standard unit in S.I. is $\text{kN/m}^3$.

| Variant | Symbol | Formula | Typical Range ($\text{kN/m}^3$) |
|---------|--------|---------|--------------------------------|
| Bulk / Moist Unit Weight | $\gamma$ | $W / V$ | $16 - 22$ |
| Dry Unit Weight | $\gamma_d$ | $W_s / V$ | $14 - 18$ |
| Saturated Unit Weight | $\gamma_{sat}$ | $(W_s + W_w) / V$ (when $V_w = V_v$) | $18 - 23$ |
| Submerged / Buoyant Unit Weight | $\gamma'$ | $\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ | $8 - 13$ |

> **Conceptual Analogy:** $\gamma_d$ is the weight of the soil skeleton alone (think of a dried-out brick). $\gamma_{sat}$ is when every void is filled with water (a fully soaked brick weighs more). $\gamma'$ is what the soil effectively weighs when underwater — the water pushes up (Archimedes' principle), reducing its apparent weight.

---

### G. Dry Density (ρ_d) vs. Dry Unit Weight (γ_d)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Dry density is the mass of solids per unit total volume of soil.
> $$\rho_d = \frac{M_s}{V} \quad \text{(units: kg/m}^3\text{)}$$

The corresponding weight expression is:
$$\gamma_d = \rho_d \cdot g = \frac{W_s}{V} \quad \text{(units: kN/m}^3\text{)}$$

> **Conceptual Analogy:** Dry density is the soil skeleton's "compactness indicator." Two soils can have the same total weight, but the one with higher dry density is more tightly packed. This is why compaction quality in road subgrades is measured by $\gamma_d$ (or $\rho_d$), not by total weight.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Phase Diagram — Visual Logic

```
        Volume (V)                          Mass (M)
   ┌──────────────────┐               ┌──────────────────┐
   │       Va         │  Air          │                  │
   ├──────────────────┤  ←── Vv       │       Mw         │  Water
   │       Vw         │  Water        │                  │
   ├──────────────────┤               ├──────────────────┤
   │                  │               │                  │
   │       Vs         │  Solids       │       Ms         │  Solids
   │                  │               │                  │
   └──────────────────┘               └──────────────────┘
```

The **left block** represents **volumes**; the **right block** represents **masses**. The total volume $V = V_s + V_v$ and total mass $M = M_s + M_w$ (we ignore air mass). Every index property is a ratio between two quantities taken from this diagram.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### Step 1 — Why both `e` and `n` exist

- $e = V_v / V_s$ → compares voids to the *solid skeleton only*.
- $n = V_v / V$ → compares voids to the *entire soil mass*.
- They are **not independent** — they are linked by simple algebra (see Section 3.1 derivation).

### Step 2 — Why water content is based on mass of solids, not total mass

- The solid skeleton is the **stable, unchanging framework** of the soil.
- Water mass is variable (rainfall, drying, pumping). Using solids as the reference gives us a property that does not "shift" due to wetting/drying of a different sample of the same soil.

### Step 3 — Why specific gravity is taken at $4^\circ C$

- Water has its **maximum density** at $4^\circ C$ ($1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$ exactly).
- This makes $G_s$ a **standardized, temperature-independent** identifier.
- In practice, we still use $G_s = \rho_s / \rho_w$ at room temperature, but the reference frame is fixed.

### Step 4 — The master relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most important equation connecting all index properties is:
> $$\boxed{S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s}$$
> This equation is the **Rosetta Stone** of soil mechanics. It links void ratio, saturation, water content, and specific gravity. If you know any three, you can find the fourth.

### Step 5 — Why we need separate definitions for unit weight

- The *total* weight of a soil sample depends on **how much water** is in its voids.
- For engineering design (earth dams, foundations, retaining walls), we need to know how the soil *behaves under load* — this depends on its **dry skeleton weight** ($\gamma_d$), not the variable water weight.
- Underwater, the buoyant force changes everything — hence $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — Memorize before every test.** Standard S.I. units assumed: $\gamma_w = 9.81\ \text{kN/m}^3$ (often rounded to $9.81$ or $10$); $\rho_w = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$.

| # | Property | Formula | Notes / Boundary |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Void Ratio | $e = V_v / V_s$ | $0 \le e < \infty$; dimensionless |
| 2 | Porosity | $n = V_v / V$ | $0 \le n < 1$; dimensionless |
| 3 | $e \leftrightarrow n$ | $e = n/(1-n)$, $n = e/(1+e)$ | Derived from volume relations |
| 4 | Water Content | $w = M_w / M_s$ | Expressed in % |
| 5 | Specific Gravity | $G_s = \gamma_s / \gamma_w$ | Typical $2.60 - 2.80$ |
| 6 | Degree of Saturation | $S = V_w / V_v$ | Expressed in %; $0\% \le S \le 100\%$ |
| 7 | Bulk Unit Weight | $\gamma = W / V$ | — |
| 8 | Dry Unit Weight | $\gamma_d = W_s / V$ | — |
| 9 | Saturated Unit Weight | $\gamma_{sat} = (W_s + W_w)/V$ | When $S = 100\%$ |
| 10 | Submerged Unit Weight | $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ | For soils below water table |
| 11 | Relation: $\gamma_d$ to $\gamma$ | $\gamma_d = \gamma / (1 + w)$ | Most-used relation |
| 12 | Relation: $\gamma$ to $\gamma_d$ | $\gamma = \gamma_d (1 + w)$ | — |
| 13 | Relation: $\gamma_d$ to $G_s, e$ | $\gamma_d = G_s \cdot \gamma_w / (1+e)$ | — |
| 14 | Relation: $\gamma_{sat}$ to $G_s, e$ | $\gamma_{sat} = (G_s + e)\gamma_w / (1+e)$ | — |
| 15 | Master Equation | $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ | **CRITICAL — memorize it** |
| 16 | Air Content ($a_c$) | $a_c = (V_a / V_v) = 1 - S$ | Often used with S = 1 |
| 17 | Percentage Air Voids | $n_a = (V_a / V) \times 100$ | — |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Which Property? | Why? |
|-------------|----------------|------|
| **Compaction control** (roads, earth dams) | $\gamma_d$ | Indicates how dense the soil skeleton is after rolling. |
| **Slope stability analysis** | $\gamma_{sat}$, $\gamma'$ | Pore water pressures and effective stresses depend on submerged weight. |
| **Bearing capacity of foundations** | $\gamma$ (or $\gamma_{sat}$ below water table) | Drives the overburden stress. |
| **Classification of soils** | $w$, $G_s$, $e$ | Helps distinguish clays (high $w$, high $e$) from sands (low $w$, low $e$). |
| **Liquefaction assessment** | $S$, $e$, $\gamma_d$ | Loose, saturated sands are vulnerable. |
| **Volume-change prediction** (swelling clays) | $e$, $w$ | High $e$ clays shrink/swell dramatically. |

---

## 2.5 Simple Worked Demonstration (Conceptual)

Suppose a soil sample has:
- $V_s = 0.5\ \text{m}^3$, $V_v = 0.5\ \text{m}^3$, $M_s = 850\ \text{kg}$, $M_w = 250\ \text{kg}$

Quick calculations:
- $e = V_v / V_s = 0.5 / 0.5 = 1.0$
- $n = V_v / V = 0.5 / 1.0 = 0.5 = 50\%$
- $w = M_w / M_s = 250/850 = 0.294 = 29.4\%$
- $G_s = M_s / (V_s \cdot \rho_w) = 850 / (0.5 \times 1000) = 1.7$ (low — organic soil likely)
- $S = V_w / V_v$. We need $V_w = M_w / \rho_w = 250/1000 = 0.25\ \text{m}^3$. So $S = 0.25/0.5 = 0.5 = 50\%$
- Check master relation: $S \cdot e = 0.5 \times 1.0 = 0.5$; $w \cdot G_s = 0.294 \times 1.7 = 0.5$ ✓

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Void Ratio (e) and Porosity (n)

**Given:**
- Void ratio: $e = V_v / V_s$ ... (i)
- Porosity: $n = V_v / V$ ... (ii)
- Total volume: $V = V_s + V_v$ ... (iii)

**Find:** Express $e$ in terms of $n$, and vice versa.

**Step 1:** From (i), rearrange to get $V_v = e \cdot V_s$.

**Step 2:** Substitute into (iii):
$$V = V_s + V_v = V_s + e \cdot V_s = V_s (1 + e)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $V_s$ from (i) — i.e., $V_s = V_v / e$ — into the expression for $V$:
$$V = \frac{V_v}{e}(1 + e)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the porosity formula (ii):
$$n = \frac{V_v}{V} = \frac{V_v}{\frac{V_v}{e}(1 + e)} = \frac{e}{1+e}$$

$$\boxed{n = \frac{e}{1+e}}$$

**Step 5:** Invert this result algebraically:
$$n(1+e) = e \implies n + n \cdot e = e \implies n = e(1-n) \implies \boxed{e = \frac{n}{1-n}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Since $0 \le n < 1$, the denominator $1-n$ is always positive, so $e$ is always positive. Also, $n < e$ for all real soils (because $1+e > 1$), which is consistent with the conceptual analogy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Master Relationship $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$

**Given:**
- $S = V_w / V_v$ ... (i)
- $e = V_v / V_s$ ... (ii)
- $w = M_w / M_s$ ... (iii)
- $G_s = M_s / (V_s \cdot \rho_w)$ ... (iv)

**Step 1:** Multiply (i) and (ii):
$$S \cdot e = \frac{V_w}{V_v} \cdot \frac{V_v}{V_s} = \frac{V_w}{V_s}$$

**Step 2:** Express $M_w$ in terms of $V_w$:
$$M_w = \rho_w \cdot V_w$$

**Step 3:** Multiply (iii) and (iv):
$$w \cdot G_s = \frac{M_w}{M_s} \cdot \frac{M_s}{V_s \cdot \rho_w} = \frac{M_w}{V_s \cdot \rho_w}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $M_w = \rho_w \cdot V_w$ into the result:
$$w \cdot G_s = \frac{\rho_w \cdot V_w}{V_s \cdot \rho_w} = \frac{V_w}{V_s}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with Step 1: both equal $V_w / V_s$. Therefore:
$$\boxed{S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This is the *only* fundamental inter-relationship between the four key index properties. It allows you to compute any unknown if you know the other three — the basis for most KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Dry Unit Weight in Terms of $G_s$ and $e$

**Given:** $\gamma_d = W_s / V$.

**Step 1:** Express $W_s = M_s \cdot g = G_s \cdot V_s \cdot \rho_w \cdot g = G_s \cdot V_s \cdot \gamma_w$.

**Step 2:** From the volume relation derived in 3.1, $V = V_s (1+e)$.

**Step 3:** Substitute into the dry unit weight formula:
$$\gamma_d = \frac{W_s}{V} = \frac{G_s \cdot V_s \cdot \gamma_w}{V_s(1+e)}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $V_s$:
$$\boxed{\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** This shows that $\gamma_d$ **decreases** as $e$ increases (looser packing) and **increases** as $G_s$ increases (heavier minerals).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Saturated Unit Weight in Terms of $G_s$ and $e$

**Given:** When soil is saturated, $V_w = V_v$ and $S = 100\%$, so $W_w = \gamma_w \cdot V_v$.

**Step 1:** Total weight at saturation:
$$W_{sat} = W_s + W_w = G_s \cdot V_s \cdot \gamma_w + \gamma_w \cdot V_v$$

**Step 2:** Express $V_v$ in terms of $V_s$: $V_v = e \cdot V_s$. So:
$$W_{sat} = G_s \cdot V_s \cdot \gamma_w + \gamma_w \cdot e \cdot V_s = \gamma_w \cdot V_s (G_s + e)$$

**Step 3:** Total volume $V = V_s (1+e)$.

**Step 4:** Therefore:
$$\gamma_{sat} = \frac{W_{sat}}{V} = \frac{\gamma_w \cdot V_s (G_s + e)}{V_s(1+e)} = \boxed{\frac{(G_s + e)\gamma_w}{1+e}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Submerged (Buoyant) Unit Weight

**Given:** $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$.

**Step 1:** Substitute the derived $\gamma_{sat}$:
$$\gamma' = \frac{(G_s + e)\gamma_w}{1+e} - \gamma_w$$

**Step 2:** Common denominator $(1+e)$:
$$\gamma' = \frac{(G_s + e)\gamma_w - \gamma_w(1+e)}{1+e}$$

**Step 3:** Expand the second term:
$$= \frac{(G_s + e)\gamma_w - \gamma_w - e\gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w + e\gamma_w - \gamma_w - e\gamma_w}{1+e}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $e\gamma_w$:
$$= \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w - \gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{(G_s - 1)\gamma_w}{1+e}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma' = \frac{(G_s - 1)\gamma_w}{1+e}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the cleanest form for submerged unit weight. Notice that for a typical soil with $G_s = 2.65$, the numerator is $(2.65 - 1)\gamma_w = 1.65\gamma_w$, so the submerged unit weight is roughly $60\%$ of the dry unit weight.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Relation $\gamma_d = \gamma / (1+w)$

**Given:** $\gamma = W/V = (W_s + W_w)/V$ and $\gamma_d = W_s / V$.

**Step 1:** Express $W_w = w \cdot W_s$ (from $w = M_w/M_s$, since $g$ cancels).

**Step 2:** Then:
$$\gamma = \frac{W_s + w W_s}{V} = \frac{W_s(1+w)}{V} = \gamma_d (1+w)$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\gamma_d$:
$$\boxed{\gamma_d = \frac{\gamma}{1+w}}$$

This is the **most-tested conversion** in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

> **Problem:** A saturated soil sample has $G_s = 2.70$ and void ratio $e = 0.65$. Compute:
> (a) Porosity, bulk unit weight, dry unit weight, saturated unit weight, submerged unit weight, and water content.
> (b) If the same soil is partially saturated with $S = 60\%$, find the new water content and the new bulk unit weight.

### Part (a) — Saturated State

**Step 1 — Porosity:**
$$n = \frac{e}{1+e} = \frac{0.65}{1.65} = 0.3939 = 39.39\%$$

**Step 2 — Dry Unit Weight (using $\gamma_w = 9.81\ \text{kN/m}^3$):**
$$\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{2.70 \times 9.81}{1.65} = \frac{26.487}{1.65} = 16.05\ \text{kN/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Saturated Unit Weight:**
$$\gamma_{sat} = \frac{(G_s + e)\gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{(2.70 + 0.65) \times 9.81}{1.65} = \frac{3.35 \times 9.81}{1.65} = \frac{32.864}{1.65} = 19.92\ \text{kN/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Submerged Unit Weight:**
$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 19.92 - 9.81 = 10.11\ \text{kN/m}^3$$

(Or directly: $\gamma' = \frac{(G_s - 1)\gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{1.70 \times 9.81}{1.65} = 10.11\ \text{kN/m}^3$ ✓)

**Step 5 — Water Content (using $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$, with $S = 1$):**
$$w = \frac{S \cdot e}{G_s} = \frac{1 \times 0.65}{2.70} = 0.2407 = 24.07\%$$

**Step 6 — Bulk Unit Weight at Saturation:**
For saturated soil, $\gamma = \gamma_{sat} = 19.92\ \text{kN/m}^3$.

### Part (b) — Partially Saturated State ($S = 60\%$)

**Step 1 — New Water Content:**
$$w = \frac{S \cdot e}{G_s} = \frac{0.60 \times 0.65}{2.70} = \frac{0.39}{2.70} = 0.1444 = 14.44\%$$

**Step 2 — New Bulk Unit Weight:**
$$\gamma = \gamma_d(1+w) = 16.05 \times (1 + 0.1444) = 16.05 \times 1.1444 = 18.37\ \text{kN/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Verification using master equation:**
$S \cdot e = 0.60 \times 0.65 = 0.39$
$w \cdot G_s = 0.1444 \times 2.70 = 0.39$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Summary Table:**

| Property | Saturated State | Partially Saturated ($S = 60\%$) |
|----------|-----------------|-----------------------------------|
| $n$ | $39.39\%$ | $39.39\%$ (unchanged — depends only on volume ratio) |
| $e$ | $0.65$ | $0.65$ (unchanged) |
| $G_s$ | $2.70$ | $2.70$ |
| $w$ | $24.07\%$ | $14.44\%$ |
| $\gamma_d$ | $16.05\ \text{kN/m}^3$ | $16.05\ \text{kN/m}^3$ (unchanged) |
| $\gamma_{sat}$ | $19.92\ \text{kN/m}^3$ | — (no longer saturated) |
| $\gamma$ | $19.92\ \text{kN/m}^3$ | $18.37\ \text{kN/m}^3$ |
| $\gamma'$ | $10.11\ \text{kN/m}^3$ | — (not submerged anymore) |

---

## 3.8 Determination of Water Content by Oven Drying (IS 2720 Part 2)

### Procedure (Step-by-Step)

1. **Weigh** an empty, clean, dry moisture container with its lid. Record as $M_1$.
2. **Place** the wet soil sample in the container. Reweigh (with lid). Record as $M_2$.
3. **Place** the container (lid removed or placed below) in a **thermostatically controlled oven** at $105^\circ C$ to $110^\circ C$ for **24 hours** (overnight is typical).
4. **Cool** the container in a desiccator (to prevent re-absorption of moisture).
5. **Reweigh** the container with the dried soil. Record as $M_3$.

### Formula

$$w = \frac{M_2 - M_3}{M_3 - M_1} \times 100\%$$

**Why each mass:**
- $M_2 - M_3$ = mass of water evaporated
- $M_3 - M_1$ = mass of dry soil solids

> [!NOTE]
> **Apparatus:** Moisture containers (corrosion-resistant, non-absorbent), oven ($105-110^\circ C$), desiccator, balance (accuracy $0.01\ \text{g}$ for fine soils, $0.1\ \text{g}$ for coarse).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Cases:**
> - **Organic soils, gypsiferous soils, soils with high salt content:** Oven temperature may be reduced to $60 - 80^\circ C$ to avoid charring organic matter or losing crystal water. KTU examiners often test awareness of this.
> - **Sandy soils:** Drying time may be reduced (6-8 hours may suffice) because water escapes easily.
> - **Clayey soils:** Full 24 hours is required.

---

## 3.9 Determination of Specific Gravity Using Pycnometer (IS 2720 Part 3 Section 1)

### Apparatus
- Pycnometer (a glass bottle of $\sim 500\ \text{mL}$ to $1\ \text{L}$ with a conical cap having a small hole at the top).
- Vacuum desiccator or de-airing device.

### Procedure

1. **Weigh** the empty, clean, dry pycnometer (with cap). Record as $M_1$.
2. **Transfer** about $200 - 400\ \text{g}$ of oven-dry soil into the pycnometer. Weigh as $M_2$.
3. **Add** de-aired distilled water to cover the soil. **Remove entrapped air** using a vacuum pump or by gently boiling for $\sim 10$ minutes.
4. **Fill** the pycnometer completely with de-aired water up to the brim. Place the cap; water will spurt out of the small hole, ensuring a known volume.
5. **Dry** the outside and **weigh** as $M_3$.
6. **Empty** the pycnometer, **clean it**, then fill with **only de-aired water** to the brim (same procedure, with cap). Weigh as $M_4$.

### Formula

$$G_s = \frac{M_2 - M_1}{(M_2 - M_1) - (M_3 - M_4)}$$

**Derivation logic:**

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|------|------------------|
| $M_2 - M_1$ | Mass of dry soil solids |
| $M_4 - M_1$ | Mass of water that fills the pycnometer to capacity |
| $M_3 - M_1$ | Mass of (soil + water) filling the pycnometer to capacity |
| $(M_4 - M_1) - (M_3 - M_1) = M_4 - M_3$ | Mass of water displaced by the soil solids = mass of water having volume equal to $V_s$ |

So: 
$$G_s = \frac{M_s}{V_s \cdot \rho_w} = \frac{\text{mass of solids}}{\text{mass of equal-volume water}}$$

The denominator in the formula above equals $(M_2 - M_1) - (M_3 - M_4)$ which is the mass of water displaced.

---

## 3.10 Determination of Specific Gravity Using Specific Gravity Bottle

This is essentially the **same procedure** as the pycnometer method, except the apparatus is a **smaller glass bottle (50 mL / 100 mL / 250 mL)** with a stopper having a small hole. The same formula applies:

$$G_s = \frac{M_2 - M_1}{(M_2 - M_1) - (M_3 - M_4)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **When is the bottle preferred over the pycnometer?**
> - For **fine-grained soils** (silts, clays) where smaller samples suffice.
> - When a **more accurate determination** is needed for a small quantity of soil.
> - The pycnometer is preferred for **coarse-grained soils** (gravel, sand) requiring larger samples for representativeness.

---

## 3.11 Grain Size Analysis by Sieve Analysis (IS 2720 Part 4)

### Principle
- **Coarse fraction** ($> 75\ \mu\text{m}$ or $0.075\ \text{mm}$): determined by sieve analysis.
- **Fine fraction** ($< 75\ \mu\text{m}$): determined by **sedimentation** (hydrometer analysis) — covered later in Module 2.
- This section covers **sieve analysis only** (per KTU Module 1 syllabus).

### Standard IS Sieve Sizes (in mm)

A **standard sieve set** includes: 4.75 mm, 2.36 mm, 1.18 mm, 600 µm, 425 µm, 300 µm, 212 µm, 150 µm, 75 µm.

(Plus larger "coarse" sieves: 100 mm, 63 mm, 20 mm, 10 mm for gravel fraction, depending on sample.)

### Procedure

1. **Take** an oven-dry soil sample (typically $200 - 500\ \text{g}$ for fine-grained, more for gravelly soil).
2. **Weigh** the sample accurately: $M_{\text{total}}$.
3. **Arrange** the sieves in **descending order of aperture** (largest on top, smallest at the bottom, with a pan at the bottom).
4. **Pour** the soil into the topmost sieve.
5. **Shake** the stack on a **mechanical sieve shaker** for $\sim 10 - 15$ minutes.
6. **Weigh** the soil retained on each sieve carefully.

### Calculations Table Format

| Sieve No. | Aperture (mm) | Mass Retained (g) | Cumulative Mass Retained (g) | Cumulative % Retained | % Finer |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------|------------------------------|----------------------|---------|
| 4.75 mm | 4.75 | $m_1$ | $m_1$ | $\frac{m_1}{M_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | $100 - C_1$ |
| 2.36 mm | 2.36 | $m_2$ | $m_1 + m_2$ | $\frac{m_1+m_2}{M_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | $100 - C_2$ |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 75 µm | 0.075 | $m_n$ | $\sum m_i$ | $\frac{\sum m_i}{M_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | $100 - C_n$ |

**Critical Check:** Sum of all "cumulative mass retained" should be within $\sim 1\%$ of $M_{\text{total}}$. If not, the test is rejected.

### Coefficients from the Grain Size Curve

Plot **% finer (Y-axis) vs. log of grain size in mm (X-axis)** to get the **grain size distribution curve**.

- **$D_{10}$** = grain size at 10% finer (the "**effective size**")
- **$D_{30}$** = grain size at 30% finer
- **$D_{60}$** = grain size at 60% finer

#### Coefficient of Uniformity ($C_u$)

$$\boxed{C_u = \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}}$$

- $C_u \ge 4$ for gravels; $C_u \ge 6$ for sands → well-graded.
- $C_u < 4$ (gravel) or $< 6$ (sand) → poorly graded (uniform).

#### Coefficient of Curvature ($C_c$)

$$\boxed{C_c = \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{60} \cdot D_{10}}$$

- For well-graded soil: $1 \le C_c \le 3$.
- $C_c < 1$ or $C_c > 3$ → poorly graded in the fine range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 - Why these matter:** A soil's gradation affects permeability, compressibility, and shear strength. Well-graded soils pack better, are less permeable, and are preferred for embankments.

---

## 3.12 Worked Sieve Analysis Example

**Problem:** A sieve analysis of 500 g of dry soil yields the following data. Plot the grain size curve and find $C_u$, $C_c$.

| Sieve (mm) | Mass Retained (g) | Cumulative Retained (g) | Cumulative % Retained | % Finer |
|------------|-------------------|-------------------------|----------------------|---------|
| 4.75 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 100.0 |
| 2.36 | 50 | 50 | 10.0 | 90.0 |
| 1.18 | 75 | 125 | 25.0 | 75.0 |
| 0.600 | 100 | 225 | 45.0 | 55.0 |
| 0.425 | 60 | 285 | 57.0 | 43.0 |
| 0.300 | 50 | 335 | 67.0 | 33.0 |
| 0.212 | 40 | 375 | 75.0 | 25.0 |
| 0.150 | 50 | 425 | 85.0 | 15.0 |
| 0.075 | 50 | 475 | 95.0 | 5.0 |
| Pan | 25 | 500 | 100.0 | 0.0 |

**Reading the grain size curve (log scale):**
- $D_{10}$ (size at 10% finer) ≈ **0.09 mm** (interpolated between 0.075 and 0.150)
- $D_{30}$ (size at 30% finer) ≈ **0.27 mm** (near 0.300 mm sieve)
- $D_{60}$ (size at 60% finer) ≈ **0.66 mm** (near 0.600 mm sieve)

**Coefficient of Uniformity:**
$$C_u = \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \frac{0.66}{0.09} = 7.33$$

**Coefficient of Curvature:**
$$C_c = \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{60} \cdot D_{10}} = \frac{(0.27)^2}{0.66 \times 0.09} = \frac{0.0729}{0.0594} = 1.23$$

**Classification Check:** For sand, $C_u \ge 6$ ✓ and $1 \le C_c \le 3$ ✓ → **Well-graded sand (SW)**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A, Module 1, Q.1]**

Define the following terms with their typical ranges: (i) Void ratio, (ii) Porosity, (iii) Degree of saturation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) **Void ratio ($e$):** It is defined as the ratio of the volume of voids to the volume of solids in a given soil mass.
$$e = \frac{V_v}{V_s}$$
Range: $0 \le e < \infty$ (typically $0.3$ to $1.5$ for most natural soils). Dimensionless. **[1 mark]**

(ii) **Porosity ($n$):** It is defined as the ratio of the volume of voids to the total volume of the soil mass.
$$n = \frac{V_v}{V}$$
Range: $0 \le n < 1$ (often expressed as a percentage, typically $30\%$ to $60\%$). Dimensionless. **[1 mark]**

(iii) **Degree of saturation ($S$):** It is defined as the ratio of the volume of water to the volume of voids, expressed as a percentage.
$$S = \frac{V_w}{V_v} \times 100\%$$
Range: $0\%$ (dry soil) to $100\%$ (fully saturated soil). **[1 mark]**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Set B, Module 1, Q.2]**

Differentiate between **specific gravity bottle** and **pycnometer** with respect to the determination of $G_s$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Specific Gravity Bottle | Pycnometer |
|--------|------------------------|------------|
| **Capacity** | Small, $50$ to $250\ \text{mL}$ | Large, $500$ to $1000\ \text{mL}$ | 
| **Sample size required** | Small ($10 - 50\ \text{g}$ for fine soils) | Large ($200 - 400\ \text{g}$ for coarse soils) | 
| **Best suited for** | Fine-grained soils (silts, clays) | Coarse-grained soils (sand, gravel) | 
| **Accuracy** | Higher accuracy for small samples | Better representative sampling for coarse soils | 
| **Design** | Glass bottle with stopper having a small hole (Bleed hole) | Wider-mouthed bottle with conical cap having a central bleed hole | 

**[3 marks - 1.5 for each type's characteristic features, 0.5 mark for conclusion]**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. You must attempt **any ONE** of the two choices provided.

### **CHOICE A: Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q.7A]**

**(a)** A soil sample in its natural state has a mass of $1290\ \text{g}$ and a volume of $600\ \text{cm}^3$. When oven-dried, its mass reduces to $1080\ \text{g}$. The specific gravity of the soil solids is $2.70$. Determine:
   (i) Water content
   (ii) Void ratio
   (iii) Porosity
   (iv) Degree of saturation

**(b)** Discuss the procedure for the determination of specific gravity of soil solids using a pycnometer, with the formula and significance of each weighing.

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Wet mass, $M = 1290\ \text{g}$
- Dry mass (after oven drying), $M_s = 1080\ \text{g}$
- Volume, $V = 600\ \text{cm}^3 = 600 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^3$
- Specific gravity, $G_s = 2.70$

**Step 1 — Mass of water:**
$$M_w = M - M_s = 1290 - 1080 = 210\ \text{g}$$ **[0.5 mark]**

**Step 2 — Water content:**
$$w = \frac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100 = \frac{210}{1080} \times 100 = 19.44\%$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 3 — Volume of solids:**
$$V_s = \frac{M_s}{G_s \cdot \rho_w} = \frac{1080}{2.70 \times 1\ \text{g/cm}^3} = \frac{1080}{2.70} = 400\ \text{cm}^3$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 4 — Volume of voids:**
$$V_v = V - V_s = 600 - 400 = 200\ \text{cm}^3$$ **[0.5 mark]**

**Step 5 — Void ratio:**
$$e = \frac{V_v}{V_s} = \frac{200}{400} = 0.50$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 6 — Porosity:**
$$n = \frac{V_v}{V} = \frac{200}{600} = 0.333 = 33.33\%$$ **[1 mark]**

(Or: $n = e/(1+e) = 0.50/1.50 = 0.333$ ✓)

**Step 7 — Volume of water:**
$$V_w = \frac{M_w}{\rho_w} = \frac{210\ \text{g}}{1\ \text{g/cm}^3} = 210\ \text{cm}^3$$ **[0.5 mark]**

**Step 8 — Degree of saturation:**
$$S = \frac{V_w}{V_v} \times 100 = \frac{210}{200} \times 100 = 105\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **$S = 105\%$ is physically impossible** (S cannot exceed 100%). This indicates either (i) measurement error, or (ii) the soil contains dissolved salts/heavy minerals with effective $\rho_w > 1$, or (iii) air is present and the calculation reveals an inconsistency. KTU examiners often test whether students notice this. **[1 mark]**

> [!WARNING]
> **Correct interpretation:** With the given data, the soil is **supersaturated** (impossible physically). A more realistic scenario: the soil is saturated ($S = 100\%$), so $V_w = V_v = 200\ \text{cm}^3$, which means $M_w = 200\ \text{g}$ (not $210\ \text{g}$). The "extra" $10\ \text{g}$ suggests a measurement error, or that the soil has some entrapped air reducing actual water mass.

---

#### Part (b) — Pycnometer Procedure (7 marks)

**Apparatus:** Pycnometer (500-1000 mL), vacuum pump / boiling arrangement, desiccator, balance (0.01 g accuracy), distilled water. **[0.5 mark]**

**Procedure (each step carries marks):**

1. **Weigh empty, clean, dry pycnometer (with cap): $M_1$** **[0.5 mark]**
2. **Place about 200-400 g of oven-dry soil into the pycnometer and weigh: $M_2$** **[0.5 mark]**
3. **Add de-aired distilled water to cover the soil. Apply vacuum (or boil gently for ~10 min) to remove entrapped air.** **[1 mark]**
4. **Fill the pycnometer completely with de-aired water; place the conical cap (a small amount of water spurt out of the bleed hole).** **[0.5 mark]**
5. **Wipe dry the outside and weigh: $M_3$.** **[0.5 mark]**
6. **Empty and clean the pycnometer. Fill it with de-aired water only (to the cap), and weigh: $M_4$.** **[0.5 mark]**

**Formula:**
$$G_s = \frac{M_2 - M_1}{(M_2 - M_1) - (M_3 - M_4)}$$ **[1.5 marks]**

**Significance of each mass:**

| Mass | Represents |
|------|------------|
| $M_1$ | Empty pycnometer (tare) |
| $M_2 - M_1$ | Mass of oven-dry soil solids ($M_s$) |
| $M_4$ | Mass of pycnometer + water only (calibration mass) |
| $M_4 - M_1$ | Mass of water filling the full pycnometer |
| $M_3$ | Mass of pycnometer + soil + water (full) |
| $M_3 - M_1$ | Mass of (soil + water) filling the full pycnometer |
| $(M_2 - M_1) - (M_3 - M_4) = (M_4 - M_3)$ | Mass of water **displaced** by soil solids = mass of water having volume $V_s$ |

The denominator represents the mass of water whose volume equals $V_s$, so the ratio gives $M_s / M_{w, \text{equal } V} = \rho_s / \rho_w = G_s$. **[2 marks]**

---

### **CHOICE B: Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q.7B]**

**(a)** A saturated soil has $G_s = 2.68$ and water content $w = 30\%$. Calculate: (i) Void ratio, (ii) Porosity, (iii) Dry unit weight, (iv) Saturated unit weight, and (v) Submerged unit weight. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81\ \text{kN/m}^3$.

**(b)** Describe with a neat sketch the determination of grain size distribution by **sieve analysis** as per IS 2720 (Part 4). How are the **coefficient of uniformity ($C_u$) and coefficient of curvature ($C_c$)** calculated from the grain size curve? What is the significance of these coefficients?

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $G_s = 2.68$
- $w = 30\% = 0.30$
- Soil is **saturated** → $S = 100\% = 1$
- $\gamma_w = 9.81\ \text{kN/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Void ratio using master equation $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$:**
$$e = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{S} = \frac{0.30 \times 2.68}{1.0} = 0.804$$ **[1.5 marks]**

**Step 2 — Porosity:**
$$n = \frac{e}{1+e} = \frac{0.804}{1.804} = 0.4457 = 44.57\%$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 3 — Dry unit weight:**
$$\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{2.68 \times 9.81}{1.804} = \frac{26.291}{1.804} = 14.57\ \text{kN/m}^3$$ **[1.5 marks]**

**Step 4 — Saturated unit weight:**
$$\gamma_{sat} = \frac{(G_s + e) \cdot \gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{(2.68 + 0.804) \times 9.81}{1.804} = \frac{3.484 \times 9.81}{1.804} = 18.94\ \text{kN/m}^3$$ **[1.5 marks]**

**Step 5 — Submerged unit weight:**
$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 18.94 - 9.81 = 9.13\ \text{kN/m}^3$$

(Or directly: $\gamma' = \frac{(G_s - 1) \cdot \gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{1.68 \times 9.81}{1.804} = 9.13\ \text{kN/m}^3$ ✓) **[1.5 marks]**

---

#### Part (b) — Sieve Analysis Description (7 marks)

**Apparatus:** Set of IS sieves (4.75 mm down to 75 µm), lid, pan, mechanical sieve shaker, balance (0.1 g accuracy), oven-dried soil sample. **[0.5 mark]**

**Procedure:**

1. **Weigh** the oven-dried soil sample accurately (typically $200 - 500\ \text{g}$ for fine-grained soils; more for gravelly soil). Record as total mass $M_{\text{total}}$. **[0.5 mark]**
2. **Arrange** the sieves in **descending order** of aperture (largest on top, smallest at the bottom). Place a **pan at the bottom** to collect fines passing 75 µm. **[0.5 mark]**
3. **Pour** the soil onto the topmost sieve, place the lid, and **mount on the mechanical shaker**. **[0.5 mark]**
4. **Shake for 10 - 15 minutes** (until less than 1% of the soil mass passes any sieve in 1 minute of further shaking). **[0.5 mark]**
5. **Weigh** the soil retained on each sieve and on the pan. Record as $m_1, m_2, ..., m_n, m_{\text{pan}}$. **[0.5 mark]**

**Calculations:**

| Sieve (mm) | Mass Retained (g) | Cum. Retained (g) | Cum. % Retained | % Finer |
|------------|-------------------|------------------|-----------------|---------|
| 4.75 | $m_1$ | $m_1$ | $\frac{m_1}{M_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | $100 - C_1$ |
| 2.36 | $m_2$ | $m_1 + m_2$ | ... | ... |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 75 µm | $m_n$ | $\sum m_i$ | ... | ... |
| Pan | $m_{\text{pan}}$ | $\sum m_i + m_{\text{pan}}$ | $\approx 100\%$ | $\approx 0$ |

**Check:** Sum of all "mass retained" = $M_{\text{total}}$ (within $\pm 1\%$). **[0.5 mark]**

**Plotting the curve:**
- X-axis: **Grain size in mm (log scale)**
- Y-axis: **% finer (linear scale, arithmetic)**
- This gives the **grain size distribution curve**. **[0.5 mark]**

**Coefficient of Uniformity:**
$$C_u = \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$$
- $D_{10}$ = grain size at 10% finer (the *effective size*).
- $D_{60}$ = grain size at 60% finer. **[0.5 mark]**

**Coefficient of Curvature:**
$$C_c = \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{60} \cdot D_{10}}$$
- $D_{30}$ = grain size at 30% finer. **[0.5 mark]**

**Significance (0.5 marks each):**
- $C_u$ indicates the **range of grain sizes**. Higher $C_u$ → wider range → well-graded.
  - For sands: $C_u \ge 6$ (well-graded); $C_u < 6$ (poorly graded).
  - For gravels: $C_u \ge 4$ (well-graded); $C_u < 4$ (poorly graded).
- $C_c$ indicates the **shape of the curve** (whether the soil is well-graded in the central range).
  - Well-graded: $1 \le C_c \le 3$.
  - $C_c < 1$ or $C_c > 3$ → gap-graded or poorly graded.
- A soil is classified as **well-graded** only if **both** conditions are satisfied simultaneously.
- These coefficients directly control **permeability, shear strength, and compressibility** of granular soils.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units!** $G_s$, $e$, $n$, $S$ are dimensionless. $\gamma$ values must be in $\text{kN/m}^3$ or $\text{N/m}^3$ consistently.
> 2. **Confusing $e$ and $n$:** $e$ divides by $V_s$ (solids only); $n$ divides by $V$ (total). Mixing these is the most common single error.
> 3. **Using $S = 1$ implicitly without stating it** for "saturated" problems — always mention $S = 100\%$.
> 4. **In oven drying, NOT mentioning** the $105-110^\circ C$ temperature range or the use of a desiccator to prevent moisture re-absorption.
> 5. **In sieve analysis, plotting % finer on a log scale for grain size** — linear X-axis is wrong and loses marks.
> 6. **Not verifying** the master equation $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ when all four values are computed — this is a free correctness check and a great way to grab partial marks even if other steps have errors.
> 7. **In pycnometer formula, mixing up** $(M_2 - M_1) - (M_3 - M_4)$ — the order of subtraction matters. Always check sign conventions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it the morning of your exam.**

### 🔹 The "Big Three" Foundational Equations (MUST MEMORIZE)

1. **$S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$** → The master relationship. Use it constantly.
2. **$\gamma_d = \gamma / (1 + w)$** → Most-used conversion in numericals.
3. **$n = e / (1+e)$** and **$e = n / (1-n)$** → Volume skeleton relations.

### 🔹 The "Magic Six" Derived Unit Weight Formulas (in terms of $G_s$ and $e$)

| Formula | Application |
|---------|-------------|
| $\gamma_d = G_s \gamma_w / (1+e)$ | Always valid |
| $\gamma_{sat} = (G_s + e) \gamma_w / (1+e)$ | When $S = 100\%$ |
| $\gamma' = (G_s - 1) \gamma_w / (1+e)$ | When fully submerged |
| $\gamma = \gamma_d (1 + w)$ | Bulk to dry conversion |
| $\gamma = \gamma_{sat}$ | When soil is saturated |
| $\gamma_{sat} = \gamma_d + n \gamma_w$ | Equivalent form, useful for conceptual clarity |

### 🔹 Determination Methods — Quick Reference

| Property | Method | Key Temperature | Key Formula |
|----------|--------|-----------------|-------------|
| Water content | Oven drying | $105 - 110^\circ C$, 24 hrs | $w = (M_2 - M_3)/(M_3 - M_1) \times 100\%$ |
| Specific gravity (coarse) | Pycnometer | Room temp | $G_s = (M_2-M_1)/[(M_2-M_1)-(M_3-M_4)]$ |
| Specific gravity (fine) | S.G. bottle | Room temp | Same as pycnometer formula |
| Grain size (coarse) | Sieve analysis | Oven-dried sample | % finer = $100 - $ cumulative % retained |

### 🔹 IS Code Numbers (Frequently Asked)

- **IS 2720 Part 2** → Water content (oven drying)
- **IS 2720 Part 3** → Specific gravity (Sections 1 and 2 for pycnometer and bottle respectively)
- **IS 2720 Part 4** → Grain size analysis (sieve analysis)
- **IS 2720 Part 14** → Grain size analysis (sedimentation/hydrometer — covered in Module 2)

### 🔹 Typical Numerical Values (Memorize for "Order of Magnitude" Check)

| Property | Sand | Silt | Clay |
|----------|------|------|------|
| $G_s$ | $2.65 - 2.67$ | $2.67 - 2.70$ | $2.70 - 2.80$ |
| $e$ (natural) | $0.4 - 0.7$ | $0.5 - 0.9$ | $0.6 - 1.5$ |
| $\gamma_d$ ($\text{kN/m}^3$) | $15 - 18$ | $14 - 17$ | $12 - 16$ |
| $w$ (%) | $5 - 25$ | $20 - 40$ | $30 - 100+$ |

### 🔹 Common "Trick" Traps in KTU Problems

1. **Volume given in $\text{cm}^3$, answer expected in $\text{kN/m}^3$** → Convert carefully: $1\ \text{cm}^3 = 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^3$.
2. **"Saturated" but $S$ is not given** → Assume $S = 1.0$.
3. **"Dry" but $M_w$ is not given** → Assume $M_w = 0$, $w = 0\%$.
4. **$M_2 - M_3$ gives $M_w$** in oven drying, **not** $M_2 - M_1$.
5. **In pycnometer, $M_4$ is the *water-only* calibration weighing** — do not confuse with $M_1$.

### 🔹 Quick-Conversion Factors to Remember

- $1\ \text{g/cm}^3 = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3 = 1\ \text{t/m}^3$
- $1\ \text{kN/m}^3 = 101.97\ \text{kgf/m}^3 \approx 0.102\ \text{tf/m}^3$
- $\rho_w = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3 = 1\ \text{g/cm}^3$
- $\gamma_w = 9.81\ \text{kN/m}^3$ (some problems use $9.81$ or $10$)

### 🔹 The "Three-Minute Diagram" — Draw This for Every Numerical

Before solving ANY soil mechanics numerical, draw the three-phase box with $V_s, V_w, V_a$ on the left and $M_s, M_w$ on the right. Mark in **all known** values. Then the unknown will jump out. **This habit alone can save you 1-2 marks per numerical.**

### 🔹 Final Tip — "If you can't remember, derive it!"

If you forget the formula for $\gamma_{sat}$ in terms of $G_s$ and $e$, just go back to basics:
$$\gamma_{sat} = \frac{W_s + W_w}{V} = \frac{G_s V_s \gamma_w + V_v \gamma_w}{V_s + V_v}$$

Substitute $V_v = e V_s$:
$$= \frac{G_s V_s \gamma_w + e V_s \gamma_w}{V_s(1+e)} = \frac{(G_s + e) \gamma_w}{1+e}$$

Same for $\gamma'$:
$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = \frac{(G_s + e) \gamma_w}{1+e} - \gamma_w = \frac{G_s \gamma_w + e\gamma_w - \gamma_w(1+e)}{1+e} = \frac{(G_s - 1)\gamma_w}{1+e}$$

> **These derivations are ALWAYS examinable. Memorize the logic, not just the formula.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sedimentation Analysis — Hydrometer & Pipette Methods

### Hydrometer Analysis — Formal Definition
A **sedimentation analysis** test used in geotechnical engineering to determine the **grain-size distribution** of soil particles finer than **75 µm (passing IS 200 sieve)**. A **soil hydrometer** is a specially calibrated glass float used to measure the **specific gravity (or density)** of a soil-water suspension at a known depth and at known time intervals. The test is governed by **IS 2720 (Part 4) – 1985** and is based on **Stokes' Law**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stokes' Law (1851):** The terminal velocity of a sphere settling in a viscous fluid is proportional to the square of its diameter. Soil particles are *idealized as smooth spheres* for this calculation.

### Pipette Analysis — Formal Definition
An **alternate sedimentation test** in which a fixed volume of soil suspension is drawn off from a **standard depth (10 cm)** at **predetermined time intervals** using a specially designed **pipette**. The drawn sample is then evaporated and weighed to determine the percentage of particles finer than a corresponding "equivalent diameter" (from Stokes' Law). Governed by **IS 2720 (Part 4)**.

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The Muddy Water Race":** Imagine you stir a handful of fine clay and silt into a glass of water and watch it settle. The **heaviest, largest sand grains** hit the bottom within seconds. The **medium silts** settle in minutes. The **microscopic clay particles** stay suspended for hours. By measuring the *density* of the suspension at a particular depth after a known time, you are essentially asking: *"How much fine stuff is still floating up there?"* — That answer tells you what percentage of fines exists. The hydrometer is just a calibrated "density sensor" floating in the muddy water.

### Physical Constants Used
- **Viscosity of water (η)** ≈ **1.0 × 10⁻⁶ kN·s/m²** at 27 °C
- **Specific gravity of soil particles (Gs)** ≈ **2.65 to 2.70** (assumed if not given)
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g)** = **9.81 m/s²**
- **Density of water (ρw)** = **1000 kg/m³ = 1 g/cc**

---

## 1.2 Atterberg Limits and Indices

### Formal Definition
**Atterberg Limits** are the **water content boundaries** at which a fine-grained soil changes its *state/behavior* from *solid* to *semi-solid* to *plastic* and finally to *liquid*. Defined by the Swedish scientist **Albert Atterberg (1911)** and later standardized by **A. Casagrande**.

The four classical limits are:

| Limit | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Shrinkage Limit** | $W_S$ | Max. water content at which soil is *fully saturated*. Below this, soil volume decreases on drying. |
| **Plastic Limit** | $W_P$ | Min. water content at which soil behaves *plastically* (can be rolled into 3 mm threads without crumbling). |
| **Liquid Limit** | $W_L$ | Min. water content at which soil behaves like a *liquid* and flows under its own weight. |
| **Sticky Limit** | $W_T$ | Max. water content at which soil just stops sticking to a metal blade. *(Less commonly used)* |

### Derived Indices
- **Plasticity Index:** $I_P = W_L - W_P$
- **Liquidity Index:** $I_L = \dfrac{W_n - W_P}{I_P}$
- **Consistency Index:** $I_C = \dfrac{W_L - W_n}{I_P}$
- **Shrinkage Index:** $I_S = W_P - W_S$
- **Flow Index:** $I_F$ = slope of flow curve (log of blows vs water content)
- **Toughness Index:** $I_T = I_P / I_F$

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The Butter Analogy":** Take a stick of cold butter (frozen = **solid**). As it warms, it becomes moldable like clay — that's the **plastic** state. Add enough heat and it flows like syrup — that's the **liquid** state. The temperatures at which these state changes happen are the Atterberg Limits. Soils behave the same way with *water content* instead of temperature.

---

## 1.3 Visual Identification by Simple Field Tests

### Formal Definition
A set of **qualitative field tests** used by geotechnical engineers to *visually and manually* classify soils *in-situ* (at the site) without laboratory equipment. Helps in preliminary assessment of soil type, gradation, plasticity, and organic content.

### Common Field Tests
1. **Visual Examination** – Color, grain size, stratification
2. **Smell Test** – Organic soils give a *musty/peaty* smell
3. **Touch/Feel Test** – Gritty (sand), smooth (silt), sticky (clay)
4. **Shaking (Pat) Test** – Silt gives a *glossy, heaving* reaction with water
5. **Thread/Roll Test** – Plasticity assessment
6. **Dilatancy Test** – Reaction of saturated fine soil to shaking
7. **Dry Strength Test** – Crushing dry pat — clay = high strength
8. **Dispersion Test** – Settling rate in water

> [!NOTE]
> These tests form the basis of the **IS 1498 – 1970** field classification system for preliminary identification.

---

## 1.4 Field Density by Core Cutter and Sand Replacement Methods

### Core Cutter Method
A small **steel cylinder (core cutter)** of known volume (usually **1000 cc or 1300 cc**) is driven into the ground. The soil within is trimmed, weighed, and the in-situ **bulk density (γbulk)** and **dry density (γdry)** are determined. Suitable for **soft, fine-grained, cohesive soils**. Governed by **IS 2720 (Part XXIX)**.

### Sand Replacement Method
Used for **cohesive and cohesionless soils**, including those with gravel. A hole is dug in the ground, the excavated soil is weighed, and the **volume of the hole** is determined by filling it with **calibrated uniform sand** poured from a sand-pouring cylinder. Governed by **IS 2720 (Part XXVIII)**.

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The Cup-Cake Analogy":** Imagine you have a muffin (the in-situ soil) and you want to know its density without disturbing it. The **core cutter** is like using a cookie cutter to *pluck a perfect, volume-known cylinder* out of the muffin. The **sand replacement** is like: scoop out a hole, fill the hole with sugar, count how much sugar you needed — that *sugar volume = the hole volume*. Both methods give you *mass* (direct) and *volume* (cleverly) to compute density.

---

## 1.5 Classification of Soils — Necessity & Principles

### Necessity
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Soil classification is the **"Rosetta Stone" of geotechnical engineering**. Soils are naturally highly variable. Without a *standardized classification system*, engineers in different regions would describe soils differently, making communication and design *impossible*. Classification groups soils with *similar engineering behavior* together, enabling:
> - Quick *identification* of soil type
> - *Correlation* with engineering properties
> - *Empirical design* of foundations, pavements, earthworks
> - Smooth *communication* between geologists, engineers, and contractors

### Principles of Classification
Most systems (IS, USCS, AASHTO) classify soils based on:
1. **Grain size distribution** (gradational characteristics)
2. **Plasticity characteristics** (Atterberg Limits)
3. **Group Index (GI)** — empirical quality rating
4. **Organic content**

### IS Classification (IS 1498 – 1970)
Soils are broadly divided into:
- **Coarse-grained** (Gravel + Sand) — > 50 % retained on 75µm sieve
  - Gravels (G) — > 50 % of coarse fraction retained on 4.75 mm
  - Sands (S) — > 50 % of coarse fraction passes 4.75 mm
- **Fine-grained** (Silt + Clay) — > 50 % passes 75 µm
  - Sub-divisions: M (silt), C (clay), O (organic)
- **Highly organic soils** (Pt) — Peat

Sub-classified with secondary letters: **W (well-graded), P (poorly-graded), L (low plasticity), H (high plasticity)**, etc.

### Plasticity Charts
A graph of **Plasticity Index (Ip)** on Y-axis vs. **Liquid Limit (WL)** on X-axis. Used to **differentiate silt from clay** in fine-grained soils. The famous **"A-line"** (Equation: $I_P = 0.73(W_L - 20)$) separates clays (above) from silts (below). The **"U-line"** (upper bound) defines the maximum possible $I_P$ for any $W_L$.

### Group Index (GI)
An **empirical formula** used to rate the *quality* of a subgrade soil (0 = best, 20 = worst). Originally developed for **AASHTO classification** but widely used as a quality indicator.

$$GI = 0.2\,a + 0.005\,ac + 0.01\,bd$$

where the constants depend on % passing 75µm, $W_L$, and $I_P$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sedimentation Analysis — Underlying Theory

### Stokes' Law — Step-by-Step Logic

A soil particle falling through water experiences three forces:
1. **Weight (W)** acting downward = $V \cdot \gamma_s$
2. **Buoyancy (Fb)** acting upward = $V \cdot \gamma_w$
3. **Viscous Drag (Fd)** acting upward = $6\pi \eta r v$

where:
- $V$ = volume of particle
- $\gamma_s$ = unit weight of soil particle
- $\gamma_w$ = unit weight of water
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity of water
- $r$ = radius of particle
- $v$ = terminal velocity

At **terminal velocity**, net force = 0:
$$V\gamma_s = V\gamma_w + 6\pi \eta r v$$

For a sphere: $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$, hence:

$$v = \frac{(\gamma_s - \gamma_w)}{18\eta} \cdot D^2$$

where $D$ = diameter of the particle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stokes' Law is valid only for:**
> - Particle diameter **< 0.2 mm** (fine sand limit)
> - Reynolds Number **< 1** (laminar flow regime)
> - Particle concentration **< 5 %** (avoid hindered settling)

### Hydrometer Equation — Derivation
The hydrometer measures the **density of the suspension** at depth $z$. From the law of conservation of mass, the percentage of particles finer than diameter $D$ at time $t$ is given by:

$$N\% \text{ finer than } D = \frac{G_s}{G_s - 1} \cdot \frac{V}{M} \cdot (R - R_w) \cdot 100$$

where:
- $V$ = volume of suspension (typically 1000 mL)
- $M$ = total mass of soil used (oven-dry)
- $R$ = hydrometer reading in suspension (corrected for meniscus)
- $R_w$ = hydrometer reading in clear water
- $G_s$ = specific gravity of soil solids

The **equivalent particle diameter** $D$ (in mm) settling at depth $z$ in time $t$ (minutes):

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18\eta \cdot z}{(G_s - 1)\gamma_w \cdot t}} \text{ (mm)}$$

Often simplified using **combined correction factor**:

$$D(\text{mm}) = \sqrt{\frac{30\eta \cdot z}{(G_s - 1) \cdot t}}$$

where $z$ is in cm, $t$ in minutes, $\eta$ in poise.

### Temperature Correction
Hydrometer readings must be corrected for **temperature variation** from the calibration temperature (usually 27 °C). A composite correction $C$ is applied:

$$R_{\text{corrected}} = R_h + C$$

where $C$ accounts for:
- **Meniscus correction** ($C_m$)
- **Temperature correction** ($C_t$)
- **Dispersing agent correction** (if Na-hexametaphosphate is used)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite correction** = meniscus correction + temperature correction ± dispersant correction. The sign convention: temperature above 27 °C → positive; below → negative.

---

## 2.2 Atterberg Limits — Theory & Computation

### Liquid Limit — Casagrande's Apparatus
The **Casagrande cup** is lifted and dropped from a height of **10 mm** at a rate of **2 blows/second**. The liquid limit is the **water content at which a 12.5 mm wide groove** closes over a length of **12.5 mm** after exactly **25 blows**.

The **flow curve** is a plot of **water content (Y-axis)** vs. **log(N) (X-axis)** where N = number of blows. A straight line is fitted (best-fit), and:

$$W_L = w_{25} \text{ (water content at 25 blows)}$$

The slope of the flow curve is the **Flow Index (IF)**:

$$I_F = \frac{w_1 - w_2}{\log(N_2) - \log(N_1)}$$

### Liquid Limit — One-Point Method (Quick)
A simplified formula used when only one trial is performed:

$$W_L = w_n \cdot \left(\frac{N}{25}\right)^{\tan\beta}$$

where $\tan\beta$ = 0.121 (or equivalently, $I_F \approx 1.0$ average). Acceptable only when N is between **15 and 35 blows**.

### Plastic Limit Test
Soil is rolled into a **3 mm diameter thread** on a glass plate. The plastic limit is the water content at which the thread **just starts crumbling** at 3 mm and cannot be re-rolled.

### Shrinkage Limit
The shrinkage limit is determined using a **porcelain dish / mercury displacement** method. Volume of soil pat is measured at different moisture contents. The shrinkage limit is the water content at which the soil mass becomes **fully saturated** — beyond this, further drying causes no volume change.

$$W_S = W_0 - \frac{(V_0 - V_d)\rho_w}{M_d}$$

where:
- $W_0$ = initial water content of saturated pat
- $V_0$ = initial volume of saturated pat
- $V_d$ = volume of oven-dried pat
- $M_d$ = mass of oven-dried pat
- $\rho_w$ = density of water

---

## 2.3 Field Density — Theory

### Core Cutter Method
$$\gamma_{\text{bulk}} = \frac{M_{\text{wet soil}} - M_{\text{empty cutter}}}{V_{\text{cutter}}}$$

$$\gamma_{\text{dry}} = \frac{\gamma_{\text{bulk}}}{1 + w}$$

where $w$ is the water content (determined separately by oven drying).

### Sand Replacement Method
Volume of hole is determined by:

$$V_{\text{hole}} = \frac{M_1 - M_2 - M_3}{\gamma_{\text{sand}}}$$

where:
- $M_1$ = mass of sand-pouring cylinder + sand *before* pouring
- $M_2$ = mass of sand-pouring cylinder + sand *after* pouring (residual in cylinder)
- $M_3$ = mass of sand filling the **cone** of the pouring apparatus (calibration)
- $\gamma_{\text{sand}}$ = calibrated bulk density of sand (in g/cc or kN/m³)

Then:
$$\gamma_{\text{bulk}} = \frac{M_{\text{soil}}}{V_{\text{hole}}}; \quad \gamma_{\text{dry}} = \frac{\gamma_{\text{bulk}}}{1 + w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Calibration of sand:** The sand used (typically **Ottawa sand**, passing 600 µm, retained on 300 µm) must be **calibrated** to know its bulk density. This is done using a container of known volume before field use.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

### Table A — Sedimentation Analysis

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\eta \cdot z}{(G_s - 1)\gamma_w \cdot t}}$ | Stokes' particle diameter | mm |
| 2 | $D(\text{mm}) = \sqrt{\dfrac{30\eta \cdot z_{\text{cm}}}{(G_s-1) \cdot t_{\min}}}$ | Simplified form (K constant absorbed) | mm |
| 3 | $N\% = \dfrac{G_s}{G_s - 1} \cdot \dfrac{V}{M} \cdot (R - R_w) \cdot 100$ | Hydrometer percent finer | % |
| 4 | $z = z_R + \dfrac{1}{2}\left(h - \dfrac{V_B}{A}\right)$ | Effective depth correction | cm |
| 5 | $R_{\text{corrected}} = R_h + C_m \pm C_t$ | Composite correction | g/L |
| 6 | $I_P = W_L - W_P$ | Plasticity Index | % |
| 7 | $I_L = \dfrac{w_n - W_P}{I_P}$ | Liquidity Index | — |
| 8 | $I_C = \dfrac{W_L - w_n}{I_P}$ | Consistency Index | — |
| 9 | $I_S = W_P - W_S$ | Shrinkage Index | % |
| 10 | $I_T = I_P / I_F$ | Toughness Index | — |

### Table B — Field Density & Soil Classification

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 11 | $\gamma_{\text{bulk}} = \dfrac{W_s}{V_c}$ | Bulk density (core cutter) | g/cc |
| 12 | $\gamma_{\text{dry}} = \dfrac{\gamma_{\text{bulk}}}{1 + w}$ | Dry density | g/cc |
| 13 | $V_{\text{hole}} = \dfrac{M_1 - M_2 - M_3}{\gamma_{\text{sand}}}$ | Hole volume (sand replacement) | cc |
| 14 | $A\text{-line: } I_P = 0.73(W_L - 20)$ | Clay/Silt boundary | — |
| 15 | $U\text{-line: } I_P = 0.9(W_L - 8)$ | Upper bound | — |
| 16 | $GI = 0.2a + 0.005ac + 0.01bd$ | Group Index | — |
| 17 | $e \cdot S \cdot G_s = w \cdot G_s$ | Phase relationship | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where a, b, c, d for Group Index:**
> - **a** = portion of % passing 75µm between **35 and 75**, expressed as a positive integer (0 to 40)
> - **b** = portion of % passing 75µm between **15 and 35**, expressed as a positive integer (0 to 40)
> - **c** = portion of $W_L$ greater than 40 and up to 60 (0 to 20)
> - **d** = portion of $I_P$ greater than 10 and up to 30 (0 to 20)

---

## 2.5 Plasticity Chart — Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The plasticity chart is *the single most important* tool for fine-grained soil classification in KTU exams. Above the **A-line** with $W_L > 50$ → **CH** (high plasticity clay). Below A-line with $W_L < 50$ → **ML** (low plasticity silt). The chart is a graphical implementation of **Casagrande's classification** adopted by **IS 1498**.

### Engineering Applications
1. **Foundation Design:** CH clays have *low permeability* and *high compressibility* — poor for shallow foundations; need *deep* or *piled* foundations.
2. **Embankment Stability:** Soils above the A-line are *clayey*; susceptible to *shrink-swell* — must be stabilized.
3. **Pavement Subgrade:** Low GI = good subgrade; high GI = poor, needs replacement.
4. **Earth Dam Cores:** CH clays preferred for *impervious cores* due to low permeability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Stokes' Law — Complete Derivation

### Setting Up the Force Balance

A spherical soil particle of diameter $D$ and radius $r$ is settling at terminal velocity $v$ in a viscous fluid. The three forces acting on it are:

**1. Weight of the particle (downward):**
$$W = V \cdot \gamma_s = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \cdot \rho_s \cdot g$$

**2. Buoyant force (upward) = weight of displaced water:**
$$F_b = V \cdot \gamma_w = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \cdot \rho_w \cdot g$$

**3. Viscous drag (upward) — Stokes' drag law for laminar flow:**
$$F_d = 6\pi \eta r v = 3\pi \eta D v$$

### Equilibrium Condition

At **terminal (constant) velocity**, the acceleration is zero. By Newton's Second Law:

$$\sum F = 0$$
$$W = F_b + F_d$$
$$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_s g = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_w g + 3\pi \eta D v$$

### Solving for Velocity

Rearranging:

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 (\rho_s - \rho_w) g = 3\pi \eta D v$$

Substituting $r = D/2$, so $r^3 = D^3/8$:

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi \frac{D^3}{8} (\rho_s - \rho_w) g = 3\pi \eta D v$$

$$\frac{\pi D^3}{6}(\rho_s - \rho_w)g = 3\pi \eta D v$$

Dividing both sides by $\pi D$:

$$\frac{D^2}{6}(\rho_s - \rho_w)g = 3\eta v$$

Solving for $v$:

$$v = \frac{D^2 (\rho_s - \rho_w)g}{18\eta}$$

Or equivalently in terms of unit weights:

$$\boxed{v = \frac{D^2 (\gamma_s - \gamma_w)}{18\eta}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental Stokes' velocity equation** that forms the backbone of both hydrometer and pipette analyses.

### Deriving the Particle Diameter

If a particle settles a distance $z$ in time $t$, then $v = z/t$:

$$\frac{z}{t} = \frac{D^2 (\gamma_s - \gamma_w)}{18\eta}$$

$$\boxed{D = \sqrt{\frac{18\eta \cdot z}{(\gamma_s - \gamma_w) \cdot t}}}$$

Or substituting $\gamma_s = G_s \cdot \gamma_w$:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18\eta \cdot z}{(G_s - 1)\gamma_w \cdot t}}$$

---

## 3.2 Hydrometer — Numerical Worked Example

### Problem
A **50 g** oven-dry soil sample ($G_s = 2.70$) is dispersed in **1000 mL** of water. A hydrometer reading after **4 minutes** gave a corrected reading of **15 g/L** at a depth of **12 cm**. The viscosity of water at test temperature is **1.0 × 10⁻⁶ kN·s/m²** (10 poise in CGS). Compute the **percentage finer** and the **equivalent particle diameter**.

### Step 1: Calculate Particle Diameter D

Using the simplified formula with combined constant:

$$D(\text{mm}) = \sqrt{\frac{30 \cdot \eta \cdot z}{(G_s - 1) \cdot t}}$$

where:
- $\eta = 10^{-4}$ poise (we will use CGS units: $\eta$ in poise = 0.01; $t$ in minutes; $z$ in cm)
- For water at 20 °C: $\eta \approx 1.005$ centipoise = 0.01005 poise
- $z = 12$ cm, $t = 4$ min, $G_s = 2.70$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{30 \times 0.01005 \times 12}{(2.70 - 1) \times 4}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{3.618}{6.80}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{0.5321}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 0.0729 \text{ mm} = 72.9\,\mu m}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Percentage Finer (N)

Using the hydrometer equation:

$$N = \frac{G_s}{G_s - 1} \cdot \frac{V}{M} \cdot (R - R_w) \cdot 100$$

Assume $R_w$ (hydrometer in clear water) = 0 (or use given value).

$$N = \frac{2.70}{2.70 - 1} \cdot \frac{1000}{50} \cdot (15 - 0) \cdot 100$$

$$N = \frac{2.70}{1.70} \cdot 20 \cdot 15 \cdot 100$$

$$N = 1.5882 \cdot 20 \cdot 15 \cdot 100$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 47{,}647\% \text{ (calculation needs } M \text{ in grams)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention check:** If we use $M$ in grams and $V$ in mL, the equation simplifies. The "100" factor is **already in percentage**. A common mistake is double-multiplying by 100.

> [!NOTE]
> **Correct form (commonly used):**
> $$N(\%) = \frac{G_s \cdot \rho_w}{G_s - 1} \cdot \frac{R - R_w}{M/V}$$
> With $M = 50$ g, $V = 1000$ mL, $R - R_w = 15$ g/L:
> $$N = \frac{2.70 \times 1}{1.70} \times \frac{15}{50/1000} = 1.588 \times \frac{15}{0.05} = 1.588 \times 300 = 476.5\%$$
> This shows we need **$R - R_w$ in g/L** and **$M/V$ in g/mL** for a proper check.

The standard KTU-board form is:
$$N = \frac{G_s}{G_s - 1} \cdot \frac{V}{M_s} \cdot (R_h - R_w + C) \times 100$$

where $C$ is the composite correction, and $V/M_s$ has units of (mL/g) and $(R_h + C)$ in g/L. The "100" converts to %.

---

## 3.3 Atterberg Limits — Comprehensive Computation

### Liquid Limit (Casagrande's Multi-Point Method)

| Trial | No. of blows (N) | Water content w (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12 | 56.4 |
| 2 | 18 | 51.2 |
| 3 | 28 | 46.8 |
| 4 | 42 | 42.5 |

The flow curve is plotted with $w$ (Y-axis) vs. $\log N$ (X-axis). A best-fit straight line yields:

$$I_F = \frac{w_1 - w_2}{\log(N_2) - \log(N_1)} = \frac{56.4 - 42.5}{\log 12 - \log 42} = \frac{13.9}{1.0792 - 1.6232} = \frac{13.9}{-0.544} \approx -25.5$$

Taking the magnitude: $|I_F| \approx 25.5$ (% drop per log cycle).

At $N = 25$:
$$\log(25) = 1.3979$$

$$w_{25} = w_4 + I_F \cdot (\log 25 - \log 42) = 42.5 + 25.5 \cdot (1.3979 - 1.6232)$$
$$= 42.5 + 25.5 \cdot (-0.2253) = 42.5 - 5.74$$
$$\boxed{W_L = 36.76\% \text{ (at 25 blows)}}$$

### Plastic Limit Computation

Average water content of 4 trials: 22.8 %, 23.1 %, 22.5 %, 22.9 %

$$W_P = \frac{22.8 + 23.1 + 22.5 + 22.9}{4} = \frac{91.3}{4} = 22.83\%$$

### Plasticity Index

$$\boxed{I_P = W_L - W_P = 36.76 - 22.83 = 13.93\%}$$

### IS Classification (using Plasticity Chart)
- $W_L = 36.76\%$ (< 50) → Low plasticity
- $I_P = 13.93$
- A-line value at $W_L = 36.76$: $I_{P,A} = 0.73(36.76 - 20) = 0.73 \times 16.76 = 12.23$
- Since $I_P$ (13.93) > $I_{P,A}$ (12.23) → **Above A-line** → **Clay**
- Therefore: **CL** (Low plasticity clay)

---

## 3.4 Field Density — Sand Replacement Method (Complete Calculation)

### Given Data
- Mass of soil from hole ($M_s$) = **2850 g**
- Mass of sand-pouring cylinder + sand *before* pouring = **5850 g**
- Mass of sand-pouring cylinder + sand *after* pouring = **2050 g**
- Mass of sand filling the cone (calibration) = **350 g**
- Calibrated bulk density of sand = **1.55 g/cc**
- Water content of soil = **12.5 %**

### Step 1: Calculate Mass of Sand Filling the Hole

$$M_{\text{sand in hole}} = M_1 - M_2 - M_3$$
$$M_{\text{sand in hole}} = 5850 - 2050 - 350 = 3450 \text{ g}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Volume of Hole

$$V_{\text{hole}} = \frac{M_{\text{sand in hole}}}{\gamma_{\text{sand}}} = \frac{3450}{1.55} = 2225.81 \text{ cc}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Bulk Density

$$\gamma_{\text{bulk}} = \frac{M_s}{V_{\text{hole}}} = \frac{2850}{2225.81} = 1.280 \text{ g/cc}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Dry Density

$$\gamma_{\text{dry}} = \frac{\gamma_{\text{bulk}}}{1 + w} = \frac{1.280}{1 + 0.125} = \frac{1.280}{1.125}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{\text{dry}} = 1.138 \text{ g/cc} = 11.17 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

---

## 3.5 Group Index — Full Step-by-Step Computation

### Given Data for a Soil Sample
- Percent passing 75 µm sieve = **62 %**
- Liquid Limit $W_L$ = **48 %**
- Plasticity Index $I_P$ = **22 %**

### Step 1: Compute Constant 'a'
$a$ = portion of % passing 75µm **between 35 and 75**, expressed as a positive integer:
- % passing 75µm = 62
- Portion above 35 = 62 - 35 = 27
- Maximum value of $a$ is 40, and we take the integer part of (62 - 35) = 27
- $$\boxed{a = 27}$$

### Step 2: Compute Constant 'b'
$b$ = portion of % passing 75µm **between 15 and 35**, expressed as positive integer:
- % passing 75µm = 62
- Portion between 15 and 35 = min((62-15), 35-15) = min(47, 20) = 20
- $$\boxed{b = 20}$$

### Step 3: Compute Constant 'c'
$c$ = portion of $W_L$ **greater than 40 and up to 60**, expressed as positive integer:
- $W_L$ = 48
- Portion above 40 = 48 - 40 = 8
- $$\boxed{c = 8}$$

### Step 4: Compute Constant 'd'
$d$ = portion of $I_P$ **greater than 10 and up to 30**, expressed as positive integer:
- $I_P$ = 22
- Portion above 10 = 22 - 10 = 12
- $$\boxed{d = 12}$$

### Step 5: Apply Group Index Formula

$$GI = 0.2a + 0.005ac + 0.01bd$$

$$GI = 0.2(27) + 0.005(27)(8) + 0.01(20)(12)$$

$$GI = 5.4 + 1.08 + 2.40$$

$$\boxed{GI = 8.88 \approx 9 \text{ (rounded to nearest integer)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always round off the Group Index to the nearest whole number.** KTU expects integer or at most one decimal place.

### Interpretation
- GI = 0 → Excellent subgrade
- GI = 1–4 → Good
- GI = 5–9 → Fair (our sample)
- GI = 10–20 → Poor to very poor
