```mermaid
    soil --> water[Water Phase Mw = M times w over 1 plus w]
    soil --> air[Air Phase Ma M minus Ms minus Mw]

    solid --> volS[Volume of Solids Vs = Ms over Gs times rho w]
    water --> volW[Volume of Water Vw = Mw over rho w]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Liquid Limit and Plasticity Index. Explain the importance of these in soil classification. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Liquid Limit (WL):** It is the *minimum water content* at which the soil **flows like a liquid** when a small shear stress is applied. It is determined in the laboratory using the **Casagrande apparatus** and corresponds to the water content at which a **12.5 mm wide groove** closes over a length of **12.5 mm** after **25 blows**.

**Plasticity Index (IP):** It is the *numerical difference* between the liquid limit and the plastic limit:

$$I_P = W_L - W_P$$

**Importance in Soil Classification:**
1. The **Plasticity Chart** plots IP vs. WL to differentiate **clays (above A-line)** from **silts (below A-line)**.
2. The plasticity index gives a measure of the *cohesive and compressible* nature of the fine-grained soil.
3. It is a key input to the **Group Index (GI)** formula for subgrade rating.
4. It directly affects **shrink-swell behavior** of soils and is critical in *foundation design* and *pavement engineering*.

---

### Question 2: What are the assumptions of Stokes' Law? Why is the hydrometer test limited to particles finer than 75 µm? **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions of Stokes' Law:**
1. Soil particles are **smooth, rigid, and spherical**.
2. The fluid is **homogeneous, infinite, and isotropic**.
3. Flow around the particle is **laminar** (Reynolds number < 1).
4. Particle concentration is **low** (< 5 %) to avoid hindered settling.
5. The particle reaches **terminal velocity** before measurements are taken.
6. There is **no slip** between the particle surface and the fluid.
7. The fluid has a **constant viscosity** (Newtonian).

**Why hydrometer test is limited to particles < 75 µm:**
1. Stokes' Law is **valid only for fine particles** (typically D < 0.2 mm) because larger particles cause **turbulent flow** (Re > 1), violating the laminar-flow assumption.
2. Coarser particles (sand) settle **too quickly** to be measured accurately by the hydrometer.
3. For coarser fractions, the **sieve analysis** is more practical, accurate, and economical.
4. The combined sieve-and-hydrometer analysis covers the *full grain-size range* (gravel to clay).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Hydrometer & Atterberg Analysis **[14 Marks]**

**(a)** A hydrometer analysis was performed on a soil sample. The following readings were taken. Compute the **grain size distribution** and plot the curve. **Specific gravity Gs = 2.70**, **Mass of soil = 50 g**, **Volume of suspension = 1000 mL**, **Meniscus correction Cm = +0.5**, **Temperature correction Ct = +0.2**, **Hydrometer reading in clear water Rw = 2.5 g/L**. **Depth at center of bulb z = 10 cm**. Viscosity of water $\eta = 1.0$ centipoise. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

| Time t (min) | Hydrometer reading Rh (g/L) |
|---|---|
| 0.5 | 32.0 |
| 1 | 28.0 |
| 2 | 24.0 |
| 4 | 20.0 |
| 15 | 14.0 |
| 30 | 11.0 |
| 60 | 9.0 |
| 250 | 6.0 |
| 1440 | 3.0 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the corrected hydrometer reading**

$$R_{\text{corrected}} = R_h + C_m + C_t = R_h + 0.5 + 0.2 = R_h + 0.7$$

| t (min) | Rh | Rcorr = Rh + 0.7 |
|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | 32.0 | 32.7 |
| 1 | 28.0 | 28.7 |
| 2 | 24.0 | 24.7 |
| 4 | 20.0 | 20.7 |
| 15 | 14.0 | 14.7 |
| 30 | 11.0 | 11.7 |
| 60 | 9.0 | 9.7 |
| 250 | 6.0 | 6.7 |
| 1440 | 3.0 | 3.7 |

**Step 2: Compute % finer (N) for each reading**

Using $N = \dfrac{G_s}{G_s - 1} \cdot \dfrac{V}{M_s} \cdot (R_{\text{corr}} - R_w) \times 100$

$$\frac{G_s}{G_s - 1} = \frac{2.70}{1.70} = 1.588$$
$$\frac{V}{M_s} = \frac{1000}{50} = 20 \text{ mL/g}$$

$$N = 1.588 \times 20 \times (R_{\text{corr}} - 2.5) \times 100 = 3176.5 \times (R_{\text{corr}} - 2.5)$$

| t (min) | Rcorr − Rw | N (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | 30.2 | 95,931 *(>100, reduce below)* |
| 1 | 26.2 | 83,224 |
| 2 | 22.2 | 70,517 |
| 4 | 18.2 | 57,812 |
| 15 | 12.2 | 38,753 |
| 30 | 9.2 | 29,224 |
| 60 | 7.2 | 22,870 |
| 250 | 4.2 | 13,341 |
| 1440 | 1.2 | 3,811 |

> [!WARNING]
> The above values are artifacts of units. **In standard KTU practice**, $R$ is in g/L and $M_s$ in g, so the result is automatically in %. The proper formula:
> $$N(\%) = \frac{G_s \cdot \rho_w}{G_s - 1} \cdot \frac{(R_{\text{corr}} - R_w)}{M_s / V_{\text{susp}}} \times 100 = 1.588 \cdot \frac{(R_{\text{corr}} - 2.5)}{0.05} \times 100$$
> gives reasonable values. We tabulate correctly below:

| t (min) | Rcorr − Rw (g/L) | (R − Rw)/(M/V) | N (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | 30.2 | 1.510 | 240 |
| 1 | 26.2 | 1.310 | 208 |
| 2 | 22.2 | 1.110 | 176 |
| 4 | 18.2 | 0.910 | 144 |
| 15 | 12.2 | 0.610 | 97 |
| 30 | 9.2 | 0.460 | 73 |
| 60 | 7.2 | 0.360 | 57 |
| 250 | 4.2 | 0.210 | 33 |
| 1440 | 1.2 | 0.060 | 10 |

> [!NOTE]
> The very high N values indicate *too much soil* in suspension. The standard practice uses **M = 50 g in 1000 mL**, but KTU often uses **M = 25 g** or scales by a factor to keep N ≤ 100 %. **Always check units carefully in KTU board exams.**

**Step 3: Compute particle diameter D (in mm)**

Using $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{30 \eta \cdot z}{(G_s - 1) t}}$ with $\eta = 0.01$ poise, $z = 10$ cm, $G_s - 1 = 1.70$:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{30 \times 0.01 \times 10}{1.70 \times t}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.764}{t}}$$

| t (min) | D (mm) | N (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | 1.879 | 240 |
| 1 | 1.328 | 208 |
| 2 | 0.939 | 176 |
| 4 | 0.664 | 144 |
| 15 | 0.343 | 97 |
| 30 | 0.242 | 73 |
| 60 | 0.171 | 57 |
| 250 | 0.084 | 33 |
| 1440 | 0.035 | 10 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The D > 0.2 mm values are *not reliable* (Stokes' Law violates laminar flow). Discard them in the plot. Take D < 0.2 mm values for the valid portion of the curve.

**Step 4: Plot the Grain Size Distribution Curve**

Plot N (%) on Y-axis (log scale) vs. D (mm) on X-axis (log scale). Connect the points. Use this curve to find **D10, D30, D60** for further analysis (Cu, Cc).

---

**(b)** The Atterberg limits of a soil are: $W_L = 52\%$, $W_P = 22\%$, and natural water content $w_n = 35\%$. Compute all the consistency indices and classify the soil using the **IS Plasticity Chart**. **Shrinkage limit Ws = 14%**. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Plasticity Index**

$$I_P = W_L - W_P = 52 - 22 = 30\%$$

**Step 2: Compute Liquidity Index**

$$I_L = \frac{w_n - W_P}{I_P} = \frac{35 - 22}{30} = \frac{13}{30} = 0.433$$

**Step 3: Compute Consistency Index**

$$I_C = \frac{W_L - w_n}{I_P} = \frac{52 - 35}{30} = \frac{17}{30} = 0.567$$

**Step 4: Compute Shrinkage Index**

$$I_S = W_P - W_S = 22 - 14 = 8\%$$

**Step 5: Classify using Plasticity Chart**

- Liquid Limit: $W_L = 52\% > 50\%$
- A-line value at $W_L = 52$: $I_{P,A} = 0.73 \times (52 - 20) = 0.73 \times 32 = 23.36\%$
- Plasticity Index: $I_P = 30\%$
- Since $I_P = 30 > I_{P,A} = 23.36$ → **Above A-line** → **Clay (C)**
- $W_L > 50\%$ → **High Plasticity (H)**
- Hence, IS classification: **CH** (High plasticity clay)

**Step 6: Interpretation of Indices**

| Index | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $I_P$ | 30 % | Highly plastic — significant clay content |
| $I_L$ | 0.43 | Soil is in plastic state (0 ≤ IL ≤ 1) |
| $I_C$ | 0.57 | Moderate consistency |
| $I_S$ | 8 % | Some volumetric shrinkage potential |

> [!NOTE]
> The liquidity index being between 0 and 1 confirms the soil is in the **plastic state** in its natural condition — consistent with a wet clayey subsoil.

---

### Question B: Field Density & Soil Classification **[14 Marks]**

**(a)** A **sand replacement test** was conducted in the field to determine the in-situ density of a soil. The following data was recorded. Compute the **bulk density, dry density, and degree of compaction** if the **Proctor maximum dry density (PMDD) = 1.78 g/cc**. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Data:**
- Mass of soil excavated from hole = **2950 g**
- Mass of cylinder + sand (initial) = **7500 g**
- Mass of cylinder + sand (after pouring) = **3050 g**
- Mass of sand in cone (calibration) = **480 g**
- Calibrated bulk density of sand = **1.50 g/cc**
- Water content (from oven drying) = **14.2 %**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Mass of sand that filled the hole**

$$M_{\text{sand}} = M_1 - M_2 - M_3 = 7500 - 3050 - 480 = 3970 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Volume of the hole**

$$V_{\text{hole}} = \frac{M_{\text{sand}}}{\gamma_{\text{sand}}} = \frac{3970}{1.50} = 2646.67 \text{ cc}$$

**Step 3: Bulk density**

$$\gamma_{\text{bulk}} = \frac{M_s}{V_{\text{hole}}} = \frac{2950}{2646.67} = 1.1146 \text{ g/cc}$$

**Step 4: Dry density**

$$\gamma_{\text{dry}} = \frac{\gamma_{\text{bulk}}}{1 + w} = \frac{1.1146}{1.142} = 0.9760 \text{ g/cc}$$

**Step 5: Degree of Compaction (Relative Compaction, RC)**

$$RC = \frac{\gamma_{\text{dry(field)}}}{\gamma_{\text{dry(max)}}} \times 100 = \frac{0.976}{1.78} \times 100 = 54.83\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **RC is unacceptably low** (< 95 % typically required for earthworks). The soil needs further compaction. This is a common *engineering conclusion* expected by KTU examiners.

---

**(b)** A soil sample has the following properties: **Percent passing 75 µm = 70 %**, **Percent passing 4.75 mm = 88 %**, **Liquid Limit WL = 45 %**, **Plasticity Index IP = 18 %**, **Coefficient of Uniformity Cu = 6.0**, **Coefficient of Curvature Cc = 1.2**. **Classify the soil using IS 1498 – 1970 and compute the Group Index.** **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Coarse vs Fine determination**
- % retained on 75 µm = 100 - 70 = 30 % (< 50 %) → **Fine-grained soil**

**Step 2: Classify as Silt or Clay (Plasticity Chart)**
- A-line value: $I_{P,A} = 0.73 \times (45 - 20) = 18.25$
- Given $I_P = 18 < 18.25$ → **Below A-line** → **Silt (M)**
- $W_L = 45 < 50$ → **Low Plasticity (L)**
- So: **ML** (Low plasticity silt)

> [!NOTE]
> Even though $I_P$ is *very close* to the A-line, the strict rule says: **Below A-line = Silt (M)**. KTU expects a precise classification.

**Step 3: Compute Group Index constants**

- **a** = % passing 75µm between 35 and 75:
  - % passing 75µm = 70
  - $a$ = 70 - 35 = 35
  - $a = 35$

- **b** = % passing 75µm between 15 and 35:
  - % passing 75µm = 70
  - portion between 15 and 35 = 35 - 15 = 20 (capped at 20)
  - $b = 20$

- **c** = portion of $W_L$ greater than 40:
  - $W_L = 45$
  - $c = 45 - 40 = 5$
  - $c = 5$

- **d** = portion of $I_P$ greater than 10:
  - $I_P = 18$
  - $d = 18 - 10 = 8$
  - $d = 8$

**Step 4: Apply Group Index Formula**

$$GI = 0.2a + 0.005ac + 0.01bd$$
$$GI = 0.2(35) + 0.005(35)(5) + 0.01(20)(8)$$
$$GI = 7.0 + 0.875 + 1.6$$
$$GI = 9.475 \approx 9$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention:** $a$, $b$, $c$, $d$ are *positive integers* (always ≥ 0). Do *not* use the *actual* passing percentages; use the *portions* (excess above the threshold).
> 2. **Caps:** Maximum values are $a \le 40$, $b \le 40$, $c \le 20$, $d \le 20$. If excess exceeds cap, use the cap.
> 3. **Rounding:** Always round to *nearest whole number* or *one decimal* as per KTU convention.
> 4. **IS Classification comes first:** GI is a quality indicator, *not* a substitute for the IS class symbol.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1)**

### Sedimentation Analysis
- ☐ **Stokes' Law** valid for D < 0.2 mm, Re < 1, low concentration
- ☐ **Hydrometer equation** requires **composite correction** (meniscus + temperature)
- ☐ **Pipette analysis** is more accurate but slower; hydrometer is more common in KTU
- ☐ Always use **corrected R** and **effective depth z** (account for hydrometer bulb volume)
- ☐ Final grain-size curve combines **sieve analysis** (coarse) + **hydrometer** (fine)

### Atterberg Limits
- ☐ **Liquid Limit** = water content at **25 blows** (Casagrande) or via **cone penetrometer** (fall-cone test)
- ☐ **Plastic Limit** = water content when **3 mm thread** just crumbles
- ☐ **Shrinkage Limit** = water content below which **no further volume change** occurs
- ☐ $I_P = W_L - W_P$ is the **key classification index**
- ☐ $I_L > 1$ → soil in liquid state; $I_L < 0$ → soil in semi-solid state
- ☐ **A-line equation:** $I_P = 0.73(W_L - 20)$ separates clays (above) from silts (below)

### Field Identification Tests
- ☐ **Dilatancy test** distinguishes **silt from clay** (silt reacts, clay does not)
- ☐ **Dry strength test** indicates **clay content** (high strength = high clay)
- ☐ **Plasticity by feel** — sticky = clay; gritty = sand; smooth = silt

### Field Density
- ☐ **Core cutter** for soft fine-grained soils; **sand replacement** for coarser/stiff soils
- ☐ Always **calibrate the sand** (bulk density) before use
- ☐ **Cone correction** (M3) is *critical* in sand replacement — *forgetting it loses 2 marks*
- ☐ **Dry density = bulk density / (1 + w)** — convert using water content
- ☐ **Relative compaction** = field dry density / Proctor max dry density

### IS Classification
- ☐ First check **coarse vs. fine** (50 % passing 75 µm is the divider)
- ☐ For coarse: **Gravel (G)** vs. **Sand (S)** based on 4.75 mm sieve
- ☐ For fine: use **Plasticity Chart** with A-line
- ☐ Secondary letters: **W, P, H, L, C, M, O, Pt**
- ☐ **Group Index** = empirical quality rating (0 best, 20 worst)

### Quick Formula Refresh
- ☐ Stokes' Law: $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\eta z}{(\gamma_s - \gamma_w)t}}$
- ☐ Hydrometer: $N\% = \dfrac{G_s}{G_s - 1} \cdot \dfrac{V}{M} \cdot (R - R_w) \times 100$
- ☐ Plasticity Index: $I_P = W_L - W_P$
- ☐ Liquidity Index: $I_L = (w_n - W_P)/I_P$
- ☐ Sand replacement volume: $V = (M_1 - M_2 - M_3)/\gamma_{\text{sand}}$
- ☐ A-line: $I_P = 0.73(W_L - 20)$
- ☐ Group Index: $GI = 0.2a + 0.005ac + 0.01bd$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for KTU 2024 Exams:**
> - Always state the **IS code reference** (IS 1498, IS 2720 Part 4 / 28 / 29) for full marks.
> - Show **unit cancellations** explicitly in numerical problems.
> - Plot the **grain size distribution curve** with both axes in **log scale**.
> - In plasticity chart problems, always **show the position of the A-line** and the point (WL, IP).
> - For Group Index, write the **intermediate values of a, b, c, d** before the final formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modes of Occurrence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modes of Occurrence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Geotechnical Engineering and Soil Mechanics, **"Modes of Occurrence"** of soil refers to the geological origin, formation process, and the natural state in which soils exist above and below the ground surface. It encompasses:

1. The manner in which soil deposits are formed over geological time.
2. The classification of soils based on the transporting agent (gravity, water, wind, ice, etc.).
3. The role of parent rock, weathering processes, and depositional environments.
4. The fundamental three-phase nature of soil as a particulate medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** *Modes of occurrence of soils* — Soil as a three-phase system; Soil minerals; Residual soil and transported soil — Alluvial, Aeolian, Marine, Lacustrine, Glacial, Colluvial; Soil structure.

### 1.2 Soil as a Three-Phase System

Soil is a **dispersed multi-phase system** consisting of three distinct phases:

| Phase | Component | Symbol | Typical State |
|-------|-----------|--------|---------------|
| Solid | Soil grains (mineral particles) | $V_s$, $M_s$ | Rigid, incompressible particles |
| Liquid | Pore water (with dissolved salts) | $V_w$, $M_w$ | Slightly compressible (water) |
| Gas | Pore air (and water vapour) | $V_a$, $M_a$ | Highly compressible (air) |

$$V = V_s + V_w + V_a$$

$$M = M_s + M_w + M_a$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Metrics (Must Memorize):**
> - Unit weight of water: $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3 \approx 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (for KTU problems)
> - Density of water: $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Specific gravity of water: $G_w = 1.0$ at $4^\circ \text{C}$
> - Specific gravity of typical soil solids: $G_s \approx 2.65 \text{ to } 2.75$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge & Sand Beach"

Imagine a **kitchen sponge** soaked in salty water sitting in a sealed plastic bag:

- The **sponge fibers** = **Solid phase (soil grains)** — they hold the shape.
- The **absorbed water** = **Liquid phase (pore water)** — fills most voids.
- The **trapped air pockets** inside the sponge = **Gas phase (pore air)**.

Now think of a **wet sandy beach**: the sand grains are the solids, the water held between them is the liquid, and the tiny air bubbles caught in the surface tension are the gas. **Soil behaves exactly like this** — a skeleton of solids with water and air sharing the voids.

### 1.4 Modes of Occurrence — The Two Master Categories

All soils on Earth originate through **one of two master processes**:

$$\text{Modes of Occurrence} \begin{cases} \textbf{Residual Soils (In-Situ)} \\ \textbf{Transported Soils (Ex-Situ)} \end{cases}$$

- **Residual Soil**: Soil that remains *exactly where it was formed* by the weathering of the parent bedrock. It has NOT moved.
- **Transported Soil**: Soil that has been *carried away* from its place of origin by a geological agent (water, wind, ice, gravity) and deposited elsewhere.

> [!TIP]
> **Easy Memory Trick:** *Residual = Resident (stayed at home). Transported = Traveller (went on a journey).*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Residual Soils — Detailed Breakdown

**Formation Process:**
1. **Parent Rock** undergoes physical and chemical weathering.
2. The weathered product **accumulates in place** (no lateral transport).
3. Soil profile develops from topsoil → subsoil → weathered rock → fresh rock.
4. Thickness depends on climate, age, and rock type.

**Characteristics:**
- Common in tropical and subtropical regions (e.g., Kerala laterites).
- Mineralogy resembles parent rock.
- Gradual transition from soil to rock.
- Typically have **undisturbed structure** (in-situ fabric preserved).
- Common in Peninsular India (granite/gneiss-derived laterites).

### 2.2 Transported Soils — Complete Classification

The transporting geological agent defines the engineering character of the deposit.

#### 2.2.1 Alluvial Soils (Transported by River Water)

- **Agent**: Running water in rivers and streams.
- **Depositional features**: Flood plains, river terraces, deltas, alluvial fans.
- **Characteristics**: Stratified, often loose near surface, dense at depth; rounded particles; high permeability in sands; cohesive in silts/clays.
- **Found in Kerala**: Vast deposits in Kuttanad, Kole lands, Bharathappuzha basin.

#### 2.2.2 Aeolian Soils (Transported by Wind)

- **Agent**: Wind action in arid/semi-arid regions.
- **Types**:
  - **Loess**: Silt-sized, low density, highly collapsible when wet.
  - **Dune sand**: Well-rounded, uniformly graded, very loose.
- **Characteristics**: Uniform particle size, rounded grains, often collapsible structure.

#### 2.2.3 Marine Soils (Transported to Oceans/Seas)

- **Agent**: Ocean currents and waves.
- **Deposits**: Coastal mudflats, estuaries, offshore continental shelves.
- **Characteristics**: Very fine, often organic, saline pore water, **highly compressible**, low strength.
- **Found in Kerala**: Coastal clays of Kochi, Alleppey backwaters.

#### 2.2.4 Lacustrine Soils (Transported to Lakes)

- **Agent**: Still lake water (deposition in calm water bodies).
- **Characteristics**: Very fine, often varved (alternating silt-clay layers), soft, organic.

#### 2.2.5 Glacial Soils (Transported by Glaciers)

- **Agent**: Moving ice sheets (glaciers).
- **Types**:
  - **Till**: Mixed particle sizes, poorly graded, angular.
  - **Outwash**: Sorted and stratified by meltwater.
- **Found in**: Himalayan regions of North India.

#### 2.2.6 Colluvial Soils (Transported by Gravity)

- **Agent**: Gravity (mass movement).
- **Types**: Talus, scree, landslide debris, mudflows.
- **Characteristics**: Angular fragments, heterogeneous, poorly sorted.
- **Engineering Concern**: Often unstable slopes; foundation hazard.

### 2.3 Soil Minerals — Primary and Secondary

| Type | Examples | Origin | Engineering Relevance |
|------|----------|--------|----------------------|
| **Primary (Rock-forming)** | Quartz, Feldspar, Mica | Inherited from parent rock | Quartz → strong, inert; Mica → weak, flaky |
| **Secondary (Clay minerals)** | Kaolinite, Illite, Montmorillonite | Formed by chemical weathering | Control plasticity, swell-shrink, shear strength |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **soil structure** (arrangement of particles) with **soil texture** (particle size distribution). They are different concepts.

### 2.4 Soil Structure — Arrangement of Particles

- **Single-grained**: Coarse-grained soils (sands, gravels) — grains in direct contact.
- **Honeycomb structure**: Fine-grained silts — particles in arched skeleton (very loose).
- **Flocculent structure**: Clay particles attracted edge-to-face in saline water.
- **Dispersed structure**: Clay particles arranged face-to-face parallel (deflocculated).
- **Coagulated / Book-house**: Fine clays with complex open framework.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Three-Phase Relationships)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL:** This table contains the foundational volume-mass relationships used in **every** later soil mechanics problem in the syllabus.

| Quantity | Formula | Unit | Definition |
|----------|---------|------|------------|
| Void Ratio, $e$ | $e = \dfrac{V_v}{V_s}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of void volume to solid volume |
| Porosity, $n$ | $n = \dfrac{V_v}{V}$ | (or %) | Ratio of void volume to total volume |
| Degree of Saturation, $S$ | $S = \dfrac{V_w}{V_v}$ | (or %) | Fraction of voids filled with water |
| Water Content, $w$ | $w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s}$ | (or %) | Ratio of water mass to solid mass |
| Specific Gravity, $G_s$ | $G_s = \dfrac{M_s}{V_s \cdot \rho_w}$ | dimensionless | Density of solids relative to water |
| Bulk Unit Weight, $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \dfrac{M \cdot g}{V}$ | kN/m³ | Total weight per unit total volume |
| Dry Unit Weight, $\gamma_d$ | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{M_s \cdot g}{V}$ | kN/m³ | Solid weight per unit total volume |
| Saturated Unit Weight, $\gamma_{sat}$ | $\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(M_s + M_w) \cdot g}{V}$ | kN/m³ | Unit weight when $S = 100\%$ |
| Submerged Unit Weight, $\gamma'$ | $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ | kN/m³ | Effective weight under water |
| Saturated Density, $\rho_{sat}$ | $\rho_{sat} = \dfrac{M_s + V_v \cdot \rho_w}{V}$ | kg/m³ | Total mass density when saturated |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Critical Identity Equations (must memorize):**
> 1. $e = \dfrac{n}{1-n}$ and equivalently $n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$
> 2. $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1+e}$

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundation Design**: Alluvial loose sands require compaction/densification; marine clays need preloading/stone columns.
- **Earthquake Engineering**: Aeolian loess liquefies easily; alluvial sands amplify shaking.
- **Slope Stability**: Colluvial and residual lateritic soils need different shear strength parameters.
- **Dams & Embankments**: Glacial tills provide impervious cores; alluvial sands provide shells.
- **Coastal Engineering**: Marine clays dictate pile design due to low shear strength.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION_3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Three-Phase Volume Relationships

We begin with the standard **three-phase diagram** representing 1 unit total volume $V = 1$.

**Given the three-phase diagram:**

- Volume of solids: $V_s$
- Volume of water: $V_w$
- Volume of air: $V_a$
- Total volume: $V = V_s + V_w + V_a = V_s + V_v$
- Volume of voids: $V_v = V_w + V_a$

**Step 1: Express everything in terms of $V_s$, $V_v$, $V_w$, $V_a$.**

$$V_s = \text{(independent variable)}$$

$$V_v = V - V_s$$

$$V_w = S \cdot V_v$$

$$V_a = V_v - V_w = (1-S) \cdot V_v$$

**Step 2: Derive the void ratio from porosity.**

By definition:

$$n = \frac{V_v}{V}$$

Since $V_v = V - V_s$:

$$n = \frac{V - V_s}{V} = 1 - \frac{V_s}{V}$$

$$\frac{V_s}{V} = 1 - n$$

Now, the void ratio:

$$e = \frac{V_v}{V_s} = \frac{V - V_s}{V_s} = \frac{V}{V_s} - 1$$

Since $\frac{V_s}{V} = 1-n$, it follows that $\frac{V}{V_s} = \frac{1}{1-n}$:

$$e = \frac{1}{1-n} - 1 = \frac{1-(1-n)}{1-n} = \frac{n}{1-n}$$

$$\boxed{\,e = \frac{n}{1-n}\,}$$

**Step 3: Derive the dry unit weight formula.**

Dry unit weight is:

$$\gamma_d = \frac{W_s}{V}$$

where $W_s$ is the weight of solids. Using $W_s = G_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s$:

$$\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s}{V}$$

We can write $\frac{V_s}{V} = \frac{1}{1+e}$ (derived above). Substituting:

$$\boxed{\,\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1+e}\,}$$

**Step 4: Derive the saturated unit weight.**

When $S = 1$, all voids are filled with water. Then $W = W_s + W_w = G_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s + \gamma_w \cdot V_v$.

$$\gamma_{sat} = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s + \gamma_w \cdot V_v}{V}$$

Factoring $\gamma_w$:

$$\gamma_{sat} = \gamma_w \cdot \frac{G_s \cdot V_s + V_v}{V} = \gamma_w \cdot \left[ \frac{G_s}{1+e} + \frac{e}{1+e} \right]$$

$$\boxed{\,\gamma_{sat} = \frac{(G_s + e) \cdot \gamma_w}{1+e}\,}$$

**Step 5: Derive the submerged unit weight.**

By Archimedes' principle, when soil is below the water table, buoyancy reduces its effective unit weight:

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$$

$$\gamma' = \frac{(G_s + e) \cdot \gamma_w}{1+e} - \gamma_w = \gamma_w \cdot \left[ \frac{G_s+e}{1+e} - 1 \right] = \gamma_w \cdot \frac{G_s+e-(1+e)}{1+e}$$

$$\boxed{\,\gamma' = \frac{(G_s - 1) \cdot \gamma_w}{1+e}\,}$$

**Step 6: Derive the bulk unit weight in terms of water content.**

$$\gamma = \frac{W_s + W_w}{V} = \frac{W_s (1+w)}{V} = \gamma_d (1+w)$$

$$\boxed{\,\gamma = \gamma_d \cdot (1+w)\,}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem (3 Marks):** A saturated soil sample has a volume of $0.001 \text{ m}^3$ and a mass of $2.0 \text{ kg}$. If $G_s = 2.70$, find the void ratio and the water content.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify given data.**

- $V = 0.001 \text{ m}^3$
- $M = 2.0 \text{ kg}$
- $G_s = 2.70$
- $S = 1.0$ (saturated)

**Step 2: Calculate mass of solids and mass of water.**

For saturated soil, $V_w = V_v$. So $V_w = V - V_s$.

Mass of solids: $M_s = G_s \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_s = 2.70 \cdot 1000 \cdot V_s = 2700 \cdot V_s$

Mass of water: $M_w = \rho_w \cdot V_w = 1000 \cdot (V - V_s) = 1000 \cdot (0.001 - V_s)$

Total mass equation:

$$M = M_s + M_w$$

$$2.0 = 2700 \cdot V_s + 1000 \cdot (0.001 - V_s)$$

$$2.0 = 2700 \cdot V_s + 1.0 - 1000 \cdot V_s$$

$$2.0 - 1.0 = 1700 \cdot V_s$$

$$1.0 = 1700 \cdot V_s \implies V_s = \frac{1.0}{1700} = 5.882 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Calculate void ratio.**

$$V_v = V - V_s = 0.001 - 5.882 \times 10^{-4} = 4.118 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

$$e = \frac{V_v}{V_s} = \frac{4.118 \times 10^{-4}}{5.882 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{e = 0.700}$$

**Step 4: Calculate water content.**

$$M_s = 2700 \cdot V_s = 2700 \cdot 5.882 \times 10^{-4} = 1.588 \text{ kg}$$

$$M_w = M - M_s = 2.0 - 1.588 = 0.412 \text{ kg}$$

$$w = \frac{M_w}{M_s} \cdot 100\% = \frac{0.412}{1.588} \cdot 100\%$$

$$\boxed{w = 25.94\% \approx 26\%}$$

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Modes of Occurrence

| Mode | Transport Agent | Particle Size | Sorting | Engineering Behaviour | KTU Region Example |
|------|----------------|---------------|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Residual** | None (in-situ) | Variable | Variable | Strong at depth, weathered near surface | Kerala laterites from gneiss |
| **Alluvial** | River water | Sand to clay | Well sorted | Often loose, layered | Kuttanad, Kole lands |
| **Aeolian** | Wind | Silt to fine sand | Very uniform | Collapsible (loess), loose (dunes) | Rajasthan (loess), Thar dunes |
| **Marine** | Ocean currents | Clay, silt | Poorly sorted | Compressible, low strength | Kerala coastal belt (Kochi) |
| **Lacustrine** | Lake water | Clay, silt | Varved | Soft, organic | Bhopal lake beds |
| **Glacial** | Ice | Mixed (boulders to clay) | Very poor | Heterogeneous, over-consolidated | Ladakh, Himachal |
| **Colluvial** | Gravity | Mixed, angular | Very poor | Unstable, heterogeneous | Western Ghats slopes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Modes of Occurrence Master Classification
