<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` *What are residual soils? How do they differ from transported soils?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Residual soils** are those that remain *at the location of their parent rock formation*, formed by in-situ weathering (physical, chemical, biological) without any lateral movement. (1.5 Marks)
- **Difference**: Transported soils are those which have been *carried away* from their place of origin by natural agents (water, wind, ice, gravity) and deposited elsewhere, acquiring a different structure and mineralogy from the parent rock. (1.5 Marks)

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` *List any six modes of occurrence of transported soils with the transporting agent.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):** *(½ Mark Each)*
1. **Alluvial** — River water
2. **Marine** — Ocean water / sea waves
3. **Lacustrine** — Lake water
4. **Aeolian** — Wind
5. **Glacial** — Glaciers (ice sheets)
6. **Colluvial** — Gravity (mass wasting)

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the different modes of occurrence of soils with neat sketches. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Residual Soils** (2 Marks)
   - Formed in-situ by weathering of parent rock.
   - No transport involved.
   - Properties depend on parent rock type.
   - Example: Laterite soils of Kerala derived from gneiss.

2. **Transported Soils** (5 Marks — 1 Mark each)
   - **Alluvial**: Transported by rivers. Deposits in floodplains and deltas. Example: Indo-Gangetic plains.
   - **Marine**: Deposited in ocean/sea. Soft, organic, saline clays. Example: Kerala coastal belt.
   - **Aeolian**: Wind-deposited. Includes loess (collapsible silt) and dune sand.
   - **Lacustrine**: Lake-deposited. Often varved clays.
   - **Glacial**: Ice-transported tills. Heterogeneous, over-consolidated.
   - **Colluvial**: Gravity-transported. Talus and landslide debris.

**(b)** A saturated soil sample has volume $0.002 \text{ m}^3$ and mass $3.6 \text{ kg}$. Given $G_s = 2.72$, determine the void ratio, water content, and saturated unit weight. Take $\gamma_w = 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 0.002 \text{ m}^3$
- $M = 3.6 \text{ kg}$
- $G_s = 2.72$
- $S = 1.0$ (saturated)
- $\gamma_w = 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$

**Step 1: Find mass of solids and water.**

$M_s = G_s \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_s = 2720 \cdot V_s$

$M_w = \rho_w \cdot (V - V_s) = 1000 \cdot (0.002 - V_s)$

$M = M_s + M_w = 3.6 \text{ kg}$

$$3.6 = 2720 \cdot V_s + 1000 \cdot (0.002 - V_s)$$

$$3.6 = 2720 \cdot V_s + 2.0 - 1000 \cdot V_s$$

$$1.6 = 1720 \cdot V_s$$

$$V_s = 9.302 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Void Ratio.**

$$V_v = V - V_s = 0.002 - 9.302 \times 10^{-4} = 1.0698 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$e = \frac{V_v}{V_s} = \frac{1.0698 \times 10^{-3}}{9.302 \times 10^{-4}} = \boxed{1.150}$$

**Step 3: Water Content.**

$$M_s = 2720 \cdot 9.302 \times 10^{-4} = 2.530 \text{ kg}$$

$$M_w = 3.6 - 2.530 = 1.070 \text{ kg}$$

$$w = \frac{1.070}{2.530} \cdot 100\% = \boxed{42.29\%}$$

**Step 4: Saturated Unit Weight.**

$$\gamma_{sat} = \frac{(G_s + e) \cdot \gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{(2.72 + 1.150) \cdot 10}{1 + 1.150} = \frac{38.70}{2.150}$$

$$\gamma_{sat} = \boxed{18.0 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a three-phase diagram, derive the relationship between void ratio $(e)$ and porosity $(n)$. Also derive the expression for dry unit weight $\gamma_d$ in terms of $G_s$, $e$, and $\gamma_w$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Three-phase diagram (1 Mark)**

[Shown above in Section 4] — Total volume $V$ divided into $V_s$ and $V_v$; $V_v$ divided into $V_w$ and $V_a$.

**Step 2: Relationship between $e$ and $n$ (3 Marks)**

By definition:
$$e = \frac{V_v}{V_s}, \quad n = \frac{V_v}{V}$$

From the three-phase diagram, $V = V_s + V_v$. Therefore:

$$\frac{V_s}{V} = \frac{V - V_v}{V} = 1 - n$$

Taking reciprocals:

$$\frac{V}{V_s} = \frac{1}{1-n}$$

Now:

$$e = \frac{V_v}{V_s} = \frac{V_v}{V} \cdot \frac{V}{V_s} = n \cdot \frac{1}{1-n}$$

$$\boxed{e = \frac{n}{1-n}}$$

Equivalently, $n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$.

**Step 3: Derivation of $\gamma_d$ (3 Marks)**

Dry unit weight:

$$\gamma_d = \frac{W_s}{V}$$

Mass of solids: $W_s = G_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s$. Therefore:

$$\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s}{V} = G_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot \frac{V_s}{V} = G_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot \frac{1}{1+e}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1+e}}$$

**(b)** A soil sample has $V = 0.015 \text{ m}^3$, $M = 26 \text{ kg}$, $w = 18\%$, and $G_s = 2.65$. Compute $\gamma$, $\gamma_d$, $e$, $n$, and $S$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 0.015 \text{ m}^3$, $M = 26 \text{ kg}$, $w = 0.18$, $G_s = 2.65$

**Step 1: Mass of solids and water.**

$w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s} \implies M_w = 0.18 \cdot M_s$

Also $M_s + M_w = 26 \text{ kg}$:

$$M_s + 0.18 \cdot M_s = 26 \implies 1.18 \cdot M_s = 26 \implies M_s = 22.034 \text{ kg}$$

$$M_w = 26 - 22.034 = 3.966 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Bulk unit weight ($\gamma$).**

$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{26 \cdot 9.81}{0.015} = \frac{255.06}{0.015} = \boxed{17.0 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

(Or with $\gamma_w = 10$: $\gamma = 260/0.015 = 17.33 \text{ kN/m}^3$)

**Step 3: Dry unit weight ($\gamma_d$).**

$$\gamma_d = \frac{W_s}{V} = \frac{22.034 \cdot 9.81}{0.015} = \boxed{14.41 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

**Step 4: Void ratio ($e$).**

Volume of solids: $V_s = \dfrac{M_s}{G_s \cdot \rho_w} = \dfrac{22.034}{2.65 \cdot 1000} = 8.315 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$

$$V_v = V - V_s = 0.015 - 8.315 \times 10^{-3} = 6.685 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$e = \frac{V_v}{V_s} = \frac{6.685 \times 10^{-3}}{8.315 \times 10^{-3}} = \boxed{0.804}$$

**Step 5: Porosity ($n$).**

$$n = \frac{e}{1+e} = \frac{0.804}{1.804} = \boxed{0.4456 \text{ or } 44.56\%}$$

**Step 6: Degree of saturation ($S$).**

Volume of water: $V_w = \dfrac{M_w}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{3.966}{1000} = 3.966 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$

$$S = \frac{V_w}{V_v} = \frac{3.966 \times 10^{-3}}{6.685 \times 10^{-3}} = \boxed{0.5932 \text{ or } 59.32\%}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing volume and mass** — always check whether the question gives $V$ or $M$, and convert units consistently ($\text{cm}^3$ to $\text{m}^3$, g to kg).
> 2. **Forgetting $\gamma_w$ units** — use $9.81$ kN/m³ for SI or $10$ kN/m³ for "approximate" KTU exam problems (read the question!).
> 3. **Mixing up $\gamma_{sat}$ and $\gamma_d$** — $\gamma_{sat}$ includes water weight; $\gamma_d$ does not.
> 4. **Wrong void-ratio formulas** — for saturated soil use $e = \dfrac{w \cdot G_s}{S}$; for $S=1$, this simplifies to $e = w \cdot G_s$.
> 5. **Not drawing the three-phase diagram** in derivations — KTU examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if the diagram is missing in 14-mark questions.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Two master modes**: Residual (in-situ) vs Transported (moved by agent).
- [x] **Six transported types**: Alluvial, Marine, Lacustrine, Aeolian, Glacial, Colluvial.
- [x] **Three-phase system**: Solid + Liquid + Gas; $V = V_s + V_w + V_a$.
- [x] **Key identities**: $e = \dfrac{n}{1-n}$, $n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$, $Se = wG_s$.
- [x] **Master unit weight formulas**:
  - $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \gamma_w}{1+e}$
  - $\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(G_s + e)\gamma_w}{1+e}$
  - $\gamma' = \dfrac{(G_s - 1)\gamma_w}{1+e}$
  - $\gamma = \gamma_d(1+w)$
- [x] **Clay minerals** (secondary) control plasticity: Kaolinite (low), Illite (medium), Montmorillonite (very high — expansive).
- [x] **Soil structure types**: Single-grained, Honeycomb, Flocculent, Dispersed, Coagulated.
- [x] **Kerala-specific contexts**: Laterite (residual), Kuttanad alluvium, coastal marine clays — expect regional questions.
- [x] **$\gamma_w$**: Always use $9.81$ unless problem specifies $10$.
- [x] **Always draw the three-phase diagram** in derivations to secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adsorbed and Capillary Water Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Adsorbed and Capillary Water Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Adsorbed Water (Bound Water)

**Formal Definition:**
Adsorbed water (also called **bound water** or **hydroscopic film water**) is the thin film of water that adheres tightly to the surfaces of fine soil particles (especially clay particles) under the influence of electrochemical forces of attraction originating from the charged clay mineral surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme (PCAGT402): *Adsorbed water is the water held in the immediate vicinity of the particle surface by electrochemical (intermolecular) forces. Its removal requires oven-drying at **105 °C – 110 °C**, and its presence governs the plastic behaviour of cohesive soils.*

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Magnet & Iron Filings":**
Imagine a tiny bar magnet (a clay particle) and sprinkling iron filings (water molecules) around it. The filings closest to the magnet cling so tightly that you have to actively pull them off (oven drying). They are stuck by invisible forces of attraction. This "stuck water" is **adsorbed water**. Further out, filings are held a little less tightly (loose film), and beyond a certain distance, they fall off freely as **free water**.

**Key Physical Properties of Adsorbed Water:**

| Property | Behaviour |
|----------|-----------|
| Density | **Greater than 1.0 g/cc** (approx **1.2 – 1.7 g/cc**, sometimes reported up to 2.0) |
| Freezing Point | Below **0 °C** (does not freeze at normal temperatures) |
| Viscosity | **Much higher** than free water |
| Solvent Ability | Acts as a **very poor solvent** (does not dissolve salts easily) |
| Removal Method | Oven drying at **105 °C – 110 °C** for 24 hrs |

**Types of Adsorbed Water:**
1. **Hygroscopic Water** – Water absorbed from atmospheric humidity; tightly held; corresponds roughly to the shrinkage limit.
2. **Film Water** – Further layers outside the hygroscopic film, forming a thicker water film around particles.
   - **Inner (Tight) Film** – Strongly held; behaves like a viscous solid.
   - **Outer (Loose) Film** – Weakly held; behaves like a viscous liquid.

---

### 1.2 Capillary Water

**Formal Definition:**
Capillary water is the free water that rises into the small interconnected voids of a soil mass above the **water table** (phreatic surface) due to **surface tension (capillarity)** acting at the air-water meniscus within the pore channels.

> [!NOTE]
> Capillary water is **NOT** held by electrochemical forces. It is held by **capillary tension (matric suction)** at the curved air–water interface (meniscus). It is mobile and can move under suction gradients.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Straw in a Glass":**
Take a thin drinking straw and dip one end in a glass of water. You will see water climb *inside* the straw above the level of water in the glass — the thinner the straw, the higher the climb. Soil pores act like thousands of tiny interconnected straws; the smaller the pore, the higher the capillary rise.

**Engineering Significance:**
- Causes **capillary siphoning** in excavations.
- Creates **apparent cohesion (capillary cohesion)** in moist sands above the water table.
- Leads to **frost heave** and **swelling/shrinkage** problems in fine-grained soils.
- Affects the **effective stress** profile in shallow foundations.

> [!WARNING]
> Capillary rise in **fine sands and silts** can be **up to several metres**, while in **clays** with extremely small pores it may theoretically be very high but is **time-dependent** (takes months/years to develop).

---

### 1.3 Comparative Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Feature | Adsorbed Water | Capillary Water |
> |---|---|---|
> | Holding Force | Electrochemical / van der Waals | Surface Tension |
> | Location | On particle surfaces | In pore spaces |
> | Freezing Point | Below 0 °C | At 0 °C |
> | Density | 1.2 – 1.7 g/cc | 1.0 g/cc |
> | Removal | Oven drying 105–110 °C | Evaporation / Drainage |
> | Role | Plasticity (Atterberg Limits) | Effective Stress, Capillary Cohesion |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of Adsorbed Water

Clay particles carry a **net negative charge** on their surfaces due to isomorphous substitution and broken bonds at edges. To maintain electrical neutrality, **cations (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, K⁺, Mg²⁺)** from the pore water are attracted and form a **diffuse double layer** around each particle. Water molecules are polar (H–O–H dipole) and orient themselves around these cations, producing a tightly held water film.

**The Diffuse Double Layer (Gouy–Chapman Model):**
- **Inner Layer (Stern Layer):** Cations tightly bound to particle surface.
- **Outer Diffuse Layer:** Cations gradually diffuse into pore water; water here transitions from "adsorbed" to "free."

### 2.2 Origin of Capillary Water

Capillarity arises from two interfacial phenomena:
1. **Surface Tension (σ)** of water against air.
2. **Contact Angle (θ)** between water and the solid pore wall.

The combination produces a **concave meniscus** in most soils (θ < 90°), creating a **pressure deficit (negative pore water pressure)** below the meniscus. This suction pulls water upward.

### 2.3 Factors Affecting Capillary Rise

- **Pore size (smaller ⇒ higher rise)** — most dominant.
- **Surface tension of water** — increases at lower temperature.
- **Contact angle** — depends on mineralogy & water chemistry.
- **Pore continuity & connectivity**.
- **Evaporation rate** at the ground surface.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All standard formulas for Adsorbed and Capillary Water (KTU Board Examiner Frequency: Very High)**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_c \;=\; \dfrac{4\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{\gamma_w \, d}$ | Capillary rise in a tube of diameter *d* | $h_c$ = rise (m), $\sigma$ = surface tension (N/m) ≈ **0.075 N/m @ 20 °C**, $\theta$ = contact angle, $\gamma_w$ = **9.81 kN/m³**, $d$ = tube diameter (m) |
| 2 | $h_c \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{\gamma_w \, r}$ | Capillary rise using radius *r* | $r$ = tube radius (m) |
| 3 | $h_c \;=\; \dfrac{C}{e \, D_{10}}$ | Capillary rise in soil (empirical, Terzaghi) | $C$ = constant (**0.1 – 0.5 cm²**, typically **0.5 cm²**), $e$ = void ratio, $D_{10}$ = effective grain size (cm) |
| 4 | $u \;=\; -\dfrac{2\,\sigma}{R}$ | Matric suction at meniscus | $u$ = pore water pressure (kPa), $R$ = meniscus radius (m) |
| 5 | $u \;=\; -h_c \,\gamma_w$ | Pore pressure from capillary rise | $u$ = suction (kPa, negative) |
| 6 | $\sigma' \;=\; \sigma - u$ | Effective stress with capillary suction | $\sigma'$ = effective stress, $\sigma$ = total stress, $u$ = suction (negative) |
| 7 | $h_s \;=\; \dfrac{4\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{\gamma_w \, D_{10}}$ | Maximum capillary rise (Hazen, using $D_{10}$) | $D_{10}$ in metres |
| 8 | $t \;=\; \dfrac{h_c}{k} \cdot n$ | Approx. time to reach steady-state rise | $t$ = time, $k$ = permeability, $n$ = porosity |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shallow Foundations:** Capillary cohesion gives moist sand apparent stability; drying causes collapse.
- **Earth Dams:** Capillary saturation of downstream shell after drawdown → loss of strength (quick condition risk in upstream shell).
- **Pavement Design:** Capillary rise brings water into subgrade → reduces CBR.
- **Agricultural & Environmental Geotechnics:** Moisture redistribution due to evapotranspiration.
- **Soil Stabilisation:** Removal/control of adsorbed water via additives (lime, cement) reduces plasticity.

---

### 2.6 Structural Mechanics Behind Capillary Rise (Why it rises)

The phenomenon is fundamentally a **force/pressure balance**:

1. The **curved meniscus** has a higher pressure on the air side than on the water side (Laplace's Law).
2. This creates a **negative gauge pressure** (suction) just below the meniscus.
3. Atmospheric pressure pushes the water column upward until the weight of the risen column balances the suction.

Mathematically (Section 3 develops this step-by-step):

$$
h_c \, \gamma_w \;=\; \Delta u \;=\; \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r}
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Capillary Rise in a Single Tube (Laplace–Jurin's Law)

**Setup:** A thin glass tube of internal radius $r$ is dipped into a reservoir of water. Water rises to a height $h_c$ inside the tube above the free water surface. The contact angle between water and glass is $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Identify the meniscus geometry**

The water meniscus inside the tube is approximately spherical. Let $R$ be the radius of curvature of the meniscus.

$$
R \;=\; \dfrac{r}{\cos\theta}
$$

*Reason:* The tube radius $r$ and the meniscus radius $R$ are related by the contact angle. At $\theta = 0$ (perfect wetting), $R = r$; as $\theta$ increases, $R$ grows.

**Step 2 — Apply Laplace's equation for pressure jump across a curved interface**

Across a spherical air–water interface:

$$
\Delta u \;=\; u_{air} - u_{water} \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\sigma}{R}
$$

*Reason:* For a sphere, the two principal radii of curvature are equal ($R_1 = R_2 = R$).

**Step 3 — Substitute $R$ in terms of $r$ and $\theta$**

$$
\Delta u \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{r}
$$

*Reason:* $\Delta u$ is the pressure difference between the air side (atmospheric) and the water side just below the meniscus. Because air is at atmospheric pressure, the **water pressure just below the meniscus is lower than atmospheric** by this amount — i.e., it is a suction.

**Step 4 — Hydrostatic equilibrium of the water column**

The risen water column of height $h_c$ is in static equilibrium. The pressure at the base of the column (free water surface) must equal atmospheric on both sides.

$$
\text{Pressure at base of column (water side)} \;=\; \text{Pressure at base (reservoir side)}
$$

$$
P_{atm} - \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r} + h_c \, \gamma_w \;=\; P_{atm}
$$

*Reason:* Going down through the column from the meniscus to the free surface adds hydrostatic pressure $h_c \, \gamma_w$.

**Step 5 — Solve for $h_c$**

$$
h_c \, \gamma_w \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{r}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,h_c \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{\gamma_w \, r}\,}
$$

Since diameter $d = 2r$, the equivalent form used in textbooks is:

$$
\boxed{\,h_c \;=\; \dfrac{4\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{\gamma_w \, d}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify dimensions**

$$
[h_c] \;=\; \dfrac{[\sigma]}{[\gamma_w][r]} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{N/m}}{(\text{N/m}^3)(\text{m})} \;=\; \text{m} \;\;\checkmark
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

**Problem:** A clean sand has an effective grain size $D_{10} = 0.06 \text{ mm}$. Estimate the maximum capillary rise using Hazen's empirical formula. Take $\sigma = 0.073 \text{ N/m}$, $\theta = 0°$, and $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

**Solution:**

Using Jurin's formula (Equation 1 from the Cheat Sheet):

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{4\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{\gamma_w \, d}
$$

Convert $D_{10}$ to metres:

$$
d \;=\; D_{10} \;=\; 0.06 \text{ mm} \;=\; 0.06 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \;=\; 6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

Substitute values ($\cos 0° = 1$):

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{4 \times 0.073 \times 1}{9.81 \times 10^{3} \times 6 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{0.292}{0.5886}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,h_c \;\approx\; 0.496 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 50 \text{ cm}\,}
$$

Cross-check with Hazen's empirical formula ($C = 0.5 \text{ cm}^2$):

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{C}{e \, D_{10}} \;\;(\text{in cm})
$$

Assuming $e = 0.7$:

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{0.5}{0.7 \times 0.006} \;\;(\text{since } D_{10} = 0.006 \text{ cm})
$$

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{0.5}{0.0042} \;\approx\; 119 \text{ cm}
$$

The discrepancy reflects that Jurin's formula uses an idealised pore size, while Hazen's empirical form absorbs pore-network and connectivity effects.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Water Types in Soil

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comprehensive Properties Matrix (Highly Examinable in KTU Board)**

| Property | Adsorbed Water | Capillary Water | Free (Gravitational) Water |
|---|---|---|---|
| Binding force | Electrochemical / van der Waals | Surface tension | Gravity |
| Location | Particle surface (≤ 0.5 μm film) | Pore spaces above WT | Below water table |
| Density (g/cc) | **1.2 – 1.7** | **1.0** | **1.0** |
| Viscosity | Very high | Normal | Normal |
| Freezing point | < 0 °C | 0 °C | 0 °C |
| Solvent power | Poor | Good | Good |
| Effect on properties | Plasticity, Swelling | Apparent cohesion, Capillary siphoning | Buoyancy, Seepage |
| Removal | Oven @ 105–110 °C | Evaporation / drainage | Pumping / gravity flow |
| Governing test | Atterberg Limits, Shrinkage Limit | Capillary rise tests | Permeability, Consolidation |
| Engineering concern | Plastic behaviour of clay | Frost heave, moisture migration | Effective stress, piping |

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Capillary Pressure (Matric Suction) in Soil

For an idealised cylindrical pore of radius $r$ in a soil:

**Step 1** — The capillary pressure (suction) is:

$$
u_c \;=\; -\dfrac{2\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{r}
$$

(Negative sign indicates suction relative to atmospheric.)

**Step 2** — Convert to equivalent head:

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{u_c}{\gamma_w} \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{r \, \gamma_w}
$$

**Step 3** — In terms of effective grain size $D_{10}$ (assuming pore size ≈ $D_{10}/5$ to $D_{10}/2$, commonly $D_{10}/5$ for clean sand):

$$
h_c \;\approx\; \dfrac{10\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{\gamma_w \, D_{10}}
$$

This is the form frequently used in foundation engineering for estimating the depth of capillary saturation above the water table.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Capillary Rise in Soil Column (Kerala Typical Soil)

**Problem:** A silty sand deposit has $D_{10} = 0.02 \text{ mm}$, void ratio $e = 0.85$. Estimate the capillary rise using (a) Hazen's empirical formula with $C = 0.5 \text{ cm}^2$, and (b) Jurin's formula with $\sigma = 0.073 \text{ N/m}$, $\theta = 20°$, and $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

**Solution (a) — Hazen:**

$$
D_{10} \;=\; 0.02 \text{ mm} \;=\; 0.002 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{C}{e \cdot D_{10}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5}{0.85 \times 0.002} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5}{0.0017} \;\approx\; 294 \text{ cm} \;\approx\; 2.94 \text{ m}
$$

**Solution (b) — Jurin:**

$$
D_{10} \;=\; 0.02 \text{ mm} \;=\; 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}, \quad \cos 20° \approx 0.9397
$$

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{4 \times 0.073 \times 0.9397}{9810 \times 2 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{0.2744}{0.1962} \;\approx\; 1.40 \text{ m}
$$

The difference is **expected**, because Hazen's constant $C$ was derived empirically for natural soils, whereas Jurin's formula is idealised.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Soil Water — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[SOIL WATER] --> nodeB[Adsorbed Water]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Capillary Water]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Gravitational Free Water]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Chemically Combined Water]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Hygroscopic Water]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Film Water]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1a[Inner Tight Film]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1b[Outer Loose Film]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Water in Capillary Fringe]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Water at Grain Contact Menisci]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Capillary Water in Tension Zone]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[Below Water Table]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Percolating Downward]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[Removed only above 300 C]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Part of Mineral Lattice]
```

---

### 4.2 Capillary Rise Mechanism — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Water Reservoir] -->|dip tube| stepB[Glass Capillary Tube radius r]
    stepB -->|wetting| stepC[Concave Meniscus Forms]
    stepC -->|surface tension sigma| stepD[Pressure Deficit 2 sigma cos theta / r]
    stepD -->|suction pulls column up| stepE[Rising Water Column]
    stepE -->|weight increases| stepF[Hydrostatic Balance]
    stepF -->|equilibrium reached| stepG[Steady Capillary Rise h_c]
    stepG -->|factors| stepH[Pore Size, Contact Angle, Temperature]
```

---

### 4.3 Soil Profile with Capillary Zone — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    surfA[Ground Surface Drying Zone Evaporation] --> surfB[Unsaturated Zone Capillary Fringe]
    surfB --> surfC[Water Table Phreatic Surface]
    surfC --> surfD[Saturated Zone Below WT]

    subgraph ZoneA[Above WT Capillary Tension]
        z1[Hygroscopic Water] --> z2[Adsorbed Films on Grains]
        z2 --> z3[Capillary Menisci at Contacts]
    end

    subgraph ZoneB[Below WT Hydrostatic]
        z4[Pore Water at Atmospheric plus Hydrostatic]
    end
```

---

### 4.4 Engineering Implications of Water Types — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    engA[Soil Water Type] --> engB[Adsorbed]
    engA --> engC[Capillary]
    engA --> engD[Gravitational]

    engB --> engB1[Plasticity]
    engB --> engB2[Swelling Shrinkage]
    engB --> engB3[Atterberg Limits]

    engC --> engC1[Apparent Cohesion in Sand]
    engC --> engC2[Frost Heave]
    engC --> engC3[Effective Stress Modification]
    engC --> engC4[Capillary Siphoning in Pits]

    engD --> engD1[Seepage Force]
    engD --> engD2[Quick Condition]
    engD --> engD3[Effective Stress Reduction]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Distinguish between adsorbed water and capillary water in soils. List any four differences.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — Examiner's Key Pattern):**

| S.No. | Adsorbed Water | Capillary Water |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Held by **electrochemical** forces | Held by **surface tension** |
| 2 | Adheres to **particle surfaces** | Occupies **pore spaces** above WT |
| 3 | Density ≈ **1.2 – 1.7 g/cc** | Density ≈ **1.0 g/cc** |
| 4 | Removed by oven drying @ **105–110 °C** | Removed by evaporation / drainage |
| 5 | Governs **plasticity** of clays | Governs **apparent cohesion** of moist sand |

*(Any 4 differences × 0.5 marks each = 2 marks + definition 1 mark = 3 marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define capillary rise in soils. State the formula for capillary rise in a tube of diameter $d$.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Capillary rise is the vertical upward movement of water into the interconnected voids of a soil mass above the water table, driven by **surface tension** acting at the air–water meniscus within the pore channels.

**Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{4\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{\gamma_w \, d}
$$

Where:
- $h_c$ = capillary rise (m)
- $\sigma$ = surface tension of water (≈ **0.075 N/m**)
- $\theta$ = angle of contact between water and pore wall
- $\gamma_w$ = unit weight of water (**9.81 kN/m³**)
- $d$ = diameter of capillary tube (m)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU ESE Module Internal Choice — Module 2]**

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Theory + Application*

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **types of adsorbed water** in a soil mass. Discuss the **diffuse double layer theory** and its influence on the engineering behaviour of clay soils. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fine sand has an effective size $D_{10} = 0.05 \text{ mm}$ and void ratio $e = 0.7$. Estimate the maximum capillary rise using (i) **Jurin's formula**, and (ii) **Hazen's empirical formula** with $C = 0.5 \text{ cm}^2$. Take $\sigma = 0.073 \text{ N/m}$, $\theta = 0°$, and $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$. Comment on the difference. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**(a) Adsorbed Water & Diffuse Double Layer Theory (7 marks)**

**1. Types of Adsorbed Water (3 marks):**

- **Hygroscopic Water:** Absorbed from atmospheric humidity; forms the innermost layer; tightly held by ionic and van der Waals forces; corresponds to the shrinkage limit. Removed only at temperatures > 100 °C.
- **Film Water:** Layers of water molecules beyond the hygroscopic film, forming a gradient of decreasing binding energy.
  - *Inner (Tight) Film:* Strongly held; behaves like a highly viscous solid; has density up to ~1.7 g/cc.
  - *Outer (Loose) Film:* Weakly held; behaves like a viscous liquid; density approaches 1.0 g/cc.

**2. Diffuse Double Layer Theory (3 marks):**

Clay particles (especially montmorillonite) carry a **net negative charge** on their surfaces due to isomorphous substitution within the silica-alumina lattice. To maintain electro-neutrality, **exchangeable cations** (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, K⁺) from the pore water are attracted toward the particle surface.

These cations are not rigidly fixed but distribute themselves in a **diffuse cloud** with concentration decreasing exponentially with distance from the particle surface. This forms the **diffuse double layer**:

- **Stern Layer (Inner):** Cations strongly adsorbed; water here = adsorbed water.
- **Gouy Diffuse Layer (Outer):** Cations dispersed; water gradually transitions to free water.

**3. Engineering Influence (1 mark):**

- A thicker double layer ⇒ higher **plasticity**, **swelling**, **shrinkage**, and **compressibility**.
- It governs **liquid limit**, **plasticity index**, and **sensitivity** of clays.
- Influenced by pore fluid chemistry (e.g., Na-montmorillonite swells more than Ca-montmorillonite).

---

**(b) Capillary Rise Calculations (7 marks)**

**Given:** $D_{10} = 0.05 \text{ mm}$, $e = 0.7$, $\sigma = 0.073 \text{ N/m}$, $\theta = 0°$, $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $C = 0.5 \text{ cm}^2$.

**(i) Jurin's Formula:**

$$
d \;=\; D_{10} \;=\; 0.05 \text{ mm} \;=\; 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{4\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{\gamma_w \, d} \;=\; \dfrac{4 \times 0.073 \times 1}{9810 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{0.292}{0.4905} \;\approx\; 0.595 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 60 \text{ cm}
$$

**(ii) Hazen's Empirical Formula:**

Convert $D_{10}$ to cm:

$$
D_{10} \;=\; 0.005 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{C}{e \cdot D_{10}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5}{0.7 \times 0.005} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5}{0.0035} \;\approx\; 142.86 \text{ cm} \;\approx\; 1.43 \text{ m}
$$

**Comment (2 marks):**
The empirical result is significantly larger because Hazen's formula implicitly accounts for:
- Pore network **tortuosity**,
- **Non-uniform pore sizes**,
- **Connectivity** of voids in natural soil.

Jurin's formula assumes an idealised single cylindrical pore and therefore underestimates the rise.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Define **capillary water**. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the phenomenon of capillary rise in soils. Derive the expression for the height of capillary rise in a tube of diameter $d$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silty sand deposit has the following characteristics: $D_{10} = 0.03 \text{ mm}$, $e = 0.8$, surface tension $\sigma = 0.072 \text{ N/m}$, and contact angle $\theta = 15°$. Compute the capillary rise using (i) Jurin's formula and (ii) Hazen's formula with $C = 0.5 \text{ cm}^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**(a) Capillary Water and Derivation (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Capillary water is the water held in the **pore spaces** of a soil mass **above the water table** by **surface tension** acting at the **curved air–water menisci** within the interconnected voids.

**Sketch and Explanation (3 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid Block for a textual representation.)*
A thin capillary tube dipped in water shows:
- A **concave meniscus** at the top of the water column inside the tube.
- The water rises to a height $h_c$ above the free water surface in the reservoir.
- The smaller the tube diameter, the higher the rise.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

The pressure difference across a curved meniscus (Laplace's equation) is:

$$
\Delta u \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\sigma}{R} \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{r}
$$

where $R = r/\cos\theta$.

For hydrostatic equilibrium of the risen column:

$$
h_c \,\gamma_w \;=\; \Delta u \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{r}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,h_c \;=\; \dfrac{4\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{\gamma_w \, d}\,}
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $D_{10} = 0.03 \text{ mm}$, $e = 0.8$, $\sigma = 0.072 \text{ N/m}$, $\theta = 15°$, $C = 0.5 \text{ cm}^2$, $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

**(i) Jurin's Formula:**

$$
d \;=\; 3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}, \quad \cos 15° \;=\; 0.9659
$$

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{4 \times 0.072 \times 0.9659}{9810 \times 3 \times 10^{-5}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.2782}{0.2943} \;\approx\; 0.945 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) Hazen's Formula:**

$$
D_{10} \;=\; 0.003 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
h_c \;=\; \dfrac{0.5}{0.8 \times 0.003} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5}{0.0024} \;\approx\; 208.33 \text{ cm} \;\approx\; 2.08 \text{ m}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Hazen's empirical value is more realistic for natural soils, capturing pore-network effects not represented in Jurin's idealised equation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing cm and m in the same calculation. **Always convert to SI (m, N/m, kN/m³) before substituting.** $D_{10}$ in formulas must be in **metres** for Jurin and **centimetres** for Hazen.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Contact Angle (–1 mark):** Setting $\theta = 0$ when the problem specifies a different value (e.g., $\theta = 15°$, 20°). Always read carefully.
>
> 3. **Confusing Adsorbed and Capillary Water (–2 marks):** Mixing up the **forces of retention** (electrochemical vs surface tension) — this is a high-frequency conceptual trap.
>
> 4. **Not Commenting on Discrepancy (–1 mark):** In numerical problems where Jurin and Hazen give different answers, **always add a 1–2 line comment** explaining the difference (real vs idealised pore structure).
>
> 5. **Omitting Density Values (–1 mark):** When asked about adsorbed water's unusual properties, examiners expect you to mention **density > 1.0 g/cc** and **freezing point < 0 °C**.
>
> 6. **No Sketch in 7-Mark Theory Questions (–1 mark):** Capillary rise derivations **must** be accompanied by a labelled diagram (tube, meniscus, contact angle, height $h_c$) — omitting it costs easy marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Adsorbed Water (Bound Water):**
> - Held by **electrochemical / van der Waals** forces on **particle surfaces**.
> - Has **density > 1.0 g/cc (1.2 – 1.7)**, **freezes below 0 °C**, **high viscosity**, **poor solvent**.
> - Two sub-types: **Hygroscopic** + **Film (Tight + Loose)**.
> - Controlled by **Diffuse Double Layer Theory** (Gouy–Chapman).
> - Governs **plasticity, swelling, shrinkage, Atterberg limits** of clay.
> - Removal: **oven drying @ 105–110 °C for 24 hours**.
>
> **Capillary Water:**
> - Held by **surface tension** at **air–water menisci** in pore spaces **above water table**.
> - Three sub-zones: capillary fringe / contact menisci / tension zone.
> - Rise height $h_c = \dfrac{4\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{\gamma_w \, d}$ **(Jurin)**, $h_c = \dfrac{C}{e \, D_{10}}$ **(Hazen)**.
> - Larger rise in **fine-grained soils** (small pores).
> - Creates **apparent cohesion** in moist sand → loss on drying.
> - Major cause of **frost heave**, **capillary siphoning**, **moisture migration**.
> - Modifies **effective stress** as $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ where $u$ is negative (suction).
>
> **Standard Values to Memorise:**
> - $\sigma_{water} \approx 0.075 \text{ N/m}$ @ 20 °C, $0.073 \text{ N/m}$ @ 25 °C.
> - $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (or **9.807** in high-precision problems).
> - Hazen's constant $C = 0.1$ – $0.5 \text{ cm}^2$ (use **0.5** unless otherwise stated).
> - Density of adsorbed water: **1.2 – 1.7 g/cc**.
> - Oven drying temperature: **105 °C – 110 °C**.
>
> **One-Line Takeaway:**
> *Adsorbed water = electrochemistry + plasticity; Capillary water = surface tension + apparent cohesion — both must be quantified using the right formula and the right units!*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Stress - Effective Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Total Stress - Effective Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Total Stress (σ or σ_total)

The **Total Stress** at a point in a soil mass is defined as the total force per unit area acting at that point due to the combined weight of the overlying soil particles, the water present in the voids, and any externally applied loads. It is the gross stress measured on a horizontal plane before any stress transfer mechanics between the soil skeleton and the pore fluid are considered.

Mathematically, for a homogeneous soil layer of thickness $H$ and unit weight $\gamma$:

$$\sigma = \gamma \cdot H$$

For a multi-layered soil profile:

$$\sigma = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma_i \cdot H_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Total stress acts on the entire cross-section (both solids + voids), and is therefore calculated using the **bulk (or moist/saturated) unit weight** of the soil mass, NOT just the weight of solids alone.

### 1.2 Pore Water Pressure (u) — Neutral Stress

**Pore Water Pressure** (also called **Neutral Stress**) is the pressure exerted by the water filling the voids between soil particles. It acts equally in all directions and is independent of the soil skeleton.

For a static water column of height $h_w$ above the point of interest:

$$u = \gamma_w \cdot h_w$$

where $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (commonly taken as $10 \text{ kN/m}^3$ in KTU numericals).

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrostatic Principle**: Pore water pressure follows Pascal's law — it is isotropic and cannot sustain shear. Hence, it does NOT contribute to the shear strength of the soil. Only the granular skeleton contributes to shear resistance.

### 1.3 Effective Stress (σ' or σ_effective)

**Effective Stress** is the stress that is actually transmitted through the soil skeleton (grain-to-grain contacts). It is the portion of total stress that controls the **volume change** (compression, swelling, consolidation) and **shear strength** behavior of the soil.

Proposed by **Karl Terzaghi (1925)** — the "Father of Soil Mechanics":

$$\boxed{\sigma' = \sigma - u}$$

This simple yet profound equation is considered the **most important equation in all of soil mechanics**.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Explanation for First-Time Learners**
>
> Imagine a saturated sponge sitting in a bathtub of water:
> - **Total Stress (σ)** = the entire weight of the wet sponge pressing down on your hand.
> - **Pore Water Pressure (u)** = the water pressure pushing back up from within the sponge's holes (Archimedes-like upward push).
> - **Effective Stress (σ')** = the *actual* squeezing force your fingers feel from the sponge's solid rubbery structure alone.
>
> The water in the pores pushes outward and reduces the inter-particle contact force. Squeeze the sponge harder (increase σ), but if the water pressure also rises (u increases), the sponge feels NO additional compression. The fibers only "know" the **net squeezing force = σ − u**.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (KTU Convention) |
|-----------|--------|-------------------------------|
| Unit weight of water | $\gamma_w$ | **9.81 kN/m³** (often rounded to **10 kN/m³** in exams) |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m³** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breakdown of the Terzaghi Principle

The total stress at any point in a saturated soil mass can be partitioned into two distinct physical components:

**Step 1 — Stress Partitioning:**
- The water in the continuous voids forms an interconnected hydraulic network that carries a portion of the applied load hydrostatically.
- The solid particles form a discontinuous granular skeleton that carries the remainder of the load through inter-particle contact forces.

**Step 2 — Why Effective Stress Controls Behavior:**
- Soil shear strength ($\tau_f$) is governed by inter-particle friction and interlocking → these are activated ONLY by effective stress.
- Soil compression/consolidation occurs when grains rearrange and slide past each other → again governed by effective stress.
- Pore water cannot resist shear because water has **zero shear strength**.

**Step 3 — Conditions for Validity:**
- The soil must be **saturated** (or nearly so) for the simple $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ to apply directly.
- For **partially saturated soils**, the equation is modified to $\sigma' = \sigma - u_a + \chi(u_a - u_w)$ (Bishop's equation — beyond KTU scope for this module).

### 2.2 Submerged Soil — Buoyancy Concept

When a soil mass lies below the water table, the soil grains experience a buoyant (uplift) force equal to the weight of water displaced by the solids. The effective unit weight becomes:

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$$

This is the **submerged (or buoyant) unit weight**, which must be used to compute effective stress below the water table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Pitfall**: Students often incorrectly use $\gamma_{dry}$ or $\gamma_{bulk}$ below the water table. Always use $\gamma_{sat}$ for total stress calculation (because voids are filled with water) and then subtract $\gamma_w$ to get the effective contribution of the soil skeleton below the water table.

### 2.3 Capillary Rise Effect

Due to surface tension, water rises above the phreatic surface (water table) into the capillary fringe. In this zone:
- The soil appears **saturated** but pore pressure is **negative** (suction).
- This **increases** effective stress compared to a dry condition above water table.

$$\sigma' = \sigma - u \quad \text{where } u < 0 \text{ in capillary zone}$$

$$\sigma' = \sigma + \vert u \vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application**: Capillary suctions in fine-grained soils (silts, clays) just above the water table make foundations "feel" effectively stronger near the water table than they do deeper in the dry zone.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | When to Apply |
|---|---------|---------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Total Stress (single layer) | $\sigma = \gamma \cdot H$ | kN/m² | Above water table (use $\gamma_{dry}$ or $\gamma_{moist}$) |
| 2 | Total Stress (multi-layer) | $\sigma = \sum \gamma_i H_i$ | kN/m² | Layered soil profile |
| 3 | Pore Water Pressure (hydrostatic) | $u = \gamma_w \cdot h_w$ | kN/m² | Below water table, static conditions |
| 4 | Effective Stress (Terzaghi) | $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ | kN/m² | Saturated soil |
| 5 | Submerged Unit Weight | $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ | kN/m³ | Below water table for incremental stress |
| 6 | Effective Stress w/ Capillarity | $\sigma' = \sigma + \gamma_w \cdot h_c$ | kN/m² | Within capillary fringe (above WT) |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Shallow Foundation Design**: Bearing capacity $\propto \sigma'$ → cannot ignore water table influence.
- **Settlement Calculations**: Consolidation settlement depends entirely on effective stress increase ($\Delta\sigma'$).
- **Earth Pressure (Retaining Walls)**: Lateral earth pressures on basement walls below water table must be computed using effective unit weights.
- **Slope Stability**: Factor of safety against landslides depends on effective stress along the failure surface.
- **Earthquake Liquefaction**: When pore pressure builds up under cyclic loading, $\sigma' \to 0$ and soil behaves like a liquid — entirely an effective-stress phenomenon.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Stress from First Principles (Physical Argument)

Consider a saturated soil element at depth $z$ below the ground surface. Cut an arbitrarily oriented plane through the soil. The plane passes through **both solid particles and voids** filled with water.

**Step 1**: Let $A$ = total cross-sectional area of the plane. The vertical force on this plane equals the weight of soil and water above it:

$$V = W_{solids} + W_{water} = \gamma_{sat} \cdot A \cdot z$$

**Step 2**: Define $A_s$ = area occupied by solid-to-solid contacts, and $A_w = A - A_s$ = area through voids.

**Step 3**: The total vertical force $V$ is carried partly by the soil skeleton ($V_s$) and partly by the pore water ($V_w$):

$$V = V_s + V_w$$

**Step 4**: The pore water transmits stress equal to the pore water pressure $u$ over the entire area $A$ (since water fills the voids):

$$V_w = u \cdot A$$

**Step 5**: The soil skeleton transmits stress only through the contact area $A_s$, but conceptually we define an **average inter-granular stress** $\sigma'$ acting over the full area:

$$V_s = \sigma' \cdot A$$

**Step 6**: Substitute and equate:

$$V = \sigma' \cdot A + u \cdot A$$

$$V/A = \sigma' + u$$

$$\sigma = \sigma' + u$$

$$\boxed{\sigma' = \sigma - u}$$

This is the **Terzaghi effective stress equation** — derived rigorously from force equilibrium at a horizontal plane.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example 1: Homogeneous Submerged Soil

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style)**:
A saturated clay layer of thickness **5 m** lies below the water table. The saturated unit weight is $\gamma_{sat} = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$. The water table coincides with the ground surface. Compute the total stress, pore pressure, and effective stress at the **mid-depth (z = 2.5 m)** and at the **bottom (z = 5 m)**. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

**Solution — At mid-depth (z = 2.5 m)**:

Step 1: Total stress calculation.

$$\sigma = \gamma_{sat} \cdot z = 19 \times 2.5$$

$$\sigma = 47.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step 2: Pore water pressure (water column above = 2.5 m since WT is at surface).

$$u = \gamma_w \cdot h_w = 9.81 \times 2.5$$

$$u = 24.525 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step 3: Effective stress (Terzaghi's principle).

$$\sigma' = \sigma - u = 47.5 - 24.525$$

$$\boxed{\sigma' = 22.975 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 23.0 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

**Solution — At bottom (z = 5 m)**:

Step 1: Total stress.

$$\sigma = 19 \times 5 = 95 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step 2: Pore water pressure.

$$u = 9.81 \times 5 = 49.05 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step 3: Effective stress.

$$\sigma' = 95 - 49.05 = 45.95 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Alternative method using $\gamma'$** (verification):

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 19 - 9.81 = 9.19 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

At mid-depth: $\sigma' = \gamma' \cdot z = 9.19 \times 2.5 = 22.975 \text{ kN/m}^2$ ✓ (matches!)

At bottom: $\sigma' = 9.19 \times 5 = 45.95 \text{ kN/m}^2$ ✓ (matches!)

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example 2: Layered Soil with Water Table

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style)**:
A soil profile consists of:
- **Layer 1 (0 – 2 m)**: Moist sand with $\gamma_{moist} = 17 \text{ kN/m}^3$, water table at 2 m depth.
- **Layer 2 (2 – 5 m)**: Saturated sand with $\gamma_{sat} = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$.
- **Layer 3 (5 – 8 m)**: Saturated clay with $\gamma_{sat} = 20 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

Compute $\sigma$, $u$, and $\sigma'$ at depths $z = 2 \text{ m}, 5 \text{ m}, 8 \text{ m}$. Take $\gamma_w = 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

**Solution — At z = 2 m (just above water table)**:

$$\sigma = \gamma_{moist} \times 2 = 17 \times 2 = 34 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$u = 0 \quad \text{(point is at water table level, no water column above)}$$

$$\sigma' = \sigma - u = 34 - 0 = 34 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Solution — At z = 5 m (bottom of Layer 2)**:

$$\sigma = (17 \times 2) + (19 \times 3) = 34 + 57 = 91 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$u = \gamma_w \times h_w = 10 \times 3 = 30 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\sigma' = 91 - 30 = 61 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Solution — At z = 8 m (bottom of Layer 3)**:

$$\sigma = 34 + 57 + (20 \times 3) = 34 + 57 + 60 = 151 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$u = 10 \times 6 = 60 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\sigma' = 151 - 60 = 91 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example 3: Capillary Rise Effect

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style)**:
The water table is at depth 4 m below ground surface. Due to capillary action, the soil is saturated up to 1 m above the water table (i.e., up to depth 3 m). Moist unit weight above capillary fringe (0–3 m): $\gamma_{moist} = 16 \text{ kN/m}^3$. Saturated unit weight (3–4 m and below): $\gamma_{sat} = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$. Compute effective stress at depth 3.5 m. Take $\gamma_w = 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

**Solution**:

Step 1: Identify zones.
- Zone A (0–3 m): Moist soil above capillary fringe → dry/moist condition.
- Zone B (3–4 m): Saturated, within capillary fringe → **pore pressure is negative** (suction).
- Zone C (below 4 m): Below water table → normal hydrostatic pore pressure.

Step 2: Total stress at z = 3.5 m.

$$\sigma = (\gamma_{moist} \times 3) + (\gamma_{sat} \times 0.5) = (16 \times 3) + (18 \times 0.5)$$

$$\sigma = 48 + 9 = 57 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step 3: Pore water pressure at z = 3.5 m (in capillary zone, 0.5 m above water table).

$$u = -\gamma_w \cdot h_c = -10 \times 0.5 = -5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

The negative sign indicates suction (pore pressure below atmospheric).

Step 4: Effective stress.

$$\sigma' = \sigma - u = 57 - (-5) = 57 + 5$$

$$\boxed{\sigma' = 62 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight**: Notice that effective stress is **higher** in the capillary zone than it would be at the same depth if the soil were dry. Capillary suction "pulls" grains together, increasing inter-particle contact forces.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Stress Components at Various Conditions

| Condition | Total Stress $\sigma$ | Pore Pressure $u$ | Effective Stress $\sigma'$ | Remarks |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------------|---------------------------|---------|
| Soil above WT (dry) | $\gamma_d \cdot z$ | 0 | $\gamma_d \cdot z$ | $\sigma = \sigma'$ |
| Soil above WT (moist) | $\gamma_{moist} \cdot z$ | 0 (approx) | $\gamma_{moist} \cdot z$ | $\sigma = \sigma'$ |
| Submerged (below WT) | $\gamma_{sat} \cdot z$ | $\gamma_w \cdot (z - z_{wt})$ | $(\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w) \cdot (z - z_{wt}) + \sigma'_{wt}$ | Use $\gamma'$ |
| Capillary fringe | $\gamma_{sat} \cdot z$ | $-\gamma_w \cdot h_c$ | $\sigma + \gamma_w \cdot h_c$ | Suction increases $\sigma'$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stress Partitioning Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Stress Sigma Applied Load"] --> B["Stress at Point in Soil Mass"]
    B --> C["Pore Water Pressure u Acts in Voids"]
    B --> D["Inter-granular Stress Sigma Prime Acts at Grain Contacts"]
    C --> E["Neutral Component No Shear Resistance"]
    D --> F["Effective Component Controls Strength and Compression"]
    B --> G["Terzaghi Equation Sigma equals Sigma Prime plus u"]
    G --> H["Engineering Application"]
    H --> I["Bearing Capacity"]
    H --> J["Settlement Analysis"]
    H --> K["Slope Stability"]
    H --> L["Liquefaction Assessment"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Effective Stress Calculation Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Identify Soil Profile and Water Table Depth"] --> S2["Determine Unit Weights for Each Layer"]
    S2 --> S3{"Is the Point Above or Below the Water Table"}
    S3 -->|Above WT| S4["Compute Total Stress using Moist or Dry Unit Weight"]
    S4 --> S5["Set Pore Pressure u equals 0 (unless in capillary fringe)"]
    S5 --> S6["Effective Stress equals Total Stress"]
    S3 -->|Below WT| S7["Compute Total Stress using Saturated Unit Weight"]
    S7 --> S8["Compute Pore Pressure u equals gamma_w times h_w"]
    S8 --> S9["Apply Terzaghi Equation Sigma Prime equals Sigma minus u"]
    S6 --> S10["Report Final Effective Stress Value"]
    S9 --> S10["Report Final Effective Stress Value"]
    S3 -->|Capillary Zone| S11["Use Negative Pore Pressure u equals minus gamma_w times h_c"]
    S11 --> S12["Effective Stress increases by capillary suction"]
    S12 --> S10["Report Final Effective Stress Value"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stress Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["Layer 1 Moist Sand gamma moist"] --> M1["Mid Layer Interface 2m"]
    L2["Layer 2 Saturated Sand gamma sat"] --> M2["Mid Layer Interface 5m"]
    L3["Layer 3 Saturated Clay gamma sat"] --> M3["Base Interface 8m"]
    M1 --> P1["Sigma 34 kN per sqm"]
    M1 --> U1["u 0 kN per sqm"]
    M1 --> E1["Sigma Prime 34 kN per sqm"]
    M2 --> P2["Sigma 91 kN per sqm"]
    M2 --> U2["u 30 kN per sqm"]
    M2 --> E2["Sigma Prime 61 kN per sqm"]
    M3 --> P3["Sigma 151 kN per sqm"]
    M3 --> U3["u 60 kN per sqm"]
    M3 --> E3["Sigma Prime 91 kN per sqm"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description: Vertical Stress Distribution Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Vertical Stress Profile** (from Worked Example 2):
>
> Imagine a vertical cross-section of the soil profile with depth on the y-axis and stress magnitude on the x-axis.
>
> - From **0 to 2 m**: Linear increase in $\sigma$ from 0 to 34 kN/m². $u = 0$, so $\sigma'$ coincides with $\sigma$.
> - From **2 to 5 m**: $\sigma$ continues rising from 34 to 91 kN/m². $u$ rises linearly from 0 to 30 kN/m². The vertical gap between $\sigma$ and $u$ (which equals $\sigma'$) widens at a rate of $\gamma' = 9$ kN/m³.
> - From **5 to 8 m**: $\sigma$ rises from 91 to 151 kN/m². $u$ rises from 30 to 60 kN/m². The gap continues widening at rate $\gamma' = 10$ kN/m³.
>
> The **gap between the total stress line and the pore pressure line, at any depth, is the effective stress** — this is the geometric visual interpretation of Terzaghi's equation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the terms: (a) Total stress, (b) Effective stress, and (c) Neutral stress. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer**:
(a) **Total Stress (σ)**: The total force per unit area carried by the soil mass at a point, including contributions from both solid particles and pore fluid. Computed as $\sigma = \gamma \cdot z$.

(b) **Effective Stress (σ')**: The stress transmitted through the soil skeleton (grain-to-grain contacts). It is the portion of total stress that controls volume change and shear strength.

(c) **Neutral Stress / Pore Water Pressure (u)**: The pressure in the pore water, equal to $\gamma_w \cdot h_w$ under hydrostatic conditions. It acts equally in all directions and does not contribute to shear strength.

**Relationship (Terzaghi's Principle)**:

$$\sigma = \sigma' + u \quad \text{or} \quad \sigma' = \sigma - u$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is effective stress? Explain its importance in soil mechanics. Why is pore water pressure called "neutral stress"?**

**Model Answer**:
**Effective Stress** is the stress carried by the soil skeleton at the inter-granular contacts, given by $\sigma' = \sigma - u$.

**Importance**:
- Governs **shear strength** of soils (Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion uses $\sigma'$).
- Controls **volume change** (consolidation, swelling, compression).
- Determines **bearing capacity** of foundations.
- Influences **lateral earth pressure** on retaining structures.
- Critical in **liquefaction analysis** under seismic loading.

**Why "Neutral"?**
Pore water pressure is called "neutral stress" because:
1. It acts equally in all directions (isotropic).
2. It does not influence the shear strength of the soil.
3. It merely "neutralizes" a portion of the total stress, reducing the inter-particle contact forces without contributing to frictional resistance.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**
**(a)** A soil profile consists of three layers:
- Layer 1: 0 – 2 m, Moist sand with $\gamma = 17 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- Layer 2: 2 – 5 m, Saturated sand with $\gamma_{sat} = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- Layer 3: 5 – 8 m, Saturated clay with $\gamma_{sat} = 20 \text{ kN/m}^3$

The water table is at 2 m below the ground surface. Take $\gamma_w = 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

Compute the total stress, pore water pressure, and effective stress at depths **2 m, 5 m, and 8 m** below the ground surface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the concept of effective stress as proposed by Terzaghi. How is effective stress modified in the capillary fringe zone? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a)**:

**At z = 2 m (at water table)**:

$$\sigma = \gamma_{moist} \times 2 = 17 \times 2 = 34 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$u = 0 \quad (\text{water column above point} = 0)$$

$$\sigma' = \sigma - u = 34 - 0 = 34 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**At z = 5 m**:

$$\sigma = (17 \times 2) + (19 \times 3) = 34 + 57 = 91 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$u = \gamma_w \times h_w = 10 \times (5 - 2) = 10 \times 3 = 30 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\sigma' = 91 - 30 = 61 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**At z = 8 m**:

$$\sigma = 34 + 57 + (20 \times 3) = 34 + 57 + 60 = 151 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$u = 10 \times (8 - 2) = 10 \times 6 = 60 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\sigma' = 151 - 60 = 91 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Tabulated Result**:

| Depth (m) | $\sigma$ (kN/m²) | $u$ (kN/m²) | $\sigma'$ (kN/m²) |
|-----------|------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 2         | 34               | 0           | 34                |
| 5         | 91               | 30          | 61                |
| 8         | 151              | 60          | 91                |

**Model Solution for Part (b)**:

Terzaghi's principle states that effective stress is the stress transmitted through the soil skeleton at inter-granular contacts:

$$\sigma' = \sigma - u$$

**Physical Explanation**: When a saturated soil element is sectioned horizontally, the total force over the section is shared between the pore water (acting over the entire area with pressure $u$) and the solid skeleton (transmitting force through grain contacts). The inter-granular stress, when averaged over the total area, is the effective stress.

**Sketch (refer Section 4.4)**: A vertical soil profile showing total stress line rising linearly from surface, pore pressure rising linearly from water table downward, and the gap between them representing effective stress.

**Capillary Fringe Modification**: In the capillary zone above the water table:
- Soil is saturated but pore pressure is **negative** ($u < 0$).
- This negative pressure creates **suction**, pulling grains together.
- Effective stress **increases**:

$$\sigma' = \sigma - u = \sigma + \gamma_w \cdot h_c$$

where $h_c$ is the height of the capillary fringe above the water table.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**
**(a)** A 6 m thick saturated clay layer lies below the water table with $\gamma_{sat} = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$. The water table is at the ground surface. Compute the total stress, pore water pressure, and effective stress at every 1 m interval from 0 to 6 m depth. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the phenomenon of quick sand (boiling of soil) using the effective stress concept. Under what conditions does effective stress become zero? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a)**:

For each depth $z$ (where $0 \leq z \leq 6$):

$$\sigma = \gamma_{sat} \cdot z = 18z$$

$$u = \gamma_w \cdot z = 9.81z$$

$$\sigma' = \sigma - u = (\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w) \cdot z = 8.19z$$

**Tabulated Computation**:

| Depth z (m) | $\sigma$ (kN/m²) | $u$ (kN/m²) | $\sigma'$ (kN/m²) |
|-------------|------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 0           | 0.00             | 0.00        | 0.00              |
| 1           | 18.00            | 9.81        | 8.19              |
| 2           | 36.00            | 19.62       | 16.38             |
| 3           | 54.00            | 29.43       | 24.57             |
| 4           | 72.00            | 39.24       | 32.76             |
| 5           | 90.00            | 49.05       | 40.95             |
| 6           | 108.00           | 58.86       | 49.14             |

**Model Solution for Part (b)**:

**Quick Sand Phenomenon**:

Quick sand (or "boiling" of soil) occurs when the upward seepage force in a soil mass equals or exceeds the submerged weight of the soil, causing the effective stress to drop to zero.

**Mathematical Derivation**:

Consider upward seepage with hydraulic gradient $i$. The seepage force per unit volume is:

$$J = i \cdot \gamma_w$$

The effective stress at depth $z$ with upward seepage becomes:

$$\sigma' = (\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w) \cdot z + (i \cdot \gamma_w \cdot z) \cdot (-1)$$

Wait — for upward seepage, the seepage force acts upward, reducing effective stress:

$$\sigma' = \gamma' \cdot z - i \cdot \gamma_w \cdot z = (\gamma' - i\gamma_w) \cdot z$$

**Critical Condition** ($\sigma' = 0$):

$$\gamma' - i_{cr} \cdot \gamma_w = 0$$

$$i_{cr} = \frac{\gamma'}{\gamma_w} = \frac{\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w}{\gamma_w}$$

For typical sand with $\gamma_{sat} = 20 \text{ kN/m}^3$ and $\gamma_w = 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$:

$$i_{cr} = \frac{20 - 10}{10} = 1.0$$

When $i > i_{cr}$: soil grains lose contact, soil behaves like a viscous liquid → **quicksand condition**.

**Real-world examples**: Excavations below water table, dewatering failures, cofferdams with seepage beneath.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Using wrong unit weight below water table**: Students often use $\gamma_{dry}$ or $\gamma_{moist}$ below the water table. The correct choice is **$\gamma_{sat}$** for total stress and **$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$** for incremental effective stress.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to sum layered contributions**: For multi-layer profiles, total stress is the **cumulative sum** of $\gamma_i \cdot H_i$ for all layers above the point.
>
> 3. **Pore pressure calculation error**: Pore pressure is $\gamma_w \times h_w$ where $h_w$ is the **vertical distance from the water table to the point**, not the total depth from ground surface.
>
> 4. **Sign convention in capillary zone**: Pore pressure above the water table in capillary zone is **negative**. Forgetting the negative sign leads to underestimation of effective stress.
>
> 5. **Unit inconsistency**: Mixing $\gamma_w = 9.81$ and $\gamma_w = 10$ in the same problem. Stay consistent — usually KTU exams expect $\gamma_w = 9.81$ unless specifically stated otherwise.
>
> 6. **Reporting intermediate steps**: KTU valuation expects all steps. Don't just write the final answer. Always show the formula, substitution, and result separately.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> ✅ **Terzaghi's Effective Stress Equation**: $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ — the cornerstone of soil mechanics.
>
> ✅ **Total Stress** = weight of everything above per unit area = $\gamma \cdot z$ (or summed for layers).
>
> ✅ **Pore Water Pressure** = hydrostatic water pressure = $\gamma_w \cdot h_w$ (zero at WT, positive below, negative above in capillary zone).
>
> ✅ **Submerged Unit Weight** = $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w \approx 9$ to $10 \text{ kN/m}^3$ — used to compute $\sigma'$ directly below water table.
>
> ✅ **Above water table** (dry/moist, no capillary): $u = 0$, so $\sigma' = \sigma$.
>
> ✅ **Capillary fringe**: $u$ is negative, so $\sigma' > \sigma$ (suction increases contact forces).
>
> ✅ **Quick sand / Boiling**: Occurs when upward seepage makes $\sigma' = 0$, at critical hydraulic gradient $i_{cr} = \gamma'/\gamma_w \approx 1.0$.
>
> ✅ **Standard Values**: $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (or 10 for simplicity in KTU), $\gamma_{water} \approx 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$.
>
> ✅ **Why effective stress matters**: It alone controls shear strength, volume change, and bearing capacity — pore pressure does NOT contribute to shear resistance.
>
> ✅ **Three-zone analysis** for any depth: (1) Above WT → $\sigma' = \sigma$, (2) Capillary fringe → $\sigma' = \sigma + \gamma_w h_c$, (3) Below WT → $\sigma' = \sigma - \gamma_w h_w$.
>
> ✅ **Equation validity**: Strictly for **saturated soils** under **steady-state seepage** or **static** conditions. Transient (consolidation) conditions require separate analysis using excess pore pressure.
>
> ✅ **Layered soil totals**: Always sum contributions of each layer separately using its appropriate unit weight.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pore Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pore Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Pore Water Pressure (Pore Pressure, u)**
> The pressure exerted by the water filling the void spaces (pores) between soil grains is termed **Pore Water Pressure** or simply **Pore Pressure**, denoted by the symbol `u` and expressed in units of **kN/m²** or **kPa**. It is an isotropic pressure (acts equally in all directions) and represents the neutral stress component in a soil mass.

Mathematically, pore pressure is the pressure of the pore fluid measured relative to the **atmospheric pressure**. When measured above atmospheric, it is called **excess pore pressure** ($\Delta u$); when at hydrostatic equilibrium, it is the **hydrostatic pore pressure**.

## 1.2 The Governing Principle — Terzaghi's Effective Stress Equation

The fundamental equation governing soil behaviour is:

$$\sigma = \sigma' + u$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = **Total Stress** (stress due to overburden weight and applied loads)
- $\sigma'$ = **Effective Stress** (stress carried by the soil skeleton)
- $u$ = **Pore Water Pressure**

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight — Course Outcome Mapping**
> This topic is directly mapped to **CO2** of PCAGT402: *"Apply the concepts of effective stress and pore pressure for solving geotechnical engineering problems."*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge Under Water" Model

Imagine a fully saturated kitchen sponge sitting under water:

- The **sponge skeleton** (solid grains) carries the "effective" load → analogous to **effective stress** ($\sigma'$).
- The **water inside the sponge's pores** pushes outwards against the grains → this is **pore pressure** ($u$).
- The **total weight pressing down** on the sponge → **total stress** ($\sigma$).

If you gently squeeze the sponge but allow water to freely escape (high permeability, e.g., sand), $u$ remains essentially hydrostatic. If you squeeze it suddenly while trapping the water (low permeability, e.g., clay), $u$ shoots up — this is the **excess pore pressure** that engineers must dissipate before construction to prevent failure.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Unit weight of water, $\gamma_w$ | **9.81** (often taken as **10** for KTU problems) | kN/m³ |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | **101.325** | kPa |
| Density of water, $\rho_w$ | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Gravitational acceleration, $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Pore Pressure

Soil deposits exhibit **three distinct categories** of pore pressure, each requiring a different engineering treatment:

### (a) Hydrostatic Pore Pressure ($u_0$)
The pressure exerted by **stationary groundwater** at a given depth. It depends purely on the height of the water column above the point of interest.

$$u_0 = h_w \cdot \gamma_w$$

Where $h_w$ is the height of the water table above the point.

### (b) Excess Pore Pressure ($\Delta u$)
The pressure **above hydrostatic** developed due to:
- Applied structural loads (buildings, embankments)
- Construction activities (excavation, pile driving)
- Seismic events (earthquakes)
- Changes in groundwater table

### (c) Negative Pore Pressure (Matric Suction, $-u$)
Found in **unsaturated soils** above the water table. The pore water is under tension due to capillary action. Critical in studying:
- Compacted earth structures
- Expansive (black cotton) soils
- Rainfall-induced slope failures

## 2.2 Initial Pore Pressure (Before Construction)

In natural deposits, the initial vertical effective stress at depth $z$ is:

$$\sigma'_0 = \sigma_0 - u_0 = \gamma_{sat} \cdot z - \gamma_w \cdot h_w$$

Where:
- $\gamma_{sat}$ = saturated unit weight of soil
- $z$ = depth below ground surface
- $h_w$ = depth of water table below ground surface

## 2.3 Skempton's Pore Pressure Coefficients (A and B)

When a soil element is loaded, the induced excess pore pressure is given by **Skempton's Equation** (1954):

$$\Delta u = B \left[ \Delta \sigma_3 + A(\Delta \sigma_1 - \Delta \sigma_3) \right]$$

### Coefficient B (Pore Pressure Response to Equal All-Round Stress)

$$B = \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta \sigma_3}$$

### Coefficient A (Pore Pressure Response to Deviator Stress)

$$A = \frac{\Delta u_{deviator}}{B \cdot (\Delta \sigma_1 - \Delta \sigma_3)}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Quick Reference

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Range / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \sigma' + u$ | Terzaghi's Effective Stress | Universal |
| 2 | $u_0 = h_w \cdot \gamma_w$ | Hydrostatic Pore Pressure | Below water table |
| 3 | $\Delta u = B[\Delta\sigma_3 + A(\Delta\sigma_1 - \Delta\sigma_3)]$ | Skempton's Pore Pressure Eqn. | Under applied loading |
| 4 | $B = 1.0$ | Saturated soil | Soft clays (CU test) |
| 5 | $B = 0.0$ | Dry soil | Completely dry condition |
| 6 | $0 < B < 1$ | Partially saturated soil | Compacted fills |
| 7 | $A = \frac{1}{3}$ to $\frac{2}{3}$ | Loose sand / Normally consolidated clay | At failure |
| 8 | $A < 0$ | Overconsolidated clay (dense) | Sensitive to stress history |
| 9 | $u = u_0 + \Delta u$ | Total pore pressure | Sum of hydrostatic + excess |
| 10 | $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ | Effective stress calculation | Most important in KTU |

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> Pore pressure principles govern the design of **embankments on soft clays** (Kerala backwaters), **deep excavations** (Kochi Metro), **tunnel linings** (Western Ghats tunnels), and **earth dams** (Idukki Dam). Incorrect pore pressure estimation is the leading cause of **undrained failures** during monsoon-driven construction.

## 2.5 Pore Pressure Under Special Loading Conditions

For **Triaxial Test conditions**, three classical stress paths arise:

$$\text{(i) Isotropic loading: } \Delta u = B \cdot \Delta \sigma_3$$

$$\text{(ii) Uniaxial loading: } \Delta u = B \cdot \left[\Delta \sigma_3 + A(\Delta \sigma_1 - \Delta \sigma_3)\right]$$

$$\text{(iii) Uniaxial deformation (oedometric): } \Delta u = \Delta \sigma_1 \quad \text{(since } \Delta \sigma_3 = 0\text{)}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Effective Stress Principle (Terzaghi, 1936)

### Step 1: Consider a Saturated Soil Element
Consider a horizontal cross-section of area $A$ passing through a saturated soil mass at depth $z$ below the ground surface.

### Step 2: Identify the Force Components
The total downward force on the cross-section is:
$$P_{total} = W_{solids} + W_{water} = A \cdot z \cdot \gamma_{sat}$$

This force is shared between:
- **Soil grains** at inter-particle contact points (effective force, $P'$)
- **Pore water** filling the voids (neutral force, $P_w$)

### Step 3: Equate the Stresses
Total stress is the force per unit area:
$$\sigma = \frac{P_{total}}{A} = \gamma_{sat} \cdot z$$

The pore water pressure at depth $z$ (with water table at depth $z_w$):
$$u = \gamma_w \cdot (z - z_w) \quad \text{or} \quad u = h_w \cdot \gamma_w$$

### Step 4: Apply Equilibrium
Since forces must balance, and pore water carries part of the load:
$$P_{total} = P' + P_w \implies \sigma \cdot A = \sigma' \cdot A + u \cdot A$$

Dividing by area $A$:
$$\sigma = \sigma' + u$$

### Step 5: Final Form (Rearranging)
$$\boxed{\sigma' = \sigma - u}$$

This is **Terzaghi's Principle of Effective Stress** — the single most important equation in geotechnical engineering.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of Skempton's Pore Pressure Equation

### Step 1: Setup of Triaxial Loading
A soil specimen is subjected to:
- Increase in cell pressure: $\Delta \sigma_3$ (all-around)
- Increase in axial stress: $\Delta \sigma_1$ (additional deviator)

Total induced excess pore pressure: $\Delta u$

### Step 2: Decompose the Loading
The loading is decomposed into two stages:

**Stage I — Isotropic (equal all-round) loading:**
Apply $\Delta \sigma_3$ equally in all directions.

The induced pore pressure is:
$$\Delta u_1 = B \cdot \Delta \sigma_3$$

Where $B$ is the pore pressure coefficient for equal all-round stress.

### Step 3: Stage II — Deviatoric Loading
Apply additional axial stress $\Delta \sigma_d = \Delta \sigma_1 - \Delta \sigma_3$.

The induced pore pressure from deviatoric stress alone:
$$\Delta u_2 = B \cdot A \cdot (\Delta \sigma_1 - \Delta \sigma_3)$$

Where $A$ is the pore pressure coefficient for deviatoric stress.

### Step 4: Superposition (Total Pore Pressure)
By the principle of superposition:
$$\Delta u = \Delta u_1 + \Delta u_2$$

Substituting:
$$\Delta u = B \cdot \Delta \sigma_3 + B \cdot A \cdot (\Delta \sigma_1 - \Delta \sigma_3)$$

Factoring out $B$:
$$\boxed{\Delta u = B \left[\Delta \sigma_3 + A(\Delta \sigma_1 - \Delta \sigma_3)\right]}$$

### Step 5: Special Case — Saturated Soil
For **fully saturated soil**, $B = 1.0$, simplifying to:
$$\Delta u_{sat} = \Delta \sigma_3 + A(\Delta \sigma_1 - \Delta \sigma_3)$$

### Step 6: Special Case — Dry Soil
For **completely dry soil**, $B = 0$, so:
$$\Delta u_{dry} = 0$$

(No pore pressure develops because there is no pore fluid to pressurize.)

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Style

**Problem:** A 10 m thick clay layer lies below a water table at 2 m depth. The saturated unit weight is 19 kN/m³. A surcharge load of 50 kN/m² is suddenly applied on the ground surface. Determine the initial effective stress at the **mid-depth** of the clay layer, taking $A = 0.6$ and $B = 1.0$.

**Given:**
- Depth of clay layer, $H = 10$ m (from 2 m to 12 m below GL)
- Surcharge load, $q = 50$ kN/m²
- $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³
- $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³
- Point of interest: mid-depth = $2 + 5 = 7$ m below GL

### Step 1: Total Stress at Mid-Depth
The total stress consists of the overburden pressure from soil above the point and the applied surcharge:
$$\sigma = \gamma_{sat} \cdot z_{clay} + q$$

Where $z_{clay}$ is the depth of clay above the mid-depth = 5 m.

$$\sigma = (19 \times 5) + 50 = 95 + 50 = 145 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 2: Hydrostatic Pore Pressure at Mid-Depth
Water table is at 2 m below GL; the point is at 7 m below GL, so the height of water column:
$$h_w = 7 - 2 = 5 \text{ m}$$

$$u_0 = h_w \cdot \gamma_w = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 3: Excess Pore Pressure Due to Surcharge (Sudden Loading)
For a surcharge applied over a large area (no lateral strain — oedometric condition):
$$\Delta \sigma_1 = q = 50 \text{ kN/m}^2, \quad \Delta \sigma_3 = 0$$

$$\Delta u = B \cdot [0 + A \cdot (50 - 0)] = 1.0 \times 0.6 \times 50 = 30 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 4: Total Pore Pressure
$$u = u_0 + \Delta u = 49.05 + 30 = 79.05 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 5: Initial Effective Stress
$$\sigma' = \sigma - u = 145 - 79.05 = 65.95 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{\sigma'_{initial} = 65.95 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Insight**
> The applied surcharge of 50 kN/m² instantaneously increases total stress by 50 kPa, but only $50 - 30 = 20$ kPa is transferred to the soil skeleton initially. The remaining **30 kPa is carried by the pore water**. As consolidation progresses, this excess pore pressure dissipates and the effective stress increases to the full 50 kPa — illustrating why soft clay sites require **preloading with vertical drains** before construction.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Values of A and B

| Soil Type / Condition | B Value | A Value (at failure) | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Saturated soft clay | **1.0** | **0.5 – 1.0** | High excess pore pressure under load |
| Saturated stiff clay (OC) | **1.0** | **-0.5 – 0.5** | May develop negative $\Delta u$ |
| Saturated loose sand | **1.0** | **0.5 – 0.7** | Tendency to liquefy under cyclic load |
| Partially saturated clay | **0.5 – 0.9** | **0.2 – 0.5** | Lower excess pore pressure |
| Dry soil | **0.0** | **0.0** | No pore pressure response |
| Sensitive clay (extra-sensitive) | **1.0** | **> 1.5** | Highly thixotropic behaviour |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Total Stress, Effective Stress, Pore Pressure Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Stress sigma"] --> B["Effective Stress sigma prime"]
    A --> C["Pore Pressure u"]
    B --> D["Carried by Soil Skeleton"]
    B --> E["Controls Shear Strength"]
    B --> F["Controls Compressibility"]
    C --> G["Acts in Pore Water"]
    C --> H["Neutral Stress"]
    C --> I["Isotropic in Nature"]
    D --> J["Engineering Behaviour of Soil"]
    E --> J
    F --> J
    G --> K["Total Pore Pressure u equals u0 plus Delta u"]
    H --> K
    I --> K
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Pore Pressure Build-Up & Dissipation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["LOADING STAGE"] --> L2["Total Stress Increases"]
    L2 --> L3["Excess Pore Pressure Delta u Develops"]
    L3 --> L4["Effective Stress Decreases Temporarily"]
    L4 --> L5["Soil Skeleton is Unstable"]
    
    M1["CONSOLIDATION STAGE"] --> M2["Water Exits the Soil"]
    M2 --> M3["Excess Pore Pressure Dissipates"]
    M3 --> M4["Effective Stress Increases"]
    M4 --> M5["Soil Skeleton Gains Strength"]
    
    L5 --> M1
    M5 --> N["Stable Soil Mass - Long Term Condition"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Applied Loading Delta sigma 1 and Delta sigma 3"] --> Q{"Soil Saturation State?"}
    Q -->|Saturated| R["B equals 1.0"]
    Q -->|Partially Saturated| S["B between 0 and 1"]
    Q -->|Dry| T["B equals 0"]
    
    R --> U["Excess Pore Pressure depends on A"]
    S --> V["Lower Excess Pore Pressure"]
    T --> W["No Pore Pressure Response"]
    
    U --> X1["NC Clay A between 0.5 and 1.0"]
    U --> X2["OC Clay A between negative 0.5 and 0.5"]
    U --> X3["Loose Sand A around 0.5 to 0.7"]
```

    MP1 --> MP1A["Simple and Economical"]
