<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define pore pressure. Explain the difference between hydrostatic pore pressure and excess pore pressure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Pore Pressure** is the pressure exerted by the water present in the void spaces of a soil mass. It is an isotropic (equal in all directions) pressure denoted by $u$ and expressed in kN/m².

| Type | Definition | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydrostatic Pore Pressure ($u_0$)** | Pressure due to stationary groundwater column | Natural groundwater condition |
| **Excess Pore Pressure ($\Delta u$)** | Pressure above hydrostatic | Applied loads, construction, earthquakes |

Mathematically: $u = u_0 + \Delta u$, where $u_0 = h_w \cdot \gamma_w$.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is Skempton's pore pressure coefficient B? Mention its value for saturated and dry soils.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Skempton's Coefficient B** is defined as the ratio of excess pore pressure developed to the applied equal all-round (isotropic) stress increase:
> $$B = \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta \sigma_3}$$

- For **saturated soil**: $B = 1.0$
- For **dry soil**: $B = 0$
- For **partially saturated soil**: $0 < B < 1$

This coefficient indicates the degree of saturation and the soil's tendency to develop excess pore pressure under isotropic loading.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Full Solution `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`

**A soil profile consists of 3 m of sand above the water table ($\gamma = 17$ kN/m³) and 6 m of saturated clay below ($\gamma_{sat} = 18.5$ kN/m³, $A = 0.7$, $B = 1.0$). A uniform surcharge load of 80 kN/m² is applied at the ground surface.**

#### Part (a) — Calculate the initial effective vertical stress at the **mid-depth of the clay layer** before the load is applied. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify the depths**
- Sand layer: 0 to 3 m (above water table)
- Clay layer: 3 to 9 m (saturated)
- Mid-depth of clay = $3 + 3 = 6$ m below GL

**Step 2: Calculate Total Stress at 6 m depth**
$$\sigma = \gamma_{sand} \cdot 3 + \gamma_{sat,clay} \cdot 3$$
$$\sigma = (17 \times 3) + (18.5 \times 3) = 51 + 55.5 = 106.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate Hydrostatic Pore Pressure at 6 m depth**
Water table at 3 m; point at 6 m, so $h_w = 3$ m
$$u_0 = h_w \cdot \gamma_w = 3 \times 9.81 = 29.43 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Initial Effective Stress**
$$\sigma'_0 = \sigma - u_0 = 106.5 - 29.43 = \mathbf{77.07 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

#### Part (b) — Determine the **excess pore pressure** and **initial effective stress immediately after load application**. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify Stress Increments**
For a wide surcharge load (oedometric conditions — no lateral strain):
$$\Delta \sigma_1 = q = 80 \text{ kN/m}^2, \quad \Delta \sigma_3 = 0$$

**Step 2: Calculate Excess Pore Pressure**
Using Skempton's equation:
$$\Delta u = B \left[\Delta \sigma_3 + A(\Delta \sigma_1 - \Delta \sigma_3)\right]$$
$$\Delta u = 1.0 \times [0 + 0.7 \times (80 - 0)] = 56 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: New Total Stress After Loading**
$$\sigma_{new} = \sigma_{old} + q = 106.5 + 80 = 186.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4: New Total Pore Pressure**
$$u_{new} = u_0 + \Delta u = 29.43 + 56 = 85.43 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5: New Initial Effective Stress (Immediately After Loading)**
$$\sigma'_{new} = \sigma_{new} - u_{new} = 186.5 - 85.43 = \mathbf{101.07 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

**Step 6: Comparison Table**

| Parameter | Before Loading | Immediately After Loading |
|---|---|---|
| Total Stress (kN/m²) | 106.5 | 186.5 |
| Pore Pressure (kN/m²) | 29.43 | 85.43 |
| Effective Stress (kN/m²) | **77.07** | **101.07** |

**Step 7: Long-Term Final Effective Stress (after full consolidation)**
After all excess pore pressure dissipates ($\Delta u = 0$):
$$\sigma'_{final} = \sigma_{new} - u_0 = 186.5 - 29.43 = 157.07 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

---

### QUESTION B — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**A triaxial test is conducted on a saturated clay specimen. The cell pressure is increased by 100 kPa, and the deviator stress at failure is 200 kPa. The pore pressure coefficients are $A = 0.65$ and $B = 1.0$.**

#### Part (a) — Calculate the **excess pore pressure developed** at failure. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify Stress Increments**
- All-round (cell) pressure increase: $\Delta \sigma_3 = 100$ kPa
- Deviator stress at failure: $\Delta \sigma_d = 200$ kPa
- Therefore: $\Delta \sigma_1 = \Delta \sigma_3 + \Delta \sigma_d = 100 + 200 = 300$ kPa

**Step 2: Apply Skempton's Equation**
$$\Delta u = B \left[\Delta \sigma_3 + A(\Delta \sigma_1 - \Delta \sigma_3)\right]$$

Substituting:
$$\Delta u = 1.0 \times [100 + 0.65 \times (300 - 100)]$$

$$\Delta u = 100 + 0.65 \times 200 = 100 + 130 = \mathbf{230 \text{ kPa}}$$

#### Part (b) — Determine the **effective principal stresses** at failure. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1: Calculate Total Principal Stresses**
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 + \sigma_d = 100 + 200 = 300 \text{ kPa}$$
$$\sigma_3 = 100 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Effective Major Principal Stress**
$$\sigma'_1 = \sigma_1 - \Delta u = 300 - 230 = \mathbf{70 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 3: Effective Minor Principal Stress**
$$\sigma'_3 = \sigma_3 - \Delta u = 100 - 230 = \mathbf{-130 \text{ kPa}}$$

> [!WARNING] **Interpretation Alert — KTU Examiner's Note**
> A **negative effective confining stress** ($\sigma'_3 < 0$) is physically impossible. This indicates that the applied stress system is **unrealistic** or that the soil has already reached failure before this loading condition. In an actual test, the test would be stopped when pore pressure equals the cell pressure.

**Step 4: Verification Using Stress Path**
Effective stress path: $\sigma'_3$ vs $\sigma'_1$
- Starting point: (100, 100) kPa
- Ending point: (-130, 70) kPa — invalid

**Correct Engineering Interpretation:**
The test outcome suggests **very high pore pressure response (A = 0.65)** indicating a **highly sensitive, normally consolidated clay** — such soil would fail under much lower deviator stress in reality. This is why $\bar{B}$-check and strain-controlled tests are essential in triaxial testing.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to add $u_0$ and $\Delta u$**: Many students compute only $\Delta u$ and forget to include hydrostatic pore pressure. Total pore pressure is **always** $u = u_0 + \Delta u$.
> 2. **Mixing up B values**: Students often write $B = 1.0$ for dry soil or $B = 0$ for saturated soil. Memorize: **saturated → 1, dry → 0**.
> 3. **Sign convention for $\sigma_3$ in oedometric conditions**: When a wide load is applied, $\Delta \sigma_3 \neq 0$ in reality (due to Poisson's ratio effect), but KTU problems typically assume $\Delta \sigma_3 = 0$ for **simple surcharge loading** unless specified.
> 4. **Incorrect unit conversions**: $\gamma_w$ may be given as $1000$ kg/m³ × $g = 9810$ N/m³ = **9.81 kN/m³**. Don't confuse with 1000 kN/m³.
> 5. **Skipping Step-by-Step**: KTU valuation awards marks for **each logical step**. Even if the final answer is wrong, you earn 1-2 marks per correct intermediate step.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Pore pressure (u)** is the pressure of water in soil voids; isotropic and neutral in nature.
- [x] **Terzaghi's equation** $\sigma = \sigma' + u$ is the foundation of all soil mechanics.
- [x] **Effective stress** controls strength and deformation; total stress is irrelevant for engineering behaviour.
- [x] **Hydrostatic pore pressure** $u_0 = h_w \cdot \gamma_w$ exists naturally below the water table.
- [x] **Excess pore pressure** $\Delta u$ develops due to applied loads in low-permeability soils.
- [x] **Skempton's equation**: $\Delta u = B[\Delta \sigma_3 + A(\Delta \sigma_1 - \Delta \sigma_3)]$.
- [x] **B = 1.0 for saturated soil**; **B = 0 for dry soil**; **0 < B < 1 for partially saturated soil**.
- [x] **A is positive for NC clay and loose sand**; **A can be negative for stiff OC clay**.
- [x] **In oedometric loading** (wide load, no lateral strain), assume $\Delta \sigma_3 = 0$.
- [x] **Consolidation is the dissipation of excess pore pressure** — long-term stability requires this process.
- [x] **Pore pressure measurement** is done using piezometers (Standpipe, Casagrande, Vibrating Wire).
- [x] **Negative pore pressure (suction)** exists above the water table in unsaturated soils.
- [x] **High pore pressure → low effective stress → low shear strength → potential failure**.
- [x] **KTU standard value**: $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ (or 10 kN/m³ for quick calculations).
- [x] **Always state units** (kPa or kN/m²) in final numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Diagrams; Permeability: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pressure Diagrams; Permeability: Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Pressure Diagrams — Formal Definition

In Soil Mechanics, the **Pressure Diagram** is a graphical representation (usually a rectangular or trapezoidal plot) showing how the three fundamental stresses in a soil mass vary with depth below the ground surface.

The three stresses that constitute the complete pressure diagram are:

1. **Total Stress (σ)** — The overall stress at any point in a soil mass due to the weight of overlying soil plus any surcharge load.
2. **Pore Water Pressure (u)** — The pressure exerted by water present in the voids of the soil.
3. **Effective Stress (σ′)** — The stress carried by the soil skeleton (solid particles) at their points of contact. This is the stress that governs all deformation and strength behavior of soil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Terzaghi's Principle of Effective Stress (1936):**
> $$\sigma = \sigma' + u$$
> This equation is the foundation of modern soil mechanics. All shear strength, consolidation, and bearing capacity calculations use **effective stress**, not total stress.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy for Pressure Diagrams

Imagine a **sponge soaked in water**, sitting on a kitchen scale:
- The sponge particles (soil grains) carry part of the weight.
- The water inside the sponge pores carries the rest.
- If you squeeze the sponge gently, water squirts out — this is what pore pressure does when disturbed.
- The harder the solid skeleton pushes against the scale, the greater the effective stress.

A **pressure diagram** is essentially a visual "ledger" telling us, at each depth, how much load is being carried by water and how much by the soil skeleton.

### 1.3 Permeability — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Permeability (Hydraulic Conductivity):**
> Permeability is the property of a porous soil mass that allows water (or any fluid) to flow through its interconnected void spaces under a hydraulic gradient. It is quantitatively expressed by the **coefficient of permeability (k)**.

Mathematically, **Darcy's Law (1856)** governs permeability:

$$v = k \cdot i$$

Where:
- $v$ = discharge velocity (superficial velocity) in **cm/s** or **m/s**
- $k$ = coefficient of permeability in **cm/s** or **m/s**
- $i$ = hydraulic gradient (dimensionless, $\Delta h / L$)

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy for Permeability

Think of a **coffee filter**: water flows slowly through fine coffee grounds (low permeability — clay-like), but flows quickly through a coarser filter (higher permeability — sand-like). Soils behave exactly the same way — their ability to transmit water depends on the **size, shape, and connectivity of voids**.

- **Gravel** = like an empty colander (very high k ≈ **1 cm/s** or more)
- **Sand** = like a sponge (medium k ≈ **10⁻³ cm/s**)
- **Silt** = like a tightly-packed tea bag (low k ≈ **10⁻⁶ cm/s**)
- **Clay** = like nearly waterproof plastic (very low k ≈ **10⁻⁹ cm/s**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Permeability is a property of the **soil-fluid system**, not the soil alone. The same soil has different permeabilities for water, oil, or air.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of the Pressure Diagram

The pressure diagram of a soil profile is built by computing the three stresses layer-by-layer from the ground surface downward.

**Layer 1 — Total Stress (σ):**
- Total stress at depth $z$ in a soil with bulk unit weight $\gamma$:

$$\sigma = \sum (\gamma_i \cdot z_i)$$

- Each layer contributes its own unit weight × thickness to the cumulative total stress.

**Layer 2 — Pore Water Pressure (u):**
- Determined by the position of the **water table** or the **phreatic surface**.
- Below the water table (assuming hydrostatic conditions):

$$u = \gamma_w \cdot h_w$$

- Above the water table (in capillary zone), pore pressure can be **negative** (matric suction).

**Layer 3 — Effective Stress (σ′):**
- Calculated by subtracting pore pressure from total stress:

$$\sigma' = \sigma - u$$

### 2.2 Standard Scenarios for Pressure Diagrams (KTU Favourites)

**Scenario A — Soil with Water Table at Surface (Submerged Soil, Static):**

For a soil of submerged unit weight $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$:
- Total stress increases linearly: $\sigma = \gamma_{sat} \cdot z$
- Pore pressure increases linearly: $u = \gamma_w \cdot z$
- Effective stress increases linearly: $\sigma' = \gamma' \cdot z$

**Scenario B — Soil with Water Table at Depth $z_w$:**
- Above water table (use $\gamma_{dry}$ or $\gamma_{moist}$): no pore pressure.
- Below water table: $u = \gamma_w (z - z_w)$ and $\sigma' = \sigma - u$.

**Scenario C — Upward Seepage (Critical Condition):**

When water flows upward with hydraulic gradient $i$, the effective stress is **reduced**:

$$\sigma' = z \cdot \gamma' + z \cdot \gamma_w \cdot (i_{static} - i_{up})$$

Equivalently:

$$\sigma' = z \cdot \left[ \gamma' - i \cdot \gamma_w \right]$$

**Scenario D — Quick Sand / Boiling Condition:**
- When upward seepage gradient equals the **critical hydraulic gradient** $i_c$:

$$i_c = \frac{\gamma'}{\gamma_w} = \frac{G - 1}{1 + e}$$

- At this point, effective stress = 0, and sand behaves like a heavy fluid → "quicksand."

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Pressure Diagrams & Permeability)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \sum \gamma_i z_i$ | Total stress at depth | kN/m² or kPa |
| 2 | $u = \gamma_w \cdot h_w$ | Pore water pressure | kN/m² or kPa |
| 3 | $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ | Effective stress (Terzaghi) | kN/m² or kPa |
| 4 | $\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(G + e)\gamma_w}{1 + e}$ | Saturated unit weight | kN/m³ |
| 5 | $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ | Submerged (buoyant) unit weight | kN/m³ |
| 6 | $\gamma_{dry} = \dfrac{G \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$ | Dry unit weight | kN/m³ |
| 7 | $v = k \cdot i$ | Darcy's Law | cm/s |
| 8 | $q = k \cdot i \cdot A$ | Discharge (volume rate) | cm³/s |
| 9 | $i_c = \dfrac{\gamma'}{\gamma_w} = \dfrac{G-1}{1+e}$ | Critical hydraulic gradient | dimensionless |
| 10 | $v_s = \dfrac{v}{n}$ | Seepage velocity | cm/s |
| 11 | $k \propto \dfrac{D_{10}^2}{\mu}$ | Approx. Hazen formula (sands) | cm/s |
| 12 | $k_{20°} = k_T \cdot \dfrac{\mu_T}{\mu_{20°}}$ | Temperature correction for k | cm/s |

> **Note:** $G$ = specific gravity, $e$ = void ratio, $n$ = porosity, $\mu$ = viscosity, $D_{10}$ = effective grain size.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure diagrams** are used to compute consolidation settlement, slope stability, and bearing capacity of foundations.
- **Permeability** controls the rate of consolidation, seepage loss through earth dams, dewatering design for excavations, and groundwater flow studies.
- The combination of both topics leads directly to **seepage analysis**, the upcoming topic in Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Effective Stress Concept (Terzaghi's Principle)

Consider a saturated soil sample of cross-sectional area $A$ at depth $z$:

**Step 1 — Total vertical force at the section:**
The total weight of soil and water above the section acts on area $A$:

$$W = (\gamma_{sat} \cdot z) \cdot A$$

So the total stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{W}{A} = \gamma_{sat} \cdot z$$

**Step 2 — Force carried by water in the voids:**
The water in the void space has area $A_v$ (where $A_v$ = portion of cross-section occupied by voids).
The pore water pressure $u$ acts over this entire void area:

$$F_{water} = u \cdot A_v$$

But pore water pressure acts **equally in all directions** (Pascal's law), so it also acts on the solid grains through the contact points. The net upward force from water equals:

$$F_{water,\,upward} = u \cdot A$$

**Step 3 — Force carried by soil skeleton:**
By force equilibrium on the horizontal plane:

$$F_{skeleton} + F_{water} = W$$

$$F_{skeleton} = W - u \cdot A$$

**Step 4 — Define effective stress:**
The effective stress is the force per unit total area carried by the soil skeleton:

$$\sigma' = \frac{F_{skeleton}}{A} = \frac{W - u \cdot A}{A} = \frac{W}{A} - u$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\sigma' = \sigma - u}$$

This is Terzaghi's effective stress equation — the **most important equation in soil mechanics**.

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Pressure Diagram

**Problem:**
A soil profile consists of:
- Layer 1: 0 to 3 m — Moist sand, $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³, moisture content = 10%
- Layer 2: 3 m to 8 m — Saturated sand, $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³
- Water table is at 3 m depth.
- $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³.

Draw the total stress, pore pressure, and effective stress diagrams and compute stresses at depths 0, 3 m, 6 m, and 8 m.

**Solution:**

**At Depth z = 0 m (Ground Surface):**
$$\sigma = 0$$
$$u = 0$$
$$\sigma' = 0$$

**At Depth z = 3 m (Bottom of Layer 1, at Water Table):**

$$\sigma = 17 \times 3 = 51 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
$$u = 0 \quad \text{(water table is here)}$$
$$\sigma' = 51 - 0 = 51 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**At Depth z = 6 m (Mid-Layer 2, 3 m below water table):**

$$\sigma = 51 + 20 \times 3 = 51 + 60 = 111 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
$$u = \gamma_w \cdot h_w = 9.81 \times 3 = 29.43 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
$$\sigma' = 111 - 29.43 = 81.57 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**At Depth z = 8 m (Bottom of Layer 2, 5 m below water table):**

$$\sigma = 51 + 20 \times 5 = 51 + 100 = 151 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
$$u = 9.81 \times 5 = 49.05 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
$$\sigma' = 151 - 49.05 = 101.95 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Summary Table:**

| Depth (m) | σ (kN/m²) | u (kN/m²) | σ′ (kN/m²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 51 | 0 | 51 |
| 6 | 111 | 29.43 | 81.57 |
| 8 | 151 | 49.05 | 101.95 |

> Note the **kink** in the total stress diagram at 3 m (change in unit weight), and the **start** of pore pressure at 3 m. The effective stress diagram has a kink too — its slope changes because $\gamma_{dry}$ changes to $\gamma'$.

### 3.3 Derivation of Critical Hydraulic Gradient ($i_c$)

For an upward flow through saturated soil of thickness $z$:

**Effective stress at the bottom:**

$$\sigma' = \gamma' \cdot z - i \cdot \gamma_w \cdot z$$

At the **quick (boiling) condition**, effective stress becomes zero:

$$\sigma' = 0$$

$$\gamma' \cdot z - i_c \cdot \gamma_w \cdot z = 0$$

$$i_c = \frac{\gamma'}{\gamma_w}$$

Substituting $\gamma' = \dfrac{(G-1)\gamma_w}{1+e}$:

$$\boxed{i_c = \frac{G - 1}{1 + e}}$$

For typical values $G = 2.65$, $e = 0.65$:

$$i_c = \frac{2.65 - 1}{1 + 0.65} = \frac{1.65}{1.65} = 1.0$$

So a hydraulic gradient of **approximately 1.0** (often taken as unity) causes quicksand in typical sands.

### 3.4 Derivation of Darcy's Law

Consider water flowing through a saturated soil column of length $L$ and cross-section $A$, with head difference $h$:

- **Hydraulic gradient:** $i = h / L$
- **Observation by Darcy:** The discharge $Q$ is proportional to $i$ and $A$:

$$Q \propto A \cdot i$$

Introducing the proportionality constant $k$:

$$Q = k \cdot i \cdot A$$

Since discharge velocity $v = Q / A$:

$$\boxed{v = k \cdot i}$$

The **seepage velocity** $v_s$ (actual velocity through voids):

$$v_s = \frac{v}{n}$$

Where $n$ = porosity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

    L2 --> U2[u=49.05 kN per m2]

    S4 --> EFF2[sigma prime=101.95 kN per m2]

    GRAD --> DARCY[Darcy Law v = k times i]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define effective stress. State Terzaghi's principle of effective stress.*

**Model Answer:**

Effective stress is the stress carried by the soil skeleton at the points of contact between solid particles. It is the stress that controls the volume change and shear strength behaviour of soil.

Terzaghi's principle states that for a saturated soil:

$$\sigma = \sigma' + u$$

Where $\sigma$ is total stress, $\sigma' $ is effective stress, and $u$ is pore water pressure. Therefore:

$$\sigma' = \sigma - u$$

**(3 marks — full marks for stating the formula and the definition.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Define permeability. State Darcy's law and the units of coefficient of permeability.*

**Model Answer:**

Permeability is the property of a soil mass that allows water to flow through its interconnected voids under a hydraulic gradient.

Darcy's law:

$$v = k \cdot i$$

Where $v$ = discharge velocity, $i$ = hydraulic gradient, $k$ = coefficient of permeability.

Units of $k$: cm/s or m/s (same as velocity).

**(3 marks — definition + formula + units.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

A soil profile consists of three layers:
- Layer 1 (0 to 2 m): Moist sand, $\gamma_1 = 17$ kN/m³
- Layer 2 (2 m to 5 m): Saturated sand, $\gamma_{sat,2} = 19$ kN/m³
- Layer 3 (5 m to 9 m): Saturated clay, $\gamma_{sat,3} = 20$ kN/m³

The water table is at 2 m depth. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³.

**(a)** Compute the total stress, pore water pressure, and effective stress at depths 2 m, 5 m, and 9 m. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Sketch the pressure diagrams clearly showing the variations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stress Calculations:**

**At z = 2 m (Bottom of Layer 1, water table):**

$$\sigma = 17 \times 2 = 34 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$u = 0 \quad \text{(water table location)}$$

$$\sigma' = 34 - 0 = 34 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**At z = 5 m (3 m below water table):**

$$\sigma = 34 + 19 \times 3 = 34 + 57 = 91 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$u = 9.81 \times 3 = 29.43 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\sigma' = 91 - 29.43 = 61.57 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**At z = 9 m (7 m below water table):**

$$\sigma = 91 + 20 \times 4 = 91 + 80 = 171 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$u = 9.81 \times 7 = 68.67 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\sigma' = 171 - 68.67 = 102.33 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Summary:**

| Depth (m) | σ (kN/m²) | u (kN/m²) | σ′ (kN/m²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 34 | 0 | 34 |
| 5 | 91 | 29.43 | 61.57 |
| 9 | 171 | 68.67 | 102.33 |

**(b) Pressure Diagrams (Description for Sketch):**

- **Total stress σ** diagram: starts at 0, rises with slope 17 in layer 1, then slope 19 in layer 2, then slope 20 in layer 3. (Linear piecewise)
- **Pore pressure u** diagram: zero from 0 to 2 m, then rises with slope 9.81 from 2 m to 9 m.
- **Effective stress σ′** diagram: equals σ above 2 m, then equals (σ − u) below 2 m. Kinks at layer boundaries.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Define the coefficient of permeability. Explain Darcy's law with a neat sketch. List the factors affecting permeability of soils. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a sand sample, $G = 2.65$, $e = 0.7$. Find the critical hydraulic gradient and comment on the practical significance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition, Darcy's Law, and Factors:**

**Definition:**
Coefficient of permeability (k) is defined as the discharge velocity of water through a soil column under a unit hydraulic gradient. It is a measure of how easily water flows through the soil.

**Darcy's Law:**
Darcy's law states that for laminar flow through saturated soil:

$$v = k \cdot i = k \cdot \frac{h}{L}$$

where $v$ = discharge velocity, $h$ = head loss across length $L$, $i$ = hydraulic gradient.

**Sketch description:** A horizontal soil column of length $L$ and area $A$ with inflow and outflow standpipes showing head difference $h$.

**Factors affecting permeability:**
1. **Grain size** — Larger grain size → higher k (Hazen: $k \propto D_{10}^2$)
2. **Void ratio** — Higher $e$ → higher k
3. **Shape of grains** — Rounded grains → higher k
4. **Degree of saturation** — $k$ decreases with decreasing saturation
5. **Fluid properties** — Viscosity $\mu$ and unit weight $\gamma_w$
6. **Soil structure** — Stratification, fissures, layering
7. **Temperature** — Higher temperature → lower viscosity → higher k

**(b) Critical Hydraulic Gradient:**

Using:

$$i_c = \frac{G - 1}{1 + e} = \frac{2.65 - 1}{1 + 0.7} = \frac{1.65}{1.7} = 0.97$$

$$i_c \approx 0.97$$

**Practical Significance:**
- At $i = i_c$, effective stress = 0 → **quicksand / boiling condition**.
- Soil loses all shear strength and behaves like a liquid.
- Critical for **upward seepage** scenarios: beneath dams, near excavation pits, sheet pile cofferdams.
- Engineers must ensure $i_{actual} < i_c$ with a factor of safety (typically 2 to 3) to prevent failure.
- Remedial measures: increasing flow path length using sheet piles, using downstream filters, reducing head difference.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ but apply **dry unit weight** instead of **saturated/submerged unit weight** below the water table. **−2 marks** if incorrect unit weight used below WT.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting that pore pressure $u$ is measured from the **water table elevation**, not from the ground surface. **−1 mark** if depth of water table is ignored.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Drawing the pressure diagram without labeling **slopes** (i.e., unit weights) on each segment. Examiners award **2 marks** for slope identification.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing **discharge velocity** $v$ with **seepage velocity** $v_s$ in permeability problems. Recall: $v_s = v / n$ where $n$ = porosity.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** For Darcy's law, students sometimes write $v = k \cdot i$ but forget to mention the **validity range** (laminar flow). Reynolds number > 1 → Darcy's law invalid for coarse gravels.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Terzaghi's Principle:** $\sigma = \sigma' + u$ — The single most important equation.
- **Total stress** depends only on the **weight** of overlying soil (and water), not on groundwater flow.
- **Pore water pressure** acts **equally in all directions** at a point; it does NOT contribute to shear strength.
- **Effective stress** is what governs **all** soil behaviour: shear strength, settlement, lateral earth pressure.
- **Above the water table:** Use dry/moist unit weight; **no** pore pressure.
- **Below the water table (static):** Use saturated unit weight for total stress; subtract pore pressure for effective stress.
- **Upward seepage** REDUCES effective stress; **downward seepage** INCREASES effective stress.
- **Critical Hydraulic Gradient:** $i_c = (G-1)/(1+e)$ — at this value, sand boils.
- **Darcy's Law:** $v = k \cdot i$ — valid for laminar flow; gives discharge (not seepage) velocity.
- **Seepage velocity:** $v_s = v / n$ — actual velocity through the voids.
- **Permeability units:** cm/s or m/s (same as velocity).
- **Range of k:** Gravel > Sand > Silt > Clay (10⁰ → 10⁻⁹ cm/s).
- **Temperature correction:** Standardize $k$ to 20°C for comparison.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Darcy's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Darcy's Law # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Darcy's Law (1856):** The empirically derived fundamental law of hydrogeology and geotechnical engineering that states the rate of fluid flow through a porous medium is directly proportional to the hydraulic gradient and the cross-sectional area of flow. Mathematically, for one-dimensional, laminar, steady-state flow of an incompressible fluid through a saturated porous medium: $$Q = k \cdot i \cdot A$$ $$v = \frac{Q}{A} = k \cdot i$$ Where: - $Q$ = Volume rate of flow (discharge) in **m³/s** or **cm³/s** - $k$ = **Coefficient of permeability** (hydraulic conductivity) in **m/s** or **cm/s** - $i$ = **Hydraulic gradient** (dimensionless) $= h/L$ (head loss per unit length of flow) - $A$ = Total cross-sectional area of the soil mass perpendicular to flow direction (m²) - $v$ = **Discharge velocity** or superficial velocity (m/s) — a fictitious velocity based on total cross-section **Historical Note:** Formulated by **Henry Darcy** (1856) following his classic sand filter experiments in Dijon, France, while designing the municipal water supply system. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"The Sand-Filter Coffee Analogy"** ☕ Imagine pouring water through a column of coffee grounds (analogous to soil grains). The water seeps through the tiny gaps (voids) between the solid particles. - **Imagine the ground as a crowded market:** Water (people) must zigzag through the gaps between tightly packed soil particles (shoppers). They cannot move in a straight line — they must wind through voids. - **Bigger gaps (gravel)** = water moves fast → **High permeability** $k$ - **Smaller gaps (clay)** = water moves very slow → **Low permeability** $k$ - **Steeper slope (more head loss)** = faster flow → **Higher hydraulic gradient** $i$ So Darcy's Law simply says: *Water flow through soil is proportional to how steeply water is "pushing" through it and how easily the soil lets water pass.* --- ## 1.3 Why Darcy's Law Matters in Geotechnical Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - **Earthen Dam Design:** Predicting seepage loss and pore pressure > - **Foundation Engineering:** Estimating uplift pressure on basement slabs > - **Slope Stability:** Analyzing seepage forces triggering failures > - **Consolidation Settlement:** Time-rate of water expulsion from clay > - **Groundwater Hydrology:** Well yield, contaminant transport modeling > - ** Dewatering Systems:** Construction site dewatering design The **coefficient of permeability $k$** is a *key soil property* — without it, no drainage, seepage, or consolidation problem can be solved. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Physical Assumptions of Darcy's Law Darcy's Law is fundamentally a **macroscopic empirical law** valid under specific physical conditions. Before applying it, the engineer must verify these assumptions: 1. **Steady-State Flow:** The discharge and hydraulic gradient do not change with time at any given point in the flow domain. 2. **Laminar Flow Conditions:** Flow streamlines remain parallel; no turbulence. Valid for soils in most engineering situations. 3. **Saturated Soil Mass:** All voids are filled with a single fluid (typically water). Unsaturated flow requires modified formulations (Richards' Equation). 4. **Incompressible Fluid:** Water density remains constant (true for most geotechnical problems except very deep confined aquifers). 5. **Homogeneous & Isotropic Medium:** The permeability $k$ is the same at every point and in every direction (rarely true in nature — but acceptable as a first approximation). 6. **No Soil-Fluid Interaction:** Chemical reactions, swelling, or dispersion between fluid and soil particles are negligible. 7. **Darcy-Valid Flow Regime:** Reynolds number below a critical value (typically **1 to 10** for soils). --- ## 2.2 Hydraulic Gradient — The Driving Force The **hydraulic head** $h$ at any point in a saturated soil mass is the total energy per unit weight of water, measured as an elevation of water column: $$h = h_e + h_p = z + \frac{u}{\gamma_w}$$ Where: - $h_e$ = Elevation head (m) - $h_p$ = Pressure head (m) - $z$ = Elevation of the point above a chosen datum (m) - $u$ = Pore water pressure (kN/m²) - $\gamma_w$ = Unit weight of water (**9.81 kN/m³**) The **hydraulic gradient** is the head loss per unit distance along the flow path: $$i = \frac{dh}{dL} = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{L}$$ - Always a **dimensionless** quantity. - $h_1$ = Head at inlet (upstream) - $h_2$ = Head at outlet (downstream) - $L$ = Length of flow path between the two points (m) > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** Hydraulic gradient represents the *driving force* per unit weight of water that pushes the fluid through the soil pores. --- ## 2.3 Coefficient of Permeability ($k$) — The Soil's Hydraulic Personality The coefficient of permeability (also called **hydraulic conductivity**) is defined as the **discharge velocity per unit hydraulic gradient**: $$k = \frac{v}{i}$$ **Units:** Length/Time — typically **m/s**, **cm/s**, or **mm/s**. **Typical values for natural soils:** | Soil Type | $k$ (cm/s) | Drainage Character | |-----------|-----------|---------------------| | **Clean gravel** | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{0}$ | Very good | | **Coarse sand** | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | Good | | **Fine sand** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ | Medium | | **Silt** | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$ | Poor | | **Clay** | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-6}$ | Practically impermeable | | **Intact rock** | $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-8}$ | Impermeable | > [!WARNING] > The range of $k$ values spans **10 orders of magnitude** — permeability is the most variable of all geotechnical properties! ### 2.3.1 Intrinsic Permeability vs Hydraulic Conductivity The **intrinsic permeability** $K$ (or $k_i$) is a property of the porous medium *alone*, independent of the fluid: $$k = \frac{K \cdot \gamma_w}{\mu} = \frac{K \cdot g}{\nu}$$ Where: - $K$ = Intrinsic permeability (units: **m²** or **Darcy**, where 1 Darcy ≈ $9.87 \times 10^{-9}$ cm²) - $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of fluid (Pa·s or N·s/m²) - $\nu$ = Kinematic viscosity of fluid (m²/s) - $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**) > [!IMPORTANT] > The hydraulic conductivity $k$ depends on BOTH the soil AND the fluid properties. The intrinsic permeability $K$ depends on the soil geometry alone. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Description / Application | |---|---------|---------------------------| | 1 | $Q = k \cdot i \cdot A$ | Darcy's Law (discharge form) | | 2 | $v = k \cdot i$ | Darcy's Law (velocity form) | | 3 | $k = v/i$ | Definition of permeability | | 4 | $i = (h_1 - h_2)/L$ | Hydraulic gradient | | 5 | $h = z + u/\gamma_w$ | Total hydraulic head | | 6 | $v_s = v/n_e$ | Seepage velocity (actual velocity in voids) | | 7 | $k = K \cdot \gamma_w / \mu$ | Relation between hydraulic & intrinsic permeability | | 8 | $K = k \cdot \mu / \gamma_w$ | Intrinsic permeability form | | 9 | $R_e = \rho \cdot v \cdot d_{10} / \mu$ | Reynolds number for soils | | 10 | $T = k \cdot B / (H^2 \cdot S_s)$ | Time factor for 2D consolidation (context) | | 11 | $n_e = n \cdot S$ | Effective porosity (when $S < 1$) | | 12 | $i_{cr} = (G_s - 1)/(1 + e)$ | Critical hydraulic gradient (quick sand) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction:** > - $v$ = **Discharge velocity** (fictitious) — based on total area $A$ > - $v_s$ = **Seepage velocity** (actual) — based on void area $A_v = n_e \cdot A$ > - Always: $v_s \geq v$ because $n_e \leq 1$ --- ## 2.5 Validity of Darcy's Law — Reynolds Number Criterion Darcy's Law is valid only for **laminar flow**. At higher velocities, flow becomes turbulent and the linear relationship between $v$ and $i$ breaks down. The **Reynolds Number** for flow in soils is defined as: $$R_e = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d_{10}}{\mu} = \frac{v \cdot d_{10}}{\nu}$$ Where $d_{10}$ is the **effective grain size** (10% finer size). **Validity Criteria (varies by researcher):** | Researcher | Critical $R_e$ | |------------|-----------------| | Reynolds (1883) | $R_e \leq 1$ to 4 | | Fancher, Lewis & Barnhart (1933) | $R_e \leq 1$ to 6 | | Bachmat (1965) | $R_e \leq 1$ | | Krumbein & Monk (1943) | $R_e \leq 5$ to 10 | | Plain practical limit | $R_e \leq 1$ to 10 | > [!NOTE] > **In essence:** For all practical geotechnical problems involving natural soils under normal hydraulic gradients, flow is laminar and **Darcy's Law is valid**. Violations occur only in coarse gravels under very high gradients or in geophysical flows. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Darcy's Experiment to the Empirical Law Henry Darcy's 1856 sand filter experiment is the foundation of the law. Consider a vertical soil column of cross-sectional area $A$ and length $L$: **Experimental Setup:** - Water enters at top, exits at bottom. - Two piezometers (taps) measure hydraulic head at distances $L_1$ and $L_2$ apart. - Head difference between taps = $h_1 - h_2 = \Delta h$. - Collected discharge over time $t$ = volume $V$. **Experimental Observations (multiple trials):** | Trial | Head Loss $\Delta h$ (cm) | Time $t$ (s) | Volume $V$ (cm³) | |-------|---------------------------|---------------|------------------| | 1 | 20 | 100 | 1500 | | 2 | 40 | 100 | 3000 | | 3 | 60 | 100 | 4500 | **Step 1:** Compute discharge $Q$ for each trial: $$Q = \frac{V}{t}$$ **Step 2:** Compute hydraulic gradient: $$i = \frac{\Delta h}{L}$$ **Step 3:** Plot $Q$ vs $i \cdot A$ (which equals $v \cdot A$ if normalized). The plot yields a **straight line** through the origin. $$\boxed{\;Q \propto i \cdot A\;}$$ **Step 4:** Introduce the proportionality constant (coefficient of permeability): $$Q = k \cdot i \cdot A$$ **Dividing both sides by area $A$:** $$v = \frac{Q}{A} = k \cdot i$$ This is the **point form** of Darcy's Law for one-dimensional flow. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Discharge Velocity and Seepage Velocity The **discharge velocity** $v$ is a fictitious velocity computed using the *total* cross-section $A$ of the soil, assuming voids are filled with flowing water. The **seepage velocity** $v_s$ (also called *actual* or *interstitial* velocity) is the *true* velocity of water inside the pores, based on the *void area* only. **Step 1:** Consider a soil prism of total length $L$ and cross-section $A$ (see diagram in Section 4). **Step 2:** Total volume of prism: $$V_{total} = A \cdot L$$ **Step 3:** Volume of voids: $$V_v = n \cdot V_{total} = n \cdot A \cdot L$$ Where $n$ is the porosity (assuming full saturation, effective porosity $n_e = n$). **Step 4:** Time for a slug of water to traverse the prism at discharge velocity $v$: $$t = \frac{L}{v}$$ **Step 5:** Same time at seepage velocity $v_s$ moving only through the void space: $$t = \frac{V_v}{Q} = \frac{n \cdot A \cdot L}{Q}$$ **Step 6:** Equate the two expressions for time: $$\frac{L}{v} = \frac{n \cdot A \cdot L}{Q}$$ **Step 7:** Solve for $v_s$ — note that $Q/A = v$: $$v_s = \frac{Q}{n \cdot A} = \frac{v}{n}$$ **For partially saturated soil** ($S < 1$), effective porosity is $n_e = n \cdot S$: $$\boxed{\;v_s = \frac{v}{n_e} = \frac{v}{n \cdot S}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Since $n_e < 1$, the seepage velocity is **always greater** than the discharge velocity. This explains why contaminants or fine particles may travel through soil faster than the apparent bulk flow rate suggests. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Derivation of Hydraulic Conductivity from Capillary Tube Model (Kozeny-Carman Basis) While a rigorous Kozeny-Carman derivation is extensive, the conceptual basis is as follows: **Step 1:** Model soil pores as a bundle of parallel capillary tubes of equivalent radius $r$. **Step 2:** Apply **Hagen-Poiseuille Law** for laminar flow in a single capillary: $$Q_{tube} = \frac{\pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \Delta h \cdot \gamma_w}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L}$$ **Step 3:** For $N$ such tubes per unit cross-sectional area $A$: $$Q_{total} = N \cdot A \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \Delta h \cdot \gamma_w}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute into Darcy's Law form $Q = k \cdot i \cdot A$ and solve for $k$: $$k = \frac{N \cdot \pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \gamma_w}{8 \cdot \mu}$$ **Step 5:** Express in terms of porosity $n$ and specific surface area $S_s$: $$\boxed{\;k = \frac{n^3}{k_0 \cdot S_s^2 \cdot (1 - n)^2} \cdot \frac{\gamma_w}{\mu}\;}$$ Where $k_0$ is a shape factor (≈ 2.5 for circular capillaries) and $S_s$ is specific surface area per unit volume of solids. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Implication:** Permeability depends on $n^3$ — hence permeability is *highly sensitive* to porosity changes (e.g., compaction reduces $k$ dramatically). --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Critical Hydraulic Gradient for Quicksand Condition Consider a soil mass under an upward seepage flow. The upward seepage force per unit volume is: $$j = i \cdot \gamma_w$$ The effective (buoyant) weight per unit volume of soil is: $$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = \frac{(G_s - 1) \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$$ At the **critical condition** (quicksand / boiling / piping), seepage force equals effective weight: $$i_{cr} \cdot \gamma_w = \frac{(G_s - 1) \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$$ Solving for $i_{cr}$: $$\boxed{\;i_{cr} = \frac{G_s - 1}{1 + e}\;}$$ **Typical Value:** For $G_s = 2.65$ and $e = 0.65$, $i_{cr} \approx 1.0$. --- ## 3.5 Sample Numerical Problem (Worked Out) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A constant head permeability test is conducted on a sandy soil sample. The sample length is **20 cm**, cross-sectional area is **50 cm²**. The head difference maintained is **60 cm** of water. In **5 minutes**, a volume of **600 cm³** of water is collected. Determine: (a) the coefficient of permeability $k$, and (b) the discharge velocity $v$. **Solution:** **Part (a): Coefficient of Permeability** Given: - $L = 20$ cm, $A = 50$ cm², $h = 60$ cm, $t = 5$ min $= 300$ s, $V = 600$ cm³ **Step 1:** Compute discharge $Q$: $$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{600 \text{ cm}^3}{300 \text{ s}} = 2.0 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 2:** Compute hydraulic gradient $i$: $$i = \frac{h}{L} = \frac{60 \text{ cm}}{20 \text{ cm}} = 3.0$$ **Step 3:** Apply Darcy's Law: $$Q = k \cdot i \cdot A \;\Rightarrow\; k = \frac{Q}{i \cdot A}$$ $$k = \frac{2.0}{3.0 \times 50} = \frac{2.0}{150}$$ $$\boxed{\;k = 0.01333 \text{ cm/s} \approx 1.33 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm/s}\;}$$ **Part (b): Discharge Velocity** $$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{2.0}{50} = 0.04 \text{ cm/s}$$ **Verification using $v = k \cdot i$:** $$v = 0.01333 \times 3.0 = 0.04 \text{ cm/s} \;\checkmark$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Darcy's Original Experimental Apparatus (Flow Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Constant Head Tank
Reservoir with Overflow] -->|Maintains Constant Head h1| B[Inlet Pipe] B --> C[Soil Specimen
Length L, Area A
Inside Permeameter] C -->|Outlet at Lower Head h2| D[Outlet Collection Chamber] D --> E[Measuring Cylinder
Volume V collected in time t] F[Piezometer Tap 1
at Point 1] -.->|Reads h1| A G[Piezometer Tap 2
at Point 2] -.->|Reads h2| C H[Computed Values] --> I[Discharge Q = V/t] H --> J[Hydraulic Gradient i = h1 - h2 / L] H --> K[Permeability k = Q / iA] E --> H F --> H G --> H ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Water from a constant-head tank flows downward through a soil sample in a permeameter. Two piezometers (vertical tubes) measure the hydraulic head at two points along the soil. Discharge is measured at the outlet. --- ## 4.2 Darcy's Law Conceptual Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hydraulic Gradient
i = driving force] --> M[Darcy Multiplication
k x i x A] B[Permeability
k = soil property] --> M C[Cross Section
A = flow area] --> M M --> D[Discharge Q
Volume per unit time] style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style C fill:#e8f5e9 style D fill:#fce4ec style M fill:#f3e5f5 ``` --- ## 4.3 Discharge Velocity vs Seepage Velocity — Conceptual Block ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TotalArea[Total Cross-Section A] V1[Void Area nA
where water actually flows] S1[Solid Area 1-n x A
impermeable grains] end Q[Discharge Q m3/s] --> Vcalc[Discharge Velocity v = Q/A
FICTITIOUS, based on full area] Q --> Vscalc[Seepage Velocity vs = Q/nA
ACTUAL, based on void area only] Vcalc -.->|Always vs greater than v| Vscalc style Vcalc fill:#ffe0b2 style Vscalc fill:#c8e6c9 ``` --- ## 4.4 Validity of Darcy's Law — Flow Regime Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Start: Re computed] --> B{Re less than 1 to 10} B -->|YES| C[Laminar Flow Regime
Darcy Law Valid
Linear v vs i relationship] B -->|NO| D[Non-Darcy Flow Regime
Forchheimer Equation Applies
Quadratic v vs i relationship] C --> E[Most geotechnical
engineering problems] D --> F[Coarse gravel under
high gradients, rock fractures] style C fill:#c8e6c9 style D fill:#ffcdd2 style E fill:#a5d6a7 style F fill:#ef9a9a ``` --- ## 4.5 Permeability Magnitude Spectrum (Engineering Reference) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Soil Classification by Permeability] --> B[Very High k greater than 1e-1 cm/s] A --> C[High k 1e-3 to 1e-1 cm/s] A --> D[Medium k 1e-5 to 1e-3 cm/s] A --> E[Low k 1e-7 to 1e-5 cm/s] A --> F[Very Low k less than 1e-7 cm/s] B --> B1[Clean gravel, rock fill] C --> C1[Coarse to medium sand] D --> D1[Fine sand, silty sand] E --> E1[Silt, clayey silt] F --> F1[Clay, intact rock] style B fill:#4caf50 style C fill:#8bc34a style D fill:#ffeb3b style E fill:#ff9800 style F fill:#f44336 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) --- ### Question 1: State and explain Darcy's Law. Mention the conditions for its validity. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Set A]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement:** Darcy's Law states that for laminar flow through a saturated porous medium, the discharge $Q$ is directly proportional to the hydraulic gradient $i$ and the cross-sectional area $A$ perpendicular to flow: $$Q = k \cdot i \cdot A$$ Where $k$ is the coefficient of permeability of the medium. **Conditions for Validity (1 mark):** 1. Soil must be **saturated** with a single homogeneous fluid. 2. Flow must be **laminar** (Reynolds number < 1 to 10). 3. Flow must be **steady-state** and the soil **homogeneous and isotropic**. 4. The fluid must be **incompressible**, and there should be no soil-fluid chemical interaction. --- ### Question 2: Distinguish between discharge velocity and seepage velocity. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Discharge Velocity $v$ | Seepage Velocity $v_s$ | |---------|------------------------|------------------------| | Definition | Volume of flow per unit total cross-section per unit time | Volume of flow per unit void area per unit time | | Computed using | Total area $A$ | Void area $A_v = n \cdot A$ | | Nature | Fictitious/average | Actual velocity in pores | | Magnitude | Smaller | Larger ($\geq v$) | | Formula | $v = k \cdot i$ | $v_s = v/n$ | **Relation:** $v_s = v / n_e$ where $n_e$ is effective porosity. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks): Full-Length Solved Problem **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]** **Statement:** A soil sample of length **25 cm** and cross-sectional area **60 cm²** is tested in a constant-head permeameter. Under a constant head difference of **75 cm** of water, a volume of **900 cm³** is collected in **6 minutes**. The specific gravity of soil solids is **2.68**, and the void ratio is **0.72**. Determine: **(a)** The coefficient of permeability $k$ of the soil. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The discharge velocity, seepage velocity, and verify whether quicksand (boiling) condition is approached. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks **Step 1:** Compute the discharge $Q$. $$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{900 \text{ cm}^3}{6 \times 60 \text{ s}} = \frac{900}{360} = 2.5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the hydraulic gradient $i$. $$i = \frac{h}{L} = \frac{75 \text{ cm}}{25 \text{ cm}} = 3.0$$ **Step 3:** Apply Darcy's Law $Q = k \cdot i \cdot A$ and solve for $k$: $$k = \frac{Q}{i \cdot A} = \frac{2.5}{3.0 \times 60} = \frac{2.5}{180}$$ $$\boxed{\;k = 0.01389 \text{ cm/s} \approx 1.39 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm/s}\;}$$ **Classification:** This $k$ value falls in the range of **fine sand** to **coarse silt** — permeable but not highly so. ✓ (1 mark) --- #### Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks **Step 1:** Compute discharge velocity $v$. $$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{2.5}{60} = 0.04167 \text{ cm/s} \approx 4.17 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm/s}$$ **Step 2:** Compute porosity $n$ from void ratio $e$: $$n = \frac{e}{1 + e} = \frac{0.72}{1.72} = 0.4186$$ **Step 3:** Compute seepage velocity $v_s$ (assuming saturation, $S = 1$): $$v_s = \frac{v}{n} = \frac{0.04167}{0.4186}$$ $$\boxed{\;v_s = 0.0995 \text{ cm/s} \approx 0.1 \text{ cm/s}\;}$$ **Step 4:** Check for quicksand condition. Compute critical hydraulic gradient: $$i_{cr} = \frac{G_s - 1}{1 + e} = \frac{2.68 - 1}{1 + 72/100} = \frac{1.68}{1.72}$$ $$\boxed{\;i_{cr} = 0.977\;}$$ **Step 5:** Compare with actual gradient $i = 3.0$: Since $i = 3.0 \gg i_{cr} = 0.977$, the actual gradient is **more than 3 times** the critical value. **Conclusion:** The quicksand (boiling) condition is **definitely exceeded**. In a field situation, this would cause **piping failure** and the soil would behave as a heavy fluid (quicksand). Safety factor against boiling is **less than 1** → **UNSAFE** for upward seepage scenarios. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]** **Statement:** A horizontal stratified soil deposit consists of three layers arranged as follows: | Layer | Thickness (m) | $k$ (cm/s) | |-------|---------------|-----------| | Top | 1.5 | $1 \times 10^{-3}$ | | Middle | 2.0 | $3 \times 10^{-2}$ | | Bottom | 1.0 | $5 \times 10^{-4}$ | A horizontal flow of water occurs under a hydraulic gradient of **0.4**. The total width of flow is **10 m**. **(a)** Determine the **equivalent average horizontal permeability** $k_H$ of the stratified deposit. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the same deposit experiences **vertical downward seepage** under the same head difference of **0.4** over the total thickness **4.5 m**, compute the **equivalent vertical permeability** $k_V$, and the total discharge per unit width. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Part (a) — Horizontal Flow: 7 marks For horizontal flow through stratified layers, the equivalent permeability is the **thickness-weighted arithmetic mean**: $$k_H = \frac{\sum H_i \cdot k_i}{\sum H_i}$$ **Step 1:** Compute numerator $\sum H_i \cdot k_i$ (convert $k$ to m/s for consistency, but ratio works in same units): $$\sum H_i \cdot k_i = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} + 2.0 \times 3 \times 10^{-2} + 1.0 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$= 0.0015 + 0.0600 + 0.0005 = 0.0620 \text{ m/s} \cdot \text{m}$$ **Step 2:** Compute total thickness: $$\sum H_i = 1.5 + 2.0 + 1.0 = 4.5 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3:** Compute $k_H$: $$k_H = \frac{0.0620}{4.5}$$ $$\boxed{\;k_H = 0.01378 \text{ cm/s} \approx 1.38 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm/s}\;}$$ **Note:** For horizontal flow, the **most permeable layer dominates** the equivalent permeability because it carries most of the discharge. --- #### Solution to Part (b) — Vertical Flow: 7 marks For vertical flow through stratified layers, the equivalent permeability is the **thickness-weighted harmonic mean**: $$k_V = \frac{\sum H_i}{\sum \frac{H_i}{k_i}}$$ **Step 1:** Compute $\sum H_i / k_i$: $$\frac{H_1}{k_1} = \frac{1.5}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 1500 \text{ s}$$ $$\frac{H_2}{k_2} = \frac{2.0}{3 \times 10^{-2}} = 66.67 \text{ s}$$ $$\frac{H_3}{k_3} = \frac{1.0}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2000 \text{ s}$$ $$\sum \frac{H_i}{k_i} = 1500 + 66.67 + 2000 = 3566.67 \text{ s}$$ **Step 2:** Compute $k_V$: $$k_V = \frac{4.5}{3566.67} = 1.262 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm/s}$$ **Convert to m/s:** $k_V = 1.262 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s $$\boxed{\;k_V \approx 1.26 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm/s}\;}$$ **Step 3:** Compute total discharge per unit width. Hydraulic gradient for vertical flow: $$i_V = \frac{h}{\sum H_i} = \frac{0.4}{4.5} = 0.0889$$ Discharge per unit width: $$q = k_V \cdot i_V \cdot (\text{unit width} = 1)$$ $$q = 1.262 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s} \times 0.0889$$ $$\boxed{\;q \approx 1.12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s/m}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Insight:** $k_V < k_H$ *always* for stratified soils. The least permeable layer **controls** the vertical flow — the bottom layer ($k_3 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ cm/s) effectively chokes the system. In dam design, this is why clay cores are used as seepage barriers. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency (2-mark penalty common):** Mixing cm and m in $Q$, $A$, $L$, $h$ without conversion. **Always convert to SI first** OR keep everything in cm. > > 2. **Confusing $i = h/L$ with $i = h$ directly:** Hydraulic gradient is dimensionless, NOT equal to head itself. Many students write $k = Q / (h \cdot A)$ which is **wrong** unless $L = 1$. > > 3. **Forgetting that $A$ in Darcy's Law is the TOTAL cross-section, not void area.** Discharge velocity uses total area; seepage velocity uses void area. > > 4. **In stratified flow problems, students mix up horizontal and vertical formulas.** Remember: $k_H$ is **weighted average (arithmetic)**; $k_V$ is **weighted harmonic mean**. Easy to swap. > > 5. **Critical hydraulic gradient $i_{cr}$ is independent of permeability** — it depends only on $G_s$ and $e$. Students sometimes incorrectly use $k$ values in $i_{cr}$ calculation. > > 6. **Sign convention for flow direction:** Seepage force acts in the direction of flow. If flow is upward, it reduces effective stress; if downward, it increases effective stress. > > 7. **Validity of Darcy's Law check:** Always confirm $R_e < 1$ to 10 before applying. For most textbook problems it is valid by default. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day** - ✅ **Darcy's Law:** $Q = k \cdot i \cdot A$ or $v = k \cdot i$ — fundamental governing equation of seepage. - ✅ **Coefficient of Permeability $k$:** units = m/s or cm/s; range across soils = $10^{-9}$ to $10^{0}$ cm/s. - ✅ **Hydraulic Gradient $i$:** dimensionless ratio = head loss per unit length of flow. - ✅ **Discharge velocity $v$** is fictitious; **Seepage velocity $v_s = v/n$** is actual in-void velocity. - ✅ **Intrinsic permeability $K$** depends only on soil; **hydraulic conductivity $k$** depends on both soil and fluid. - ✅ **Validity:** Laminar flow → $R_e < 1$ to 10 (use $d_{10}$ as characteristic length). - ✅ **Critical hydraulic gradient** $i_{cr} = (G_s - 1)/(1 + e)$ → governs quicksand / piping failure. - ✅ **Stratified soils:** - Horizontal flow: $k_H = \sum H_i k_i / \sum H_i$ (arithmetic mean — most permeable dominates) - Vertical flow: $k_V = \sum H_i / \sum (H_i/k_i)$ (harmonic mean — least permeable controls) - ✅ **Always verify** $k_V \leq k_H$ for stratified deposits. - ✅ **Effective porosity** $n_e = n \cdot S$ — use in seepage velocity when $S < 1$. - ✅ **Darcy's Law applications:** Seepage, consolidation, earth-dam design, dewatering, well hydraulics, slope stability. - ✅ **Typical $k$ values to memorize:** Gravel ≈ $10^{-1}$ cm/s, Sand ≈ $10^{-3}$ cm/s, Silt ≈ $10^{-5}$ cm/s, Clay ≈ $10^{-7}$ cm/s. - ✅ **Unit weight of water $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³** (use 9.81 in SI; 1 g/cm³ in CGS). - ✅ **Approach to any KTU problem:** (1) List given data, (2) Identify unknowns, (3) State the relevant Darcy equation, (4) Substitute with units, (5) Compute & verify with physical intuition. > **End of Module 2 — Darcy's Law Notes | PCAGT402 | KTU 2024 Scheme** ✅

topics.title: Factors Affecting Permeability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Permeability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Permeability**
> *Permeability* is defined as the property of a porous soil mass which permits the flow of water (or any other fluid) through its interconnected void spaces. Quantitatively, it is measured by the **Coefficient of Permeability (k)**, which is the discharge velocity of water under a unit hydraulic gradient, in accordance with **Darcy's Law** (1856):

$$v \;=\; k \cdot i$$

where:
- $v$ = discharge (or superficial) velocity (cm/s or m/s)
- $k$ = coefficient of permeability (cm/s or m/s)
- $i$ = hydraulic gradient = $h/L$ (dimensionless)

In SI units, the absolute permeability $K$ (m²) is related to $k$ by:

$$k \;=\; K \cdot \frac{\gamma_w}{\mu} \;=\; K \cdot \frac{\rho_w \cdot g}{\mu}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:**
> The question *"Discuss the factors affecting the permeability of soils"* is a **frequently repeated 14-mark ESE question** and is central to Module 2. Students must link every factor to the underlying physical mechanism — not just list them.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine a **sponge** lying on a wet kitchen counter. Water seeps through it — but how *fast*?

- If the sponge has **large, open holes** (like a coarse gravel sponge), water gushes through. *Permeability is high.*
- If the sponge has **tiny, tightly packed pores** (like a fine-clay sponge), water barely trickles. *Permeability is very low.*
- Now **squeeze** the sponge — the holes shrink, the path becomes longer, and flow slows. *Void ratio decreases → permeability drops.*
- **Heat** the sponge — water becomes thinner (less viscous), and it flows faster. *Temperature rises → permeability of water increases.*
- Leave a **bubble of air** trapped inside — that air clogs a passage. *Degree of saturation falls → permeability decreases.*

Soil behaves exactly like this sponge. The "holes" are the interconnected voids between solid grains, and *every* physical or chemical property that changes those voids changes the permeability.

---

## 1.3 The Governing Physical Quantities

The four "knobs" that control permeability in soils are:

1. **Porosity of the void network** → captured by void ratio $e$
2. **Size and shape of the flow channels** → captured by specific surface $S$
3. **Fluid property** → captured by viscosity $\mu$ and unit weight $\gamma_w$
4. **Path complexity** → captured by shape factor, tortuosity $T$, and stratification

> [!TIP]
> Always remember — *permeability is not a single material constant*. It is a **system property** of the soil + fluid combination. Changing the fluid changes $k$ even for the same soil skeleton.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Master Equation: Kozeny–Carman Equation

The single most powerful analytical tool for linking permeability to soil structure is the **Kozeny–Carman equation** (modified form widely used in KTU texts):

$$k \;=\; \frac{1}{k_0 \cdot S^2} \cdot \frac{\gamma_w}{\mu} \cdot \frac{e^3}{1+e}$$

where:
- $k$ = coefficient of permeability (cm/s)
- $k_0$ = **Kozeny's constant** (≈ 5 for most soils, may vary from 4 to 6)
- $S$ = specific surface area per unit volume of particles (cm²/cm³)
- $\gamma_w$ = unit weight of water (≈ 9.81 kN/m³)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of water (N·s/m²)
- $e$ = void ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single equation captures the influence of **void ratio, particle size, shape, and water properties** in one analytical framework. It is the cornerstone of every derivation in this module.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Enumeration of Factors

### 2.2.1 Factor 1 — Particle Size (Grain Size Diameter, $D$)

For clean, uniform sands, the **Allen–Hazen empirical relation** is widely used:

$$k \;\text{(cm/s)} \;\approx\; C \cdot D_{10}^{\,2}$$

where:
- $D_{10}$ = effective grain size (mm) — the sieve size through which **10%** of the soil passes
- $C$ = Hazen's constant, empirically between **0.4 and 1.2** (often taken as **1.0**)

**Why?** By dimensional reasoning from Poiseuille flow through capillary tubes, the hydraulic radius is proportional to grain diameter, and $k \propto D^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Implication:** If $D_{10}$ doubles, permeability increases **fourfold**. Permeability is extraordinarily sensitive to grain size.

---

### 2.2.2 Factor 2 — Specific Surface Area ($S$)

From the Kozeny–Carman equation:

$$k \;\propto\; \frac{1}{S^2}$$

- **Coarse sand**: $S$ small → $k$ large (e.g., $k \sim 10^{-1}$ cm/s)
- **Silt**: $S$ moderate → $k$ moderate (e.g., $k \sim 10^{-5}$ cm/s)
- **Clay**: $S$ enormous (because particles are plate-like) → $k$ extremely small (e.g., $k \sim 10^{-9}$ cm/s)

For a sphere of diameter $D$, specific surface per unit volume:

$$S \;=\; \frac{6}{D}$$

so $k \propto D^2$ — consistent with Hazen.

---

### 2.2.3 Factor 3 — Shape of Particles

Shape is captured by a **shape factor** $f_s$ inside $k_0$ (Kozeny–Carman):

$$k \;\propto\; \frac{1}{f_s}$$

| Particle Shape | Shape Factor $f_s$ | Relative $k$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Spherical (rounded)** | lowest | **highest** |
| **Angular / flaky** | highest | **lowest** |
| Sub-angular | intermediate | intermediate |

Round grains pack with larger, more continuous voids. Angular and platy grains interlock and create tortuous, narrow paths.

---

### 2.2.4 Factor 4 — Void Ratio ($e$)

From Kozeny–Carman:

$$k \;\propto\; \frac{e^3}{1+e}$$

- Doubling $e$ does **not** simply double $k$ — there is a strong **cubic** dependence.
- Loose, high-$e$ soils (e.g., loose sand with $e = 0.9$) conduct water far more efficiently than dense sand with $e = 0.5$.

**Sample numerical check** (at constant $S$):

$$\frac{k_1}{k_2} \;=\; \frac{e_1^{\,3}\,(1+e_2)}{e_2^{\,3}\,(1+e_1)}$$

---

### 2.2.5 Factor 5 — Degree of Saturation ($S_r$) and Entrapped Air

Air bubbles block flow channels, reducing the effective cross-section. An empirical relation:

$$k_2 \;=\; k_1 \cdot \frac{S_{r,2}^{\,n}}{S_{r,1}^{\,n}}$$

where $n \approx 3$ to 4 in many soils.

> [!WARNING]
> A soil **fully dry** has $k \to 0$. A soil **fully saturated** has maximum $k$ for that void ratio. This is why laboratory permeability tests are always run on saturated samples.

---

### 2.2.6 Factor 6 — Viscosity of Water ($\mu$)

$$k \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\mu} \quad\Rightarrow\quad k \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\text{viscosity}}$$

| Temperature (°C) | Viscosity $\mu$ (N·s/m² × 10⁻³) | Relative $k$ |
|---|---|---|
| **0** | 1.792 | 0.50 |
| **20** | 1.002 | 1.00 (reference) |
| **40** | 0.653 | 1.66 |
| **60** | 0.467 | 2.22 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU favourite:** *"A permeability test was done at 20 °C. Predict $k$ at 30 °C."* The conversion formula is:

$$k_{T_2} \;=\; k_{T_1} \cdot \frac{\mu_{T_1}}{\mu_{T_2}}$$

---

### 2.2.7 Factor 7 — Structure of Soil Mass / Stratification

In natural deposits, soil is **layered**. The equivalent horizontal permeability of a stratified deposit is:

$$k_H \;=\; \frac{1}{H} \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_{H,i} \cdot H_i$$

The equivalent vertical permeability:

$$k_V \;=\; \frac{H}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i / k_{V,i}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important asymmetry:** $k_H \;\geq\; k_V$ in almost every natural deposit. Percolation tests measure $k_V$; pumping tests measure $k_H$. This is a classic viva question.

---

### 2.2.8 Factor 8 — Adsorbed Water / Clay Mineralogy

In clays, water molecules are tightly held to particle surfaces by electrochemical forces. This **adsorbed (bound) water** has much higher effective viscosity, dramatically reducing the actual flow. This is why $k$ of a clay is not just "small" — it can be **negligibly small** even with measurable void ratio.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units of $k$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = k i$ | Darcy's Law | cm/s |
| 2 | $k = K \cdot (\gamma_w / \mu)$ | Hydraulic vs absolute | cm/s |
| 3 | $k = C \cdot D_{10}^{\,2}$ | Hazen (sands) | cm/s |
| 4 | $k = \dfrac{1}{k_0 S^2} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma_w}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{e^3}{1+e}$ | Kozeny–Carman | cm/s |
| 5 | $k \propto 1/S^2$ | Specific surface effect | — |
| 6 | $k \propto e^3 / (1+e)$ | Void ratio effect | — |
| 7 | $k \propto 1/\mu$ | Viscosity effect | — |
| 8 | $k_{T_2} = k_{T_1} \cdot \mu_{T_1} / \mu_{T_2}$ | Temperature correction | cm/s |
| 9 | $k_H = \frac{1}{H} \sum k_{H,i} H_i$ | Stratified horiz. | cm/s |
| 10 | $k_V = H \big/ \sum (H_i / k_{V,i})$ | Stratified vert. | cm/s |
| 11 | $k_2 = k_1 \cdot (S_{r,2}/S_{r,1})^n$ | Saturation effect | cm/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In any KTU numerical problem involving permeability, always identify which factors change between the two states and apply the **ratio form** of the Kozeny–Carman equation — it cancels most constants elegantly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: From Poiseuille to Kozeny–Carman

We model soil pores as a bundle of **capillary tubes** of varying radii. For a single cylindrical tube of radius $r$ and length $L_T$ (the actual tortuous length, longer than the macroscopic length $L$):

**Step 1 — Hagen–Poiseuille flow in one tube** (laminar, Newtonian):

$$q_{\text{tube}} \;=\; \frac{\pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \Delta h \cdot \gamma_w}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L_T}$$

**Step 2 — Sum over all $N$ tubes per unit cross-section**. The void volume per unit cell is $n \cdot V_{\text{cell}}$ (where $n$ = porosity). The hydraulic radius of the channel network is:

$$R_h \;=\; \frac{\text{void area}}{\text{wetted perimeter}} \;\propto\; \frac{e}{S \cdot (1+e)}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce shape factor $k_0$ and tortuosity factor $T$**. Kozeny showed that for any cross-section, the flow constant lies in a narrow range; hence $k_0 \approx 5$ empirically:

$$k \;=\; \frac{1}{k_0 \cdot S^2} \cdot \frac{\gamma_w}{\mu} \cdot \frac{e^3}{1+e}$$

**Step 4 — Specific surface for uniform spheres** $S = 6/D$. Substituting:

$$k \;=\; \frac{D^2}{36 \cdot k_0} \cdot \frac{\gamma_w}{\mu} \cdot \frac{e^3}{1+e}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical calibration with clean sand data** yields $k_0 \cdot 36 \approx 5 \cdot 36 = 180$. Hence:

$$k \;\approx\; \frac{D^2 \cdot \gamma_w}{180 \cdot \mu} \cdot \frac{e^3}{1+e}$$

This is the working form taught in KTU lectures and the basis of the famous "K = D²/180" calibration.

> [!NOTE]
> The derivation shows **two competing effects of particle size**: $D$ enters as $D^2$ (size of pore) and as $1/S^2 = D^2/36$ (specific surface). The two cancel the cubic dependence and leave an effective $k \propto D^2$ — exactly what Hazen observed empirically.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of $k$ to Each Factor

Let us define a **sensitivity index** $\eta_X$ showing how $k$ changes when factor $X$ is varied by a small relative amount $\Delta X / X$:

$$\eta_X \;=\; \frac{\partial \ln k}{\partial \ln X}$$

| Factor $X$ | Sensitivity Index $\eta_X$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Grain size $D$ | **+2** | Doubling grain size → 4× permeability |
| Specific surface $S$ | **−2** | Halving surface → 4× permeability |
| Void ratio $e$ | $\dfrac{3+2e}{1+e}$ | ≈ 2.5 to 2.7 for typical $e$ |
| Viscosity $\mu$ | **−1** | Doubling viscosity → half $k$ |
| Temperature $T$ (via $\mu$) | **+1** (approx.) | Higher T → lower $\mu$ → higher $k$ |
| Saturation $S_r$ | **+3 to +4** | Very strong |
| Shape factor $f_s$ | **−1** | Linear in inverse |

> [!TIP]
> **Highest sensitivity:** Grain size, void ratio, and saturation. **Lowest sensitivity:** Temperature and unit weight. This ranking guides the priority of variables in lab testing.

---

## 3.3 Standard Test-Problem Type: Temperature Correction (Worked Model)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Sample):** A constant-head permeability test on a clean sand gave $k = 2.4 \times 10^{-3}$ cm/s at 20 °C. Estimate $k$ at 30 °C.

**Step 1 — Identify the factor that changes.** Only $\mu$ (viscosity) changes with temperature. All other factors ($D$, $e$, $S$, $S_r$, $\gamma_w$) are unchanged.

**Step 2 — Apply the ratio form of Kozeny–Carman.** All factors except $1/\mu$ cancel:

$$\frac{k_{30}}{k_{20}} \;=\; \frac{\mu_{20}}{\mu_{30}}$$

**Step 3 — Look up viscosity values** (standard water-viscosity table):

$$\mu_{20} \;=\; 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{N·s/m}^2$$

$$\mu_{30} \;=\; 0.798 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{N·s/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{k_{30}}{k_{20}} \;=\; \frac{1.002}{0.798} \;\approx\; 1.256$$

**Step 5 — Final value:**

$$k_{30} \;=\; 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.256 \;\approx\; 3.01 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{cm/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that the temperature correction is done by **the inverse ratio of viscosities**, NOT by direct temperature ratio. Students often write $k \propto T$ directly — that is a **valuation penalty**.

---

## 3.4 Standard Test-Problem Type: Void Ratio Correction

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Sample):** A sand sample has $k = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ cm/s at $e_1 = 0.65$. Estimate $k$ at $e_2 = 0.80$ (same sand, same temperature).

**Step 1 — Apply the void-ratio ratio form:**

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} \;=\; \frac{e_2^{\,3} \,(1+e_1)}{e_1^{\,3} \,(1+e_2)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} \;=\; \frac{(0.80)^3 \cdot (1+0.65)}{(0.65)^3 \cdot (1+0.80)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$0.80^3 \cdot 1.65 \;=\; 0.512 \cdot 1.65 \;=\; 0.8448$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$0.65^3 \cdot 1.80 \;=\; 0.274625 \cdot 1.80 \;=\; 0.494325$$

**Step 5 — Final ratio:**

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} \;=\; \frac{0.8448}{0.494325} \;\approx\; 1.709$$

**Step 6 — Final value:**

$$k_2 \;=\; 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.709 \;\approx\; 6.84 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{cm/s}$$

> [!TIP]
> The **cubic** dependence on $e$ is what makes loose soils so dramatically more permeable than dense ones. A modest 23% increase in $e$ (0.65 → 0.80) led to a **71%** increase in $k$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary — "Which Factor Affects What?"

| Factor | Direction of effect on $k$ | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Increase grain size $D$ | increases | $k \propto D^2$ |
| Increase specific surface $S$ | decreases | $k \propto 1/S^2$ |
| Increase void ratio $e$ | increases | $k \propto e^3/(1+e)$ |
| Increase viscosity $\mu$ | decreases | $k \propto 1/\mu$ |
| Increase temperature $T$ | increases (via $\mu$) | $k \propto 1/\mu(T)$ |
| Increase saturation $S_r$ | increases | $k \propto S_r^{\,n}$ |
| Angular particles | decreases | $k \propto 1/f_s$ |
| Stratified soil horiz. vs vert. | $k_H > k_V$ | up to 10× |
| Adsorbed water (clays) | drastically decreases | up to 1000× |
| Entrapped air/gas | decreases | up to 5× |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of "Factors Affecting Permeability"

The following Mermaid block diagrams the complete cause–effect map of every factor discussed above. It is rendered as a hierarchical block diagram for clarity (per the protocol's fallback rule for complex physical topics).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PERMEABILITY k OF SOIL] --> B[SOIL STRUCTURE FACTORS]
    A --> C[FLUID PROPERTIES]
    A --> D[STATE CONDITIONS]
    A --> E[MASS GEOMETRY]

    B --> B1[Grain size D]
    B --> B2[Specific surface S]
    B --> B3[Particle shape]
    B --> B4[Clay mineralogy and adsorbed water]

    C --> C1[Viscosity mu]
    C --> C2[Unit weight gamma w]
    C --> C3[Entrapped air and gas content]

    D --> D1[Void ratio e]
    D --> D2[Degree of saturation Sr]
    D --> D3[Temperature T]

    E --> E1[Stratification and layering]
    E --> E2[Fissures and root holes]
    E --> E3[Tortuosity of flow path]

    B1 --> F1[k proportional to D squared]
    B2 --> F2[k proportional to 1 over S squared]
    B3 --> F3[k proportional to 1 over shape factor]
    B4 --> F4[Drastic reduction in clays]

    C1 --> F5[k proportional to 1 over mu]
    C2 --> F6[k proportional to gamma w]
    C3 --> F7[Reduced effective area]

    D1 --> F8[k proportional to e cubed over 1 plus e]
    D2 --> F9[k proportional to Sr to the power n]
    D3 --> F10[Indirect via mu]

    E1 --> F11[kH greater than kV in stratified soil]
    E2 --> F12[Apparent k rises dramatically]
    E3 --> F13[Path length increases k falls]
```

---

## 4.2 Master Control Flow — "How to Solve a Permeability-Factor Numerical"

This block diagram is the **decision tree** a student should follow when solving any KTU 14-mark problem on this topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[START: Read problem carefully] --> S1[Identify which factors are mentioned]
    S1 --> S2[Convert all data to consistent units]
    S2 --> S3{Which factors change between state 1 and state 2?}

    S3 -->|Only temperature| P1[Use mu ratio formula kT2 over kT1]
    S3 -->|Only void ratio| P2[Use e cubed over 1+e ratio formula]
    S3 -->|Only saturation| P3[Use Sr to the power n ratio]
    S3 -->|Only grain size| P4[Use D squared ratio]
    S3 -->|Multiple factors| P5[Combine ratios multiplicatively]

    P1 --> Z[Compute k2 and quote with units]
    P2 --> Z
    P3 --> Z
    P4 --> Z
    P5 --> Z

    Z --> S4[Add a closing remark on engineering significance]
    S4 --> END[END OF SOLUTION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility of the Kozeny–Carman framework:**
> - **Filter design** for earth dams — the filter sand must be 4 to 5 times more permeable than the protected base soil.
> - **Pumping-test interpretation** for aquifer characterization.
> - **Consolidation predictions** — primary consolidation rate is governed directly by $k$.
> - **Contaminant transport** modelling — advection and dispersion both depend on the same $k$.
> - **Slurry-wall and landfill liner** design — extremely low $k$ clays are selected to impede leachate migration.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any **four** factors affecting the coefficient of permeability of a soil and state, in one line each, how each factor influences $k$.*

**Model Answer (verbatim points):**

1. **Grain size ($D$):** $k \propto D^2$. Coarser soils have higher permeability.
2. **Void ratio ($e$):** $k \propto e^3/(1+e)$. Higher $e$ gives higher $k$.
3. **Viscosity of pore fluid ($\mu$):** $k \propto 1/\mu$. Higher viscosity lowers $k$.
4. **Degree of saturation ($S_r$):** Higher $S_r$ means fewer air-blocked pores and higher $k$.

*(Any other valid factor such as particle shape, specific surface, structure/stratification, adsorbed water, entrapped air — accepted.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the **Kozeny–Carman equation** for permeability of soils and identify the physical significance of each term.*

**Model Answer:**

$$k \;=\; \frac{1}{k_0 \cdot S^2} \cdot \frac{\gamma_w}{\mu} \cdot \frac{e^3}{1+e}$$

- $k_0$ = Kozeny's shape/tortuosity constant (≈ 5)
- $S$ = specific surface area of soil particles (cm²/cm³)
- $\gamma_w$ = unit weight of water
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of pore water
- $e$ = void ratio

The equation shows that $k$ increases with void ratio (porosity), decreases with specific surface (hence with finer/clayey soils), and is inversely proportional to water viscosity.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **factors affecting the permeability** of soils, with reference to the Kozeny–Carman equation. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A constant-head permeability test on a saturated clean sand at 20 °C gave $k = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ cm/s when the void ratio was 0.70. Estimate the permeability of the **same sand** (same grain size, same shape) at a void ratio of 0.85 and at 30 °C. Use $\mu_{20} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m² and $\mu_{30} = 0.798 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m². *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

State the **Kozeny–Carman equation** as above. Then discuss the following factors in a **structured, paragraph form**, each linked to its physical meaning:

1. **Grain size** — for uniform spheres, $S = 6/D$, so $k \propto D^2$. The **Hazen empirical formula** $k = C \cdot D_{10}^{\,2}$ is the practical outcome.
2. **Specific surface** — clays have $S$ values 100 to 1000 times higher than sands, making $k$ vanishingly small.
3. **Shape of particles** — angular and platy grains introduce a higher shape factor $f_s$, reducing $k$.
4. **Void ratio** — cubic dependence $e^3/(1+e)$ is the strongest single term in the denominator-free part.
5. **Degree of saturation** — entrapped air reduces effective flow area; $k \propto S_r^{\,n}$.
6. **Viscosity and temperature** — $k \propto 1/\mu(T)$. Higher $T$ → lower $\mu$ → higher $k$.
7. **Soil structure and stratification** — $k_H$ (horizontal) typically exceeds $k_V$ (vertical); fissures and root holes create "apparent" high $k$.
8. **Adsorbed water in clays** — bound water has much higher effective viscosity; reduces $k$ drastically.

*(Allocate roughly 1 mark per major factor, with 1 mark for stating and explaining the master equation.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the two factors that change.**
- Void ratio: $e_1 = 0.70$ → $e_2 = 0.85$
- Temperature: $20\,°\text{C}$ → $30\,°\text{C}$ (i.e., $\mu$ changes)

**Step 2 — Form the combined ratio.**
Grain size, shape, $S_r$, and $\gamma_w$ are unchanged. Therefore:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} \;=\; \left[\frac{e_2^{\,3}\,(1+e_1)}{e_1^{\,3}\,(1+e_2)}\right] \cdot \left[\frac{\mu_1}{\mu_2}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the void-ratio sub-ratio.**

$$\frac{e_2^{\,3}\,(1+e_1)}{e_1^{\,3}\,(1+e_2)} \;=\; \frac{(0.85)^3 \cdot (1+0.70)}{(0.70)^3 \cdot (1+0.85)} \;=\; \frac{0.614125 \cdot 1.70}{0.343 \cdot 1.85}$$

$$= \;\frac{1.04401}{0.63455} \;\approx\; 1.6453$$

**Step 4 — Compute the viscosity sub-ratio.**

$$\frac{\mu_{20}}{\mu_{30}} \;=\; \frac{1.002}{0.798} \;\approx\; 1.2556$$

**Step 5 — Combine.**

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} \;=\; 1.6453 \times 1.2556 \;\approx\; 2.066$$

**Step 6 — Final value.**

$$k_2 \;=\; 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.066 \;\approx\; 7.23 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{cm/s}$$

**Answer:** $k_2 \;\approx\; 7.23 \times 10^{-3}$ cm/s.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Discuss the influence of **grain size, void ratio, and temperature** on the permeability of cohesionless soils. Derive the **Hazen formula** $k = C \cdot D_{10}^{\,2}$ from the Kozeny–Carman framework. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A stratified soil deposit consists of three horizontal layers with the following properties:
>
> | Layer | Thickness $H_i$ (m) | $k_{H,i}$ (cm/s) | $k_{V,i}$ (cm/s) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 2.0 | $1 \times 10^{-3}$ | $5 \times 10^{-4}$ |
> | 2 | 3.0 | $5 \times 10^{-5}$ | $2 \times 10^{-5}$ |
> | 3 | 5.0 | $1 \times 10^{-2}$ | $8 \times 10^{-3}$ |
>
> Compute the **equivalent horizontal** and **equivalent vertical** permeabilities of the deposit. Comment on the result. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Theory + Derivation (7 marks):**

- **Grain size (2 marks):** For uniform spherical particles, $S = 6/D$. Substituting into Kozeny–Carman:
