$$k \;=\; \frac{D^2}{36 \, k_0} \cdot \frac{\gamma_w}{\mu} \cdot \frac{e^3}{1+e}$$

Since $\gamma_w$, $\mu$, $e$, $k_0$ are constants for a given test, the proportionality $k \propto D^2$ emerges. For natural sands, taking $D \approx D_{10}$ and lumping all constants into a single empirical constant $C$:

$$k \;=\; C \cdot D_{10}^{\,2}$$

This is **Hazen's formula**. The constant $C$ is found experimentally (typically 0.4 to 1.2).

- **Void ratio (2 marks):** $k \propto e^3/(1+e)$ — a cubic-like growth. A loose sand with $e = 0.9$ has roughly 4 to 5 times the permeability of a dense sand with $e = 0.5$, holding grain size constant.
- **Temperature (2 marks):** $k \propto 1/\mu(T)$. As $T$ rises from 20 °C to 30 °C, $\mu$ drops by about 20%, so $k$ rises by about 25%. Used in correcting lab results to a standard temperature (usually 20 °C).
- **Derivation completion (1 mark):** Show the explicit substitution of $S = 6/D$ into Kozeny–Carman to recover $k \propto D^2$.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Equivalent horizontal permeability** (arithmetic mean weighted by thickness):

$$k_H \;=\; \frac{1}{H} \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_{H,i} \cdot H_i$$

$$k_H \;=\; \frac{(1 \times 10^{-3})(2) \;+\; (5 \times 10^{-5})(3) \;+\; (1 \times 10^{-2})(5)}{2 + 3 + 5}$$

$$k_H \;=\; \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-3} \;+\; 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \;+\; 5.0 \times 10^{-2}}{10}$$

$$k_H \;=\; \frac{0.05215}{10} \;=\; 5.215 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{cm/s}$$

**Equivalent vertical permeability** (harmonic mean weighted by thickness):

$$k_V \;=\; \frac{H}{\sum H_i / k_{V,i}}$$

$$\sum \frac{H_i}{k_{V,i}} \;=\; \frac{2}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \;+\; \frac{3}{2 \times 10^{-5}} \;+\; \frac{5}{8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$= \; 4000 \;+\; 150000 \;+\; 625 \;=\; 154625$$

$$k_V \;=\; \frac{10}{154625} \;\approx\; 6.47 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{cm/s}$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
$k_H$ is **about 80 times larger** than $k_V$. This is typical of natural deposits: the most impermeable layer (Layer 2 — a silty/clayey seam) controls vertical flow, but its effect is diluted in horizontal flow. The result has major implications for **drainage design, settlement analysis, and contaminant migration**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Temperature correction direction:** Students often write $k_{T_2} = k_{T_1} \cdot \mu_{T_2}/\mu_{T_1}$. The **correct** direction is the **inverse** — higher $T$ → lower $\mu$ → higher $k$. Wrong direction = **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing $k_H$ and $k_V$ formulas:** Some students write the harmonic mean for $k_H$ and arithmetic for $k_V$. Reverse the formulas and you lose the full 7 marks in the numerical.
> 3. **Forgetting to cube $e$:** Writing $k \propto e/(1+e)$ instead of $e^3/(1+e)$ is a very common slip and yields numerically wrong answers.
> 4. **Unit conversion in viscosity correction:** Always use **consistent units** for $\mu$ (N·s/m² or poise). A factor-of-10 slip is typical.
> 5. **"List" answers in 14-mark questions:** Examiners expect **explanation and linking to the master equation**, not a bullet-list dump. A bare list = capped at 7 out of 14 marks.
> 6. **Hazen's constant:** Stating only $k = D_{10}^{\,2}$ without mentioning the constant $C$ and its typical range is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Darcy's Law** $v = ki$ is the starting point — every permeability problem anchors here.
- [ ] **Kozeny–Carman equation** is the *master framework*: $k = \dfrac{1}{k_0 S^2} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma_w}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{e^3}{1+e}$.
- [ ] **Hazen formula** $k = C \cdot D_{10}^{\,2}$ is valid only for **clean, uniform sands** ($C \approx 1$, $k$ in cm/s, $D_{10}$ in mm).
- [ ] **Specific surface** has the **strongest** impact on $k$ in clays — $k \propto 1/S^2$.
- [ ] **Void ratio** has a **cubic** effect on $k$ — small changes in $e$ → big changes in $k$.
- [ ] **Temperature correction:** $k_{T_2} = k_{T_1} \cdot (\mu_{T_1}/\mu_{T_2})$.
- [ ] **Saturation:** $k \propto S_r^{\,n}$ with $n \approx 3$ to 4; air bubbles always **reduce** $k$.
- [ ] **Stratification:** Always $k_H > k_V$. Use weighted arithmetic mean for $k_H$ and weighted harmonic mean for $k_V$.
- [ ] **Adsorbed water in clays** is the reason clay permeability is *vanishingly small* — not just "small".
- [ ] **Shape of particles:** rounded → high $k$; angular/platy → low $k$.
- [ ] **Permeability of soil ranges** (must memorize): Gravel $\sim 1$ to $10^{-2}$ cm/s; Sand $\sim 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ cm/s; Silt $\sim 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ cm/s; Clay $< 10^{-7}$ cm/s.
- [ ] **Engineering uses:** filter design, earth-dam drainage, pumping test analysis, consolidation rate prediction, landfill liner selection.
- [ ] Always state **units** in the final answer — examiners do not give marks for a bare number.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laboratory Determination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Laboratory Determination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Laboratory Determination" in Soil Mechanics?

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PCAGT402 – Soil Mechanics and Foundations), **Laboratory Determination** refers to the set of standardized experimental procedures conducted in a geotechnical engineering laboratory to evaluate the physical, index, and engineering properties of soils. These properties form the empirical backbone of all foundation design, slope stability analysis, earthwork specification, and pavement design computations.

The principal quantities that are experimentally determined in the lab include:

- **Water Content (w)** — by Oven Drying, Sand Bath, Calcium Carbide, Torsional/Infrared methods
- **Specific Gravity (G)** — by Pycnometer and Density Bottle methods
- **Atterberg Limits (LL, PL, SL)** — Liquid Limit, Plastic Limit, Shrinkage Limit
- **Grain Size Distribution** — by Sieve Analysis and Sedimentation (Hydrometer) Analysis
- **Compaction Characteristics** — by Standard Proctor (IS Light Compaction) and Modified Proctor (Heavy Compaction) tests
- **California Bearing Ratio (CBR)** — for pavement subgrade evaluation
- **Unconfined Compressive Strength (q_u)** — for cohesive soil shear strength
- **Coefficient of Permeability (k)** — by Constant Head and Falling Head tests
- **Consolidation Parameters (C_c, C_v, m_v)** — by Oedometer test
- **Shear Strength Parameters (c, φ)** — by Direct Shear and Triaxial tests

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 Context):**
> Module 2 broadly covers **Compaction, Stabilization, and Laboratory Determination of index/properties**. The lab methods discussed here are codified in the relevant **IS Codes of Practice (Bureau of Indian Standards)** — students are expected to know the IS code number, the apparatus used, the procedure outline, and the formula for calculation for the End Semester Examination.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Soil Report Card"

Imagine soil as a student in school. Before a teacher (the foundation engineer) can recommend which "class" (foundation type) the student should attend, the teacher must measure the student's "marks" (properties) using standardized "exams" (lab tests).

- **Water content test** → measures the "moisture level" of the student
- **Specific gravity** → measures the "DNA identity" of the soil grains
- **Atterberg limits** → define the "behavioural zones" — whether the soil is sleepy (liquid), cooperative (plastic), or rigid (shrinkage)
- **Sieve & Hydrometer** → a "census" of grain sizes
- **Compaction test** → measures how well the soil can be "compressed and densified" under effort
- **CBR** → measures the "load-bearing capacity" of the student on a "stress test bench"
- **UCS** → the soil's "crushing strength" under unconfined compression
- **Permeability** → measures the "drainage speed" of the soil

Just as no engineer can design a building on unknown ground, **no B.Tech student of Soil Mechanics can pass Module 2 without mastering the lab determination of these properties**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Conventions Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - Unit weight of water: **γ_w = 9.81 kN/m³** (or 10 kN/m³ for quick problems)
> - Density of water: **ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - All mass measurements are recorded to **0.01 g** precision
> - All temperature references are at **27 °C** unless otherwise stated

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Each Laboratory Test

### A. Water Content Determination (IS 2720 Part 2)

**Why it matters:** Water content is the single most variable index property in soil mechanics — even a small change in w dramatically alters strength, compressibility, and permeability.

**How it works (Oven Drying Method):**
1. Take a clean, dry, empty container → weigh as **W₁**
2. Place ~30 g of wet soil in it → weigh as **W₂**
3. Place container in **thermostatically controlled oven** at **105 °C to 110 °C** for 24 hours
4. Remove, cool in **desiccator** (to prevent moisture re-absorption)
5. Re-weigh as **W₃**
6. Calculate using the formula below

> [!NOTE]
> The **desiccator** contains silica gel desiccant that maintains a moisture-free environment. The cooling step is non-negotiable — weighing a hot container causes convective air currents that cause erroneous readings.

---

### B. Specific Gravity Determination (IS 2720 Part 3)

**Why it matters:** Specific gravity (G) is used in the calculation of void ratio, density index, and is needed for phase diagram relationships.

**How it works (Pycnometer Method):**
1. Weigh empty, clean, dry pycnometer → **W₁**
2. Place ~200 g of oven-dry soil in it → **W₂**
3. Add de-aired water to submerge soil, soak for ~24 hours
4. Fill pycnometer fully with water → weigh → **W₃**
5. Empty pycnometer, clean, fill with **only de-aired water** at the same temperature → weigh → **W₄**
6. Calculate G using the standard formula

---

### C. Liquid Limit Determination (IS 2720 Part 5)

**Why it matters:** Liquid Limit (LL) is the water content at which soil transitions from liquid to plastic state. It is critical for **Casagrande's classification** and **Atterberg's plasticity chart**.

**How it works (Casagrande's Apparatus):**
1. Soil paste is placed in the standard brass cup
2. A standard groove is cut using the grooving tool
3. The cup is dropped from a height of **10 mm** at a rate of **2 blows/sec**
4. Record the number of blows (N) required to close the groove over a length of **12 mm**
5. Repeat for 4 to 5 different water contents (N ranging from 15 to 35)
6. Plot **flow curve**: water content (y-axis) vs. log N (x-axis)
7. **Liquid Limit = water content corresponding to N = 25 blows**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The flow curve must be a **straight line** on a semi-log plot. KTU examiners frequently test whether a student knows the **single-point method** (correction formula) and the **multipoint method**.

**Single-Point Method Correction (Correction factor):**
The water content corresponding to **N = 25** is computed as:

$$w_{LL} = w_N \times \left( \frac{N}{25} \right)^{0.121}$$

Where **w_N** is the water content at N blows.

---

### D. Plastic Limit Determination (IS 2720 Part 5)

**How it works:**
1. Roll a small portion of soil paste on a glass plate
2. When the thread **crumbles at a diameter of 3 mm**, the water content at that moment is the **Plastic Limit**
3. Measure by oven drying the crumbled thread

**Plasticity Index (I_P):**
$$I_P = w_L - w_P$$

This is the most frequently tested relationship in KTU exams.

---

### E. Shrinkage Limit Determination (IS 2720 Part 6)

The Shrinkage Limit (w_S) is the water content below which further loss of moisture does NOT cause a reduction in volume. Determined using the **shrinkage dish method**:

$$w_S = w_1 - \left[ \frac{(V_1 - V_2) \, \rho_w}{M_S} \right] \times 100$$

Where:
- **w₁** = initial water content
- **V₁** = initial volume of wet soil (mL)
- **V₂** = final volume of dry soil pat (mL) — measured by mercury displacement
- **M_S** = mass of dry soil (g)

---

### F. Sieve Analysis (IS 2720 Part 4) — for Gravel & Sand

**How it works:**
1. Oven-dry the soil sample
2. Weigh → **W_total**
3. Stack sieves in descending order of opening: 4.75 mm, 2.36 mm, 1.18 mm, 600 µm, 425 µm, 212 µm, 75 µm (with pan at bottom)
4. Shake in a **mechanical sieve shaker** for 10–15 minutes
5. Weigh soil retained on each sieve → **W_retained**
6. Calculate **% retained** and **cumulative % retained**
7. Compute **% finer = 100 − cumulative % retained**
8. Plot **grain size distribution curve** on semi-log paper (log of grain size on x-axis)

**Coefficient of Uniformity (C_u) and Coefficient of Curvature (C_c):**

$$C_u = \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$$

$$C_c = \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}$$

Where **D₁₀, D₃₀, D₆₀** are the grain sizes (in mm) at which 10%, 30%, and 60% of the soil is finer, respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Soil Classification Thresholds (IS Classification):**
> - **C_u ≥ 4** for gravels, **C_u ≥ 6** for sands → soil is **well-graded**
> - **1 ≤ C_c ≤ 3** → soil is **well-graded**
> - Otherwise the soil is **poorly graded (uniformly graded or gap-graded)**

---

### G. Hydrometer Analysis (IS 2720 Part 4) — for Silt & Clay

**Why sieves fail:** The finest sieve is 75 µm. Soils finer than this (silts and clays) pass through sieves and must be analysed by **sedimentation** based on **Stokes' Law**.

**Stokes' Law (Terminal Velocity of a falling sphere in a viscous fluid):**

$$v = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \, g \, D^2}{18 \, \mu}$$

Where:
- **v** = terminal settling velocity (cm/s)
- **ρ_s** = density of soil grain (g/cm³)
- **ρ_w** = density of water (g/cm³)
- **g** = acceleration due to gravity (cm/s²)
- **D** = diameter of soil grain (cm)
- **μ** = dynamic viscosity of water (poise)

**Diameter of particle at depth H after time t:**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \, \mu \, H}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \, g \, t}} \text{ (mm)}$$

For KTU, this is usually expressed in the simplified form using **H/e** where **e = 100 cm** for the standard 1000 mL jar and using **μ = 0.0009 poise** at 27 °C.

---

### H. Standard Proctor Compaction Test (IS 2720 Part 7)

**Why it matters:** Compaction tests establish the **Maximum Dry Density (MDD)** and **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC)** — these are the two most critical parameters for earthwork and embankment construction.

**How it works:**
1. Compact soil in a **1000 cm³ mould** in **3 equal layers**
2. Each layer receives **25 blows** from a **2.6 kg rammer** dropped from **310 mm**
3. **5 trials** at increasing water content (typically 2% increments)
4. For each trial, determine the **bulk density (ρ)** and water content (w)
5. Compute **dry density: ρ_d = ρ / (1 + w)**
6. Plot **dry density (y-axis) vs. water content (x-axis)**
7. The peak of the curve gives **MDD and OMC**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modified Proctor Test (IS 2720 Part 8)** uses:
> - **4 layers** in the same mould
> - **5 layers** in the larger mould
> - **25 or 56 blows** per layer
> - **4.9 kg rammer** dropped from **450 mm**
>
> Modified Proctor yields **higher MDD** and **lower OMC** than Standard Proctor because of greater compactive effort.

**Compactive Effort Formula:**

$$E = \frac{(N_b \times N_L \times W \times H)}{V}$$

Where:
- **N_b** = number of blows per layer
- **N_L** = number of layers
- **W** = weight of rammer
- **H** = height of drop
- **V** = volume of mould

---

### I. California Bearing Ratio (CBR) Test (IS 2720 Part 16)

**Why it matters:** CBR is the standard **pavement subgrade strength** parameter used in the design of flexible pavements as per **IRC:37**.

**How it works:**
1. Compact soil in CBR mould (150 mm dia × 175 mm height) at OMC
2. Soak the specimen in water for **4 days** (96 hours) under a surcharge weight
3. Place the mould in the loading machine
4. Apply load through a **piston of 50 mm diameter** at **1.25 mm/min**
5. Record loads at penetrations of: **0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 7.5, 10.0, 12.5 mm**
6. Plot load-penetration curve
7. **CBR (%) = (Test load / Standard load) × 100** at 2.5 mm and 5.0 mm penetration
8. **Adopt the higher value**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard loads** (as per IS):
> - At 2.5 mm penetration: **1370 kg** (13.44 kN)
> - At 5.0 mm penetration: **2055 kg** (20.16 kN)
>
> If CBR at 5 mm > CBR at 2.5 mm, the test is **repeated**. If still higher, the 5 mm value is adopted.

---

### J. Unconfined Compression Test (IS 2720 Part 10)

**Why it matters:** UCS provides a **rapid estimate of shear strength** for cohesive soils without requiring a confining cell. It is essentially a **special case of triaxial test with σ₃ = 0**.

**How it works:**
1. Prepare a cylindrical sample (38 mm dia × 76 mm height) from undisturbed soil
2. Place in the loading apparatus
3. Apply axial strain at a rate of **0.5 to 1.0% per minute**
4. Record load and deformation
5. Compute **strain ε = ΔL/L₀** and **stress σ = P/A_c** (corrected area)
6. Plot **stress-strain curve**
7. **q_u (UCS)** = peak stress from curve
8. **Shear strength (c_u) = q_u / 2** (assuming φ = 0 for saturated clays)
9. **Sensitivity (S_t) = q_u(undisturbed) / q_u(remoulded)**

---

### K. Permeability Tests

#### K.1 Constant Head Test (IS 2720 Part 36) — for coarse-grained soils

$$k = \frac{Q \, L}{A \, t \, h}$$

Where:
- **Q** = quantity of water collected (mL)
- **L** = length of soil sample (cm)
- **A** = cross-sectional area of sample (cm²)
- **t** = time (s)
- **h** = constant head difference (cm)

#### K.2 Falling Head Test (IS 2720 Part 17) — for fine-grained soils

$$k = \frac{2.303 \, a \, L}{A \, t} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right)$$

Where:
- **a** = cross-sectional area of standpipe (cm²)
- **A** = cross-sectional area of soil sample (cm²)
- **L** = length of sample (cm)
- **t** = time for head to fall from h₁ to h₂ (s)

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Master Table)

| # | Property | Formula | Key Variables | Units | IS Code |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Water Content | $w = \dfrac{W_2 - W_3}{W_3 - W_1} \times 100$ | $W_1$ = container, $W_2$ = wet, $W_3$ = dry | % | IS 2720-2 |
| 2 | Specific Gravity | $G = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$ | $W_1$ to $W_4$ from pycnometer | Dimensionless | IS 2720-3 |
| 3 | Liquid Limit (single point) | $w_{LL} = w_N \cdot \left( \dfrac{N}{25} \right)^{0.121}$ | $w_N$ at N blows | % | IS 2720-5 |
| 4 | Plasticity Index | $I_P = w_L - w_P$ | $w_L, w_P$ from LL, PL tests | % | IS 2720-5 |
| 5 | Shrinkage Index | $I_S = w_P - w_S$ | Plastic & Shrinkage Limits | % | IS 2720-6 |
| 6 | Shrinkage Ratio | $SR = \dfrac{M_S/V_S}{\rho_w}$ | Dry mass / dry volume | Dimensionless | IS 2720-6 |
| 7 | Coefficient of Uniformity | $C_u = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$ | From grain size curve | Dimensionless | IS 2720-4 |
| 8 | Coefficient of Curvature | $C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}$ | From grain size curve | Dimensionless | IS 2720-4 |
| 9 | Stokes' Particle Diameter | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \, \mu \, H}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \, g \, t}}$ | Settling velocity approach | mm | IS 2720-4 |
| 10 | Dry Density (compaction) | $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1+w}$ | Bulk density & water content | g/cm³ | IS 2720-7 |
| 11 | Zero Air Voids Line | $\rho_{d, ZAV} = \dfrac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + w \cdot G}$ | Saturation condition | g/cm³ | Theory |
| 12 | Compactive Effort | $E = \dfrac{N_b \cdot N_L \cdot W \cdot H}{V}$ | No. of blows, layers, etc. | kJ/m³ | IS 2720-7,8 |
| 13 | CBR | $CBR = \dfrac{\text{Test load}}{\text{Std. load}} \times 100$ | Loads at 2.5/5 mm | % | IS 2720-16 |
| 14 | Unconfined Compressive Strength | $q_u = \dfrac{P}{A_c}$ | Axial load / corrected area | kPa | IS 2720-10 |
| 15 | Undrained Shear Strength | $c_u = \dfrac{q_u}{2}$ | For $\phi = 0$ condition | kPa | Theory |
| 16 | Sensitivity | $S_t = \dfrac{q_u \, (\text{undisturbed})}{q_u \, (\text{remoulded})}$ | UCS of intact vs remoulded | Dimensionless | IS 2720-10 |
| 17 | Constant Head Permeability | $k = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot t \cdot h}$ | Flow parameters | cm/s | IS 2720-36 |
| 18 | Falling Head Permeability | $k = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{h_1}{h_2}\right)$ | Standpipe parameters | cm/s | IS 2720-17 |
| 19 | Consolidation Coefficient | $C_v = \dfrac{T_v \cdot H_{dr}^2}{t}$ | Time factor & drainage path | cm²/s | IS 2720-15 |
| 20 | Compression Index | $C_c = \dfrac{\Delta e}{\log_{10}(p_2/p_1)}$ | Void ratio vs log p | Dimensionless | IS 2720-15 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> Every formula above is the **decision threshold** for foundation type selection. For example:
> - **CBR < 2%** → soil is unsuitable for pavement → needs stabilization
> - **LL > 50%** → soil is **CH or MH** (high plasticity) → unsuitable as subgrade
> - **MDD/OMC** controls every specification of embankment construction in highways
> - **k (permeability)** decides whether a soil is suitable for **earth dam core** (low k required) or **drainage layer** (high k required)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Liquid Limit Flow Equation (Single-Point Correction)

The empirical single-point correction formula is derived from the **assumption of a linear flow curve** on a semi-log plot.

**Starting Relationship:**
On the semi-log flow curve, the relationship between water content (w) and number of blows (N) is:

$$w = a - b \cdot \log_{10}(N)$$

**At Liquid Limit (N = 25):**

$$w_L = a - b \cdot \log_{10}(25) = a - b \cdot (1.39794)$$

**At any test point (N):**

$$w_N = a - b \cdot \log_{10}(N)$$

**Subtracting the second from the first:**

$$w_L - w_N = -b \cdot \log_{10}(25) + b \cdot \log_{10}(N) = b \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{25}\right)$$

**By definition of Liquid Limit slope, b = 0.121 × w_L** (an empirical value fitted from thousands of experiments):

$$w_L - w_N = 0.121 \cdot w_L \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{25}\right)$$

**Rearranging:**

$$w_L \left[1 - 0.121 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{25}\right)\right] = w_N$$

$$\boxed{w_L = \frac{w_N}{1 - 0.121 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{25}\right)} = w_N \cdot \left(\frac{N}{25}\right)^{0.121}}$$

This is the famous **Casagrande single-point formula** tested frequently in KTU exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stokes' Law for Hydrometer Analysis

Consider a **single spherical soil grain** settling in a viscous fluid (water). The forces acting on the grain at terminal velocity are:

**Forces Acting:**

1. **Weight of the grain (downward):**

$$F_W = \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot D^3 \cdot \rho_s \cdot g$$

2. **Buoyancy force (upward):**

$$F_B = \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot D^3 \cdot \rho_w \cdot g$$

3. **Viscous drag (upward, by Stokes' Law for low Reynolds number):**

$$F_D = 3 \pi \cdot \mu \cdot D \cdot v$$

Where **v** is the terminal velocity.

**At Terminal Velocity (Net Force = 0):**

$$F_W = F_B + F_D$$

$$\frac{\pi}{6} \cdot D^3 \cdot \rho_s \cdot g = \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot D^3 \cdot \rho_w \cdot g + 3 \pi \cdot \mu \cdot D \cdot v$$

**Simplifying:**

$$\frac{\pi}{6} \cdot D^3 \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g = 3 \pi \cdot \mu \cdot D \cdot v$$

**Solving for v:**

$$v = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot D^2}{18 \, \mu}$$

**Substituting v = H/t** (where H = depth of fall in time t):

$$\frac{H}{t} = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot D^2}{18 \, \mu}$$

**Solving for D (in cm):**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \, \mu \cdot H}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot t}} \text{ (cm)}$$

**Converting to mm by multiplying by 10:**

$$\boxed{D = \sqrt{\frac{1800 \, \mu \cdot H}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot t}} \text{ (mm)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **H used in the hydrometer test is the EFFECTIVE depth**, not the actual depth of the jar. KTU students often make the mistake of using the full depth. Effective depth is read from a **calibration chart** supplied with the hydrometer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Zero Air Voids Line (Compaction Theory)

The Zero Air Voids (ZAV) line represents the theoretical **maximum dry density** achievable if **all air voids could be eliminated** (i.e., soil is 100% saturated).

**Starting from Phase Diagram Relations:**

For a saturated soil, volume of air **V_a = 0**. The void ratio **e = V_w/V_s**.

**Volume of voids per unit volume of solids:**

$$V_v = V_w = e \cdot V_s$$

**Volume of water (mass basis):**

$$M_w = w \cdot M_s = w \cdot G \cdot V_s \cdot \rho_w$$

**Volume of water (volume basis):**

$$V_w = \frac{M_w}{\rho_w} = w \cdot G \cdot V_s$$

**From V_v = V_w:**

$$e \cdot V_s = w \cdot G \cdot V_s$$

$$e = w \cdot G$$

**Dry Density (mass of solids per unit total volume):**

$$\rho_d = \frac{M_s}{V_s + V_v} = \frac{G \cdot V_s \cdot \rho_w}{V_s(1 + e)} = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + e}$$

**Substituting e = w·G:**

$$\boxed{\rho_{d, ZAV} = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + w \cdot G}}$$

This is plotted as a curve on the compaction graph — it always lies **above** the practical compaction curve, showing the theoretical limit.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Compaction Test (KTU Style)

**Question:** A Standard Proctor compaction test was conducted on a soil sample. The following data was obtained:

| Trial | Mass of wet soil in mould (kg) | Water content (%) |
|-------|-------------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 1.85 | 8.0 |
| 2 | 1.95 | 10.5 |
| 3 | 2.00 | 12.8 |
| 4 | 1.98 | 14.9 |
| 5 | 1.92 | 17.2 |

**Volume of mould = 1000 cm³.** Find the **MDD** and **OMC**.

**Solution Step 1: Compute Bulk Density (ρ) for each trial**

For Trial 1: ρ = M/V = 1.85/1000 = 1.85 × 10⁻³ kg/cm³ = **1.85 g/cm³**

| Trial | Bulk Density (g/cm³) | Water Content (%) |
|-------|---------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 1.85 | 8.0 |
| 2 | 1.95 | 10.5 |
| 3 | 2.00 | 12.8 |
| 4 | 1.98 | 14.9 |
| 5 | 1.92 | 17.2 |

**Solution Step 2: Compute Dry Density (ρ_d) using ρ_d = ρ / (1 + w)**

For Trial 1: $\rho_d = \dfrac{1.85}{1 + 0.08} = \dfrac{1.85}{1.08} = 1.713$ g/cm³

| Trial | Bulk Density (g/cm³) | Water Content (%) | Dry Density (g/cm³) |
|-------|---------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 1.85 | 8.0 | 1.713 |
| 2 | 1.95 | 10.5 | 1.765 |
| 3 | 2.00 | 12.8 | 1.773 |
| 4 | 1.98 | 14.9 | 1.723 |
| 5 | 1.92 | 17.2 | 1.638 |

**Solution Step 3: Identify the Peak**

From the table, the **maximum dry density** is at **Trial 3: 1.773 g/cm³** at water content **12.8%**.

**Answer:**
- **Maximum Dry Density (MDD) ≈ 1.77 g/cm³**
- **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC) ≈ 12.8%**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Board Examination Tip: The examiner often gives only **5 to 6 trials**. Always show the **computation table** as part of the answer — marks are awarded for the systematic tabulation, not just the final answer.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — CBR Calculation

**Question:** The following readings were obtained from a CBR test on a soaked soil sample:

| Penetration (mm) | Load (kg) |
|------------------|-----------|
| 0.5 | 30 |
| 1.0 | 75 |
| 1.5 | 110 |
| 2.0 | 145 |
| 2.5 | 180 |
| 3.0 | 215 |
| 4.0 | 245 |
| 5.0 | 275 |
| 7.5 | 310 |
| 10.0 | 335 |
| 12.5 | 360 |

Standard loads: **1370 kg at 2.5 mm** and **2055 kg at 5.0 mm**. Determine the CBR of the soil.

**Solution:**

**CBR at 2.5 mm penetration:**

$$CBR_{2.5} = \frac{180}{1370} \times 100 = 13.14\%$$

**CBR at 5.0 mm penetration:**

$$CBR_{5.0} = \frac{275}{2055} \times 100 = 13.38\%$$

**Adopt the higher value:**

$$\boxed{CBR = 13.38\% \approx 13.4\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since CBR at 5.0 mm is **higher** than CBR at 2.5 mm, the result is acceptable without re-testing. If CBR at 2.5 mm > CBR at 5.0 mm (which is the more common occurrence), then CBR at 2.5 mm is adopted directly.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Laboratory Determination Workflow

The following block-level flow diagram captures the **end-to-end logic** by which any soil sample is processed through a chain of laboratory determinations to yield the engineering parameters needed for foundation design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Soil Sample Received in Lab] --> B[Visual Identification and Soil Description IS 1498]
    B --> C{Is Sample Cohesive or Non-Cohesive}
    C -->|Non-Cohesive Sand Gravel| D[Index Property Tests]
    C -->|Cohesive Clay Silt| E[Index Property Tests]
    D --> F[Water Content w IS 2720-2]
    D --> G[Specific Gravity G IS 2720-3]
    D --> H[Sieve Analysis IS 2720-4]
    E --> I[Water Content w IS 2720-2]
    E --> J[Specific Gravity G IS 2720-3]
    E --> K[Hydrometer Analysis IS 2720-4]
    F --> L[Classification and Grain Size Curve]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Engineering Property Tests Required}
    M -->|Pavement Design| N[Compaction Test IS 2720-7 or 8]
    M -->|Pavement Design| O[CBR Test IS 2720-16]
    M -->|Foundation Bearing Capacity| P[UCS Test IS 2720-10]
    M -->|Foundation Bearing Capacity| Q[Direct Shear or Triaxial IS 2720-13 or 11]
    M -->|Settlement Analysis| R[Consolidation Oedometer IS 2720-15]
    M -->|Drainage or Earth Dam| S[Permeability Test IS 2720-17 or 36]
    N --> T[Compute MDD and OMC]
    O --> U[Compute CBR Percentage]
    P --> V[Compute q_u and c_u]
    Q --> W[Compute c and phi]
    R --> X[Compute C_c, m_v, C_v]
    S --> Y[Compute k Permeability Coefficient]
    T --> Z[Foundation Design Inputs Ready]
    U --> Z
    V --> Z
    W --> Z
    X --> Z
    Y --> Z
    Z --> AA[Submit Report with All Test Data and Sign Off]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Compaction Test Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Proctor Test] --> B[Compact Soil in 3 Layers for IS Light]
    B --> C[25 Blows per Layer 2.6 kg Rammer 310 mm Drop]
    C --> D[Weigh Mould Plus Soil]
    D --> E[Take Sample for Water Content w]
    E --> F{At Least 5 Trials Completed}
    F -->|No| G[Increase Water Content by 2 percent]
    G --> B
    F -->|Yes| H[Compute Bulk Density rho for Each Trial]
    H --> I[Compute Dry Density rho_d using rho divided by 1 plus w]
    I --> J[Plot Dry Density versus Water Content]
    J --> K[Identify Peak Point on Compaction Curve]
    K --> L[Record MDD Maximum Dry Density]
    K --> M[Record OMC Optimum Moisture Content]
    L --> N[Report Submitted]
    M --> N
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Atterberg Limits Determination Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air Dry Soil Sample] --> B[Sieve Through 425 Micron IS Sieve]
    B --> C[Mix with Distilled Water to Form Paste]
    C --> D[Casagrande Liquid Limit Test]
    C --> E[Plastic Limit Roll Test 3 mm Thread]
    C --> F[Shrinkage Limit Test Using Mercury]
    D --> G[Record N and w for 4 to 5 Trials]
    G --> H[Plot Flow Curve w versus log N]
    H --> I[Read w at N equals 25 as Liquid Limit]
    E --> J[Record w at Thread Crumbles as Plastic Limit]
    F --> K[Compute Shrinkage Limit from Mass and Volume Data]
    I --> L[Compute Plasticity Index I_P equals w_L minus w_P]
    J --> L
    K --> M[Compute Shrinkage Index I_S equals w_P minus w_S]
    L --> N[Classify Soil Using IS Plasticity Chart]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Final Classification CL CI CH ML MI MH etc]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Note:** These schematics map the procedural control flow rather than physical laboratory bench layouts. This is the standard format used in answer scripts when the question asks for a "flowchart" or "sequence of operations" in the lab. It is also the diagram type that is **most commonly required in 14-mark derivation questions** in KTU 2024 ESE papers.
