# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Water Content. Briefly explain the Oven Drying method of determining water content. Mention the relevant IS Code.

**[Model Answer — 3 Marks]:**

**Water content (w)** of a soil is defined as the **ratio of the mass of water present in the soil voids to the mass of solid particles**, expressed as a percentage.

$$w = \frac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100$$

**Oven Drying Method (IS 2720 Part 2):**
1. A clean, dry, empty container is weighed (W₁).
2. A representative wet soil sample (~30 g for fine-grained, ~300 g for coarse-grained) is placed in the container and weighed (W₂).
3. The container with the soil is placed in a **thermostatically controlled oven** at a temperature of **105 °C to 110 °C** for **24 hours**.
4. The container is removed, cooled in a **desiccator** containing silica gel, and reweighed (W₃).

$$w = \frac{W_2 - W_3}{W_3 - W_1} \times 100$$

**IS Code:** IS 2720 (Part 2) — 1973 (Reaffirmed).

---

### Q2. What is Liquid Limit? How is it determined in the laboratory?

**[Model Answer — 3 Marks]:**

**Liquid Limit (w_L or LL)** is defined as the **minimum water content at which the soil behaves like a liquid** — i.e., the soil just begins to flow upon application of a small shearing stress. Below LL, the soil exhibits plastic behaviour.

**Determination by Casagrande's Apparatus (IS 2720 Part 5):**
- A soil paste is placed in the **standard brass cup** of the Casagrande apparatus.
- A **standard groove** is cut through the paste using a grooving tool.
- The cup is dropped from a height of **10 mm** at a rate of **2 blows per second** by rotating the cam.
- The number of blows (N) required to **close the groove over a length of 12 mm** is recorded.
- The test is repeated at 4 to 5 different water contents, and the **flow curve** (w vs log N) is plotted.
- The **Liquid Limit** is the water content corresponding to **N = 25 blows**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Compaction + CBR Cluster

#### (a) Describe with a neat sketch the procedure of the Standard Proctor Compaction Test. List the apparatus used and the relevant IS Code. Also explain how the test results are plotted and interpreted. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus Used (IS 2720 Part 7):**
1. Standard mould — **1000 cm³** volume, **100 mm diameter**, **127.3 mm height**, with detachable base plate and collar
2. Rammer — **2.6 kg mass**, **310 mm free fall height**
3. Mechanical or hand-operated compactor
4. Balance — accuracy **0.01 g**
5. Oven — thermostatically controlled at **105–110 °C**
6. Measuring cylinder, mixing tray, spatula, knife, moisture cans

**Procedure:**
1. Take **~3 kg** of air-dried soil passed through the **4.75 mm IS sieve**.
2. Mix thoroughly with a known quantity of water (e.g., start with 6% and increase by 2% in each subsequent trial).
3. **Compact the soil in 3 equal layers** in the mould assembled with the collar attached.
4. Each layer receives **25 uniformly distributed blows** of the rammer dropped from 310 mm.
5. Remove the collar, trim the surface flush with the mould top, and weigh the mould with the compacted soil.
6. **Extract a small sample** from the top, middle, and bottom of the compacted soil to determine the **water content (w)** by oven drying.
7. **Repeat steps 2–6 for at least 5 trials** with progressively increasing water content.
8. **Compute the bulk density (ρ)** and **dry density (ρ_d = ρ / 1 + w)** for each trial.
9. **Plot the compaction curve** with **water content (w) on the x-axis** and **dry density (ρ_d) on the y-axis**.

**Interpretation:**
- The peak of the compaction curve gives the **Maximum Dry Density (MDD)**.
- The water content corresponding to MDD is the **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC)**.
- The shape of the curve: dry side (left) shows insufficient water for lubrication; wet side (right) shows excess pore water pressure replacing solid contact.

---

#### (b) What is California Bearing Ratio (CBR)? How is it determined in the laboratory? How is the CBR value used in flexible pavement design? [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
CBR is defined as the **ratio of the test load required to penetrate a soil mass with a standard plunger at a specified rate to a standard load required to achieve the same penetration on a standard crushed stone**, expressed as a percentage.

**Apparatus (IS 2720 Part 16):**
- CBR mould — **150 mm dia × 175 mm height** with detachable base and collar
- Plunger — **50 mm diameter**
- Loading machine — capacity **50 kN**, penetration rate **1.25 mm/min**
- Surcharge weights — **5 kg slotted annular and 2.5 kg slotted**
- Dial gauges, soaking tank

**Procedure:**
1. Take ~6 kg of soil passing 20 mm sieve, mix at OMC ± 1%, and compact in **5 layers** using the **Modified Proctor** rammer for **55, 25, 16, 12, 5 blows/layer** (or as per IS).
2. Place the mould in a **soaking tank** for **4 days (96 hours)** with surcharge weights on top to simulate the worst field condition.
3. After soaking, measure the swell and place the mould in the loading machine.
4. Seat the **50 mm plunger** on the soil surface, apply a small initial seating load.
5. Apply load at a uniform rate of **1.25 mm/min** and record loads at penetrations of 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 7.5, 10.0, 12.5 mm.
6. Plot **load vs penetration** curve.

**Calculation:**
- **Standard load at 2.5 mm** = 1370 kg
- **Standard load at 5.0 mm** = 2055 kg

$$CBR_{2.5} = \frac{\text{Load at 2.5 mm}}{1370} \times 100\%$$

$$CBR_{5.0} = \frac{\text{Load at 5.0 mm}}{2055} \times 100\%$$

- **Adopt the higher value** of the two CBRs (if 5 mm > 2.5 mm consistently after re-test, then 5 mm is used).

**Use in Flexible Pavement Design (IRC:37):**
- The CBR of the subgrade is the **primary design input** for flexible pavement thickness.
- Higher CBR → thinner pavement crust required.
- Typical design chart in **IRC:37-2018** relates **CBR (%)** to the **total pavement thickness (mm)** for a given traffic (in MSA — Million Standard Axles).
- Example: For a 100 MSA traffic road with subgrade CBR of 8%, total pavement thickness ≈ 660 mm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Index Properties + Permeability Cluster (Alternative Choice)

#### (a) Explain in detail the determination of Specific Gravity of soil using the Pycnometer method. How is it used in the calculation of void ratio and density index? [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
**Specific Gravity (G)** of soil solids is the **ratio of the mass of soil solids to the mass of an equal volume of water at 27 °C**.

$$G = \frac{\text{Mass of soil solids}}{\text{Mass of equal volume of water at 27°C}}$$

**Apparatus (IS 2720 Part 3):**
- Pycnometer (specific gravity bottle) — **500 mL or 1000 mL**
- Balance — accuracy **0.01 g**
- Desiccator, de-aired distilled water, oven, vacuum pump

**Procedure:**
1. **Calibrate the pycnometer:** Wash, dry, fill with de-aired water at 27 °C up to the mark, weigh → **W₄**.
2. Empty, dry the pycnometer, and weigh empty → **W₁**.
3. Place about **200 g of oven-dry soil** (passed through 4.75 mm sieve) in the pycnometer → weigh → **W₂**.
4. Add de-aired water to cover the soil, soak for **24 hours** to allow air to escape.
5. Apply **vacuum** to remove entrapped air completely.
6. Top up the pycnometer with de-aired water to the mark, weigh → **W₃**.

**Calculation:**

$$G = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$$

**Use in Void Ratio Calculation:**
From the phase diagram, knowing G, ρ (bulk density), and ρ_w:

$$e = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{\rho_d} - 1$$

**Use in Density Index (I_D) Calculation:**

$$I_D = \frac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100$$

where e_max and e_min are determined separately by IS 2720 Part 14. I_D is a key parameter for granular soils indicating their **compactness**.

---

#### (b) Describe the Constant Head and Falling Head methods for determining the coefficient of permeability (k) of soil. State the relevant IS Codes. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
**Permeability** is the property of soil that allows the flow of water through its interconnected void spaces. The **coefficient of permeability (k)** is defined as the **discharge velocity under a unit hydraulic gradient**.

**Darcy's Law:**

$$v = k \cdot i$$

where **i = h/L** is the hydraulic gradient.

---

**A. Constant Head Test (IS 2720 Part 36):**

**Suitability:** Granular soils (coarse sand, gravel).

**Apparatus:**
- Permeameter mould (circular cross-section of area A)
- Constant head water tank
- Measuring cylinder and stopwatch

**Procedure:**
1. Saturate the soil sample in the mould completely.
2. Establish a **constant head (h)** of water above the sample.
3. Allow water to flow through the soil; collect the **outflow (Q)** over a measured **time (t)**.
4. Note the **length of the sample (L)** and **head difference (h)**.

**Formula:**

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot t \cdot h}$$

---

**B. Falling Head Test (IS 2720 Part 17):**

**Suitability:** Fine-grained soils (silt, fine sand, clay).

**Apparatus:**
- Permeameter mould with sample of length L and area A
- Standpipe of cross-section **a** attached to the top of the sample
- Scale to read water head in standpipe

**Procedure:**
1. Saturate the soil sample.
2. Fill the standpipe to a head **h₁** above the outlet level.
3. Start a stopwatch.
4. Allow the head to fall; note the time **t** when the head reaches **h₂**.

**Formula:**

$$k = \frac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing units:** k is in **cm/s**, not m/s. Always convert lengths to cm.
> 2. **Wrong standpipe area:** In falling head, **a** is the area of the standpipe, NOT the sample.
> 3. **Mixing up LL and PL:** Liquid Limit = water content at N=25 blows; Plastic Limit = 3 mm thread crumbling. These are independent tests.
> 4. **Forgetting the desiccator step:** Cooling the sample in air after oven-drying causes moisture re-absorption and gives **erroneously low water content**.
> 5. **CBR mistake:** Forgetting to compare 2.5 mm and 5.0 mm values; in IS code, 5.0 mm value is used only if it is consistently higher than 2.5 mm after re-test.
> 6. **Sieve mistake:** Forgetting that the **pan** at the bottom of the sieve stack collects particles passing the **75 µm (200-mesh)** sieve. Anything finer needs **Hydrometer** analysis.
> 7. **Stokes' Law mistake:** Using the **full depth of the hydrometer** instead of the **effective depth** from the calibration chart — this introduces a 5–10% error in computed particle diameter.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [ ] Water Content formula: **w = (W₂ − W₃) / (W₃ − W₁) × 100** — always in **percentage**
- [ ] Specific Gravity formula: **G = (W₂ − W₁) / [(W₄ − W₁) − (W₃ − W₂)]**
- [ ] Oven temperature: **105 °C to 110 °C** for **24 hours**
- [ ] Liquid Limit = water content at **N = 25** blows using **Casagrande apparatus**
- [ ] Single-point LL correction: **w_LL = w_N × (N/25)^0.121**
- [ ] Plasticity Index: **I_P = w_L − w_P** (the most used relation in KTU)
- [ ] Shrinkage Limit: water content below which volume change is zero
- [ ] Sieve sizes to remember: **4.75 mm, 2.36 mm, 600 µm, 425 µm, 212 µm, 75 µm**
- [ ] D₁₀, D₃₀, D₆₀: particle sizes corresponding to 10%, 30%, 60% finer
- [ ] **C_u = D₆₀ / D₁₀** → ≥4 for gravel, ≥6 for sand → well-graded
- [ ] **C_c = (D₃₀)² / (D₁₀ · D₆₀)** → 1 ≤ C_c ≤ 3 → well-graded
- [ ] Stokes' Law derivation requires three forces: weight, buoyancy, viscous drag
- [ ] Standard Proctor: **2.6 kg rammer, 310 mm drop, 3 layers, 25 blows/layer, 1000 cm³ mould**
- [ ] Modified Proctor: **4.9 kg rammer, 450 mm drop, 4 or 5 layers, 25 or 56 blows/layer**
- [ ] Compaction formula: **ρ_d = ρ / (1 + w)**
- [ ] Zero Air Voids line: **ρ_d,ZAV = (G · ρ_w) / (1 + w · G)** — always lies ABOVE compaction curve
- [ ] CBR plunger diameter: **50 mm**, penetration rate: **1.25 mm/min**, soaking: **4 days**
- [ ] CBR standard loads: **1370 kg at 2.5 mm, 2055 kg at 5.0 mm**
- [ ] UCS test strain rate: **0.5 to 1% per minute**, c_u = q_u / 2 (for φ=0 clays)
- [ ] Sensitivity: **S_t = q_u(undisturbed) / q_u(remoulded)** — classifies clays as low/medium/high sensitive
- [ ] Constant head k (granular): **k = QL / (A·t·h)**
- [ ] Falling head k (fine): **k = 2.303·a·L / (A·t) × log(h₁/h₂)**
- [ ] IS Codes at a glance:
  - **IS 2720 Part 2** → Water Content
  - **IS 2720 Part 3** → Specific Gravity
  - **IS 2720 Part 4** → Grain Size (Sieve + Hydrometer)
  - **IS 2720 Part 5** → Liquid & Plastic Limits
  - **IS 2720 Part 6** → Shrinkage Limit
  - **IS 2720 Part 7** → Standard Proctor Compaction
  - **IS 2720 Part 8** → Modified Proctor Compaction
  - **IS 2720 Part 10** → Unconfined Compression
  - **IS 2720 Part 13** → Direct Shear
  - **IS 2720 Part 11** → Triaxial
  - **IS 2720 Part 15** → Consolidation (Oedometer)
  - **IS 2720 Part 16** → CBR
  - **IS 2720 Part 17** → Falling Head Permeability
  - **IS 2720 Part 36** → Constant Head Permeability
  - **IS 1498** → Soil Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Module 2 typically contributes **15–20% of the question paper** in ESE. A 14-mark question is highly likely to be on **Compaction + CBR** or **Index Properties + Permeability**. Memorize the **formulas, IS codes, and apparatus specifications** — these are the three pillars of high marks. The numerical problems (worked examples in Section 3 above) mirror the exact KTU style: data table → computation table → plot → final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stratified Soils: Average Permeability. Compaction: Definition and Objectives of Compaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stratified Soils: Average Permeability. Compaction: Definition and Objectives of Compaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Stratified Soils — Formal Definition

In natural geological deposits and engineered earthworks, soils are rarely homogeneous. They exist as a sequence of distinct layers (strata) that vary in grain size, density, mineralogy, and—most importantly for this module—**hydraulic conductivity (permeability)**. A *stratified soil deposit* is a soil mass composed of two or more superimposed horizontal layers of differing permeabilities, formed either by natural sedimentary processes (alluvial deposits, lacustrine clays) or by man-made compaction lifts (earth dams, embankments, pavement subgrades).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:**
> *Stratified soils* refer specifically to a layered system in which each layer is treated as **homogeneous and isotropic within itself**, but the permeability differs from one layer to the next. The bedding planes are assumed horizontal.

### 1.2 Average (Equivalent) Permeability — Definition

Because each stratum has its own permeability `k_i`, it becomes impractical to test or model flow through every individual seam. Instead, geotechnical engineers compute an **average permeability (k_avg)**—a single equivalent hydraulic conductivity that represents the entire stratified mass for seepage analysis. The numerical value of this average depends critically on the **direction of flow** relative to the bedding planes.

There are two canonical flow cases (both are KTU board-exam favourites):

1. **Flow Parallel to the Stratification (Horizontal Flow, k_H)** — water flows *along* the layers (e.g., seepage through the core of an earth dam parallel to its base).
2. **Flow Perpendicular to the Stratification (Vertical Flow, k_V)** — water flows *across* the layers (e.g., seepage under a weir or through a clay liner beneath a landfill).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Take-away:** For the *same* stratified deposit, `k_H` is **always greater than or equal to** `k_V`. The horizontal permeability is dominated by the most permeable (sandy) seams, while the vertical permeability is throttled by the least permeable (clayey) seams.

### 1.3 Compaction — Formal Definition

**Compaction** is the process of densifying a soil mass by the application of mechanical energy (static weight, impact, kneading, or vibration), thereby expelling air from the voids without significantly changing the water content. It is a *mechanical, instantaneous* process, distinct from *consolidation*, which is a time-dependent expulsion of pore water under sustained load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> Compaction is the process of increasing the density of soil by mechanically packing the particles closer together with a reduction in the volume of air voids at essentially constant water content.

### 1.4 Objectives of Compaction

The purpose of compacting soil in the field is to engineer a material whose in-place properties satisfy design criteria for stability, serviceability, and durability. The principal objectives (as listed in KTU Module 2) are:

1. **Increase Shear Strength** — denser packing yields higher inter-particle friction and cohesion, improving slope and embankment stability.
2. **Reduce Compressibility (Settlement)** — fewer air voids mean less subsequent volume change under structural loads.
3. **Decrease Permeability** — compacted clays form low-permeability barriers (liners, cores of dams).
4. **Increase Bearing Capacity** — a denser subgrade resists punching shear failure under foundations.
5. **Control Swelling and Shrinkage** — compaction at optimum moisture minimises detrimental volume changes in expansive clays.
6. **Improve Stability of Pavements and Earth Structures** — uniform density prevents localised weak zones.
7. **Reduce Frost Susceptibility** — denser soils have fewer voids for ice-lens formation.

### 1.5 Intuitive Analogies (Real-World Picture)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — Stratified Soils as a Multi-Storey Parking Lot:**
> Imagine a parking structure where each floor is built with different porosity concrete. Cars (water) entering horizontally zoom through the porous floors easily but get blocked by the dense floors when moving vertically from floor to floor. **Horizontal flow is fast, vertical flow is slow** — exactly how `k_H > k_V` in real stratified soils.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — Compaction as Packing a Suitcase:**
> Throwing clothes loosely leaves lots of air gaps (high void ratio, low density). Roll them tightly and tap the suitcase on the floor (mechanical energy): the volume of air shrinks, the mass per unit volume increases, and the suitcase becomes rigid. The clothes are still the *same mass* (constant water content), but now **denser and stronger**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Setup of a Stratified Deposit

Consider a stratified soil profile consisting of `n` horizontal layers, each with:
- Thickness: `H_i` (where `i = 1, 2, 3, …, n`)
- Coefficient of permeability: `k_i`
- Total thickness: `H = Σ H_i`
- Cross-sectional area perpendicular to flow: `A`

The hydraulic gradient driving flow is `i`.

### 2.2 Case 1 — Flow Parallel to Stratification (Horizontal Flow, k_H)

**Operating Logic:**
- The hydraulic gradient `i` is **identical** in every layer (same head loss across the same horizontal length).
- The total discharge equals the sum of discharges through each layer.
- Each layer acts like a parallel pipe carrying water.

**Why?** Because parallel flow lets the high-permeability layers carry disproportionately more water. The composite behaviour is *weighted by thickness*, since thicker layers contribute more cross-sectional area.

**Theoretical Expression:**

$$q_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i$$

$$q_i = k_i \cdot i \cdot A \cdot \text{(area fraction)} \implies q_{total} = i \cdot A \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i H_i$$

If we treat the whole deposit as a single equivalent layer of permeability `k_H` and total thickness `H`:

$$q_{total} = k_H \cdot i \cdot A \cdot H$$

Equating the two:

$$k_H = \frac{1}{H} \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i H_i$$

### 2.3 Case 2 — Flow Perpendicular to Stratification (Vertical Flow, k_V)

**Operating Logic:**
- The **same discharge `q`** must pass through every layer (continuity).
- The **head loss `h_i`** differs from layer to layer because each layer has a different permeability.
- The total head loss `h = Σ h_i` acts over the total thickness `H`.

**Why?** A clay seam acts as a bottleneck — it consumes most of the hydraulic head while transmitting only a little flow. Vertical flow is therefore dominated by the *least* permeable layer.

**Theoretical Expression:**

For each layer:

$$q = k_i \cdot i_i \cdot A, \quad \text{where} \quad i_i = \frac{h_i}{H_i}$$

Therefore:

$$h_i = \frac{q \cdot H_i}{k_i \cdot A}$$

Summing head losses:

$$h = \sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i = \frac{q}{A} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{H_i}{k_i}$$

The equivalent vertical permeability for the entire deposit:

$$k_V = \frac{q}{i \cdot A} = \frac{q \cdot H}{h \cdot A} = \frac{H}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{H_i}{k_i}}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average horizontal permeability (parallel flow) | $k_H = \dfrac{1}{H} \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i H_i$ | m/s or cm/s | Flow parallel to bedding |
| 2 | Average vertical permeability (perpendicular flow) | $k_V = \dfrac{H}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{H_i}{k_i}}$ | m/s or cm/s | Flow perpendicular to bedding |
| 3 | For **two layers** (n = 2): horizontal | $k_H = \dfrac{k_1 H_1 + k_2 H_2}{H_1 + H_2}$ | m/s | Most common exam form |
| 4 | For **two layers** (n = 2): vertical | $k_V = \dfrac{(H_1 + H_2)}{\dfrac{H_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{H_2}{k_2}}$ | m/s | Most common exam form |
| 5 | Inequality theorem | $k_V \le k_H$ | — | Equality only when all `k_i` are equal |
| 6 | Weighted-harmonic vs weighted-arithmetic | $k_V$ = harmonic mean; $k_H$ = arithmetic mean | — | Memorise this classification |
| 7 | Discharge in parallel flow | $q = k_H \cdot i \cdot A \cdot H$ | m³/s | For full section |
| 8 | Discharge in vertical flow | $q = k_V \cdot i \cdot A$ | m³/s | Independent of H in cross-section |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Memory Trick:**
> • **Horizontal (parallel) flow → use weighted ARITHMETIC mean.**
> • **Vertical (perpendicular) flow → use weighted HARMONIC mean.**
> • Harmonic mean is *always smaller* than arithmetic mean for positive numbers — explaining why `k_V ≤ k_H`.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Earthen Dam Design:** The dam has a low-permeability clay core sandwiched between pervious shells. Engineers compute `k_V` to estimate seepage loss through the foundation and `k_H` to estimate pore pressures in the downstream shell.
- **Landfill Liners:** Compacted clay liners (CCL) are placed in *lifts*. Engineers ensure the **vertical permeability** stays below regulatory thresholds (typically 1 × 10⁻⁹ m/s).
- **Pavement Subgrade:** Permeability anisotropy (`k_H/k_V`) governs drainage of infiltrated water — a critical input for flexible pavement design.
- **Seepage and Piping Analysis:** Stratified foundations under weirs or cofferdams are analysed using `k_V` to compute uplift pressures and exit gradients.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Average Horizontal Permeability (k_H)

**Given:** A stratified deposit with `n` horizontal layers. Each layer `i` has thickness `H_i`, permeability `k_i`, and the same cross-sectional area `A`. Hydraulic gradient `i` acts parallel to the bedding planes.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing flow principle.**
For horizontal flow, the hydraulic gradient is the *same* across all layers because the upstream and downstream faces of every layer lie in the same equipotential surfaces.

$$i_1 = i_2 = i_3 = \cdots = i_n = i$$

**Step 2 — Write Darcy's law for each individual layer.**
The velocity of flow through layer `i` is:

$$v_i = k_i \cdot i$$

**Step 3 — Write the discharge through each layer.**
Discharge per unit width (or for full area `A`):

$$q_i = v_i \cdot A_i = k_i \cdot i \cdot A$$

When summed over the full cross-section of the deposit, each layer contributes a discharge proportional to its thickness-weighted area. For a unit width:

$$q_i = k_i \cdot i \cdot H_i \quad (\text{per unit width})$$

**Step 4 — Sum the discharges (continuity in parallel pipes).**

$$q_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i \cdot i \cdot H_i = i \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i H_i$$

**Step 5 — Express the total discharge using the equivalent permeability.**

$$q_{total} = k_H \cdot i \cdot H$$

where `H = Σ H_i` is the total thickness.

**Step 6 — Equate and solve for `k_H`.**

$$k_H \cdot i \cdot H = i \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i H_i$$

Since `i ≠ 0`, cancel it from both sides:

$$\boxed{k_H = \frac{1}{H} \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i H_i = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i H_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i}}$$

This is the **weighted arithmetic mean** of the layer permeabilities, weighted by layer thickness.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Average Vertical Permeability (k_V)

**Given:** The same stratified deposit but now water flows vertically (perpendicular to bedding).

**Step 1 — Identify the governing flow principle.**
The discharge `q` must be the *same* through every layer (continuity), but the head loss `h_i` and gradient `i_i` differ between layers.

$$q_1 = q_2 = \cdots = q_n = q$$

**Step 2 — Apply Darcy's law to each layer.**

$$q = k_i \cdot i_i \cdot A$$

Solve for the gradient in layer `i`:

$$i_i = \frac{q}{k_i \cdot A}$$

**Step 3 — Express head loss in each layer.**
Head loss equals gradient times path length:

$$h_i = i_i \cdot H_i = \frac{q \cdot H_i}{k_i \cdot A}$$

**Step 4 — Sum head losses across all layers (energy conservation).**

$$h = \sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{q \cdot H_i}{k_i \cdot A} = \frac{q}{A} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{H_i}{k_i}$$

**Step 5 — Express total head loss using equivalent permeability.**

$$h = i \cdot H = \frac{q}{k_V \cdot A} \cdot H$$

**Step 6 — Equate and solve for `k_V`.**

$$\frac{q}{k_V \cdot A} \cdot H = \frac{q}{A} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{H_i}{k_i}$$

Cancel `q/A` from both sides:

$$\frac{H}{k_V} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{H_i}{k_i}$$

$$\boxed{k_V = \frac{H}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{H_i}{k_i}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{H_i}{k_i}}}$$

This is the **weighted harmonic mean** of the layer permeabilities.

---

### 3.3 Special Case — Two-Layer System (Most Common KTU Exam Form)

For a deposit with only **two strata** of thicknesses `H_1` and `H_2`, and permeabilities `k_1` and `k_2`:

**Horizontal (parallel flow):**

$$k_H = \frac{k_1 H_1 + k_2 H_2}{H_1 + H_2}$$

**Vertical (perpendicular flow):**

$$k_V = \frac{H_1 + H_2}{\dfrac{H_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{H_2}{k_2}}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For Self-Study / Internal Practice)

**Problem Statement:**
A stratified soil deposit consists of three horizontal layers:
- Layer 1: `H_1 = 2 m`, `k_1 = 1 \times 10^{-4}` cm/s
- Layer 2: `H_2 = 3 m`, `k_2 = 3 \times 10^{-2}` cm/s
- Layer 3: `H_3 = 1 m`, `k_3 = 1 \times 10^{-5}` cm/s

Compute the average horizontal and vertical permeabilities.

**Solution:**

Total thickness:
$$H = 2 + 3 + 1 = 6 \text{ m}$$

**Horizontal permeability (arithmetic mean):**

$$k_H = \frac{1}{6}\left[(1 \times 10^{-4})(2) + (3 \times 10^{-2})(3) + (1 \times 10^{-5})(1)\right]$$

$$k_H = \frac{1}{6}\left[2 \times 10^{-4} + 9 \times 10^{-2} + 1 \times 10^{-5}\right]$$

$$k_H = \frac{1}{6}\left[0.0002 + 0.09 + 0.00001\right] = \frac{0.09021}{6} = 0.015035 \text{ cm/s}$$

$$k_H \approx 1.504 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm/s}$$

**Vertical permeability (harmonic mean):**

$$\frac{1}{k_V} = \frac{1}{6}\left[\frac{2}{1 \times 10^{-4}} + \frac{3}{3 \times 10^{-2}} + \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-5}}\right]$$

$$\frac{1}{k_V} = \frac{1}{6}\left[2 \times 10^{4} + 100 + 1 \times 10^{5}\right]$$

$$\frac{1}{k_V} = \frac{1}{6}\left[20000 + 100 + 100000\right] = \frac{120100}{6} = 20016.67 \text{ s/cm}$$

$$k_V = \frac{1}{20016.67} \approx 4.995 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm/s}$$

**Verification of inequality:** `k_V (4.995 × 10⁻⁵) << k_H (1.504 × 10⁻²)` ✓ The relationship `k_V ≤ k_H` is satisfied, and the dominance of the sand layer (Layer 2) in horizontal flow versus the clay layer (Layer 3) in vertical flow is clearly demonstrated.

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Objectives of Compaction (Conceptual Framework)

The objectives are not independent — they are all *corollaries* of one physical fact: **compaction reduces void ratio (e), which improves almost every engineering property.**

**Step 1 — Density ↑:** Mechanical energy rearranges grains, expelling air voids.

$$\rho_d \uparrow \quad \Rightarrow \quad e \downarrow \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \downarrow$$

**Step 2 — Shear strength ↑:** From Terzaghi's shear strength equation:

$$\tau_f = c' + \sigma' \tan \phi'$$

Densification increases effective inter-particle contact stresses and apparent cohesion, raising `τ_f`.

**Step 3 — Compressibility ↓:** From the compression index relation:

$$\Delta e = -C_c \cdot \log\left(\frac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta \sigma'}{\sigma'_0}\right)$$

A denser initial fabric yields a smaller compression index `C_c` and therefore smaller settlement.

**Step 4 — Permeability ↓:** Kozeny-Carman equation shows:

$$k \propto \frac{e^3}{1+e}$$

Reducing `e` dramatically lowers `k`, especially in fine-grained soils.

**Step 5 — Bearing capacity ↑:** Terzaghi's equation for general shear:

$$q_u = 1.3 \, c \, N_c + q \, N_q + 0.4 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$$

Increased `c` and `γ` from compaction raise `q_u`.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stratified Soil & Flow Directions

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of flow regimes** in a stratified deposit, mapping how water interacts with each layer depending on flow direction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STRATIFIED SOIL DEPOSIT"] --> B["Layer 1: Top Stratum"]
    A --> C["Layer 2: Middle Stratum"]
    A --> D["Layer 3: Bottom Stratum"]

    B --> B1["Thickness H1 | Permeability k1"]
    C --> C1["Thickness H2 | Permeability k2"]
    D --> D1["Thickness H3 | Permeability k3"]

    A --> E["FLOW DIRECTION DECISION"]
    E -->|Parallel to Bedding| F["HORIZONTAL FLOW CASE kH"]
    E -->|Perpendicular to Bedding| G["VERTICAL FLOW CASE kV"]

    F --> F1["Same hydraulic gradient i in all layers"]
    F1 --> F2["Discharge q = sum of qi across layers"]
    F2 --> F3["kH = Arithmetic Mean = Sum ki Hi / Sum Hi"]

    G --> G1["Same discharge q through all layers"]
    G1 --> G2["Head loss h = sum of hi across layers"]
    G2 --> G3["kV = Harmonic Mean = H / Sum Hi/ki"]

    F3 --> H["Inequality Check: kV is less than or equal to kH"]
    G3 --> H

    H --> I["Engineering Application: Earth Dams, Landfill Liners, Pavement Subgrades"]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Flow Representation — Two-Layer Case

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PARA["Case A: Parallel Flow kH"]
        A1["Inflow i"] --> A2["Layer 1: k1, H1"]
        A2 --> A3["Layer 2: k2, H2"]
        A3 --> A4["Outflow"]
        A2 -.->|"Same gradient i"| A3
    end

    subgraph PERP["Case B: Perpendicular Flow kV"]
        B1["Inflow q"] --> B2["Layer 1: k1, H1, head h1"]
        B2 --> B3["Layer 2: k2, H2, head h2"]
        B3 --> B4["Outflow q"]
        B2 -.->|"Same discharge q"| B3
    end
```

### 4.3 Compaction Process Flow — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["LOOSE SOIL DEPOSIT"] --> P2["APPLICATION OF MECHANICAL ENERGY"]
    P2 --> P3["ROLLER / RAMMER / VIBRATOR"]
    P3 --> P4["PARTICLE REARRANGEMENT"]
    P4 --> P5["EXPULSION OF AIR VOIDS"]
    P5 --> P6["DENSER SOIL MASS"]
    P6 --> Q1["Void Ratio e DECREASES"]
    P6 --> Q2["Dry Density rho_d INCREASES"]
    P6 --> Q3["Shear Strength tau_f INCREASES"]
    P6 --> Q4["Permeability k DECREASES"]
    P6 --> Q5["Compressibility Cc DECREASES"]
    P6 --> Q6["Bearing Capacity q_u INCREASES"]

    Q1 --> R["STABLE ENGINEERED EARTH STRUCTURE"]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R
    Q5 --> R
    Q6 --> R
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define stratified soil. Explain why the average permeability of a stratified deposit differs for horizontal and vertical flow.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **stratified soil** is a natural or engineered deposit composed of two or more distinct horizontal layers, each having its own hydraulic conductivity (permeability) and thickness.

The average permeability differs for horizontal and vertical flow because:

- **For horizontal (parallel) flow:** the hydraulic gradient is the same across all layers, but the discharge differs in each layer. The composite permeability is a **weighted arithmetic mean**:
$$k_H = \frac{\sum k_i H_i}{\sum H_i}$$

- **For vertical (perpendicular) flow:** the discharge is the same across all layers, but the head loss differs in each layer. The composite permeability is a **weighted harmonic mean**:
$$k_V = \frac{\sum H_i}{\sum (H_i / k_i)}$$

Since the arithmetic mean of positive numbers is always greater than or equal to their harmonic mean, **`k_H ≥ k_V`** for any stratified deposit.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for horizontal reasoning, 1 mark for vertical reasoning + inequality.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

State any **six** objectives of compaction of soil.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The objectives of compaction are:

1. To **increase the shear strength** of the soil, improving slope and embankment stability.
2. To **reduce compressibility**, thereby minimising settlement under loads.
3. To **decrease permeability**, particularly in clay liners for water-retaining structures.
4. To **increase bearing capacity** of foundations and pavements.
5. To **control swelling and shrinkage** behaviour of expansive soils.
6. To provide **uniform density** and stability to earth structures.
7. To **reduce frost susceptibility** by eliminating large interconnected voids.

*(Any six objectives — ½ mark each.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Part B questions carry internal choice. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

A stratified soil deposit consists of **three horizontal layers** with the following properties:

| Layer | Thickness (m) | Permeability (cm/s) |
|-------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 (top) | 4 | `4 × 10⁻³` |
| 2 (middle) | 6 | `2 × 10⁻⁵` |
| 3 (bottom) | 2 | `6 × 10⁻²` |

**(a)** Determine the average coefficient of permeability for flow **parallel** to the stratification. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Determine the average coefficient of permeability for flow **perpendicular** to the stratification and comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Question A

**Given Data:**
- Layer 1: `H₁ = 4 m`, `k₁ = 4 × 10⁻³ cm/s`
- Layer 2: `H₂ = 6 m`, `k₂ = 2 × 10⁻⁵ cm/s`
- Layer 3: `H₃ = 2 m`, `k₃ = 6 × 10⁻² cm/s`
- Total thickness: `H = H₁ + H₂ + H₃ = 4 + 6 + 2 = 12 m`

---

**Part (a) — Parallel (Horizontal) Flow — 7 Marks**

Using the arithmetic mean formula:

$$k_H = \frac{k_1 H_1 + k_2 H_2 + k_3 H_3}{H_1 + H_2 + H_3}$$

Substituting values:

$$k_H = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-3})(4) + (2 \times 10^{-5})(6) + (6 \times 10^{-2})(2)}{12}$$

Computing each numerator term:

$$= \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-2}) + (1.2 \times 10^{-4}) + (1.2 \times 10^{-1})}{12}$$

$$= \frac{0.016 + 0.00012 + 0.12}{12} = \frac{0.13612}{12}$$

$$\boxed{k_H = 0.011343 \text{ cm/s} \approx 1.134 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm/s}}$$

**Mark Distribution (a):**
- Formula: 2 marks
- Substitution: 2 marks
- Computation: 2 marks
- Final answer with units: 1 mark

---

**Part (b) — Perpendicular (Vertical) Flow — 7 Marks**

Using the harmonic mean formula:

$$\frac{1}{k_V} = \frac{1}{H}\left[\frac{H_1}{k_1} + \frac{H_2}{k_2} + \frac{H_3}{k_3}\right]$$

$$\frac{1}{k_V} = \frac{1}{12}\left[\frac{4}{4 \times 10^{-3}} + \frac{6}{2 \times 10^{-5}} + \frac{2}{6 \times 10^{-2}}\right]$$

Computing each fraction:

$$= \frac{1}{12}\left[1000 + 300000 + 33.33\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{12}\left[301033.33\right] = 25086.11 \text{ s/cm}$$

$$k_V = \frac{1}{25086.11}$$

$$\boxed{k_V = 3.986 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm/s} \approx 3.99 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm/s}}$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
- `k_H ≈ 1.134 × 10⁻² cm/s` vs. `k_V ≈ 3.99 × 10⁻⁵ cm/s`
- The ratio `k_H/k_V ≈ 284`, demonstrating extreme anisotropy.
- The middle clay layer (`k₂ = 2 × 10⁻⁵ cm/s`) governs the vertical flow, choking seepage, while the bottom sand (`k₃ = 6 × 10⁻² cm/s`) dominates horizontal flow.
- This confirms the general inequality `k_V ≤ k_H`.

**Mark Distribution (b):**
- Formula: 2 marks
- Substitution: 2 marks
- Computation: 2 marks
- Comment / discussion: 1 mark

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023 — Alternative Set]**

A stratified soil deposit has **two horizontal layers**:
- Layer 1 (sandy): `H₁ = 5 m`, `k₁ = 1 × 10⁻³ cm/s`
- Layer 2 (clayey): `H₂ = 3 m`, `k₂ = 1 × 10⁻⁶ cm/s`

**(a)** Compute the equivalent horizontal permeability `k_H`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If a head difference of **3 m** is applied over a horizontal length of **50 m** (parallel flow), compute the discharge per metre width of the deposit. Also state why such deposits are difficult to dewater using vertical wells alone. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Question B

**Given:**
- `H₁ = 5 m`, `k₁ = 1 × 10⁻³ cm/s`
- `H₂ = 3 m`, `k₂ = 1 × 10⁻⁶ cm/s`
- `H = 8 m`
- Head difference `h = 3 m`, Length `L = 50 m`

---

**Part (a) — Equivalent Horizontal Permeability — 7 Marks**

$$k_H = \frac{k_1 H_1 + k_2 H_2}{H_1 + H_2} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-3})(5) + (1 \times 10^{-6})(3)}{8}$$

$$= \frac{5 \times 10^{-3} + 3 \times 10^{-6}}{8} = \frac{0.005003}{8}$$

$$\boxed{k_H = 6.254 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm/s} \approx 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm/s}}$$

*(Note: the clay layer's contribution to kH is negligible because of its very low k₂.)*

**Mark Distribution (a):** Formula (2) + Substitution (2) + Computation (2) + Answer (1).

---

**Part (b) — Discharge Computation & Concept — 7 Marks**

Hydraulic gradient:

$$i = \frac{h}{L} = \frac{3}{50} = 0.06$$

Convert `k_H` to m/s for unit consistency:

$$k_H = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm/s} = 6.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}$$

Discharge per unit width using Darcy's law:

$$q = k_H \cdot i \cdot H$$

$$q = (6.25 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.06 \times 8$$

$$\boxed{q = 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s per metre width}}$$

**Why vertical wells fail (2 marks):**

Vertical dewatering wells extract water by establishing a vertical hydraulic gradient. In this stratified deposit, the **clay layer has extremely low vertical permeability** (≈ `1 × 10⁻⁶ cm/s`). Vertical flow is therefore throttled by the clay, and water from the underlying sand cannot easily rise to the well. The discharge through the clay layer for vertical flow would be:

$$k_V = \frac{H_1 + H_2}{\dfrac{H_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{H_2}{k_2}} = \frac{8}{\dfrac{5}{10^{-3}} + \dfrac{3}{10^{-6}}} = \frac{8}{5000 + 3 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{8}{3.005 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$k_V \approx 2.66 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm/s} = 2.66 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m/s}$$

So `k_V << k_H`, confirming that vertical wells will be largely ineffective — horizontal drains (or sand drains / prefabricated vertical drains) must be used instead.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting between cm/s and m/s is the *#1 source of mark loss* in KTU board exams. Always state units at every step.
> 2. **Formula Mix-up:** Students frequently apply the **horizontal** formula for **vertical** flow (or vice-versa). Memorise: *parallel → arithmetic mean*; *perpendicular → harmonic mean*.
> 3. **Total Thickness:** The denominator of `k_H` is `H = Σ H_i`, not just `H₁ + H₂`. Missing the third layer in three-layer problems costs **3 full marks**.
> 4. **Final Comment:** Examiners explicitly award **1 mark** for the concluding remark on the inequality `k_V ≤ k_H`. Skipping this is a common oversight.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Stratified Soils & Compaction):**

- ✅ **Stratified Soil:** Horizontal layers of differing permeability; treated as homogeneous within each layer.
- ✅ **Average Permeability:** A single equivalent `k` representing the whole stratified mass.
- ✅ **Horizontal (Parallel) Flow:** Same gradient `i`, different discharge → **weighted arithmetic mean** → `k_H = Σ(k_i H_i) / Σ H_i`.
- ✅ **Vertical (Perpendicular) Flow:** Same discharge `q`, different head loss → **weighted harmonic mean** → `k_V = Σ H_i / Σ(H_i / k_i)`.
- ✅ **Inequality Theorem:** Always `k_V ≤ k_H` (equality only if all `k_i` are equal).
- ✅ **Two-Layer Special Case:** Most commonly tested in KTU exams — memorise both formulas explicitly.
- ✅ **Dominance Principle:** Horizontal flow is controlled by the *most permeable* layer; vertical flow is controlled by the *least permeable* layer.
- ✅ **Compaction Definition:** Mechanical densification with reduction in air voids at *constant water content*.
- ✅ **Compaction ≠ Consolidation:** Compaction is instantaneous (air expulsion); consolidation is time-dependent (water expulsion).
- ✅ **Compaction Objectives (Mnemonic — *S-S-P-B-C-S*):** Shear strength ↑, Settlement ↓, Permeability ↓, Bearing capacity ↑, Compressibility ↓, Swelling control.
- ✅ **Engineering Utilities:** Earth dams, landfill liners, pavements, embankments — all rely on the principles above.
- ✅ **Common Exam Numbers to Remember:** `k_H ≥ k_V`; harmonic mean ≤ arithmetic mean; ratio `k_H/k_V` is the *anisotropy index*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard and Modified Proctor Tests - Concept of OMC and Maximum Dry Density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standard and Modified Proctor Tests - Concept of OMC and Maximum Dry Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Compaction** is the process of increasing the density of soil by mechanical means (static, vibratory, or impact) by expelling air from the voids without significantly changing the water content. The compaction characteristics of a soil are determined in the laboratory by **Proctor's Compaction Test** (Standard / Modified), governed by **IS 2720 (Part 7) – 1980 (Reaffirmed 2021)** for Light Compaction and **IS 2720 (Part 8) – 1983 (Reaffirmed 2020)** for Heavy Compaction.

The test establishes two engineering parameters:
- **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC)** – the moisture content at which a soil attains its **Maximum Dry Density (MDD)** for a given compactive effort.
- **Maximum Dry Density (γd,max or MDD)** – the highest dry unit weight achievable under a specified compaction energy.

### 1.2 The Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Sponge & the Towel Analogy**
>
> Imagine you have a dry kitchen sponge. If you try to press it with your hand, it does not flatten much because the hollow pores are full of air. Now dip the sponge in water and squeeze it out until it is **just damp** — at this exact "perfect dampness", the water acts like a microscopic lubricant that allows the sponge particles to slide and pack tightly. Press it now, and it becomes a thin, dense disc. If you add *too much* water, the sponge becomes slippery, the water itself occupies space, and you cannot compress it further.
>
> **The dampness = OMC. The thin dense disc = MDD.** The hand-pressing = Compactive Effort (Standard or Modified).

This is exactly what happens in soil. A little water lubricates grains, allowing them to pack densely. Too much water fills the voids, preventing further densification. The **compactive effort** is the energy you put in (the "hand press" — which can be a light hand or a heavy machine).

### 1.3 Standard vs Modified Proctor at a Glance

| Parameter | **Standard Proctor Test (SPT)** | **Modified Proctor Test (MPT)** |
|---|---|---|
| IS Code | IS 2720 (Part 7) | IS 2720 (Part 8) |
| Rammer Mass | **2.5 kg** | **4.9 kg** |
| Drop Height | **300 mm** | **450 mm** |
| Number of Layers | 3 | 5 |
| Blows per Layer | 25 | 25 |
| Mould Volume | 1000 cc (small) / 2250 cc (large) | 1000 cc (small) / 2250 cc (large) |
| Compactive Energy | ~ 595 kJ/m³ | ~ 2675 kJ/m³ |
| Typical MDD | Lower | Higher (≈ 5–15% higher) |
| Typical OMC | Higher | Lower |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modified Proctor** was developed during **World War II** by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers because Standard Proctor could not replicate the dense fields produced by modern heavy rollers. Modern highway and airfield specifications **mandate Modified Proctor**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants Used

- **Density of water, ρw = 1.0 g/cc = 1000 kg/m³**
- **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Unit weight of water, γw = 9.81 kN/m³** (often taken as 10 kN/m³ in practice)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theory of Compaction — Why OMC Exists

The compaction curve (dry density vs moisture content) shows a **unimodal peak**. This is governed by **two opposing physical phenomena**:

1. **Lubrication Effect (Rising limb of the curve):**
   As moisture is added, water films coat soil grains. Surface tension and viscosity reduce inter-particle friction, allowing grains to slide into denser packing. Hence γd increases.

2. **Displacement Effect (Falling limb of the curve):**
   Beyond a certain point, additional water occupies the void space that *could* have been filled by soil grains. Water is incompressible and cannot be expelled easily. The dry density falls.

3. **Peak Point:**
   The moisture at which these two effects are optimally balanced is the **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC)**, and the corresponding density is the **Maximum Dry Density (MDD)**.

### 2.2 The Three Crucial Lines on a Compaction Graph

- **Compaction Curve** — Plot of γd (y-axis) vs w (x-axis), the actual experimental curve.
- **Zero Air Voids Line (ZAV) / Saturation Line** — Theoretical curve when **Sr = 100%** (no air in voids). It lies *above* the compaction curve and acts as an upper bound. No experimental point should ever cross it.
- **Line of Optimums** — Connects peaks of compaction curves for varying compactive efforts.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **KTU Board-favourite formulas**. Memorize the table — questions of **3, 7, and 14 marks** are set directly from these.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma_{bulk} = \dfrac{M_{wet}}{V}$ | Bulk (wet) density | g/cc or kN/m³ |
| 2 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{\gamma_{bulk}}{1 + w}$ | Dry density (from wet density and water content) | g/cc or kN/m³ |
| 3 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$ | Dry density (from specific gravity and void ratio) | g/cc or kN/m³ |
| 4 | $S_r \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ | Phase-relation fundamental | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\gamma_{d,zav} = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$ | Zero Air Voids Dry Density (Saturation line) | g/cc or kN/m³ |
| 6 | $E = \dfrac{N_b \cdot N_l \cdot W \cdot h}{V}$ | Compactive Energy per unit volume | kJ/m³ |
| 7 | $R_c = \dfrac{\gamma_{d,field}}{\gamma_{d,max,lab}} \times 100$ | Relative Compaction (Field Control) | % |

> **Where:**
> - $M_{wet}$ = Mass of wet soil in mould (g)
> - $V$ = Volume of mould (cc)
> - $w$ = Water content (decimal or %)
> - $G_s$ = Specific gravity of soil solids
> - $e$ = Void ratio
> - $N_b$ = Number of blows per layer
> - $N_l$ = Number of layers
> - $W$ = Weight of rammer (N or kg)
> - $h$ = Height of fall (m)
> - $R_c$ = Relative Compaction

### 2.4 Derivation of the Zero Air Voids Line (Important for KTU Board)

Starting from the phase relations:

$$S_r = \dfrac{w \cdot G_s}{e}$$

At zero air voids, $S_r = 1$ (or 100%), therefore:

$$e = w \cdot G_s$$

Substitute into the dry density formula:

$$\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e} = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$$

This is the equation of the ZAV line. At $w = 0$, $\gamma_{d,zav} = G_s \cdot \gamma_w$ (the theoretical maximum at zero water), and as $w$ increases, $\gamma_{d,zav}$ decreases hyperbolically.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Earthen Dam Construction:** Field compaction is targeted at OMC ± 2% to achieve near-MDD density, ensuring stability and low permeability.
- **Highway Embankments & Runways:** Modified Proctor OMC/MDD is used. Specification is **95–98% relative compaction** (relative to MPT).
- **Quality Control:** A Sand Cone or Nuclear Density Gauge is used in the field; the field γd is compared to lab MDD.
- **Pre-loading & Ground Improvement:** Compaction pre-consolidates loose fills, reducing future settlement.

> **Engineering Insight:** A higher compactive effort (MPT) shifts the compaction curve **upwards and to the left** — i.e., higher MDD at lower OMC. This is critical when selecting specifications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Bulk Density to Dry Density

**Given:** Mass of empty mould $M_1$, mass of mould + wet compacted soil $M_2$, volume of mould $V$, water content of compacted soil $w$ (decimal).

**Step 1 — Mass of wet compacted soil:**

$$M_{wet} = M_2 - M_1$$

**Step 2 — Bulk (wet) density:**

$$\gamma_{bulk} = \dfrac{M_{wet}}{V} = \dfrac{M_2 - M_1}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to dry density:**

The dry mass is related to wet mass via water content $w$:

$$M_{dry} = \dfrac{M_{wet}}{1 + w}$$

Therefore:

$$\gamma_d = \dfrac{M_{dry}}{V} = \dfrac{M_{wet}}{V(1 + w)} = \dfrac{\gamma_{bulk}}{1 + w}$$

**Logic Explanation:** The bulk density contains the weight of both solids and water. To isolate the density of the soil *solids alone* (which is the true indicator of compaction), we divide by $(1+w)$, since the wet mass is $(1+w)$ times the dry mass.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical — Finding OMC and MDD from a Compaction Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a classic **14-mark KTU Board question pattern**. Master this numerical.

A Standard Proctor test was conducted on a soil sample using a 1000 cc mould. The following observations were recorded. Determine OMC and MDD.

| Trial No. | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mass of empty mould, $M_1$ (g) | 1800 | 1800 | 1800 | 1800 | 1800 |
| Mass of mould + wet soil, $M_2$ (g) | 3370 | 3460 | 3525 | 3515 | 3470 |
| Water content, $w$ (%) | 6.0 | 9.0 | 12.0 | 15.0 | 18.0 |

**Step 1 — Calculate Mass of Wet Soil ($M_{wet}$):**

$$M_{wet} = M_2 - M_1$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Trial 1: } M_{wet} &= 3370 - 1800 = 1570 \text{ g} \\
\text{Trial 2: } M_{wet} &= 3460 - 1800 = 1660 \text{ g} \\
\text{Trial 3: } M_{wet} &= 3525 - 1800 = 1725 \text{ g} \\
\text{Trial 4: } M_{wet} &= 3515 - 1800 = 1715 \text{ g} \\
\text{Trial 5: } M_{wet} &= 3470 - 1800 = 1670 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Bulk Density ($\gamma_{bulk}$):**

Since $V = 1000$ cc, $\gamma_{bulk} = M_{wet}/1000$ (in g/cc):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{bulk,1} &= 1.570 \text{ g/cc} \\
\gamma_{bulk,2} &= 1.660 \text{ g/cc} \\
\gamma_{bulk,3} &= 1.725 \text{ g/cc} \\
\gamma_{bulk,4} &= 1.715 \text{ g/cc} \\
\gamma_{bulk,5} &= 1.670 \text{ g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Dry Density ($\gamma_d$):**

Using $\gamma_d = \dfrac{\gamma_{bulk}}{1 + w}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{d,1} &= \dfrac{1.570}{1 + 0.060} = \dfrac{1.570}{1.060} = 1.481 \text{ g/cc} \\
\gamma_{d,2} &= \dfrac{1.660}{1 + 0.090} = \dfrac{1.660}{1.090} = 1.523 \text{ g/cc} \\
\gamma_{d,3} &= \dfrac{1.725}{1 + 0.120} = \dfrac{1.725}{1.120} = 1.540 \text{ g/cc} \\
\gamma_{d,4} &= \dfrac{1.715}{1 + 0.150} = \dfrac{1.715}{1.150} = 1.491 \text{ g/cc} \\
\gamma_{d,5} &= \dfrac{1.670}{1 + 0.180} = \dfrac{1.670}{1.180} = 1.415 \text{ g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Tabulate and Identify Peak:**

| Trial | $w$ (%) | $\gamma_d$ (g/cc) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6.0 | 1.481 |
| 2 | 9.0 | 1.523 |
| 3 | 12.0 | **1.540 ← MDD** |
| 4 | 15.0 | 1.491 |
| 5 | 18.0 | 1.415 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:**
> - **Maximum Dry Density, MDD = 1.540 g/cc = 15.40 kN/m³**
> - **Optimum Moisture Content, OMC = 12.0 %**

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Key Parameters on OMC and MDD

| Parameter Changed | Effect on OMC | Effect on MDD | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Increase compactive effort** | Decreases | Increases | More energy → more packing at lower lubrication need |
| **Increase in soil plasticity (more clay)** | Increases | Decreases | Clays need more water for lubrication; they are inherently compressible |
| **Coarse-grained soil (sand/gravel)** | Lower OMC | Higher MDD | Grains interlock easily, less water needed |
| **Heavy clay (CH)** | High OMC (20–30%) | Low MDD (1.3–1.5) | High surface area, adsorptive water films |
| **Addition of fines to coarse soil** | Increases | Decreases initially | Fines disrupt grain-to-grain contact |
| **Undercompaction in field** | — | Drastically lower | Poor quality control |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Proctor Compaction Test

The following block diagram maps the complete operational flow of a Standard / Modified Proctor test in the lab, highlighting the **inputs**, **process blocks**, and **outputs** as required by IS 2720.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Soil Sample Air Dried and Pulverized] --> B[Pass through 4.75 mm IS Sieve]
    B --> C[Take 5 Sub-samples of ~ 2.5 kg each]
    C --> D[Add Varying Water Content 4% to 16% w in Steps]
    D --> E[Compact in Standard Mould 1000 cc]
    E --> F[3 Layers for SPT / 5 Layers for MPT]
    F --> G[25 Blows per Layer with Rammer]
    G --> H[Trim Mould Flush to Top Edge]
    H --> I[Weigh Mould + Wet Soil M2]
    I --> J[Subtract Empty Mould Mass M1]
    J --> K[Compute Bulk Density gamma bulk]
    K --> L[Take Soil Sample for Oven Drying 24 hrs]
    L --> M[Determine Water Content w]
    M --> N[Compute Dry Density gamma d]
    N --> O[Plot gamma d vs w Curve]
    O --> P[Identify Peak Point]
    P --> Q[OMC = x-coordinate of Peak]
    P --> R[MDD = y-coordinate of Peak]
    R --> S[Draw Zero Air Voids Line]
    S --> T[Final Report with OMC and MDD]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style E fill:#fff4e1
    style K fill:#e8f5e9
    style N fill:#e8f5e9
    style P fill:#ffcccc
    style Q fill:#c8e6c9
    style R fill:#c8e6c9
```

```mermaid

    subgraph ASSEMBLY[Compaction Mould Assembly IS 2720]
        A1[Base Plate] --> A2[Cylindrical Mould 100 mm dia x 127.3 mm height]
        A2 --> A3[Collar 60 mm height]
        A3 --> A4[Rammer 2.5 kg SPT or 4.9 kg MPT]
        A4 --> A5[Drop Height 300 mm SPT or 450 mm MPT]
    end
    A2 --- A6[Soil Compacted in 3 layers SPT or 5 layers MPT]

```

### 4.3 Compaction Curve — Concept Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Water Content w increasing] --> Y[Dry Density gamma d Response]
    Y --> Y1[Low w: grains stuck, high friction - LOW gamma d]
    Y --> Y2[Optimal w: lubrication helps packing - PEAK gamma d]
    Y --> Y3[Excess w: water fills voids - gamma d FALLS]
    Y1 --- P1[Ascending Limb]
    Y2 --- P2[PEAK = OMC, MDD]
    Y3 --- P3[Descending Limb]

```

    Q1 -->|Highway, Runway, Dam| Q2[Use Modified Proctor Test]
    Q1 -->|Small Building, Low Traffic Road| Q3[Use Standard Proctor Test]
    Q1 -->|Both Comparative Study Needed| Q4[Perform Both SPT and MPT]
    Q2 --> END[Report OMC and MDD as per IS 2720 Part 8]
    Q3 --> END2[Report OMC and MDD as per IS 2720 Part 7]
    Q4 --> END3[Plot Both Curves - MPT will be Higher and Left of SPT]
