<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set A]**

**Q1. Define Optimum Moisture Content (OMC) and Maximum Dry Density (MDD). What is the practical significance of these parameters in earthwork construction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**OMC:** Optimum Moisture Content is the moisture content at which a soil attains its **Maximum Dry Density (MDD)** for a given compactive effort in a Proctor's compaction test. Below OMC, water is insufficient to lubricate grains; above OMC, water displaces soil particles in the voids.

**MDD:** Maximum Dry Density is the **highest dry unit weight** of the soil achieved at OMC under a specified compaction energy. It is computed as $\gamma_{d,max} = \gamma_{bulk,max} / (1 + w_{OMC})$.

**Practical Significance:**
- Field compaction is controlled by targeting a moisture content within **OMC ± 2%**.
- Required **relative compaction** $R_c = \gamma_{d,field} / \gamma_{d,lab} \times 100$ is typically specified (e.g., 95% for highways).
- Ensures adequate **shear strength**, **low compressibility**, and **low permeability** in earthen structures such as embankments, dams, and subgrades.
- Used to specify **roller type, passes, and lift thickness** in specifications.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Distinguish between Standard Proctor Test and Modified Proctor Test. List at least four points of difference.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S. No. | Aspect | Standard Proctor Test (SPT) | Modified Proctor Test (MPT) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | IS Code | IS 2720 (Part 7) | IS 2720 (Part 8) |
| 2 | Rammer Weight | 2.5 kg (Light) | 4.9 kg (Heavy) |
| 3 | Drop Height | 300 mm | 450 mm |
| 4 | Number of Layers | 3 | 5 |
| 5 | Compactive Energy | ~ 595 kJ/m³ | ~ 2675 kJ/m³ |
| 6 | MDD obtained | Lower | Higher |
| 7 | OMC obtained | Higher | Lower |
| 8 | Application | Small fills, embankments | Highways, airfields, heavy fills |

(Any four points carry full 3 marks.)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme, students answer ONE question out of two. Both options given below are KTU-pattern.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the procedure for conducting the Standard Proctor Test as per IS 2720 (Part 7). List the apparatus required.

**(b) [7 marks]** During a Standard Proctor Test on a soil sample, the following data were obtained for a 1000 cc mould (mass of empty mould = 1920 g):

| Trial | Mass of mould + wet soil (g) | Water content (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3540 | 5.2 |
| 2 | 3640 | 8.0 |
| 3 | 3710 | 11.0 |
| 4 | 3725 | 14.0 |
| 5 | 3690 | 17.0 |

Determine the **OMC** and **MDD** of the soil. Also, draw the Zero Air Voids line if $G_s = 2.70$.

---

### **Model Solution for Q1(a) — 7 Marks**

**Apparatus Required (2 marks):**
1. Cylindrical mould — 1000 cc capacity, internal diameter 100 mm, height 127.3 mm, with detachable collar and base plate.
2. Rammer — 2.5 kg mass, 50 mm dia face, 300 mm drop.
3. Balance — accuracy 1 g.
4. Oven — thermostatically controlled at 105–110°C.
5. IS Sieve 4.75 mm.
6. Steel straight edge, mixing tray, moisture containers, desiccator.
7. Graduated cylinder / measuring jar for water.

**Procedure (5 marks):**
1. Take **~ 20 kg** of air-dried soil, pulverize and sieve through 4.75 mm.
2. Divide into **5 equal portions** (~ 2.5 kg each).
3. Add **varying water content** to each portion in increments of 2–4% (typically 4% to 16%).
4. Fill the mould in **3 equal layers**.
5. Compact each layer with **25 uniformly distributed blows** of the 2.5 kg rammer dropped from 300 mm.
6. After compaction of the third layer, remove the collar and **trim the surface flush** with a straight edge.
7. Weigh the mould + compacted wet soil ($M_2$).
8. **Eject** the soil, take a representative sample from the middle for **water content** determination.
9. Repeat for all five trials with increasing water content.
10. Compute $\gamma_{bulk}$ and $\gamma_d$ for each trial; plot $\gamma_d$ vs $w$ curve; identify the peak.

---

### **Model Solution for Q1(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Mass of wet soil and bulk density:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Trial 1: } M_{wet} &= 3540 - 1920 = 1620 \text{ g}; \quad \gamma_{bulk} = 1.620 \text{ g/cc} \\
\text{Trial 2: } M_{wet} &= 3640 - 1920 = 1720 \text{ g}; \quad \gamma_{bulk} = 1.720 \text{ g/cc} \\
\text{Trial 3: } M_{wet} &= 3710 - 1920 = 1790 \text{ g}; \quad \gamma_{bulk} = 1.790 \text{ g/cc} \\
\text{Trial 4: } M_{wet} &= 3725 - 1920 = 1805 \text{ g}; \quad \gamma_{bulk} = 1.805 \text{ g/cc} \\
\text{Trial 5: } M_{wet} &= 3690 - 1920 = 1770 \text{ g}; \quad \gamma_{bulk} = 1.770 \text{ g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Dry density using $\gamma_d = \gamma_{bulk}/(1+w)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{d,1} &= 1.620 / 1.052 = 1.540 \text{ g/cc} \\
\gamma_{d,2} &= 1.720 / 1.080 = 1.593 \text{ g/cc} \\
\gamma_{d,3} &= 1.790 / 1.110 = 1.613 \text{ g/cc} \\
\gamma_{d,4} &= 1.805 / 1.140 = 1.583 \text{ g/cc} \\
\gamma_{d,5} &= 1.770 / 1.170 = 1.513 \text{ g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Tabulate and Identify Peak:**

| Trial | $w$ (%) | $\gamma_d$ (g/cc) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5.2 | 1.540 |
| 2 | 8.0 | 1.593 |
| 3 | 11.0 | **1.613 ← MDD** |
| 4 | 14.0 | 1.583 |
| 5 | 17.0 | 1.513 |

**Step 4: Result:**

- **Maximum Dry Density, MDD = 1.613 g/cc ≈ 15.82 kN/m³**
- **Optimum Moisture Content, OMC ≈ 11.0 %**

**Step 5: Zero Air Voids (ZAV) line at selected water contents:**

Using $\gamma_{d,zav} = G_s \cdot \gamma_w / (1 + w \cdot G_s)$ with $G_s = 2.70$ and $\gamma_w = 1$ g/cc:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } w = 5\%: \quad \gamma_{d,zav} &= 2.70 / (1 + 0.05 \times 2.70) = 2.70 / 1.135 = 2.379 \text{ g/cc} \\
\text{At } w = 8\%: \quad \gamma_{d,zav} &= 2.70 / (1 + 0.08 \times 2.70) = 2.70 / 1.216 = 2.220 \text{ g/cc} \\
\text{At } w = 11\%: \quad \gamma_{d,zav} &= 2.70 / (1 + 0.11 \times 2.70) = 2.70 / 1.297 = 2.082 \text{ g/cc} \\
\text{At } w = 14\%: \quad \gamma_{d,zav} &= 2.70 / (1 + 0.14 \times 2.70) = 2.70 / 1.378 = 1.959 \text{ g/cc} \\
\text{At } w = 17\%: \quad \gamma_{d,zav} &= 2.70 / (1 + 0.17 \times 2.70) = 2.70 / 1.459 = 1.851 \text{ g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

The ZAV line lies **above** the compaction curve, validating the experimental results.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of the **Zero Air Voids (ZAV) line**. Derive its equation. Why does the experimental compaction curve always lie below the ZAV line?

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **factors affecting compaction** of soils. How does Modified Proctor differ in the field-equivalent compaction of modern pavements?

---

### **Model Solution for Q2(a) — 7 Marks**

**Concept (2 marks):**
The Zero Air Voids (ZAV) line, also called the **Saturation Line** or **100% Saturation Line**, represents the theoretical relationship between dry density and water content when the soil is **fully saturated (Sr = 100%)**, i.e., no air remains in the voids. It is the **upper boundary** of all possible compaction states for a given soil (a soil cannot be denser than its saturated state).

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from the phase relation:

$$S_r = \dfrac{w \cdot G_s}{e}$$

At zero air voids, $S_r = 1$:

$$e = w \cdot G_s$$

Dry density in terms of $G_s$ and $e$:

$$\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$$

Substituting $e = w G_s$:

$$\boxed{\gamma_{d,zav} = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}}$$

**Why compaction curve lies below ZAV (2 marks):**
During Proctor compaction, air is **expelled but never fully removed**. A small amount of air always remains trapped in the voids ($S_r < 100\%$). Since dry density is inversely related to void ratio, the presence of this entrapped air increases $e$ above the theoretical minimum, lowering $\gamma_d$ below the ZAV value. Physically, the ZAV corresponds to a state requiring complete expulsion of air — which Proctor's mechanical compaction cannot achieve, although it can be approached by kneading or vibratory methods.

---

### **Model Solution for Q2(b) — 7 Marks**

**Factors Affecting Compaction (5 marks):**

1. **Water Content:** Dominant factor. Governs the lubrication/displacement balance — peak at OMC.
2. **Compactive Effort:** Higher energy → higher MDD at lower OMC. MPT > SPT.
3. **Soil Type / Gradation:** Well-graded soils compact to higher densities than poorly graded or uniform soils.
4. **Plasticity of Fines:** Highly plastic clays have higher OMC and lower MDD; sands have low OMC and high MDD.
5. **Specific Gravity (Gs):** Heavier solids → higher achievable MDD.
6. **Method of Compaction:** Impact (Proctor), kneading, static, vibratory — each produces a different curve.
7. **Layer Thickness in Field:** Thinner lifts → more uniform density.

**MPT in Modern Pavements (2 marks):**
Modern highway and airfield construction uses **heavy vibratory rollers and pneumatic-tired rollers** capable of delivering compactive effort close to the Modified Proctor level. Specifications (e.g., **MORTH, IRC, FAA**) require field compaction equivalent to **95–98% of MPT-MDD** at **OMC ± 2%**. This ensures:
- High shear strength of subgrade and base course.
- Reduced long-term settlement (rutting) under traffic.
- Lower permeability of clay liners in embankments.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert water content to decimal** in the formula $\gamma_d = \gamma_{bulk}/(1+w)$. Always use $w$ in decimal (e.g., 12% = 0.12). *(−1 mark penalty)*
> 2. **Mixing up Mould Volume** — Standard mould is 1000 cc, but the large mould used for coarser soils is 2250 cc. Using the wrong $V$ gives a wrong $\gamma_{bulk}$. *(−1 to −2 marks)*
> 3. **Plotting the ZAV line without unit consistency** — if $G_s$ is unitless and $\gamma_w = 1$ g/cc, then $\gamma_{d,zav}$ is in g/cc. Convert to kN/m³ by multiplying by 9.81 only when required. *(−1 mark)*
> 4. **Stating "Modified Proctor is always better"** — this is WRONG. MPT is *not* always appropriate; for moisture-sensitive expansive clays, the MPT may overstress the soil. Explain contextually. *(−1 mark)*
> 5. **Forgetting to identify the peak** clearly with a circle on the graph and a label "OMC, MDD" — examiners reward neat graphs. *(−1 mark)*

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Important Things to Remember (Memory Anchors for KTU Board):**

- **Definition Anchor:** Compaction = mechanical densification by expelling **air** (NOT water).
- **IS Codes to Memorize:** SPT → **IS 2720 Part 7**; MPT → **IS 2720 Part 8**.
- **Numerical Anchor:** SPT uses 2.5 kg, 300 mm, 3 layers, 25 blows; MPT uses 4.9 kg, 450 mm, 5 layers, 25 blows.
- **Core Formula Trio:** (1) $\gamma_{bulk} = M_{wet}/V$; (2) $\gamma_d = \gamma_{bulk}/(1+w)$; (3) $\gamma_{d,zav} = G_s \gamma_w / (1 + w G_s)$.
- **Sign Convention for $w$:** Always enter as a **decimal** in formulas.
- **Position of Curves:** ZAV line is **above** compaction curve. MPT curve is **above and to the left** of SPT curve.
- **Field Control Equation:** $R_c = \gamma_{d,field}/\gamma_{d,lab,max} \times 100\%$. Typical specification = **95% (highways), 98% (airfields)**.
- **Engineering Use:** Earth dams, embankments, subgrades, canal linings, landfill clay liners, railway ballast subgrade.
- **The "Lubrication vs Displacement" Story:** Always explain the OMC peak using these two competing physical effects.
- **Common Confusions to Avoid:**
  - Compaction ≠ Consolidation. (Compaction is *immediate*; consolidation is time-dependent due to water expulsion.)
  - Compaction ≠ Densification by vibration alone — Proctor uses **impact**.
  - Higher compactive effort → **lower OMC**, **higher MDD**.
- **Codal Caution:** Use Modified Proctor for all modern KTU projects unless specifically instructed otherwise. The Board **favours** MPT-based questions.
- **Quick Mnemonic — "3-25-300-2.5"** (SPT): **3** layers, **25** blows, **300** mm drop, **2.5** kg rammer.
- **Quick Mnemonic — "5-25-450-4.9"** (MPT): **5** layers, **25** blows, **450** mm drop, **4.9** kg rammer.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic Notes — Standard and Modified Proctor Tests (Module 2)**
> These notes are tuned to **KTU 2024 Scheme** assessment style and valuation patterns. For numerical practice, redo the worked examples with self-generated data and verify ZAV line position. For conceptual reinforcement, redraw the compaction curve from memory and label OMC, MDD, and ZAV line before every exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zero Air Voids Line
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Zero Air Voids Line

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Zero Air Voids Line (ZAVL)** is a theoretical compaction curve that represents the relationship between the **dry unit weight (γ_d)** and the **water content (w)** of a soil under the hypothetical condition that **all air voids have been completely expelled** from the soil mass. In other words, the ZAVL depicts a state of **100% saturation (S = 1)** where the volume of air, $V_a = 0$, and the void space is entirely filled with water.

Mathematically, the ZAVL is the **upper boundary (theoretical maximum)** that no field compaction curve can ever cross. Any practical compaction curve on a Proctor plot must always lie *on or below* this line, because it is physically impossible to achieve a state of zero air voids through mechanical compaction — some air always remains entrapped between soil grains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Zero Air Voids Line is treated as the **theoretical saturation reference** in compaction theory. It is essential for evaluating the quality of field compaction and understanding the maximum achievable dry density for a given water content.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Sponge Principle:**
> Imagine a dry kitchen sponge (representing soil with air voids). As you slowly pour water onto it, the sponge soaks up water and gets heavier, but its overall volume stays roughly the same. At some point, the sponge becomes **completely saturated** — every single pore (void) is filled with water, and not a single bubble of air remains. The sponge cannot absorb any more water without expanding its volume. The Zero Air Voids Line represents this exact saturated state for a soil — it's the **densest possible condition** at a given water content, achievable only when every void between soil grains is water-filled.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Unit weight of water, $\gamma_w$** = **9.81 kN/m³** (or 10 kN/m³ for simplified calculations in KTU problems)
- **Specific gravity of soil solids, $G_s$** — typically ranges from **2.65 to 2.75** for most mineral soils
- **Saturation, $S = 1$ (or 100%)** — the defining condition of the ZAVL
- **Dry unit weight, $\gamma_d$** — the dependent variable plotted on the y-axis

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Identification Trick:** If a KTU problem statement mentions "zero air voids," "saturation line," or "theoretical maximum dry density," the answer must use the ZAVL equation: $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Theoretical Foundation: Why Does the ZAVL Exist?

The derivation of the Zero Air Voids Line is built upon three fundamental phase relationships in soil mechanics:

1. **Void Ratio Definition:** $e = \dfrac{V_v}{V_s}$ — where $V_v$ is the volume of voids and $V_s$ is the volume of solids.
2. **Water Content Definition:** $w = \dfrac{W_w}{W_s}$ — where $W_w$ is the weight of water and $W_s$ is the weight of solids.
3. **Fundamental Phase Equation:** $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ — the master relationship connecting saturation, void ratio, water content, and specific gravity.

When the condition of **zero air voids** is imposed, two key consequences follow:
- The void space is entirely water-filled: $V_v = V_w$ (the volume of voids equals the volume of water).
- The degree of saturation becomes unity: $S = 1$ (100% saturated).

Substituting $S = 1$ into the fundamental phase equation:

$$S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s \implies 1 \cdot e = w \cdot G_s \implies e = w \cdot G_s$$

This means that under the ZAVL condition, **the void ratio is directly proportional to the water content** with the proportionality constant being $G_s$. As water content increases, the void ratio also increases (the soil becomes more "diluted" by water), which actually causes the dry density to **decrease** at higher water contents — this is why the ZAVL has a **negative slope** on a dry density vs. water content plot (similar to the practical compaction curve beyond OMC).

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Impose the saturation condition:** $V_a = 0 \implies S = 1$. This is the physical definition of "zero air voids."
- **Step 2 — Apply the phase relation:** Using $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ with $S = 1$ gives $e = w \cdot G_s$. This is the **bridge** between the void ratio (a volume parameter) and water content (a weight parameter).
- **Step 3 — Express dry unit weight in terms of void ratio:** From the phase diagram, $\gamma_d = \dfrac{W_s}{V} = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$. This equation holds universally for any soil state.
- **Step 4 — Substitute and finalize:** Replace $e$ with $w \cdot G_s$ to obtain the ZAVL equation: $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$.
- **Step 5 — Plot on the compaction graph:** The ZAVL is plotted as $\gamma_d$ (y-axis) versus $w$ (x-axis) for a given $G_s$. The resulting curve is a **hyperbola** that always lies to the right and above any practical Proctor compaction curve.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$ | **Zero Air Voids Equation** (Direct form) — gives dry unit weight for a given water content at S = 1 | kN/m³ |
| 2 | $e = w \cdot G_s$ | Void ratio at zero air voids (when S = 1) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $w = \dfrac{\gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - \dfrac{1}{G_s}$ | Rearranged ZAVL — gives water content for a given dry unit weight | Dimensionless (decimal or %) |
| 4 | $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ | Fundamental phase relation (always true) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$ | Universal dry unit weight formula (valid for any state) | kN/m³ |
| 6 | $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ (or 10 kN/m³ approx.) | Unit weight of water at standard conditions | kN/m³ |
| 7 | $S_{field} = \dfrac{w_{field} \cdot G_s}{e_{field}}$ | Degree of saturation check — must be < 1.0 in field | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Vigilance:** Water content $w$ in the ZAVL equation must be in **decimal form** (e.g., 0.15 for 15%), NOT in percentage form. This is a very common error in KTU answer sheets.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

The Zero Air Voids Line is not merely a theoretical construct — it has critical practical applications:

- **Compaction Quality Control:** Field compaction test results (from sand cone or nuclear density gauge tests) are plotted against the ZAVL. If a field point lies *above* the ZAVL, it indicates a **measurement error** (overestimated dry density or underestimated water content).
- **Maximum Theoretical Dry Density:** For a given water content, the ZAVL gives the absolute maximum dry density achievable, helping engineers set realistic compaction specifications.
- **Degree of Saturation Assessment:** By comparing field compaction data to the ZAVL, engineers can estimate how close the field compaction came to full saturation (i.e., how much air was expelled).
- **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC) Selection:** The ZAVL provides a reference for choosing OMC and maximum dry density (MDD) targets in earthwork specifications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Exhaustive Derivation of the Zero Air Voids Line Equation

**Starting Point — The Soil Phase Diagram:**

Consider a representative elementary volume of soil with:
- Volume of solids: $V_s$
- Volume of water: $V_w$
- Volume of air: $V_a$
- Total volume: $V = V_s + V_w + V_a$
- Total void volume: $V_v = V_w + V_a$
- Weight of solids: $W_s$
- Weight of water: $W_w$
- Total weight: $W = W_s + W_w$

**Step 1 — Apply the Zero Air Voids Condition:**

By definition of the ZAVL, the volume of air is zero:

$$V_a = 0$$

This means the entire void space is occupied by water:

$$V_v = V_w + V_a = V_w + 0 = V_w$$

Consequently, the degree of saturation becomes:

$$S = \frac{V_w}{V_v} = \frac{V_w}{V_w} = 1 \quad \text{(i.e., 100\% saturated)}$$

**Step 2 — Invoke the Fundamental Phase Equation:**

The general phase relationship in soil mechanics is:

$$S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$$

This is derived from:
$$S = \frac{V_w}{V_v}, \quad e = \frac{V_v}{V_s}, \quad w = \frac{W_w}{W_s}, \quad G_s = \frac{W_s}{V_s \cdot \gamma_w}$$

Substituting these definitions:

$$S \cdot e = \frac{V_w}{V_v} \cdot \frac{V_v}{V_s} = \frac{V_w}{V_s} = \frac{W_w / \gamma_w}{W_s / (G_s \cdot \gamma_w)} = \frac{W_w \cdot G_s}{W_s} = w \cdot G_s$$

With $S = 1$ (from Step 1):

$$1 \cdot e = w \cdot G_s \implies e = w \cdot G_s$$

**Step 3 — Express Dry Unit Weight in Terms of Void Ratio:**

The dry unit weight is defined as the weight of solids per unit total volume:

$$\gamma_d = \frac{W_s}{V}$$

Now, the total volume can be written in terms of $V_s$ and $e$:

$$V = V_s + V_v = V_s + e \cdot V_s = V_s(1 + e)$$

The weight of solids in terms of $G_s$ and $\gamma_w$ is:

$$W_s = G_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s$$

Substituting into the expression for $\gamma_d$:

$$\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s}{V_s(1 + e)} = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Zero Air Voids Void Ratio:**

Replacing $e$ with $w \cdot G_s$ (from Step 2):

$$\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$$

This is the **Zero Air Voids Line equation**. $\blacksquare$

---

### Rearranged Form (Solving for Water Content)

Starting from:

$$\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$$

Cross-multiply:

$$\gamma_d(1 + w \cdot G_s) = G_s \cdot \gamma_w$$

Expand the left side:

$$\gamma_d + \gamma_d \cdot w \cdot G_s = G_s \cdot \gamma_w$$

Isolate the $w$ term:

$$\gamma_d \cdot w \cdot G_s = G_s \cdot \gamma_w - \gamma_d$$

Divide both sides by $\gamma_d \cdot G_s$:

$$w = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w - \gamma_d}{\gamma_d \cdot G_s} = \frac{\gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - \frac{1}{G_s}$$

This form is useful when the KTU problem gives $\gamma_d$ and asks for the corresponding $w$ on the ZAVL.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A soil has a specific gravity $G_s = 2.70$. Taking $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³, calculate the dry unit weight on the Zero Air Voids Line for water contents of 5%, 10%, 15%, 20%, 25%, and 30%. Also, plot the ZAVL.

**Solution — Systematic Tabulation:**

Using $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$ with $G_s \cdot \gamma_w = 2.70 \times 9.81 = 26.487$ kN/m³.

| Water Content $w$ | $w \cdot G_s$ | $1 + w \cdot G_s$ | $\gamma_d = 26.487 / (1 + w \cdot G_s)$ (kN/m³) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0.05 | 0.135 | 1.135 | 23.34 |
| 0.10 | 0.270 | 1.270 | 20.86 |
| 0.15 | 0.405 | 1.405 | 18.85 |
| 0.20 | 0.540 | 1.540 | 17.20 |
| 0.25 | 0.675 | 1.675 | 15.81 |
| 0.30 | 0.810 | 1.810 | 14.63 |

**Observation:** As water content increases, the dry unit weight on the ZAVL **decreases monotonically** — confirming the negative-slope hyperbolic nature of the ZAVL.

---

### Comparison: ZAVL vs. Practical Compaction Curve

| Property | Zero Air Voids Line | Practical Compaction Curve |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Air voids | $V_a = 0$ (none) | $V_a > 0$ (air always entrapped) |
| Saturation | $S = 1$ (100%) | $S < 1$ (typically 80–95% at OMC) |
| Achievability | Theoretically (not practically) | Practically achievable in lab/field |
| Position on plot | Upper-right boundary | Lies on or below the ZAVL |
| Shape | Hyperbola (monotonically decreasing) | Bell-shaped (rises to peak at OMC, then falls) |
| Significance | Theoretical limit | Actual compaction result |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** Think of the ZAVL as the "ceiling" of compaction — the Proctor curve can touch the ZAVL asymptotically but never pierce it.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Block-Level Flow Architecture — From Phase Diagram to ZAVL Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Phase Diagram\nVs, Vw, Va, Ws, Ww"] --> B["Apply Zero Air Voids\nVa = 0"]
    B --> C["Consequence 1\nVv = Vw"]
    B --> D["Consequence 2\nS = Vw / Vv = 1"]
    D --> E["Fundamental Phase Relation\nS × e = w × Gs"]
    E --> F["Substitute S = 1\ne = w × Gs"]
    F --> G["Dry Unit Weight Formula\ngamma_d = Gs × gamma_w / 1 + e"]
    G --> H["Substitute e = w × Gs"]
    H --> I["ZAVL Equation\ngamma_d = Gs × gamma_w / 1 + w × Gs"]
    I --> J["Plot on Compaction Graph\ngamma_d vs w Hyperbola"]
    J --> K["Field Compaction Check\nField curve must lie below ZAVL"]
```

### Diagram 2: Conceptual Position of ZAVL on the Compaction Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph compaction["Dry Density vs Water Content Plot"]
        direction LR
        axis1["X-AXIS: Water Content w percent"] --- axis2["Y-AXIS: Dry Unit Weight gamma_d kN/m3"]
    end

    subgraph curves["Key Curves on the Plot"]
        direction TB
        proctor["Proctor Compaction Curve\nBell Shaped with Peak at OMC"]
        zavl["Zero Air Voids Line ZAVL\nHyperbola Decreasing\nS = 1 Boundary"]
        saturation100["100 percent Saturation Line\nSame as ZAVL"]
    end

    proctor -->|"Lies on or below"| zavl
    zavl -->|"Theoretical Upper Limit"| saturation100
```

### Diagram 3: Physical Interpretation — Saturation States in a Soil Mass

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph dry["State 1: Dry Soil"]
        d1["Solids: 60 percent"]
        d2["Water: 0 percent"]
        d3["Air: 40 percent"]
    end

    subgraph optimum["State 2: Optimum Compaction (Practical)"]
        o1["Solids: 60 percent"]
        o2["Water: 12 percent"]
        o3["Air: 5 to 10 percent"]
        o4["Saturation S = 85 to 90 percent"]
    end

    subgraph zavState["State 3: Zero Air Voids (Theoretical)"]
        z1["Solids: 55 percent"]
        z2["Water: 18 percent"]
        z3["Air: 0 percent"]
        z4["Saturation S = 100 percent"]
    end

    dry -->|"Add water and compact"| optimum
    optimum -->|"Add water remove all air"| zavState
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Diagram 1 shows the complete logical derivation flow. Diagram 2 illustrates the spatial relationship between the practical Proctor curve and the theoretical ZAVL. Diagram 3 conceptually compares three saturation states — from dry soil through practical optimum compaction to the theoretical zero air voids state.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the Zero Air Voids Line. What is its significance in compaction of soils?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Zero Air Voids Line (ZAVL)** is a theoretical curve on a dry density versus water content plot that represents the relationship $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$ under the condition that all air voids in the soil are eliminated, i.e., the soil is **100% saturated** ($S = 1$, $V_a = 0$).

**Significance:**
- It represents the **theoretical upper boundary** for any compaction curve. No practical compaction result can cross or exceed this line.
- It serves as a **reference for quality control** — if field data plots above the ZAVL, it indicates a measurement error.
- It helps estimate the **maximum possible dry density** for a given water content and the **degree of saturation** achieved in the field.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the equation for the Zero Air Voids Line and explain the meaning of each term. Why does the ZAVL slope downward at higher water contents?**

**Model Answer:**

The Zero Air Voids Line equation is:

$$\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$$

Where:
- $\gamma_d$ = dry unit weight of soil (kN/m³)
- $G_s$ = specific gravity of soil solids
- $\gamma_w$ = unit weight of water (**9.81 kN/m³**)
- $w$ = water content (in decimal form)

**Why the downward slope at higher water contents:** As water content increases, the void ratio $e = w \cdot G_s$ also increases (the soil structure becomes more "open" because water occupies more void space). Since $\gamma_d = G_s \cdot \gamma_w / (1 + e)$, a larger $e$ in the denominator causes $\gamma_d$ to **decrease**. Thus, the ZAVL slopes downward for higher water contents.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) (7 Marks)** Derive the expression for the Zero Air Voids Line starting from the fundamental phase relationship $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A soil sample compacted in the field has a water content of 14% and a dry unit weight of 18.5 kN/m³. The specific gravity of soil solids is 2.70 and $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³. Calculate: (i) the theoretical maximum dry unit weight on the ZAVL at this water content, and (ii) the degree of saturation achieved in the field. Comment on the quality of compaction.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The soil is fully saturated ($S = 1$), meaning $V_a = 0$.
2. The void space is entirely filled with water ($V_v = V_w$).
3. The phase relationships $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ and $\gamma_d = G_s \cdot \gamma_w / (1 + e)$ are valid.

**Derivation:**

Starting from the fundamental phase equation:

$$S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$$

Applying the zero air voids condition $S = 1$:

$$e = w \cdot G_s$$

From the universal dry unit weight formula:

$$\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$$

Substituting $e = w \cdot G_s$:

$$\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s} \quad \blacksquare$$

This equation, when plotted as $\gamma_d$ versus $w$, yields the Zero Air Voids Line.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $w = 0.14$, $\gamma_d = 18.5$ kN/m³, $G_s = 2.70$, $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³.

**Part (i) — Theoretical maximum dry unit weight on ZAVL:**

$$\gamma_{d,\text{ZAVL}} = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$1 + w \cdot G_s = 1 + 0.14 \times 2.70 = 1 + 0.378 = 1.378$$

Compute the numerator:

$$G_s \cdot \gamma_w = 2.70 \times 9.81 = 26.487 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

Therefore:

$$\gamma_{d,\text{ZAVL}} = \frac{26.487}{1.378} = 19.22 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Part (ii) — Degree of saturation in the field:**

First, calculate the void ratio from the field dry unit weight:

$$\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e} \implies 18.5 = \frac{26.487}{1 + e}$$

$$1 + e = \frac{26.487}{18.5} = 1.4317$$

$$e = 0.4317$$

Now apply the phase relation:

$$S = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} = \frac{0.14 \times 2.70}{0.4317} = \frac{0.378}{0.4317} = 0.8756$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 87.6\%}$$

**Comment on compaction quality:** The field dry unit weight (18.5 kN/m³) is below the ZAVL value (19.22 kN/m³), which is consistent and physically valid. The degree of saturation is 87.6%, which is typical of well-compacted soil at or near OMC. Approximately 12.4% of the void space is still occupied by air, indicating reasonable but not perfect compaction. The compaction can be considered **satisfactory** if the project specification requires 90–95% relative compaction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) (7 Marks)** Plot the Zero Air Voids Line for a soil with $G_s = 2.65$ and $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ for water contents ranging from 8% to 28% (in steps of 4%). Tabulate all calculations.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A Standard Proctor test on the same soil gave a maximum dry density (MDD) of 18.0 kN/m³ at an OMC of 16%. (i) Calculate the dry unit weight on the ZAVL at OMC. (ii) Find the degree of saturation at OMC. (iii) Is the compaction result physically acceptable? Justify.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Given: $G_s = 2.65$, $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³, $G_s \cdot \gamma_w = 2.65 \times 9.81 = 25.9965$ kN/m³.

Using $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$:

| Water Content $w$ (decimal) | $w \cdot G_s$ | $1 + w \cdot G_s$ | $\gamma_d$ (kN/m³) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0.08 | 0.2120 | 1.2120 | 21.45 |
| 0.12 | 0.3180 | 1.3180 | 19.72 |
| 0.16 | 0.4240 | 1.4240 | 18.26 |
| 0.20 | 0.5300 | 1.5300 | 16.99 |
| 0.24 | 0.6360 | 1.6360 | 15.89 |
| 0.28 | 0.7420 | 1.7420 | 14.92 |

**Plot:** A graph of $\gamma_d$ (y-axis) versus $w$ (x-axis) shows a smoothly decreasing hyperbola. (In a KTU exam, drawing this curve with labeled axes and points earns full credit.)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $G_s = 2.65$, $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³, MDD = 18.0 kN/m³, OMC = 16% = 0.16.

**Part (i) — Dry unit weight on ZAVL at OMC:**

$$\gamma_{d,\text{ZAVL}} = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s} = \frac{2.65 \times 9.81}{1 + 0.16 \times 2.65} = \frac{25.9965}{1 + 0.424} = \frac{25.9965}{1.424}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{d,\text{ZAVL}} = 18.26 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

**Part (ii) — Degree of saturation at OMC:**

First, find the void ratio from the MDD:

$$\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e} \implies 18.0 = \frac{25.9965}{1 + e}$$

$$1 + e = \frac{25.9965}{18.0} = 1.4443$$

$$e = 0.4443$$

Now compute saturation:

$$S = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} = \frac{0.16 \times 2.65}{0.4443} = \frac{0.424}{0.4443} = 0.9543$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 95.4\%}$$

**Part (iii) — Physical Acceptability:**

Since the field/lab dry unit weight (18.0 kN/m³) is **less than** the ZAVL value (18.26 kN/m³), the compaction result is **physically acceptable**. The ZAVL has not been crossed, which is the required condition. The degree of saturation of 95.4% is very high — indicating that the Standard Proctor compaction was efficient in expelling air. The small difference between MDD and the ZAVL value (only 0.26 kN/m³) shows that the compaction came close to the theoretical maximum. This is a **good quality compaction result**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency trap:** Students frequently plug in water content as a *percentage* (e.g., 16) instead of a *decimal* (0.16) in the ZAVL equation. This gives wildly incorrect answers and **zero marks** for the numerical part. Always convert $w$ to decimal.
> 2. **Reversing the equation:** Confusing the universal dry unit weight formula $\gamma_d = G_s \cdot \gamma_w / (1 + e)$ with the ZAVL-specific form. The ZAVL requires the substitution $e = w \cdot G_s$ — omitting this step leads to an incomplete derivation and **loss of 3–4 marks**.
> 3. **Crossing the ZAVL — student panic:** If a calculated field $\gamma_d$ is *above* the ZAVL, students often try to "fix" their numbers. **Do not do this.** Instead, explicitly state that the result is **physically impossible** and the data must contain measurement errors. Examiners reward this critical analysis.
> 4. **Forgetting the S = 1 assumption:** The ZAVL equation is ONLY valid at S = 1. Using it for partially saturated conditions is a fundamental conceptual error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Zero Air Voids Line (ZAVL)** is the theoretical compaction curve at **100% saturation** ($S = 1$, $V_a = 0$).
- **Core Equation:** $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$ — always use $w$ in **decimal** form.
- **Derivation Bridge:** $S = 1 \implies e = w \cdot G_s \implies \gamma_d = G_s \cdot \gamma_w / (1 + w \cdot G_s)$.
- **Shape:** Hyperbola with a **negative slope** — $\gamma_d$ decreases as $w$ increases.
- **Position:** ZAVL is the **upper-right boundary** on a compaction plot; the Proctor curve always lies **on or below** it.
- **Physical Meaning:** Represents the **maximum possible dry density** for a given water content if all air could be removed.
- **Practical Check:** If field $\gamma_d$ > ZAVL $\gamma_d$ → **measurement error** or **impossible data** (flag it in the exam).
- **Degree of Saturation Check:** $S_{field} = w \cdot G_s / e_{field}$ — should be less than 1 (typically 80–95% at OMC).
- **Rearranged Form:** $w = \gamma_w / \gamma_d - 1 / G_s$ — useful when $\gamma_d$ is given and $w$ is sought.
- **Standard Constants:** $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ (or 10 kN/m³ for quick approximations); $G_s = 2.65$–$2.75$ for most mineral soils.
- **Common KTU Problem Types:** (1) Derive the ZAVL equation, (2) Calculate ZAVL $\gamma_d$ at a given $w$, (3) Calculate $S$ for field data, (4) Tabulate and plot the ZAVL, (5) Verify physical acceptability of compaction data.
- **Memory Anchor:** **"Zero Air = S = 1 = e = wGs = ZAVL Equation"** — chain this sequence in your mind during exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Influencing Compaction - Effect of Compaction on Soil Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Influencing Compaction - Effect of Compaction on Soil Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Compaction** is the process of densification of soil by reducing the volume of air voids through the application of mechanical energy (static, vibratory, impact, or kneading), at a suitable moisture content, without significantly altering the water content. It is a **mechanical means of soil stabilization** that improves engineering properties such as strength, stiffness, and resistance to deformation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Compaction is a *short-term, mechanical process* applied to *unsaturated soils* primarily to improve geotechnical performance of fills, embankments, subgrades, and backfills behind retaining structures. It is fundamentally different from *consolidation*, which is a long-term, hydraulic process in saturated soils.

## 1.2 Factors Influencing Compaction — The "Big Five"

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mandates study of the following factors:

| Factor | Nature of Influence |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Water content (w)** | Governs the *achievable* dry density; produces the OMC–MDD curve |
| **Compactive effort (E)** | Energy applied per unit volume; alters OMC and MDD |
| **Soil type** | Gradation, plasticity, organic content, and mineralogy |
| **Method of compaction** | Kneading, static, impact, vibratory — different grain rearrangements |
| **Additives / admixtures** | Lime, cement, fly ash — chemical modification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Compactness vs Compaction:** *Compactness* is the present state of packing of a soil mass; *compaction* is the *process* of improving it. KTU board questions often test this distinction.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy — the "wet sand castle" effect:** Have you ever tried to build a sand castle? *Dry sand* collapses immediately because grains slide over each other (high air voids, no suction). *Fully saturated sand* flows like a liquid (water pushes grains apart). However, *slightly moist sand* holds shape perfectly — water forms tiny "bridges" (menisci) between grains, creating apparent cohesion. **This is exactly the role of water in compaction:** optimum moisture content (OMC) gives maximum dry density (MDD). Too little water → grains cannot slide into a denser packing. Too much water → water occupies space meant for soil grains, density falls. The **Proctor's curve** is literally a mathematical description of the sand castle effect!

**Memory hook — the "G-E-S-W-M" rule:** Grains, Energy, Soil-type, Water, Method — these are the five levers a geotechnical engineer pulls to control field density.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factor 1: Water Content (The Dominant Variable)

### 2.1.1 Mechanism — Three-Stage Behavior

- **Stage I — Dry side (w < OMC):** Water exists as *thin films* around soil grains. Surface tension creates **capillary tension (negative pore water pressure)** that resists grain rearrangement. Insufficient lubrication → low dry density.
- **Stage II — Optimum (w ≈ OMC):** Water films reach optimum thickness. Capillary tension drops to a *threshold value*, grains slide easily under compactive effort, and air voids are expelled efficiently → **MDD is achieved**.
- **Stage III — Wet side (w > OMC):** Pore water occupies space meant for solids. The soil approaches *saturation*; air voids cannot be further reduced without expelling water. Density falls.

### 2.1.2 The Zero-Air-Voids (ZAV) Line

The ZAV line represents the *theoretical upper bound* — the dry density of a soil compacted with **zero air voids** (i.e., 100 % saturation, S = 100 %). It is calculated as:

$$\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + w \cdot G}$$

where $G$ is the specific gravity of soil solids and $\rho_w$ is the density of water (**1.00 g/cc or 1000 kg/m³**). The compaction curve must always lie *below* this line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** If the compaction curve crosses the ZAV line, the soil has been compacted *beyond saturation* — this is physically impossible and indicates an **experimental error** in the Proctor test.

## 2.2 Factor 2: Compactive Effort (E)

### 2.2.1 Mathematical Definition

$$E = \frac{N \cdot n \cdot W \cdot h}{V}$$

where:
- $N$ = number of blows per layer
- $n$ = number of layers
- $W$ = weight of the hammer
- $h$ = height of fall
- $V$ = volume of the mould

**Standard Proctor Energy** (ASTM D698 / IS 2720 Part 7) ≈ **593 kJ/m³ (12,375 ft-lbf/ft³)**.

**Modified Proctor Energy** (ASTM D1557 / IS 2720 Part 8) ≈ **2,675 kJ/m³ (56,250 ft-lbf/ft³)** — roughly **4.5 × standard**.

### 2.2.2 Effect of Increasing Compactive Effort

- **MDD increases** (curve shifts upward).
- **OMC decreases** (curve shifts leftward).
- The compaction curve becomes **flatter** near optimum.
- For **cohesionless soils**, excessively high energy may *crush* grains — leading to a *decrease* in density (a phenomenon observed in the **Humped Proctor curve**).

## 2.3 Factor 3: Type of Soil

| Soil Type | OMC Trend | MDD Trend | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Well-graded sand (SW)** | 8 – 12 % | 1.80 – 2.10 g/cc | Excellent compactibility |
| **Poorly-graded sand (SP)** | 10 – 14 % | 1.55 – 1.75 g/cc | Vibratory methods preferred |
| **Silt (ML)** | 12 – 18 % | 1.65 – 1.85 g/cc | Sensitive to water; capillary effects strong |
| **Clay (CL)** | 15 – 22 % | 1.60 – 1.80 g/cc | Low permeability → difficult to expel air |
| **Heavy clay (CH)** | 20 – 35 % | 1.35 – 1.60 g/cc | Highest OMC, lowest MDD |
| **Organic soil (OL/OH)** | > 30 % | < 1.40 g/cc | Compaction damages structure — usually avoided |

> [!NOTE]
> **Burminster's Principle (KTU favorite):** *Coarse-grained soils are best compacted by vibration; fine-grained cohesive soils are best compacted by kneading or impact.* This is because vibratory energy destabilizes grain-to-grain contact in coarse soils, allowing rearrangement, while kneading action in clays breaks up clay peds and forces out trapped air.

## 2.4 Factor 4: Method of Compaction

Four fundamental methods:
1. **Static** — smooth-wheel roller; pressure applied slowly.
2. **Impact** — sheepsfoot / rammer; sudden dynamic loading.
3. **Vibratory** — vibrating plate / drum; high-frequency oscillation.
4. **Kneading** — sheepsfoot / padfoot roller; pushes soil sideways, ideal for clays.

## 2.5 Factor 5: Additives / Admixtures

- **Lime:** For highly plastic clays (CH). Reduces plasticity, increases workability, raises OMC.
- **Cement:** Granular or low-plasticity soils. Increases strength, stiffness, and erosion resistance.
- **Fly ash / Pond ash:** Improves granular soils and reduces swell in expansive soils.
- **Bitumen:** Waterproofing surface layers.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Relation | Expression | Variables & Units | KTU Exam Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Dry density | $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1+w}$ | $\rho_d, \rho$ in g/cc; $w$ as ratio | Backbone of compaction problems |
| Bulk density | $\rho = \dfrac{M}{V}$ | $M$ in g; $V$ in cc | From mould mass & volume |
| Zero-Air-Voids density | $\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} = \dfrac{G \rho_w}{1+wG}$ | $G$ dimensionless; $\rho_w = 1$ g/cc | Theoretical upper limit |
| Degree of saturation at MDD | $S_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{w \cdot G}{\dfrac{G \rho_w}{\rho_d} - 1}$ | Dimensionless | Find saturation at OMC |
| Compactive effort (Standard Proctor) | $E = \dfrac{N \cdot n \cdot W \cdot h}{V}$ | kJ/m³ | 593 kJ/m³ for SP |
| Modified Proctor (approx.) | $E_{\text{mod}} \approx 4.5 \times E_{\text{SP}}$ | — | Compare standard vs modified |
| Relative compaction (field) | $RC = \dfrac{\gamma_{d,\text{field}}}{\gamma_{d,\text{lab,MDD}}} \times 100$ | % | Typical spec: 95 %–98 % |
| Proctor needle penetration | $R = \dfrac{W_{\text{proctor}}}{A_{\text{tip}}}$ | kPa | Field density verification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-mark-loss rule:** KTU examiners deduct marks if students *omit the OMC determination procedure* in a compaction numerical. Always show: (i) plot $e$ vs $w$, OR (ii) compute $\rho_d$ and plot $\rho_d$ vs $w$, then identify the peak.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highway embankments:** Subgrade must reach ≥ 95 % Modified Proctor MDD at OMC ± 2 %.
- **Earth dams:** Core of dam compacted to ≥ 98 % Standard Proctor MDD on the wet side (w > OMC) to minimize permeability.
- **Railway ballast subgrade:** 100 % Modified Proctor MDD for high-speed corridors.
- **Landfill liners:** Compacted clay liners (CCLs) at 90 %–95 % MDD, *wet of OMC*, to maximize hydraulic resistance.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Zero-Air-Voids (ZAV) Line Equation

The ZAV line is the locus of dry densities corresponding to **zero air voids** ($V_a = 0$, i.e., $S = 100\%$). Let us derive the governing equation rigorously.

### Step 1 — Phase Diagram Foundation

Take a soil mass of total volume $V$, mass $M$. Let $V_s$, $V_w$, and $V_a$ denote volumes of solids, water, and air respectively:

$$V = V_s + V_w + V_a$$

For zero air voids:

$$V_a = 0 \implies V = V_s + V_w$$

### Step 2 — Express Volumes in Terms of $V_s$

By definition of void ratio $e = V_v / V_s$, where $V_v = V_w + V_a$. With $V_a = 0$:

$$V_w = e \cdot V_s$$

Therefore:

$$V = V_s + e \cdot V_s = V_s (1 + e)$$

### Step 3 — Mass Relationship

The mass of solids is $M_s = G \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_s$ and the mass of water is $M_w = w \cdot M_s = w \cdot G \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_s$. The total mass is:

$$M = M_s + M_w = G \rho_w V_s (1 + w)$$

### Step 4 — Bulk Density at Zero Air Voids

$$\rho = \frac{M}{V} = \frac{G \rho_w V_s (1+w)}{V_s (1+e)} = \frac{G \rho_w (1+w)}{1+e}$$

### Step 5 — Dry Density

By definition:

$$\rho_d = \frac{\rho}{1+w} = \frac{G \rho_w (1+w)}{(1+e)(1+w)} = \frac{G \rho_w}{1+e}$$

But for the *saturated* condition $S = 1$, we use the saturation relation:

$$S \cdot e = w \cdot G \implies e = wG \quad (\text{when } S = 1)$$

Substituting $e = wG$ into $\rho_d$:

$$\rho_d = \frac{G \rho_w}{1 + wG}$$

This is the **Zero-Air-Voids dry density** as a function of water content.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The ZAV line is a *straight line in the $\rho_d$ vs $w$ plot* (slightly curved because $wG$ is in the denominator). All experimentally measured compaction points must lie *below or on* this line.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Compaction Curve Characteristics for Different Soils

The following matrix is a KTU high-yield reference for inter-comparing soils:

| Soil Description | OMC Range (%) | MDD Range (kN/m³) | Compaction Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Well-graded sandy gravel | 6 – 9 | 20.0 – 22.0 | Steep peak, narrow range |
| Poorly-graded sand | 10 – 13 | 17.0 – 19.0 | Flat peak, vibration essential |
| Silty sand | 8 – 12 | 18.5 – 20.5 | Good compactibility |
| Low-plasticity silt (ML) | 13 – 18 | 17.0 – 19.0 | Sensitive to water |
| Low-plasticity clay (CL) | 15 – 22 | 16.5 – 18.5 | Air-permeability limited |
| High-plasticity clay (CH) | 22 – 35 | 13.5 – 16.5 | Slow densification |
| Organic clay (OH) | > 30 | < 14.0 | Unstable under compaction |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Finding OMC and MDD from Raw Data

**Problem (KTU Model Style):** A Standard Proctor test gave the following observations for a soil ($G_s = 2.70$):

| Trial | Mass of wet soil in mould (kg) | Mould volume (cc) | Water content (%) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 1.85 | 1000 | 8.0 |
| 2 | 1.98 | 1000 | 11.0 |
| 3 | 2.05 | 1000 | 13.5 |
| 4 | 2.04 | 1000 | 16.0 |
| 5 | 1.99 | 1000 | 18.5 |

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Compute bulk density $\rho = M / V$:

$$\rho_1 = 1.85 \, \text{g/cc}; \quad \rho_2 = 1.98 \, \text{g/cc}; \quad \rho_3 = 2.05 \, \text{g/cc}; \quad \rho_4 = 2.04 \, \text{g/cc}; \quad \rho_5 = 1.99 \, \text{g/cc}$$

**Step 2:** Compute dry density $\rho_d = \rho / (1 + w)$ where $w$ is the decimal water content:

$$\rho_{d,1} = \frac{1.85}{1.08} = 1.713 \, \text{g/cc}$$

$$\rho_{d,2} = \frac{1.98}{1.11} = 1.784 \, \text{g/cc}$$

$$\rho_{d,3} = \frac{2.05}{1.135} = 1.806 \, \text{g/cc}$$

$$\rho_{d,4} = \frac{2.04}{1.16} = 1.759 \, \text{g/cc}$$

$$\rho_{d,5} = \frac{1.99}{1.185} = 1.679 \, \text{g/cc}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the peak dry density:

$$\rho_{d,\max} = \rho_{d,3} = 1.806 \, \text{g/cc} \quad \text{at} \quad w = 13.5\%$$

**Step 4:** State the answer:

$$\boxed{\text{MDD} = 1.806 \, \text{g/cc}; \quad \text{OMC} = 13.5\%}$$

**Step 5 (Verification — ZAV check):** Compute $\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}}$ at $w = 0.135$:

$$\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} = \frac{2.70 \times 1.00}{1 + 0.135 \times 2.70} = \frac{2.70}{1.3645} = 1.979 \, \text{g/cc}$$

Since $\rho_{d,\max} = 1.806 < 1.979$, the test point lies *below* the ZAV line — **the result is physically valid**.

## 3.4 Effect of Compaction on Engineering Properties — Detailed Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Section:** The syllabus mandates a clear understanding of how compaction alters soil properties. The relationship is *non-monotonic* — increasing compaction does **not** always improve performance.

### 3.4.1 Effect on Permeability ($k$)

Compaction reduces void space and aligns clay particles horizontally, producing a more tortuous flow path. Therefore:

$$k_{\text{compacted}} \ll k_{\text{loose}}$$

- For *clayey soils*, $k$ can drop by **1 to 3 orders of magnitude**.
- Compaction **on the dry side** produces a *flocculated* structure (high $k$); compaction **on the wet side** produces a *dispersed* structure (low $k$).
- **Engineering choice:** Earth-dam cores are compacted *wet of OMC* to minimize seepage.

### 3.4.2 Effect on Compressibility

Increased density reduces the compressibility index $C_c$ and the recompression index $C_r$. However, **compaction on the dry side** of optimum can create *meta-stable* structures that yield large settlements upon saturation — the **"wet-side collapse" phenomenon**.

$$C_{c,\text{compacted}} < C_{c,\text{loose}} \quad \text{(for same soil)}$$

### 3.4.3 Effect on Shear Strength

Unconfined compressive strength $q_u$ and CBR both increase with compaction:

$$q_u \uparrow; \quad \text{CBR} \uparrow; \quad \phi \uparrow; \quad c \uparrow \text{ (in clays)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering rule:** Subgrade is *always* compacted to a specified relative compaction (typically 95–98 % MDD) to ensure adequate CBR.

### 3.4.4 Effect on Swelling & Shrinkage

| Compaction State | Swell Potential | Shrinkage Potential |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Dry of OMC (flocculated) | **Lower** initially, but **higher** upon wetting | Lower |
| Wet of OMC (dispersed) | **Higher** initially | Higher |
| High dry density, wet side | Highest swell | Highest shrinkage |

This is why **expansive clays** must be compacted *slightly dry of OMC* and at controlled density (often 90–95 % MDD) to minimize heave.

### 3.4.5 Effect on Pore Water Pressure

During rapid compaction in saturated soils, **excess pore water pressure ($u$)** develops. In partially saturated soils, **matric suction (negative pressure)** initially exists and decreases with water content.

$$\text{Dry side: } (u_a - u_w) \gg 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{ soil is "stiff"}$$

$$\text{Wet side: } (u_a - u_w) \to 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{ soil becomes "soft"}$$

### 3.4.6 Effect on Soil Structure (Flocculated vs Dispersed)

This is a **KTU high-yield point**:
- **Dry of OMC:** Clay particles form **flocculated** (edge-to-face) structure — high permeability but high strength at low strain.
- **Wet of OMC:** Clay particles align into **dispersed** (face-to-face) structure — low permeability, lower strength.

### 3.4.7 Effect on Shrinkage Limit and Plasticity

Compaction at high density alters the soil's water-retention behavior. Generally, **shrinkage limit decreases** with compaction, while the **plastic limit remains roughly unchanged** unless additives are introduced.

## 3.5 Master Summary Table: Property vs Compaction Condition

| Engineering Property | Dry of OMC, low density | Optimum, max density | Wet of OMC, max density |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Dry density | Low | **Highest (MDD)** | High but lower than MDD |
| Permeability | High | Moderate | **Lowest** |
| Compressibility | High (collapsible) | Moderate | Low |
| Shear strength | Moderate (suction-aided) | **Highest** | Moderate (lower effective stress) |
| Swell potential | Low initially | Moderate | **Highest** |
| Pore pressure (during compaction) | Highly negative | Near-zero | Positive |
| Soil structure | Flocculated | Transitional | Dispersed |
| CBR | Low | **Highest** | High but slightly less |
