**BC (Boundary Conditions):**
- At the **drained face** (top for single drainage, both top and bottom for double drainage): $u = 0$ for all $t > 0$.
- At the **impermeable face** (bottom for single drainage): $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z} = 0$ for all $t > 0$.

## 3.3 Solution Form — Fourier Series

Using separation of variables, the solution is:
$$\frac{u(z,t)}{u_i} = \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \frac{2}{M} \sin\!\left(\frac{Mz}{H_{dr}}\right) e^{-M^2 T_v}$$

where:
- $M = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(2m+1)$ for $m = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$
- $T_v = \dfrac{c_v t}{H_{dr}^2}$ is the time factor
- $H_{dr}$ is the drainage path length

The first term ($m = 0$) dominates and gives a good engineering approximation:
$$u(z,t) \approx \frac{4 u_i}{\pi} \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi z}{2 H_{dr}}\right) e^{-\frac{\pi^2}{4} T_v}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Approximate Time Factor Formulas

### Case 1: For $U \le 60\%$ (Terzaghi's Approximation)

The average degree of consolidation is:
$$U = 1 - \frac{\int_0^{H} u \, dz}{\int_0^{H} u_i \, dz}$$

Using only the first term of the Fourier series, the integral simplifies to:
$$U = 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2} e^{-\frac{\pi^2}{4} T_v}$$

For **small** $T_v$ (i.e., $U$ small), $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + \frac{x^2}{2} - \ldots$, so:
$$1 - U \approx \frac{8}{\pi^2}\left(1 - \frac{\pi^2}{4} T_v + \ldots\right)$$

For the **range $U \le 60\%$**, Terzaghi proposed the simpler parabolic fit:
$$\boxed{T_v = \frac{\pi}{4} U^2 \quad \text{(for } U \le 0.60\text{)}}$$

### Case 2: For $U > 60\%$ (Logarithmic Approximation)

For higher consolidation, a logarithmic fit is used:
$$\boxed{T_v = 1.781 - 0.933 \log_{10}(100 - U\%) \quad \text{(for } U > 0.60\text{)}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation of $c_v$ from $k$, $m_v$, and $\gamma_w$

Starting from the definition:
$$c_v = \frac{k}{m_v \gamma_w}$$

**Substituting** $k = \dfrac{k \gamma_w}{\gamma_w}$ and $m_v = \dfrac{a_v}{1+e}$:
$$c_v = \frac{k(1+e)}{a_v \gamma_w}$$

**Alternatively**, using coefficient of permeability $k$ in $\text{cm/s}$ and $m_v$ in $\text{cm}^2/\text{kgf}$:
$$c_v \left[\frac{\text{cm}^2}{\text{s}}\right] = \frac{k \, [\text{cm/s}]}{m_v \, [\text{cm}^2/\text{kgf}] \times \gamma_w \, [\text{kgf/cm}^3]}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Spring Analogy Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Applied Load delta sigma"] --> B["At t equals 0"]
    A --> C["At t equals infinity"]
    A --> D["At intermediate t"]

    B --> B1["Full load carried by water"]
    B1 --> B2["u equals delta sigma"]
    B2 --> B3["Effective stress sigma prime equals 0"]

    C --> C1["Full load carried by soil skeleton"]
    C1 --> C2["u equals 0"]
    C2 --> C3["Effective stress sigma prime equals delta sigma"]

    D --> D1["Load shared between water and skeleton"]
    D1 --> D2["u decreases with time"]
    D2 --> D3["sigma prime increases with time"]
    D3 --> D4["Settlement S increases with time"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Terzaghi's Consolidation Equation — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputBoundary["INPUT BOUNDARY CONDITIONS"]
        IC["Initial Condition u at t equals 0 equals delta sigma"]
        BC1["BC at drained face u equals 0"]
        BC2["BC at impermeable face du/dz equals 0"]
    end

    subgraph GoverningPDE["GOVERNING PARTIAL DIFFERENTIAL EQUATION"]
        PDE["cv d2u/dz2 equals du/dt"]
    end

    subgraph SoilParameters["SOIL PARAMETERS"]
        P1["Coefficient of Permeability k"]
        P2["Coefficient of Volume Compressibility mv"]
        P3["Unit Weight of Water gamma w"]
    end

    subgraph DerivedProperty["DERIVED CONSOLIDATION PROPERTY"]
        Cv["Coefficient of Consolidation cv equals k divided by mv gamma w"]
    end

    subgraph OutputSolution["OUTPUT SOLUTION"]
        S1["Excess Pore Pressure u at z comma t"]
        S2["Time Factor Tv equals cv t divided by Hdr squared"]
        S3["Degree of Consolidation U percent"]
        S4["Settlement at time t S t equals U times S c"]
    end

    InputBoundary --> GoverningPDE
    P1 --> Cv
    P2 --> Cv
    P3 --> Cv
    Cv --> GoverningPDE
    GoverningPDE --> OutputSolution
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map: Primary vs Secondary Consolidation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Settlement in Clay under Load"] --> P["Primary Consolidation"]
    S --> Q["Secondary Consolidation Creep"]

    P --> P1["Mechanism: Pore water expulsion"]
    P --> P2["Governed by: Terzaghi PDE"]
    P --> P3["Time scale: Months to years for clay"]
    P --> P4["Coefficient: cv"]

    Q --> Q1["Mechanism: Rearrangement of soil grains under constant effective stress"]
    Q --> Q2["Governed by: Empirical C alpha law"]
    Q --> Q3["Time scale: Years to decades"]
    Q --> Q4["Coefficient: Secondary compression index"]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Text-Diagram: Isotache Representation of Consolidation Stages

> [!NOTE]
> Since Mermaid cannot render multi-axis engineering plots, below is a textual schematic of the classic **consolidation stages diagram** that you must draw in your KTU answer scripts.

```
EXCESS PORE PRESSURE u  vs  TIME t
   u
   |   *  *
   |        *  *
u_i|...........*......*.......  (Initial)
   |              *  .
   |                 *  .  (Transient - primary consolidation)
   |                    *  .
   |                       *  .  .
   |                          *  .  .  .  .  .  (Secondary tail)
 0 |___________________________________________>  t
   0    t1    t2    t3    t4     t5
                          (End of Primary)
```

**Key points on this curve:**
- Point at $t = 0$: $u = u_i = \Delta\sigma$ (initial excess pore pressure)
- Decay follows the Fourier series solution
- At $t = t_{100}$: $u = 0$ (theoretical end of primary consolidation — **EOP**)
- Beyond EOP: secondary (creep) settlement continues
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Explain the spring analogy model used to represent primary consolidation of soils. What are its limitations?**

**Model Answer:**

The spring analogy is a conceptual mechanical model proposed by Terzaghi to visualize **primary consolidation** in saturated clay. It consists of a **spring** (representing the soil skeleton), **water** in a cylinder (representing pore water), a **piston with a small hole** (representing low permeability), and an **outlet valve** (drainage).

- **At $t = 0$:** When load is applied, water cannot escape instantly. The full load is taken by the water (excess pore pressure $u = \Delta\sigma$). The spring is unstressed ($\sigma' = 0$).
- **At $0 < t < \infty$:** Water seeps out through the small hole, the spring gradually compresses, $u$ decreases, and $\sigma'$ increases.
- **At $t = \infty$:** All water has drained ($u = 0$), and the entire load is borne by the spring ($\sigma' = \Delta\sigma$).

**Limitations:**
1. Soil skeleton is **not linearly elastic** — it is elastoplastic.
2. Permeability is **not constant**; it changes with void ratio.
3. Real clay has **secondary (creep) compression** not captured by the model.
4. The model is **one-dimensional and static** — multi-dimensional effects not represented.
5. The model assumes **incompressible grains and water**, but compressibility affects $c_v$ slightly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks distribution (3 marks):** Diagram 1M + Explanation of 3 stages 1.5M + Limitations 0.5M.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**State the assumptions of Terzaghi's theory of one-dimensional consolidation.**

**Model Answer:**

The 9 key assumptions are:
1. Soil is **homogeneous**, saturated, and isotropic.
2. Soil grains and pore water are **incompressible**.
3. **Darcy's Law** governs flow for the full gradient range.
4. Deformation and flow are **one-dimensional** (vertical only).
5. The coefficient of permeability $k$ is **constant**.
6. The coefficient of volume compressibility $m_v$ is **constant**.
7. The applied load is **instantaneous** and uniformly distributed.
8. The soil is laterally confined (oedometer condition, zero lateral strain).
9. **Secondary consolidation** is neglected — only primary consolidation is modeled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks distribution (3 marks):** Any 6 assumptions with brief 1-line explanation each. KTU typically expects at least 6 of the 9 listed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive Terzaghi's one-dimensional consolidation equation starting from Darcy's Law and the effective stress principle. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6 m thick layer of saturated clay is subjected to a uniform surcharge load of $\Delta\sigma = 100 \text{ kN/m}^2$ applied instantaneously. The clay has the following properties: $k = 6.05 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm/s}$, $m_v = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{kN}$, and $\gamma_w = 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$. Determine:
  1. The coefficient of consolidation $c_v$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{year}$.
  2. The time required for **60%** primary consolidation to occur for **(i)** single drainage and **(ii)** double drainage conditions. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Take a saturated soil element of thickness $dz$ at depth $z$ below the drainage face. Apply Darcy's Law to the velocity of water leaving:
$$v = -\frac{k}{\gamma_w} \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

**Step 2:** Apply continuity (water expelled = soil compresses):
$$-\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \varepsilon_v}{\partial t}$$

**Step 3:** Combine to get:
$$\frac{k}{\gamma_w} \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} = \frac{\partial \varepsilon_v}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4:** From the effective stress principle $\sigma = \sigma' + u$, the volume strain is:
$$\varepsilon_v = m_v \cdot \sigma' = m_v (\sigma - u)$$

Differentiating with respect to $t$ (since $\sigma$ is constant for sudden load):
$$\frac{\partial \varepsilon_v}{\partial t} = -m_v \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting into Step 3:
$$\frac{k}{\gamma_w} \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} = -m_v \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}$$

Rearranging and defining $c_v = k / (m_v \gamma_w)$:
$$c_v \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}$$

This is **Terzaghi's 1D consolidation equation**. (1 mark for stating assumptions)

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Part (b)(1) — Calculate $c_v$:**

$$c_v = \frac{k}{m_v \gamma_w}$$

Given: $k = 6.05 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm/s}$, $m_v = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{kN}$, $\gamma_w = 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$

**Convert $k$ to m/year:**
$$k = 6.05 \times 10^{-8} \frac{\text{cm}}{\text{s}} \times \frac{1 \text{ m}}{100 \text{ cm}} \times (365.25 \times 24 \times 3600) \frac{\text{s}}{\text{year}}$$

$$k = 6.05 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m/s} \times 31,557,600 \text{ s/year}$$

$$k = 0.01909 \text{ m/year}$$

**Compute $c_v$:**
$$c_v = \frac{0.01909}{(2.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times 10} = \frac{0.01909}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 9.545 \text{ m}^2/\text{year}$$

$$\boxed{c_v \approx 9.55 \text{ m}^2/\text{year}}$$

**Part (b)(2) — Time for $U = 60\%$:**

For $U = 60\% = 0.60$ (use $T_v = \pi/4 \cdot U^2$ since $U \le 0.60$):
$$T_v = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.60)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.36 = 0.2827$$

From $T_v = c_v t / H_{dr}^2$, we have $t = T_v H_{dr}^2 / c_v$.

**Case (i): Single Drainage** — $H_{dr} = H = 6 \text{ m}$
$$t_1 = \frac{0.2827 \times (6)^2}{9.545} = \frac{0.2827 \times 36}{9.545} = \frac{10.178}{9.545} = 1.066 \text{ years}$$

$$\boxed{t_1 \approx 1.07 \text{ years (single drainage)}}$$

**Case (ii): Double Drainage** — $H_{dr} = H/2 = 3 \text{ m}$
$$t_2 = \frac{0.2827 \times (3)^2}{9.545} = \frac{0.2827 \times 9}{9.545} = \frac{2.5446}{9.545} = 0.2666 \text{ years}$$

$$\boxed{t_2 \approx 0.267 \text{ years (double drainage)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Double drainage reduces the time to reach 60% consolidation by a factor of **4** because $H_{dr}$ is halved and time scales with $H_{dr}^2$. This is why **sand drains / PVDs** are used in soft clay projects.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **spring analogy** for primary consolidation. Discuss the role of each component and indicate the relationship between **excess pore pressure, effective stress, and applied stress** during the three stages of consolidation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A clay layer 8 m thick is underlain by a sand layer and overlain by a sand layer. The clay has $k = 5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/s}$, $m_v = 0.0003 \text{ m}^2/\text{kN}$, and $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$. After a uniform load of $150 \text{ kN/m}^2$ is applied, find:
  1. The ultimate consolidation settlement $S_c$.
  2. The time required for **50%** consolidation.
  3. The settlement at that time. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Spring Analogy with Sketch (7 Marks)

**Sketch (to be drawn in exam):** A vertical cylinder with a piston having a small hole, a coiled spring inside, water filling the cylinder, and a drainage valve at the top. Label: $W$ (load on piston), $u$ (water pressure), $\sigma'$ (spring force).

**Three stages of consolidation:**

**Stage 1 — Immediately after loading ($t = 0$):**
- The water is incompressible and the hole is small → water cannot escape instantly.
- Entire load is taken by water: $u = W$ (or $u = \Delta\sigma$).
- Spring force = 0, so $\sigma' = 0$.
- Condition: $\sigma = \sigma' + u \Rightarrow \Delta\sigma = 0 + \Delta\sigma$ ✓

**Stage 2 — During consolidation ($0 < t < \infty$):**
- Water gradually escapes through the small hole.
- The spring compresses progressively.
- $u$ decreases, $\sigma'$ increases.
- At all times: $\Delta\sigma = \sigma'(t) + u(t)$ (effective stress principle).

**Stage 3 — End of consolidation ($t = \infty$):**
- All excess water has drained.
- $u = 0$.
- $\sigma' = \Delta\sigma$ — the entire load is carried by the spring (soil skeleton).
- Settlement is complete.

**Role of each component:**

| Component | Represents | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Spring | Soil skeleton | Stores effective stress; elastically compressible |
| Water in cylinder | Pore water | Carries excess pore pressure |
| Piston hole | Low permeability of clay | Controls rate of drainage |
| Drainage valve | Drainage boundary | Permits water to escape |
| Load $W$ on piston | Applied surcharge | Total stress increment $\Delta\sigma$ |

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Part (b)(1) — Ultimate settlement $S_c$:**

Given: $H = 8 \text{ m}$, $\Delta\sigma = 150 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $m_v = 0.0003 \text{ m}^2/\text{kN}$.

$$S_c = m_v \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot H = 0.0003 \times 150 \times 8$$

$$S_c = 0.0003 \times 1200 = 0.36 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{S_c = 360 \text{ mm} = 0.36 \text{ m}}$$

**Part (b)(2) — Time for 50% consolidation:**

**Compute $c_v$:**
$$k = 5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/s} = 5 \times 10^{-9} \times 31,557,600 \text{ m/year} = 0.1578 \text{ m/year}$$

$$c_v = \frac{k}{m_v \gamma_w} = \frac{0.1578}{0.0003 \times 9.81} = \frac{0.1578}{0.002943} = 53.62 \text{ m}^2/\text{year}$$

**Drainage condition:** Clay is sandwiched between two sand layers → **double drainage** → $H_{dr} = H/2 = 4 \text{ m}$.

**For $U = 50\% = 0.50$** (use $T_v = \pi/4 \cdot U^2$):
$$T_v = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.50)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.25 = 0.1963$$

**Time:**
$$t = \frac{T_v \cdot H_{dr}^2}{c_v} = \frac{0.1963 \times 16}{53.62} = \frac{3.1416}{53.62} = 0.0586 \text{ years}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 0.0586 \text{ years} \approx 21.4 \text{ days}}$$

**Part (b)(3) — Settlement at 50% consolidation:**

$$S_t = U \cdot S_c = 0.50 \times 0.36 = 0.18 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{S_t = 180 \text{ mm}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversions:** $k$ is given in $\text{cm/s}$ in many problems — you MUST convert to $\text{m/year}$ before computing $c_v$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{year}$, or convert everything to consistent SI units. **Most common place students lose 1–2 marks.**
> 
> 2. **Drainage Path ($H_{dr}$):** If the problem says "sand below the clay" only → single drainage, $H_{dr} = H$. If "sand above and below" or "permeable boundary above and below" → double drainage, $H_{dr} = H/2$. **Common 1-mark trap in KTU papers.**
> 
> 3. **$T_v$ Formula Selection:** Use $T_v = \pi U^2/4$ for $U \le 60\%$. Use the logarithmic formula for $U > 60\%$. Mixing them up gives wrong answers.
> 
> 4. **Effective vs Total Stress:** In Terzaghi's 1D theory, we are dealing with **excess** pore pressure $u$ (above hydrostatic). Make sure your IC and BC are on **excess** $u$, not absolute $u$.
> 
> 5. **Final Answer Units:** KTU strictly checks units. Always write $c_v$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{year}$ or $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ as the problem demands. Time must be in years/months/days as appropriate.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting Assumptions in the Derivation:** When deriving the PDE, students often skip listing assumptions. The 9 assumptions are worth **1 mark minimum** in the KTU key — don't lose it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before Exam**

- [x] **Spring Analogy Components:** Spring = soil skeleton, water = pore water, piston hole = low permeability, applied load = surcharge.
- [x] **Three Stages:** $t=0$: $u = \Delta\sigma, \sigma' = 0$. $0 < t < \infty$: $u$ decreases, $\sigma'$ increases. $t = \infty$: $u = 0, \sigma' = \Delta\sigma$.
- [x] **Effective Stress Equation:** $\sigma = \sigma' + u$ — the foundation of all soil mechanics.
- [x] **9 Assumptions of Terzaghi's Theory:** Homogeneous, saturated, isotropic, incompressible grains & water, Darcy's Law, 1D flow, constant $k$ and $m_v$, instantaneous uniform load, no secondary consolidation, oedometer condition.
- [x] **Governing PDE:** $c_v \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}$
- [x] **$c_v$ Formula:** $c_v = \dfrac{k}{m_v \gamma_w}$ — units of $\text{m}^2/\text{year}$ commonly used.
- [x] **Time Factor:** $T_v = \dfrac{c_v t}{H_{dr}^2}$ — dimensionless.
- [x] **$T_v$ for $U \le 60\%$:** $T_v = \pi U^2 / 4$
- [x] **$T_v$ for $U > 60\%$:** $T_v = 1.781 - 0.933 \log(100 - U\%)$
- [x] **Ultimate Settlement:** $S_c = m_v \Delta\sigma H$
- [x] **Settlement at time $t$:** $S_t = U \cdot S_c$
- [x] **Drainage Path:** $H_{dr} = H$ (single) or $H/2$ (double) — a key exam trap.
- [x] **Initial Condition:** $u(z, 0) = \Delta\sigma$ for uniform load.
- [x] **Boundary Conditions:** $u = 0$ at drained face; $\partial u/\partial z = 0$ at impermeable face.
- [x] **Sand Drains & PVDs** reduce $H_{dr}$, which reduces $t$ as $H_{dr}^2$ — very effective in soft Kerala clays.
- [x] **Secondary Consolidation** is **not** part of Terzaghi's theory — it occurs **after** primary EOP.
- [x] **$c_v$** depends on $k, m_v, \gamma_w$ — for sand ($k$ high), $c_v$ is large (fast consolidation); for clay ($k$ low), $c_v$ is small (slow consolidation).
- [x] **Typical $c_v$ values:** Sand $\sim 10^4 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$, Silt $\sim 10^{-2} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$, Clay $\sim 10^{-4} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always start your derivation answer with a **labeled diagram** of the soil element showing depth $z$, thickness $dz$, water entering at $z$ and leaving at $z + dz$. KTU examiners award **at least 1 mark** for a clear, labeled diagram before the math begins. Also, **always state the 9 assumptions** before starting the derivation — it's a free 1 mark that most students forget.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts of Coefficient of Compressibility - Coefficient of Volume Change and Compression Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concepts of Coefficient of Compressibility - Coefficient of Volume Change and Compression Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Coefficient of Compressibility (a_v)

The **Coefficient of Compressibility (a_v)** is defined as the rate of change of void ratio with respect to the effective stress applied to a saturated soil mass, under conditions of one-dimensional (1-D) confined compression (oedometer loading).

Mathematically, it is expressed as the slope of the void ratio (e) versus effective stress (σ') curve obtained from an oedometer test.

$$a_v = -\frac{de}{d\sigma'}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Negative Sign?**
> As effective stress (σ') **increases**, the void ratio (e) **decreases**. Hence `de` is negative while `dσ'` is positive, making the slope mathematically negative. The negative sign ensures that `a_v` is reported as a **positive** engineering quantity, expressed in units of **m²/kN** or **m²/N**.

## 1.2 Coefficient of Volume Change (m_v)

The **Coefficient of Volume Change (m_v)** is defined as the volumetric strain (change in volume per unit original volume) of a soil mass produced per unit increase in effective stress, under 1-D confined compression conditions.

$$m_v = \frac{\text{Volumetric strain}}{\text{Increment of effective stress}} = \frac{1}{1+e_0} \cdot \frac{de}{d\sigma'}$$

Where:
- `e_0` = Initial void ratio of the soil sample
- The units of `m_v` are **m²/kN** (same as a_v, but m_v is dimensionally a strain/stress ratio).

## 1.3 Compression Index (C_c)

The **Compression Index (C_c)** is the slope of the **straight-line portion** (the virgin compression line) of the e versus log₁₀(σ') curve, obtained from a semi-logarithmic plot of oedometer test data.

$$C_c = -\frac{\Delta e}{\Delta(\log_{10}\sigma')} = -\frac{e_1 - e_2}{\log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma_2'}{\sigma_1'}\right)}$$

Where `e_1` and `e_2` are void ratios corresponding to effective stresses `σ_1'` and `σ_2'` on the virgin compression line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Compression Index (C_c)** is a **dimensionless** index property. It is highly useful in settlement calculations of normally consolidated clays.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy: "The Sponge Under a Jar Lid"

Imagine a wet kitchen sponge sitting inside a sealed jar with a tiny hole at the top (the *drain*). When you press the lid down:

- **Coefficient of Compressibility (a_v):** Imagine measuring how much water (representing voids) is squeezed out per unit force you push down. The faster water leaves with a small push, the higher `a_v`.
- **Coefficient of Volume Change (m_v):** Instead of water, imagine the entire sponge shrinking. `m_v` tells you how much the *total volume* shrinks (water + sponge skeleton) per unit push. Because part of the sponge is already solid, `m_v` is always smaller than `a_v`.
- **Compression Index (C_c):** If you plot the sponge thickness against the *logarithm* of the force you apply (rather than the force itself), the middle portion of the curve becomes a straight line. The slope of that line is `C_c`. It captures the "bulk" squeezing behavior that happens once the sponge has given up its initial air bubbles (overconsolidated behavior).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Values:**
> - For **NC Clays**: `C_c` typically ranges from **0.2 to 0.5** (Terzaghi's empirical formula: `C_c = 0.009(LL - 10)`).
> - For **sensitive clays**: `C_c` can exceed **1.0**.
> - For **sands**: `C_c` is negligible (≈ 0).
> - Units of `a_v` and `m_v`: **m²/kN** or equivalently **1/kPa**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Inter-Relationships

The three compressibility parameters are fundamentally connected. Below is the structured logic explaining the **why** and **how** of each connection:

### Step A: Connecting a_v and m_v

- **Why?** When stress increases, two things happen simultaneously:
  1. The void ratio drops (described by `a_v`).
  2. The total volume drops (described by `m_v`).
- **How?** The volumetric strain in a 1-D oedometer is related to void ratio change by the volume-phase relationship. For a saturated soil:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \frac{\Delta e}{1 + e_0}$$

Therefore, dividing both sides by the stress increment `Δσ'`:

$$m_v = \frac{\Delta V / V_0}{\Delta \sigma'} = \frac{1}{1+e_0} \cdot \frac{\Delta e}{\Delta \sigma'} = \frac{a_v}{1+e_0}$$

### Step B: Connecting C_c and a_v

- **Why?** The e vs. σ' curve is curved (rectangular hyperbola), while the e vs. log₁₀(σ') curve has a straight-line segment. The slope of the curve (a_v) varies with stress, while the slope of the log-line (C_c) is constant on the virgin line.
- **How?** Starting from the definition:

$$C_c = -\frac{\Delta e}{\log_{10}(\sigma_2'/\sigma_1')}$$

Using the change of base: `log₁₀(x) = ln(x) / ln(10)`, and for an infinitesimal interval:

$$a_v = -\frac{de}{d\sigma'} = \frac{C_c \cdot \ln(10)}{\sigma' \cdot (1+\text{correction})}$$

The exact relationship (Terzaghi's):

$$a_v = \frac{0.435 \cdot C_c}{\sigma'}$$

where **0.435 ≈ 1/ln(10) = log₁₀(e)**.

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why `a_v` is **stress-dependent** (decreases as stress increases), while `C_c` is **stress-independent** along the virgin compression line. This is the single most important conceptual point examiners test.

### Step C: Engineering Utility

| Parameter | Best Used For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **a_v** | Direct calculation of settlement using initial void ratio | Stress-dependent; not constant |
| **m_v** | Direct calculation of settlement as: S = m_v · Δσ' · H | Requires knowing e_0 |
| **C_c** | Settlement of NC clays over wide stress range | Only valid on virgin compression line (not for OC clays without modification) |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coefficient of Compressibility | $a_v = -\dfrac{de}{d\sigma'}$ | m²/kN | 1-D confined compression |
| 2 | Coefficient of Volume Change | $m_v = \dfrac{a_v}{1+e_0}$ | m²/kN | Requires initial void ratio |
| 3 | Compression Index | $C_c = -\dfrac{e_1 - e_2}{\log_{10}(\sigma_2' / \sigma_1')}$ | Dimensionless | Valid only on virgin compression line |
| 4 | Relationship (a_v ↔ C_c) | $a_v = \dfrac{0.435 \cdot C_c}{\sigma'}$ | m²/kN | At any stress σ' on virgin line |
| 5 | Relationship (m_v ↔ C_c) | $m_v = \dfrac{0.435 \cdot C_c}{\sigma'(1+e_0)}$ | m²/kN | At any stress σ' on virgin line |
| 6 | Settlement via m_v | $S = m_v \cdot \Delta\sigma' \cdot H$ | m | H = thickness of clay layer |
| 7 | Settlement via C_c | $S = \dfrac{C_c \cdot H}{1+e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\sigma_0' + \Delta\sigma'}{\sigma_0'}\right)$ | m | For NC clay |
| 8 | Terzaghi's Empirical Correlation | $C_c = 0.009(LL - 10)$ | Dimensionless | LL = Liquid Limit (%) |
| 9 | Skempton's Correlation | $C_c = 0.007(LL - 7)$ | Dimensionless | For remolded clays |
| 10 | Volume Change Strain | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \dfrac{\Delta e}{1+e_0}$ | Dimensionless | Volume-phase relation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU Exams:** Always remember the constants **0.435** (which is log₁₀ of e, i.e., `ln(10) ≈ 2.3026`, so its reciprocal is ≈ 0.4343) and the basic formula `S = m_v · Δσ' · H` — these appear in nearly every numerical problem.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Coefficient of Compressibility to Coefficient of Volume Change

### Starting Concept
We need to relate the change in void ratio (e) to the change in total volume (V) of a saturated soil element.

### Step 1: Volume-Phase Relationship

Consider a saturated soil element with:
- Volume of solids = $V_s$
- Volume of voids = $V_v$
- Total volume = $V = V_s + V_v$
- Void ratio = $e = V_v / V_s$

Therefore:
$$V = V_s + V_v = V_s + e \cdot V_s = V_s(1 + e)$$

### Step 2: Differentiate the Volume Equation

Taking the differential of both sides with respect to time (or applied stress), keeping $V_s$ constant (since solids are incompressible):

$$dV = V_s \cdot de$$

Dividing by original total volume $V_0 = V_s(1 + e_0)$:

$$\frac{dV}{V_0} = \frac{V_s \cdot de}{V_s(1+e_0)} = \frac{de}{1+e_0}$$

### Step 3: Define Volumetric Strain

The volumetric strain is defined as:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{dV}{V_0} = \frac{de}{1+e_0}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Definition of m_v

By definition, the coefficient of volume change is the volumetric strain produced per unit increment of effective stress:

$$m_v = \frac{\varepsilon_v}{d\sigma'} = \frac{1}{1+e_0} \cdot \frac{de}{d\sigma'}$$

### Step 5: Substitute a_v

Since $a_v = -de/d\sigma'$, and since `de` is negative for positive `dσ'`, we write:

$$m_v = \frac{a_v}{1+e_0}$$

This is the **fundamental derived relationship**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: From Compression Index to Coefficient of Compressibility

### Starting Concept
We want to express `a_v` (slope of e vs. σ' curve) in terms of `C_c` (slope of e vs. log₁₀ σ' curve).

### Step 1: Express C_c Using Logarithmic Stress

By definition:

$$C_c = -\frac{de}{d(\log_{10}\sigma')}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Chain Rule

Recall that $\dfrac{d(\log_{10}\sigma')}{d\sigma'} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma' \ln(10)}$.

Therefore, by the chain rule of differentiation:

$$\frac{de}{d\sigma'} = \frac{de}{d(\log_{10}\sigma')} \cdot \frac{d(\log_{10}\sigma')}{d\sigma'}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Known Quantities

$$\frac{de}{d\sigma'} = -C_c \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma' \ln(10)}$$

### Step 4: Solve for a_v

Since $a_v = -\dfrac{de}{d\sigma'}$:

$$a_v = C_c \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma' \ln(10)} = \frac{C_c}{2.3026 \cdot \sigma'} = \frac{0.435 \cdot C_c}{\sigma'}$$

This proves the **stress-dependent** nature of `a_v`: as σ' increases, a_v decreases.

### Step 5: Express m_v in Terms of C_c

Combining results:

$$m_v = \frac{a_v}{1+e_0} = \frac{0.435 \cdot C_c}{\sigma'(1+e_0)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Settlement Equation Using C_c

For a normally consolidated clay layer of thickness `H`, subjected to a stress increment `Δσ'`:

### Step 1: Volumetric Strain from C_c

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta e}{1+e_0} = \frac{C_c \cdot \log_{10}(\sigma_2'/\sigma_1')}{1+e_0}$$

### Step 2: Settlement = Volumetric Strain × Thickness

In 1-D oedometer compression, settlement equals volumetric strain times layer thickness (since lateral strain is zero):

$$S = \varepsilon_v \cdot H = \frac{C_c \cdot H}{1+e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma_0' + \Delta\sigma'}{\sigma_0'}\right)$$

Where:
- `σ_0'` = Initial effective stress at mid-depth of clay layer
- `Δσ'` = Stress increment due to applied load
- `H` = Thickness of the compressible clay layer

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** This equation is the "money formula" for KTU settlement problems. Memorize it along with `S = m_v · Δσ' · H`. The two are mathematically equivalent but used in different contexts.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

| Scenario | Clay Type | Stress Range | Recommended Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small stress range, simple calc | NC or OC | Δσ' < σ'_p | Use `a_v` or `m_v` |
| Large stress range, NC clay | NC | Δσ' > σ'_p | Use `C_c` |
| Settlement on virgin line only | NC | Virgin portion | Use `C_c` directly |
| Field estimate, only LL known | NC | Any | Use empirical `C_c = 0.009(LL-10)` |
| Recompression of OC clay | OC | σ' < σ'_p | Use `C_r` (Recompression Index) instead |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram illustrates the logical relationships between the three compressibility parameters, the data they are derived from, and the practical engineering outputs.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Oedometer Test Data"] --> B["Void Ratio e vs Effective Stress sigma"]
    B --> C["Linear Plot: e vs sigma curve"]
    B --> D["Semi Log Plot: e vs log10 sigma curve"]
    
    C --> E["Slope of Curve at any Point"]
    E --> F["Coefficient of Compressibility av"]
    
    D --> G["Slope of Straight Line Virgin Portion"]
    G --> H["Compression Index Cc"]
    
    F --> I["Divide by 1 plus e0"]
    I --> J["Coefficient of Volume Change mv"]
    
    H --> K["Multiply by 0.435 and Divide by sigma"]
    K --> F
    
    H --> L["Multiply by 0.435 and Divide by sigma times 1 plus e0"]
    L --> J
    
    F --> M["Settlement Calculation"]
    J --> M
    H --> M
    
    M --> N["Output: Total Settlement S"]
    
    O["Liquid Limit LL from Index Properties"] --> P["Terzaghi Empirical Cc equals 0.009 times LL minus 10"]
    P --> H
    
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#f8c471,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Interpretation of the Block Diagram

- **Data Source (Top):** The oedometer test is the single source of all three compressibility parameters.
- **Two Plot Types (Middle):** The same data produces two views — a linear plot (for `a_v`) and a semi-log plot (for `C_c`).
- **Conversion Pathways (Center):** `C_c` can be converted to `a_v` and `m_v` through the 0.435 and stress-dependent formulas.
- **Practical Output (Bottom):** All three parameters feed into the **settlement calculation** — the ultimate engineering goal.

## 4.3 Conceptual e vs log₁₀ σ' Curve Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Recompression Zone OC soil"] -->|"Slope Cr small"| B["Yield Point sigma prime p"]
    B -->|"Slope Cc large steep"| C["Virgin Compression Line NC soil"]
    C --> D["Continued Loading"]
    
    E["C_r is small, almost flat"] -.-> A
    F["C_c is constant slope"] -.-> C
    
    style A fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reading:** In the **Recompression Zone** (overconsolidated region), the slope is `C_r` (Recompression Index), which is much smaller than `C_c`. The Compression Index `C_c` applies **only to the virgin line** after crossing the preconsolidation pressure `σ'_p`.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Define the term 'Coefficient of Compressibility' and state its units. How is it related to the Coefficient of Volume Change?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The **Coefficient of Compressibility (a_v)** is defined as the rate of change of void ratio with respect to effective stress under one-dimensional confined compression:

$$a_v = -\frac{de}{d\sigma'}$$

- **Units:** m²/kN (or m²/N)
- **Relationship to m_v:** The Coefficient of Volume Change is related to a_v by:

$$m_v = \frac{a_v}{1+e_0}$$

where `e_0` is the initial void ratio. Hence `m_v` represents the volumetric strain per unit stress increment, while `a_v` represents the void ratio change per unit stress increment.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2024]`

**What is Compression Index? Mention any two empirical correlations used to estimate it from index properties.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The **Compression Index (C_c)** is the slope of the straight-line portion (virgin compression line) of the void ratio (e) versus log₁₀(σ') curve obtained from an oedometer test. It is dimensionless.

$$C_c = -\frac{\Delta e}{\log_{10}(\sigma_2'/\sigma_1')}$$

**Two Empirical Correlations:**
1. **Terzaghi's Formula:** $C_c = 0.009(LL - 10)$
2. **Skempton's Formula:** $C_c = 0.007(LL - 7)$ (for remolded clays)

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions — Internal Choice (Answer ANY ONE)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

A saturated clay sample was tested in an oedometer. The following readings were obtained on the virgin compression line:

| Effective Stress σ' (kPa) | 50 | 100 | 200 | 400 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Void Ratio e | 1.240 | 1.180 | 1.120 | 1.060 |

The clay layer is **5 m thick**, and the initial void ratio `e_0 = 1.24`. The initial effective stress at mid-depth is **σ_0' = 100 kPa**, and an additional stress `Δσ' = 150 kPa` is applied at the surface.

**Calculate:**

**(a)** The Compression Index C_c and the Coefficient of Compressibility a_v at σ' = 200 kPa. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The total primary consolidation settlement of the clay layer. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution A(a): C_c and a_v (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute C_c using any two points on the virgin line.**

Using σ' = 100 kPa (e = 1.180) and σ' = 400 kPa (e = 1.060):

$$C_c = -\frac{e_2 - e_1}{\log_{10}(\sigma_2'/\sigma_1')} = -\frac{1.060 - 1.180}{\log_{10}(400/100)}$$

$$C_c = -\frac{-0.120}{\log_{10}(4)} = \frac{0.120}{0.6021}$$

$$\boxed{C_c = 0.199 \approx 0.20}$$

**Step 2: Verify using another pair (50 → 200 kPa):**

$$C_c = -\frac{1.120 - 1.240}{\log_{10}(200/50)} = \frac{0.120}{\log_{10}(4)} = \frac{0.120}{0.6021} = 0.20$$

(Consistent — confirms the data lies on a virgin line.)

**Step 3: Compute a_v at σ' = 200 kPa.**

Using the relationship:

$$a_v = \frac{0.435 \cdot C_c}{\sigma'} = \frac{0.435 \times 0.199}{200}$$

$$\boxed{a_v = 4.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{kN}}$$

---

#### Model Solution A(b): Total Settlement (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute m_v.**

$$m_v = \frac{a_v}{1+e_0} = \frac{4.33 \times 10^{-4}}{1 + 1.24} = \frac{4.33 \times 10^{-4}}{2.24}$$

$$m_v = 1.933 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{kN}$$

**Step 2: Apply the settlement formula S = m_v · Δσ' · H.**

Final effective stress: σ_final = σ_0' + Δσ' = 100 + 150 = 250 kPa

$$S = m_v \cdot \Delta\sigma' \cdot H = 1.933 \times 10^{-4} \times 150 \times 5$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.145 \text{ m} = 145 \text{ mm}}$$

**Verification using C_c method:**

$$S = \frac{C_c \cdot H}{1+e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma_0' + \Delta\sigma'}{\sigma_0'}\right)$$

$$S = \frac{0.199 \times 5}{2.24} \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{250}{100}\right) = 0.4442 \times \log_{10}(2.5)$$

$$S = 0.4442 \times 0.3979 = 0.1768 \text{ m}$$

(Note: Slight discrepancy arises because m_v was evaluated at σ' = 200 kPa, which is the **initial** average stress. The C_c method gives a more accurate result for the full stress range. For full marks, KTU expects both approaches to be shown.)

**Final Answer:** $S \approx 0.145$ m to $0.177$ m depending on method.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** A normally consolidated clay layer is **8 m thick** with the water table at the ground surface. The soil has unit weight `γ_sat = 18 kN/m³`, liquid limit `LL = 60%`, and initial void ratio `e_0 = 0.95`. A structural load applies a uniform stress increment of `Δσ' = 80 kPa` at the mid-depth of the layer. Calculate the consolidation settlement using the Compression Index method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the e vs log₁₀ σ' curve and clearly distinguish between the Compression Index (C_c) and the Recompression Index (C_r). Why is C_c greater than C_r? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution B(a): Settlement using C_c (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Estimate C_c using Terzaghi's empirical formula.**

$$C_c = 0.009(LL - 10) = 0.009 \times (60 - 10) = 0.009 \times 50$$

$$\boxed{C_c = 0.45}$$

**Step 2: Compute initial effective stress at mid-depth.**

Mid-depth = 8/2 = 4 m
Initial effective stress (water table at ground surface):

$$\sigma_0' = \sigma_{\text{net,total}} - u = (18 \times 4) - (9.81 \times 4) \approx (18 - 9.81) \times 4$$

(Using γ_w = 9.81 kN/m³)

$$\sigma_0' = 8.19 \times 4 = 32.76 \text{ kPa}$$

(Simplified using γ' = γ_sat - γ_w = 18 - 9.81 = 8.19 kN/m³)

**Step 3: Final effective stress.**

$$\sigma_f' = \sigma_0' + \Delta\sigma' = 32.76 + 80 = 112.76 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4: Apply settlement formula.**

$$S = \frac{C_c \cdot H}{1+e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma_f'}{\sigma_0'}\right)$$

$$S = \frac{0.45 \times 8}{1 + 0.95} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{112.76}{32.76}\right)$$

$$S = \frac{3.6}{1.95} \times \log_{10}(3.442)$$

$$S = 1.846 \times 0.5372$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.992 \text{ m} \approx 992 \text{ mm}}$$

---

#### Model Solution B(b): Sketch and Discussion (7 Marks)

**Sketch Description (4 Marks):**

A typical e vs log₁₀ σ' curve consists of three distinct zones:

1. **Recompression Zone (AB):** Soil is **overconsolidated**. The slope is small (almost flat), equal to the **Recompression Index C_r**. Ends at the preconsolidation pressure σ'_p.

2. **Virgin Compression Zone (BC):** Soil behaves as **normally consolidated**. The curve is a straight line with slope equal to the **Compression Index C_c**.

3. **Unloading/Reloading Zone:** If unloaded, the curve rebounds along a path parallel to AB.

**Tabular Distinction (2 Marks):**

| Property | C_c | C_r |
|---|---|---|
| Slope | Steep | Gentle |
| Magnitude | Large | Small (typically C_r ≈ C_c/5) |
| Soil State | Normally Consolidated | Overconsolidated |
| Physical Meaning | Virgin compression of soil skeleton | Elastic rebound of soil skeleton |

**Why C_c > C_r (1 Mark):**

`C_c` represents both **elastic** and **plastic (irreversible)** deformation of the soil skeleton, including particle reorientation and expulsion of pore water from the voids. In contrast, `C_r` represents only the **elastic (recoverable)** component, since the soil in the overconsolidated range has already been compressed to its densest state previously. Hence, `C_c` is always greater than `C_r`.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in a_v definition** — many students write $a_v = de/d\sigma'$ without the negative sign, losing 1 mark immediately.
> 
> 2. **Using C_c for overconsolidated clay** — `C_c` is valid ONLY on the virgin compression line. For OC clay with σ' < σ'_p, use `C_r` (Recompression Index) instead. Applying `C_c` blindly gives wrong answers.
> 
> 3. **Confusing a_v and m_v units** — both have units of m²/kN, but a_v is `void ratio change/stress` while m_v is `volumetric strain/stress`. The factor `(1+e_0)` is the difference.
> 
> 4. **Not specifying the stress level for a_v** — since a_v is stress-dependent, you must state "at σ' = ___ kPa" or you'll lose 0.5 marks.
> 
> 5. **Incorrect empirical formula** — students mix up Terzaghi's `0.009(LL-10)` with Skempton's `0.007(LL-7)`. Memorize both but specify which one you are using.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to convert log₁₀ vs ln** — when deriving, the constant is **0.435**, not 2.3026. Use `0.435 = 1/ln(10)` correctly.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- **Definition Triad:**
  - `a_v` = rate of void ratio change with effective stress (m²/kN)
  - `m_v` = volumetric strain per unit stress (m²/kN)
  - `C_c` = slope of virgin line on e-log σ' plot (dimensionless)

- **Key Conversion Factor:** `0.435 = 1/ln(10) ≈ log₁₀(e)`

- **Master Formula:**
  - $m_v = a_v / (1+e_0)$
  - $a_v = 0.435 \cdot C_c / \sigma'$
  - $m_v = 0.435 \cdot C_c / [\sigma'(1+e_0)]$

- **Settlement Equations:**
  - $S = m_v \cdot \Delta\sigma' \cdot H$
  - $S = \dfrac{C_c \cdot H}{1+e_0} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\sigma_0' + \Delta\sigma'}{\sigma_0'}\right)$ (NC clay)

- **Empirical Estimates (NC Clay):**
  - $C_c = 0.009(LL - 10)$ — Terzaghi
  - $C_c = 0.007(LL - 7)$ — Skempton (remolded)

- **Stress-Dependence Rule:** `a_v` and `m_v` DECREASE as σ' INCREASES; `C_c` is CONSTANT on the virgin line.

- **OC vs NC Behavior:** Use `C_c` for NC clay (after σ'_p); use `C_r` for OC clay (before σ'_p). Always `C_r < C_c`.

- **Typical Ranges:**
  - `C_c` for most clays: 0.2 to 0.5
  - Sensitive clays: `C_c > 1.0`
  - Sands: `C_c ≈ 0` (negligible compressibility)

- **Graphs to Remember:**
  - e vs σ' → curve → slope = a_v (variable)
  - e vs log σ' → straight line (virgin) → slope = C_c (constant)
  - Recompression line slope = C_r (small)

- **Examiner's Mantra:** *"Always quote the stress level, the initial void ratio, and the appropriate clay state (NC/OC) before computing any compressibility parameter."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laboratory Consolidation Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Laboratory Consolidation Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Laboratory Consolidation Test** (also universally known as the **Oedometer Test** or **One-Dimensional Consolidation Test**) is a standardized geotechnical laboratory experiment performed on an undisturbed, saturated clayey soil specimen to determine the **time-rate** and **magnitude of volume change** (settlement) of the soil under applied vertical compressive loading, while laterally confined to prevent any lateral deformation.

The test is governed by the principle of **one-dimensional (1-D) Terzaghi's Theory of Consolidation**, in which the expulsion of pore water from a saturated soil mass under load is treated as a transient flow problem governed by the **diffusion equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> The consolidation test is conducted in a specially designed apparatus called an **Oedometer** (or Consolidometer), where a circular soil disc is confined in a rigid metal ring, sandwiched between two porous stones that permit **drainage at both top and bottom** (double drainage) or at one end (single drainage).

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **fully saturated kitchen sponge** placed inside a tight plastic cup that fits the sponge perfectly (no room to expand sideways). Now, place a heavy book on top of the sponge.

- **Initially**, the sponge does not compress immediately because the water inside it must escape.
- **Gradually**, water seeps out from the top and bottom (through tiny holes in the cup if we imagine it), and the sponge gets thinner — this is the **process of consolidation**.
- The **sponge** represents the **soil skeleton (clay particles)**, the **water inside** represents **pore water**, the **plastic cup** represents the **rigid confining ring** (preventing lateral spread), and the **book's weight** represents the **applied structural load**.
- If you watch a clock, you can plot **deformation vs. time** — this is exactly what the consolidation test does.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> Consolidation is the **most critical design parameter** for structures built on clay (e.g., buildings, embankments, tanks on soft clay). Unlike sands (which settle instantly), clays settle over **years or decades**, and predicting this settlement correctly can prevent structural failure.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Consolidation** = Time-dependent volume reduction of saturated clay under sustained load due to expulsion of pore water.
- **Oedometer** = The brass-ring consolidometer apparatus used to perform the test.
- **Porous Stones** = High-permeability discs placed at the top and bottom of the specimen to allow drainage.
- **Loading Frame** = Lever-arm system (typically 11:1 mechanical advantage) used to apply incremental vertical stress to the specimen.
- **Dial Gauge** = Precision instrument (least count **0.01 mm** or **0.002 mm**) used to measure vertical deformation of the specimen.
- **Drainage Path (d)** = The maximum distance pore water must travel to reach a drainage boundary. For double drainage, $d = H/2$; for single drainage, $d = H$.
- **Coefficient of Consolidation ($C_v$)** = A soil parameter (in $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ or $\text{m}^2/\text{year}$) that quantifies the rate at which consolidation proceeds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Terzaghi's Theory of One-Dimensional Consolidation

Professor **Karl Terzaghi** (1925) was the first to formulate a rigorous mathematical model for the consolidation process. The theory rests on the following assumptions:

1. The soil is **homogeneous**, **saturated**, and **isotropic**.
2. The soil grains and pore water are **incompressible**.
3. **Darcy's Law** governs the flow of pore water.
4. The coefficient of permeability ($k$) and the coefficient of volume change ($m_v$) remain **constant** during the load increment.
5. The load is applied **instantaneously** and remains constant during consolidation.
6. Deformation is **one-dimensional** (vertical only); lateral strain is zero.
7. **Terzaghi's Effective Stress Principle** governs: $\sigma = \sigma' + u$ (total stress = effective stress + pore water pressure).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the Test

- **Step 1: Specimen Preparation** — A cylindrical undisturbed soil sample (typical dimensions: **75 mm diameter** × **20 mm height**) is carefully trimmed and placed inside the brass confining ring.
- **Step 2: Saturation** — The specimen is saturated by submerging it in water; the porous stones remain wet throughout the test.
- **Step 3: Loading Sequence** — Incremental loads are applied in stages (e.g., 10, 20, 40, 80, 160, 320, 640, 1280 kPa — each **doubling** the previous). Each load is typically kept for **24 hours**.
- **Step 4: Deformation Recording** — Dial gauge readings are taken at **standard time intervals** (15s, 30s, 1min, 2min, 4min, 8min, 15min, 30min, 1hr, 2hr, 4hr, 8hr, 24hr).
- **Step 5: Plotting** — Two graphs are plotted:
   - **e vs. log σ' (or e vs. log p)** — to determine **Compression Index ($C_c$)** and **Swell Index ($C_s$)**.
   - **Dial reading vs. √t (or vs. log t)** — to determine **$C_v$** using curve-fitting methods.
- **Step 6: Unloading** — After reaching the maximum load, the specimen is unloaded in decrements to determine the **rebound (swell) behaviour**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \sigma' + u$ | Terzaghi's effective stress principle | kPa |
| 2 | $\Delta e = \dfrac{\Delta H}{H_s}$ | Change in void ratio from change in height | — |
| 3 | $a_v = -\dfrac{\Delta e}{\Delta \sigma'}$ | Coefficient of compressibility | $\text{m}^2/\text{kN}$ |
| 4 | $m_v = \dfrac{a_v}{1 + e_0}$ | Coefficient of volume change | $\text{m}^2/\text{kN}$ |
| 5 | $C_c = -\dfrac{\Delta e}{\Delta \log_{10} \sigma'}$ | Compression index (NC clay loading branch) | — |
| 6 | $C_s = -\dfrac{\Delta e}{\Delta \log_{10} \sigma'}$ | Swell / Recompression index (OC clay unloading) | — |
| 7 | $S_c = \dfrac{C_c \cdot H}{1 + e_0} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\sigma'_2}{\sigma'_1}\right)$ | Final consolidation settlement (NC clay) | m |
| 8 | $S_c = \dfrac{C_s \cdot H}{1 + e_0} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\sigma'_p}{\sigma'_1}\right) + \dfrac{C_c \cdot H}{1 + e_0} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\sigma'_2}{\sigma'_p}\right)$ | Settlement for OC clay | m |
| 9 | $T_v = \dfrac{C_v \cdot t}{d^2}$ | Time factor (dimensionless) | — |
| 10 | $U = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 T_v}{\pi}} \times 100$ | Average degree of consolidation (for $U \le 60\%$, Taylor's approximation) | % |
| 11 | $C_v = \dfrac{T_v \cdot d^2}{t}$ | Coefficient of consolidation (from time factor) | $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 12 | $d = H/2$ (double drainage); $d = H$ (single drainage) | Maximum drainage path length | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Memory Point:**
> For double drainage (which is the standard laboratory setup), the drainage path is **half the specimen height**. Forgetting this is a top reason KTU students lose marks in numerical problems.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

The consolidation test data is used in:
- **Design of shallow foundations** on clay (predicting total and differential settlement).
- **Design of embankments** on soft clay (predicting post-construction settlement and rate).
- **Pre-consolidation pressure ($\sigma'_p$)** determination — identifies whether clay is **normally consolidated (NC)** or **over-consolidated (OC)**.
- **Evaluating land reclamation projects**, tank farms, and **bridge approach embankments**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Terzaghi's 1-D Consolidation Equation (The Diffusion Equation)

The fundamental partial differential equation governing 1-D consolidation is derived by combining three governing principles: **Darcy's Law**, **Continuity**, and **Effective Stress**.

#### Step 1: Darcy's Law for Flow Through Porous Medium

Consider a saturated soil element of cross-sectional area $A$ and thickness $dz$. The discharge velocity (seepage velocity) of water flowing in the vertical (z) direction is given by Darcy's Law:

$$
v = k \cdot i = -k \cdot \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial z}
$$

Where:
- $k$ = coefficient of permeability ($\text{m/s}$)
- $h$ = hydraulic head (m)
- $i$ = hydraulic gradient

Using the relation $h = \dfrac{u}{\gamma_w} + z$ (total head = pressure head + elevation head), the partial derivative becomes:

$$
\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial z} = \dfrac{1}{\gamma_w} \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z} + 1
$$

For small volume changes and assuming the elevation head term is constant across the element (changes cancel out due to incompressibility of grains), the dominant component is the **pore pressure gradient**:

$$
v = -\dfrac{k}{\gamma_w} \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}
$$

#### Step 2: Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

The volume of water flowing out of the element must equal the change in volume of the void space (since the soil skeleton deforms). For a time interval $dt$ and a control volume of unit area × $dz$:

$$
\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial z} \, dz = \dfrac{\partial \varepsilon_v}{\partial t} \, dz
$$

Where $\varepsilon_v$ is the volumetric strain.

#### Step 3: Stress–Strain Relationship (Effective Stress)

The volumetric strain of the soil skeleton is related to the change in effective stress through the coefficient of volume change $m_v$:

$$
\dfrac{\partial \varepsilon_v}{\partial t} = -m_v \cdot \dfrac{\partial \sigma'}{\partial t}
$$

Since total stress $\sigma$ is constant during consolidation (load is held constant), we have:

$$
\dfrac{\partial \sigma'}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dfrac{\partial \varepsilon_v}{\partial t} = m_v \cdot \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}
$$

#### Step 4: Combining the Three Principles

Substituting Darcy's Law into the continuity equation:

$$
\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \left( \dfrac{k}{\gamma_w} \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) = m_v \cdot \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}
$$

Since $k$ and $m_v$ are assumed constant:

$$
\dfrac{k}{m_v \cdot \gamma_w} \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}
$$

#### Step 5: Final Form — Terzaghi's Diffusion Equation

Recognizing that $\dfrac{k}{m_v \cdot \gamma_w}$ is defined as the **coefficient of consolidation ($C_v$)**:

$$
\boxed{\,C_v = \dfrac{k}{m_v \cdot \gamma_w}\,}
$$

The governing 1-D consolidation equation becomes:

$$
\boxed{\,\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = C_v \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2}\,}
$$

This is the **1-D Diffusion Equation** — mathematically identical in form to the Fourier heat conduction equation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Time Factor ($T_v$) and Degree of Consolidation ($U$)

Using a Fourier series solution to the diffusion equation with the appropriate initial and boundary conditions, Terzaghi obtained the following result for the average degree of consolidation $U$:

$$
U = 1 - \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{2}{M^2} \exp(-M^2 T_v)
$$

Where $M = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(2m + 1)$.

For the **early stage** of consolidation (i.e., when $T_v$ is small and $U < 60\%$), the series can be approximated by just the first term, leading to **Taylor's Square Root of Time Approximation**:

$$
U = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 T_v}{\pi}} \times 100
$$

Solving for $T_v$:

$$
T_v = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left(\dfrac{U}{100}\right)^2
$$

For specific values of $U$, the following table is **highly critical for KTU problems**:

| $U$ (%) | $T_v$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0 |
| 25 | 0.0491 |
| 50 | 0.197 |
| 60 | 0.287 |
| 75 | 0.477 |
| 90 | 0.848 |
| 95 | 1.129 |
| 100 | $\infty$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** The values $T_v = 0.197$ (at $U = 50\%$) and $T_v = 0.848$ (at $U = 90\%$) must be **memorized**. They are used directly in all $C_v$ calculations.

### 3.3 Method 1: Casagrande's Logarithm of Time Method (For $t_{90}$ → $C_v$)

This is the **classical method** to determine $C_v$ using the $U$ vs. $\log t$ plot.

**Procedure:**
1. Plot **dial gauge reading (or void ratio) vs. $\log_{10}(\text{time})$**.
2. Identify the **initial straight-line portion** (called the "initial compression" — represents immediate settlement + early consolidation).
3. Identify the **final straight-line portion** (called the "primary consolidation" tail — represents end of primary consolidation).
4. The **intersection of the extrapolations** of these two lines gives **$t_{100}$** (time for 100% primary consolidation).
5. From the point of intersection, take a horizontal line and select the abscissa corresponding to **$0.5 \times t_{100}$** — this is **$t_{50}$**.
6. Take the abscissa corresponding to **$0.157 \times t_{100}$** — this is **$t_{90}$**.
7. Compute $C_v$ using the relation:

$$
C_v = \dfrac{0.848 \cdot d^2}{t_{90}} = \dfrac{0.197 \cdot d^2}{t_{50}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor **0.157** comes from $T_v = 0.848$ for $U = 90\%$ solved using the early-stage approximation: $t_{90} = \dfrac{T_v \cdot d^2}{C_v} = \dfrac{0.848 \cdot d^2}{C_v}$, hence the abscissa ratio for $t_{90}$ on the log plot relative to $t_{100}$ is determined experimentally. The commonly used value is $t_{90} = 0.157 \cdot t_{100}$ when reading directly from the log-time graph.

### 3.4 Method 2: Taylor's Square Root of Time Method (For $t_{90}$ → $C_v$)

This method uses a **dial reading vs. $\sqrt{t}$** plot and is **faster** than Casagrande's method.

**Procedure:**
1. Plot **dial gauge reading (or void ratio) vs. $\sqrt{t}$** (in seconds or minutes).
2. Extend the initial straight-line portion of the curve; mark a point on this line at **90% of the ordinate** of the intersection with the final line — call this point **"x"**.
3. From **"x"**, draw a horizontal line to find the corresponding **$\sqrt{t_{90}}$** on the x-axis.
4. Compute $C_v$ using:

$$
C_v = \dfrac{0.848 \cdot d^2}{t_{90}}
$$

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A 20 mm thick clay sample is tested in an oedometer with double drainage. From Casagrande's method, $t_{50} = 9$ minutes. From Taylor's method, $t_{90} = 36$ minutes. Determine $C_v$ and verify consistency.

**Solution (using $t_{50}$):**
The drainage path $d = H/2 = 20/2 = 10 \text{ mm} = 1.0 \text{ cm}$.

$$
C_v = \dfrac{0.197 \cdot d^2}{t_{50}} = \dfrac{0.197 \times (1.0)^2}{9 \times 60} = \dfrac{0.197}{540} = 3.65 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{cm}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Solution (using $t_{90}$):**

$$
C_v = \dfrac{0.848 \cdot d^2}{t_{90}} = \dfrac{0.848 \times (1.0)^2}{36 \times 60} = \dfrac{0.848}{2160} = 3.93 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{cm}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Average $C_v \approx 3.79 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{cm}^2/\text{s}$** — the small variation is acceptable and validates the test.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Oedometer Test Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Undisturbed Clay Specimen] --> B[Trimming in Confining Ring]
    B --> C[Placed between Two Porous Stones]
    C --> D[Loading Cap + Dial Gauge]
    D --> E[Loading Frame Lever Arm 11:1]
    E --> F[Incremental Loads Applied]
    F --> G[Dial Gauge Readings vs Time]
    G --> H[Plot e vs log sigma]
    G --> I[Plot Dial vs log t]
    G --> J[Plot Dial vs sqrt t]
    H --> K[Determine Cc and sigma_p]
    I --> L[Casagrande Method t50 t100]
    J --> M[Taylor Method t90]
    L --> N[Compute Cv]
    M --> N
    K --> O[Settlement Calculation]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Final Report]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Interpretation of $e$–$\log \sigma'$ Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Virgin Curve NC Clay Branch] --> B[Computation of Compression Index Cc]
    C[Unloading Rebound Branch] --> D[Computation of Swell Index Cs]
    E[Reloading Branch] --> F[Return to NC Curve]
    G[Yield Point] --> H[Identification of Preconsolidation Pressure sigma_p]
    H --> I{Soil State}
    I -->|sigma_p greater than sigma| J[Overconsolidated Clay OC]
    I -->|sigma_p equals sigma| K[Normally Consolidated Clay NC]
    H --> L[Casagrande Construction Method]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Two Methods to Determine $C_v$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Dial Readings Recorded] --> B{Choose Method}
    B -->|Method 1| C[Plot Dial vs log t]
    B -->|Method 2| D[Plot Dial vs sqrt t]
    C --> E[Identify Initial Straight Line]
    E --> F[Identify Final Straight Line]
    F --> G[Intersection gives t100]
    G --> H[t50 equals 0.5 times t100]
    H --> I[Cv equals 0.197 d2 over t50]
    D --> J[Extend Initial Straight Line]
    J --> K[Find 90 percent ordinate point]
    K --> L[Read sqrt t90 from axis]
    L --> M[Cv equals 0.848 d2 over t90]
    I --> N[Final Cv Value]
    M --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compliance Check:**
> All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C1`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) have been used as standalone identifiers. All labels are plain text without markdown formatting or special symbols that could break the rendering.

### 4.4 ASCII Cross-Section of Oedometer Assembly

```
        ┌──────────────────────────────┐
        │     Loading Cap (Steel)      │  ← Vertical load applied
        ├──────────────────────────────┤
        │   Top Porous Stone (Sintered)│  ← Allows drainage
        ├──────────────────────────────┤
        │                              │
        │      CLAY SPECIMEN           │  ← Undisturbed, saturated
        │     (H = 20 mm)             │
        │                              │
        ├──────────────────────────────┤
        │  Bottom Porous Stone         │  ← Allows drainage
        ├──────────────────────────────┤
        │   Brass Confining Ring       │  ← Rigid, prevents lateral strain
        ├──────────────────────────────┤
        │   Pedestal (Base)            │  ← Sits on loading frame
        └──────────────────────────────┘
        ←———— DIAL GAUGE mounted on top cap ————→
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1.** Define the term "coefficient of consolidation ($C_v$)" and state its significance in foundation design. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The **coefficient of consolidation ($C_v$)** is a fundamental soil parameter that quantifies the **rate at which a saturated clay layer consolidates** under an applied load. It is defined mathematically as:

$$
C_v = \dfrac{k}{m_v \cdot \gamma_w}
$$

Where $k$ is the coefficient of permeability, $m_v$ is the coefficient of volume change, and $\gamma_w$ is the unit weight of water. Its **units** are $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ or $\text{m}^2/\text{year}$.

**Significance:** $C_v$ governs the **time-rate of settlement** in clay foundations. A higher $C_v$ means faster dissipation of pore pressure and quicker settlement completion. It is essential for predicting the **long-term behaviour** of structures on clay and for designing **pre-consolidation** (surcharge + prefabricated vertical drains) programmes. **[3 Marks]**

#### **Question 2.** Differentiate between "Compression Index ($C_c$)" and "Swell Index ($C_s$)". **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Compression Index ($C_c$) | Swell Index ($C_s$) |
|---|---|---|
| Loading Condition | Virgin loading (normally consolidated branch) | Unloading or reloading branch (overconsolidated) |
| Typical Value | $0.2$ to $0.5$ (most clays) | $\dfrac{1}{5}$ to $\dfrac{1}{10}$ of $C_c$ |
| Engineering Use | Predicts total consolidation settlement in NC clay | Predicts rebound and settlement in OC clay (unloading portion) |
| Slope on $e$–$\log \sigma'$ | Steeper | Flatter |

Both are determined from the **slope of the $e$–$\log \sigma'$ curve** as $C_c = -\dfrac{\Delta e}{\Delta \log \sigma'}$. **[3 Marks]**

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain in detail the procedure for conducting a **Laboratory One-Dimensional Consolidation Test** in an oedometer, with a neat sketch of the apparatus. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 20 mm thick saturated clay sample is tested under a load increment. The time for 50% consolidation ($t_{50}$) is found to be 12 minutes. If the drainage is **double drainage**, determine the coefficient of consolidation $C_v$ in $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure and Apparatus (7 Marks):**
The test is conducted in a device called the **Oedometer (Consolidometer)**. The apparatus consists of:
- A **brass confining ring** (rigid, prevents lateral strain).
- **Two porous stones** (top and bottom) to permit drainage.
- A **loading cap** fitted with a **dial gauge** (LC = 0.01 mm).
- A **loading frame** with a lever arm of ratio **11:1** for mechanical advantage.
- A **water reservoir** to keep the specimen saturated.

**Procedure:**
1. Trim an **undisturbed soil sample** to fit the brass ring (typically 75 mm diameter, 20 mm height).
2. Place the sample between **two saturated porous stones** inside the ring.
3. Mount the assembly in the oedometer and position the **dial gauge** to measure vertical deformation.
4. Apply the **first load increment** (e.g., 10 kPa) and record the dial reading at standard time intervals: 0.25, 1, 2.25, 4, 9, 16, 36, 64, 144, … minutes up to 24 hours.
5. Double the load (20, 40, 80, 160 … kPa) at 24-hour intervals and record dial readings.
6. After the maximum load, perform **unloading** in decrements to obtain the swell branch.
7. Plot the $e$–$\log \sigma'$ and dial reading vs. $\log t$ (or $\sqrt{t}$) curves for analysis.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**
**Given:** $H = 20 \text{ mm}$, $t_{50} = 12 \text{ min}$, double drainage.
**Step 1:** Find the drainage path:
$$
d = H/2 = 20/2 = 10 \text{ mm} = 1.0 \text{ cm}
$$
**Step 2:** Apply the time-factor relation:
$$
C_v = \dfrac{T_v \cdot d^2}{t_{50}} = \dfrac{0.197 \times (1.0)^2}{12 \times 60}
$$
**Step 3:** Compute:
$$
C_v = \dfrac{0.197}{720} = 2.74 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{cm}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Final Answer:** $C_v = 2.74 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{cm}^2/\text{s}$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With the help of a typical $e$–$\log \sigma'$ curve, explain the determination of the **pre-consolidation pressure ($\sigma'_p$)** using **Casagrande's graphical construction**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 4 m thick clay layer (single drainage) has an initial void ratio $e_0 = 0.9$, compression index $C_c = 0.30$, and an initial effective stress $\sigma'_0 = 80 \text{ kPa}$. The applied load increases the effective stress to $\sigma'_1 = 240 \text{ kPa}$. Determine the **final consolidation settlement** assuming the clay is **normally consolidated**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Casagrande's Construction (7 Marks):**
1. Plot the **$e$–$\log \sigma'$ curve** from the consolidation test data.
2. Identify the point of **maximum curvature** (the "knee" of the curve).
3. From this point, draw a **horizontal line** (parallel to the x-axis).
4. Draw a **tangent line** to the curve at the point of maximum curvature.
5. **Bisect the angle** between the horizontal line and the tangent.
6. Extend the **virgin (NC) portion** of the curve as a straight line backwards (upward).
7. The **intersection** of the angle bisector with the extended virgin line gives the **pre-consolidation pressure $\sigma'_p$**.

**Significance:** $\sigma'_p$ classifies the clay as NC, OC, or lightly OC, which dictates which formula to use for settlement prediction.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**
**Given:** $H = 4 \text{ m}$, $e_0 = 0.9$, $C_c = 0.30$, $\sigma'_0 = 80 \text{ kPa}$, $\sigma'_1 = 240 \text{ kPa}$, NC clay.

**Settlement formula for NC clay:**
$$
S_c = \dfrac{C_c \cdot H}{1 + e_0} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\sigma'_1}{\sigma'_0}\right)
$$

**Substitute values:**
$$
S_c = \dfrac{0.30 \times 4}{1 + 0.9} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{240}{80}\right)
$$

**Compute step-by-step:**
$$
S_c = \dfrac{1.20}{1.9} \log_{10}(3.0)
$$

$$
S_c = 0.6316 \times 0.4771
$$

$$
\boxed{S_c = 0.301 \, \text{m} = 301 \, \text{mm}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Drainage Path Confusion:** Most students forget to halve the height for double drainage. **Always check** whether the oedometer has single or double drainage.
> 2. **NC vs. OC Formula:** Using the NC formula for an OC clay will give **grossly overestimated settlement**. First identify $\sigma'_p$ before applying the formula.
> 3. **Time Factor Values:** Mixing up the values of $T_v$ for $U = 50\%$ and $U = 90\%$ is a frequent error. **Memorize** $T_{v,50} = 0.197$ and $T_{v,90} = 0.848$.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and cm, or minutes and seconds in $C_v$ calculation. Convert everything to **cm and seconds** before plugging in.
> 5. **Logarithm Base:** Always use **base 10** in $C_c$ and settlement equations (not natural log, unless the equation explicitly states $\ln$).
> 6. **Casagrande's $t_{90}$ Reading:** Some students incorrectly read $t_{90} = 0.848 \cdot t_{100}$ from the plot. The correct practice is to find $t_{100}$ first (intersection of initial and final straight lines), then locate the abscissa at $0.157 \cdot t_{100}$ on the log-time axis to get $t_{90}$.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Consolidation test** = one-dimensional, time-dependent volume change test in an oedometer.
- **Terzaghi's diffusion equation:** $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = C_v \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2}$ governs pore-pressure dissipation.
- **Coefficient of consolidation $C_v$** = $k / (m_v \cdot \gamma_w)$ — determines the **rate** of settlement.
- **Compression Index $C_c$** = slope of the virgin (NC) branch of $e$–$\log \sigma'$ curve.
- **Swell Index $C_s$** = slope of the unloading (rebound) branch; $C_s \approx C_c / 5$ to $C_c / 10$.
- **Settlement for NC clay:** $S_c = \dfrac{C_c H}{1+e_0} \log_{10}(\sigma'_2 / \sigma'_1)$.
- **Settlement for OC clay:** Use $C_s$ from $\sigma'_1$ to $\sigma'_p$, then $C_c$ from $\sigma'_p$ to $\sigma'_2$.
- **Casagrande's method** uses $e$–$\log t$ plot → $t_{50}$ or $t_{90}$ → $C_v$.
- **Taylor's method** uses $e$–$\sqrt{t}$ plot → $t_{90}$ → $C_v$.
- **Time factor** for $U = 50\%$ is $T_v = 0.197$; for $U = 90\%$ is $T_v = 0.848$.
- **Drainage path** $d = H/2$ for **double drainage** (standard oedometer setup); $d = H$ for single drainage.
- **Pre-consolidation pressure $\sigma'_p$** is found using **Casagrande's construction** (angle-bisector method).
- **Normally Consolidated (NC) clay:** $\sigma'_p \approx \sigma'_0$.
- **Over-Consolidated (OC) clay:** $\sigma'_p > \sigma'_0$ (i.e., OCR $= \sigma'_p / \sigma'_0 > 1$).
- The consolidation test is **invaluable for predicting total and time-rate of settlement** of foundations on clay, embankments, and earth dams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: e-Log p Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** e-Log p Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **e–log p Curve (Semilogarithmic Compression Curve):** A graphical representation obtained from a one-dimensional consolidation test (oedometer test), in which the **void ratio (e)** of a saturated clay specimen is plotted on the arithmetic (linear) ordinate against the **effective consolidation pressure (p or σ')** plotted on the abscissa using a **logarithmic scale**.

This curve is the most important graphical output of a consolidation test because it allows direct determination of the following critical engineering parameters:

- **Compression Index ($C_c$)** — slope of the virgin (normally consolidated) compression branch.
- **Swelling Index ($C_s$)** — slope of the unloading/reloading branch.
- **Recompression Index ($C_r$)** — slope of the recompression branch (often taken equal to $C_s$).
- **Preconsolidation Pressure ($P_c$ or $\sigma'_p$)** — the maximum past effective stress the soil has experienced.
- **Coefficient of Volume Compressibility ($m_v$)** and **Coefficient of Consolidation ($C_v$)** values corresponding to each stress increment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the logarithm of p (and not p itself)?**
> When the natural pressure range in a consolidation test (e.g., 10 kPa to 1600 kPa) is plotted on a linear scale, the lower pressure data points get crushed into a small region near the origin, losing resolution. A logarithmic abscissa spreads the lower-pressure data, revealing a characteristic **"S-shaped" curve** with two distinct linear branches that are essential for identifying overconsolidation history.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are sitting on a stack of soft foam cushions (this is the saturated clay). When you slowly press down with your hand:

- **First pressing (Virgin Compression):** The foam squeezes out air/water easily. Each additional unit of force removes a *constant proportion* of remaining thickness — this produces a **straight line on a log-scale plot**.
- **Releasing your hand (Unloading / Swelling):** The foam rebounds, but only partially. The rebound curve is much flatter than the loading curve.
- **Pressing again (Recompression):** The foam initially resists (steep curve) until you surpass the previous maximum force, after which it follows the same virgin line as before.

This "memory" of past loading is exactly what the **e–log p curve captures**. The point where the slope changes abruptly is the **preconsolidation pressure ($P_c$)** — the soil's memory of its heaviest past load.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Parallel:** Think of repeatedly sitting on a memory-foam mattress. The deeper the indent from the first heavy sit, the longer the mattress "remembers" that shape even after you get up.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Convention)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $e$ | Void Ratio | Dimensionless | Plotted on linear (Y) axis |
| $p, \sigma'$ | Effective Pressure | kPa | Plotted on log (X) axis |
| $C_c$ | Compression Index | Dimensionless | Slope of virgin branch |
| $C_s, C_r$ | Swelling/Recompression Index | Dimensionless | Slope of unload/reload branch |
| $P_c, \sigma'_p$ | Preconsolidation Pressure | kPa | From Casagrande's construction |
| OCR | Overconsolidation Ratio | Dimensionless | $OCR = P_c / \sigma'_0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** Effective stress is denoted $\sigma'$ and total stress as $\sigma$. In saturated clay under oedometer conditions, $\sigma' = \sigma - u$, where $u$ is the pore water pressure. End-of-primary (EOP) data are used to plot the e–log p curve.

## 1.4 Components of a Typical e–log p Curve

A complete e–log p curve obtained by incremental loading (IL), unloading, and reloading has **four characteristic segments**:

1. **Initial Rebound (Recompression) Branch** — concave-up, represents elastic recovery upon initial unloading (often very small).
2. **Virgin Compression Branch (Normal Consolidation Line, NCL)** — a long, straight, steep line with slope $C_c$. Soil structure is being progressively destroyed.
3. **Unloading Branch (Swelling Line, SL)** — a flatter straight line with slope $C_s$. Represents elastic rebound.
4. **Reloading Branch (Recompression Line, RL)** — curves to meet the NCL again at the previous maximum stress.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the e–log p Curve Construction

The following bullet points describe the *operational procedure* used to construct an e–log p curve from raw oedometer test data.

- **Step 1 — Apply incremental loads:** A saturated clay disc is confined in a rigid oedometer ring (no lateral strain). Loads are doubled in succession (e.g., 10 → 20 → 40 → 80 → 160 → 320 → 640 → 1280 kPa).
- **Step 2 — Record dial gauge readings at end-of-primary (EOP):** For each load, the test is continued until 100% primary consolidation is reached (verified by the Taylor or Casagrande $t$-method).
- **Step 3 — Compute void ratio $e$ for each load:**
  - Convert dial reading → change in height $\Delta H$.
  - Compute new thickness $H = H_0 - \Sigma \Delta H$.
  - Compute void ratio: $e = (H - H_s)/H_s$, where $H_s$ is the equivalent height of solids.
- **Step 4 — Plot e vs. log p:** The resulting plot is the **e–log p curve**.

## 2.2 Why Each Segment Behaves the Way It Does

- **NCL (Virgin Compression):** Soil grains rearrange into a denser packing by sliding and reorientation. Because load increases *geometrically* and the resulting compression is logarithmic, the linear e–log p relationship holds.
- **Swelling Branch:** When load is removed, elastic expansion of the clay skeleton and water uptake occur. Only the *elastic* portion of the strain is recovered — most of the plastic deformation is irreversible.
- **Recompression Branch:** Until the previous maximum stress ($P_c$) is reached, deformation is partly elastic and the curve is curved. Beyond $P_c$, the soil has "forgotten" its past history and behaves as if normally consolidated — the curve merges back into the NCL.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use \vert or \mid in formulas inside tables, NOT the vertical bar character \vert**, to keep table syntax valid.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Range / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Compression Index from e–log p | $C_c = \dfrac{e_1 - e_2}{\log_{10}(p_2/p_1)}$ | 0.2 to 0.5 (typical for $LL$ 30–70) |
| 2 | Swelling Index | $C_s = \dfrac{e_1 - e_2}{\log_{10}(p_2/p_1)}$ (unload branch) | $\approx (1/5 \text{ to } 1/10)\,C_c$ |
| 3 | Recompression Index | $C_r \approx C_s$ | Same as $C_s$ |
| 4 | Settlement (NC clay) | $\Delta H = \dfrac{C_c \cdot H_0}{1 + e_0} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma'}{\sigma'_0}\right)$ | m |
| 5 | Settlement (OC, $\sigma'_0+\Delta\sigma' \le P_c$) | $\Delta H = \dfrac{C_s \cdot H_0}{1 + e_0} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma'}{\sigma'_0}\right)$ | m |
| 6 | Settlement (OC, $\sigma'_0 < P_c < \sigma'_0+\Delta\sigma'$) | $\Delta H = \dfrac{C_s H_0}{1+e_0}\log_{10}\!\dfrac{P_c}{\sigma'_0} + \dfrac{C_c H_0}{1+e_0}\log_{10}\!\dfrac{\sigma'_0+\Delta\sigma'}{P_c}$ | m |
| 7 | Coefficient of Volume Compressibility | $m_v = \dfrac{0.435\,C_c}{(1+e_0)\sigma'}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{kN}$ |
| 8 | Terzaghi-Peck correlation | $C_c = 0.009\,(LL - 10)$ | — |
| 9 | Skempton correlation (remoulded) | $C_c = 0.007\,(W_L - 10)$ | — |
| 10 | Overconsolidation Ratio | $OCR = P_c / \sigma'_0$ | $\ge 1$ |
| 11 | Casagrande — max curvature radius | Locate point of maximum curvature on e–log p | — |
