| 12 | Coefficient of Consolidation (from $C_c$) | $C_v = \dfrac{k \cdot (1+e_0)}{m_v \cdot \gamma_w} = \dfrac{k}{\gamma_w \cdot 0.435\,C_c/\sigma'}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{yr}$ or $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When solving numerical problems, students often confuse the *slope* of the curve ($C_c$) with the *change in void ratio* ($\Delta e$). Remember: $C_c$ is dimensionless and equals $\Delta e$ *per log cycle of p*. That is, if p increases by 10× (one full log cycle), then $\Delta e = C_c$ exactly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundation Settlement Prediction:** The e–log p curve is the backbone of one-dimensional consolidation settlement calculations for buildings, embankments, tanks, and bridge piers founded on clay.
- **OCR Estimation for Site Characterization:** The preconsolidation pressure reveals whether a clay deposit is normally consolidated (NC), lightly overconsolidated (LOC), or heavily overconsolidated (HOC) — directly informing allowable bearing capacity.
- **Land Reclamation & Pre-loading Design:** Engineers use the e–log p curve to design surcharge pre-loading programs to consolidate soft marine clays before construction.
- **Soil Stress History Reconstruction:** Paleo-stresses (e.g., from vanished glaciers) can be deduced from $P_c$, aiding geological and geotechnical interpretation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Compression Index from the e–log p Curve

The virgin compression branch is approximately a straight line when e is plotted against $\log_{10}(p)$. Let two points on this line be $(p_1, e_1)$ and $(p_2, e_2)$. The slope of the line in the $(e, \log_{10} p)$ plane is:

$$
C_c = \frac{e_1 - e_2}{\log_{10}(p_2) - \log_{10}(p_1)}
$$

Using the logarithm identity $\log_{10}(p_2) - \log_{10}(p_1) = \log_{10}(p_2 / p_1)$, we obtain:

$$
C_c = \frac{e_1 - e_2}{\log_{10}(p_2 / p_1)}
$$

**Conversion Logic Step:**
- The numerator $e_1 - e_2$ is the change in void ratio (always taken as a positive number when $p_2 > p_1$).
- The denominator $\log_{10}(p_2/p_1)$ is the change in abscissa on the e–log p plot.
- The slope of a straight line is "rise over run", i.e., $\Delta e / \Delta(\log_{10} p)$.
- Therefore $C_c$ is dimensionless and equals the *change in void ratio per decade of pressure*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In KTU numerical problems, students are usually given either *two points* (p, e) on the curve OR a tabulated list of pressures and corresponding dial readings. Practice both formats.

## 3.2 Derivation of Consolidation Settlement from the e–log p Curve

The settlement of a clay layer of thickness $H_0$ under an applied stress increment is related to the change in void ratio as follows.

**Volume-Void Ratio Relationship (basic definition):**
For a soil element of unit cross-sectional area and height $H$, the volume of voids $V_v = e \cdot V_s$. Since $V_s$ is constant (grains are incompressible), the change in height is:

$$
\Delta H = \frac{\Delta e}{1 + e_0} \cdot H_0
$$

where $\Delta e = e_0 - e_1$ and $e_0$ is the initial void ratio.

**Substituting the Compression Index Relation:**
From the e–log p curve (NC branch):

$$
\Delta e = C_c \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma'}{\sigma'_0}\right)
$$

Combining the two equations:

$$
\Delta H = \frac{C_c \cdot H_0}{1 + e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma'}{\sigma'_0}\right)
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- The term $(1+e_0)$ in the denominator converts void-ratio change to *strain* (volumetric strain, in the 1-D sense).
- The logarithmic term captures the fact that *each tenfold increase in stress produces the same change in $e$* along the NCL.
- For overconsolidated cases, we substitute $C_s$ for $\Delta\sigma'$ values below $P_c$, and use the two-segment formula in the cheat sheet (rows 5 and 6).

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Form Using $m_v$:** Sometimes KTU problems give the Coefficient of Volume Compressibility $m_v$ directly, in which case $\Delta H = m_v \cdot H_0 \cdot \Delta\sigma'$ (a simpler linear relation, valid only for small stress increments within the same log decade).

## 3.3 Casagrande's Construction — Step-by-Step Derivation of $P_c$

Casagrande (1936) proposed a graphical procedure to estimate the preconsolidation pressure from the e–log p curve. The method is empirical but remains the standard in KTU examinations.

**Procedure (Visual Step-by-Step):**

1. **Locate the point of maximum curvature** on the e–log p curve. This is the point where the curve transitions from the curved recompression portion to the straight virgin compression portion. Geometrically, this is the point where the *radius of curvature is minimum*.

2. **Draw a horizontal line** through this maximum-curvature point (parallel to the X-axis, i.e., parallel to the $\log p$ axis).

3. **Draw a tangent line** to the curve at this same maximum-curvature point.

4. **Bisect the angle** between the horizontal line and the tangent line.

5. **Extend the straight-line portion of the virgin compression branch** upward and backward (i.e., toward lower pressures).

6. **The intersection of the angle bisector with the extended virgin line** defines the preconsolidation pressure $P_c$.

**Mathematical Justification (Empirical):**
The angle bisector represents the locus of points equidistant (in angle) from the horizontal and the tangent. Because the recompression curve asymptotically approaches the NCL at $P_c$, the bisector reliably intersects the NCL at the geometric transition point corresponding to the maximum past stress. The exact value of $P_c$ is read off the $\log p$ axis at this intersection.

**Sample Numerical Calculation Framework:**

$$
P_c = 10^{\,x_c}
$$

where $x_c$ is the abscissa of the intersection point on the $\log p$ axis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often draw the tangent at the *inflection point* of the S-curve rather than at the point of *maximum curvature*. These are *different points*. The maximum curvature point is where the curve appears to "bend the most" — typically near the upper end of the curved recompression segment.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Consolidation Settlement)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Problem.** A normally consolidated clay layer is 6 m thick, with $e_0 = 1.20$, $C_c = 0.30$, and $\sigma'_0 = 80$ kPa. The applied stress increase is $\Delta\sigma' = 120$ kPa. Compute the primary consolidation settlement.

**Solution Step 1 — Identify the regime.**
Since the clay is NC, use the NC settlement formula:

$$
\Delta H = \frac{C_c \cdot H_0}{1 + e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma'}{\sigma'_0}\right)
$$

**Solution Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
\Delta H = \frac{0.30 \times 6.0}{1 + 1.20} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{80 + 120}{80}\right)
$$

**Solution Step 3 — Simplify denominator.**

$$
1 + e_0 = 1 + 1.20 = 2.20
$$

**Solution Step 4 — Compute the log term.**

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{200}{80}\right) = \log_{10}(2.5) \approx 0.39794
$$

**Solution Step 5 — Multiply all factors.**

$$
\Delta H = \frac{1.80}{2.20} \times 0.39794 = 0.81818 \times 0.39794 \approx 0.3256 \text{ m}
$$

**Solution Step 6 — Final Answer.**

$$
\boxed{\Delta H \approx 326 \text{ mm}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** A 6 m clay layer settling by ~326 mm under an applied pressure of 120 kPa is realistic for soft, high-$C_c$ clay. If you obtained, say, 5 mm or 5 m, you almost certainly misapplied the formula.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Three OCR Scenarios (Comparison)

The following matrix is essential for selecting the correct settlement equation in KTU problems.

| Scenario | Condition | Settlement Formula | Regime |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A. Normally Consolidated (NC)** | $P_c = \sigma'_0$ | $\Delta H = \dfrac{C_c H_0}{1+e_0}\log_{10}\!\dfrac{\sigma'_0+\Delta\sigma'}{\sigma'_0}$ | Full virgin branch only |
| **B. Overconsolidated (OC), low stress** | $\sigma'_0+\Delta\sigma' \le P_c$ | $\Delta H = \dfrac{C_s H_0}{1+e_0}\log_{10}\!\dfrac{\sigma'_0+\Delta\sigma'}{\sigma'_0}$ | Recompression branch only |
| **C. Overconsolidated (OC), high stress** | $\sigma'_0 < P_c < \sigma'_0+\Delta\sigma'$ | $\Delta H = \dfrac{C_s H_0}{1+e_0}\log_{10}\!\dfrac{P_c}{\sigma'_0} + \dfrac{C_c H_0}{1+e_0}\log_{10}\!\dfrac{\sigma'_0+\Delta\sigma'}{P_c}$ | Recompression + Virgin |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Test:** If your problem statement mentions OCR (e.g., OCR = 2), compute $P_c = OCR \cdot \sigma'_0$ first, then compare $\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma'$ with $P_c$ to choose the correct row of the matrix.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — e–log p Curve Architecture (Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *behavioral architecture* of a typical e–log p curve during a multi-stage consolidation test. Each block represents a phase of soil behavior, and the arrows represent transitions governed by effective-stress history.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Initial State: e0, sigma0]) --> Stage1[Stage 1: Initial Loading]
    Stage1 --> Recomp{Recompression Phase?}
    Recomp -- Yes --> RecompBranch[Curved Branch: Slope approx Cs - Elastic Rebound Region]
    Recomp -- No --> NCL[Virgin Compression Line: Slope Cc - NCL]
    RecompBranch --> CritPoint{Exceeds Preconsolidation Pressure Pc?}
    CritPoint -- No --> RecompBranch
    CritPoint -- Yes --> NCL
    NCL --> Stage3[Stage 3: Unloading]
    Stage3 --> Swell[Swelling Branch: Slope Cs - Elastic Recovery Only]
    Swell --> Stage4[Stage 4: Reloading]
    Stage4 --> Reload[Reloading Branch: Curves toward NCL]
    Reload --> End([End of Test: e_final, p_final])

    style Start fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366
    style End fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style NCL fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style Swell fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style Reload fill:#e6d9ff,stroke:#4d0099
```

**How to Read This Diagram:**

- The **Virgin Compression Line (NCL)** is highlighted in red — this is the *primary structural backbone* of the e–log p curve and yields $C_c$.
- The **Swelling Branch** in yellow yields $C_s$.
- The **Reloading Branch** in purple demonstrates the soil's *memory effect*.
- The decision diamonds represent *physical transitions* in the soil skeleton.

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Casagrande's Construction Procedure

The following sequence diagram walks through Casagrande's graphical procedure step by step. Use it as a mental checklist before drawing the construction in your KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Locate Point of Max Curvature on e log p Curve] --> B[Step 2: Draw Horizontal Line Through This Point]
    B --> C[Step 3: Draw Tangent Line at Same Point]
    C --> D[Step 4: Construct Angle Bisector Between Horizontal and Tangent]
    D --> E[Step 5: Extend Virgin Compression Line Upward Left]
    E --> F[Step 6: Locate Intersection of Bisector with Extended NCL]
    F --> G[Result: Pc Read Off log p Axis at Intersection]

    style A fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366
    style G fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When drawing Casagrande's construction, always use a **sharp pencil and ruler** for the horizontal, tangent, and bisector lines. Sloppy lines cost easy marks in the practical/graph sections of KTU papers.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How e–log p Drives Foundation Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Data]
        A1[Oedometer Test Results]
        A2[Initial void ratio e0]
        A3[Effective Stress sigma0]
        A4[Applied Stress Increment dsigma]
    end

    subgraph Processing[Curve Analysis]
        B1[Plot e vs log p]
        B2[Determine Cc from NCL Slope]
        B3[Determine Cs from Swelling Slope]
        B4[Estimate Pc via Casagrande Method]
        B5[Compute OCR = Pc/sigma0]
    end

    subgraph Output[Engineering Output]
        C1[Settlement Delta H]
        C2[Time Factor: Cv from log t Method]
        C3[Bearing Capacity Adjustment]
        C4[Preloading Design Decisions]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B4
    A3 --> B5
    A4 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> B5
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B4
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    B5 --> C3

    style Input fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366
    style Processing fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style Output fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
```

**Interpretation:**

- The **Input** block represents the raw laboratory and field data.
- The **Processing** block represents the analytical workflow on the e–log p curve.
- The **Output** block represents the design decisions that the e–log p curve directly informs.

## 4.4 ASCII Sketch of a Typical e–log p Curve (for Quick Mental Reference)

```
  e |
    |*
    | *
    |  *
    |   *  Recompression (curved, slope ~Cs)
    |    *
    |     *____________________________ Virgin Compression Line (NCL)
    |      *                          /  (slope = Cc, steep)
    |       *                       /
    |        *                    /
    |         *                 /     <-- Pc (preconsolidation pressure)
    |          *   Tangent    /
    |           *  *  *  *  /
    |            *  Bisector
    |   Swelling * /
    |   (slope=Cs)/
    |_________*_________________________ log p
              ^                  ^
              p1                 p2
```

> [!NOTE]
> This ASCII sketch is a rough guide. In KTU exams, always draw the curve properly on **semilog graph paper** (provided in the lab manual), with at least 4-5 clearly plotted points defining the virgin compression line.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Define the term "Compression Index" ($C_c$) as obtained from an e–log p curve. State any one empirical correlation used to estimate $C_c$ from the Liquid Limit.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Compression Index ($C_c$) is defined as the slope of the virgin (normally consolidated) compression branch of the e–log p curve, where void ratio ($e$) is plotted on the arithmetic ordinate and effective consolidation pressure ($p$) on the abscissa on a logarithmic scale.
>
> Mathematically:
> $$C_c = \frac{e_1 - e_2}{\log_{10}(p_2/p_1)}$$
>
> **Empirical Correlation (Terzaghi and Peck):**
> $$C_c = 0.009 \times (LL - 10)$$
>
> where $LL$ is the Liquid Limit (in percent).
>
> (3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark correlation)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**What is "Preconsolidation Pressure"? Briefly explain Casagrande's graphical method to determine it.**

**Model Answer:**

> Preconsolidation Pressure ($P_c$ or $\sigma'_p$) is the maximum effective vertical stress that a soil has experienced in its past geological history. It marks the boundary between overconsolidated and normally consolidated behavior on the e–log p curve.
>
> **Casagrande's Method (1936):**
> 1. Locate the point of maximum curvature on the e–log p curve.
> 2. Draw a horizontal line through this point.
> 3. Draw a tangent to the curve at the same point.
> 4. Bisect the angle between the horizontal line and the tangent.
> 5. Extend the straight-line portion of the virgin compression curve backward (toward lower pressures).
> 6. The point of intersection of the bisector with the extended virgin curve gives $P_c$.
>
> (3 marks: 1 mark definition, 2 marks procedure)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Format:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts (typically part (a) for 7 marks and part (b) for 7 marks). Answer either **Question A** *or* **Question B** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]

The following data were obtained from an oedometer test on a saturated clay sample (initial height $H_0 = 25$ mm, initial void ratio $e_0 = 1.10$):

| Pressure $p$ (kPa) | 10 | 25 | 50 | 100 | 200 | 400 | 800 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Void Ratio $e$ | 1.080 | 1.040 | 0.985 | 0.900 | 0.790 | 0.640 | 0.490 |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Plot the e–log p curve and determine the Compression Index $C_c$ using the straight-line portion of the curve.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the straight-line (virgin) portion.**
Looking at the data, the lower-pressure points (10, 25, 50, 100 kPa) show a curved trend (recompression), while the higher-pressure points (200, 400, 800 kPa) lie on a straight line (virgin compression). We will use the latter.

**Step 2 — Pick two well-separated points on the virgin line.**
- Point 1: $(p_1 = 200 \text{ kPa}, e_1 = 0.790)$
- Point 2: $(p_2 = 800 \text{ kPa}, e_2 = 0.490)$

**Step 3 — Apply the compression index formula.**

$$
C_c = \frac{e_1 - e_2}{\log_{10}(p_2/p_1)}
$$

$$
C_c = \frac{0.790 - 0.490}{\log_{10}(800/200)}
$$

$$
C_c = \frac{0.300}{\log_{10}(4)}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(4) \approx 0.6021
$$

$$
C_c = \frac{0.300}{0.6021} \approx 0.498 \approx 0.50
$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{C_c \approx 0.50}$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**The same clay exists as a 4 m thick layer in the field with an initial effective stress $\sigma'_0 = 50$ kPa. An additional uniform stress of $\Delta\sigma' = 150$ kPa is applied at the surface due to a proposed building. Compute the primary consolidation settlement assuming the clay is normally consolidated.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the regime.**
Since the clay is normally consolidated (NC), we use:

$$
\Delta H = \frac{C_c \cdot H_0}{1 + e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma'}{\sigma'_0}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
\Delta H = \frac{0.50 \times 4.0}{1 + 1.10} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{50 + 150}{50}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
\Delta H = \frac{2.0}{2.10} \cdot \log_{10}(4)
$$

$$
\Delta H = 0.9524 \times 0.6021 \approx 0.573 \text{ m}
$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{\Delta H \approx 573 \text{ mm}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly forget that $H_0$ is the *field layer thickness*, NOT the oedometer specimen thickness (25 mm). Using the wrong $H_0$ yields settlement values off by a factor of ~160. Always convert the specimen data into *equivalent field layer* information.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, Alternative Set]

A 5 m thick saturated clay layer in the field has the following properties:
- Compression Index $C_c = 0.35$
- Swelling Index $C_s = 0.06$
- Initial void ratio $e_0 = 1.05$
- Initial effective overburden pressure $\sigma'_0 = 60$ kPa
- Preconsolidation Pressure $P_c = 140$ kPa (determined from Casagrande's construction)

A proposed construction applies a uniform surface load that increases the vertical effective stress by $\Delta\sigma' = 120$ kPa.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Classify the clay deposit (NC, LOC, or HOC) based on its OCR, and determine which settlement formula is applicable.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute OCR.**

$$
OCR = \frac{P_c}{\sigma'_0} = \frac{140}{60} \approx 2.33
$$

**Step 2 — Classify the clay.**
Since $OCR > 1$, the clay is **Overconsolidated (OC)**. Specifically, with OCR $\approx 2.33$, it is classified as **Lightly Overconsolidated (LOC)** (typical range: 1 to ~3).

**Step 3 — Determine which formula applies.**
Compute the final effective stress:

$$
\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma' = 60 + 120 = 180 \text{ kPa}
$$

Compare with $P_c = 140$ kPa:

$$
\sigma'_0 = 60 \text{ kPa} < P_c = 140 \text{ kPa} < \sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma' = 180 \text{ kPa}
$$

Since the stress increment *crosses* $P_c$, we must use the **two-stage formula** (Scenario C in the analytical matrix):

$$
\Delta H = \frac{C_s H_0}{1+e_0}\log_{10}\!\frac{P_c}{\sigma'_0} + \frac{C_c H_0}{1+e_0}\log_{10}\!\frac{\sigma'_0+\Delta\sigma'}{P_c}
$$

**Answer:** OC clay, OCR = 2.33, Scenario C formula applies.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the primary consolidation settlement.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Substitute into the two-stage formula.**

$$
\Delta H = \frac{C_s H_0}{1+e_0}\log_{10}\!\frac{P_c}{\sigma'_0} + \frac{C_c H_0}{1+e_0}\log_{10}\!\frac{\sigma'_0+\Delta\sigma'}{P_c}
$$

$$
\Delta H = \frac{0.06 \times 5.0}{1 + 1.05}\log_{10}\!\frac{140}{60} + \frac{0.35 \times 5.0}{1 + 1.05}\log_{10}\!\frac{180}{140}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify each term.**

Term 1:
$$
\frac{0.30}{2.05}\log_{10}(2.333) = 0.1463 \times 0.36798 \approx 0.0539 \text{ m}
$$

Term 2:
$$
\frac{1.75}{2.05}\log_{10}(1.2857) = 0.8537 \times 0.10914 \approx 0.0932 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the two terms.**

$$
\Delta H = 0.0539 + 0.0932 \approx 0.1471 \text{ m}
$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{\Delta H \approx 147 \text{ mm}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to use only $C_c$ throughout (treating the clay as NC). This would overestimate the settlement. Always check whether the applied stress range crosses $P_c$ before selecting the formula. Also, do not confuse $\log_{10}$ with $\ln$ — KTU numerical answers are based on $\log_{10}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Card** — review these points before entering the exam hall.

- [ ] **Definition:** e–log p curve = void ratio (Y, linear) vs. $\log_{10}$ effective pressure (X, log). It is obtained from the oedometer test.
- [ ] **Compression Index ($C_c$):** Slope of the straight-line virgin compression branch. Computed using $C_c = (e_1 - e_2) / \log_{10}(p_2/p_1)$.
- [ ] **Swelling Index ($C_s$):** Slope of the unloading branch. Typically $C_s \approx (1/5 \text{ to } 1/10) \times C_c$.
- [ ] **Preconsolidation Pressure ($P_c$):** Determined by Casagrande's method — horizontal + tangent + bisector construction.
- [ ] **OCR = $P_c / \sigma'_0$.** OCR > 1 → OC clay; OCR = 1 → NC clay.
- [ ] **Three Settlement Formulas** (NC, OC low stress, OC high stress — see Analytical Matrix in §3.5).
- [ ] **Terzaghi-Peck correlation:** $C_c = 0.009 (LL - 10)$.
- [ ] **Skempton correlation (remoulded clays):** $C_c = 0.007 (W_L - 10)$.
- [ ] **Coefficient of Volume Compressibility:** $m_v = 0.435 C_c / [(1+e_0) \sigma']$.
- [ ] **Units:** $C_c, C_s, e, OCR$ are dimensionless. Settlement in metres when $H_0$ is in metres.
- [ ] **Use $\log_{10}$, not $\ln$.** Most KTU problems specify the logarithmic base.
- [ ] **Always check if $\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma'$ exceeds $P_c$ before selecting the OC two-stage formula.**
- [ ] **Sanity-check numerical answers:** A typical NC clay settlement is in the range of 50–500 mm for a 4–6 m layer.
- [ ] **Field vs. Lab $H_0$:** Use the *field* layer thickness in the settlement formula, not the oedometer specimen thickness.
- [ ] **Casagrande's Point of Max Curvature ≠ Inflection Point.** Maximum curvature point is on the upper curved recompression branch; inflection point lies on the S-shaped transition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** The e–log p curve is a high-weightage topic. Master the *three settlement formulas* (NC, OC-low, OC-high) and *Casagrande's construction procedure*. These are guaranteed to appear in some form in every KTU ESE paper for PCAGT402.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stress Due to Self Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Geostatic Stress (or Self-Weight Stress / Overburden Pressure)** is the vertical stress induced at any horizontal plane within a semi-infinite, homogeneous, isotropic soil mass solely due to the **weight of the overlying soil column** and any water contained within it. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is resolved into two distinct stress components acting on a soil skeleton:

1. **Total Stress ($\sigma$)** — The overall stress at a point, including the contribution from both soil solids and the pore water.
2. **Pore Water Pressure ($u$)** — The pressure transmitted through the water filling the voids (assumed hydrostatic under static conditions).
3. **Effective Stress ($\sigma'$)** — The portion of total stress carried by the soil particle-to-particle contacts (the soil skeleton). This is the stress that actually governs **shear strength** and **volume change** (compression) in soils.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Terzaghi's Principle of Effective Stress (1936) — The Cornerstone Equation of Soil Mechanics:**
>
> $$\sigma' = \sigma - u$$
>
> This equation is universally accepted and forms the basis of nearly all problems in settlement analysis, bearing capacity, slope stability, and earth-retaining structures under the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Stack of Sandbags

Imagine a vertical stack of identical, fully-saturated sandbags standing in a shallow pool of water.

* Each sandbag has a **total weight** (solids + water inside the pores).
* The **water in the pool** also pushes sideways and upward against the lowest sandbag — this is the **pore water pressure** (buoyancy).
* What actually **crushes** the bottom sandbag, deforms it, and makes it bulge is *not* the full weight of the stack, but the weight *minus* the upward push of the surrounding water.

> Translation: Total Stress (full weight on top) − Pore Water Pressure (buoyancy) = **Effective Stress (the real squashing force on the soil skeleton)**.

This is why a fully submerged soil feels *lighter* and *stronger* per unit volume than a dry one — buoyancy is doing the work.

### 1.3 Key Standard Constants & Units

> [!NOTE]
> - **Unit weight of water, $\gamma_w$** = **9.81 kN/m³** (often rounded to **10 kN/m³** in KTU numerical problems for simplicity).
> - **Specific gravity of water, $G_w$** = **1.0** (dimensionless).
> - All stresses are expressed in **kN/m² (kPa)** or **kN/m³** for unit weights.
> - Standard acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**.
> - The relationship $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ ties **mass density ($\rho$)** to **unit weight ($\gamma$)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Stress Components — A Layered Breakdown

#### Step 1: Total Stress ($\sigma$)
Total stress is purely geometric and gravitational. For a **single homogeneous layer** of soil of thickness $z$ and unit weight $\gamma$:

$$\sigma = \gamma \cdot z$$

For a **stratified soil profile** with $n$ layers, the total vertical stress at the bottom of the $n$-th layer is the **summation of weights** of all overlying columns:

$$\sigma = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma_i \cdot h_i$$

where $h_i$ and $\gamma_i$ are the thickness and unit weight of the $i$-th layer respectively.

#### Step 2: Pore Water Pressure ($u$)
Under **static** (no-flow) conditions, pore water follows hydrostatics. The pressure at a depth $h_w$ below the **water table** is:

$$u = \gamma_w \cdot h_w$$

* **Above the water table** (in the dry/moist zone): $u = 0$ (atmospheric).
* **At the water table**: $u = 0$ (reference datum).
* **Below the water table**: $u$ increases linearly with depth at a rate of $\gamma_w \approx 9.81$ kN/m³ per metre.

#### Step 3: Effective Stress ($\sigma'$) — The Decisive Quantity
By Terzaghi's principle, for the **fully saturated zone** below the water table:

$$\sigma' = \sigma - u$$

For a point **above the water table** in dry soil, $u = 0$ and therefore $\sigma' = \sigma$.

#### Step 4: Submerged (Buoyant) Unit Weight
When soil lies **below the water table**, the effective unit weight used in calculations is the **buoyant (submerged) unit weight**:

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$$

where $\gamma_{sat}$ is the saturated unit weight of the soil. This represents the **net downward force per unit volume** transmitted through the grain skeleton once buoyancy is subtracted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capillary Rise Phenomenon:** Above the water table, capillary action can suck water *upward* into the voids, creating a **negative pore water pressure** (matric suction). In the capillary-saturated zone:
> $$u = -\gamma_w \cdot h_c$$
> where $h_c$ is the height of capillary rise. This *increases* effective stress — a phenomenon crucial in **desiccation cracks** and **shallow foundation design** in fine-grained soils.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Governing Equation** | **Boundary / Validity** | **Unit** |
|:-----:|:-------------|:----------:|:-----------------------|:------------------------|:--------:|
| 1 | Total stress (single layer) | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = \gamma \cdot z$ | $z$ = depth from ground surface | kN/m² |
| 2 | Total stress (multi-layer) | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = \sum \gamma_i h_i$ | Add weight of every layer above point | kN/m² |
| 3 | Pore pressure (hydrostatic) | $u$ | $u = \gamma_w h_w$ | $h_w$ = depth below water table | kN/m² |
| 4 | Capillary pore pressure | $u_c$ | $u_c = -\gamma_w h_c$ | Negative above water table | kN/m² |
| 5 | Effective stress (Terzaghi) | $\sigma'$ | $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ | Saturated soil, static water | kN/m² |
| 6 | Buoyant unit weight | $\gamma'$ | $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ | Submerged soil | kN/m³ |
| 7 | Dry unit weight | $\gamma_d$ | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$ | $G$ = specific gravity, $e$ = void ratio | kN/m³ |
| 8 | Saturated unit weight | $\gamma_{sat}$ | $\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(G + e)\gamma_w}{1 + e}$ | Voids fully water-filled | kN/m³ |
| 9 | Bulk (moist) unit weight | $\gamma_{bulk}$ | $\gamma_{bulk} = \dfrac{(G + Se)\gamma_w}{1 + e}$ | $S$ = degree of saturation | kN/m³ |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World KTU Practice

* **Settlement analysis of foundations** — The effective stress *increase* due to a footing load is added to the pre-construction $\sigma'$, and the difference governs one-dimensional consolidation via $\Delta e = -C_c \log(\sigma'_0/\sigma'_1)$.
* **Lateral earth pressure design** — $K_0 \sigma'_0$ at-rest pressure depends on the *effective* overburden stress, not total.
* **Slope stability** — Pore pressures at the slip surface dictate the factor of safety.
* **Excavation safety** — Dewatering or sheet piling aims to lower $u$, raising $\sigma'$ and improving stability.

> [!NOTE]
> The single most important takeaway for KTU exams: **Total stress and pore water pressure are *not* independent — they must be computed at the *same* point, and the water-table datum must be tracked meticulously.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Effective Stress from First Principles

Consider a horizontal cross-section of area $A$ at depth $z$ inside a saturated soil. Three categories of force act on this plane:

* **Weight of solids above the plane** = $W_s = \gamma_{dry} \cdot A \cdot z \cdot (1-n)$ where $n$ is porosity.
* **Weight of water in the voids above the plane** = $W_w = \gamma_w \cdot A \cdot z \cdot n$.
* **Buoyancy force (upward) on the soil skeleton** = $W_b = \gamma_w \cdot A \cdot z \cdot (1-n)$ (Archimedes).

The **total downward force per unit area** (total stress) is the sum of the first two:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{W_s + W_w}{A} \\
       &= \gamma_{dry} \cdot z \cdot (1-n) + \gamma_w \cdot z \cdot n
\end{aligned}
$$

The **upward buoyancy pressure** at the plane (pore water pressure) is:

$$u = \gamma_w \cdot z$$

The **net inter-particle contact force** (effective stress) is the total downward force minus the buoyancy acting on the grains:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma' &= \sigma - u \\
        &= \left[ \gamma_{dry} \cdot z \cdot (1-n) + \gamma_w \cdot z \cdot n \right] - \gamma_w \cdot z
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma' &= \gamma_{dry} \cdot z \cdot (1-n) - \gamma_w \cdot z \cdot (1-n) \\
        &= (\gamma_{dry} - \gamma_w) \cdot z \cdot (1-n)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ and $\gamma_{sat} = \gamma_{dry} + n \cdot \gamma_w$, the expression reduces elegantly to:

$$\boxed{\sigma' = \gamma' \cdot z \quad \text{(below the water table)}}$$

This is the **fundamental result**: effective stress in a submerged soil increases linearly with depth at a rate of the **buoyant unit weight**, not the bulk or saturated unit weight.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Demonstration — A Layered Profile

**Problem Statement (typical KTU numerical type):**
A soil profile consists of the following:

* **Layer 1 (0 to 2 m):** Moist sand with $\gamma_{moist} = 18$ kN/m³, water table at **1.5 m** below ground.
* **Layer 2 (2 m to 5 m):** Saturated clay with $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³.
* **Water conditions:** $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³.

**Required:** Calculate $\sigma$, $u$, and $\sigma'$ at depths **0 m, 1.5 m, 2 m, and 5 m**.

---

#### Step 1: At $z = 0$ m (Ground Surface)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= 0 \\
u &= 0 \quad (\text{atmospheric datum}) \\
\sigma' &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 2: At $z = 1.5$ m (Water Table — still inside Layer 1)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \gamma_{moist} \cdot 1.5 = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
u &= 0 \quad (\text{exactly at the water-table datum}) \\
\sigma' &= 27 - 0 = 27 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 3: At $z = 2$ m (Bottom of Layer 1 / Top of Layer 2)

Here, the point is 0.5 m **below** the water table, still within Layer 1 (which is moist sand). The moist sand *above* the water table is moist; *below* the water table in Layer 1, it is effectively saturated — but for the simpler model we treat Layer 1 as moist throughout for the $\sigma$ computation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \gamma_{moist} \cdot 2 = 18 \times 2 = 36 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
u &= \gamma_w \cdot (2 - 1.5) = 9.81 \times 0.5 = 4.905 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
\sigma' &= 36 - 4.905 = 31.095 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 4: At $z = 5$ m (Bottom of Layer 2)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= (18 \times 2) + (20 \times 3) = 36 + 60 = 96 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
u &= \gamma_w \cdot (5 - 1.5) = 9.81 \times 3.5 = 34.335 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
\sigma' &= 96 - 34.335 = 61.665 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification via buoyant unit weight** for the submerged part of Layer 2 (from 2 m to 5 m, entirely below the water table):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma'_{\text{from 2 m to 5 m}} &= \gamma' \cdot \Delta z = (\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w) \cdot 3 \\
&= (20 - 9.81) \times 3 = 10.19 \times 3 = 30.57 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding the $\sigma'$ contribution from the upper 2 m (where $u = 0$ above the water table for the 0 to 1.5 m portion, and the 1.5–2 m portion above the saturated Layer 2):

$$
\sigma'_{\text{total at 5 m}} = (18 \times 1.5) + (18 \times 0.5 - 4.905) + 30.57 = 27 + 4.095 + 30.57 = 61.665 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \checkmark
$$

Both methods agree, confirming the calculation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — The Five Water-Table Scenarios

| **Scenario** | **Water Table Position** | **Pore Pressure $u$** | **Effective Stress Behaviour** |
|:-------------|:--------------------------|:----------------------|:-------------------------------|
| A | Far below point of interest | $u = 0$ | $\sigma' = \sigma = \gamma \cdot z$ (full dry) |
| B | At ground surface | $u = \gamma_w z$ | $\sigma' = \gamma' z$ (full buoyant) |
| C | At intermediate depth $z_w$ above the point | $u = \gamma_w (z - z_w)$ | $\sigma' = \sigma - \gamma_w (z - z_w)$ |
| D | At the point itself | $u = 0$ (datum) | $\sigma' = \sigma$ |
| E | Capillary saturation above WT | $u = -\gamma_w h_c$ | $\sigma' = \sigma + \gamma_w h_c$ (elevated) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Stress Component Resolution Architecture

The following flowchart maps how a vertical stress at depth decomposes into its governing components, mirroring the KTU 2024 analytical approach.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vertical Stress at Depth z] --> B{Is the Point Below the Water Table?}
    B -- No --> C[Soil Above WT Zone]
    C --> D[Total Stress sigma equals gamma times z]
    D --> E[Pore Pressure u equals 0]
    E --> F[Effective Stress sigma dash equals sigma]
    B -- Yes --> G[Soil Below WT Zone]
    G --> H[Total Stress sigma equals sum of gamma i times h i]
    G --> I[Pore Pressure u equals gamma w times h w]
    I --> J[Buoyant Unit Weight gamma dash equals gamma sat minus gamma w]
    H --> K[Effective Stress sigma dash equals sigma minus u]
    J --> K
    K --> L[sigma dash equals gamma dash times delta z]
    A --> M{Capillary Rise Present?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Negative Pore Pressure u equals minus gamma w times h c]
    N --> O[Effective Stress increases above hydrostatic]
    F --> P[Final sigma dash used in settlement and shear design]
    L --> P
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Decision Tree for Water-Table Position (Layered Soil)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute Total Stress sigma] --> B[Locate Water Table Depth z w]
    B --> C{Is point at depth z above or below z w?}
    C -- Above --> D[u equals 0]
    D --> E[sigma dash equals sigma]
    C -- Below --> F[Compute h w equals z minus z w]
    F --> G[u equals gamma w times h w]
    G --> H[Is the point in the same layer as z w?]
    H -- Yes --> I[Use gamma moist above and gamma sat below in summation]
    H -- No --> J[Use gamma sat and gamma dash for that layer]
    I --> K[sigma dash equals sigma minus u]
    J --> K
    E --> L[Report sigma, u, sigma dash]
    K --> L
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Failure Mode Mapping for Exam Pitfalls

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Common KTU Mistakes] --> B[Using gamma sat instead of gamma dash below WT]
    A --> C[Forgetting u is measured from WT not ground surface]
    A --> D[Mixing dry and saturated layers in summation]
    A --> E[Ignoring capillary rise in clayey silts]
    B --> F[Marks Lost in Effective Stress]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Read Question Carefully and Tabulate Layer by Layer]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**State and explain Terzaghi's principle of effective stress. Why is effective stress, and not total stress, the controlling parameter in soil-strength problems?**

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**
Terzaghi's principle (1936) states that for a saturated soil mass under static loading, the **effective stress** (inter-granular stress) acting on the soil skeleton is given by:

$$\sigma' = \sigma - u$$

where $\sigma$ = total stress at a point, and $u$ = pore water pressure. Effective stress controls **shear strength** (via $\tau_f = c' + \sigma' \tan \phi'$) and **volume change** (consolidation: $\Delta e \propto \log \sigma'$). Total stress alone is irrelevant because the water within the voids carries a portion of the load hydrostatically without producing inter-particle friction. **(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for equation, 1 for engineering justification.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**Define submerged (buoyant) unit weight. How is it different from saturated unit weight? When is each used in stress computations?**

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**
The **submerged (buoyant) unit weight** is the effective unit weight of soil below the water table after subtracting the upward hydrostatic buoyancy:

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$$

It differs from $\gamma_{sat}$ in that $\gamma_{sat}$ is the *actual* weight per unit volume of the saturated soil mass, while $\gamma'$ is the *net* weight transmitted through grain contacts. In computations: $\gamma_{sat}$ is used to find **total stress** below the water table, whereas $\gamma'$ is used to find the **effective stress increment per unit depth** below the water table. **(3 marks.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3]**

**(a)** A 4 m thick layer of sand overlies a 6 m thick layer of saturated clay. The sand has $\gamma_{dry} = 16$ kN/m³ above the water table and $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³ below it. The water table is at 2 m below ground surface. For the clay, $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³. Calculate the total stress, pore water pressure, and effective stress at depths **3 m, 5 m, and 10 m** below the ground surface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Briefly explain the phenomenon of **capillary rise** in soils. How does it affect the effective stress distribution above the water table in fine-grained soils? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a):

**Layer breakdown:**
- 0 to 2 m: dry sand, $\gamma_d = 16$ kN/m³
- 2 m to 4 m: saturated sand, $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³
- 4 m to 10 m: saturated clay, $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³

**At z = 3 m** (in saturated sand, 1 m below WT):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= (16 \times 2) + (19 \times 1) = 32 + 19 = 51 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
u &= \gamma_w \times (3 - 2) = 9.81 \times 1 = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
\sigma' &= 51 - 9.81 = 41.19 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**At z = 5 m** (1 m into clay):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= (16 \times 2) + (19 \times 2) + (20 \times 1) = 32 + 38 + 20 = 90 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
u &= \gamma_w \times (5 - 2) = 9.81 \times 3 = 29.43 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
\sigma' &= 90 - 29.43 = 60.57 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**At z = 10 m** (bottom of clay):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= (16 \times 2) + (19 \times 2) + (20 \times 6) = 32 + 38 + 120 = 190 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
u &= \gamma_w \times (10 - 2) = 9.81 \times 8 = 78.48 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
\sigma' &= 190 - 78.48 = 111.52 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

##### Model Solution to (b):

Capillary rise is the upward migration of water into the soil voids above the water table due to **surface tension** at the meniscus of pore water and the **matric suction** of fine-grained soils. The height of capillary rise $h_c$ is given by:

$$h_c = \frac{4 T \cos \theta}{\gamma_w \cdot d_{10}}$$

where $T$ = surface tension, $\theta$ = contact angle, $d_{10}$ = effective grain size. Capillary rise is significant in **silty and fine-sandy soils** (a few decimetres to metres) and very high in clays (theoretically many metres, but takes years in practice).

**Effect on effective stress:** Within the capillary zone, the pore water pressure is *negative*:

$$u = -\gamma_w h_c$$

Therefore, by Terzaghi's principle:

$$\sigma' = \sigma - (-\gamma_w h_c) = \sigma + \gamma_w h_c$$

The effective stress in the capillary zone is **higher** than the total stress. This explains:
* Apparent cohesion in moist sands (the "stickiness" of damp beach sand).
* Desiccation cracks in clayey soils as evaporation removes the meniscus support.
* Apparent temporary strength gain in partially saturated slopes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> 1. **Most common error (–2 marks):** Students use $\gamma_d = 16$ kN/m³ for the sand between 2 m and 4 m, ignoring that this portion is now **saturated** (use $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³). Read the problem statement layer by layer.
> 2. **Second common error (–1 mark):** Pore pressure depth is measured from the **water table**, *not* the ground surface. Always identify the WT datum first.
> 3. **Third common error (–1 mark):** The transition from sand to clay at 4 m does **not** reset the pore pressure; $u$ is a continuous hydrostatic function of *depth below WT*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 3 Alternative]**

**(a)** A soil profile has three layers. Layer 1 (top): moist sand, 3 m thick, $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³, with the water table 1 m below the top of Layer 1. Layer 2: saturated sand, 4 m thick, $\gamma_{sat} = 19.5$ kN/m³. Layer 3: saturated clay, 5 m thick, $\gamma_{sat} = 20.5$ kN/m³. Calculate the **effective stress at the mid-height of Layer 3**, taking $\gamma_w = 10$ kN/m³ (simplified). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a sketch, describe how the **total, pore water, and effective stress** vary with depth in a homogeneous soil deposit in which the water table is at the ground surface. Comment on the magnitudes of the gradients. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a):

**Mid-height of Layer 3** is at depth: 3 + 4 + 2.5 = **9.5 m** below ground surface. The water table is at 1 m, so $h_w = 9.5 - 1 = 8.5$ m.

**Total stress at z = 9.5 m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= (17 \times 1) + (17 \times 2) + (19.5 \times 4) + (20.5 \times 2.5) \\
       &= 17 + 34 + 78 + 51.25 = 180.25 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

(Here, the 0–1 m portion is dry sand, the 1–3 m portion is moist sand above WT — both use $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³; the 3–7 m portion is saturated sand; the 7–9.5 m portion is saturated clay.)

**Pore water pressure:**

$$u = 10 \times 8.5 = 85 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Effective stress:**

$$\sigma' = 180.25 - 85 = 95.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

##### Model Solution to (b):

**Sketch description (to be drawn on answer sheet):**
A graph of depth $z$ (vertical axis, downward positive) versus stress (horizontal axis). Three lines emanate from the origin (or from the WT datum):

* **Total stress $\sigma$:** Begins at zero at the WT (= ground surface) and increases linearly with depth at slope $\gamma_{sat}$.
* **Pore water pressure $u$:** Also begins at zero at the WT and increases linearly with slope $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³.
* **Effective stress $\sigma'$:** The difference between the two, increasing linearly with depth at slope $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$.

**Gradients:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\sigma}{dz} &= \gamma_{sat} \quad \text{(e.g., 18 to 22 kN/m}^3) \\
\frac{du}{dz} &= \gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3 \\
\frac{d\sigma'}{dz} &= \gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w \quad \text{(typically 8 to 12 kN/m}^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **total stress grows fastest**, **effective stress grows slowest** (since buoyancy removes the water component), and the **gap between $\sigma$ and $\sigma'$ widens** with depth, equal to $u$ at any depth.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> 1. **Sketch quality (–2 marks):** Examiners explicitly deduct marks if the three lines are not clearly labelled and the slopes ($d\sigma/dz$, $du/dz$, $d\sigma'/dz$) are not annotated.
> 2. **Mid-height trap (–1.5 marks):** "Mid-height of Layer 3" means the *centre* of that layer's thickness, not its bottom. Add half the layer thickness to the top of the layer.
> 3. **Mixing $\gamma_{moist}$ and $\gamma_{sat}$ (–1 mark):** Above the WT, use the *moist* or *dry* unit weight; below the WT, use $\gamma_{sat}$ for $\sigma$ computation and $\gamma'$ for $\sigma'$ increment.
> 4. **Numerical simplification:** KTU often uses $\gamma_w = 10$ kN/m³ in problems to speed up hand calculation. If the problem says "use $\gamma_w = 10$ kN/m³", use that — do not substitute 9.81 kN/m³ unless the problem demands it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam Hall:**
>
> - **Terzaghi's equation $\sigma' = \sigma - u$** is the *single most tested* formula in this module — memorize it and its derivation.
> - **Pore pressure is measured from the water table**, not the ground surface. Always identify the WT datum first.
> - **Total stress** uses the appropriate unit weight for each layer in the column: $\gamma_d$ or $\gamma_{moist}$ above WT, $\gamma_{sat}$ below WT.
> - **Effective stress** is computed as $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ (numerical subtraction) **or** by accumulating $\gamma' \cdot \Delta z$ for each fully-submerged layer.
> - **Buoyant unit weight** $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ is the slope of the effective stress line below the water table.
> - **Capillary rise** produces *negative* pore pressure, which *increases* effective stress above the WT — critical for fine-grained soils.
> - **Layer-wise summation** is the workhorse numerical technique; tabulate (depth, $\gamma$, $\sigma$, $u$, $\sigma'$) to avoid arithmetic slips.
> - **Standard KTU simplification**: $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ (or 10 kN/m³ if stated) — the difference is rarely penalized.
> - The **effective stress principle is the only mechanism** by which soil skeleton carries load; pore water does not contribute to shear strength or compression.
> - Always state units (kN/m²) for clarity; KTU examiners reward dimensional discipline.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concentrated Load - Boussinesq Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Boussinesq Equations** (formulated by **Joseph Valentin Boussinesq**, 1885) constitute the foundational classical solution of the **Theory of Elasticity** applied to a **homogeneous, isotropic, semi-infinite, weightless, elastic medium** (the soil mass). The equations describe the state of stress and displacement at any interior point of such a medium when a **concentrated point load ($Q$)** is applied normal to the horizontal surface of the half-space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:**
> The Boussinesq solution forms the theoretical backbone for all **stress distribution methods** in soil mechanics, including the Newmark's influence chart, the 2:1 method, and Westergaard's solution. It assumes the soil behaves as a **linear elastic material**, which is an *idealization* — actual soil is non-linear, inelastic, and anisotropic. However, the equations provide acceptably accurate results for working stress levels.

## 1.2 Fundamental Assumptions of Boussinesq Theory

1. The soil mass is a **semi-infinite** half-space (extends infinitely in horizontal and downward directions).
2. The soil is **homogeneous** (same properties at every point) and **isotropic** (same properties in all directions).
3. The soil is **elastic** — obeys **Hooke's Law** — characterized by **Young's Modulus ($E$)** and **Poisson's Ratio ($\mu$)**.
4. The soil is **weightless** — self-weight stresses are neglected.
5. The applied load $Q$ is a **point load** (concentrated force) acting perpendicular to the surface.
6. The surface of the half-space is **free of shear stresses** and **free of normal stresses** outside the point of load application.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Pressing Your Thumb into a Soft Cake**
>
> Imagine pushing your **thumb vertically downward** into the surface of a **soft, uniformly textured cake** (the semi-infinite elastic medium). Your thumb applies a concentrated force $Q$ at a single point.
>
> - The cake surface **directly beneath your thumb** experiences the **maximum vertical stress** ($\sigma_z$ is maximum at $z$ just below $r = 0$).
> - As you probe **deeper into the cake** (increasing $z$), the stress spreads outward in a **bulb-shaped zone** (the **pressure bulb** or **isobar**).
> - The stress at points **far away horizontally** (large $r$) is much smaller.
> - The cake also **bulges outward** at the surface near the load — this is the **radial displacement**.
>
> The Boussinesq equations mathematically quantify *exactly* how this "stress bulb" decays with depth and horizontal distance, allowing engineers to compute settlement of footings and stresses beneath foundations.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Concentrated Point Load ($Q$)** — measured in **kN** or **N** (Force units).
- **Depth ($z$)** — measured in **m** (meters); measured vertically downward from the surface.
- **Radial distance ($r$)** — measured in **m**; horizontal distance from the line of action of $Q$.
- **Poisson's Ratio ($\mu$)** — dimensionless; for most soils $\mu \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$ (saturated undrained clay $\approx 0.5$; dry sand $\approx 0.25$–$0.35$).
- **Young's Modulus ($E$)** — measured in **kN/m²** or **kPa** (relevant for displacement equations).
- **Resultant radial distance** $R = \sqrt{r^2 + z^2}$ (the straight-line distance from the point of load application to the point of interest in the medium).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Setup & Coordinate System

Let the point load $Q$ act at the **origin $O$** on the **horizontal surface** of the elastic half-space. We seek stresses and displacements at any arbitrary point $P$ located at:
- Depth $z$ below the surface
- Radial distance $r$ from the vertical axis through $O$

The straight-line distance from $O$ to $P$ is:

$$R = \sqrt{r^{2} + z^{2}}$$

Let $\beta$ be the angle that $OP$ makes with the **vertical axis** (so $\tan\beta = r/z$). This angle is critical because all Boussinesq stress components can be expressed as functions of $\beta$ alone.

## 2.2 The Six Boussinesq Stress & Displacement Equations

### 2.2.1 Vertical Normal Stress ($\sigma_z$) — The Most Important Equation

$$\sigma_{z} = \frac{3Q}{2\pi z^{2}} \cdot \cos^{5}\beta = \frac{3Q}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{z^{3}}{R^{5}}$$

> This is the **most heavily tested** Boussinesq equation in KTU exams. It gives the vertical stress increase at depth $z$ on the vertical line passing through the load.

### 2.2.2 Radial Normal Stress ($\sigma_r$)

$$\sigma_{r} = \frac{Q}{2\pi}\left[\frac{3r^{2}z}{R^{5}} - \frac{(1-2\mu)}{R(R+z)}\right]$$

### 2.2.3 Tangential (Hoop) Normal Stress ($\sigma_\theta$)

$$\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{Q}{2\pi}\left[\frac{(1-2\mu)z}{R^{3}} - \frac{(1-2\mu)}{R(R+z)}\right]$$

### 2.2.4 Shear Stress on Vertical and Horizontal Planes ($\tau_{rz}$)

$$\tau_{rz} = \frac{3Q}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{r z^{2}}{R^{5}}$$

### 2.2.5 Vertical Displacement (Settlement Component) ($w_z$)

$$w_{z} = \frac{Q}{2\pi E}\left[\frac{(1+\mu)z^{2}}{R^{3}} + \frac{2(1-\mu^{2})}{R}\right]$$

> This equation is the foundation for **elastic settlement calculations** of shallow foundations (used in KTU Module 4 and Module 5).

### 2.2.6 Radial Displacement ($u_r$)

$$u_{r} = \frac{Q(1+\mu)}{2\pi E}\left[\frac{r z}{R^{3}} - \frac{(1-2\mu)r}{R(R+z)}\right]$$

## 2.3 The Influence Factor Concept (KTU High-Yield)

The **vertical stress influence factor** $K_B$ (or $I_B$) is defined as:

$$K_{B} = \frac{\sigma_{z}}{Q/z^{2}} = \frac{3}{2\pi}\left[\frac{1}{1+(r/z)^{2}}\right]^{5/2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> $K_B$ is **dimensionless** and depends **only** on the ratio $r/z$. By tabulating $K_B$ against $r/z$, engineers can rapidly compute vertical stress at any point in the soil mass without recalculating complex trigonometric expressions. This is the **standard KTU examination approach** for solving numerical problems.

## 2.4 The Pressure Bulb Concept (Isobar)

An **isobar** is a contour line connecting all points in the soil mass that experience the **same vertical stress** $\sigma_z$ (typically expressed as a fraction of $Q/z^2$ or as a fraction of the surface stress $q$). The **pressure bulb** for $\sigma_z = 0.1 q$ or $\sigma_z = 0.2 q$ is widely used in foundation design to identify the **significant depth of stress influence** (usually taken as the depth where $\sigma_z = 0.1 q$ or $0.2 q$).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **KTU Frequency** |
|:-----:|:-------------|:------------|:------------------|
| 1 | Vertical Stress $\sigma_z$ | $\dfrac{3Q}{2\pi}\cdot\dfrac{z^{3}}{R^{5}}$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Vertical Stress (angle form) | $\dfrac{3Q}{2\pi z^{2}}\cos^{5}\beta$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Influence Factor $K_B$ | $\dfrac{3}{2\pi}\left[\dfrac{1}{1+(r/z)^{2}}\right]^{5/2}$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Radial Stress $\sigma_r$ | $\dfrac{Q}{2\pi}\left[\dfrac{3r^{2}z}{R^{5}} - \dfrac{(1-2\mu)}{R(R+z)}\right]$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Tangential Stress $\sigma_\theta$ | $\dfrac{Q}{2\pi}\left[\dfrac{(1-2\mu)z}{R^{3}} - \dfrac{(1-2\mu)}{R(R+z)}\right]$ | ⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Shear Stress $\tau_{rz}$ | $\dfrac{3Q}{2\pi}\cdot\dfrac{r z^{2}}{R^{5}}$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Vertical Displacement $w_z$ | $\dfrac{Q}{2\pi E}\left[\dfrac{(1+\mu)z^{2}}{R^{3}} + \dfrac{2(1-\mu^{2})}{R}\right]$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Radial Displacement $u_r$ | $\dfrac{Q(1+\mu)}{2\pi E}\left[\dfrac{r z}{R^{3}} - \dfrac{(1-2\mu)r}{R(R+z)}\right]$ | ⭐⭐ |
| 9 | Geometric Relation | $R = \sqrt{r^{2}+z^{2}}$, $\tan\beta = r/z$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of shallow and deep foundations** — determination of stress at the level of the foundation base of an adjacent footing.
- **Computation of consolidation settlement** — vertical stress increase is integrated over the compressible layer thickness.
- **Evaluation of stress influence on adjacent structures** — critical in **urban construction** where new footings may induce settlement in neighbouring buildings.
- **Pile group analysis** — stress superposition from multiple piles uses Boussinesq influence factors.
- **Construction of Newmark's Influence Chart** — direct integration of the Boussinesq solution over a loaded area.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Boussinesq Vertical Stress Equation

The full derivation uses the **Boussinesq-Papkovitch potential function** approach. For a KTU exam, we present the **classical derivation** based on **invertigation of the Kelvin problem** combined with **stress function** $\phi = C/\sqrt{r^2 + z^2}$.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Choice of Stress Function

We need a stress function $\phi$ that satisfies:
- **Biharmonic equation** $\nabla^{4}\phi = 0$ throughout the half-space.
- **Boundary conditions**: $\sigma_z = 0$, $\tau_{rz} = 0$, $\tau_{\theta z} = 0$ at $z = 0$ for all $r \neq 0$.
- **Equilibrium**: A point load $Q$ at the origin must be balanced by the integrated stresses over any hemispherical surface.

The chosen stress function is:

$$\phi = \frac{C}{R} = \frac{C}{\sqrt{r^{2}+z^{2}}}$$

where $C$ is a constant to be determined from boundary conditions.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Stress Components from Stress Function

For axisymmetric problems, the stress components are obtained from $\phi$ using Love's strain function approach. The stresses are:

$$\sigma_{r} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\mu\nabla^{2}\phi - \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial r^{2}}\right)$$

$$\sigma_{z} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left((2-\mu)\nabla^{2}\phi - \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial z^{2}}\right)$$

$$\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\mu\nabla^{2}\phi - \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r}\right)$$

$$\tau_{rz} = \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left((1-\mu)\nabla^{2}\phi - \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial z^{2}}\right)$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compute the Laplacian

The Laplacian of $\phi = C/R$ in cylindrical coordinates:

$$\nabla^{2}\phi = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial z^{2}}$$

First, compute the partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} = -\frac{Cr}{R^{3}}, \quad \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial r^{2}} = \frac{C(3r^{2}-R^{2})}{R^{5}} = \frac{C(2r^{2}-z^{2})}{R^{5}}$$

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = -\frac{Cz}{R^{3}}, \quad \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial z^{2}} = \frac{C(3z^{2}-R^{2})}{R^{5}} = \frac{C(2z^{2}-r^{2})}{R^{5}}$$

Therefore:

$$\nabla^{2}\phi = \frac{C(2z^{2}-r^{2})}{R^{5}} + \frac{C(2z^{2}-r^{2})}{R^{5}} = 0 \quad \text{(for } r \neq 0, z \neq 0\text{)}$$

This confirms $\phi = C/R$ satisfies the **biharmonic equation** and the equation of compatibility. The Laplacian is zero everywhere except at the origin (which represents the point load).

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Compute $\sigma_z$

Since $\nabla^{2}\phi = 0$ (away from origin):

$$\sigma_{z} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(-\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial z^{2}}\right) = -\frac{\partial^{3}\phi}{\partial z^{3}}$$

Compute $\partial^{3}\phi/\partial z^{3}$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial z^{2}} = \frac{C(2z^{2}-r^{2})}{R^{5}}$$

Differentiating again with respect to $z$:

$$\frac{\partial^{3}\phi}{\partial z^{3}} = C\cdot \frac{(4z)R^{5} - (2z^{2}-r^{2})\cdot 5R^{3}\cdot(z/R)}{R^{10}}$$

Simplifying using $R^{2} = r^{2}+z^{2}$:

$$\frac{\partial^{3}\phi}{\partial z^{3}} = \frac{C(4zR^{2} - 5z(2z^{2}-r^{2}))}{R^{7}} = \frac{Cz(4R^{2}-10z^{2}+5r^{2})}{R^{7}}$$

Substitute $r^{2} = R^{2}-z^{2}$:

$$4R^{2} - 10z^{2} + 5(R^{2}-z^{2}) = 9R^{2} - 15z^{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial^{3}\phi}{\partial z^{3}} = \frac{Cz(9R^{2}-15z^{2})}{R^{7}} = \frac{3Cz(3R^{2}-5z^{2})}{R^{7}}$$

And:

$$\sigma_{z} = -\frac{3Cz(3R^{2}-5z^{2})}{R^{7}}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Apply Boundary Condition (Resultant Force = $Q$)

Consider a horizontal plane at depth $z$. The total vertical force on this plane must equal $Q$:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{\infty}\sigma_{z}\cdot 2\pi r\,dr = \int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{-3Cz(3R^{2}-5z^{2})}{R^{7}}\cdot 2\pi r\,dr$$

Using the substitution $R^{2} = r^{2}+z^{2}$, $2R\,dR = 2r\,dr$, and integrating:

$$Q = -6\pi Cz \int_{z}^{\infty}\frac{(3R^{2}-5z^{2})}{R^{6}}\,dR = -6\pi Cz\left[-\frac{3}{4R^{4}} + \frac{5z^{2}}{5R^{5}}\right]_{z}^{\infty}$$

$$Q = -6\pi Cz\left[0 - \left(-\frac{3}{4z^{4}} + \frac{1}{z^{4}}\right)\right] = -6\pi Cz\left[\frac{-1}{4z^{4}}\right] = \frac{6\pi C}{4z^{3}} = \frac{3\pi C}{2z^{3}}$$

Solving for $C$:

$$C = \frac{2Q z^{3}}{3\pi}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Final Form of $\sigma_z$

Substituting $C$ back:

$$\sigma_{z} = -\frac{3z(3R^{2}-5z^{2})}{R^{7}}\cdot \frac{2Qz^{3}}{3\pi} = \frac{2Qz^{4}(3R^{2}-5z^{2})(-1)}{\pi R^{7}}$$

Since $3R^{2} - 5z^{2} = 3(r^{2}+z^{2}) - 5z^{2} = 3r^{2} - 2z^{2}$, and noting sign conventions (compressive positive in soils):

$$\sigma_{z} = \frac{3Q z^{3}}{2\pi R^{5}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusive Derivation Result:**
> The final Boussinesq equation for vertical stress due to a point load is:
> $$\boxed{\sigma_{z} = \frac{3Q}{2\pi z^{2}}\left[\frac{1}{1+(r/z)^{2}}\right]^{5/2}}$$

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Derivation of the Angle Form

Using $\cos\beta = z/R$ and $R = z/\cos\beta$:

$$\sigma_{z} = \frac{3Q}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{z^{3}}{(z/\cos\beta)^{5}} = \frac{3Q}{2\pi z^{2}}\cos^{5}\beta$$

This angle form is particularly useful for computing stress on a line making a constant angle with the vertical.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** A concentrated load of **$Q = 100$ kN** acts at the surface of a soil mass. Compute the vertical stress increase at a point **$P$** located at **depth $z = 4$ m** and **radial distance $r = 3$ m** from the line of action of the load. Use the Boussinesq equation.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute the resultant distance $R$.

$$R = \sqrt{r^{2}+z^{2}} = \sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}} = \sqrt{9+16} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio $r/z$.

$$\frac{r}{z} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75$$

**Step 3:** Apply the influence factor formula.

$$K_{B} = \frac{3}{2\pi}\left[\frac{1}{1+(0.75)^{2}}\right]^{5/2} = \frac{3}{2\pi}\left[\frac{1}{1+0.5625}\right]^{5/2}$$

$$K_{B} = \frac{3}{2\pi}\left[\frac{1}{1.5625}\right]^{2.5} = \frac{3}{2\pi}\left[0.64\right]^{2.5}$$

$$K_{B} = \frac{3}{2\pi}(0.3277) = \frac{0.9831}{6.2832} \approx 0.1565$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\sigma_z$.

$$\sigma_{z} = K_{B}\cdot\frac{Q}{z^{2}} = 0.1565\cdot\frac{100}{4^{2}} = 0.1565\cdot 6.25 = 0.978 \text{ kN/m}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{z} \approx 0.978 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 5 (Cross-check using direct formula):**

$$\sigma_{z} = \frac{3Q}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{z^{3}}{R^{5}} = \frac{3\times 100}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{4^{3}}{5^{5}} = \frac{300}{6.2832}\cdot\frac{64}{3125}$$

$$= 47.746 \cdot 0.02048 = 0.978 \text{ kPa} \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Variation of $\sigma_z$ Along the Vertical Axis ($r = 0$)

| **Depth $z$ (m)** | **$r$ (m)** | **$r/z$** | **$K_B$** | **$\sigma_z$ (kPa) for $Q = 100$ kN** |
|:-----------------:|:-----------:|:---------:|:---------:|:--------------------------------------:|
| 0 (theoretical) | 0 | 0 | 0.4775 | ∞ (singularity) |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0.4775 | 47.75 |
| 2 | 0 | 0 | 0.4775 | 11.94 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 0.4775 | 5.31 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0.4775 | 2.98 |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | 0.4775 | 1.91 |
| 2 | 2 | 1.0 | 0.0844 | 2.11 |
| 2 | 4 | 2.0 | 0.0085 | 0.213 |
| 4 | 3 | 0.75 | 0.1565 | 0.978 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Along the vertical axis ($r=0$), the influence factor $K_B$ is **constant** at $0.4775$ for *all* depths, but $\sigma_z$ decreases as $1/z^2$ due to the $Q/z^2$ term. This is a **very common KTU conceptual question**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Boussinesq Solution Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Concentrated Point Load Q Applied at Origin O on Surface] --> B[Soil Mass - Homogeneous Isotropic Elastic Semi-Infinite Half Space]
    B --> C[Selection of Stress Function phi = C divided by R]
    C --> D[Verify Biharmonic Equation nabla squared phi equals 0]
    D --> E[Derive Stress Components sigma sub r, sigma sub z, sigma sub theta, tau sub rz]
    E --> F[Apply Boundary Condition Resultant Vertical Force equals Q on Horizontal Plane]
    F --> G[Determine Constant C equals 2Q z cubed divided by 3 pi]
    G --> H[Final Boussinesq Equations]
    H --> H1[Vertical Stress sigma sub z equals 3Q over 2 pi times z cubed over R to the 5]
    H --> H2[Radial Stress sigma sub r]
    H --> H3[Tangential Stress sigma sub theta]
    H --> H4[Shear Stress tau sub rz]
    H --> H5[Vertical Displacement w sub z]
    H --> H6[Radial Displacement u sub r]
    H1 --> I[Engineering Applications]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    H6 --> I
    I --> I1[Settlement Analysis of Shallow Foundations]
    I --> I2[Stress Influence on Adjacent Structures]
    I --> I3[Construction of Newmarks Influence Chart]
    I --> I4[Design of Pile Groups]
    I --> I5[Pressure Bulb Construction for Foundation Design]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress Distribution Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Point Load Q at Origin O] -->|Vertical Axis| B[Depth z Direction]
    A -->|Horizontal Direction| C[Radial Distance r]
    B --> D[Resultant Vector R equals square root of r squared plus z squared]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Angle beta between OP and Vertical Axis]
    E --> F[tan beta equals r divided by z]
    E --> G[cos beta equals z divided by R]
    E --> H[sin beta equals r divided by R]
    F --> I[sigma sub z equals 3Q over 2 pi z squared times cos to the 5 of beta]
    G --> I
    H --> J[tau sub rz equals 3Q over 2 pi times r z squared over R to the 5]
    I --> K[Pressure Bulb Construction]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Significant Depth Identified at sigma z equals 0.1 q or 0.2 q]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description of Pressure Bulb (Isobar)

The Boussinesq pressure bulb is a **3D bulb-shaped surface** representing contours of equal vertical stress $\sigma_z$. The key isobars (typically $\sigma_z = 0.1 q$, $0.2 q$, $0.3 q$, $0.5 q$ of the equivalent surface stress) define the **zone of significant stress influence**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> The depth of significant stress influence is conventionally taken as the depth where $\sigma_z = 0.1 q$ ($q$ = equivalent surface stress). Beyond this depth, the stress increase is **negligible** for engineering purposes. This depth is approximately **1.5 to 2.0 times the width of the loaded area** for square/circular footings, and **2.0 to 3.0 times the width** for strip footings.
