# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Shear Strength — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shear Strength of Soil** is defined as the maximum internal resistance per unit area that a soil mass can offer against failure or sliding along any plane within the soil body. It is the magnitude of **shearing stress (τ)** at the instant of failure on a critical plane. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, shear strength is the *ultimate* or *peak* shearing resistance mobilized on the failure surface, expressed in **kN/m²** or **kPa**.

Mathematically, the shear strength is represented as a function of the normal effective stress acting on the failure plane. The general form is:

$$
\tau_f = f(\sigma', \text{soil properties})
$$

where $\sigma'$ is the **effective normal stress** and $f$ is a constitutive function dependent on the soil type, drainage conditions, density, and stress history.

### Real-World Analogy — The "Stack of Books" Concept

Imagine a stack of heavy textbooks placed on a tilted table. As you tilt the table gradually, there comes a moment when the books start to slide. The force resisting this sliding is **friction** between the books and the table. Soil shear strength works similarly — every soil particle is in frictional and cohesive contact with its neighbors. When a shearing force is applied (like a building load transferring to the ground), the soil resists up to a limit, after which it **fails by sliding** along internal surfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Shear Strength important in Civil Engineering?**
> - Bearing capacity of foundations depends directly on shear strength.
> - Slope stability of embankments, dams, and natural hillsides.
> - Earth pressure on retaining structures.
> - Stability of tunnels, excavations, and underground openings.
> - Pavement design and subgrade strength evaluation.

## 1.2 Mohr's Circle of Stress — The Graphical Stress Representation

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Mohr's Circle of Stress** is a two-dimensional graphical representation of the state of stress at a point, developed by **Otto Mohr (1882)**. It plots normal stress (σ) on the abscissa (x-axis) and shear stress (τ) on the ordinate (y-axis). Every point on the circle represents a particular plane passing through the point, and the circle completely defines the stress state.

For a 2D stress element subjected to major and minor principal stresses $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$:

$$
\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2} + \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\cos(2\theta)
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\sin(2\theta)
$$

where $\theta$ is the angle of the plane measured from the major principal plane.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Stress Compass"

Think of a stress element as a tiny cube buried in the ground. On different faces of this cube, different combinations of normal and shear stresses act. Mohr's circle is like a **compass** that lets you read off the stresses on *any* inclined plane through a point, just by rotating a radius on the circle. The whole stress state is captured in one picture.

## 1.3 Origin of Planes

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Origin of Planes** refers to the physical concept that the planes of failure (or any other plane) in a soil element originate from the point under consideration. The orientation of a plane through a point in 2D can be uniquely defined by its **inclination (θ)** with respect to the major principal plane.

The **pole method** or **origin of planes technique** allows us to locate the stress point on Mohr's circle representing any plane without trigonometric calculations. The origin O of the σ-τ coordinate axes, when joined to a point P on Mohr's circle, defines a line OP. The plane corresponding to P is **parallel to the line OP**.

## 1.4 Mohr's Envelope (Failure Envelope)

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Mohr's Envelope** (also called the **Failure Envelope**) is the locus of all stress states at which failure (rupture) occurs in a soil. It is the envelope tangent to a series of Mohr's circles representing failure stress states obtained from multiple tests on identical specimens under different confining pressures.

For most soils, the Mohr's envelope is **curved**, but in practical engineering, a **linear approximation** is used within the working stress range:

$$
\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi
$$

where $c$ is **cohesion** (kPa) and $\phi$ is the **angle of internal friction** (degrees).

## 1.5 Mohr-Coulomb Strength Theory

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Mohr-Coulomb Strength Theory** combines Coulomb's frictional law (1773) with Mohr's stress circle concept. It states that **failure occurs when the maximum shear stress on any plane reaches a value equal to the shear strength of the soil on that plane**, governed by the normal stress on that plane. The theory postulates a linear relationship between shear strength and normal stress.

The Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion in its general form:

$$
\tau_f = c + \sigma'_n \tan\phi'
$$

where $\sigma'_n$ is the **effective normal stress** and $\phi'$ is the **effective angle of internal friction**.

### Why "Mohr-Coulomb"?

- **Coulomb** (1773) proposed the linear shear strength equation based on laboratory observations.
- **Mohr** (1900) generalized it by showing that the failure envelope need not be linear but is the tangent to Mohr's circles at failure.
- The combination gives us a practical, engineering-friendly strength criterion used worldwide.

## 1.6 Direct Shear Test

> [!DEFINITION]
> The **Direct Shear Test** is the oldest and simplest laboratory test to determine the shear strength parameters of soil. A soil specimen is placed in a **two-part split-box** (shear box), a normal load is applied vertically, and a horizontal force is gradually applied to cause shear failure along a predetermined horizontal plane.

**Standard Apparatus:** Casagrande-type shear box (60 mm × 60 mm square, with porous stones for drainage).

**Advantages:** Simple, quick, inexpensive; suitable for sandy soils and gravels.
**Disadvantages:** Forced failure plane; non-uniform stress distribution; drainage control is approximate; stress concentration at the shear plane edges.

## 1.7 Triaxial Shear Test — Introduction

> [!DEFINITION]
> The **Triaxial Shear Test** is the most versatile and reliable laboratory test for determining shear strength parameters. A cylindrical soil specimen is enclosed in a thin rubber membrane and placed inside a **triaxial cell** filled with water (or glycerine). An **all-around confining pressure** ($\sigma_3$) is applied through the cell fluid, and an **axial load** is applied through a loading ram to increase the major principal stress $\sigma_1$ until failure occurs.

**Key Features:**
- Stress distribution is **uniform** throughout the specimen.
- Failure plane is **not forced** — it develops naturally.
- **Pore water pressure** can be measured accurately.
- Three types of tests possible based on drainage: **UU, CU, and CD**.

## 1.8 Measurement of Pore Pressure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pore Water Pressure (u)** is the pressure exerted by water filling the voids between soil particles. In triaxial testing, pore pressure is measured using a **pore pressure transducer** or a **Bishop's pore pressure apparatus** connected to the base of the specimen through a saturated porous disc.

The **Skempton’s pore pressure coefficient B** quantifies pore pressure response under undrained loading:

$$
\Delta u = B \cdot \Delta \sigma_3 + A \cdot B \cdot (\Delta \sigma_1 - \Delta \sigma_3)
$$

For **saturated soils**, $B \approx 1.0$; for **dry soils**, $B = 0$.

## 1.9 Total and Effective Stress Strength Parameters

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Effective Stress Principle (Terzaghi, 1925):** The shearing resistance of soil is governed by the **effective stress** ($\sigma' = \sigma - u$), not the total stress ($\sigma$). This is the most fundamental principle in soil mechanics.

| Parameter | Symbol | Description | Used For |
|-----------|--------|-------------|----------|
| Total cohesion | $c$ | Cohesion in terms of total stress | Undrained analysis (short-term) |
| Effective cohesion | $c'$ | Cohesion in terms of effective stress | Drained analysis (long-term) |
| Total friction angle | $\phi$ | Friction angle in terms of total stress | $\phi = 0$ analysis for clays |
| Effective friction angle | $\phi'$ | Friction angle in terms of effective stress | All long-term stability problems |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Students often confuse $c$ and $c'$, $\phi$ and $\phi'$. The "primed" symbols **always** refer to **effective stress** parameters. Use $c, \phi$ for **total stress** (UU test); use $c', \phi'$ for **effective stress** (CU or CD tests).

## 1.10 UCC Test (Unconfined Compression Test)

> [!DEFINITION]
> The **Unconfined Compression Test (UCC)** is a special, simplified version of the triaxial test where the **confining pressure $\sigma_3 = 0$**. The cylindrical soil specimen is loaded axially between two platens until failure. The test is applicable only to **cohesive soils** (clays) that can stand unsupported.

The unconfined compressive strength is:

$$
q_u = 2 c_u
$$

where $c_u$ is the **undrained shear strength** of the clay.

## 1.11 Vane Shear Test

> [!DEFINITION]
> The **Vane Shear Test** is an in-situ test (and sometimes laboratory test) used to determine the **undrained shear strength ($c_u$)** of soft, sensitive clays. A four-bladed vane is pushed into the soil and rotated; the **torque** required to rotate the vane is measured and related to $c_u$.

For a **rectangular vane** of height $H$ and diameter $D$:

$$
T = c_u \cdot \pi D H \left(\frac{D}{3} + \frac{H}{2}\right)
$$

The **sensitivity ($S_t$)** of clay is determined by performing the test on remolded soil:

$$
S_t = \frac{c_u \text{ (undisturbed)}}{c_u \text{ (remolded)}}
$$

## 1.12 Choice of Test Conditions for Field Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Selecting the right test for a field problem is critical for accurate design. The choice depends on:
> 1. **Soil type** (sand, clay, silt, gravel).
> 2. **Loading rate** relative to drainage.
> 3. **Field drainage conditions** (long-term or short-term).
> 4. **Stress history** (normally consolidated or overconsolidated).
> 5. **Project type** (foundation, slope, retaining wall, pavement).

| Field Situation | Test Recommended | Parameters |
|-----------------|------------------|------------|
| Immediate end-of-construction, clay | UU Test | $c_u$ (undrained) |
| Long-term stability, clay | CU Test (with pore pressure) | $c', \phi'$ |
| Long-term stability, sand | CD Test | $c', \phi'$ |
| Soft sensitive clay, in-situ | Vane Shear | $c_u$ |
| Soft clay, quick laboratory check | UCC Test | $c_u = q_u/2$ |
| Granular soil, free-draining | Direct Shear or CD Triaxial | $c', \phi'$ |
| Earth dam during construction | CU Test | $c', \phi'$ |
| Earth dam after steady seepage | CD Test | $c', \phi'$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Why Soil Fails in Shear — The Failure Mechanism

Soil is a **frictional and cohesive material** consisting of:
- **Solid particles** (the mineral grains).
- **Water** in the voids (pore water).
- **Air** in the voids (in partially saturated soils).

The shearing resistance arises from three fundamental sources:

1. **Frictional resistance** between soil grains (depends on effective normal stress and surface roughness).
2. **Cohesion** — electrochemical bonding between clay particles (independent of normal stress).
3. **Interlocking** — mechanical resistance due to particle arrangement (more in dense sands and overconsolidated clays).

When the applied shear stress on any plane exceeds the available shear strength, the soil yields, and sliding occurs on the **failure surface**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept:** Failure is *not* synonymous with breaking. In soils, failure means a state where **large deformations occur without further increase in stress** — this is called the **plastic state** or **yield state**.

## 2.2 Mohr Circle Construction — Step-by-Step Logic

The Mohr circle approach is built on the following logic:

- **Step 1:** Identify the principal stresses $\sigma_1$ (major) and $\sigma_3$ (minor) acting on an element.
- **Step 2:** Compute the **center** of Mohr's circle on the σ-axis: 
$$C = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2}$$
- **Step 3:** Compute the **radius** of Mohr's circle: 
$$R = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$
- **Step 4:** Plot a circle with center $C$ and radius $R$ in the $\sigma$-$\tau$ plane.
- **Step 5:** For any plane inclined at angle $\theta$ with the major principal plane, rotate $2\theta$ on the circle (in the *same* sense for compression) to find the stress point.

The **pole (origin of planes)** is a unique point on Mohr's circle such that a line drawn from this pole to any point on the circle gives a line **parallel to the plane** represented by that point. For the pole, the rotation from a known plane is $1:1$ in physical and Mohr space.

## 2.3 Failure Theories Compared

Several theories have been proposed to predict soil failure. KTU students should know the most important ones:

| Theory | Failure Criterion | Application |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Tresca** | $\tau_{max} = \text{const}$ | Metals, saturated clay (UU) |
| **Von Mises** | $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_3) = \text{const}$ | Ductile materials |
| **Mohr-Coulomb** | $\tau_f = c + \sigma'_n \tan\phi'$ | Soils (most general) |
| **Maximum Stress Ratio** | $\sigma_1 / \sigma_3 = \text{const}$ | Sand (approx.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Mohr-Coulomb is preferred for soils:**
> - It accounts for the **frictional** nature of soil (unlike Tresca).
> - It allows for **cohesion** in clays.
> - It works for both **drained and undrained** conditions.
> - It is **empirically validated** by decades of laboratory and field data.

## 2.4 Direct Shear Test — Detailed Analysis

### Working Principle
The soil specimen is confined in a **shear box** consisting of upper and lower halves. A **normal force N** is applied through a loading cap (creating normal stress $\sigma = N/A$, where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the specimen). A **horizontal shear force F** is applied gradually to the lower half of the box, while the upper half is held by a proving ring.

### Failure
Failure occurs along the **horizontal plane** between the upper and lower halves of the box. The shear stress at failure is:

$$
\tau = \frac{F}{A}
$$

### Test Procedure
1. **Sample preparation:** Remolded or undisturbed sample is placed in the shear box.
2. **Normal load application:** A normal stress is applied and allowed to consolidate (if needed).
3. **Shearing:** A horizontal force is applied at a **constant rate of strain** (usually 0.5 to 1.25 mm/min for clays, faster for sands).
4. **Measurements:** Shear force, horizontal displacement, and vertical displacement (dilation/contraction) are recorded.
5. **Repetition:** The test is repeated on 3 to 4 identical specimens under different normal stresses.
6. **Plotting:** Shear stress vs. horizontal displacement and vertical displacement vs. horizontal displacement curves are drawn. Peak values give $\tau_f$ for each normal stress.
7. **Failure envelope:** Plot $\tau_f$ vs. $\sigma$ — the slope gives $\tan\phi$ and the intercept gives $c$.

## 2.5 Triaxial Shear Test — Detailed Working

### Apparatus Components
- **Triaxial cell:** A cylindrical pressure chamber with a loading ram at the top.
- **Loading frame:** For applying the axial load.
- **Pressure control system:** For confining pressure and back pressure.
- **Pore pressure measuring device:** Transducer or null indicator.
- **Volume change measuring device:** For drained tests.
- **Rubber membrane:** Encloses the specimen to isolate it from the cell fluid.

### Three Types of Triaxial Tests

| Test Type | Drainage during Compression | Drainage during Shear | Pore Pressure Measured? | Parameters |
|-----------|----------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|------------|
| **UU** (Unconsolidated Undrained) | No | No | No (assumed zero) | $c_u, \phi_u = 0$ |
| **CU** (Consolidated Undrained) | Yes | No | Yes | $c', \phi'$ (from effective stresses) |
| **CD** (Consolidated Drained) | Yes | Yes | Yes (but zero excess) | $c', \phi'$ |

### Stage-by-Stage Operation
1. **Saturation:** Apply back pressure to dissolve all air in the void space ($B \geq 0.95$).
2. **Consolidation:** Apply confining pressure $\sigma_3$ and allow drainage until primary consolidation is complete.
3. **Shearing:** Apply axial load (increasing $\sigma_1$) at a controlled rate until failure.

## 2.6 Skempton’s Pore Pressure Coefficients — Detailed Logic

In triaxial testing, when a saturated soil specimen is loaded, the applied stress is partially carried by the **pore water** (as excess pore pressure) and partially by the **soil skeleton** (as effective stress). The total stress increment is:

$$
\Delta\sigma_1 = \Delta\sigma_3 + \Delta\sigma_d
$$

where $\Delta\sigma_d = \Delta\sigma_1 - \Delta\sigma_3$ is the **deviator stress**.

The **excess pore pressure** generated is:

$$
\Delta u = B \cdot \Delta\sigma_3 + A \cdot B \cdot \Delta\sigma_d
$$

where:
- $B$ = pore pressure coefficient for confining pressure.
- $A$ = pore pressure coefficient for deviator stress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical $A$ values at failure:**
> - Sensitive clays: $A > 1$
> - Normally consolidated clays: $A = 0.5$ to $1.0$
> - Overconsolidated clays: $A = -0.5$ to $0$
> - Dense sands: $A = -0.3$ to $0$
> - Loose sands: $A = 0$ to $0.5$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\tau_f = c + \sigma'_n \tan\phi'$ | Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion | kPa |
| 2 | $C = (\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)/2$ | Center of Mohr's circle | kPa |
| 3 | $R = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)/2$ | Radius of Mohr's circle | kPa |
| 4 | $\sigma_n = C + R \cos 2\theta$ | Normal stress on plane at angle $\theta$ | kPa |
| 5 | $\tau = R \sin 2\theta$ | Shear stress on plane at angle $\theta$ | kPa |
| 6 | $\theta_f = 45° + \phi'/2$ | Failure plane angle (from major principal plane) | degrees |
| 7 | $\sigma_1' = \sigma_3' \tan^2(45° + \phi'/2) + 2c' \tan(45° + \phi'/2)$ | Effective stress at failure | kPa |
| 8 | $q_u = 2 c_u$ | UCC test relationship | kPa |
| 9 | $c_u = \pi D^2 H / 2 [T / \pi D H (D/3 + H/2)]$ | Vane shear derivation | kPa |
| 10 | $T = c_u \cdot \pi D H (D/3 + H/2)$ | Vane shear torque | N·m |
| 11 | $S_t = c_{u, \text{undisturbed}} / c_{u, \text{remolded}}$ | Sensitivity of clay | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\Delta u = B[\Delta\sigma_3 + A(\Delta\sigma_1 - \Delta\sigma_3)]$ | Skempton's pore pressure equation | kPa |
| 13 | $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ | Effective stress (Terzaghi) | kPa |
| 14 | $\phi'_m = \sin^{-1}[(\sigma_1' - \sigma_3')/(\sigma_1' + \sigma_3')]$ | Effective friction angle from CU test | degrees |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications:**
> - **Formula 1** — Used in every geotechnical design (foundations, slopes, retaining walls).
> - **Formula 6** — Determines the geometry of slip surfaces.
> - **Formula 7** — Used in bearing capacity equations.
> - **Formula 8** — Standard UCC test interpretation.
> - **Formula 10** — Used for in-situ vane shear in soft clays.

## 2.8 The Physics of Shear Strength in Different Soils

### Cohesionless Soils (Sands, Gravels)
- Shear strength arises **entirely from inter-particle friction** and **interlocking**.
- $c = 0$ for clean sands (or negligible).
- $\phi$ depends on **relative density**, **grain shape**, and **gradation**.
- Typical $\phi$ values: Loose sand = 28°–32°; Dense sand = 35°–45°.

### Cohesive Soils (Clays)
- Shear strength arises from **electrochemical bonds** and **friction**.
- $c > 0$ (apparent cohesion due to electrochemical forces).
- $\phi$ depends on **mineralogy**, **stress history**, and **pore water chemistry**.
- For **normally consolidated clays**, $c' \approx 0$, and $\phi'$ governs strength.
- For **overconsolidated clays**, $c' > 0$ and $\phi' < \phi'_{NC}$.

### Intermediate Soils (Silts)
- Behavior depends on **gradation** and **plasticity**.
- Silts often exhibit **partial drainage** behavior and are susceptible to **liquefaction** under cyclic loading.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Stresses on an Inclined Plane (Mohr's Circle Logic)

### Problem Setup
Consider a two-dimensional soil element under principal stresses $\sigma_1$ (vertical, compressive) and $\sigma_3$ (horizontal, compressive). We want to find the normal and shear stresses on a plane inclined at angle $\theta$ with the horizontal (or $\theta$ with the minor principal plane).

### Step 1: Geometry of the Wedge
Cut a wedge from the element with one face at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. The wedge has:
- One face of area $dA$ (the inclined plane).
- A horizontal face of area $dA \cos\theta$.
- A vertical face of area $dA \sin\theta$.

### Step 2: Force Equilibrium — Horizontal Direction
Sum of forces in the horizontal (x) direction must equal zero:

$$
\sigma_3 \cdot dA \sin\theta = \sigma_n \cdot dA \sin\theta \cdot \sin\theta - \tau \cdot dA \cos\theta \cdot \cos\theta
$$

Wait, let me correct this. Let me carefully apply equilibrium on a wedge.

### Step 1 (Revised): Geometry
- Inclined face: Area = $dA$, with outward normal at angle $\theta$ to horizontal.
- The angle the inclined face makes with the horizontal = $\theta$.
- Horizontal projection area = $dA \cdot \cos\theta$ (the bottom face area).
- Vertical projection area = $dA \cdot \sin\theta$ (the side face area).

### Step 2 (Revised): Force Equilibrium — Vertical Direction
The vertical equilibrium gives:

$$
\sigma_1 \cdot (dA \cos\theta) = \sigma_n \cdot dA \cdot \cos\theta + \tau \cdot dA \cdot \sin\theta
$$

Dividing through by $dA$:

$$
\sigma_1 \cos\theta = \sigma_n \cos\theta + \tau \sin\theta
$$

### Step 3: Force Equilibrium — Horizontal Direction

$$
\sigma_3 \cdot (dA \sin\theta) = \sigma_n \cdot dA \cdot \sin\theta - \tau \cdot dA \cdot \cos\theta
$$

Wait, I need to be careful with the sign convention. Let me redo this more carefully.

### Proper Sign Convention Setup
- $\sigma_1$ acts on the **top horizontal face** (compressive, pushing down).
- $\sigma_3$ acts on the **vertical face** (compressive, pushing in from the side).
- On the inclined face (with outward normal making angle $\theta$ with horizontal), the stresses are $\sigma_n$ (normal) and $\tau$ (shear, positive when acting upward along the inclined face).

### Resolving Forces on the Inclined Face
- Normal direction force: $\sigma_n \cdot dA$
- Shear direction force: $\tau \cdot dA$

For equilibrium in the vertical direction:

$$
\sigma_1 \cdot (dA \cos\theta) = (\sigma_n \cos\theta + \tau \sin\theta) \cdot dA
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\sigma_1 \cos\theta = \sigma_n \cos\theta + \tau \sin\theta
$$

For equilibrium in the horizontal direction:

$$
\sigma_3 \cdot (dA \sin\theta) = (\sigma_n \sin\theta - \tau \cos\theta) \cdot dA
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\sigma_3 \sin\theta = \sigma_n \sin\theta - \tau \cos\theta
$$

### Step 4: Solve the System of Equations
We have two equations:

$$
\sigma_n \cos\theta + \tau \sin\theta = \sigma_1 \cos\theta
$$

$$
\sigma_n \sin\theta - \tau \cos\theta = \sigma_3 \sin\theta
$$

Multiply equation (1) by $\cos\theta$ and equation (2) by $\sin\theta$:

$$
\sigma_n \cos^2\theta + \tau \sin\theta \cos\theta = \sigma_1 \cos^2\theta
$$

$$
\sigma_n \sin^2\theta - \tau \sin\theta \cos\theta = \sigma_3 \sin^2\theta
$$

Add the two equations:

$$
\sigma_n (\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) = \sigma_1 \cos^2\theta + \sigma_3 \sin^2\theta
$$

Since $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$:

$$
\sigma_n = \sigma_1 \cos^2\theta + \sigma_3 \sin^2\theta
$$

Using the identity $\cos^2\theta = (1 + \cos 2\theta)/2$ and $\sin^2\theta = (1 - \cos 2\theta)/2$:

$$
\sigma_n = \sigma_1 \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2} + \sigma_3 \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2} + \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} \cos 2\theta
$$

### Step 5: Derivation of Shear Stress
Multiply equation (1) by $-\sin\theta$ and equation (2) by $\cos\theta$:

$$
-\sigma_n \cos\theta \sin\theta - \tau \sin^2\theta = -\sigma_1 \cos\theta \sin\theta
$$

$$
\sigma_n \sin\theta \cos\theta - \tau \cos^2\theta = \sigma_3 \sin\theta \cos\theta
$$

Add the two equations:

$$
-\tau (\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) = -\sigma_1 \cos\theta \sin\theta + \sigma_3 \sin\theta \cos\theta
$$

$$
-\tau = (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1) \sin\theta \cos\theta
$$

$$
\tau = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3) \sin\theta \cos\theta
$$

Using the identity $\sin\theta \cos\theta = \sin 2\theta / 2$:

$$
\tau = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} \sin 2\theta
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — Mohr's Circle Equations:**
> $$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2} + \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} \cos 2\theta$$
> $$\tau = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} \sin 2\theta$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Failure Plane Angle (θ_f)

### Starting Point
Failure occurs when the Mohr circle touches the Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope. The angle between the failure plane and the major principal plane is the **failure plane angle**, denoted $\theta_f$.

### Geometric Construction
In Mohr's circle:
- The center of the circle is at $C = (\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)/2$.
- The radius is $R = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)/2$.
- The failure envelope makes an angle $\phi'$ with the $\sigma$-axis.
- At the point of tangency, the radius is **perpendicular to the envelope** (since the envelope is tangent to the circle).

### Step-by-Step
At the point of tangency, draw a line from the center C to the tangent point P. The angle this line CP makes with the $\sigma$-axis is $90° - \phi'$ (since CP is perpendicular to the envelope).

Now, the diameter of the circle passing through P and parallel to the $\sigma$-axis represents the **major principal plane** at the point of tangency. The angle from the major principal plane to the failure plane (which is parallel to the tangent at P) is half the central angle:

$$
\theta_f = 45° + \frac{\phi'}{2}
$$

This is a **fundamental result** in soil mechanics: the failure plane is inclined at $45° + \phi'/2$ with the major principal plane (or $45° - \phi'/2$ with the horizontal in most common cases).

## 3.3 Derivation of Vane Shear Equation

### Setup
A vane with **four blades** of height $H$ and diameter $D$ is embedded in the soil. When rotated, shear stress is mobilized:
- On the **two cylindrical surfaces** of diameter $D$ and height $H$.
- On the **two end surfaces** (top and bottom) — each an annulus of diameter $D$.

### Step 1: Shear on the Cylindrical Surface
The lateral surface area is:

$$
A_{\text{lateral}} = \pi D H
$$

The force on the lateral surface (per unit area shear stress $c_u$):

$$
F_{\text{lateral}} = c_u \cdot \pi D H
$$

The moment arm for this force is $D/2$ (the radius). Torque from lateral surfaces:

$$
T_{\text{lateral}} = F_{\text{lateral}} \cdot \frac{D}{2} = c_u \cdot \pi D H \cdot \frac{D}{2}
$$

### Step 2: Shear on the End Surfaces
Each end surface is an annulus of outer radius $D/2$ and inner radius $0$. Consider an elemental ring of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$. Its area is $2\pi r \, dr$. The force on this ring is $c_u \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$, and the moment arm is $r$.

Torque from one end:

$$
T_{\text{end}} = \int_0^{D/2} c_u \cdot 2\pi r \cdot r \, dr = c_u \cdot 2\pi \int_0^{D/2} r^2 \, dr
$$

$$
T_{\text{end}} = c_u \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{(D/2)^3}{3} = c_u \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{D^3}{24} = \frac{c_u \pi D^3}{12}
$$

For **two end surfaces**:

$$
T_{\text{ends}} = 2 \cdot \frac{c_u \pi D^3}{12} = \frac{c_u \pi D^3}{6}
$$

### Step 3: Total Torque

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{lateral}} + T_{\text{ends}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = \frac{c_u \pi D^2 H}{2} + \frac{c_u \pi D^3}{6}
$$

Factor out $c_u \pi D^2 / 2$:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = c_u \pi D^2 \left( \frac{H}{2} + \frac{D}{6} \right)
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = c_u \pi D H \left( \frac{D}{3} + \frac{H}{2} \right)
$$

### Step 4: Solve for c_u

$$
c_u = \frac{T}{\pi D H \left( \frac{D}{3} + \frac{H}{2} \right)}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Skempton's Pore Pressure Coefficient A

### Setup
A saturated soil specimen is loaded under triaxial conditions. The total stress increment in the axial direction is $\Delta\sigma_1$, and the confining stress increment is $\Delta\sigma_3$.

The excess pore pressure $\Delta u$ can be decomposed into two parts:
1. **Part 1** — due to increase in confining pressure $\Delta\sigma_3$.
2. **Part 2** — due to deviator stress $(\Delta\sigma_1 - \Delta\sigma_3)$.

### Step 1: Confining Pressure Component
For an isotropic compression of a saturated soil:

$$
\Delta u_1 = B \cdot \Delta\sigma_3
$$

where $B$ is the Skempton B-coefficient.

### Step 2: Deviator Stress Component
The deviator stress induces shear distortion in the soil skeleton, generating additional pore pressure:

$$
\Delta u_2 = A \cdot B \cdot (\Delta\sigma_1 - \Delta\sigma_3)
$$

where $A$ is the Skempton A-coefficient (depends on soil type and stress level).

### Step 3: Total Excess Pore Pressure

$$
\Delta u = \Delta u_1 + \Delta u_2 = B \cdot \Delta\sigma_3 + A \cdot B \cdot (\Delta\sigma_1 - \Delta\sigma_3)
$$

Factoring out $B$:

$$
\Delta u = B \left[ \Delta\sigma_3 + A(\Delta\sigma_1 - \Delta\sigma_3) \right]
$$

### Step 4: Determining B
For **fully saturated soil**, $B = 1.0$. For **dry soil**, $B = 0$. For **partially saturated soil**, $B$ is between 0 and 1.

For verification in the lab, $B$ is measured by applying an increment of cell pressure $\Delta\sigma_3$ with drainage valves closed and measuring the resulting $\Delta u$:

$$
B = \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta\sigma_3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A $B$ value of 0.95 or higher is considered adequate saturation for most triaxial tests.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

### Problem
A saturated clay specimen was tested in a CU triaxial test. The following data are obtained at failure:
- Cell pressure: $\sigma_3 = 100 \text{ kPa}$
- Deviator stress at failure: $\Delta\sigma_d = 250 \text{ kPa}$
- Pore water pressure at failure: $u_f = 80 \text{ kPa}$

**Compute:**
1. The effective major principal stress at failure.
2. The effective minor principal stress at failure.
3. The effective friction angle $\phi'$.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute Total Stresses**

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 + \Delta\sigma_d = 100 + 250 = 350 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_3 = 100 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Effective Stresses**

$$
\sigma_3' = \sigma_3 - u_f = 100 - 80 = 20 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_1' = \sigma_1 - u_f = 350 - 80 = 270 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Effective Friction Angle**

$$
\phi' = \sin^{-1}\left( \frac{\sigma_1' - \sigma_3'}{\sigma_1' + \sigma_3'} \right) = \sin^{-1}\left( \frac{270 - 20}{270 + 20} \right)
$$

$$
\phi' = \sin^{-1}\left( \frac{250}{290} \right) = \sin^{-1}(0.8621)
$$

$$
\phi' = 59.5° \quad (\text{very high — likely a dense sand or stiff OC clay})
$$

**Step 4: Compute Effective Cohesion (assuming c' = 0 for NC clay)**

For a normally consolidated clay, $c' \approx 0$. The Mohr-Coulomb equation at failure is:

$$
\tau_f = \sigma'_n \tan\phi' = 270 \cdot \tan(59.5°) \approx 459.6 \text{ kPa}
$$

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mohr's Circle — Block Architecture Flow

This Mermaid diagram shows the **block-level functional architecture** of the Mohr circle construction process and its relationship with the failure envelope.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Soil Element with Principal Stresses sigma1 and sigma3] --> B[Compute Center C = sigma1+sigma3 over 2]
    A --> C[Compute Radius R = sigma1-sigma3 over 2]
    B --> D[Plot Mohrs Circle in sigma-tau Plane]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Does Circle Touch Failure Envelope?}
    E -->|No| F[Soil is Safe - Add Load and Repeat]
    E -->|Yes| G[Failure State Reached]
    G --> H[Identify Tangent Point P]
    H --> I[Compute Failure Plane Angle theta_f = 45 + phi over 2]
    H --> J[Read Shear Strength tau_f from Tangent Point]
    I --> K[Determine Orientation of Failure Plane in Soil]
    J --> L[Output c and phi Parameters]
    K --> L
    F --> D
```

## 4.2 Triaxial Test — Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Soil Specimen Preparation] --> B[Saturation Stage - Apply Back Pressure]
    B --> C{B-value Check}
    C -->|B less than 0.95| B
    C -->|B at least 0.95| D[Consolidation Stage - Apply sigma3]
    D --> E{Consolidation Complete?}
    E -->|No| D
    E -->|Yes| F[Shearing Stage - Apply Axial Load]
    F --> G{Test Type?}
    G -->|UU| H[No Drainage - Measure Deviator Stress only]
    G -->|CU| I[Drainage Closed - Measure Pore Pressure and Deviator Stress]
    G -->|CD| J[Drainage Open - Measure Volume Change and Deviator Stress]
    H --> K[Compute cu and phi_u from Total Stresses]
    I --> L[Compute c-prime and phi-prime from Effective Stresses]
    J --> L
    K --> M[Plot Mohr Circles and Failure Envelope]
    L --> M
```

## 4.3 Direct Shear Test — Apparatus Functional Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Soil Specimen] --> B[Shear Box - Split into Upper and Lower Halves]
    B --> C[Porous Stone Below - Allows Drainage]
    B --> D[Porous Stone Above - Allows Drainage]
    C --> E[Loading Platen]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Normal Load N - Applied Vertically]
    B --> G[Lower Half Pushed Horizontally]
    G --> H[Horizontal Shear Force F - Applied Gradually]
    H --> I[Proving Ring - Measures Shear Force]
    I --> J[Recording System - Plots tau vs Displacement]
    F --> K[Normal Stress sigma = N over A]
    H --> L[Shear Stress tau = F over A]
    K --> M[Failure Envelope - tau_f vs sigma]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Slope gives tan phi and Intercept gives c]
```

## 4.4 UCC Test — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cylindrical Clay Specimen] --> B[Place Between Two Platens]
    B --> C[Apply Axial Load at Constant Rate]
    C --> D[Measure Axial Displacement delta h]
    D --> E[Compute Axial Strain epsilon = delta h over h0]
    C --> F[Measure Axial Load P]
    F --> G[Compute Stress sigma = P over A_corrected]
    G --> H[Plot Stress-Strain Curve]
    H --> I{Identify Peak or Failure}
    I -->|Peak Identified| J[Read qu - Unconfined Compressive Strength]
    I -->|No Peak - Brittle| K[Read Stress at 20 percent Strain]
    J --> L[Compute cu = qu over 2]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Sensitivity Check - Repeat on Remolded Specimen]
    M --> N[St = cu_undisturbed over cu_remolded]
```

## 4.5 Vane Shear Test — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Four-Bladed Vane of Height H and Diameter D] --> B[Push Vane into Soft Clay to Test Depth]
    B --> C[Apply Rotation at Slow Rate]
    C --> D[Measure Torque T Required]
    D --> E[Compute Shear Strength cu = T over pi times D times H times D over 3 plus H over 2]
    E --> F{Test Type?}
    F -->|Undisturbed| G[Record cu_undisturbed]
    F -->|Remolded| H[Rotate Vane Many Times to Remold Clay]
    H --> I[Measure Torque T_remolded]
    I --> J[Record cu_remolded]
    G --> K[Compute Sensitivity St = cu_undisturbed over cu_remolded]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Classify Clay as Low Medium High or Extra Sensitive]
```

## 4.6 Pore Pressure Measurement in Triaxial — System Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Soil Specimen in Triaxial Cell] --> B[Rubber Membrane Sealing Specimen]
    B --> C[Base Pedestal with Saturated Porous Disc]
    C --> D[Pore Pressure Line]
    D --> E[Pore Pressure Transducer]
    D --> F[Bishop Pore Pressure Apparatus Null Type]
    E --> G[Digital Readout of Pore Pressure]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Data Acquisition System]
    H --> I[Compute Effective Stresses sigma-prime = sigma minus u]
    I --> J[Plot Effective Stress Mohr Circles]
    J --> K[Determine Effective Strength Parameters c-prime and phi-prime]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the following terms:
(a) Shear strength of soil
(b) Mohr's circle of stress
(c) Effective stress

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Shear strength of soil:** It is the maximum internal resistance per unit area that the soil mass can offer against failure or sliding along any plane. Mathematically, it is the maximum value of shear stress $\tau$ that the soil can sustain. For a Mohr-Coulomb material, $\tau_f = c' + \sigma'_n \tan\phi'$.

**(b) Mohr's circle of stress:** It is a two-dimensional graphical representation of the state of stress at a point, where normal stress $\sigma$ is plotted on the abscissa and shear stress $\tau$ on the ordinate. For principal stresses $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$, the circle has center $C = (\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)/2$ and radius $R = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)/2$. Each point on the circle represents the stress state on a particular plane through the point.

**(c) Effective stress:** Proposed by Karl Terzaghi (1925), effective stress is the stress carried by the soil skeleton (i.e., the inter-particle contacts). It is computed as $\sigma' = \sigma - u$, where $\sigma$ is the total stress and $u$ is the pore water pressure. Effective stress governs the shearing resistance, compression, and volume change behavior of soils.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion? Write its mathematical expression and explain each term.

**Model Answer:**

The **Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion** is the most widely used strength criterion in soil mechanics. It states that failure occurs on a plane when the shear stress on that plane reaches a value equal to the shear strength of the soil, which is a linear function of the normal effective stress on that plane.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$
\tau_f = c' + \sigma'_n \tan\phi'
$$

**Explanation of terms:**
- $\tau_f$ = shear strength of soil on the failure plane (in kPa).
- $c'$ = effective cohesion of soil (in kPa) — represents the inherent inter-particle bonding (electrochemical forces) that is independent of normal stress.
- $\sigma'_n$ = effective normal stress on the failure plane (in kPa) — computed as $\sigma_n - u$.
- $\phi'$ = effective angle of internal friction (in degrees) — represents the frictional resistance between soil grains.
- $\tan\phi'$ = coefficient of internal friction.

For **cohesionless soils** (sands): $c' \approx 0$.
For **saturated normally consolidated clays** under long-term loading: $c' \approx 0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** 

(a) **(7 Marks)** Explain the **Mohr-Coulomb strength theory** in detail. With the help of a neat sketch, describe the procedure to determine the **shear strength parameters** ($c'$ and $\phi'$) from a **triaxial test** result.

(b) **(7 Marks)** A series of **direct shear tests** were conducted on a sandy soil. The results are:

| Test No. | Normal Stress (kPa) | Shear Stress at Failure (kPa) |
|----------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | 50 | 32 |
| 2 | 100 | 64 |
| 3 | 150 | 96 |
| 4 | 200 | 128 |

Determine the **shear strength parameters** $c$ and $\phi$ for the soil. Also, find the **shear strength** at a normal stress of 175 kPa.

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) Model Answer:

**Mohr-Coulomb Strength Theory:**

The Mohr-Coulomb strength theory combines Coulomb's empirical law with Mohr's graphical representation. According to this theory:

1. The **failure of soil** is governed by the shear stress on the critical plane.
2. The **shear strength** on a plane is a function of the **normal stress** on that plane.
3. The **failure envelope** is approximated as a straight line in the working stress range.
4. The **Mohr's circle** representing the stress state at failure is **tangent** to the failure envelope.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$
\tau_f = c' + \sigma'_n \tan\phi'
$$

**Procedure to determine c' and φ' from triaxial test:**

1. Conduct **at least three triaxial tests** on identical specimens at three different confining pressures ($\sigma_3$).
2. Plot the **Mohr's circle** for each test using total or effective stresses as the failure criterion requires.
3. Draw the **failure envelope** as a **best-fit straight line** tangent to all three circles.
4. The **y-intercept** of the failure envelope gives $c'$.
5. The **angle** made by the failure envelope with the horizontal ($\sigma$-axis) gives $\phi'$.

**Diagram (describe verbally):** A $\sigma$-$\tau$ plot with the x-axis representing normal stress and the y-axis representing shear stress. Three Mohr's circles of different sizes (representing three confining pressures) are drawn. A common tangent line is drawn touching all three circles, with intercept $c'$ on the y-axis and angle $\phi'$ with the horizontal.

### Part (b) Model Solution:

**Given Data:**

| Test No. | $\sigma_n$ (kPa) | $\tau_f$ (kPa) |
|----------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | 50 | 32 |
| 2 | 100 | 64 |
| 3 | 150 | 96 |
| 4 | 200 | 128 |

**Step 1: Observe the pattern**

The shear stress at failure increases **linearly** with normal stress. Compute the slope:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{64 - 32}{100 - 50} = \frac{32}{50} = 0.64
$$

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.64) = 32.62°
$$

**Verify with other data points:**

From test 1 to test 3:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{96 - 32}{150 - 50} = \frac{64}{100} = 0.64 \quad \checkmark
$$

From test 1 to test 4:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{128 - 32}{200 - 50} = \frac{96}{150} = 0.64 \quad \checkmark
$$

The slope is consistent — confirming the linear Mohr-Coulomb relationship.

**Step 2: Find cohesion c**

Using test 1 data:

$$
\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan\phi
$$

$$
32 = c + 50 \cdot 0.64
$$

$$
32 = c + 32
$$

$$
c = 0 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Verification with test 2:**

$$
\tau_f = 0 + 100 \cdot 0.64 = 64 \text{ kPa} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Verification with test 3:**

$$
\tau_f = 0 + 150 \cdot 0.64 = 96 \text{ kPa} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Verification with test 4:**

$$
\tau_f = 0 + 200 \cdot 0.64 = 128 \text{ kPa} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3: Compute shear strength at $\sigma_n = 175$ kPa**

$$
\tau_f = 0 + 175 \cdot 0.64 = 112 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\phi = 32.62°, \quad c = 0 \text{ kPa}, \quad \tau_{f, 175} = 112 \text{ kPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The cohesion $c = 0$ is consistent with a **clean sand** (cohesionless soil). The angle of internal friction $\phi \approx 32.6°$ indicates a **medium-dense sand**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

(a) **(7 Marks)** Describe the **Unconfined Compression Test (UCC)** in detail. List its **advantages and limitations**. Derive the relationship between **unconfined compressive strength ($q_u$)** and **undrained shear strength ($c_u$)**.

(b) **(7 Marks)** A **vane shear test** was conducted in a soft clay deposit using a vane of diameter 60 mm and height 120 mm. The torque required to shear the soil was 0.04 N·m for the **undisturbed** sample and 0.012 N·m for the **remolded** sample. Determine the **undrained shear strength** of the clay in both cases and the **sensitivity** of the clay. Classify the clay based on sensitivity.

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) Model Answer:

**Unconfined Compression Test (UCC) — Detailed Description:**

The UCC test is a special case of the triaxial test where the **confining pressure $\sigma_3 = 0$**. A cylindrical clay specimen (typically 38 mm diameter, 76 mm height) is placed between two loading platens and loaded axially until failure.

**Apparatus:**
- Loading frame with proving ring or load cell.
- Specimen setup with platens.
- Dial gauges for deformation measurement.
- Sample extruder and trimming tools.

**Procedure:**
1. Prepare an **undisturbed cylindrical sample** (or remolded, as required).
2. Measure **initial dimensions** (diameter $D_0$, height $H_0$).
3. Place the sample between the platens.
4. Apply axial load at a rate of **0.5% to 2% strain per minute**.
5. Record load and deformation at regular intervals.
6. Continue until the sample **fails** (peak load) or until **20% strain** is reached for brittle samples.
7. Plot **stress-strain curve**.

**Derivation of q_u = 2 c_u:**

For a triaxial test at failure, the Mohr-Coulomb criterion (with $\phi_u = 0$ for saturated clay under undrained loading) gives:

$$
\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 2 c_u
$$

For UCC, $\sigma_3 = 0$, so:

$$
\sigma_1 = 2 c_u
$$

The maximum stress applied is the **unconfined compressive strength**:

$$
q_u = \sigma_1 = 2 c_u
$$

Therefore:

$$
c_u = \frac{q_u}{2}
$$

**Advantages of UCC Test:**
1. **Simple and quick** to perform.
2. **No need** for a triaxial cell or confining pressure system.
3. **Inexpensive** apparatus.
4. Useful for **routine site investigations**.
5. Can also give **sensitivity** by repeating on remolded sample.

**Limitations of UCC Test:**
1. Applicable **only to cohesive soils** (clays) that can stand unsupported.
2. **Confining pressure is zero** — does not represent actual field stress conditions.
3. Cannot measure **pore water pressure** at failure.
4. Sample may **fail along a predefined** failure plane (between platens) rather than a true shear plane.
5. **Strain rate effects** are not well controlled.
6. **Saturation cannot be guaranteed** during the test (no membrane or confining fluid).

### Part (b) Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- Vane diameter, $D = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}$
- Vane height, $H = 120 \text{ mm} = 0.120 \text{ m}$
- Torque (undisturbed), $T_u = 0.04 \text{ N·m}$ (Note: this is a small value — for a typical vane, $T$ would be in the order of 10s of N·m. We will use the given value.)
- Torque (remolded), $T_r = 0.012 \text{ N·m}$

**Step 1: Vane shear formula**

$$
T = c_u \cdot \pi D H \left( \frac{D}{3} + \frac{H}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 2: Compute the geometric factor**

$$
\frac{D}{3} = \frac{0.060}{3} = 0.020 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\frac{H}{2} = \frac{0.120}{2} = 0.060 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\frac{D}{3} + \frac{H}{2} = 0.020 + 0.060 = 0.080 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\pi D H = \pi \cdot 0.060 \cdot 0.120 = 0.02262 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\pi D H \left( \frac{D}{3} + \frac{H}{2} \right) = 0.02262 \cdot 0.080 = 0.001810 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Compute $c_u$ for undisturbed sample**

$$
c_{u,u} = \frac{T_u}{\pi D H \left( \frac{D}{3} + \frac{H}{2} \right)} = \frac{0.04}{0.001810} = 22.10 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $c_u$ for remolded sample**

$$
c_{u,r} = \frac{T_r}{\pi D H \left( \frac{D}{3} + \frac{H}{2} \right)} = \frac{0.012}{0.001810} = 6.63 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Sensitivity**

$$
S_t = \frac{c_{u,u}}{c_{u,r}} = \frac{22.10}{6.63} = 3.33
$$

**Step 6: Classify the Clay**

Based on the classification by Skempton and Northey (1952):

| Sensitivity, $S_t$ | Classification |
|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Insensitive |
| 1–2 | Low sensitivity |
| 2–4 | Medium sensitivity |
| 4–8 | High sensitivity |
| 8–16 | Very high sensitivity |
| >16 | Quick clays |

Since $S_t = 3.33$ (between 2 and 4), the clay is classified as **medium sensitive**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing total and effective stress parameters:** Always use $c', \phi'$ for long-term/drained analysis and $c, \phi$ for short-term/undrained analysis. Mixing them is a common error.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in UCC:** $c_u = q_u / 2$ is THE most-failed equation. Students often write $c_u = q_u$, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Sign error in Mohr circle angle:** The failure plane angle is $\theta_f = 45° + \phi'/2$ (with the major principal plane), NOT $45° - \phi'/2$. Read the question carefully — it may ask for the angle with the minor principal plane.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units:** Torque in N·mm should be converted to N·m; vane dimensions in mm should be converted to m. Unit inconsistency leads to $c_u$ being off by factors of 10, 100, or 1000.
> 5. **Skipping the interpretation step:** For numerical problems, always write a concluding sentence: "The cohesion is zero, so the soil is clean sand" or "The sensitivity is medium, so the clay has moderate structural bonding."
> 6. **Mixing up $\phi'$ and $\phi$ in formulas:** For effective stress analysis (CU and CD tests), $\phi'$ is used. For total stress analysis (UU test on saturated clay), $\phi = 0$ and $c = c_u$ is used.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Shear Strength**

### Core Concepts
- **Shear strength** is the maximum shear stress a soil can sustain before failure.
- The **Mohr-Coulomb criterion** is: $\tau_f = c' + \sigma'_n \tan\phi'$.
- **Failure plane angle** with major principal plane: $\theta_f = 45° + \phi'/2$.
- **Effective stress** (Terzaghi): $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ — governs all strength behavior.

### Mohr's Circle
- Center: $C = (\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)/2$.
- Radius: $R = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)/2$.
- Tangent circle to failure envelope = failure state.
- Pole (origin of planes) connects to a point on the circle parallel to the plane represented.

### Tests
- **Direct Shear:** Simple, forced failure plane, drained/un-drained both possible.
- **Triaxial:** Versatile, uniform stress, pore pressure measurable (CU, CD, UU).
- **UCC:** $\sigma_3 = 0$ case, $q_u = 2c_u$, applicable only to clays.
- **Vane Shear:** In-situ, soft clays, gives $c_u$ and sensitivity $S_t$.

### Pore Pressure
- Skempton's equation: $\Delta u = B[\Delta\sigma_3 + A(\Delta\sigma_1 - \Delta\sigma_3)]$.
- $B \approx 1.0$ for saturated soils.
- $A$ varies with soil type and stress history.

### Test Selection Rules
- **Short-term (end of construction), clay:** UU test, use $c_u, \phi = 0$.
- **Long-term, clay slope/foundation:** CU test with pore pressure, use $c', \phi'$.
- **Long-term, sand:** CD test or direct shear, use $c' = 0, \phi'$.
- **Soft sensitive clay, in-situ:** Vane shear test.
- **Quick check on clay:** UCC test.

### Common Numerical Values
- Loose sand: $\phi' = 28°-32°$.
- Dense sand: $\phi' = 35°-45°$.
- Normally consolidated clay: $\phi' = 20°-30°$, $c' \approx 0$.
- Overconsolidated clay: $\phi' = 25°-35°$, $c' > 0$.
- Soft clay: $c_u = 10$ to $50 \text{ kPa}$.
- Stiff clay: $c_u = 100$ to $500 \text{ kPa}$.

### Symbols — Do Not Confuse
- $c$ vs. $c'$, $\phi$ vs. $\phi'$.
- $q_u$ (unconfined compressive strength) vs. $c_u$ (undrained shear strength) — note the **subscript**.
- $\theta_f$ (failure plane angle, from major principal plane) vs. $\theta$ (general plane angle).
- $B$ (Skempton B-coefficient, for confining pressure) vs. $A$ (Skempton A-coefficient, for deviator stress).

### Equations to Memorize Cold
1. $\tau_f = c' + \sigma'_n \tan\phi'$
2. $q_u = 2c_u$
3. $\theta_f = 45° + \phi'/2$
4. $T = c_u \pi D H (D/3 + H/2)$
5. $S_t = c_{u,u} / c_{u,r}$
6. $\sigma' = \sigma - u$
7. $\phi' = \sin^{-1}[(\sigma_1' - \sigma_3')/(\sigma_1' + \sigma_3')]$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** For the KTU B.Tech exam, the most frequently tested topics in Module 3 are:
> 1. **Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion and Mohr circle drawing** (always appears).
> 2. **Triaxial test** with effective stress analysis (numerical problem).
> 3. **Direct shear test** interpretation (plotting $\tau_f$ vs $\sigma$).
> 4. **Vane shear test** numerical on sensitivity.
> 5. **UCC test** derivation of $c_u = q_u/2$.
> Master these and you will secure maximum marks in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lateral Earth Pressure: Earth Pressure at Rest - Active and Passive Earth Pressure for Cohesionless and Cohesive Soils - Rankine's and Coulomb's Theories - Point of Application of Earth Pressure for Cases With and Without Surcharge in Cohesionless and Cohesive Soils - Culmann's and Rebhan's Graphical Construction for Active Earth Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lateral Earth Pressure: Earth Pressure at Rest - Active and Passive Earth Pressure for Cohesionless and Cohesive Soils - Rankine's and Coulomb's Theories - Point of Application of Earth Pressure for Cases With and Without Surcharge in Cohesionless and Cohesive Soils - Culmann's and Rebhan's Graphical Construction for Active Earth Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Lateral Earth Pressure?

When a soil mass is retained by a structural element (such as a **retaining wall**, **sheet pile**, **basement wall**, or **bridge abutment**), the soil exerts a horizontal force on the structure. This horizontal force per unit area of the structure is termed **Lateral Earth Pressure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> Lateral Earth Pressure is the horizontal stress component ($\sigma_h$) developed within a soil mass, acting perpendicular to a vertical plane, caused by the self-weight of the soil and any superimposed loads (surcharge). It is fundamentally related to the vertical effective stress ($\sigma_v'$) through a coefficient of lateral earth pressure.

$$\sigma_h = K \cdot \sigma_v'$$

where **K** is the **Coefficient of Lateral Earth Pressure** — a dimensionless scalar that depends on the soil type, density, stress history, and the wall's movement.

## 1.2 The Three Limiting States of Lateral Pressure

The value of **K** is NOT constant — it varies dramatically with how much the wall moves relative to the soil. KTU 2024 Scheme requires mastery of three discrete states:

> [!NOTE]
> **State 1 — Earth Pressure at Rest ($K_0$):**
> The wall is **perfectly rigid and does not move at all**. The soil behaves as if it were in semi-infinite extension. The horizontal strain is **zero** ($\varepsilon_h = 0$). This is the in-situ lateral stress condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **State 2 — Active Earth Pressure ($K_a$):**
> The wall **moves AWAY from the soil mass** (rotates or translates outward). The soil is allowed to **expand laterally**, mobilizing its full shear strength. This is the **MINIMUM** possible lateral pressure because the soil helps itself by failing in shear.

> [!NOTE]
> **State 3 — Passive Earth Pressure ($K_p$):**
> The wall is **pushed INTO the soil mass** (rotates or translates toward the soil). The soil is **compressed laterally**, mobilizing its full shear strength in the opposite sense. This is the **MAXIMUM** possible lateral pressure because the soil resists being squeezed.

$$K_a < K_0 < K_p$$

## 1.3 Cohesionless vs Cohesive Soils — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cohesionless Soils** (e.g., **Sand, Gravel, Silt**):
> Shear strength is governed **solely by internal friction**: $\tau_f = \sigma \tan\phi$. No tensile strength between particles. These soils **cannot sustain tension**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cohesive Soils** (e.g., **Clay, Silty Clay**):
> Shear strength has TWO components: $\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi$. The cohesion (**c**) introduces a **finite tensile strength** that allows the soil to resist tension up to a critical depth. This gives rise to **negative pressures (tension)** and the phenomenon of **tension cracks**.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Bookshelf" Intuition

Imagine you are trying to push a **bookshelf** filled with books:
- **At-Rest Condition**: The bookshelf is welded to the floor. The books press sideways with a "natural" force that depends only on how tightly they were originally stacked.
- **Active Condition**: Now you let the bookshelf tilt slightly forward (away from the books). The books settle and slide downward slightly — they "relax." The sideways push on the shelf **decreases** dramatically.
- **Passive Condition**: Now you push the bookshelf HARD against the books. The books get crushed and compacted sideways. The sideways push on the shelf **increases** enormously because you are forcing them.

The **bookshelf is the retaining wall**; the **books are the soil grains**; the **tilting/pushing is the wall movement**.

## 1.5 Rankine's Theory vs Coulomb's Theory — The Two Giants

> [!NOTE]
> **Rankine's Theory (1857)** — A *stress-state* approach.
> Assumes the wall is **smooth** (frictionless), the backfill surface may be **inclined**, and the soil is in a **state of plastic equilibrium** (failure). Yields **closed-form analytical equations** for $K_a$ and $K_p$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Coulomb's Theory (1776)** — A *wedge-equilibrium* approach.
> Considers a **trial wedge** of soil bounded by the wall, the ground surface, and an assumed planar failure surface. The wall may be **rough** (with wall friction angle $\delta$) and **inclined**. Solves for the critical wedge that maximizes (active) or minimizes (passive) the thrust.

## 1.6 Graphical Methods — Culmann's & Rebhann's

For complex backfill profiles, stratified soils, or surcharge loads that are not uniform, graphical constructions provide **visual, intuitive solutions** without closed-form math:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Culmann's Construction (1866):**
> Plots the weight of trial wedges (W) against the orientation of the failure plane ($\alpha$) on a **Culmann Curve**. The **maximum value** (for active) corresponds to the critical failure plane. Highly versatile for irregular geometries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rebhann's Construction (1871):**
> A **refined graphical method** that uses auxiliary lines and a **sliding plane diagram** to rapidly locate the critical wedge. Faster than Culmann's for routine problems but requires careful drafting.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Earth Pressure at Rest — $K_0$ Conditions

The "at-rest" condition is the **reference state** in KTU problems because it represents the **in-situ stress** before any wall movement. For a normally consolidated soil, Jaky's empirical formula (1944) is universally accepted:

$$K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$$

For **overconsolidated soils**, the at-rest pressure is **higher** and may be estimated using the Mayne & Kulhawy refinement:

$$K_0 = (1 - \sin\phi) \cdot OCR^{\sin\phi}$$

where **OCR** = Overconsolidation Ratio.

### Engineering Reality of $K_0$:
- Used for designing **rigid basement walls**, **tunnel linings**, and **sheet piles** where minimal movement is permissible.
- $K_0$ for clays can be **0.5 to 1.5**; for sands typically **0.4 to 0.6**.

## 2.2 Rankine's Theory — Assumptions (KTU Exam Frequently Asked)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Watch:**
> Always list these assumptions explicitly when solving Rankine problems. Missing them costs 2–3 marks.

1. The soil is **homogeneous, isotropic, and semi-infinite**.
2. The wall is **smooth and vertical** (no wall friction, $\delta = 0$).
3. The backfill surface is a **plane** (level or inclined at angle $\beta$).
4. Failure occurs **simultaneously** at every point on a plane parallel to the ground surface.
5. The soil is in a state of **plastic equilibrium** (Mohr's circle is tangent to the failure envelope).

## 2.3 Rankine's Formulas — Complete Reference Table

| **Case** | **Soil Type** | **Active Pressure** | **Passive Pressure** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Level backfill, dry | Cohesionless | $\sigma_a = \gamma z K_a$ | $\sigma_p = \gamma z K_p$ |
| Inclined backfill ($\beta$) | Cohesionless | $\sigma_a = \gamma z K_a \cos\beta$ | $\sigma_p = \gamma z K_p \cos\beta$ |
| Submerged soil | Cohesionless | $\sigma_a' = \gamma' z K_a$ | $\sigma_p' = \gamma' z K_p$ |
| Level backfill | Cohesive (c–$\phi$ soil) | $\sigma_a = \gamma z K_a - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$ | $\sigma_p = \gamma z K_p + 2c\sqrt{K_p}$ |
| Inclined backfill ($\beta$) | Cohesive | $\sigma_a = \gamma z K_a \cos\beta - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$ | — |

### Coefficient Definitions (Rankine):

$$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

$$K_p = \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

For **inclined backfill** (cohesionless):

$$K_a = \cos\beta \cdot \frac{\cos\beta - \sqrt{\cos^2\beta - \cos^2\phi}}{\cos\beta + \sqrt{\cos^2\beta - \cos^2\phi}}$$

$$K_p = \cos\beta \cdot \frac{\cos\beta + \sqrt{\cos^2\beta - \cos^2\phi}}{\cos\beta - \sqrt{\cos^2\beta - \cos^2\phi}}$$

**Validity condition**: $\beta < \phi$ (otherwise the backfill itself is unstable and slides).

## 2.4 The Critical Depth (Depth of Tension Crack) — Cohesive Soils

For cohesive soils, Rankine's active pressure equation can yield **negative values** near the surface. Since soil cannot sustain tension, a **tension crack** develops to a critical depth $z_0$ where $\sigma_a = 0$:

$$\boxed{z_0 = \frac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}} = \frac{2c}{\gamma}\tan\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake**: Students forget that the **active pressure distribution is ZERO from the surface to depth $z_0$**, not negative. Total active thrust is computed by integrating the **triangular distribution from $z_0$ to $H$**.

## 2.5 Coulomb's Theory — Assumptions

1. The wall has a finite height $H$ and **rough back face** with wall friction angle $\delta$.
2. The wall back may be inclined at angle $\alpha$ from vertical.
3. The backfill surface may be inclined at angle $\beta$ from horizontal.
4. Failure surface is **planar** (a major simplification — true failure is curved in passive case).
5. The sliding wedge acts as a **rigid body**.

## 2.6 Coulomb's Formulas — Complete Reference

For wall inclined at angle $\alpha$ from vertical, backfill inclined at $\beta$ from horizontal, wall friction $\delta$:

$$K_a = \frac{\cos^2(\phi - \alpha)}{\cos^2\alpha \cdot \cos(\alpha + \delta) \left[1 + \sqrt{\frac{\sin(\phi+\delta)\sin(\phi-\beta)}{\cos(\alpha+\delta)\cos(\alpha-\beta)}}\right]^2}$$

$$K_p = \frac{\cos^2(\phi + \alpha)}{\cos^2\alpha \cdot \cos(\alpha - \delta) \left[1 - \sqrt{\frac{\sin(\phi+\delta)\sin(\phi+\beta)}{\cos(\alpha-\delta)\cos(\alpha+\beta)}}\right]^2}$$

### Total Thrust on Wall (Coulomb):

$$P_a = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a$$

$$P_p = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_p$$

## 2.7 Point of Application — With and Without Surcharge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Without Surcharge** (Triangular Pressure Distribution):
> Pressure varies **linearly from 0 at top to maximum at base**. Resultant acts at **H/3 from the base** (centroid of a triangle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **With Uniform Suctharge $q$** (kN/m²) on Level Backfill:
> Surcharge is converted to an **equivalent height of soil**: $h_{eq} = q/\gamma$.
> The active pressure due to surcharge alone is **uniform**: $\sigma_q = K_a \cdot q$ acting over the **full height H**.
> The resultant has TWO components:
> - Triangular (from soil): acts at **H/3** from base
> - Rectangular (from surcharge): acts at **H/2** from base
> - Combined resultant acts at: $\bar{z} = \frac{P_a \cdot (H/3) + P_q \cdot (H/2)}{P_a + P_q}$

| **Loading Condition** | **Pressure Distribution** | **Point of Application (from base)** |
|---|---|---|
| Soil only, cohesionless | Triangle, zero at top | $H/3$ |
| Soil only, cohesive (with tension crack) | Trapezoidal (after $z_0$) | Complex — centroid of trapezoid |
| Surcharge $q$, cohesionless | Triangle + Rectangle | $\frac{\frac{1}{6}\gamma H^3 K_a + \frac{1}{2}qHK_a}{\frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a + qHK_a} = \frac{H(\gamma H + 3q)}{3(\gamma H + 2q)}$ |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Theory / Concept** | **Typical Application** |
|---|---|
| Rankine's Active Pressure | Gravity retaining walls, basement walls with smooth backing |
| Rankine's Passive Pressure | Design of anchors, fixed-earth support of braced cuts |
| Coulomb's Theory | Cantilever sheet pile walls, MSE walls, bridge abutments |
| Culmann's Graphical Method | Walls with broken backfill, layered soil, line loads |
| Rebhann's Construction | Rapid estimation of active thrust in design offices |
| $K_0$ at-rest | Tunnel linings, rigid shafts, soldier pile walls with locked-in stress |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Rankine's Active Earth Pressure Coefficient ($K_a$)

### Step 1 — State of Stress at Failure
Consider a soil element at depth $z$ with the wall moving away. The vertical stress remains the overburden pressure $\sigma_v = \gamma z$. As the wall yields, the horizontal stress $\sigma_h$ **decreases** until the soil reaches failure.

The major principal stress is now the vertical (since $\sigma_v > \sigma_h$ during active failure):

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_v = \gamma z$$

$$\sigma_3 = \sigma_h = \sigma_a$$

### Step 2 — Mohr-Circle Criterion at Failure
At failure, Mohr's circle is tangent to the Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope. The geometric relationship between principal stresses at failure (cohesionless soil, $c = 0$) is:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

$$\sigma_3 = \sigma_1 \tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Substituting for Active State
Substituting $\sigma_1 = \gamma z$ and $\sigma_3 = \sigma_a$:

$$\sigma_a = \gamma z \tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}}$$

### Step 4 — Algebraic Identity Verification
Prove the equivalence using trigonometric identity:

$$\tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = \left[\frac{\tan 45° - \tan(\phi/2)}{1 + \tan 45° \tan(\phi/2)}\right]^2 = \left[\frac{1 - \tan(\phi/2)}{1 + \tan(\phi/2)}\right]^2$$

Using $\tan(\phi/2) = \frac{\sin\phi}{1 + \cos\phi}$, after rationalization we obtain:

$$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Rankine's Passive Earth Pressure Coefficient ($K_p$)

### Step 1 — Stress State Reversal
For passive failure, the wall moves into the soil. The horizontal stress **increases** beyond the vertical:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_h = \sigma_p$$

$$\sigma_3 = \sigma_v = \gamma z$$

### Step 2 — Apply Failure Criterion
From the same Mohr-Coulomb geometric identity:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Passive Stress State
$$\sigma_p = \gamma z \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{K_p = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}}$$

### Step 4 — Relationship Between $K_a$ and $K_p$
Multiply $K_a$ and $K_p$:

$$K_a \cdot K_p = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} \cdot \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{K_p = \frac{1}{K_a}}$$

This elegant identity is a **KTU favorite** for shortcut problem solving.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Rankine's Active Pressure for Cohesive (c–$\phi$) Soil

### Step 1 — Modified Failure Criterion
For $c > 0$, the Mohr-Coulomb criterion becomes:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) + 2c \tan\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Active Stress State ($\sigma_1 = \gamma z$, $\sigma_3 = \sigma_a$)
$$\gamma z = \sigma_a \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) + 2c \tan\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\sigma_a$
Rearranging and noting that $\tan(45° + \phi/2) = 1/\sqrt{K_a}$:

$$\sigma_a = \gamma z \cdot \frac{1}{\tan^2(45° + \phi/2)} - \frac{2c}{\tan(45° + \phi/2)}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_a = \gamma z K_a - 2c\sqrt{K_a}}$$

### Step 4 — Similarly, Derive Passive Pressure
$$\boxed{\sigma_p = \gamma z K_p + 2c\sqrt{K_p}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Critical Depth $z_0$ (Tension Crack Depth)

### Step 1 — Set Active Pressure to Zero
At the critical depth, the active pressure transitions from negative (theoretical tension) to positive. Setting $\sigma_a = 0$:

$$0 = \gamma z_0 K_a - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $z_0$
$$\gamma z_0 K_a = 2c\sqrt{K_a}$$

$$z_0 = \frac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}}$$

### Step 3 — Express in Trigonometric Form
Using $\sqrt{K_a} = \tan(45° - \phi/2)$, we have $1/\sqrt{K_a} = \tan(45° + \phi/2)$:

$$\boxed{z_0 = \frac{2c}{\gamma}\tan\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation**: From $z = 0$ to $z = z_0$, the soil is in **tension**. It cannot sustain this tension, so a vertical crack forms. Designers either assume the crack is filled with water (creating hydrostatic pressure) or install a **drainage layer** behind the wall.

---

## 3.5 Total Active Thrust with Surcharge — Step-by-Step

**Given**: Wall of height $H$, unit weight $\gamma$, friction angle $\phi$, surcharge $q$ (kN/m²), level backfill.

### Step 1 — Components of Pressure
Two separate mechanisms contribute to horizontal thrust:

**Component A — Weight of Soil (Triangular)**:
$$\sigma_a^{soil}(z) = \gamma z K_a \quad \text{(varies linearly from 0 at top to } \gamma H K_a \text{ at base)}$$

**Component B — Surcharge (Uniform)**:
$$\sigma_a^{surcharge} = q K_a \quad \text{(constant at every depth)}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Resultant Forces
Force from soil (per unit length of wall):

$$P_a^{soil} = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a \quad \text{(triangle area)}$$

Force from surcharge (per unit length of wall):

$$P_a^{surcharge} = q H K_a \quad \text{(rectangle area)}$$

### Step 3 — Locate Each Resultant
The **triangular** distribution has its centroid at $H/3$ from the base.

The **rectangular** distribution has its centroid at $H/2$ from the base.

### Step 4 — Combined Resultant
$$P_{total} = P_a^{soil} + P_a^{surcharge} = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a + q H K_a$$

### Step 5 — Point of Application (Moment About Base)
Taking moments about the base, and dividing by total thrust:

$$\bar{z} = \frac{P_a^{soil} \cdot (H/3) + P_a^{surcharge} \cdot (H/2)}{P_a^{soil} + P_a^{surcharge}}$$

Substituting:

$$\bar{z} = \frac{\frac{\gamma H^2 K_a}{2} \cdot \frac{H}{3} + qHK_a \cdot \frac{H}{2}}{\frac{\gamma H^2 K_a}{2} + qHK_a}$$

Simplifying by dividing numerator and denominator by $HK_a$:

$$\bar{z} = \frac{\frac{\gamma H^2}{6} + \frac{qH}{2}}{\frac{\gamma H}{2} + q}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by 6:

$$\boxed{\bar{z} = \frac{H(\gamma H + 3q)}{3(\gamma H + 2q)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check**: When $q = 0$, $\bar{z} = H/3$. ✓ (matches soil-only case)
> When $q \to \infty$, $\bar{z} \to H/2$. ✓ (matches pure-rectangle case)

---

## 3.6 Coulomb's Wedge Analysis — Conceptual Derivation

Coulomb's theory works by **assumed failure plane** through the heel of the wall. For active case with the given geometry:

### Step 1 — Trial Wedge Geometry
A planar surface makes angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. The wedge has:
- Weight: $W = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 \cdot \frac{\cos(\theta - \alpha)\cos(\alpha - \beta)}{\sin(\theta - \alpha)\cos(\alpha - \beta)} \cdot ...$ (geometric derivation)

### Step 2 — Force Polygon
Three forces act on the wedge:
- **W** (weight, vertical, downward)
- **R** (resultant reaction on failure plane, inclined at $\phi$ to normal of plane)
- **P** (reaction from wall, inclined at $\delta$ to normal of wall face)

### Step 3 — Equilibrium
Using Lami's theorem or trigonometric closure:

$$P = \frac{W \sin(\theta - \phi)}{\sin(\theta - \phi + \psi)}$$

where $\psi = 90° - \alpha - \delta$ is a geometric constant.

### Step 4 — Maximize for Critical Wedge
Setting $\frac{dP}{d\theta} = 0$ yields the critical angle $\theta_{cr}$. Substituting back:

$$\boxed{P_a = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a}$$

where $K_a$ is given by the full Coulomb expression in Section 2.6.

---

## 3.7 Culmann's Graphical Construction — Algorithmic Steps

### Step 1 — Draw Wall and Failure Surface
Draw the wall back $AB$ (length $H$) and the slope of backfill. Mark several **trial failure planes** through the heel $A$ at angles $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, ..., \alpha_n$ with the horizontal.

### Step 2 — Compute Wedge Weights
For each trial plane $AC_i$, compute the weight $W_i$ of wedge $ABC_i$.

### Step 3 — Draw Culmann Line
From $A$, draw a line at angle $\phi$ below the horizontal — this is the **Culmann line** ($\phi$-line).

### Step 4 — Construct Culmann Curve
From point $B$ (top of wall), draw a line at angle $(\phi - \theta)$ with the wall back. For each trial plane:
- Lay off weight $W_i$ along this line from $B$.
- From the end of $W_i$, draw a line parallel to the trial failure plane $AC_i$ until it intersects the $\phi$-line.
- Mark this intersection.

### Step 5 — Connect Intersection Points
The locus of these intersections forms the **Culmann curve**.

### Step 6 — Draw Parallel Tangent
Draw a line parallel to the Culmann line, **tangent** to the Culmann curve. The point of tangency defines the **critical wedge** that yields maximum thrust.

### Step 7 — Measure Active Thrust
The tangent length from $B$ to the point of tangency equals $P_a$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rebhann's Construction** uses an auxiliary "sliding plane" line at angle $(2\phi - \theta)$ from horizontal and a different reference. It is essentially Culmann's method with geometric shortcuts to **avoid plotting the full Culmann curve**, identifying the critical wedge directly.

---

## 3.8 Active Thrust on Cohesive Soil (Cohesionless failure wedge geometry)

For a cohesive backfill, **tension cracks** occur up to depth $z_0$. The Rankine active thrust is computed only from depth $z_0$ to $H$:

$$P_a = \int_{z_0}^{H} \sigma_a \, dz = \int_{z_0}^{H} (\gamma z K_a - 2c\sqrt{K_a}) \, dz$$

Integrating:

$$P_a = \frac{1}{2}\gamma (H^2 - z_0^2) K_a - 2c(H - z_0)\sqrt{K_a}$$

Using $z_0 = 2c/(\gamma\sqrt{K_a})$, simplification yields:

$$\boxed{P_a = \frac{1}{2}\gamma (H - z_0)^2 K_a}$$

This is a beautiful result: **the active thrust on a cohesive soil is identical to that on a cohesionless soil of reduced height $(H - z_0)$** with the same $\phi$ but no cohesion.

The point of application is at $\frac{H - z_0}{3}$ **above the base**, which means the resultant is shifted upward compared to a cohesionless case.
