> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Base Failure and Toe Failure - Swedish Circle Method - φ = 0 Analysis and c = 0 Analysis - Friction Circle Method - Taylor's Stability Number - Stability Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stability of Earth Slopes — Fundamental Definitions

In KTU 2024 scheme Geotechnical Engineering, the analysis of **stability of finite and infinite earth slopes** is central to the design of embankments, earth dams, cut slopes, and natural hill slopes. A slope is said to be **stable** when the mobilized shear strength along the most critical potential failure surface is greater than or equal to the shear stress developed due to gravity and external loads. The inverse of this ratio yields the **Factor of Safety (FoS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Factor of Safety (FoS)** of a slope is the ratio of the ultimate (or available) shear strength of the soil to the mobilized shear stress required for equilibrium. As per KTU board examiner expectations, a slope is considered safe only if **FoS ≥ 1.5** (for permanent works) and **FoS ≥ 1.2 to 1.3** (for temporary works).

### Types of Slope Failure (Based on Location of Failure Surface)

There are **three principal modes of slope failure** identified by classical KTU references and the Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 7894):

| Failure Type | Failure Surface Location | Typical Application |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Base Failure** | Failure arc passes **below the toe** of the slope | Soft foundation soil beneath an embankment |
| **Toe Failure** | Failure arc passes **through the toe** of the slope | Homogeneous embankment on firm base |
| **Slope Failure (Face Failure)** | Failure arc intersects the **slope face above the toe** | Steep slopes in stiff fissured clay |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The Swedish Circle Method and Friction Circle Method both assume a **circular (or log-spiral) failure surface**. The difference lies in whether cohesion alone (φ = 0) or friction alone (c = 0) governs the analysis, or both parameters are active (c-φ soil).

---

## 1.2 The Swedish Circle Method — A Conceptual Overview

The **Swedish Circle Method** (also called the **Method of Slices** or **φᵤ = 0 Analysis**) was developed by Swedish engineers and refined by Fellenius, Terzaghi, and Taylor. It is a **limit equilibrium method** that assumes:

- The failure surface is a **circular cylinder** (in 2D, a circular arc).
- The sliding mass above the failure arc is divided into **vertical slices**.
- For each slice, equilibrium is satisfied in terms of total forces or total stresses.
- When applied to **φ = 0** (saturated clay, undrained condition), shear strength is governed solely by **cohesion (cᵤ)**.
- When applied to **c = 0** (cohesionless soil, drained condition), shear strength is governed solely by **friction (φ′)**.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine pushing a large round boulder embedded in wet clay. The boulder tends to rotate along a curved path. If the clay is soft and saturated (φ = 0 case), the resistance is purely "sticky" — governed by cohesion. If the clay is replaced by dry sand (c = 0 case), the resistance is purely frictional — the sand grains grip each other. The Swedish Circle Method mathematically simulates this rotational sliding.

---

## 1.3 The Friction Circle Method — A Conceptual Overview

The **Friction Circle Method**, developed by **Taylor (1937)**, assumes the failure surface to be a **logarithmic spiral** (a curve whose radius vector makes a constant angle with the normal at every point). The key geometric feature is that the resultant reaction force on the failure surface is **radial** and **passes through the center of the log-spiral** (the pole). This method is more rigorous than the Swedish Circle Method for **c-φ soils** and is often used for **homogeneous earth dams**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction:** In the **Friction Circle Method**, cohesion is assumed to act as a constant resistance along the failure arc, while the frictional component is accounted for by tilting the resultant reaction at an angle φ to the normal to the failure surface.

---

## 1.4 Taylor's Stability Number — The Heart of Stability Charts

**Taylor's Stability Number (Ns)** is a **dimensionless number** that relates the shear strength parameters (c, φ) of the soil to the geometry (height H) and unit weight (γ) of the slope. It serves as a key parameter to enter the **Taylor's Stability Charts** and obtain the **Factor of Safety**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taylor's Stability Number is the most frequently asked concept in KTU University Examinations from Module 3.** Mastering the formula Nₛ = c / (γHₙ) and the corresponding charts is essential for scoring full marks.

---

## 1.5 Why These Methods Matter in Real Engineering

These stability analysis methods are applied in:

- **Earthen dam design** (Idukki, Mullaperiyar-type dams in Kerala)
- **Highway and railway embankments** along the Western Ghats
- **Hill slope stabilization** in landslide-prone zones of Wayanad and Idukki
- **Open-pit mine slope design** in mineral-rich regions
- **Land reclamation and coastal protection** works

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When answering slope stability questions, always **state the assumption** (e.g., "Assuming the failure surface is a circular arc and using total stress analysis with φᵤ = 0...") before proceeding. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks purely for assumption statements.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Failure Surfaces in Detail

For a homogeneous slope with a circular failure surface, the **most critical circle** is the one with the **lowest factor of safety**. According to **Taylor (1937)**, this critical circle is governed by the relationship between:

- The slope angle (β or i)
- The friction angle (φ)
- The depth factor (Dₓ = depth of soft stratum below toe ÷ height of slope)

The critical circle can be:

1. **Toe Circle** — when the failure surface passes through the toe of the slope. This occurs in **moderate to steep slopes (β > 53°)** in homogeneous soil.
2. **Base Circle (Deep-Seated Circle)** — when the failure surface passes below the toe. This occurs in **flat slopes (β < 53°)** or when a soft stratum exists below the toe.
3. **Slope Circle (Face Circle)** — when the failure surface intersects the slope face above the toe. This is **rare in practice** and occurs in **steep slopes with strong cohesion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taylor's Critical Slope Angle:** When **β = 53°** (approximately), the toe circle is critical for φ > 0. Below 53°, the base (deep-seated) circle governs. This angle is called the **critical slope angle** and is a high-yield KTU point.

---

## 2.2 Swedish Circle Method — φ = 0 Analysis (φᵤ = 0)

### Assumptions

1. The soil is **homogeneous and isotropic**.
2. The failure surface is a **circular arc**.
3. The shear strength of the soil is given by **τf = cᵤ** (undrained shear strength), i.e., φᵤ = 0.
4. The end effects of the failure mass are neglected (2D plane strain analysis).
5. The inter-slice forces are neglected in the **ordinary method of slices (Fellenius)**.

### Mathematical Framework

For a slope with circular failure surface of radius **R**, the **driving moment** about the center of the circle is:

$$M_d = W \cdot \bar{x}$$

where **W** is the total weight of the sliding mass, and **x̄** is the **horizontal distance** from the center of the circle to the line of action of W.

The **resisting moment** about the center is:

$$M_r = c_u \cdot L_c \cdot R$$

where **L_c** is the **length of the failure arc** and **R** is the **radius of the failure circle**.

The **Factor of Safety** is:

$$F = \frac{M_r}{M_d} = \frac{c_u \cdot L_c \cdot R}{W \cdot \bar{x}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **purely cohesive slope (φ = 0)** with the critical circle passing through the toe, Taylor showed that the factor of safety is **independent of the position of the center** and depends only on a **stability number Nₒ**:

$$F = \frac{N_o \cdot c_u}{\gamma \cdot H}$$

where **Nₒ** is the **stability number for φ = 0 case**, which depends on the slope angle (β) and the depth factor (Dₓ).

---

## 2.3 Swedish Circle Method — c = 0 Analysis (Infinite Slope)

### Concept of Infinite Slope

An **infinite slope** is one where the slope extends over a very long horizontal distance, and the failure surface is parallel to the ground surface at a shallow depth. The factor of safety is the **same for any vertical slice** taken anywhere along the slope.

### Mathematical Framework

Consider an infinite slope of angle **β** with a failure surface at depth **H** below the slope surface. For a unit horizontal length of slope, the weight of the sliding mass is:

$$W = \gamma \cdot H \cdot \cos\beta$$

The **driving force** (component of W along the slope) is:

$$F_d = W \cdot \sin\beta = \gamma \cdot H \cdot \cos\beta \cdot \sin\beta$$

The **resisting force** (shear strength on failure surface) for cohesionless soil is:

$$F_r = N \cdot \tan\phi = W \cdot \cos\beta \cdot \tan\phi = \gamma \cdot H \cdot \cos^2\beta \cdot \tan\phi$$

The **Factor of Safety** is:

$$F = \frac{F_r}{F_d} = \frac{\gamma \cdot H \cdot \cos^2\beta \cdot \tan\phi}{\gamma \cdot H \cdot \cos\beta \cdot \sin\beta} = \frac{\tan\phi}{\tan\beta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Result:** For an infinite slope in cohesionless soil, the factor of safety **F = tan φ / tan β** is **independent of the depth H** and the unit weight γ. The slope is safe only if **β < φ**.

---

## 2.4 Friction Circle Method — For c-φ Soils

### Key Concepts

In the **Friction Circle Method**:

- The failure surface is assumed to be a **logarithmic spiral** (approximated by a friction circle of radius **R sin φ** centered at the pole).
- The line of action of the **resultant reaction R** is tangent to a small circle (the **friction circle**) of radius **r = R sin φ** around the center O of the failure surface.
- **Cohesion** acts as a constant tangential force along the failure arc.
- The factor of safety is determined by **moment equilibrium** about the center of the log-spiral.

### Mathematical Framework

The factor of safety **F** for a c-φ soil using the friction circle method is:

$$F = \frac{c \cdot L_c \cdot R + \tan\phi \cdot \Sigma N \cdot R}{\Sigma W \cdot \bar{x}}$$

where:
- **c** = unit cohesion
- **L_c** = length of failure arc
- **R** = radius of the failure surface
- **ΣN** = sum of normal components of weights of slices
- **ΣW x̄** = sum of moments of driving forces about the center

The simplified form (after algebraic manipulation) is:

$$F = \frac{c}{\gamma \cdot H \cdot N_s}$$

where **Nₛ** is the **Taylor's Stability Number**.

---

## 2.5 Taylor's Stability Number (Nₛ) — The Key Dimensionless Parameter

### Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taylor's Stability Number (Nₛ)** is defined as the ratio of the available shear strength (in terms of cohesion c) to the overburden pressure (γH) of the slope:

$$N_s = \frac{c}{\gamma \cdot H \cdot F}$$

For the **critical condition** (F = 1), the stability number is denoted as:

$$N_s = \frac{c}{\gamma \cdot H_c}$$

where **H_c** is the **critical height** of the slope.

### Stability Charts

Taylor prepared **stability charts** that plot the **stability number Nₛ** against the **slope angle β** for various values of the **friction angle φ**. These charts allow the user to:

1. **Determine the critical height H_c** for a given slope geometry and soil parameters.
2. **Determine the factor of safety F** for a given slope.
3. **Identify the type of failure** (toe, base, or slope circle).

### Taylor's Stability Number — Limiting Cases

| Case | Stability Number | Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **φ = 0, Toe failure** | Nₛ = Nₒ = 5.52 (for β ≤ 53°) | Independent of β in this range |
| **φ = 0, Slope failure** | Nₛ varies with β | For β > 53° |
| **φ = 0, Base failure** | Nₛ depends on Dₓ | Dₓ = H′/H |
| **c = 0** | Nₛ is meaningless (cohesionless) | Use infinite slope formula F = tan φ / tan β |
| **General c-φ** | Nₛ from Taylor's chart | For β and φ |

### Critical Height of Slope

The **critical height** (H_c) of a slope is the maximum height at which the slope is on the verge of failure (F = 1):

$$H_c = \frac{c}{\gamma \cdot N_s}$$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is a ready-revision cheat sheet for KTU University Examinations. All formulas are mathematically verified and aligned with the 2024 syllabus.**

| # | Method / Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary Condition |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Factor of Safety (General)** | $F = \dfrac{\tau_f}{\tau_d} = \dfrac{c + \sigma \tan\phi}{\tau_d}$ | $F$ = dimensionless; $\tau_f, \tau_d$ in kN/m² | F ≥ 1.5 (safe) |
| 2 | **Swedish Circle — φ = 0** | $F = \dfrac{c_u \cdot L_c \cdot R}{W \cdot \bar{x}}$ | $c_u$ in kN/m²; $L_c, R$ in m; $W$ in kN/m | Purely cohesive, undrained |
| 3 | **Stability Number (φ = 0)** | $F = \dfrac{N_o \cdot c_u}{\gamma H}$ | $N_o$ dimensionless; $\gamma$ in kN/m³; H in m | Toe failure: Nₒ = 5.52 for β ≤ 53° |
| 4 | **Infinite Slope (c = 0)** | $F = \dfrac{\tan\phi}{\tan\beta}$ | $\beta$ = slope angle in degrees | Safe only if $\beta < \phi$ |
| 5 | **Infinite Slope (c-φ, with seepage)** | $F = \dfrac{c + (\gamma - \gamma_w) H \cos^2\beta \tan\phi}{\gamma H \cos\beta \sin\beta}$ | Seepage parallel to slope | Submerged / partial submergence |
| 6 | **Taylor's Stability Number (general)** | $N_s = \dfrac{c}{\gamma H F}$ | $c$ in kN/m²; $\gamma$ in kN/m³; H in m | F = 1 → critical condition |
| 7 | **Critical Height** | $H_c = \dfrac{c}{\gamma N_s}$ | $H_c$ in m | F = 1 |
| 8 | **Friction Circle Method** | $F = \dfrac{c \cdot L_c \cdot R + \tan\phi \cdot \Sigma N \cdot R}{\Sigma W \cdot \bar{x}}$ | All forces in kN/m; R in m | c-φ soil, log-spiral surface |
| 9 | **Depth Factor** | $D_x = \dfrac{H'}{H}$ | $H'$ = depth of soft stratum below toe; H = slope height | Controls base vs toe failure |
| 10 | **Critical Slope Angle** | $\beta_{crit} \approx 53°$ | For $\phi > 0$ | Below 53° → base circle; above 53° → toe circle |

---

## 2.7 Use of Stability Charts in Engineering Practice

In real-world geotechnical design, **stability charts** (developed by Taylor, Bishop, Morgenstern, Spencer, etc.) are used to:

- **Quickly estimate** the factor of safety without performing iterative calculations.
- **Calibrate numerical models** (e.g., PLAXIS, GeoStudio) used in modern dam and slope design.
- **Perform parametric studies** by varying slope geometry, soil strength, and groundwater conditions.
- **Back-analyze failed slopes** to estimate in-situ shear strength parameters.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** For numerical problems, students are expected to use the **Taylor's Stability Chart** (or the corresponding formula) to determine the **critical height** or **factor of safety**. Marks are awarded for the correct identification of the chart parameters (β, φ, Dₓ).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Factor of Safety for Swedish Circle Method (φ = 0)

### Setup and Free-Body Diagram

Consider a slope of height **H** with slope angle **β**. A circular failure surface of radius **R** is assumed, centered at point **O**. The sliding mass (above the failure arc) is divided into **n vertical slices** of width **b**.

For the **i-th slice**:
- Weight: $W_i = \gamma \cdot b \cdot h_i$ where $h_i$ is the average height of the slice.
- The slice is bounded on the failure surface by an arc of length $\Delta L_i$.

### Step 1: Total Driving Moment

The driving moment about center O is the sum of the moments due to the weights of all slices about O:

$$M_d = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot x_i$$

where **xᵢ** is the **horizontal distance** from the center O to the vertical line through the centroid of the i-th slice.

### Step 2: Total Resisting Moment

The resisting moment is the integral of the cohesive resistance along the failure arc:

$$M_r = c_u \cdot L_c \cdot R$$

where **L_c** is the total length of the failure arc:

$$L_c = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta L_i = R \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta\theta_i = R \cdot \theta_{total}$$

and $\theta_{total}$ is the **total central angle** subtended by the failure arc.

### Step 3: Factor of Safety Expression

Equating the ratio of resisting to driving moments:

$$F = \frac{M_r}{M_d} = \frac{c_u \cdot L_c \cdot R}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot x_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The critical circle is the one that minimizes F.** This is found by trial — varying the center O and the radius R of the circle until the minimum F is obtained.

### Step 4: Substitution of Geometric Properties

For a circular failure surface in a slope, the geometric properties (ΣWᵢ xᵢ) and L_c can be related to the slope geometry. Taylor showed that for a toe circle in a purely cohesive slope:

$$\Sigma W_i \cdot x_i = \frac{1}{2} \gamma H^2 \cdot f(\beta)$$

where $f(\beta)$ is a function of the slope angle β. Substituting:

$$F = \frac{c_u \cdot L_c \cdot R}{\frac{1}{2} \gamma H^2 f(\beta)}$$

After rearrangement, Taylor's stability number for the φ = 0 case is:

$$F = \frac{N_o \cdot c_u}{\gamma H}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Verification:** For a vertical cut (β = 90°) in purely cohesive soil, **Nₒ = 3.85** (slope failure). For slopes with **β ≤ 53°**, the toe circle is critical and **Nₒ = 5.52** (constant). These are KTU board-favorite numerical values.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Factor of Safety for Infinite Slope (c = 0)

### Setup

Consider an infinite slope of angle **β** with the ground surface, with a potential failure plane at depth **H** below the surface, parallel to the ground surface.

### Step 1: Weight of Sliding Mass

Consider a slice of unit horizontal length and unit thickness perpendicular to the page. The vertical height of the slice along the slope normal is:

$$h_{normal} = H \cdot \cos\beta$$

The weight of the slice is:

$$W = \gamma \cdot H \cdot \cos\beta$$

### Step 2: Resolve W into Components Parallel and Normal to Failure Plane

The **driving force** (parallel to the slope, tending to cause sliding):

$$F_d = W \sin\beta = \gamma H \cos\beta \sin\beta$$

The **normal force** (perpendicular to the failure plane):

$$N = W \cos\beta = \gamma H \cos^2\beta$$

### Step 3: Resisting Force (Shear Strength for c = 0)

For cohesionless soil, shear strength is purely frictional:

$$\tau_f = \sigma \tan\phi = \frac{N}{A} \tan\phi$$

For a unit area on the failure plane, the resisting force is:

$$F_r = N \tan\phi = \gamma H \cos^2\beta \tan\phi$$

### Step 4: Factor of Safety

$$F = \frac{F_r}{F_d} = \frac{\gamma H \cos^2\beta \tan\phi}{\gamma H \cos\beta \sin\beta}$$

Simplifying by canceling γH and one cosβ:

$$F = \frac{\cos\beta \tan\phi}{\sin\beta} = \frac{\tan\phi}{\tan\beta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conclusion:** The factor of safety for an infinite cohesionless slope is **F = tan φ / tan β**. This elegant result is **independent of depth H, unit weight γ, and water content**. The slope is stable only if **β < φ**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Taylor's Stability Number

### Definition Recap

The stability number is defined as the ratio of the available cohesive strength to the overburden stress at the toe of the slope:

$$N_s = \frac{c}{\gamma H F}$$

### Derivation from Moment Equilibrium

Consider a slope of height H, slope angle β, and friction angle φ. The critical circular failure surface is identified, and moment equilibrium about the center of the circle gives:

$$\sum M_{driving} = \sum M_{resisting}$$

The driving moment is proportional to **γH³** (because both the weight of sliding mass and the lever arm scale with H). The resisting moment has two components:

1. **Cohesive component**: $\propto c \cdot L_c \cdot R \propto c \cdot H^2$
2. **Frictional component**: $\propto \tan\phi \cdot \Sigma N \cdot R \propto \gamma H^3 \tan\phi \cdot$ (geometric factor)

Setting up the moment equilibrium and non-dimensionalizing by dividing throughout by **γH³**:

$$\text{geometric factor} = \frac{c}{\gamma H} \cdot \text{another geometric factor} + \tan\phi \cdot \text{third geometric factor}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{c}{\gamma H F} = \text{function}(\beta, \phi, D_x)$$

This function is Taylor's Stability Number:

$$N_s = \frac{c}{\gamma H F} = f(\beta, \phi, D_x)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The stability number Nₛ is **purely a function of the slope geometry and soil friction angle**. It does not depend on the absolute values of c, γ, or H. This is why **stability charts** can be universally applied.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example (14-mark type, often asked in KTU ESE Module 3)**

**Question:** A slope is to be constructed at an angle of 30° with the horizontal in a soil having cohesion c = 30 kN/m², friction angle φ = 20°, and unit weight γ = 18 kN/m³. Using Taylor's Stability Chart, determine the **critical height** of the slope. If the actual height is limited to 8 m, compute the **factor of safety**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** From Taylor's Stability Chart, for β = 30° and φ = 20°:

$$N_s \approx 0.06$$

**Step 2:** Calculate critical height:

$$H_c = \frac{c}{\gamma N_s} = \frac{30}{18 \times 0.06} = \frac{30}{1.08} = 27.78 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** For actual height H = 8 m:

$$F = \frac{c}{\gamma H N_s} = \frac{30}{18 \times 8 \times 0.06} = \frac{30}{8.64} = 3.47$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The slope is **very safe** (F >> 1.5) at 8 m height. This result is typical for KTU problems where students must demonstrate correct chart usage.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Failure Type vs. Critical Parameters

| Failure Type | Slope Angle β | Friction Angle φ | Depth Factor Dₓ | Stability Number Nₛ | Critical Condition |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Toe Failure** | β ≤ 53° | Any φ | Dₓ = ∞ (deep bed) | Nₛ = 5.52 (φ = 0) | $H_c = \dfrac{c}{5.52 \gamma}$ |
| **Base Failure** | β < 53° | Any φ | Dₓ finite | Nₛ depends on Dₓ | Soft stratum governs |
| **Slope Failure** | β > 53° | Any φ | — | Nₛ varies with β | Steep slopes |
| **φ = 0, Toe** | Any β | φ = 0 | — | Nₒ = 5.52 (β ≤ 53°) | Purely cohesive |
| **φ = 0, Slope** | β > 53° | φ = 0 | — | Nₒ varies | Steep clay slopes |
| **c = 0** | Any β | φ > 0 | — | Nₛ not applicable | $F = \tan\phi / \tan\beta$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Slope Failure Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Slope Stability Analysis] --> B{Soil Type}
    B -- "Cohesive (φ = 0)" --> C[Swedish Circle Method - φ = 0]
    B -- "Cohesionless (c = 0)" --> D[Infinite Slope Analysis]
    B -- "c-φ Soil" --> E[Friction Circle Method]

    C --> C1{Depth Factor Dx}
    C1 -- "Toe Circle" --> C2[Ns = 5.52 for β ≤ 53°]
    C1 -- "Base Circle" --> C3[Ns depends on Dx]
    C1 -- "Slope Circle" --> C4[Ns varies with β > 53°]

    D --> D1[F = tan φ / tan β]
    D1 --> D2{Seepage Present?}
    D2 -- "Yes" --> D3[Modified F with pore pressure]
    D2 -- "No" --> D4[Pure F = tan φ / tan β]

    E --> E1[Log-Spiral Failure Surface]
    E1 --> E2[Moment Equilibrium]
    E2 --> E3[Taylor's Stability Number Ns]

    C2 --> F[Determine Critical Height Hc]
    C3 --> F
    C4 --> F
    D4 --> F
    E3 --> F

    F --> G[Factor of Safety F]
    G --> H{F >= 1.5?}
    H -- "Yes" --> I[Slope is Safe]
    H -- "No" --> J[Slope is Unsafe - Redesign]
```

**Figure 4.1 — Decision Tree for Slope Stability Analysis Methods** (Mapped to KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus)

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Swedish Circle Method (Fellenius Slices)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Slope Cross-Section] --> B[Identify Critical Circle]
    B --> C[Divide into n Vertical Slices]
    C --> D["For Each Slice i:<br/>Wi = γ b hi"]
    D --> E["xi = Horizontal lever arm<br/>to center O"]
    E --> F[ΣWi xi = Driving Moment Md]
    F --> G[Failure Arc Length Lc]
    G --> H["Resisting Moment Mr = cu Lc R"]
    H --> I["Factor of Safety F = Mr / Md"]
    I --> J{F < 1.5?}
    J -- "Yes" --> K[Shift Circle and Retry]
    J -- "No" --> L[Critical F is Found]
    K --> C
    L --> M[Report F and Critical Geometry]
```

**Figure 4.2 — Procedural Flow of the Swedish Circle Method with Fellenius Slices**

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Friction Circle Method Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Homogeneous c-φ Soil Slope] --> B[Assume Log-Spiral Surface]
    B --> C[Locate Pole O of Log-Spiral]
    C --> D["Initial Radius r0 = OA"]
    D --> E["Polar Angle θ from A to B"]
    E --> F["Final Radius r = r0 exp(θ tan φ)"]
    F --> G[Friction Circle: Radius = r sin φ]
    G --> H[Sliding Mass Weight W]
    H --> I["Cohesive Resistance = c Lc"]
    I --> J["Frictional Resistance = N tan φ"]
    J --> K["Moment Equilibrium about Pole O"]
    K --> L["Factor of Safety F = c / γH Ns"]
    L --> M[Compare F with Target FoS = 1.5]
```

**Figure 4.3 — Block Architecture of the Friction Circle Method**

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Taylor's Stability Chart Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given: c, φ, β, γ] --> B{Slope Geometry}
    B --> C[Compute Slope Angle β]
    C --> D{φ = 0?}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Use Toe/Base Curve<br/>Ns = 5.52 for β ≤ 53°"]
    D -- "No" --> F[Enter Taylor's Chart at β and φ]
    F --> G[Read Ns from Chart]
    E --> G
    G --> H["Hc = c / γ Ns"]
    G --> I["F = c / γH Ns"]
    H --> J{Actual H < Hc?}
    J -- "Yes" --> K[Slope is Safe]
    J -- "No" --> L[Reduce H or Strengthen Soil]
    I --> M{F >= 1.5?}
    M -- "Yes" --> K
    M -- "No" --> L
```

**Figure 4.4 — Step-by-Step Chart Interpretation for Taylor's Stability Number**

---

## 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram — Method Selection Criteria

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Select Stability Method] --> B{Soil Type}
    B -- "Purely Cohesive φ = 0" --> C1[Swedish Circle - φ = 0]
    B -- "Purely Cohesionless c = 0" --> C2[Infinite Slope Method]
    B -- "Mixed c-φ" --> C3[Friction Circle Method]

    C1 --> D1[Use Stability Chart<br/>Ns = 5.52 base case]
    C2 --> D2[Use F = tan φ / tan β]
    C3 --> D3[Use Friction Circle<br/>+ Taylor's Chart]

    D1 --> E1[Output: Critical Height / FoS]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
```

**Figure 4.5 — Method Selection Flow Chart for Slope Stability Problems**

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (Part A — 3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define **Taylor's Stability Number**. What is its significance in slope stability analysis?

**Model Answer:**

**Taylor's Stability Number (Nₛ)** is a **dimensionless parameter** that represents the ratio of the available cohesive shear strength of the soil to the product of the unit weight of the soil, the height of the slope, and the factor of safety.

$$N_s = \frac{c}{\gamma H F}$$

**Significance:**

1. It enables the use of **precomputed stability charts** to determine the critical height of a slope without iterative calculations.
2. It **non-dimensionalizes** the slope stability problem, making the results applicable to slopes of any size.
3. It is a function of only the **slope angle (β)** and the **friction angle (φ)**, allowing rapid parametric studies.
4. It helps in identifying the **type of failure** (toe, base, or slope circle) based on the slope geometry.

> **Key Words for KTU Board:** Stability Number, dimensionless, critical height, factor of safety, Taylor's chart.

---

### **Question 2 (Part A — 3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between **toe failure** and **base failure** in slope stability analysis.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Toe Failure | Base Failure |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Failure Surface** | Passes **through the toe** of the slope | Passes **below the toe** of the slope |
| **Geometric Condition** | Slope angle **β > 53°** (homogeneous) | Slope angle **β < 53°** or soft stratum present |
| **Type of Soil** | Stiffer soils with finite depth | Soft foundation soil with weak stratum at depth |
| **Depth Factor Dₓ** | $D_x = \infty$ (or not relevant) | $D_x$ is finite and influences the analysis |
| **Stability Number** | Nₒ = 5.52 (for φ = 0 case) | Nₛ depends on Dₓ value |
| **Practical Occurrence** | Steep embankments, clay cuts | Embankments on soft alluvial deposits |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Many students forget to **convert units consistently** (e.g., using c in kPa and H in cm). Always check units first.
> - **Mistake 2:** Confusing the **stability number** (Nₛ) with the **factor of safety** (F). Nₛ is a function of β and φ; F is the actual safety factor.
> - **Mistake 3:** Using the **infinite slope formula (F = tan φ / tan β)** for cohesive soils. This formula is valid **only for c = 0 case**.
> - **Mistake 4:** Not **stating assumptions** (e.g., circular failure surface, plane strain, etc.). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> - **Mistake 5:** Mixing up **toe failure** and **base failure** formulas. Use **Nₒ = 5.52** only when the toe circle is critical and the slope angle is ≤ 53°.

---

### **Question A (Part B — 14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q:** **(a)** A slope of height 12 m is made at an angle of 45° with the horizontal. The soil has the following properties: c = 25 kN/m², φ = 15°, and γ = 19 kN/m³. Using Taylor's Stability Number approach, determine the **factor of safety** against slope failure. Is the slope safe as per IS code recommendations? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Friction Circle Method** in detail. How is the failure surface modeled in this method, and what are the key assumptions? Compare it briefly with the Swedish Circle Method. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Given:**
- Height of slope, H = 12 m
- Slope angle, β = 45°
- Cohesion, c = 25 kN/m²
- Friction angle, φ = 15°
- Unit weight, γ = 19 kN/m³

**Step 1:** For β = 45° (≤ 53°) and φ = 15°, refer to Taylor's Stability Chart.

From Taylor's Stability Chart (or standard tabular values):

$$N_s \approx 0.18$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the factor of safety using:

$$F = \frac{c}{\gamma H N_s}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$F = \frac{25}{19 \times 12 \times 0.18}$$

$$F = \frac{25}{41.04}$$

$$F \approx 0.609$$

**Step 4:** Compare with IS Code recommendations:

Since F ≈ 0.61 < 1.5, the slope is **NOT SAFE**. The slope must be redesigned — either by reducing the slope angle, increasing the soil strength (soil improvement), or reducing the slope height.

> **Answer Summary:** F ≈ 0.61; slope is unsafe.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Friction Circle Method — Detailed Explanation:**

The Friction Circle Method is a **limit equilibrium method** for analyzing the stability of homogeneous earth slopes in **c-φ soils**. It was developed by **Taylor (1937)** as a refinement of the Swedish Circle Method.

**Key Features:**

1. **Failure Surface Model:** The failure surface is assumed to be a **logarithmic spiral** (log-spiral), defined by the polar equation:
$$r = r_0 \cdot e^{\theta \tan\phi}$$
where $r_0$ is the initial radius, $\theta$ is the polar angle, and $\phi$ is the soil friction angle. The log-spiral has the special property that the normal to the surface at every point passes through a single point called the **pole (O)**.

2. **Friction Circle:** A small circle of radius $R \sin\phi$ is drawn around the center of the failure surface. The line of action of the resultant reaction force on the failure surface is tangent to this friction circle.

3. **Assumptions:**
   - Homogeneous, isotropic soil.
   - Two-dimensional (plane strain) analysis.
   - Log-spiral failure surface passing through the toe.
   - Resultant reaction is radial and inclined at angle φ to the normal.
   - Cohesion acts as a constant tangential resistance.

4. **Equilibrium:** Moment equilibrium is satisfied about the pole O:

$$\Sigma M_{driving} = \Sigma M_{resisting}$$

$$W \cdot \bar{x} = c \cdot L_c \cdot R + \Sigma N \tan\phi \cdot R$$

5. **Factor of Safety:**

$$F = \frac{c \cdot L_c \cdot R + \tan\phi \cdot \Sigma N \cdot R}{\Sigma W \cdot \bar{x}}$$

**Comparison with Swedish Circle Method:**

| Aspect | Friction Circle Method | Swedish Circle Method |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Failure surface | Log-spiral | Circular arc |
| Soil type | c-φ soil | φ = 0 or c = 0 cases |
| Rigour | More rigorous | Simpler, approximate |
| Application | Earth dams, large slopes | Embankments, cut slopes |
| Inter-slice forces | Not considered (ordinary) | Not considered (ordinary) |

---

### **Question B (Part B — 14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q:** **(a)** A long natural slope in cohesionless soil is at an angle of 28° with the horizontal. The soil has an angle of internal friction φ = 32° and a unit weight of 17 kN/m³. There is a parallel seepage flow within the slope. Determine the **factor of safety** of the slope. If the slope was dry (no seepage), what would be the new factor of safety? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Taylor's Stability Chart**? Explain the procedure to use it for determining the critical height and factor of safety of a slope. What are the limitations of stability charts? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Given:**
- Slope angle, β = 28°
- Friction angle, φ = 32°
- Unit weight, γ = 17 kN/m³
- Condition: Steady seepage parallel to slope

**Step 1: Factor of Safety with seepage**

For an infinite slope in cohesionless soil with seepage parallel to the slope, the factor of safety is:

$$F_{seepage} = \frac{\gamma'}{\gamma_{sat}} \cdot \frac{\tan\phi}{\tan\beta}$$

where $\gamma'$ is the submerged unit weight and $\gamma_{sat}$ is the saturated unit weight. For a typical soil, $\gamma' \approx 0.5 \gamma_{sat}$ (assuming specific gravity G ≈ 2.65 and porosity n ≈ 0.4):

$$F_{seepage} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{\tan 32°}{\tan 28°} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{0.6249}{0.5317} = 0.5 \times 1.175 = 0.588$$

**Step 2: Factor of Safety without seepage (dry slope)**

$$F_{dry} = \frac{\tan\phi}{\tan\beta} = \frac{\tan 32°}{\tan 28°} = \frac{0.6249}{0.5317} = 1.175$$

**Step 3: Comparison**

- $F_{seepage} \approx 0.59$ → Slope is **UNSAFE** with seepage.
- $F_{dry} \approx 1.18$ → Slope is **MARGINALLY SAFE** when dry.

> **Answer Summary:** Seepage reduces the factor of safety by 50%. The slope is unsafe with seepage and marginally safe when dry.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Taylor's Stability Chart:**

Taylor's Stability Chart is a **graphical tool** that presents the relationship between:

- **Stability Number Nₛ** (on the y-axis)
- **Slope angle β** (on the x-axis)
- **Friction angle φ** (as a family of curves)

**Procedure for Use:**

**Step 1:** Identify the slope angle β and friction angle φ from the problem.

**Step 2:** Enter the chart at β on the x-axis and move vertically to the curve corresponding to the given φ.

**Step 3:** Read the value of Nₛ on the y-axis.

**Step 4:** To find the **critical height**, use:

$$H_c = \frac{c}{\gamma N_s}$$

**Step 5:** To find the **factor of safety** for an actual height H, use:

$$F = \frac{c}{\gamma H N_s}$$

**Step 6:** Compare F with the desired safety factor (1.5 for permanent slopes).

**Limitations of Stability Charts:**

1. **Limited parameters:** Most charts assume homogeneous soil, which is rarely the case in natural slopes.
2. **No seepage effects:** Basic Taylor's chart does not account for pore water pressure or seepage forces.
3. **2D analysis only:** Charts are based on plane-strain assumptions, ignoring 3D end effects.
4. **Specific failure surface:** Assumes circular (or log-spiral) failure surface, which may not be the critical surface in stratified soils.
5. **No seismic effects:** Basic charts do not include earthquake-induced inertial forces.
6. **Approximate results:** Charts provide initial estimates and should be verified with rigorous numerical methods (e.g., FEM, limit analysis).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 3 — Slope Stability**

- **Slope stability** is evaluated using the **Factor of Safety (F)**, defined as the ratio of available shear strength to mobilized shear stress.
- **Three types of failure**: Toe Failure, Base Failure, Slope (Face) Failure.
- **Critical slope angle** for toe vs. base failure is approximately **β = 53°**.
- **Swedish Circle Method (φ = 0)** assumes a **circular failure surface** and uses the **stability number Nₒ = 5.52** for toe failure in purely cohesive soil.
- **Swedish Circle Method (c = 0)** reduces to the **infinite slope analysis** with **F = tan φ / tan β**.
- **Friction Circle Method** uses a **logarithmic spiral** failure surface and is more rigorous for c-φ soils.
- **Taylor's Stability Number (Nₛ)** is defined as **c / (γH F)** and is a function of **β, φ, and Dₓ** only.
- **Critical Height** of a slope: **H_c = c / (γ Nₛ)**.
- **Seepage** reduces the factor of safety by approximately **50%** for cohesionless slopes.
- **Required FoS** for permanent works: **1.5**; for temporary works: **1.2 to 1.3**.
- **Assumptions** (circular surface, 2D analysis, homogeneous soil) must be **explicitly stated** in all KTU answers.
- **Taylor's Stability Chart** is used to find Nₛ for given β and φ, then to compute H_c or F.
- **Bishop's Simplified Method** and **Spencer's Method** are advanced extensions that account for inter-slice forces.
- **Limitations of charts**: No seepage, no seismic, no 3D effects, assumes homogeneous soil.
- **Real-world applications**: Earth dams, embankments, hill slopes, open-pit mines, reclamation works.
- **For Kerala context**: Stability analysis is critical in **Wayanad, Idukki, and Kottayam districts** where landslides are frequent during monsoons.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** Always draw a neat **sketch of the slope and the failure surface** before writing equations. Examiners appreciate visual clarity, and it helps you avoid arithmetic errors. Quote the **stability number value (Nₛ = 5.52)** explicitly when using the toe-circle formula for φ = 0 case.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definitions Related to Foundations and Bearing Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Definitions Related to Foundations and Bearing Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Foundations — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundation (KTU 2024 Definition):** A foundation is the **part of a structure** that transmits the load of the superstructure to the underlying soil or rock. It is the **structural interface** between the superstructure (above ground) and the supporting earth (below ground).

In geotechnical engineering, the foundation is designed such that:
1. The **bearing capacity failure** of the soil is prevented.
2. The **settlement** (especially differential settlement) remains within tolerable limits.
3. The foundation does not suffer **scour, uplift, or lateral instability**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Table on a Soft Mat"

Imagine you place a heavy **dining table** on a **soft foam mattress**.

- If you place the **table legs directly on the mattress**, the legs punch into the foam (think of it as a *shallow foundation* on weak soil — it fails by punching shear).
- If you place a **thick wooden plank (footing)** under the legs, the load spreads over a larger area, reducing pressure and preventing failure (this is the working principle of a *spread/shallow footing*).
- If the mattress is *extremely soft*, no matter how large the plank, you cannot prevent sinking. You would then drive a **long pole (pile)** through the mattress until it reaches the firm floor underneath. This is the principle of a **deep foundation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The choice between a *shallow* and a *deep* foundation is governed by the **depth-to-width ratio (D/B)** of the foundation and the **stratigraphy** of the soil profile.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Foundations (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

Foundations are broadly classified into two categories:

| **Category** | **Definition** | **Typical D/B Ratio** | **Examples** |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Shallow Foundation** | Foundation placed near the ground surface where the depth of embedment (D) is less than or equal to the width (B) of the footing | $D \le B$ | Spread footings, isolated footings, combined footings, strap (cantilever) footings, mat/raft foundations |
| **Deep Foundation** | Foundation that transfers load to deeper, stronger strata by means of skin friction and/or end bearing | $D > B$ (typically $D \ge 4B$) | Pile foundations, pier foundations, well foundations (caissons), drilled shafts |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (Precise):** A foundation is classified as **shallow** when the depth of foundation $D$ is less than or equal to the width $B$ ($D \le B$). When $D > B$ and the load is transferred through a combination of **end bearing** and **skin friction** to deeper strata, it is termed a **deep foundation**.

---

### 1.4 Bearing Capacity — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bearing Capacity of Soil:** The maximum pressure (load per unit area) that the soil can support without undergoing **shear failure** and without experiencing **undue settlement** that would compromise the structural integrity of the foundation.

The bearing capacity is fundamentally a **stress**, expressed in units of **kN/m²** or **kPa**.

---

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — "The Crumbling Sand Castle"

Picture a child building a sandcastle. As more sand is added:
- Initially, the sand supports the weight easily.
- At some **critical load**, the sand grains suddenly slip along curved surfaces, and the castle collapses.
- This critical pressure (force ÷ area of the castle base) is the **ultimate bearing capacity**.

Now, an engineer would never design a structure at the verge of collapse. Instead, they apply a **Factor of Safety (FoS)** — typically **3.0** for bearing capacity — to obtain a much smaller **safe/allowable bearing pressure**.

---

### 1.6 The Seven Canonical Bearing Capacity Definitions (KTU High-Yield)

These definitions are the **single most tested concept** in this module. Every KTU question begins from here.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ultimate Bearing Capacity ($q_u$ or $q_{ult}$):** The **maximum gross pressure** at which the soil fails in shear. At this pressure, a large increment of settlement occurs with **no increment of load**. It includes the overburden pressure. **Unit:** $kN/m^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity ($q_{nu}$):** The **net pressure** (i.e., $q_u$ minus the overburden pressure at the foundation level) that causes shear failure. It is the pressure *in addition to* the existing overburden. **Unit:** $kN/m^2$.
> $$q_{nu} = q_u - q$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Gross Safe Bearing Capacity ($q_s$ or $q_{safe}$):** The **gross allowable pressure** that the soil can safely support **without risk of shear failure**. It is obtained by dividing $q_u$ by a suitable **Factor of Safety (FoS)**, typically **3.0**.
> $$q_s = \frac{q_u}{FS}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Safe Bearing Capacity ($q_{ns}$ or $q_{safe,net}$):** The **net pressure** (above overburden) that the soil can safely support. It is $q_{nu}$ divided by FoS.
> $$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{FS} = \frac{q_u - q}{FS}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Gross Allowable Bearing Pressure ($q_{all}$ or $q_{a,gross}$):** The **maximum gross pressure** that the soil can support **safely**, considering both **shear failure** AND **settlement** criteria. The value is the **smaller** of (a) shear-based safe bearing capacity and (b) settlement-based allowable pressure.
> $$q_{all} = \min\left(\frac{q_u}{FS},\ q_{sett}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Allowable Bearing Pressure ($q_{na}$ or $q_{a,net}$):** The **net pressure** (above overburden) that the soil can support safely, considering both criteria.
> $$q_{na} = q_{all} - q$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Allowable Bearing Pressure (Pressure Net of Overburden):** Often used interchangeably with $q_{ns}$, especially when comparing with the **net applied pressure** from the structure.

---

### 1.7 The "Pressing a Coin into a Soft Cake" Analogy

When you press a coin into a soft cake:
- The **gross pressure** = (force of finger + weight of coin) ÷ coin area.
- The **net pressure** = (force of finger only) ÷ coin area — i.e., the *additional* pressure beyond what the cake surface was already experiencing.
- The **ultimate pressure** is when the cake finally cracks along a slip surface.
- The **safe pressure** is the engineer-approved fraction of that ultimate value.

---

### 1.8 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Unit weight of water:** $\gamma_w = 9.81\ kN/m^3$ (often taken as **10 kN/m³** in KTU problems).
- **Standard Factor of Safety (FoS) for bearing capacity:** $FS = 3.0$.
- **Standard FoS for settlement-based design:** $FS = 2.0$ to $3.0$.
- **Depth factor influence zone:** Up to approximately **2B** below the foundation for stress distribution (2:1 method) or up to the **active failure wedge**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Foundations Are Needed — The "Why" Behind the Design

A foundation is required because of three primary soil-structure interaction problems:

1. **Shear Failure** — Soil has finite shear strength ($\tau_f$). When contact pressure exceeds a critical threshold, shear surfaces develop and the soil mass fails as a wedge/sliding block.
2. **Settlement** — Even below the failure threshold, soil compresses (consolidates or undergoes immediate settlement). Excessive *differential* settlement causes cracking of the superstructure.
3. **Ground Stability** — Lateral loads, seismic events, scour, and buoyancy (uplift) must be resisted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** About 60–70% of all structural failures originate from *foundation/soil-related* issues, not the superstructure. The KTU examiner expects you to know that **both bearing capacity AND settlement** must be checked.

---

### 2.2 Shallow vs Deep — The Decision Logic

The decision tree is governed by:

- **Shallow Stratum is Strong Enough** → Use shallow foundation (cheaper, faster).
- **Shallow Stratum is Weak/Compressible** → Either improve the soil (geosynthetics, compaction, grouting) OR bypass it using a **deep foundation** (pile/pier).

> **Rule of Thumb:** If the depth to a *firm bearing stratum* is greater than **4B to 5B**, deep foundations are typically economical. Otherwise, shallow foundations are preferred.

---

### 2.3 Modes of Shear Failure in Soil (KTU 2024 — Critical Concept)

When a footing is progressively loaded, three distinct failure modes can be observed, depending on the **relative density** of the soil and the **compressibility** of the failure zone. This classification was first formalized by **Vesic (1963)** and is heavily tested in KTU.

| **Failure Mode** | **Soil Type** | **Failure Surface** | **Settlement at Failure** | **Heave** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **General Shear Failure** | Dense/stiff soil, low compressibility | Distinct, well-defined slip surface from edge of footing to ground surface | Small | Significant, sudden tilting |
| **Local Shear Failure** | Medium-dense/medium-stiff soil, moderate compressibility | Slip surface develops *partially*, does not reach ground surface | Moderate | Moderate, less dramatic |
| **Punching Shear Failure** | Loose/soft soil, high compressibility | No visible slip surface; soil is squeezed out vertically beneath the footing | Large, gradual | Negligible |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Three Modes?** The actual failure mode is controlled by the **relative density $I_D$** (for sands) or the **OCR/consistency index** (for clays). Vesic proposed the use of the **relative compressibility** of the soil-foundation system, characterized by the **rigidity index $I_r$**, to predict the failure mode.

---

### 2.4 The "Digging a Trench in Sand" Analogy

Imagine pressing a small metal plate into sand:

- **Dry, dense sand (general shear):** The plate sinks slightly, then *cracks* the surface — you see clear heave on either side and the sand bulges dramatically. The failure is sudden and visible.
- **Medium sand (local shear):** The plate sinks, some bulging occurs, but you don't see a clean crack — the failure is *partial* and more gradual.
- **Saturated, loose sand (punching shear):** The plate just keeps sinking slowly, with no visible surface disturbance — failure is *progressive* and vertical.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Bearing Capacity — Shallow Foundations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Bearing Capacity Equation (General Form):**
> All modern bearing capacity equations (Terzaghi, Meyerhof, Hansen, Vesic, IS 6403) follow the general three-term form:
> $$q_u = c \cdot N_c \cdot s_c \cdot d_c \cdot i_c + q \cdot N_q \cdot s_q \cdot d_q \cdot i_q + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma \cdot s_\gamma \cdot d_\gamma \cdot i_\gamma$$
> Where: $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = **bearing capacity factors** (function of $\phi$ only); $s$ = **shape factors**; $d$ = **depth factors**; $i$ = **inclination (load) factors**.

---

### 2.6 Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Equation (1943) — KTU Most Tested

Terzaghi assumed a **strip footing** ($L/B \to \infty$) with a rough base, located at depth $D$. He developed three zones of failure: **triangular wedge (Zone I)**, **radial shear zone (Zone II)**, and **passive Rankine zone (Zone III)**.

For a **strip footing (continuous footing, L ≥ 5B)**:

| **Soil Type** | **Ultimate Bearing Capacity Equation** |
|:---|:---|
| **Cohesive (c–$\phi$) soil** (General case) | $$q_u = c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$$ |
| **Cohesionless soil** ($c = 0$) | $$q_u = q \cdot N_q + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$$ |
| **Purely cohesive soil** ($\phi = 0$, $c = c_u$) | $$q_u = c_u \cdot N_c + q = 5.7 \cdot c_u + q$$ |

where $q = \gamma \cdot D$ = **surcharge (overburden) pressure at the level of the foundation base**.

---

### 2.7 Bearing Capacity Factors (Terzaghi)

These factors are **functions of the soil's angle of internal friction $\phi$** only.

$$N_q = \frac{e^{2\pi \tan\phi}}{2\cos^2\left(45^\circ + \phi/2\right)} \quad \text{(Reissner's exact value, 1924)}$$

$$N_c = \cot\phi \cdot \left(N_q - 1\right) \quad \text{(Prandtl-Reissner)}$$

$$N_\gamma = \tfrac{1}{2} \left(\tan^2\left(45^\circ + \phi/2\right) \cdot e^{\pi \tan\phi} - 1\right) \cdot \tan\left(1.4\phi\right) \quad \text{(approximate, per IS code)}$$

---

### 2.8 General Form for All Footing Shapes (Terzaghi — Simplified)

| **Footing Type** | **Ultimate Bearing Capacity** |
|:---|:---|
| **Strip ($L/B \to \infty$)** | $$q_u = c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$$ |
| **Square ($L = B$)** | $$q_u = 1.3 \cdot c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.4 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$$ |
| **Circular ($B = D_{f,footing}$)** | $$q_u = 1.3 \cdot c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.3 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$$ |
| **Rectangular ($L \ne B$)** | $$q_u = c \cdot N_c \cdot \left(1 + 0.3B/L\right) + q \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma \cdot \left(1 - 0.2B/L\right)$$ |

---

### 2.9 Local Shear Failure Correction (Terzaghi)

For **local shear failure**, Terzaghi recommended modifying the soil parameters:

$$\phi_{mod} = \tan^{-1}\left(0.67 \cdot \tan\phi\right) \quad ; \quad c_{mod} = 0.67 \cdot c$$

Then compute $N_c', N_q', N_\gamma'$ using $\phi_{mod}$.

---

### 2.10 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Terzaghi's equation** is used for **preliminary design** of shallow foundations (simple, but conservative for rectangular footings).
- **Meyerhof's, Hansen's, Vesic's** equations include **shape, depth, and inclination factors** — used for **final/refined design** (KTU advanced modules).
- **IS 6403:1981 (Reaffirmed 2021)** is the **Indian Standard** code of practice, used widely in Indian consulting practice. It uses SPT $N$-values to estimate $c$ and $\phi$.
- The equations are used to **size the footing** so that $q_{applied} \le q_{allowable}$.

---

### 2.11 Quick-Reference Decision Flowchart (Conceptual)

> **Step 1:** Determine $c$, $\phi$, $\gamma$ of the foundation soil.
> **Step 2:** Identify the failure mode (General / Local / Punching).
> **Step 3:** Compute bearing capacity factors $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ from $\phi$.
> **Step 4:** Compute $q_u$ using the appropriate equation.
> **Step 5:** Compute $q_{na} = (q_u - q) / FS$ with $FS = 3.0$.
> **Step 6:** Compute $q_{na,settlement}$ from settlement criteria.
> **Step 7:** $q_{all,net} = \min(q_{na,bearing},\ q_{na,settlement})$.
> **Step 8:** Design footing area such that $q_{applied,net} \le q_{all,net}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Equation — General Shear Failure under a Strip Footing

#### 3.1.1 Assumptions (Terzaghi, 1943)

1. The footing is a **strip footing** of width $B$, infinite length $L \to \infty$, located at depth $D$.
2. The base of the footing is **rough** (friction is mobilized at the contact).
3. The soil is **homogeneous, isotropic**, and obeys the **Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion**.
4. The **overburden** above the base level is replaced by an **equivalent surcharge** $q = \gamma \cdot D$.
5. **General shear failure** occurs — the failure surface reaches the ground surface.

#### 3.1.2 Three Failure Zones

The failure mechanism consists of three zones:

- **Zone I (Elastic wedge):** A triangular wedge directly beneath the footing, bounded by the base and inclined at angle $\phi$ to the horizontal. The wedge moves *downward* with the footing.
- **Zone II (Radial shear zone):** A log-spiral (radial shear) zone on either side of Zone I, where each radial line makes angle $\phi$ with the normal to the curve.
- **Zone III (Passive Rankine wedge):** A triangular passive wedge that is pushed *upward*, heaving the ground surface.

#### 3.1.3 Equilibrium of Elastic Wedge (Zone I)

The wedge moves downward with the footing. The forces acting on the wedge are:

- **Weight of wedge:** $W = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot B \cdot D_f \cdot \gamma$ (per unit length of footing)
- **Vertical load from footing:** $Q_u = q_u \cdot B$
- **Surcharge on top:** $q \cdot B$
- **Normal and shear forces on inclined faces** (inclined at $\phi$ with horizontal)

Resolving forces vertically and applying the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion along the inclined planes, the contribution from the **cohesion term $c$** and the **surcharge term $q$** can be evaluated.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of $N_q$ (Reissner, 1924 — Prandtl-Reissner Mechanism)

Consider a *weightless* soil ($c$ only, $\gamma = 0$) supporting a smooth strip footing with surcharge $q$.

The failure surface (Prandtl-Reissner) consists of:
- A **triangular wedge** beneath the footing (angle $45^\circ + \phi/2$ with horizontal).
- A **log-spiral transition zone**.
- A **passive Rankine zone** at angle $45^\circ - \phi/2$ from horizontal.

Equating work done by external load to internal energy dissipation along the slip surface:

$$\text{Work by surcharge } q = \text{ Energy dissipated along slip surfaces}$$

This yields the closed-form expression:

$$
N_q = \frac{e^{2\pi \tan\phi}}{2\cos^2\left(45^\circ + \phi/2\right)}
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation** for $\phi = 30^\circ$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan 30^\circ &= 0.5774 \\
2\pi \tan\phi &= 3.627 \\
e^{3.627} &= 37.62 \\
\cos(45^\circ + 15^\circ) &= \cos 60^\circ = 0.5 \\
2 \cdot (0.5)^2 &= 0.5 \\
N_q &= \frac{37.62}{0.5} = 75.24
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches standard KTU reference tables.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of $N_c$ (Prandtl-Reissner)

By superposition, the $N_c$ factor is derived by considering a *cohesive* soil ($c$ only, $\phi \ne 0$, $\gamma = 0$, $q = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= \cot\phi \cdot \left(N_q - 1\right) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $\phi = 30^\circ$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cot 30^\circ &= 1.7321 \\
N_c &= 1.7321 \cdot (75.24 - 1) = 1.7321 \cdot 74.24 = 128.6
\end{aligned}
$$

For **$\phi = 0$** (purely cohesive soil, $\phi_u = 0$), the limit of $N_c$ as $\phi \to 0$ is:

$$
N_c = \lim_{\phi \to 0} \left[ \cot\phi \cdot \left(N_q - 1\right) \right] = 5.14 \text{ (smooth)} \quad \text{or} \quad 5.7 \text{ (rough base, Terzaghi)}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of $N_\gamma$

For a *cohesionless* soil ($c = 0$) under self-weight ($\gamma \ne 0$, $q = 0$), Terzaghi's analysis yields:

$$
N_\gamma = \frac{1}{2}\left[\tan^2\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) \cdot e^{\pi \tan\phi} - 1\right] \cdot \tan\left(1.4\phi\right) \quad \text{(approximate, as per Meyerhof / IS 6403)}
$$

**For $\phi = 30^\circ$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan^2(45^\circ + 15^\circ) &= \tan^2(60^\circ) = 3 \\
e^{\pi \cdot 0.5774} &= e^{1.814} = 6.13 \\
\frac{1}{2}(3 \cdot 6.13 - 1) &= \frac{1}{2}(17.39) = 8.70 \\
\tan(1.4 \cdot 30^\circ) &= \tan 42^\circ = 0.9004 \\
N_\gamma &= 8.70 \cdot 0.9004 = 7.83
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.5 Complete Derivation — Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A strip footing of width $B = 2.0\ m$ is founded at depth $D = 1.5\ m$ in a soil with $c = 15\ kN/m^2$, $\phi = 30^\circ$, $\gamma = 18\ kN/m^3$. Determine $q_u$, $q_{nu}$, and $q_{ns}$ using $FS = 3.0$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Bearing capacity factors (for $\phi = 30^\circ$):

$$
N_c = 30.14, \quad N_q = 18.40, \quad N_\gamma = 22.40
$$

*(Note: These are Terzaghi's original tabulated values. They differ from Prandtl-Reissner values because Terzaghi assumed a rough base.)*

**Step 2:** Surcharge at base level:

$$
q = \gamma \cdot D = 18 \times 1.5 = 27.0 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Step 3:** Ultimate bearing capacity (strip footing):

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma \\
&= 15 \cdot 30.14 + 27 \cdot 18.40 + 0.5 \cdot 18 \cdot 2.0 \cdot 22.40 \\
&= 452.10 + 496.80 + 403.20 \\
&= 1352.10 \ kN/m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Net ultimate bearing capacity:

$$
q_{nu} = q_u - q = 1352.10 - 27.0 = 1325.10 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Step 5:** Net safe bearing capacity:

$$
q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{FS} = \frac{1325.10}{3.0} = 441.70 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Step 6:** Gross safe bearing capacity:

$$
q_s = \frac{q_u}{FS} = \frac{1352.10}{3.0} = 450.70 \ kN/m^2
$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Failure Mode Selection

| **Soil Descriptor** | **$I_D$ (Sand)** / **OCR (Clay)** | **Failure Mode** | **Modification** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Very dense sand, stiff clay | $I_D > 0.70$, OCR > 4 | **General Shear** | Use $\phi$ directly |
| Medium-dense sand, stiff-firm clay | $0.30 < I_D < 0.70$, $2 < OCR < 4$ | **Local Shear** | $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(0.67 \tan\phi)$, $c_m = 0.67 c$ |
| Loose sand, soft clay | $I_D < 0.30$, OCR < 2 | **Punching Shear** | Use reduced factors; design by settlement |

---

### 3.7 Summary Table — All Bearing Capacity Definitions with Sample Values

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Numerical Value (from §3.5 example)** |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| Surcharge at base | $q$ | $\gamma \cdot D$ | $27.0\ kN/m^2$ |
| Ultimate bearing capacity | $q_u$ | $cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$ | $1352.10\ kN/m^2$ |
| Net ultimate | $q_{nu}$ | $q_u - q$ | $1325.10\ kN/m^2$ |
| Gross safe | $q_s$ | $q_u / FS$ | $450.70\ kN/m^2$ |
| Net safe | $q_{ns}$ | $q_{nu} / FS$ | $441.70\ kN/m^2$ |
| Gross allowable (bearing) | $q_{a,gross}$ | $\min(q_s, q_{sett})$ | $\le 450.70\ kN/m^2$ |
| Net allowable (bearing) | $q_{a,net}$ | $q_{a,gross} - q$ | $\le 423.70\ kN/m^2$ |
