## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
Define the following with proper units:
(a) Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity
(b) Net Safe Bearing Capacity
(c) Gross Allowable Bearing Pressure

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity ($q_{nu}$):** It is the *net* pressure at the base of the foundation (i.e., in addition to the overburden pressure $q = \gamma D$) at which the underlying soil fails in shear. It is computed as $q_{nu} = q_u - q$. **Unit:** $kN/m^2$.

**(b) Net Safe Bearing Capacity ($q_{ns}$):** It is the *net* pressure that the soil can safely support without risk of shear failure. It is obtained by dividing $q_{nu}$ by a suitable Factor of Safety ($FS$, typically 3.0): $q_{ns} = q_{nu} / FS$. **Unit:** $kN/m^2$.

**(c) Gross Allowable Bearing Pressure ($q_{all}$):** It is the *maximum gross* pressure that the soil can support safely, considering **both shear failure and settlement** criteria. It is the *smaller* of (i) safe bearing capacity from shear ($q_u/FS$) and (ii) allowable pressure from settlement ($q_{sett}$). **Unit:** $kN/m^2$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**
Differentiate between **General Shear Failure**, **Local Shear Failure**, and **Punching Shear Failure** in foundations.

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **General Shear** | **Local Shear** | **Punching Shear** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Soil Type** | Dense/stiff | Medium-dense/medium-stiff | Loose/soft |
| **Failure Surface** | Distinct, reaches ground | Partial, may not reach surface | Vertical, no visible slip |
| **Settlement at Failure** | Small and sudden | Moderate | Large and gradual |
| **Heave at Surface** | Significant, visible | Moderate | Negligible |
| **Modification in Design** | Use $\phi$ directly | Use $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(0.67\tan\phi)$ | Design by settlement |

**General Shear Failure** is observed in **dense sand or stiff clay**; the failure surface is clearly defined and the footing tilts suddenly. **Local Shear Failure** occurs in **medium-dense sand or firm clay**; failure is partial. **Punching Shear Failure** is observed in **loose sand or soft clay**, characterized by vertical penetration without surface heave.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Bearing Capacity Calculation — Strip Footing**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question, Modified]**

A **strip footing** of width $B = 2.5\ m$ is to be constructed at a depth $D = 1.5\ m$ in a soil with the following properties:
- Cohesion, $c = 20\ kN/m^2$
- Angle of internal friction, $\phi = 25^\circ$
- Unit weight, $\gamma = 19\ kN/m^3$

**(a)** [7 Marks] Determine the **ultimate** and **net ultimate** bearing capacities using Terzaghi's equation. Use bearing capacity factors $N_c = 25.13$, $N_q = 12.72$, $N_\gamma = 9.01$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Determine the **gross safe**, **net safe**, and **net allowable** bearing capacities assuming a Factor of Safety of **3.0** and a settlement-based allowable net pressure of $250\ kN/m^2$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Ultimate Bearing Capacity:**

Given:
- $B = 2.5\ m$, $D = 1.5\ m$, $c = 20\ kN/m^2$, $\phi = 25^\circ$, $\gamma = 19\ kN/m^3$
- $N_c = 25.13$, $N_q = 12.72$, $N_\gamma = 9.01$

**Step 1:** Compute surcharge at foundation level:

$$
q = \gamma \cdot D = 19 \times 1.5 = 28.5 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Terzaghi's strip footing equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma \\
&= 20 \times 25.13 + 28.5 \times 12.72 + 0.5 \times 19 \times 2.5 \times 9.01 \\
&= 502.60 + 362.52 + 213.99 \\
&= 1079.11 \ kN/m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Net ultimate bearing capacity:

$$
q_{nu} = q_u - q = 1079.11 - 28.5 = 1050.61 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Answer (a):** $q_u = 1079.11\ kN/m^2$; $q_{nu} = 1050.61\ kN/m^2$.

---

**Part (b) — Safe and Allowable Bearing Capacities:**

**Step 1:** Gross safe bearing capacity:

$$
q_s = \frac{q_u}{FS} = \frac{1079.11}{3.0} = 359.70 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Step 2:** Net safe bearing capacity:

$$
q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{FS} = \frac{1050.61}{3.0} = 350.20 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Step 3:** Net allowable bearing capacity (using settlement criterion):

$$
q_{na,sett} = 250 \ kN/m^2 \quad \text{(given)}
$$

**Step 4:** Net allowable bearing capacity (bearing criterion):

$$
q_{na,bearing} = q_{ns} = 350.20 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Step 5:** Net allowable (governing = smaller):

$$
q_{na} = \min(350.20, 250) = 250 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Step 6:** Gross allowable bearing capacity:

$$
q_{all} = q_{na} + q = 250 + 28.5 = 278.5 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Answer (b):**
- Gross safe: $q_s = 359.70\ kN/m^2$
- Net safe: $q_{ns} = 350.20\ kN/m^2$
- Net allowable (governing): $q_{na} = 250\ kN/m^2$ (settlement-controlled)
- Gross allowable: $q_{all} = 278.5\ kN/m^2$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-A):**
> 1. **Don't forget the surcharge term** — many students omit $q \cdot N_q$, leading to ~30% mark loss.
> 2. **Net vs Gross confusion** — when the problem gives $q_{applied}$, check whether it's *net* or *gross* before comparison.
> 3. **Settlement criterion is often the governing factor** — students who only check bearing capacity miss the controlling criterion. Always take the **minimum** of the two.
> 4. **Unit consistency** — $B$ and $D$ must be in **metres**, $\gamma$ in **$kN/m^3$**, results in **$kN/m^2$**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Bearing Capacity with Local Shear Failure Correction**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Question, Modified]**

A **square footing** of size $2.0\ m \times 2.0\ m$ is placed at depth $1.0\ m$ in a sandy soil with:
- $c = 0$, $\phi = 35^\circ$ (general shear condition)
- $\gamma = 17.5\ kN/m^3$
- Factor of Safety $= 3.0$

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compute the **ultimate** and **net safe** bearing capacities using Terzaghi's equation. Use $N_c = 46.12$, $N_q = 33.30$, $N_\gamma = 45.23$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] If **local shear failure** is suspected, recompute the values using the modified parameters $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(0.67 \tan 35^\circ) = 25.27^\circ$ with corresponding factors $N'_c = 24.95$, $N'_q = 12.50$, $N'_\gamma = 8.40$. Comment on the design implications.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — General Shear Condition:**

**Step 1:** Surcharge:

$$
q = \gamma \cdot D = 17.5 \times 1.0 = 17.5 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Step 2:** Square footing equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= 1.3 \cdot c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.4 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma \\
&= 1.3 \times 0 \times 46.12 + 17.5 \times 33.30 + 0.4 \times 17.5 \times 2.0 \times 45.23 \\
&= 0 + 582.75 + 633.22 \\
&= 1215.97 \ kN/m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Net ultimate:

$$
q_{nu} = q_u - q = 1215.97 - 17.5 = 1198.47 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Step 4:** Net safe:

$$
q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{FS} = \frac{1198.47}{3.0} = 399.49 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Answer (a):** $q_u = 1215.97\ kN/m^2$; $q_{ns} = 399.49\ kN/m^2$.

---

**Part (b) — Local Shear Failure Condition:**

**Step 1:** Modified factors with $\phi_m = 25.27^\circ$:

$N'_c = 24.95$, $N'_q = 12.50$, $N'_\gamma = 8.40$.

**Step 2:** Surcharge (same):

$$
q = 17.5 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Step 3:** Ultimate bearing capacity (local shear, square footing):

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u' &= 1.3 \cdot c \cdot N'_c + q \cdot N'_q + 0.4 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N'_\gamma \\
&= 0 + 17.5 \times 12.50 + 0.4 \times 17.5 \times 2.0 \times 8.40 \\
&= 218.75 + 117.60 \\
&= 336.35 \ kN/m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Net ultimate (local shear):

$$
q'_{nu} = 336.35 - 17.5 = 318.85 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Step 5:** Net safe (local shear):

$$
q'_{ns} = \frac{318.85}{3.0} = 106.28 \ kN/m^2
$$

**Step 6:** Comparison:

$$
\frac{q'_{ns}}{q_{ns}} = \frac{106.28}{399.49} \approx 0.266 \quad (\text{only } 27\% \text{ of general shear capacity!})
$$

**Design Implication:** If local shear failure occurs instead of general shear, the allowable bearing capacity drops to **~27%** of the value computed assuming general shear. The designer must either:
- Increase the footing size significantly (larger $B$),
- Use a deeper foundation,
- Or improve the soil (compaction, vibro-replacement, etc.).

**Answer (b):** $q'_u = 336.35\ kN/m^2$; $q'_{ns} = 106.28\ kN/m^2$. **Design must be governed by the local shear condition**, leading to a much larger footing area.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-B):**
> 1. **Shape factor for square footing = 1.3** for the $N_c$ term; **0.4** for the $N_\gamma$ term. Students often confuse these with the strip footing coefficients.
> 2. **Local shear modification** ($\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(0.67 \tan\phi)$) is **mandatory** when soil is loose/medium-dense. Skipping it = 4 marks lost.
> 3. **Commentary on design implication** is essential for full marks in KTU. Don't just compute numbers — interpret them.
> 4. **Use $c = 0$ directly** in the equation when soil is cohesionless. Don't write $c \cdot N_c = 0$ term — just skip it.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Definitions Related to Foundations and Bearing Capacity**

- **Foundation** transmits load from superstructure to soil.
- **Shallow foundation:** $D \le B$; **Deep foundation:** $D > B$ (typically $D \ge 4B$).
- **Ultimate bearing capacity ($q_u$):** Maximum *gross* pressure causing shear failure. **Includes** overburden.
- **Net ultimate ($q_{nu}$):** $q_u - q$, where $q = \gamma D$ is the surcharge.
- **Gross safe ($q_s$):** $q_u / FS$, with $FS = 3.0$ standard.
- **Net safe ($q_{ns}$):** $q_{nu} / FS$.
- **Allowable bearing pressure** = $\min$(shear-based safe value, settlement-based value).
- **Net allowable ($q_{na}$)** = $q_{all} - q$.
- **Three failure modes:** General Shear (dense), Local Shear (medium), Punching Shear (loose) — per Vesic's classification.
- **Local shear correction:** $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(0.67 \tan\phi)$, $c_m = 0.67 c$.
- **Bearing capacity factors** are functions of $\phi$ alone:
  - $N_q = \frac{e^{2\pi \tan\phi}}{2\cos^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)}$ (Reissner)
  - $N_c = \cot\phi(N_q - 1)$ (Prandtl-Reissner)
  - $N_\gamma$ (approximate) $\approx \frac{1}{2}[\tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2) \cdot e^{\pi\tan\phi} - 1]\tan(1.4\phi)$ (Meyerhof/IS 6403).
- **Terzaghi's strip equation:** $q_u = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$.
- **Square footing:** $q_u = 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.4\gamma B N_\gamma$.
- **Circular footing:** $q_u = 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.3\gamma B N_\gamma$.
- **Rectangular footing:** $q_u = cN_c(1 + 0.3B/L) + qN_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma(1 - 0.2B/L)$.
- **Purely cohesive soil ($\phi = 0$):** $N_c = 5.7$ (Terzaghi, rough) or $5.14$ (Prandtl).
- **Always include the surcharge term** in $q_u$ computation unless explicitly told otherwise.
- **Check BOTH bearing capacity AND settlement** — the smaller governs.
- **Standard FoS:** 3.0 for bearing capacity, 2.0–3.0 for settlement.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In Part A questions, definitions carry **3 marks each** — state the formula AND the unit. In Part B, always show the calculation of $q_u$ first, then derive $q_{nu}$, then $q_{ns}$, then check against settlement. **Marks are awarded for sequential steps**, not just the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Depth of Foundation - Rankine's Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Minimum Depth of Foundation - Rankine's Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Depth of Foundation (Rankine's Analysis):**
> The minimum depth of foundation, as formulated by **William John Macquorn Rankine (1857)**, is the shallowest depth at which a footing can be placed in a **cohesionless (granular) soil** such that the **ultimate bearing capacity of the soil at the foundation level** is **not less than the safe bearing capacity of the soil at the ground surface**. It is derived from Rankine's classical earth pressure theory and is exclusively applicable to **dry, non-cohesive, isotropic soils** with a horizontal ground surface.

The minimum depth $D_f$ is mathematically expressed as:

$$D_f = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma} \left( \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} \right)^2$$

where $q_s$ is the safe bearing pressure of the soil at ground level, $\gamma$ is the unit weight of soil, and $\phi$ is the angle of internal friction of the soil.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Underground Iceberg" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are placing a heavy table on a sandy beach. If you place the legs directly on the loose, dry surface sand, the table will wobble and sink — because the top layer is too weak to carry the load. However, if you dig the legs deep enough into the denser, compacted sand below, the table becomes stable. The depth at which the sand becomes "as strong as the safe load you intend to place" is conceptually the **minimum depth of foundation**.
>
> Rankine asked a brilliant question: *At what depth is the natural ground strong enough to carry the same load that the undisturbed surface soil can safely carry?* This depth is the minimum you MUST dig, regardless of structural or frost considerations.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values Used in KTU Board Problems:**
> - Unit weight of dry sand, $\gamma_d$ : **15–18 kN/m³**
> - Unit weight of moist sand, $\gamma$ : **17–20 kN/m³**
> - Unit weight of saturated sand, $\gamma_{sat}$ : **18–22 kN/m³**
> - Angle of internal friction, $\phi$ for sand : **28° – 40°**
> - Factor of Safety (FS) for bearing capacity : **3 (standard IS code value)**

### 1.4 Why This Concept Matters in KTU Examinations

Rankine's analysis is a **favourite 14-mark and 7-mark topic** in the KTU 2024 Scheme. The examiner frequently tests:
1. The ability to **re-derive** the formula from first principles.
2. The ability to compute $D_f$ numerically given $q_s$, $\gamma$, and $\phi$.
3. The ability to **critically compare** Rankine's method with modern IS code (IS 6403 / IS 8009) methods.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework of Rankine's Theory

Rankine's analysis rests on **three logical pillars**:

1. **Pillar 1 — Earth Pressure Coefficient ($K_p$):**
   Rankine defined the **passive earth pressure coefficient** for a cohesionless soil as:
   
   $$K_p = \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}$$
   
   This coefficient governs how the soil resists lateral movement — it is the "passive resistance factor."

2. **Pillar 2 — Ultimate Bearing Capacity at Depth $D_f$:**
   Rankine proposed that the **ultimate bearing capacity** of a strip footing at depth $D_f$ (assuming weightless soil above the base) equals the **passive resistance** developed in the soil mass at that depth:
   
   $$q_u = \gamma \cdot D_f \cdot K_p$$

3. **Pillar 3 — Equating Strength to Demand:**
   The fundamental Rankine postulate: *The soil at the foundation level must have a strength equal to the safe load-bearing demand of the surface soil.* Therefore:
   
   $$q_u = q_s$$
   
   This equates the bearing capacity at depth to the safe bearing pressure at the surface, and solving for $D_f$ yields the minimum depth.

### 2.2 Underlying Assumptions of Rankine's Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite — State these assumptions verbatim in 14-mark answers:**
> 1. Soil is **homogeneous**, **semi-infinite**, **dry**, and **cohesionless**.
> 2. The **ground surface is horizontal** (no slope).
> 3. The footing is a **continuous (strip) footing** of infinite length.
> 4. The **failure surface is planar** (a key simplification — Terzaghi later improved this with a curved log-spiral surface).
> 5. The **soil above the base of the foundation is weightless** and offers no shear strength contribution.
> 6. The soil at the base of the foundation fails by **general shear failure**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Passive Earth Pressure Coefficient | $K_p$ | $\dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}$ | Dimensionless | Always $\geq 1$ for granular soil |
| Active Earth Pressure Coefficient | $K_a$ | $\dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$ | Dimensionless | Always $\leq 1$ |
| Ultimate Bearing Capacity (Rankine) | $q_u$ | $\gamma \cdot D_f \cdot K_p$ | kN/m² or kPa | Weightless soil assumption above base |
| **Minimum Depth of Foundation (Rankine)** | $D_f$ | $\dfrac{q_s}{\gamma} \cdot K_a^2$ | m | $\vert K_a \vert = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$ |
| Depth with Factor of Safety | $D_f$ | $\dfrac{q_s \cdot FS}{\gamma} \cdot K_a^2$ | m | Conservative design version |
| Depth using Net Safe Bearing Capacity | $D_f$ | $\dfrac{q_{ns}}{\gamma} \cdot K_a^2$ | m | $q_{ns} = q_s - \gamma D_f$ (iterative) |
| Rankine Form of $N_q$ Factor | $N_q$ | $K_p = \left( \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} \right)^2$ | Dimensionless | Rankine's $N_q$ equals $K_p$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **How Rankine's $D_f$ is Used** |
|---|---|
| Building Foundations on Sand | Ensures footings bypass loose, organic topsoil |
| Highway Embankments on Granular Soils | Verifies bearing capacity for shallow bridge abutments |
| Transmission Tower Footings | Quickly estimates minimum embedment for guyed towers |
| Rural Residential Construction | Used by village engineers when lab testing is unavailable |
| Seismic Zone Foundation Design | Acts as the *baseline* depth before adding seismic factors |

### 2.5 Critical Limitations of Rankine's Method

> [!WARNING]
> **Why Rankine is Conservative (and Slightly Over-Predicts Depth):**
> 1. It **ignores the self-weight** of soil above the footing base, which actually contributes to bearing capacity.
> 2. It assumes a **planar failure surface**, but real soil failure is curved (log-spiral).
> 3. It is valid **only for cohesionless soils** (sand/gravel). It cannot be used for clays.
> 4. It does **not account for footing width** (B), which significantly influences bearing capacity.
> 5. The **factor of safety is not built into the formula** explicitly, leading to under-conservative values in some interpretations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rankine's Minimum Depth of Foundation

**Step 1: Define the Rankine Passive Earth Pressure Coefficient.**

The classical Rankine theory (1857) established that for a cohesionless, dry, granular soil with a horizontal ground surface, the passive earth pressure coefficient is:

$$K_p = \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}$$

> **Logic:** When a footing pushes the soil outward, the soil resists with a "passive" reaction. $K_p$ is the multiplier that converts the vertical effective stress into horizontal passive resistance. Higher $K_p$ means the soil is stiffer and resists displacement more strongly.

**Step 2: Express the Ultimate Bearing Capacity at Depth $D_f$.**

For a strip footing at depth $D_f$, Rankine proposed that the ultimate bearing capacity is generated by the **weight of the soil prism** directly beneath the footing pushing the surrounding soil into passive failure:

$$q_u = \gamma \cdot D_f \cdot K_p$$

> **Logic:** The soil column of height $D_f$ and unit weight $\gamma$ produces a vertical stress $\gamma D_f$ at the footing base. This vertical stress is amplified by the passive resistance factor $K_p$ to yield the maximum load the soil can sustain at that depth.

**Step 3: Apply the Rankine Equivalence Condition.**

The governing principle of Rankine's minimum depth analysis is that the bearing capacity at the foundation level must **at least equal the safe bearing pressure** that the undisturbed ground surface can sustain:

$$q_u = q_s$$

> **Logic:** This is the philosophical anchor of the method. Rankine believed that the *deeper soil* should not be weaker than the *shallower soil*. Otherwise, one could simply build on the shallower soil and skip excavation entirely.

**Step 4: Substitute the Bearing Capacity Expression.**

Replace $q_u$ in the equivalence condition:

$$\gamma \cdot D_f \cdot K_p = q_s$$

> **Logic:** Substituting the expression from Step 2 into the equality from Step 3 is the pivotal algebraic step.

**Step 5: Isolate the Minimum Depth $D_f$.**

Divide both sides by $\gamma \cdot K_p$:

$$D_f = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma \cdot K_p}$$

> **Logic:** This expresses the depth as a function of the demand ($q_s$), the soil weight ($\gamma$), and the passive resistance factor ($K_p$).

**Step 6: Substitute $K_p$ in Terms of $\phi$.**

Substitute the trigonometric expression for $K_p$:

$$D_f = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{1}{K_p} = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}}$$

> **Logic:** Inverting the fraction flips numerator and denominator.

**Step 7: Simplify the Fraction.**

The reciprocal of $K_p$ is:

$$\dfrac{1}{K_p} = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = K_a$$

> **Logic:** This is precisely the Rankine **active** earth pressure coefficient, $K_a$. Rankine's elegant result: the minimum depth uses the *active* coefficient, not the passive one.

**Step 8: Final Rankine Minimum Depth Formula.**

The final expression is:

$$D_f = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma} \cdot \left( \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} \right)^2$$

> **Logic:** Squaring captures the fact that the bearing capacity relationship involves $K_p$, while the depth formula must use its inverse, $K_a^2$.

**Alternative Form — With Factor of Safety:**

If the safe bearing capacity $q_s$ is provided in terms of ultimate capacity with a factor of safety, $q_s = q_u / FS$, then:

$$D_f = \dfrac{q_s \cdot FS}{\gamma} \cdot \left( \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} \right)^2$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Verification via $N_q$ Notation

The modern bearing capacity equation (after Terzaghi, Meyerhof, IS 6403) expresses ultimate capacity as:

$$q_u = c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$$

For cohesionless soil ($c = 0$) and weightless soil above the base (the Rankine assumption), this reduces to:

$$q_u = \gamma D_f \cdot N_q$$

By Rankine's theory, the bearing capacity factor is:

$$N_q = K_p = \left( \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} \right)^2$$

Equating $q_u = q_s$:

$$\gamma D_f \cdot N_q = q_s$$

Solving:

$$D_f = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma \cdot N_q} = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{1}{N_q} = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma} \cdot \left( \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} \right)^2$$

> **Logic:** This confirms that Rankine's $N_q$ factor is *identical* to the passive earth pressure coefficient, validating the formula through an independent theoretical route.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A strip footing is to be constructed on a dry sandy soil. The safe bearing capacity of the soil at the ground surface is $q_s = 150$ kN/m². The unit weight of soil is $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, and the angle of internal friction is $\phi = 30°$. Determine the **minimum depth of foundation** as per Rankine's analysis.

**Given Data:**
- $q_s = 150$ kN/m²
- $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³
- $\phi = 30°$
- $FS = 3$ (assumed; using $q_s$ directly)

**Step 1: Compute $\sin\phi$.**

$$\sin 30° = 0.500$$

**Step 2: Compute the Rankine Active Coefficient Components.**

$$1 - \sin\phi = 1 - 0.500 = 0.500$$

$$1 + \sin\phi = 1 + 0.500 = 1.500$$

**Step 3: Compute $K_a$ and $K_a^2$.**

$$K_a = \dfrac{0.500}{1.500} = 0.3333$$

$$K_a^2 = (0.3333)^2 = 0.1111$$

**Step 4: Apply the Minimum Depth Formula.**

$$D_f = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma} \cdot K_a^2 = \dfrac{150}{18} \cdot 0.1111$$

$$D_f = 8.333 \cdot 0.1111 = 0.9259 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Final Answer with Engineering Practicality.**

$$D_f \approx 0.93 \text{ m}$$

For practical construction, this is rounded up to **$D_f = 1.0$ m** to account for frost depth, scour, and workmanship tolerances.

> **Note:** The KTU board generally awards full marks for a final answer in the range of $0.90$ m to $1.00$ m, with 1 mark reserved for unit specification and engineering commentary.

---

### 3.4 Summary Table of Key Numerical Values of $K_a^2$

| **$\phi$ (degrees)** | **$\sin\phi$** | **$K_a$** | **$K_a^2$** | **$1/K_p = K_a$** | **Depth Factor: $K_a^2$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 20 | 0.342 | 0.490 | 0.240 | 0.490 | 0.240 |
| 25 | 0.423 | 0.406 | 0.165 | 0.406 | 0.165 |
| 28 | 0.469 | 0.361 | 0.130 | 0.361 | 0.130 |
| 30 | 0.500 | 0.333 | 0.111 | 0.333 | 0.111 |
| 32 | 0.530 | 0.307 | 0.094 | 0.307 | 0.094 |
| 35 | 0.574 | 0.271 | 0.073 | 0.271 | 0.073 |
| 38 | 0.616 | 0.238 | 0.057 | 0.238 | 0.057 |
| 40 | 0.643 | 0.217 | 0.047 | 0.217 | 0.047 |

> **Logic:** Notice that as $\phi$ increases (denser, stronger sand), $K_a^2$ decreases, which means **the required minimum depth is lower**. This is physically intuitive: dense sand has higher bearing capacity, so a shallower footing is acceptable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Rankine Minimum Depth Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Parameters: qs, gamma, phi] --> B[Compute sin phi from phi]
    B --> C[Compute Ka numerator: 1 minus sin phi]
    B --> D[Compute Ka denominator: 1 plus sin phi]
    C --> E[Ka equals num by den]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute Ka squared]
    F --> G[Compute Df by formula: Df = qs by gamma times Ka squared]
    A --> G
    G --> H{Df less than frost depth?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Adopt frost depth as design depth]
    H -->|No| J{Df less than scour depth?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Adopt scour depth as design depth]
    J -->|No| L[Adopt computed Df as final depth]
    L --> M[Add 10 percent construction tolerance]
    I --> M
    K --> M
    M --> N[Final Design Depth: Df design]
```

> **Logic:** The flowchart captures the complete engineering decision tree: compute the Rankine depth, then **verify it against other constraints** (frost penetration, scour depth, water table). The *largest* of these governs the final design.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture — The Rankine Analysis Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Subsystem]
        Q1[qs: kN per m2]
        G1[gamma: kN per m3]
        P1[phi: degrees]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[Computation Subsystem]
        S1[Trigonometric Module]
        S2[Ka Calculator]
        S3[Ka squared Module]
        S4[Final Df Module]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Subsystem]
        R1[Minimum Depth: Df in m]
        R2[Final Adopted Depth]
        R3[Engineering Recommendation]
    end

    Q1 --> S4
    G1 --> S4
    S1 --> S2
    P1 --> S1
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
```

> **Logic:** This block architecture shows the *functional flow* of the Rankine analysis — inputs flow into the trigonometric engine, the result is squared and combined with the soil parameters, and the final design depth is output. The **engineering recommendation** step is essential: students frequently lose marks by not rounding the depth to a practical construction value (e.g., 0.5 m, 1.0 m, 1.5 m).

### 4.3 Schematic Description of the Physical System

> **Visual Description (to be sketched in examination):**
> ```
>          Ground Surface (Original)
>          =================================
>          ^   ^   ^   ^   ^   ^   ^   ^
>          |   |   |   |   |   |   |   |  <-- Removed soil (excavated)
>          |   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
>          =================================   <-- Base of Foundation (Depth Df)
>          |   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
>          |   |   LOAD P   |   |   |   |
>          |   |   on FOOTING   |   |   |
>          |   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
>          =================================   <-- Failure zone begins
>          |    Failure Surface (Planar)   |
>          |   Inclined at 45 + phi/2     |
>          =================================
> ```
> The footing is at depth $D_f$, carrying load $P$ per unit length. The soil immediately below the footing is in **passive Rankine state**, resisting the load through the mechanism of planar failure surfaces inclined at $(45° + \phi/2)$ to the horizontal.

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Rankine vs. IS Code Methods

| **Aspect** | **Rankine Analysis** | **IS 6403 Method** | **Terzaghi Method** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Soil Type | Cohesionless only | All soils | All soils |
| Failure Surface | Planar | Curved (log-spiral) | Curved (log-spiral) |
| Considers Footing Width | No | Yes | Yes |
| Considers Shape Factor | No | Yes | Yes |
| Considers Depth Factor | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Modern Acceptance | Historical baseline | Code-approved | Classical |
| Typical Use in KTU | Theory / 14-mark derivations | Numerical design | Numerical design |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**"State the assumptions of Rankine's analysis for minimum depth of foundation."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The assumptions of Rankine's analysis are:
1. The soil is **homogeneous, semi-infinite, dry, and cohesionless**.
2. The **ground surface is horizontal**.
3. The footing is a **strip (continuous) footing** of infinite length.
4. The **failure surface is planar**, inclined at $(45° + \phi/2)$ to the horizontal.
5. The **soil above the base of the foundation is weightless** and offers no shear resistance.
6. The soil at the base fails by **general shear failure**.

> **Allocation:** 1 mark for the first three assumptions, 1 mark for the next two, 1 mark for the final assumption and any one more.

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, May 2023]**
**"What is Rankine's passive earth pressure coefficient? State its formula."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Rankine passive earth pressure coefficient $K_p$ is the ratio of the horizontal passive stress to the vertical effective stress in a cohesionless soil when the soil is on the verge of being pushed outward (passive state). It is given by:

$$K_p = \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}$$

where $\phi$ is the angle of internal friction of the soil. $K_p$ is always **greater than or equal to 1**, and it increases rapidly as $\phi$ increases. For example, at $\phi = 30°$, $K_p = 3.0$.

> **Allocation:** 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the value/explanation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Derive the Rankine formula for the **minimum depth of foundation** in a cohesionless soil. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A strip footing is to be constructed on a dry sand deposit with the following properties:
- Safe bearing capacity, $q_s = 200$ kN/m²
- Unit weight of soil, $\gamma = 17.5$ kN/m³
- Angle of internal friction, $\phi = 32°$
- Factor of safety, $FS = 3$

Compute the **minimum depth of foundation** as per Rankine's analysis. Also state the final **recommended depth** for actual construction. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions (2 Marks):**
1. Soil is homogeneous, semi-infinite, dry, and cohesionless.
2. Ground surface is horizontal; footing is a strip footing of infinite length.
3. The soil above the base of the foundation is weightless.
4. Failure surface is planar, inclined at $(45° + \phi/2)$.
5. Failure occurs by general shear.

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Rankine's passive earth pressure coefficient is:

$$K_p = \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}$$

**Step 2:** The ultimate bearing capacity at depth $D_f$ is given by:

$$q_u = \gamma \cdot D_f \cdot K_p$$

**Step 3:** According to Rankine, the bearing capacity at the foundation level must equal the safe bearing capacity at the ground surface:

$$q_u = q_s$$

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$\gamma D_f \cdot K_p = q_s$$

**Step 5:** Isolating $D_f$:

$$D_f = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma \cdot K_p} = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{1}{K_p} = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$$

**Step 6:** Squaring both sides (since $K_p$ in the original bearing equation is squared):

$$D_f = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma} \cdot \left( \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} \right)^2$$

This is the **Rankine minimum depth formula**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $q_s = 200$ kN/m²
- $\gamma = 17.5$ kN/m³
- $\phi = 32°$
- $FS = 3$

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Compute $\sin 32°$:

$$\sin 32° = 0.5299$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute the bracketed ratio:

$$1 - \sin\phi = 1 - 0.5299 = 0.4701$$

$$1 + \sin\phi = 1 + 0.5299 = 1.5299$$

$$\dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \dfrac{0.4701}{1.5299} = 0.3072$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Square the ratio:

$$\left( \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} \right)^2 = (0.3072)^2 = 0.0944$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Compute minimum depth:

$$D_f = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma} \cdot \left( \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} \right)^2 = \dfrac{200}{17.5} \cdot 0.0944$$

$$D_f = 11.4286 \cdot 0.0944 = 1.0787 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Factor of safety correction (if asked):

$$D_f = \dfrac{q_s \cdot FS}{\gamma} \cdot 0.0944 = \dfrac{200 \cdot 3}{17.5} \cdot 0.0944 = 34.2857 \cdot 0.0944 = 3.236 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Final recommendation:

For typical KTU board problems, the answer is **$D_f = 1.08$ m** (without FS) or **$D_f = 3.24$ m** (with FS). The recommended practical depth is to round up to **$D_f = 1.10$ m** or **$D_f = 3.25$ m** depending on which form is used. Always adopt the **larger** of (a) Rankine depth, (b) frost depth (1.0 m in Kerala), and (c) scour depth (if applicable).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Sample Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **physical significance** of Rankine's analysis for minimum depth of foundation. How does the formula account for the strength of granular soil? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two alternative sites are being considered for a building:
- **Site A:** $q_s = 120$ kN/m², $\gamma = 16$ kN/m³, $\phi = 28°$
- **Site B:** $q_s = 250$ kN/m³, $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³, $\phi = 35°$

Determine the **Rankine minimum depth** for each site. Comment on which site requires a **deeper foundation** and explain why. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Physical Significance (7 Marks):**

Rankine's analysis addresses a fundamental question in foundation engineering: *What is the minimum depth at which a footing must be placed to ensure that the soil at the foundation level is at least as strong as the soil at the surface?*

**Physical Significance (4 Marks):**
1. **Strength gradient with depth:** In granular soils, the bearing capacity increases with depth due to the increasing confining pressure from the overlying soil mass. Rankine's analysis provides a **quantitative** measure of this depth-strength relationship.

2. **Failure mechanism:** The analysis postulates that the soil beneath the footing is in a **passive Rankine state**, where the soil mass is pushed outward and downward, mobilizing the full passive resistance of the soil. The factor $K_p$ captures this mobilization.

3. **Equivalence principle:** The pivotal idea is that the *bearing capacity at depth* must equal the *safe bearing pressure at the surface*. This is essentially a **strength-equality condition** — the footing must reach a depth where the soil's load-carrying capacity matches the demand of the structure.

4. **Active coefficient emergence:** The mathematical elegance of Rankine's result is that the minimum depth uses the **active** earth pressure coefficient $K_a^2$, not the passive coefficient. This is because the formula inverts the $K_p$ that originally defined the bearing capacity.

**How the formula accounts for granular soil strength (3 Marks):**
- The term $\sin\phi$ inside the bracket represents the **frictional strength** of the soil grains.
- For dense sand (high $\phi$), the bracket ratio is small, leading to a **smaller** $D_f$ — the soil is strong, so shallower foundations suffice.
- For loose sand (low $\phi$), the bracket ratio is larger, leading to a **larger** $D_f$ — the soil is weak, so the footing must descend deeper to find adequate strength.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Comparison (7 Marks):**

**Site A:**
- $q_s = 120$ kN/m², $\gamma = 16$ kN/m³, $\phi = 28°$
- $\sin 28° = 0.4695$

**Step 1:** Compute bracket ratio:

$$\dfrac{1 - 0.4695}{1 + 0.4695} = \dfrac{0.5305}{1.4695} = 0.3611$$

**Step 2:** Square:

$$K_a^2 = (0.3611)^2 = 0.1304$$

**Step 3:** Compute $D_f$:

$$D_{f,A} = \dfrac{120}{16} \cdot 0.1304 = 7.5 \cdot 0.1304 = 0.978 \text{ m}$$

**Site B:**
- $q_s = 250$ kN/m², $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³, $\phi = 35°$
- $\sin 35° = 0.5736$

**Step 1:** Compute bracket ratio:

$$\dfrac{1 - 0.5736}{1 + 0.5736} = \dfrac{0.4264}{1.5736} = 0.2709$$

**Step 2:** Square:

$$K_a^2 = (0.2709)^2 = 0.0734$$

**Step 3:** Compute $D_f$:

$$D_{f,B} = \dfrac{250}{19} \cdot 0.0734 = 13.158 \cdot 0.0734 = 0.966 \text{ m}$$

**Comparison and Commentary (1 Mark):**
- **Site A:** $D_{f,A} \approx 0.98$ m
- **Site B:** $D_{f,B} \approx 0.97$ m

Both sites require **nearly the same minimum depth** (≈ 1.0 m), but for **different reasons**:
- Site A has **weaker, looser sand** (lower $\phi$) but a **lower load demand** ($q_s$).
- Site B has **stronger, denser sand** (higher $\phi$) but a **higher load demand** ($q_s$).

These two effects approximately cancel out, yielding similar minimum depths. From a practical standpoint, **Site B is preferred** because the higher $\phi$ implies **lower settlement** and better seismic performance, even though the depth is the same.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square** the bracket ratio: $K_a$ vs. $K_a^2$ — this single error causes a **3-mark deduction** in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Using degree-mode incorrectly**: $\sin 30° = 0.5$, but some students incorrectly use $\sin 30 = -0.988$. Always convert to radians or use a calculator in degree mode.
> 3. **Mixing up $K_a$ and $K_p$**: The minimum depth uses $K_a^2$ (or $1/K_p$), not $K_p$. A common error is writing $D_f = (q_s/\gamma) \cdot K_p^2$, which is dimensionally wrong.
> 4. **Not specifying units** in the final answer. Always write "$D_f = 0.93$ m" or "$D_f = 93$ cm" — not just "$D_f = 0.93$".
> 5. **Skipping the practical recommendation**: Always end with a line like "*The recommended depth for construction is rounded up to 1.0 m.*" Examiners award 1 mark for this.
> 6. **Omitting assumptions**: In derivation questions, listing assumptions is worth **2 marks**; skipping them costs heavily.
> 7. **Not commenting on limitations**: For 14-mark questions, a brief comment on the **limitations of Rankine's method** (e.g., valid only for cohesionless soils, ignores footing width) earns the **highest cognitive level (L5–L6)** marks per Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Rankine Minimum Depth**

**Core Formula (must memorize):**

$$D_f = \dfrac{q_s}{\gamma} \cdot \left( \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} \right)^2$$

**Key Symbols:**
- $D_f$ = Minimum depth of foundation (m)
- $q_s$ = Safe bearing capacity at ground surface (kN/m²)
- $\gamma$ = Unit weight of soil (kN/m³)
- $\phi$ = Angle of internal friction (degrees)
- $K_p$ = Rankine passive earth pressure coefficient $= (1 + \sin\phi)/(1 - \sin\phi)$
- $K_a$ = Rankine active earth pressure coefficient $= (1 - \sin\phi)/(1 + \sin\phi)$

**Five Critical Assumptions (verbatim for KTU answers):**
1. Cohesionless, dry, homogeneous, semi-infinite soil
2. Horizontal ground surface
3. Strip footing of infinite length
4. Planar failure surface at $(45° + \phi/2)$
5. Weightless soil above the base of foundation

**Six Quick Numerical Triggers:**
- At $\phi = 30°$, $K_a^2 = 1/9 = 0.1111$
- At $\phi = 35°$, $K_a^2 \approx 0.0734$
- At $\phi = 40°$, $K_a^2 \approx 0.0471$
- Higher $\phi$ → Lower $D_f$
- Higher $q_s$ → Higher $D_f$
- Higher $\gamma$ → Lower $D_f$

**Four Major Limitations:**
1. Valid only for cohesionless soils (NOT clays)
2. Ignores footing width ($B$)
3. Ignores shape and depth factors
4. Assumes planar (not curved) failure surface

**Practical Design Rule (post-Rankine):**
$$\boxed{D_{f,\text{design}} = \max\left( D_{f,\text{Rankine}},\ D_{f,\text{frost}},\ D_{f,\text{scour}},\ D_{f,\text{water table}} \right)}$$

**Comparison Snapshot:**
- **Rankine:** Theoretical / Historical / Cohesionless only
- **Terzaghi:** Empirical / Curved failure / All soils
- **Meyerhof:** General / Inclined factors / All soils
- **IS 6403:** Code-based / Indian Standard / All soils

**Common Numerical Values Used in KTU Problems:**
- $\gamma_{dry\ sand} = 15 - 18$ kN/m³
- $\gamma_{moist\ sand} = 17 - 20$ kN/m³
- $\phi_{loose\ sand} = 28° - 32°$
- $\phi_{dense\ sand} = 35° - 40°$
- FS = 3 (standard)

**Three Examiner's Pet Questions (most frequently asked):**
1. *"Derive Rankine's formula for minimum depth of foundation."* — appears in **70%** of past KTU papers.
2. *"What are the assumptions of Rankine's analysis?"* — appears in **60%** of papers.
3. *"A strip footing is to be placed on sand with $q_s$ = X, $\gamma$ = Y, $\phi$ = Z. Find $D_f$."* — appears in **50%** of papers.

> **Final Word:** Rankine's analysis is a **cornerstone of foundation engineering theory**. While modern codes (IS 6403) use more sophisticated methods, the Rankine formula remains the **conceptual foundation** upon which all modern bearing capacity theory is built. Master it, and you master the philosophy of bearing capacity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Bearing Capacity Failures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Bearing Capacity Failures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Bearing Capacity Failure** of a shallow foundation is defined as the catastrophic collapse of the underlying soil mass when the applied load per unit area exceeds the ultimate bearing capacity ($q_u$) of the foundation soil. The mode in which the soil beneath the foundation mobilizes its shear strength and fails is termed the **Type of Bearing Capacity Failure**.

According to **Terzaghi (1943)**, **Vesic (1963)**, and later **IS 6403:1981 (Reaffirmed 2021)** classifications, the failure mechanism is broadly categorized into three principal modes, governed by the relative density of sand and the compressibility of the foundation soil:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Definition (Use verbatim in answers):**
> *"The nature of the bearing capacity failure of a foundation depends on the relative compressibility of the soil and the depth-to-width ratio of the footing. Three classical modes are General Shear Failure, Local Shear Failure, and Punching Shear Failure."*

The three types are:
1. **General Shear Failure (GSF)**
2. **Local Shear Failure (LSF)**
3. **Punching Shear Failure (PSF)**

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **pressing your thumb into a tray of three different food items** to understand the failure modes:

- **General Shear Failure — Pressing your thumb firmly into a firm, cold Dal (lentil paste):** The surface bulges up sharply on both sides, and a clean, well-defined slip surface forms from the edge of your thumb down to the surface. There is a **sudden tilting** and the failure is **catastrophic** — happens in one go. This is what happens in **dense sand** or **stiff, overconsolidated clay**.

- **Local Shear Failure — Pressing your thumb into a soft, medium-thick Idli batter:** The surface bulges slightly, but the slip surface is **not well-defined** — it gets "lost" in the middle. Failure is **progressive**, not sudden. The thumb goes down steadily. This represents **medium-dense sand** or **medium stiff clay**.

- **Punching Shear Failure — Pressing your thumb into a glass of loose, watery Horlicks:** Your thumb simply **sinks straight down** vertically. There is **no surface bulge, no tilting, no visible slip line**. The soil flows around the edges vertically. This mimics **loose sand** or **soft, normally consolidated clay**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Tilt-Bulge-Punch" Mnemonic for Exams:**
> - **G**eneral Shear = **T**ilting (sudden tilt)
> - **L**ocal Shear = **B**ulging partially
> - **P**unching Shear = **P**iercing straight down

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard test reference:** Plate Load Test (as per **IS 1888:1982**), with plate size typically **$300 \text{ mm} \times 300 \text{ mm}$** or **$750 \text{ mm}$ diameter**.
- **Relative Density ($I_D$) thresholds (from Terzaghi & Peck):**
  - Dense sand: $I_D > 0.67$ → GSF
  - Medium sand: $0.33 < I_D < 0.67$ → LSF
  - Loose sand: $I_D < 0.33$ → PSF
- **Typical failure load unit:** $q_u$ in **$\text{kN/m}^2$** or **$\text{kPa}$**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Type 1 — General Shear Failure (GSF)

**Why and How it happens:**
- Occurs in **dense sand** (relative density $I_D \geq 0.67$) or **stiff, overconsolidated clay** ($c_u > 100 \text{ kN/m}^2$).
- The footing penetrates only a **small depth** (typically $< 10\%$ of width $B$) before failure.
- The load-settlement curve shows a **distinct peak** ($q_u$) followed by a sudden drop, after which settlement continues at constant load (residual strength).
- A **continuous failure surface** develops from one edge of the footing, curves down, and emerges at the ground surface on the other side, causing **heave** of adjacent soil.

**Visual features observed in field/lab:**
- Tilting of foundation accompanied by heave of surrounding ground.
- Audible sound and shock waves just before failure.
- Sudden, brittle failure — high load-carrying capacity.

### 2.2 Type 2 — Local Shear Failure (LSF)

**Why and How it happens:**
- Occurs in **medium-dense sand** or **clay of medium stiffness**.
- The failure surface is **partially developed** — it starts at the footing edge but gets terminated within the soil mass (does not reach the ground surface).
- Only **slight bulging** of soil adjacent to the footing is observed; no visible heave.
- Load-settlement curve has **no well-defined peak**; failure is **progressive**.
- The ultimate load is reached at a settlement of about **$15\%$ to $25\%$ of footing width $B$**.

**Terzaghi's Modification for LSF:**
Terzaghi suggested using **reduced shear strength parameters** ($c'$ and $\phi'$) and hence **modified bearing capacity factors** ($N_c'$, $N_q'$, $N_\gamma'$):

$$c'_{\text{mod}} = \frac{2}{3} \, c \qquad \text{and} \qquad \tan \phi'_{\text{mod}} = \frac{2}{3} \, \tan \phi$$

The corresponding bearing capacity factors $N_c'$, $N_q'$, $N_\gamma'$ are evaluated at the modified angle $\phi_{\text{mod}}$.

### 2.3 Type 3 — Punching Shear Failure (PSF)

**Why and How it happens:**
- Occurs in **loose sand** ($I_D < 0.33$) or **soft, normally consolidated clay** ($c_u < 50 \text{ kN/m}^2$).
- Failure occurs by **vertical shear** around the footing perimeter, with soil being compressed beneath the foundation.
- **No heave at the surface**; the footing simply sinks.
- The load-settlement curve is **steeply descending** with very large settlements; no peak value.
- Settlement at failure is **excessive** — typically $30\%$ to $50\%$ of footing width $B$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-yield, board-exam-ready summary** of the key bearing capacity formulas and failure-type parameters.

**Table 1: Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Equation for Strip Footing (General Shear Failure)**

| Term | Formula | Meaning |
|------|---------|---------|
| Ultimate Bearing Capacity | $q_u = c \, N_c + q \, N_q + 0.5 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$ | Total ultimate load per unit area |
| Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity | $q_{nu} = q_u - q = q_u - \gamma \, D_f$ | Useful for design |
| Net Safe Bearing Capacity | $q_{ns} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{F} + \gamma \, D_f$ | With factor of safety $F$ |

where $N_c$, $N_q$, $N_\gamma$ are **Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors** depending only on the angle of internal friction $\phi$ (for GSF).

**Table 2: Modified Bearing Capacity Factors for Local Shear Failure**

| Factor | Relationship to GSF Factor |
|--------|------------------------------|
| $N_c'$ | Use $\tan \phi_{\text{mod}} = \tfrac{2}{3}\tan \phi$ in standard $N_c$ table |
| $N_q'$ | Use $\tan \phi_{\text{mod}} = \tfrac{2}{3}\tan \phi$ in standard $N_q$ table |
| $N_\gamma'$ | Use $\tan \phi_{\text{mod}} = \tfrac{2}{3}\tan \phi$ in standard $N_\gamma$ table |

**Table 3: Quick Reference — Distinguishing Parameters**

| Parameter | General Shear | Local Shear | Punching Shear |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|----------------|
| Soil type | Dense sand / stiff clay | Medium sand / medium clay | Loose sand / soft clay |
| $I_D$ | $\geq 0.67$ | $0.33$ – $0.67$ | $< 0.33$ |
| Settlement at $q_u$ | Small ($\approx 7\% B$) | Moderate ($15\% B$ – $25\% B$) | Large ($> 30\% B$) |
| Failure surface | Well-defined | Partially defined | Not visible (vertical) |
| Heave at surface | Significant | Slight | None |
| Nature of failure | Sudden, brittle | Progressive | Gradual sinking |
| Peak on $q$–$S$ curve | Sharp peak | No clear peak | No peak |

**Table 4: Vesic's Classification Based on SPT/CPT**

| SPT $N$ value | CPT $q_c$ (MPa) | Type of Failure |
|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| $> 30$ | $> 4$ | General Shear |
| $10$ – $30$ | $1$ – $4$ | Local Shear |
| $< 10$ | $< 1$ | Punching Shear |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundation Design:** The type of failure dictates the choice of **Factor of Safety (FOS)**. GSF permits a lower FOS (typically $F = 3$) compared to PSF (typically $F = 4$ to $5$).
- **Predicting Settlement vs. Collapse:** Punching shear failure is a **settlement-controlled design**, not a collapse design — meaning the structure may never collapse, but serviceability is lost.
- **Plate Load Test Interpretation:** Engineers must verify whether the test result is a true $q_u$ or merely a settlement-limited value (often mistaken for $q_u$ in PSF soils).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Decision Tree for Failure Type

The following step-by-step logic is the **standard KTU Board Examination** method for identifying the type of failure from given data:

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Flow for "Given a soil, find failure type":**
>
> **Step 1:** Identify the soil type (sand or clay).
> **Step 2:** For sand — obtain the relative density $I_D$ or SPT $N$ value.
> **Step 3:** For clay — obtain the unconfined compressive strength $q_u$ or undrained cohesion $c_u$.
> **Step 4:** Apply the threshold criteria (Vesic's Table 4 above).
> **Step 5:** Conclude the failure type and its associated behavior.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Equation (General Shear)

Terzaghi (1943) was the first to develop a comprehensive theoretical equation for the ultimate bearing capacity of strip footings on a **cohesionless** ($c = 0$) and **cohesive-frictional** ($c - \phi$) soil. The derivation proceeds from **Prandtl's (1920) solution for a metal punch penetrating a weightless material**, modified by **Reissner (1924)** to include surcharge, and finally extended by Terzaghi to include the soil's **self-weight** term.

**Assumptions of Terzaghi's Theory:**

1. The footing is of **strip type** (length $L \gg$ width $B$).
2. The soil is **homogeneous** and **isotropic**.
3. The footing is **rough** at the base (no slip between footing and soil).
4. The ground surface is **horizontal**.
5. Failure occurs by **general shear** with well-defined failure surfaces.
6. Passive earth pressure acts on either side of the wedge beneath the footing.
7. The soil is considered to be of **semi-infinite extent**.

**Failure Zones (Zone-by-Zone Description):**

Beneath a strip footing at general shear failure, three distinct zones are observed:

- **Zone I (Elastic wedge / Triangular zone):** The soil directly under the footing acts as part of the footing itself. Bounded by the base of the footing and two lines inclined at an angle $\phi$ with the horizontal. This wedge stays in **elastic equilibrium**.
- **Zone II (Radial shear zone / Log-spiral zone):** A zone of **radial shear** bounded by an arc of a **logarithmic spiral**. This is where maximum shear stress concentration occurs.
- **Zone III (Passive Rankine zone):** The soil in this zone is pushed up and is in a state of **passive Rankine state of plastic equilibrium**. Bounded by straight lines at an angle $(45^\circ - \phi/2)$ with the horizontal.

**Step-by-Step Force Equilibrium Derivation:**

Let us set up the coordinate system. Let the footing be of width $B$ at depth $D_f$, and let $q = \gamma \, D_f$ be the surcharge load.

The total ultimate load $Q_u$ is the sum of three contributions:

$$Q_u = Q_c + Q_q + Q_\gamma$$

where:
- $Q_c$ = load due to **cohesion** of the soil.
- $Q_q$ = load due to **surcharge** $q = \gamma D_f$ above the footing base.
- $Q_\gamma$ = load due to the **weight of soil** in the failure wedge.

**Step 1: Cohesion Contribution ($Q_c$)**

By considering the cohesive resistance along the log-spiral failure surface, Prandtl (1920) showed that:

$$Q_c = c \, B \, N_c$$

where $N_c$ is the **cohesion bearing capacity factor** given by:

$$N_c = \cot \phi \left[ e^{\pi \tan \phi} \, \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2: Surcharge Contribution ($Q_q$)**

Reissner (1924) added the surcharge effect $q = \gamma D_f$ acting on the ground surface on both sides of the footing. The surcharge equivalent force contributes:

$$Q_q = q \, B \, N_q$$

where $N_q$ is the **surcharge bearing capacity factor** given by:

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan \phi} \, \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3: Self-Weight Contribution ($Q_\gamma$)**

Terzaghi approximated the soil in the failure zone as having a unit weight $\gamma$ and developed an **approximate** formula for the self-weight contribution:

$$Q_\gamma = 0.5 \, \gamma \, B^2 \, N_\gamma$$

where $N_\gamma$ is the **self-weight bearing capacity factor**, given by Terzaghi as:

$$N_\gamma = \frac{1}{2}\left( \frac{K_{p\gamma}}{\cos^2 \phi} - 1 \right) \tan \phi$$

with $K_{p\gamma}$ being an empirical coefficient obtained from the analysis of the failure wedge shape.

**Step 4: Ultimate Bearing Capacity (Per Unit Area)**

Dividing $Q_u$ by the footing area $B \times 1$ (unit length of strip footing):

$$q_u = \frac{Q_u}{B \cdot 1} = c \, N_c + q \, N_q + 0.5 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$$

This is the **Terzaghi General Bearing Capacity Equation** for a **strip footing** under **General Shear Failure** conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The factor $0.5$ in the third term is **only for a strip footing**. For square and circular footings, Terzaghi proposed **shape factors** of $0.4$ and $0.3$ respectively, with corresponding width/diameter terms:
>
> $$q_u \, (\text{square}) = 1.3 \, c \, N_c + q \, N_q + 0.4 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$$
>
> $$q_u \, (\text{circular}) = 1.3 \, c \, N_c + q \, N_q + 0.3 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Modified Equation for Local Shear Failure

Terzaghi observed that for soils exhibiting **local shear failure**, the actual mobilized shear strength parameters are less than the peak values because the failure surface does not fully develop. He empirically proposed reducing both the cohesion and the friction angle by a factor of $\frac{2}{3}$:

**Step 1:** Define the modified parameters:

$$c_m = \frac{2}{3} \, c \qquad \text{and} \qquad \phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left( \frac{2}{3} \, \tan \phi \right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the modified bearing capacity factors $N_c'$, $N_q'$, $N_\gamma'$ using the same equations as in Step 3.2, but substituting $\phi_m$ in place of $\phi$:

$$N_c' = \cot \phi_m \left[ e^{\pi \tan \phi_m} \, \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi_m}{2}\right) - 1 \right]$$

$$N_q' = e^{\pi \tan \phi_m} \, \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi_m}{2}\right)$$

$$N_\gamma' = \frac{1}{2}\left( \frac{K_{p\gamma}'}{\cos^2 \phi_m} - 1 \right) \tan \phi_m$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the same general form of the bearing capacity equation:

$$q_u' = c_m \, N_c' + q \, N_q' + 0.5 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma'$$

This is the **Terzaghi Local Shear Bearing Capacity Equation** for a strip footing.

### 3.4 Numerical Example: Identifying the Failure Type

**Problem Statement:** A sandy soil has a relative density $I_D = 0.45$ and SPT $N$ value of $18$. The foundation depth is $D_f = 1.2 \text{ m}$ and footing width $B = 2.0 \text{ m}$. Identify the most likely type of bearing capacity failure.

**Step 1: Soil Identification**
The soil is sand (cohesionless). The governing parameter is the relative density $I_D$ or SPT $N$ value.

**Step 2: Apply Vesic's Thresholds**

Since $0.33 < I_D = 0.45 < 0.67$, the sand is **medium-dense**.

Also, $10 < N = 18 < 30$ confirms **medium sand**.

**Step 3: Conclusion**
According to the **Vesic classification**, the failure type is **Local Shear Failure (LSF)**.

**Step 4: Recommended Design Approach**
Use the **modified Terzaghi equation** with $c_m = \frac{2}{3}c$ and $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2}{3}\tan \phi\right)$, and apply a **Factor of Safety $F = 3$ to $4$** to compute the safe bearing capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook for Numerical Problems:**
> Whenever you see "$I_D$" in a sand problem, immediately convert to a failure type using the thresholds $0.33$ and $0.67$. Whenever you see "$c_u < 50 \text{ kPa}$" in a clay problem, the answer is **Punching Shear Failure**.
