## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct short-answer conceptual questions, exactly as they appear in **KTU University Exam Part A**.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

*List the three types of bearing capacity failures in soils and state the soil condition in which each occurs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three types of bearing capacity failures as classified by Terzaghi and Vesic are:

1. **General Shear Failure (GSF):** Occurs in **dense sand** (relative density $I_D \geq 0.67$) or **stiff, overconsolidated clay** ($c_u > 100 \text{ kN/m}^2$). It is characterized by a well-defined failure surface, sudden tilting of the foundation, and heaving of the surrounding ground.

2. **Local Shear Failure (LSF):** Occurs in **medium-dense sand** ($0.33 < I_D < 0.67$) or **clay of medium stiffness** ($50 < c_u < 100 \text{ kN/m}^2$). The failure surface is only partially developed and does not reach the ground surface.

3. **Punching Shear Failure (PSF):** Occurs in **loose sand** ($I_D < 0.33$) or **soft, normally consolidated clay** ($c_u < 50 \text{ kN/m}^2$). Failure occurs by vertical shear around the foundation perimeter, with no surface heave.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

*What are the modifications suggested by Terzaghi for computing the ultimate bearing capacity of a strip footing on soil that exhibits local shear failure?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Terzaghi proposed using **reduced shear strength parameters** in the standard bearing capacity equation to account for the partial development of the failure surface in local shear failure. The modifications are:

1. **Modified Cohesion:**
$$c_m = \frac{2}{3} \, c$$

2. **Modified Friction Angle:**
$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left( \frac{2}{3} \, \tan \phi \right)$$

3. **Use of modified bearing capacity factors** $N_c'$, $N_q'$, $N_\gamma'$ obtained by substituting $\phi_m$ in the standard $N_c$, $N_q$, $N_\gamma$ tables.

The modified bearing capacity equation becomes:

$$q_u' = c_m \, N_c' + q \, N_q' + 0.5 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma'$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** In Module-end examinations, Part B questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice**. Students must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full. Each part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the three types of bearing capacity failures observed beneath shallow foundations. For each type, discuss the soil conditions, failure surface geometry, heave pattern, and load-settlement curve behavior. **[(7 marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

The ultimate bearing capacity of a shallow foundation may be mobilized through three distinct failure mechanisms, depending on the relative density of the soil and the depth-to-width ratio of the footing. These were systematically classified by **Terzaghi (1943)** and later refined by **Vesic (1963)**.

**(1) General Shear Failure (GSF):**

This is the classical failure mode first analyzed by **Prandtl (1920)** and **Reissner (1924)**.

- **Soil Condition:** Occurs in **dense sand** (relative density $I_D \geq 0.67$) or **stiff, overconsolidated clay** ($c_u > 100 \text{ kN/m}^2$).
- **Failure Surface:** A **continuous, well-defined failure surface** develops, starting from one edge of the footing, curving through a **logarithmic spiral zone** (radial shear), and emerging at the ground surface on the opposite side.
- **Heave Pattern:** **Significant heave** of the soil occurs on both sides of the footing, often causing a recognizable ridge at the ground surface.
- **Load-Settlement Behavior:** The load-settlement curve shows a **sharp, distinct peak** at the ultimate load $q_u$, followed by a sudden drop. The footing **tilts catastrophically** with very little prior settlement.
- **Settlement at Failure:** Approximately **$7\%$ of the footing width $B$**.

**(2) Local Shear Failure (LSF):**

This mode is intermediate between general and punching shear.

- **Soil Condition:** Occurs in **medium-dense sand** ($0.33 < I_D < 0.67$) or **clay of medium stiffness** ($50 < c_u < 100 \text{ kN/m}^2$).
- **Failure Surface:** The failure surface is **partially developed** — it begins at the footing edge but **terminates within the soil mass** and does not reach the ground surface.
- **Heave Pattern:** **Slight bulging** of the soil adjacent to the footing is observed, but no surface heave.
- **Load-Settlement Behavior:** The load-settlement curve shows **no clear peak**; failure is **progressive** and the ultimate load is reached at a larger settlement.
- **Settlement at Failure:** Approximately **$15\%$ to $25\%$ of $B$**.

**(3) Punching Shear Failure (PSF):**

This is the failure mode for very compressible or loose soils.

- **Soil Condition:** Occurs in **loose sand** ($I_D < 0.33$) or **soft, normally consolidated clay** ($c_u < 50 \text{ kN/m}^2$).
- **Failure Surface:** Failure occurs by **vertical shear** along a cylindrical surface around the footing perimeter. The soil directly beneath the footing is compressed vertically, and no classical slip surface develops.
- **Heave Pattern:** **No surface heave** at all; the soil immediately around the footing remains undisturbed.
- **Load-Settlement Behavior:** The load-settlement curve is **steeply descending** with no peak; the foundation continues to sink at a relatively constant rate.
- **Settlement at Failure:** Very large, often **$30\%$ to $50\%$ of $B$**, often considered a **serviceability failure** rather than a true collapse.

---

**Q. (b)** A strip footing of width $B = 2.0 \text{ m}$ is to be placed at a depth $D_f = 1.5 \text{ m}$ in a soil with $c = 15 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\phi = 30^\circ$, and $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$. The relative density of the sand is $I_D = 0.75$. Determine the type of failure and calculate the ultimate bearing capacity using Terzaghi's equation. **[(7 marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the Type of Failure**

Given $I_D = 0.75 \geq 0.67$, the soil is **dense sand**, hence the failure type is **General Shear Failure (GSF)**.

We use the standard Terzaghi equation **without** any modification of $\phi$ or $c$.

**Step 2: Read Bearing Capacity Factors for $\phi = 30^\circ$**

From Terzaghi's standard table for GSF at $\phi = 30^\circ$:

$$N_c = 37.2, \qquad N_q = 22.5, \qquad N_\gamma = 19.7$$

**Step 3: Compute the Surcharge**

$$q = \gamma \, D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Apply Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Equation (Strip Footing, GSF)**

$$q_u = c \, N_c + q \, N_q + 0.5 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$$

Substituting the values:

$$q_u = (15)(37.2) + (27)(22.5) + (0.5)(18)(2.0)(19.7)$$

$$q_u = 558.0 + 607.5 + 354.6$$

$$\boxed{q_u = 1520.1 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

**Step 5: Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity**

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma \, D_f = 1520.1 - 27 = 1493.1 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** With neat sketches, explain the three types of bearing capacity failures. Compare them on the basis of (i) soil type, (ii) failure surface pattern, (iii) nature of failure, and (iv) load-settlement curve. **[(7 marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

The three types of bearing capacity failures are **General Shear Failure (GSF)**, **Local Shear Failure (LSF)**, and **Punching Shear Failure (PSF)**, as classified by **Terzaghi (1943)**.

**Neat Sketch Description (to be drawn on answer sheet):**

```
GENERAL SHEAR FAILURE:                LOCAL SHEAR FAILURE:            PUNCHING SHEAR FAILURE:

   |~~~~~|  HEAVE                       |~~~~~|  slight bulge          |~~~~~|  no heave
   |  GS |                               |  LS |                       | PS  |
___|     |___                          __|     |__                   __|     |__
   \\     //                              \\   //                       |  |  |
    \\   //                                \\ //                        |  |  |  vertical
     \\ //                                  \\/                          |  |  |  shear
      \\/                              (partial slip)                     |__|__|
    (slip)                                                               
```

**Comparative Tabular Answer:**

| Basis | General Shear | Local Shear | Punching Shear |
|-------|---------------|-------------|-----------------|
| (i) Soil type | Dense sand, stiff clay | Medium sand, medium clay | Loose sand, soft clay |
| (ii) Failure surface | Well-defined, continuous to surface | Partially developed, ends in soil | Not visible (vertical shear) |
| (iii) Nature of failure | Sudden, brittle, catastrophic | Progressive | Gradual sinking |
| (iv) Load-settlement curve | Sharp peak at $q_u$ | No clear peak | No peak, steep descent |

---

**Q. (b)** For a sandy soil with $I_D = 0.30$ and a strip footing of width $B = 3.0 \text{ m}$ at depth $D_f = 1.0 \text{ m}$ with $\phi = 35^\circ$, $\gamma = 17 \text{ kN/m}^3$, and $c = 0$, determine the type of failure and compute the ultimate bearing capacity using the appropriate Terzaghi equation. **[(7 marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the Type of Failure**

Given $I_D = 0.30 < 0.33$, the soil is **loose sand**, hence the failure type is **Punching Shear Failure (PSF)**.

However, in **practical KTU Board Problems**, since $c = 0$ and the failure mechanism is approximately at the boundary of Local Shear, we apply **Terzaghi's modified equation for local shear** as the closest analytical approximation. (The student's answer is acceptable if they use either local shear modification or note that punching shear is to be designed by FOS.)

We will use the **Local Shear** equation as the conservative design approach.

**Step 2: Compute the Modified Friction Angle**

$$\tan \phi_m = \frac{2}{3} \tan 35^\circ = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.7002 = 0.4668$$

$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(0.4668) \approx 25.0^\circ$$

**Step 3: Read Modified Bearing Capacity Factors for $\phi_m = 25^\circ$**

From Terzaghi's standard table:

$$N_c' = 12.7, \qquad N_q' = 6.9, \qquad N_\gamma' = 3.1$$

(For $c = 0$, the $N_c'$ term vanishes: $c_m = \frac{2}{3} \times 0 = 0$.)

**Step 4: Compute the Surcharge**

$$q = \gamma \, D_f = 17 \times 1.0 = 17 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5: Apply Terzaghi's Modified Equation (Strip Footing)**

$$q_u = c_m \, N_c' + q \, N_q' + 0.5 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma'$$

$$q_u = 0 + (17)(6.9) + (0.5)(17)(3.0)(3.1)$$

$$q_u = 117.3 + 79.05$$

$$\boxed{q_u \approx 196.35 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

**Step 6: Design Recommendation**

Since this is a **punching shear prone soil**, apply **Factor of Safety $F = 4$ to $5$** in design.

$$q_{\text{safe}} = \frac{q_u}{F} = \frac{196.35}{4} \approx 49.09 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 
> - **Pitfall 1:** Using the **unmodified Terzaghi equation** for $I_D < 0.67$ — students forget to apply the $\frac{2}{3}$ modification and lose **3 to 4 marks**.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Confusing **Punching Shear** with **Local Shear** in numerical problems. When $I_D < 0.33$, the textbook approach uses a **higher FOS** rather than a separate analytical equation; however, the **modified Terzaghi equation** is the closest KTU-acceptable computation.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Forgetting to **subtract $q$** to get net bearing capacity. The question may ask for $q_{nu}$ — read carefully.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Using the **factor $0.5$** in the third term for a **square or circular footing** — students must use $0.4$ and $0.3$ respectively with the appropriate shape factor on $N_c$.
> - **Pitfall 5:** Spelling failure types incorrectly in Part A (e.g., "Punching" written as "Punching" but the **load-settlement** description is for general shear — partial credit deduction of 1 mark).
> - **Pitfall 6:** Writing **load-settlement curve in words only** without a clear peak/no-peak description. Always mention **"sharp peak"** for GSF, **"no clear peak"** for LSF, and **"no peak, gradual descent"** for PSF.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Three failure types** = General Shear (GSF), Local Shear (LSF), Punching Shear (PSF).

- ✅ **GSF:** Dense soil ($I_D \geq 0.67$, $c_u > 100$ kPa), well-defined slip surface, **sudden tilt, sharp peak, heaving**.

- ✅ **LSF:** Medium soil ($0.33 < I_D < 0.67$, $50 < c_u < 100$ kPa), partial slip surface, **progressive failure, slight bulge, no clear peak**.

- ✅ **PSF:** Loose/soft soil ($I_D < 0.33$, $c_u < 50$ kPa), **vertical shear, no heave, no peak, large settlement**.

- ✅ **Terzaghi's Modification for LSF:** $c_m = \frac{2}{3} c$ and $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(\frac{2}{3} \tan \phi)$ — **always state both substitutions** in the exam.

- ✅ **Standard Equation (GSF, strip):** $q_u = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$ — **memorize the formula in this exact form**.

- ✅ **Shape Factors (Terzaghi):** Strip = $0.5$, Square = $0.4$, Circular = $0.3$ in the third term; $1.3$ on $N_c$ for square/circular.

- ✅ **Settlement at failure:** GSF ≈ $7\%B$, LSF ≈ $15\%B$ – $25\%B$, PSF ≈ $30\%B$ – $50\%B$.

- ✅ **FOS recommendation:** GSF → $F = 3$, LSF → $F = 3$ to $4$, PSF → $F = 4$ to $5$.

- ✅ **Vesic's SPT/CPT thresholds:** $N > 30$ → GSF, $10 < N < 30$ → LSF, $N < 10$ → PSF.

- ✅ **Mnemonic:** **"Tilt-Bulge-Punch"** for GSF, LSF, PSF respectively.

- ✅ **IS Code reference:** **IS 6403:1981** (Reaffirmed 2021) for bearing capacity of shallow foundations.

- ✅ **Plate Load Test:** Conducted per **IS 1888:1982** with a **$300 \text{ mm}$ square plate** (smallest standard size).

- ✅ **"Prandtl-Reissner-Terzaghi" lineage** of the bearing capacity equation — mention all three names for full marks in derivation questions.

- ✅ **The 0.5 in the third term is for STRIP footing only** — this is the most common board-exam trap; circle this number in your answer sheet.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Water Table on Bearing Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effect of Water Table on Bearing Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Effect of Water Table on Bearing Capacity** refers to the reduction in the ultimate bearing capacity of a foundation soil caused by the presence of a groundwater table within or near the failure zone beneath the footing. This effect is a direct consequence of the **principle of effective stress** postulated by Karl Terzaghi. When pore water pressure develops, the inter-granular (effective) stress between soil particles is reduced, which in turn diminishes the shear strength of the soil and its load-carrying capacity.

Mathematically, Terzaghi's general ultimate bearing capacity equation for a strip footing under the influence of a water table is expressed as:

$$
q_{ult} = c \cdot N_c + q' \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma_{eff} \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma
$$

where:
- **$c$** = unit cohesion of soil (kN/m²)
- **$q'$** = effective surcharge at the base of the footing (kN/m²)
- **$B$** = width of the footing (m)
- **$\gamma_{eff}$** = effective (modified) unit weight of soil in the failure zone (kN/m³)
- **$N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$** = Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors (dimensionless)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The cohesion term ($c \cdot N_c$) is **NOT** affected by the position of the water table because it depends on soil cohesion, not on overburden pressure or self-weight. Only the **surcharge term** and the **self-weight term** are modified by the water table.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Swimming Pool" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a person trying to push a heavy block down into a sand pit. If the sand is **dry**, the block encounters strong resistance from the soil grains, and you need significant force. Now, imagine the same sand pit **flooded with water** — the block suddenly feels much "lighter" and slides in more easily. This is because the water exerts **buoyancy** on the soil grains, pushing them apart and reducing the effective contact stress between them.
>
> **Engineering Translation:** In soil mechanics, when the water table rises into the failure zone of a footing, the soil grains become buoyed by water. The **buoyant (submerged) unit weight** $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ is roughly half of the natural unit weight. Therefore, the soil's resistance to the foundation load — i.e., the bearing capacity — **drops significantly**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard values are used universally in KTU examination problems:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value |
|:------:|:---------|:--------------:|
| $\gamma_w$ | Unit weight of water | **9.81 kN/m³** (sometimes taken as **10 kN/m³** in KTU problems) |
| $\gamma_{sat}$ | Saturated unit weight of soil | **18 – 22 kN/m³** (typical range) |
| $\gamma$ | Natural (moist) unit weight | **16 – 20 kN/m³** (typical range) |
| $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ | Submerged/buoyant unit weight | **8 – 12 kN/m³** (typical range) |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Aid:** "$\gamma'$ is always roughly half of $\gamma_{sat}$" because $\gamma_w \approx 9.81$ kN/m³ and $\gamma_{sat} \approx 18-20$ kN/m³. This shortcut helps in multiple-choice elimination during exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Principle: Effective Stress

The phenomenon is governed by **Terzaghi's Principle of Effective Stress (1923)**:

$$
\sigma' = \sigma - u
$$

where $\sigma'$ is the effective stress, $\sigma$ is the total stress, and $u$ is the pore water pressure.

In bearing capacity, the shear strength of soil is given by Coulomb's equation:

$$
\tau_f = c' + \sigma' \tan\phi'
$$

When water rises:
1. The total overburden pressure $\sigma$ stays the same, BUT
2. The pore water pressure $u$ increases, thereby
3. **Decreasing the effective stress** $\sigma'$
4. **Decreasing the shear strength** $\tau_f$
5. **Decreasing the bearing capacity** $q_{ult}$

## 2.2 Breakdown of the Four Critical Cases

The position of the water table relative to the footing determines **which term** of the Terzaghi equation gets modified.

### **Case I: Water Table Above the Base of Footing ($D_w \leq D_f$)**

Here, the surcharge soil itself is partly or fully submerged. Both the surcharge and self-weight terms are affected.

- **Modified Surcharge:** $q' = \gamma \cdot D_f' + \gamma' \cdot (D_f - D_w)$ where $D_f'$ is the depth of soil above the water table.
- **Self-weight term:** Use submerged unit weight $\gamma'$ since the failure zone (within depth $B$ below base) is fully submerged.

$$
q_{ult} = c \cdot N_c + \left[\gamma \cdot D_f' + \gamma' \cdot (D_f - D_w)\right] \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma' \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma
$$

### **Case II: Water Table at the Base of the Footing ($D_w = D_f$, i.e., $z = 0$)**

The surcharge soil above is dry/moist, so $q$ is unchanged. But the failure zone is fully submerged.

$$
q_{ult} = c \cdot N_c + (\gamma \cdot D_f) \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma' \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma
$$

### **Case III: Water Table at Depth $z$ Below the Base ($0 < z < B$)**

The surcharge is unaffected, but the failure zone is **partially submerged**.

- The portion of thickness $z$ above the water table uses $\gamma$
- The portion of thickness $(B - z)$ below the water table uses $\gamma'$

The **effective (weighted average) unit weight** is:

$$
\gamma_{eff} = \gamma' + \frac{z}{B} \cdot (\gamma - \gamma') = \gamma - \frac{z}{B} \cdot \gamma_w
$$

Therefore:

$$
q_{ult} = c \cdot N_c + (\gamma \cdot D_f) \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma_{eff} \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma
$$

### **Case IV: Water Table at Depth $z \geq B$ Below the Base**

The water table lies **entirely below** the failure zone. **No reduction** in bearing capacity.

$$
q_{ult} = c \cdot N_c + (\gamma \cdot D_f) \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU 2024 problem on this topic uses one of these equations.**

| Case | Water Table Position | Surcharge Term $q' \cdot N_q$ | Self-Weight Term $0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$ |
|:----:|:--------------------|:------------------------------|:----------------------------------------------------------|
| **I** | Above footing base ($D_w \leq D_f$) | $\left[\gamma D_f' + \gamma'(D_f - D_w)\right] \cdot N_q$ | $0.5 \cdot \gamma' \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$ |
| **II** | At footing base ($z = 0$) | $\gamma D_f \cdot N_q$ | $0.5 \cdot \gamma' \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$ |
| **III** | Between base and depth $B$ ($0 < z < B$) | $\gamma D_f \cdot N_q$ | $0.5 \cdot \gamma_{eff} \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$ where $\gamma_{eff} = \gamma - (z/B)\gamma_w$ |
| **IV** | At or below depth $B$ ($z \geq B$) | $\gamma D_f \cdot N_q$ | $0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$ |

**Reduction Factor Form** (very common in KTU 2-mark questions):

$$
q_{ult,reduced} = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma \cdot W'
$$

where the **water table reduction factor** $W'$ is:

$$
W' = 1.0 \quad \text{if } z \geq B; \qquad W' = 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{D_w}{B} \quad \text{if } 0 \leq D_w \leq B
$$

> Here $D_w$ is the distance of the water table from the base of the footing, and $W'$ replaces the third term's contribution. **Cohesion and surcharge terms are NOT multiplied by $W'$**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|:------|:------------|
| **Shallow Foundation Design** | Predicting safe bearing capacity for footings near reservoirs, rivers, or coastal zones |
| **Basement Construction** | Designing raft foundations below the water table in urban high-rise projects |
| **Bridge Piers** | Calculating scour-induced bearing capacity loss in river piers |
| **Earthquake Engineering** | Liquefaction susceptibility analysis when water table rises |
| **Agricultural Engineering** | Evaluating footing safety in irrigated fields with high water tables |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Unit Weight $\gamma_{eff}$ in the Failure Zone

Consider a strip footing of width $B$ with its base at depth $D_f$ below the ground surface. The water table lies at a depth $z$ **below the base of the footing**, where $0 < z < B$.

The failure zone beneath the footing is assumed to extend to a depth equal to $B$ below the base (Terzaghi's assumption for general shear failure).

**Step 1: Divide the failure zone into two parts.**

- **Upper part** (thickness $z$, above the water table): Unit weight = $\gamma$ (moist)
- **Lower part** (thickness $B - z$, below the water table): Unit weight = $\gamma'$ (submerged)

**Step 2: Calculate the total weight of a soil column of unit cross-sectional area within the failure zone.**

$$
W_{total} = (\gamma \cdot z \cdot 1) + [\gamma' \cdot (B - z) \cdot 1]
$$

$$
W_{total} = \gamma \cdot z + \gamma' \cdot (B - z)
$$

**Step 3: Compute the average (effective) unit weight over the full depth $B$.**

$$
\gamma_{eff} = \frac{W_{total}}{B \cdot 1} = \frac{\gamma \cdot z + \gamma' \cdot (B - z)}{B}
$$

**Step 4: Simplify the expression.**

$$
\gamma_{eff} = \frac{\gamma \cdot z + \gamma' \cdot B - \gamma' \cdot z}{B}
$$

$$
\gamma_{eff} = \frac{\gamma' \cdot B + z \cdot (\gamma - \gamma')}{B}
$$

$$
\gamma_{eff} = \gamma' + \frac{z}{B} \cdot (\gamma - \gamma')
$$

**Step 5: Substitute $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ and simplify.**

$$
\gamma_{eff} = \gamma' + \frac{z}{B} \cdot (\gamma - \gamma')
$$

Note that $\gamma - \gamma' = \gamma - (\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w) = \gamma - \gamma_{sat} + \gamma_w$

If we assume $\gamma \approx \gamma_{sat}$ (saturated soil throughout, common in KTU problems where the soil is fully saturated):

$$
\gamma - \gamma' \approx \gamma_w
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\gamma_{eff} = \gamma' + \frac{z}{B} \cdot \gamma_w = \gamma - \frac{z}{B} \cdot \gamma_w}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Verification (always a good practice in KTU):**
> - If $z = 0$ (water at base): $\gamma_{eff} = \gamma'$ ✓
> - If $z = B$ (water at depth $B$): $\gamma_{eff} = \gamma' + \gamma_w = \gamma_{sat}$ ✓

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Comprehensive KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A strip footing of width $B = 2.0$ m is founded at depth $D_f = 1.5$ m in a soil with $c = 15$ kN/m², $\phi = 25°$, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, and $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³. For $\phi = 25°$, Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors are: $N_c = 25.1$, $N_q = 12.7$, $N_\gamma = 9.7$. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³. Compute the ultimate bearing capacity when the water table is at:
> **(a)** the base of the footing, **(b)** 1.0 m below the base, **(c)** 2.5 m below the base.

### **Solution (a): Water Table at the Base of the Footing ($z = 0$)**

**Step 1: Identify the case.**

Since the water table is exactly at the base, this is **Case II** from our formula sheet.

**Step 2: Calculate the effective surcharge.**

The surcharge soil lies entirely above the water table, so its unit weight is $\gamma$:

$$
q = \gamma \cdot D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the submerged unit weight for the self-weight term.**

$$
\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 20 - 9.81 = 10.19 \text{ kN/m}^3
$$

**Step 4: Apply Terzaghi's modified equation.**

$$
q_{ult} = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma' \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma
$$

$$
q_{ult} = (15)(25.1) + (27)(12.7) + 0.5 \times 10.19 \times 2.0 \times 9.7
$$

$$
q_{ult} = 376.5 + 342.9 + 98.84
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{ult} = 818.24 \text{ kN/m}^2}
$$

### **Solution (b): Water Table at $z = 1.0$ m Below the Base**

**Step 1: Identify the case.**

Since $0 < z = 1.0 < B = 2.0$, this is **Case III** (partial submergence).

**Step 2: Surcharge is unchanged.**

$$
q = \gamma \cdot D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the effective unit weight.**

Using the derived formula:

$$
\gamma_{eff} = \gamma' + \frac{z}{B}(\gamma - \gamma')
$$

$$
\gamma_{eff} = 10.19 + \frac{1.0}{2.0} \times (18 - 10.19)
$$

$$
\gamma_{eff} = 10.19 + 0.5 \times 7.81
$$

$$
\gamma_{eff} = 10.19 + 3.905 = 14.095 \text{ kN/m}^3
$$

**Step 4: Apply Terzaghi's equation.**

$$
q_{ult} = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma_{eff} \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma
$$

$$
q_{ult} = (15)(25.1) + (27)(12.7) + 0.5 \times 14.095 \times 2.0 \times 9.7
$$

$$
q_{ult} = 376.5 + 342.9 + 136.72
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{ult} = 856.12 \text{ kN/m}^2}
$$

### **Solution (c): Water Table at $z = 2.5$ m Below the Base**

**Step 1: Identify the case.**

Since $z = 2.5 \geq B = 2.0$, this is **Case IV** (no effect of water table).

**Step 2: Apply the original Terzaghi's equation (no modification needed).**

$$
q_{ult} = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma
$$

$$
q_{ult} = (15)(25.1) + (27)(12.7) + 0.5 \times 18 \times 2.0 \times 9.7
$$

$$
q_{ult} = 376.5 + 342.9 + 174.6
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{ult} = 894.0 \text{ kN/m}^2}
$$

## 3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Water Table Position | $q_{ult}$ (kN/m²) | % Reduction from Dry Case |
|:--------------------|:------------------:|:--------------------------:|
| **Dry condition** (no water) | **894.0** | 0% (Reference) |
| **(c)** $z = 2.5$ m below base | **894.0** | 0% (No effect) |
| **(b)** $z = 1.0$ m below base | **856.12** | 4.24% |
| **(a)** $z = 0$ m (at base) | **818.24** | **8.47%** |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Observation for KTU 2-Mark Questions:** The maximum reduction in bearing capacity due to water table occurs when the water table is **at the base of the footing**. As it rises further above the base, the surcharge term also reduces, causing even greater reduction. This is a frequently tested KTU concept.

## 3.4 General Modified Bearing Capacity Equation (Meyerhof / Skempton / IS Code Form)

For a more generalized treatment, the ultimate bearing capacity is written as:

$$
q_{ult} = c N_c s_c d_c i_c + q' N_q s_q d_q i_q + 0.5 \gamma_{eff} B N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma i_\gamma
$$

where $s, d, i$ are **shape, depth, and inclination factors** respectively. The water table effect is incorporated by replacing $q$ with $q'$ and $\gamma$ with $\gamma_{eff}$ in the third term — all other terms remain identical to the dry case.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Decision Tree for Water Table Position

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the logic flow used to select the correct bearing capacity equation based on water table position.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Water Table Effect on Bearing Capacity"] --> B{"Is water table at or above footing base? Dw less or equal Df?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Case I: Use modified surcharge and submerged unit weight"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"Is water table within failure zone? 0 less than z less than B"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Case III: Use effective unit weight = gamma prime plus z over B times gamma minus gamma prime"]
    D -- "No" --> F{"Is water table at depth z greater or equal B below base?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Case IV: No reduction. Use original Terzaghi equation"]
    F -- "No" --> H["Case II: Water at base. Use gamma prime in self-weight term"]
    C --> I["Apply Terzaghi modified equation"]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Compute ultimate bearing capacity q_ult"]
    J --> K["Apply factor of safety FS equals 3 to get safe bearing capacity q_safe"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Modification of Each Term in Terzaghi's Equation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Terzaghi Original Equation: q_ult equals cNc plus qNq plus 0.5 gamma B N gamma"] --> B["Term 1: Cohesion Term c times N_c"]
    A --> C["Term 2: Surcharge Term q times N_q"]
    A --> D["Term 3: Self Weight Term 0.5 gamma B N gamma"]
    B --> E["UNCHANGED: Water table does not affect cohesion"]
    C --> F["MODIFIED ONLY IF: Water table above footing base"]
    D --> G["MODIFIED IF: Water table is within failure zone depth B"]
    E --> H["Final q_ult"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Failure Zone & Water Table Positions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Ground Surface"] --> B["Footing Base at Depth D_f"]
    B --> C["Failure Zone Extends to Depth B Below Base"]
    subgraph Water_Table_Configurations
        direction TB
        WT1["Position A: Water table at base z equals 0"] -.-> WT1desc["Maximum reduction in self-weight term"]
        WT2["Position B: Water table at z equals B by 2"] -.-> WT2desc["Partial reduction. Effective unit weight used"]
        WT3["Position C: Water table at z greater than B"] -.-> WT3desc["No reduction. Original equation applies"]
        WT4["Position D: Water table above base Dw less than Df"] -.-> WT4desc["Surcharge also reduced. Most severe case"]
    end
    WT1desc --> Apply1["q_ult equals cNc plus gamma Df Nq plus 0.5 gamma prime B N gamma"]
    WT2desc --> Apply2["q_ult equals cNc plus gamma Df Nq plus 0.5 gamma_eff B N gamma"]
    WT3desc --> Apply3["q_ult equals cNc plus gamma Df Nq plus 0.5 gamma B N gamma"]
    WT4desc --> Apply4["q_ult equals cNc plus q prime Nq plus 0.5 gamma prime B N gamma"]
```

## 4.4 Visual Summary Table — Effect of Water Table on Each Term

| Term of Bearing Capacity Equation | Affected by Water Table? | When Affected | Modification Rule |
|:----------------------------------|:------------------------:|:--------------|:------------------|
| Cohesion term $c \cdot N_c$ | **No** | Never | Remains unchanged in all cases |
| Surcharge term $q \cdot N_q$ | **Yes** (conditionally) | When $D_w \leq D_f$ | Replace $q$ with $q' = \gamma D_f' + \gamma'(D_f - D_w)$ |
| Self-weight term $0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$ | **Yes** (conditionally) | When $D_w \geq D_f$ (i.e., $z \leq B$ below base) | Replace $\gamma$ with $\gamma_{eff}$ (or use reduction factor $W'$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The "danger zone" for a footing is when the water table lies between the **ground surface** and a depth of **$B$ below the base of the footing**. Within this critical zone, every 1 m rise in the water table causes measurable reduction in bearing capacity. KTU often tests whether students can identify this critical zone in numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Define the term "Effective Stress" and explain how the presence of a water table affects the bearing capacity of a soil.**

**Model Answer:**

**Effective Stress:** According to Terzaghi, effective stress is the stress transmitted between the solid soil grains at their contact points. It is given by:

$$
\sigma' = \sigma - u
$$

where $\sigma$ is the total stress and $u$ is the pore water pressure.

**Effect of Water Table on Bearing Capacity:** When the water table rises into the failure zone of a footing, the pore water pressure increases. This reduces the effective stress, which in turn reduces the shear strength of the soil. Consequently, the ultimate bearing capacity decreases. The reduction affects the **surcharge term** and the **self-weight term** of Terzaghi's equation, while the **cohesion term** remains unaffected. The maximum reduction occurs when the water table is at the base of the footing. `[3 Marks]`

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Q: List the various cases of water table position relative to the footing and state the corresponding modification factor $W'$ for the third term of Terzaghi's bearing capacity equation.**

**Model Answer:**

The water table can be in four positions relative to the footing:

| Case | Water Table Position | Reduction Factor $W'$ |
|:----:|:--------------------|:----------------------:|
| 1 | Above the base of footing | $W' = 0.5$ (and surcharge also reduces) |
| 2 | At the base of footing ($z = 0$) | $W' = 0.5$ |
| 3 | Between base and depth $B$ ($0 < z < B$) | $W' = 0.5 + 0.5(z/B)$ |
| 4 | At or below depth $B$ ($z \geq B$) | $W' = 1.0$ (No reduction) |

The modified third term becomes: $0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma \cdot W'$ `[3 Marks]`

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 4 Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

> **A strip footing of width 2.5 m is to be constructed at a depth of 1.8 m in a soil with cohesion $c = 20$ kN/m², angle of internal friction $\phi = 30°$, natural unit weight $\gamma = 18.5$ kN/m³, and saturated unit weight $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³.**
>
> For $\phi = 30°$, Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors are: $N_c = 37.2$, $N_q = 22.5$, $N_\gamma = 19.7$. Use $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³.
>
> **(a)** Compute the ultimate bearing capacity assuming the water table is **at the base of the footing**. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** If the water table rises to a position **1.0 m above the base of the footing** (i.e., $D_w = 0.8$ m from ground surface), recalculate the ultimate bearing capacity. Comment on the percentage reduction. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

##### **Part (a) — Water Table at Base of Footing [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Identify parameters.**

Given: $B = 2.5$ m, $D_f = 1.8$ m, $c = 20$ kN/m², $\phi = 30°$, $\gamma = 18.5$ kN/m³, $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³.

**Step 2: Compute surcharge and submerged unit weight.**

$$
q = \gamma \cdot D_f = 18.5 \times 1.8 = 33.3 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 20 - 9.81 = 10.19 \text{ kN/m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Apply Terzaghi's modified equation.**

$$
q_{ult} = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma' \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma
$$

$$
q_{ult} = (20)(37.2) + (33.3)(22.5) + 0.5 \times 10.19 \times 2.5 \times 19.7
$$

$$
q_{ult} = 744.0 + 749.25 + 250.93
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{ult} = 1744.18 \text{ kN/m}^2}
$$

##### **Part (b) — Water Table 1.0 m Above Base [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Compute depth of soil above water table.**

$D_f' = D_f - 1.0 = 1.8 - 1.0 = 0.8$ m (moist soil above water table)

Depth of submerged soil above base: $(D_f - D_w) = 1.0$ m

**Step 2: Compute modified (effective) surcharge.**

$$
q' = \gamma \cdot D_f' + \gamma' \cdot (D_f - D_w)
$$

$$
q' = (18.5)(0.8) + (10.19)(1.0)
$$

$$
q' = 14.8 + 10.19 = 24.99 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Self-weight term uses submerged unit weight (failure zone is below water table).**

$$
\text{Self-weight term} = 0.5 \times 10.19 \times 2.5 \times 19.7 = 250.93 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Compute the new ultimate bearing capacity.**

$$
q_{ult,new} = c N_c + q' N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma' \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma
$$

$$
q_{ult,new} = (20)(37.2) + (24.99)(22.5) + 250.93
$$

$$
q_{ult,new} = 744.0 + 562.275 + 250.93
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{ult,new} = 1557.21 \text{ kN/m}^2}
$$

**Step 5: Comment on reduction.**

Percentage reduction from the case in part (a):

$$
\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{1744.18 - 1557.21}{1744.18} \times 100 = 10.72\%
$$

**Conclusion:** As the water table rises above the base, both the surcharge term and the self-weight term reduce, causing a further drop in bearing capacity. This demonstrates the **critical importance of water table location in foundation design**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

> **A square footing of side 2.0 m is to be placed at a depth of 1.5 m in a cohesionless soil with $\phi = 35°$, $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³, $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³, and $c = 0$. For $\phi = 35°$, Terzaghi's factors for a square footing are: $N_c = 57.8$, $N_q = 41.4$, $N_\gamma = 45.2$.**
>
> **(a)** Calculate the safe bearing capacity with a factor of safety of 3, assuming the **water table is at a great depth** (no effect). **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** If the water table rises to the **base of the footing**, calculate the new safe bearing capacity. Compute the percentage reduction. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

##### **Part (a) — Water Table at Great Depth [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Identify case.**

Since water table is far below, **no modification** is needed.

**Step 2: Compute surcharge.**

$$
q = \gamma \cdot D_f = 17 \times 1.5 = 25.5 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Apply Terzaghi's equation (square footing, $c = 0$).**

For a square footing, the standard form of Terzaghi's equation is:

$$
q_{ult} = 1.3 \cdot c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.4 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma
$$

Since $c = 0$:

$$
q_{ult} = (25.5)(41.4) + 0.4 \times 17 \times 2.0 \times 45.2
$$

$$
q_{ult} = 1055.7 + 614.72
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{ult} = 1670.42 \text{ kN/m}^2}
$$

**Step 4: Apply factor of safety.**

$$
q_{safe} = \frac{q_{ult}}{FS} = \frac{1670.42}{3} = 556.81 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

##### **Part (b) — Water Table at Base of Footing [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Compute submerged unit weight.**

$$
\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 19 - 9.81 = 9.19 \text{ kN/m}^3
$$

**Step 2: Surcharge is unchanged (water table at base).**

$$
q = 25.5 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Apply modified equation.**

$$
q_{ult,new} = q \cdot N_q + 0.4 \cdot \gamma' \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma
$$

$$
q_{ult,new} = (25.5)(41.4) + 0.4 \times 9.19 \times 2.0 \times 45.2
$$

$$
q_{ult,new} = 1055.7 + 332.34
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{ult,new} = 1388.04 \text{ kN/m}^2}
$$

**Step 4: Apply factor of safety.**

$$
q_{safe,new} = \frac{1388.04}{3} = 462.68 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 5: Percentage reduction in safe bearing capacity.**

$$
\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{556.81 - 462.68}{556.81} \times 100 \approx 16.90\%
$$

**Conclusion:** For cohesionless soil, the reduction due to water table rise is **substantial (~17%)**, since the entire bearing capacity in sands comes from the surcharge and self-weight terms — both of which are directly affected by submergence.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to reduce the surcharge term:** When the water table is **above the base of the footing**, students often use the original $q = \gamma D_f$ without subtracting the buoyancy effect. **Always compute $q'$ using effective unit weights when $D_w \leq D_f$.** [-2 Marks penalty]
>
> 2. **Using the wrong unit weight:** A common mistake is using $\gamma_{sat}$ instead of $\gamma'$ (submerged) in the self-weight term when the failure zone is submerged. **Submerged unit weight is $\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w \approx \gamma_{sat} - 9.81$ or $\gamma_{sat} - 10$.** [-2 Marks]
>
> 3. **Mixing up "depth of water table" definitions:** Some students confuse $D_w$ (depth from ground surface) with $z$ (depth from base of footing). **KTU problems usually state $D_w$ as depth from ground surface. Convert to $z = D_w - D_f$ for use in formulas.** [-1 Mark]
>
> 4. **Ignoring the shape factor for square/circular footings:** If the problem states a **square footing** with side $B$, the **third term coefficient is 0.4** (not 0.5), and the **first term coefficient is 1.3** (not 1.0). Forgetting these will yield a wrong answer. [-2 Marks]
>
> 5. **Forgetting the final factor of safety:** A 14-mark question always expects the **safe bearing capacity** $q_{safe} = q_{ult} / FS$ as the final step. Forgetting it loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Wrong bearing capacity factors:** Always check whether the problem uses **Terzaghi's** ($N_c = 37.2$ for $\phi = 30°$) or **IS Code / Meyerhof's** ($N_c = 30.14$ for $\phi = 30°$) factors. **KTU Module 4 specifically uses Terzaghi's values.** [-2 Marks if mismatched]

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bulletins for KTU 2024 Exam:**
>
> - **Master Equation:** $q_{ult} = c N_c + q' N_q + 0.5 \gamma_{eff} B N_\gamma$ — memorize this in the modified form.
> - **Effective Unit Weight Formula:** $\gamma_{eff} = \gamma' + (z/B)(\gamma - \gamma') = \gamma - (z/B) \cdot \gamma_w$ (when $0 \leq z \leq B$).
> - **Four Cases to Memorize:** Water table at (i) above base, (ii) at base, (iii) within $B$ below base, (iv) at or below $B$.
> - **Critical Fact:** The cohesion term is **NEVER** affected by the water table.
> - **Maximum Reduction Scenario:** Water table at the base of the footing reduces the self-weight term by **50%**.
> - **Reduction Factor $W'$:** $W' = 0.5 + 0.5(D_w/B)$ for $0 \leq D_w \leq B$, where $D_w$ is measured from the base of the footing.
> - **Standard Value to Memorize:** $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ (use 10 kN/m³ if KTU problem specifies).
> - **Common Bearing Capacity Factors (Terzaghi):**
>   - $\phi = 25°$: $N_c = 25.1$, $N_q = 12.7$, $N_\gamma = 9.7$
>   - $\phi = 30°$: $N_c = 37.2$, $N_q = 22.5$, $N_\gamma = 19.7$
>   - $\phi = 35°$: $N_c = 57.8$, $N_q = 41.4$, $N_\gamma = 45.2$
> - **Square Footing:** Coefficient 1.3 for $cN_c$ and 0.4 for $\gamma B N_\gamma$ terms.
> - **Circle Footing:** Coefficient 1.3 for $cN_c$ and 0.3 for $\gamma B N_\gamma$ terms.
> - **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** If the water table is within depth $B$ below the footing base, **always** include the water table correction.
> - **End-of-Solution Rule:** Always end with $q_{safe} = q_{ult} / FS$ (typical FS = 3 for footings).
> - **Submerged Unit Weight Identification:** When you see "water table at base" or "submerged soil" in a KTU problem, immediately use $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - 9.81$ in the self-weight term.
> - **Don't Confuse:** $\gamma$ (natural) is **NOT** the same as $\gamma_{sat}$ (saturated) or $\gamma'$ (submerged). Each represents a different state of the soil.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IS Code Method for Computing Bearing Capacity; Shallow Foundations: Types of Foundations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IS Code Method for Computing Bearing Capacity; Shallow Foundations: Types of Foundations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 IS Code Method for Bearing Capacity — Formal Definition

The **IS Code Method** (formulated in **IS 6403: 1981** — *Code of Practice for Determination of Bearing Capacity of Shallow Foundations*) is the standard Indian procedure prescribed by the Bureau of Indian Standards for computing the **ultimate bearing capacity ($q_u$)** and **net safe bearing capacity ($q_{ns}$)** of **shallow foundations** resting on cohesive or cohesionless soils.

Unlike the classical Terzaghi or Meyerhof equations, the IS Code adopts a more refined, semi-empirical formulation that includes **shape factors ($S_c, S_q, S_\gamma$)** and **depth factors ($d_c, d_q, d_\gamma$)** as functions of the depth-to-width ratio ($D_f / B$) and the angle of internal friction ($\phi$). This makes it directly applicable to real field conditions where foundations are neither infinitely long strips nor perfectly square pads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The IS Code method is a **guaranteed 7-to-14 mark question** in KTU University Examinations. Students must memorize the shape factor tables and depth factor equations. Numerical problems on isolated square/rectangular footings are frequently asked in Part B.

## 1.2 Types of Shallow Foundations — Formal Definition

**Shallow foundations** are those foundations whose depth ($D_f$) is less than or equal to their width ($B$), i.e., $D_f / B \leq 1$. They transfer structural loads to the near-surface soil strata and are the most economical choice when the topsoil has adequate bearing capacity within a few meters of the ground surface.

Per KTU Module 4, shallow foundations are classified into:
1. **Spread (Pad) Footings** — Isolated or Combined
2. **Strap (Cantilever) Footings**
3. **Mat (Raft) Foundations**
4. **Wall Footings** (Continuous strip footings)

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

### 🏗️ Analogy for the IS Code Method
Imagine you are standing on snow wearing flat shoes versus boots:
- **Without shape/depth factors (Terzaghi's simplest form)** → You assume the ground "sees" you as a 1D infinite line (strip footing). This is unrealistic for square columns.
- **With IS Code factors** → The method "translates" the snow's reaction depending on whether you have ski-boots, regular boots, or snowshoes — i.e., it adjusts the pressure distribution for **shape (S)** and **embedment depth (d)**.

So the IS Code acts like a **universal adapter** that converts a strip-footing theoretical result into a realistic square/rectangular/circular footing result.

### 🏢 Analogy for Shallow Foundation Types
Think of a building standing on a soft sponge cake:
- A **single column** → just needs a small pad (isolated footing).
- Two **adjacent columns very close** → one combined pad (combined footing).
- Columns in a **row along a property line** → a beam linking two pads (strap footing).
- A **whole building with many columns on weak soil** → one giant continuous slab (raft/mat).

The **type of footing chosen** is essentially an engineering decision: *spread the load over a small isolated patch, or over the entire footprint — and the soil's bearing capacity decides which.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Unit weight of water: $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3 \approx 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$
> - Standard footing shapes assumed in IS 6403: **Strip, Square, Circular, Rectangular**
> - Standard reference tables: **IS 6403: 1981, Tables 1 to 4** (shape and depth factors)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IS Code Bearing Capacity Equation — General Form

The general bearing capacity equation (after Hansen, adopted by IS 6403) is:

$$q_u = c \cdot N_c \cdot S_c \cdot d_c \cdot i_c + q \cdot (N_q - 1) \cdot S_q \cdot d_q \cdot i_q + 0.5 \cdot B \cdot \gamma \cdot N_\gamma \cdot S_\gamma \cdot d_\gamma \cdot i_\gamma$$

For **vertical loading only** (inclination factors $i = 1$), which is the KTU Module-4 default:

$$\boxed{q_u = c \cdot N_c \cdot S_c \cdot d_c + q \cdot (N_q - 1) \cdot S_q \cdot d_q + 0.5 \cdot B \cdot \gamma \cdot N_\gamma \cdot S_\gamma \cdot d_\gamma}$$

Where:
- $c$ = **cohesion** of soil (kPa)
- $q = \gamma \cdot D_f$ = **surcharge / overburden pressure** at the footing base level
- $B$ = **width** of footing (smaller dimension for rectangular footing) in m
- $\gamma$ = **unit weight** of soil at the level of footing (kN/m³)
- $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = **bearing capacity factors** (functions of $\phi$)
- $S_c, S_q, S_\gamma$ = **shape factors**
- $d_c, d_q, d_\gamma$ = **depth factors**

The **net ultimate bearing capacity** is:

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma \cdot D_f$$

The **net safe bearing capacity** is:

$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{F} + \gamma \cdot D_f \quad \text{(or more commonly written as)}$$

$$\boxed{q_{s} = \frac{q_{nu}}{F}}$$

where $F$ = **Factor of Safety** (typically **3.0** for cohesive soils and **3.0** for cohesionless soils, per IS 6403).

> [!NOTE]
> The **safe bearing pressure (SBC)** including overburden is sometimes written $q_s = \frac{q_{nu}}{F} + \gamma D_f$. The version **without** overburden is the *net* SBC. KTU numericals usually ask for **net safe bearing capacity** unless stated otherwise.

---

## 2.2 Bearing Capacity Factors (IS 6403 / Skempton / Vesic forms)

The IS Code accepts the following expressions (originally from Vesic, 1973):

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \tan^2\left(45 + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

$$N_c = (N_q - 1) \cdot \cot\phi$$

$$N_\gamma = 2 \cdot (N_q + 1) \cdot \tan\phi$$

These are **tabulated** for ready reference in KTU examinations.

---

## 2.3 Shape Factors (IS 6403 — depend on Df / B)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU MEMORIZATION ZONE:** IS 6403 gives shape factors as functions of **$\phi$** AND **$D_f / B$**. This is the single most important table in this module. The KTU question papers almost always test these directly.

For a **strip footing** ($L/B \to \infty$): $S_c = S_q = S_\gamma = 1.0$

For **Rectangular / Square / Circular** footings, IS 6403 tabulates values. The interpolation formulas (commonly used in KTU problems) are:

### 2.3.1 Shape Factor for Cohesive Term ($S_c$)

$$S_c = 1 + \left(\frac{N_q}{N_c}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{B}{L}\right)$$

This is the **Meyerhof / Hansen** simplified expression used when IS 6403 tables are not provided in the question paper.

### 2.3.2 Shape Factor for Surcharge Term ($S_q$)

$$S_q = 1 + \left(\frac{B}{L}\right) \cdot \tan\phi$$

### 2.3.3 Shape Factor for Self-Weight Term ($S_\gamma$)

For $\phi \geq 10^\circ$:

$$S_\gamma = 1 - 0.4 \cdot \left(\frac{B}{L}\right)$$

For $\phi < 10^\circ$:

$$S_\gamma = 1.0$$

Where $B$ = width (smaller dimension), $L$ = length (larger dimension). For a **square footing**, $B = L$, giving $B/L = 1$.

---

## 2.4 Depth Factors (IS 6403)

### 2.4.1 Depth Factor for Cohesive Term

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \cdot \left(\frac{D_f}{B}\right) \quad \text{(valid for } D_f/B \leq 1\text{)}$$

### 2.4.2 Depth Factor for Surcharge Term

For $\phi \geq 10^\circ$:

$$d_q = 1 + 2 \cdot \tan\phi \cdot (1 - \sin\phi)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{D_f}{B}\right)$$

### 2.4.3 Depth Factor for Self-Weight Term

For $\phi \geq 10^\circ$:

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

For $\phi < 10^\circ$ (and $D_f/B \leq 1$): $d_c$, $d_q$, $d_\gamma$ are all taken as 1.0 in many simplified problems.

> [!NOTE]
> When $\phi = 0$ (purely cohesive soil), IS 6403 reduces to **Skempton's formula**, where $N_c = 5.0$ (square) or $N_c = 6.0$ (strip or square at $D_f/B \geq 2.5$).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Variables / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Ultimate BC (IS Code) | $q_u = cN_cS_cd_c + q(N_q-1)S_qd_q + 0.5B\gamma N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma$ | Vertical loading |
| Net Ultimate BC | $q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f$ | Subtract overburden |
| Net Safe BC | $q_{ns} = q_{nu} / F$ | $F = 3$ typical |
| $N_q$ (Vesic) | $e^{\pi\tan\phi} \tan^2(45 + \phi/2)$ | Pure function of $\phi$ |
| $N_c$ | $(N_q-1)\cot\phi$ | Pure function of $\phi$ |
| $N_\gamma$ | $2(N_q+1)\tan\phi$ | Pure function of $\phi$ |
| $S_c$ | $1 + (N_q/N_c)(B/L)$ | Rectangular |
| $S_q$ | $1 + (B/L)\tan\phi$ | Rectangular |
| $S_\gamma$ ($\phi \geq 10^\circ$) | $1 - 0.4(B/L)$ | Rectangular |
| $S_\gamma$ ($\phi < 10^\circ$) | $1.0$ | Cohesive dominated |
| $d_c$ | $1 + 0.4(D_f/B)$ | For $D_f/B \leq 1$ |
| $d_q$ ($\phi \geq 10^\circ$) | $1 + 2\tan\phi(1-\sin\phi)^2(D_f/B)$ | For $D_f/B \leq 1$ |
| $d_\gamma$ ($\phi \geq 10^\circ$) | $1.0$ | Standard |
| Purely cohesive (Skempton) | $q_{nu} = cN_c(1 + 0.4 B/L) \cdot (1 + 0.4 D_f/B)$ | When $\phi = 0$ |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Field Application** | **Why IS Code Method is Used** |
|---|---|
| Building foundation design (residential) | Direct application for $D_f \leq 3$ m footings |
| Bridge abutments | Handles both rectangular and strip footings |
| Retaining wall base design | Strip footings use $S = 1$, depth factor still applies |
| Industrial equipment foundations | Need accurate $q_{ns}$ to size machine base |
| Pre-design for tender stage | IS 6403 is the only code-prescribed method in India |

The IS Code is the **legally binding method** in India for shallow foundation design — any civil engineering design that ignores it is non-compliant for Indian projects.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Vesic's Bearing Capacity Factors

The IS Code uses **Vesic's (1973)** semi-empirical factors which were derived from the **Cavity Expansion Theory**. The general shear failure mechanism gives:

### 3.1.1 $N_q$ Derivation

Starting from the **Caquot-Kerisel** elasticity solution for a strip footing and superimposing the surcharge $q$:

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \tan^2\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

Let $K_p$ be the **passive earth pressure coefficient**:

$$K_p = \tan^2\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

Then:

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot K_p$$

The $e^{\pi \tan\phi}$ term emerges from the **log-spiral** failure surface geometry. The integration along the log-spiral gives this exponential term.

### 3.1.2 $N_c$ Derivation

Equating work done by the surcharge $q$ and cohesion $c$ along the slip surface, Prandtl-Reissner obtained:

$$N_c = (N_q - 1) \cdot \cot\phi$$

This is a **mathematical identity** derived from the boundary conditions of the slip surface where the Mohr-Coulomb envelope is tangent to the slip circle.

### 3.1.3 $N_\gamma$ Derivation (Vesic, 1973)

The self-weight term is the most difficult because it requires integration along the 3D failure wedge. Vesic proposed:

$$N_\gamma = 2 \cdot (N_q + 1) \cdot \tan\phi$$

This matches the original **Hansen (1970)** and **Meyerhof (1963)** values within a few percent for $\phi$ between 0° and 45°.

### 3.1.4 Sample Tabulation (KTU expects this)

| $\phi$ (degrees) | $N_c$ | $N_q$ | $N_\gamma$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 5.14 | 1.00 | 0.00 |
| 5 | 6.49 | 1.57 | 0.45 |
| 10 | 8.35 | 2.47 | 1.22 |
| 15 | 10.98 | 3.94 | 2.65 |
| 20 | 14.83 | 6.40 | 5.39 |
| 25 | 20.72 | 10.66 | 10.88 |
| 30 | 30.14 | 18.40 | 22.40 |
| 35 | 46.12 | 33.30 | 48.03 |
| 40 | 75.31 | 64.20 | 109.41 |
| 45 | 133.87 | 134.87 | 271.76 |

---

## 3.2 Full Analytical Worked Example (Square Footing)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Pattern]**

**Given:**
- Footing type: **Square** ($B = L = 2$ m)
- Depth: $D_f = 1.5$ m
- Soil: $c = 10$ kPa, $\phi = 30^\circ$, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³
- Factor of Safety $F = 3$

**Find:** Net ultimate and net safe bearing capacity using **IS Code method**.

---

### Step 1: Compute Bearing Capacity Factors

For $\phi = 30^\circ$:

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan 30^\circ} \cdot \tan^2(45^\circ + 15^\circ)$$

$$\tan 30^\circ = 0.5774$$

$$\pi \tan 30^\circ = \pi \times 0.5774 = 1.8138$$

$$e^{1.8138} = 6.132$$

$$\tan^2(60^\circ) = (1.7321)^2 = 3.000$$

$$N_q = 6.132 \times 3.000 = 18.40$$

$$N_c = (N_q - 1) \cdot \cot\phi = (18.40 - 1) \times \cot 30^\circ = 17.40 \times 1.7321 = 30.14$$

$$N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi = 2 \times 19.40 \times 0.5774 = 22.40$$

> Verification: matches tabulated values.

### Step 2: Compute Shape Factors (Square → B/L = 1)

$$S_c = 1 + \frac{N_q}{N_c} \cdot \frac{B}{L} = 1 + \frac{18.40}{30.14} \times 1 = 1 + 0.6105 = 1.6105$$

$$S_q = 1 + \frac{B}{L} \cdot \tan\phi = 1 + 1 \times 0.5774 = 1.5774$$

$$S_\gamma = 1 - 0.4 \cdot \frac{B}{L} = 1 - 0.4 \times 1 = 0.6$$

### Step 3: Compute Depth Factors ($D_f/B = 1.5/2 = 0.75 \leq 1$, OK)

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \cdot \frac{D_f}{B} = 1 + 0.4 \times 0.75 = 1.30$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2 \tan\phi(1 - \sin\phi)^2 \cdot \frac{D_f}{B}$$

$$\sin 30^\circ = 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1-\sin\phi)^2 = (0.5)^2 = 0.25$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2 \times 0.5774 \times 0.25 \times 0.75 = 1 + 0.2165 = 1.2165$$

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

### Step 4: Compute Surcharge Pressure

$$q = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 5: Compute Ultimate Bearing Capacity

Cohesion term:

$$T_c = c N_c S_c d_c = 10 \times 30.14 \times 1.6105 \times 1.30 = 630.88 \text{ kPa}$$

Surcharge term:

$$T_q = q (N_q - 1) S_q d_q = 27 \times 17.40 \times 1.5774 \times 1.2165 = 901.15 \text{ kPa}$$

Self-weight term:

$$T_\gamma = 0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma = 0.5 \times 2 \times 18 \times 22.40 \times 0.6 \times 1.0 = 241.92 \text{ kPa}$$

$$q_u = 630.88 + 901.15 + 241.92 = 1773.95 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 6: Compute Net Ultimate and Net Safe Bearing Capacity

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1773.95 - 27 = 1746.95 \text{ kPa}$$

$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{F} = \frac{1746.95}{3} = 582.32 \text{ kPa}$$

### Final Result
$$\boxed{q_{ns} = 582.3 \text{ kPa} \quad \text{(net safe bearing capacity)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — IS Code vs. Terzaghi vs. Meyerhof

| **Feature** | **Terzaghi (1943)** | **Meyerhof (1963)** | **IS Code (IS 6403)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shape factors | Not directly | Yes (limited) | Yes (table-based) |
| Depth factors | No | Yes (approximate) | Yes (refined, $\phi$-dependent) |
| Inclination factors | No | Yes | Yes |
| Footing shapes covered | Strip, square, circular | Strip + approx. | All 4 (strip, square, rectangular, circular) |
| $N_\gamma$ formula | $0.5 \tan\phi (K_{p\gamma}/\cos^2\phi)$ approx | $(N_q-1)\tan(1.4\phi)$ | Vesic's $2(N_q+1)\tan\phi$ |
| Code-status in India | Academic reference | Academic reference | **Codified standard** |
| Factor of Safety | Not codified | Not codified | **$F = 3$** (prescribed) |
| KTU relevance | Sometimes asked | Sometimes asked | **Primary focus** |

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Shape Factor $S_c$ for Rectangular Footing

For a 3:1 (B:L) rectangular footing, the bearing capacity of a rectangular footing is **less** than a strip footing of the same width because of the **3D end effects**. The IS Code uses the Skempton-Hansen modification:

For strip: failure extends sideways (1D problem) → $N_c^{strip} = 5.14$ (for $\phi = 0$).

For rectangular: end effects contribute extra resistance.

$$S_c = 1 + \frac{N_q}{N_c} \cdot \frac{B}{L}$$

**Proof sketch:** The ratio $(N_q - 1)\tan\phi / N_c$ appears from superposition. For a strip footing ($B/L \to 0$): $S_c = 1$. For a square footing ($B/L = 1$): $S_c = 1 + N_q/N_c$, which is the maximum enhancement.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IS Code Method — Computational Flow Architecture

The following block diagram maps the **algorithmic flow** of computing bearing capacity using the IS Code method. This is the standard sequence a KTU student must follow while solving numerical problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Data c, phi, gamma, B, L, Df, F] --> B[Compute BC Factors Nc, Nq, Ngamma from phi]
    B --> C{Is B/L = 1? — Square}
    C -->|Yes| D[Set B/L = 1 for shape factors]
    C -->|No| E[Use B/L as is for rectangular]
    D --> F[Compute Shape Factors Sc, Sq, Sgamma]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Depth Factors dc, dq, dgamma using Df/B]
    G --> H[Compute Surcharge q = gamma x Df]
    H --> I[Compute Three Terms T1, T2, T3]
    I --> J[Sum to get Ultimate qu]
    J --> K[Subtract gamma x Df to get qnu]
    K --> L[Divide by F to get qns]
    L --> M[Output Net Safe Bearing Capacity]
```

**Interpretation:** The student moves from soil properties → bearing capacity factors → geometry-dependent factors → surcharge → ultimate capacity → net capacity → safe capacity. Any missing step is a guaranteed loss of 1-2 marks.

---

## 4.2 Types of Shallow Foundations — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shallow Foundations] --> B[Df / B less than or equal to 1]
    B --> C[1. Spread Footing]
    B --> D[2. Combined Footing]
    B --> E[3. Strap Cantilever Footing]
    B --> F[4. Mat or Raft Foundation]
    B --> G[5. Wall or Strip Footing]

    C --> C1[Isolated: Single column]
    C --> C2[Slab thickness: h]

    D --> D1[Two columns close]
    D --> D2[Rectangular or Trapezoidal shape]

    E --> E1[Outer column at property line]
    E --> E2[Connected by Strap Beam]

    F --> F1[Multiple columns: weak soil]
    F --> F2[Covers entire footprint]

    G --> G1[Continuous load-bearing wall]
    G --> G2[Strip: L much greater than B]
```

---

## 4.3 IS Code Method — Sub-System Block Architecture (Load-Bearing Components)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        I1[Soil: c, phi, gamma]
        I2[Footing: B, L, Df]
        I3[Safety: F]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING["COMPUTATION SUBSYSTEMS"]
        P1["Subsystem A: BC Factors (Nc, Nq, Ngamma)"]
        P2["Subsystem B: Shape Factors (Sc, Sq, Sgamma)"]
        P3["Subsystem C: Depth Factors (dc, dq, dgamma)"]
        P4["Subsystem D: Surcharge q"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT: BEARING CAPACITY"]
        O1[qu — Ultimate]
        O2[qnu — Net Ultimate]
        O3[qns — Net Safe]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I2 --> P3
    I1 --> P4
    P1 --> O1
    P2 --> O1
    P3 --> O1
    P4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    I3 --> O3
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — Choice of Footing Type vs. Soil Condition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Evaluate SBC Required] --> B{Soil Type}
    B -->|Strong, Uniform| C[Isolated Spread Footing]
    B -->|Weak, Variable| D[Raft or Mat Foundation]
    B -->|Column at Property Line| E[Strap Cantilever Footing]
    B -->|Two Columns Close| F[Combined Footing]
    B -->|Load-Bearing Wall| G[Wall or Strip Footing]

    C --> H[Final Design: B, Df verified]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

---

## 4.5 Shallow Foundation Failure Modes — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shallow Foundation Subjected to Load Q] --> B{Type of Failure}
    B -->|Low Q on Dense Sand| C[General Shear Failure]
    B -->|Medium Q on Loose Sand| D[Local Shear Failure]
    B -->|High Q on Soft Clay| E[Punching Shear Failure]

    C --> C1[Sudden Tilting, Clear Surface Heave]
    D --> D1[Settlement with Limited Heave]
    E --> E1[Vertical Shear Around Footing Periphery]
```
