# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State the IS Code formula for computing the ultimate bearing capacity of a shallow foundation as per IS 6403. List the shape and depth factors used in the formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The IS Code (IS 6403: 1981) formula for ultimate bearing capacity is:

$$q_u = c N_c S_c d_c + q(N_q - 1) S_q d_q + 0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma$$

where:
- $c$ = cohesion, $q = \gamma D_f$, $B$ = width, $\gamma$ = unit weight
- $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = bearing capacity factors (functions of $\phi$)
- $S_c, S_q, S_\gamma$ = **shape factors** (depend on $B/L$)
- $d_c, d_q, d_\gamma$ = **depth factors** (depend on $D_f/B$)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between an isolated footing and a combined footing. When is each preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Feature** | **Isolated Footing** | **Combined Footing** |
|---|---|---|
| Columns served | One single column | Two or more columns |
| Shape | Square / Rectangular | Rectangular / Trapezoidal |
| Use | Columns far apart, uniform soil | Columns very close, soil non-uniform |
| Economy | Economical for single columns | Economical when isolated footings overlap |
| Example | A single interior column | Two adjacent columns near property line |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **different types of shallow foundations** used in civil engineering practice. Mention the situations in which each type is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **rectangular footing** of size 3 m × 2 m is founded at a depth of 1.5 m in a soil with $c = 15$ kPa, $\phi = 25^\circ$, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. Determine the **net safe bearing capacity** using the **IS Code method**. Take factor of safety = 3. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Types of Shallow Foundations:**

1. **Isolated Spread Footing (Pad Footing)** — Supports a single column. Used when columns are spaced far apart and soil has high SBC. Shapes: square, rectangular, or stepped. **Most common** type.

2. **Combined Footing** — Supports **two or more columns**. Used when isolated footings overlap due to close column spacing or when columns are near a property line. Shape: rectangular or trapezoidal.

3. **Strap (Cantilever) Footing** — Two isolated footings connected by a **rigid strap beam**. Used when an exterior column is at a property line and cannot have a symmetric footing. The strap beam transfers the eccentric load to the interior footing.

4. **Mat / Raft Foundation** — A **single large continuous slab** supporting all columns of a structure. Used when soil has low SBC, column loads are heavy, or footings would cover > 50% of the building footprint.

5. **Wall (Strip) Footing** — Continuous footing supporting a **load-bearing wall**. Strip shape with $L \gg B$. Most common in masonry buildings.

**Diagrammatic Representation (described verbally for exam):**
- Isolated: square pad under a column.
- Combined: rectangular pad under two columns.
- Strap: two pads linked by a beam.
- Mat: large slab covering the whole building.
- Wall: long strip under a wall.

---

### Solution (b) — 7 Marks (Step-by-Step)

**Given:** $B = 2$ m (smaller), $L = 3$ m, $D_f = 1.5$ m, $c = 15$ kPa, $\phi = 25^\circ$, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, $F = 3$

**Step 1: BC Factors for $\phi = 25^\circ$** (from table or formula):

$$N_c = 20.72, \quad N_q = 10.66, \quad N_\gamma = 10.88$$

**Step 2: Surcharge**

$$q = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Shape Factors** (with $B/L = 2/3 = 0.667$)

$$S_c = 1 + \frac{N_q}{N_c} \cdot \frac{B}{L} = 1 + \frac{10.66}{20.72} \times 0.667 = 1 + 0.3431 = 1.3431$$

$$S_q = 1 + \frac{B}{L} \tan\phi = 1 + 0.667 \times \tan 25^\circ = 1 + 0.667 \times 0.4663 = 1.3111$$

$$S_\gamma = 1 - 0.4 \cdot \frac{B}{L} = 1 - 0.4 \times 0.667 = 1 - 0.2667 = 0.7333$$

**Step 4: Depth Factors** ($D_f/B = 1.5/2 = 0.75 \leq 1$)

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \times 0.75 = 1.30$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2 \tan 25^\circ (1 - \sin 25^\circ)^2 \times 0.75$$

$$\sin 25^\circ = 0.4226 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1-0.4226)^2 = (0.5774)^2 = 0.3334$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2 \times 0.4663 \times 0.3334 \times 0.75 = 1 + 0.2333 = 1.2333$$

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

**Step 5: Three Terms**

Cohesion:

$$T_1 = c N_c S_c d_c = 15 \times 20.72 \times 1.3431 \times 1.30 = 542.86 \text{ kPa}$$

Surcharge:

$$T_2 = q(N_q - 1) S_q d_q = 27 \times 9.66 \times 1.3111 \times 1.2333 = 419.91 \text{ kPa}$$

Self-weight:

$$T_3 = 0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma = 0.5 \times 2 \times 18 \times 10.88 \times 0.7333 \times 1.0 = 143.61 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 6: Ultimate and Safe Capacity**

$$q_u = 542.86 + 419.91 + 143.61 = 1106.38 \text{ kPa}$$

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1106.38 - 27 = 1079.38 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{q_{ns} = \frac{1079.38}{3} = 359.79 \text{ kPa} \approx 360 \text{ kPa}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the **expressions for bearing capacity factors** $N_c$ and $N_q$ as adopted by IS 6403. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **square footing** of size 1.5 m × 1.5 m is placed at a depth of 1.0 m in a sand deposit with $c = 0$, $\phi = 35^\circ$, $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³. Compute the **net ultimate and net safe bearing capacity** as per IS Code. Take F = 3. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Derivation of $N_q$:**

The $N_q$ factor is derived assuming **weightless soil** with a surcharge $q$ applied at ground level. The failure surface consists of three zones:
- Zone I: **Active wedge** beneath the footing (angle $45 + \phi/2$ with horizontal)
- Zone II: **Log-spiral radial shear zone**
- Zone III: **Passive wedge** (angle $45 - \phi/2$ with horizontal)

Using equilibrium of forces along the slip surface and integrating along the log-spiral:

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2\left(45 + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot K_p$$

where $K_p$ is Rankine's passive earth pressure coefficient.

**Derivation of $N_c$:**

Starting from the superposition principle, the bearing capacity from cohesion alone is found by replacing the surcharge in the $N_q$ term with an equivalent cohesion contribution. Equating the work done by the surcharge and cohesion on the slip surface:

$$N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi$$

**Derivation of $N_\gamma$ (Vesic, 1973 — semi-empirical):**

The self-weight term requires integration over the 3D failure wedge. Vesic matched the result to:

$$N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1) \tan\phi$$

which closely matches experimental data for $\phi$ from 0° to 45°.

---

### Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Square footing $B = L = 1.5$ m, $D_f = 1.0$ m, $c = 0$, $\phi = 35^\circ$, $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³, $F = 3$

**Step 1: BC Factors for $\phi = 35^\circ$**

$$N_c = 46.12, \quad N_q = 33.30, \quad N_\gamma = 48.03$$

**Step 2: Surcharge**

$$q = 19 \times 1.0 = 19 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Shape Factors** (square, $B/L = 1$)

$$S_c = 1 + \frac{N_q}{N_c} \times 1 = 1 + \frac{33.30}{46.12} = 1.7220$$

$$S_q = 1 + 1 \times \tan 35^\circ = 1 + 0.7002 = 1.7002$$

$$S_\gamma = 1 - 0.4 \times 1 = 0.6$$

**Step 4: Depth Factors** ($D_f/B = 1.0/1.5 = 0.667$)

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \times 0.667 = 1.2667$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2 \tan 35^\circ (1 - \sin 35^\circ)^2 \times 0.667$$

$$\sin 35^\circ = 0.5736 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1-0.5736)^2 = 0.1817$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2 \times 0.7002 \times 0.1817 \times 0.667 = 1 + 0.1697 = 1.1697$$

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

**Step 5: Three Terms**

Cohesion term (with $c = 0$): $T_1 = 0$

Surcharge term:

$$T_2 = q(N_q - 1) S_q d_q = 19 \times 32.30 \times 1.7002 \times 1.1697 = 1220.0 \text{ kPa}$$

Self-weight term:

$$T_3 = 0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma = 0.5 \times 1.5 \times 19 \times 48.03 \times 0.6 \times 1.0 = 410.66 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 6: Final Answer**

$$q_u = 0 + 1220.0 + 410.66 = 1630.66 \text{ kPa}$$

$$q_{nu} = 1630.66 - 19 = 1611.66 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{q_{ns} = \frac{1611.66}{3} = 537.22 \text{ kPa}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose 2-3 Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $B$ and $L$:** In a rectangular footing, $B$ is **always the smaller dimension** and $L$ is the larger. Many students swap them, leading to wrong $B/L$ and incorrect shape factors.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Surcharge Term** $q(N_q - 1)$: Some students incorrectly write $q \cdot N_q$. The **-1** is critical.
>
> 3. **Applying depth factors for $D_f/B > 1$:** The IS Code depth factor formulas used here are valid **only for $D_f/B \leq 1$**. For deeper footings, the footing is no longer "shallow" and the deep foundation methodology applies.
>
> 4. **Not subtracting overburden** when computing **net** bearing capacity: This is the most common 1-mark error.
>
> 5. **Using $\phi$ in radians** in the formula $N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi}$: Always use $\phi$ in **degrees** inside the $\tan$ function, but the **$\pi$ is in radians**. KTU calculators default to degrees — be careful.
>
> 6. **Skipping units**: Always mention kPa or kN/m² with the final answer.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the Factor of Safety**: KTU has separate marks allocated for $q_{nu}$ and $q_{ns}$. Missing the division by $F$ loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- ✅ **IS 6403: 1981** is the codal method for bearing capacity in India.
- ✅ **General formula:** $q_u = cN_cS_cd_c + q(N_q-1)S_qd_q + 0.5B\gamma N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma$
- ✅ **Vesic's factors:** $N_q = e^{\pi\tan\phi}K_p$, $N_c = (N_q-1)\cot\phi$, $N_\gamma = 2(N_q+1)\tan\phi$
- ✅ **Shape factors depend on $B/L$**, not just $B/L = 1$.
- ✅ **Depth factors valid only for $D_f/B \leq 1$**.
- ✅ **$S_c = 1 + (N_q/N_c)(B/L)$** — most-tested shape factor.
- ✅ **$d_c = 1 + 0.4(D_f/B)$** — most-tested depth factor.
- ✅ **Net** bearing capacity = $q_u - \gamma D_f$.
- ✅ **Safe** bearing capacity = $q_{nu} / F$ with **$F = 3$** (default).
- ✅ **Shallow foundations** are classified into **5 types**: Isolated, Combined, Strap, Mat/Raft, Wall/Strip.
- ✅ **Isolated footing** = single column; **Combined footing** = two close columns; **Strap footing** = property-line column with a beam; **Mat** = entire building footprint; **Wall footing** = strip under masonry wall.
- ✅ **Square footing** has $B/L = 1$ → maximum shape factor enhancement.
- ✅ **Strip footing** has $L/B \to \infty$ → all shape factors = 1.
- ✅ Always check the **failure mode** (general, local, punching) before applying BC factors.
- ✅ For purely **cohesive soil** ($\phi = 0$), use **Skempton's $N_c = 5.0$ (square) / 6.0 (strip or square at $D_f/B \geq 2.5$)**.
- ✅ **Critical mistake to avoid**: Writing $q \cdot N_q$ instead of $q \cdot (N_q - 1)$ in the surcharge term.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spread Footing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Spread Footing**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. SPREAD FOOTING — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Spread Footing** (also termed an *isolated footing*, *pad footing*, or *shallow footing*) is a shallow foundation unit, typically constructed of reinforced concrete, that transmits the concentrated superimposed load from a single column or a linear load from a wall to the underlying soil by spreading (or "spreading out") the load over a sufficiently large horizontal area at a shallow depth, such that the induced contact pressure does not exceed the allowable bearing capacity of the soil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Exact):**
> A spread footing is a shallow foundation whose depth ($D_f$) is generally less than or equal to its width ($B$), i.e., $D_f \le B$, and whose primary function is to spread the structural load over an area of soil sufficient to keep the bearing pressure within safe limits.

The general classification within *Shallow Foundations* under the KTU 2024 scheme is:

$$\text{Shallow Foundations} \begin{cases} \text{Spread Footings} \begin{cases} \text{Single / Isolated / Column Footings} \\ \text{Combined Footings} \\ \text{Strap (Connecting) Footings} \\ \text{Continuous / Wall / Strip Footings} \\ \text{Mat / Raft Footings} \end{cases} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Although *Mat Footings* are technically shallow foundations, the KTU 2024 scheme treats them as a distinct topic. For **Module 4**, the focus remains on **Isolated, Combined, Strap, and Continuous (Strip/Wall) Spread Footings**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Snowshoe Principle"

Imagine a person weighing **70 kg** standing on a freshly fallen snow. If the person stands on **bare boots** with a sole area of roughly **300 cm²**, the contact pressure is enormous and the foot sinks deep into the snow. But if the same person wears **snowshoes** with an area of roughly **2,500 cm²**, the contact pressure drops dramatically, and the person walks on top of the snow without sinking.

This is precisely how a spread footing functions in soil mechanics:

- The **column** delivers a small "footprint" with a very high concentrated load.
- The **soil** is the "snow" — it has a finite capacity (the *bearing capacity*) to resist pressure before failing in shear or undergoing excessive settlement.
- The **spread footing** is the "snowshoe" — it enlarges the contact area, thereby reducing the contact pressure to a value the soil can safely sustain.

$$q = \frac{P}{A}$$

Where:
- $q$ = contact pressure (kN/m² or kPa) — must be $\le q_{allow}$
- $P$ = column load (kN)
- $A$ = footing base area (m²)

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Convention):**
> - Unit weight of concrete (RCC) used in footing self-weight: **$\gamma_c = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3$**
> - Unit weight of soil (general): **$\gamma = 15\text{–}20 \text{ kN/m}^3$**
> - Depth of foundation for residential buildings: **$D_f = 0.9 \text{ m to } 1.5 \text{ m}$**
> - Minimum depth for stability against frost/scour: **$D_f \ge 0.9 \text{ m}$ (IS 1080)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Functional Role of a Spread Footing

A spread footing performs **three engineering functions simultaneously**:

1. **Load Transfer Mechanism**: Distributes the concentrated column/wall load over a wider soil footprint.
2. **Bearing Pressure Reduction**: Lowers the induced soil pressure below the *ultimate bearing capacity* $q_u$ (with adequate Factor of Safety, FS).
3. **Structural Anchorage**: Anchors the superstructure against overturning, sliding, and uplift (in seismic/wind zones).

## 2.2 Design Philosophy — Two-Boundary Criterion (KTU Mandate)

A spread footing design must satisfy **TWO** independent criteria, both mandatory per IS 456:2000 & IS 6403:1981:

| Criterion | Inequality | KTU Reference |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| **Bearing Capacity** | $q_{net} \le \dfrac{q_u}{FS}$ | Strength criterion |
| **Settlement** | $S_{actual} \le S_{allowable}$ (usually 25 mm for isolated footings on sand, 40 mm on clay) | Serviceability criterion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently frames spread-footing problems as: *"Design an isolated footing for given column load and soil properties, satisfying both bearing capacity and settlement criteria."*

## 2.3 Types of Spread Footings — Classification Logic

| Type | Geometry | Use Case |
|------|----------|----------|
| **Isolated / Single Footing** | Square, Rectangular, or Circular pad under one column | Most common; when columns are far apart and soil is good |
| **Combined Footing** | Rectangular or Trapezoidal supporting **two columns** | Property line footings; when one column load is offset |
| **Strap / Connecting Footing** | Two isolated footings connected by a *rigid beam (strap)* | When exterior column is at property line and cannot spread laterally |
| **Continuous / Wall / Strip Footing** | Long footing under a continuous wall | Load-bearing masonry walls |

## 2.4 The Bearing Capacity Framework (Theoretical Foundation)

The most critical theoretical underpinning is **Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Theory (1943)**. Terzaghi assumed a general shear failure mechanism and proposed the ultimate bearing capacity for a *strip footing* as:

$$q_u = c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$$

Where:
- $c$ = cohesion of soil (kPa)
- $q = \gamma \cdot D_f$ = surcharge / overburden pressure at foundation level (kPa)
- $\gamma$ = unit weight of soil below foundation (kN/m³)
- $B$ = width of footing (m)
- $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = **Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors** (dimensionless), functions only of the angle of internal friction $\phi$

For different footing shapes, Terzaghi modified the equation:

### KTU Formula Sheet — Ultimate Bearing Capacity Equations

| Footing Shape | Equation (Terzaghi) |
|---------------|---------------------|
| **Strip (Continuous)** | $q_u = c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$ |
| **Square** | $q_u = 1.3 \cdot c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.4 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$ |
| **Circular** | $q_u = 1.3 \cdot c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.3 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$ |
| **Rectangular** (general) | $q_u = c \cdot N_c \cdot s_c + q \cdot N_q \cdot s_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma \cdot s_\gamma$ |

### KTU Formula Sheet — Bearing Capacity Factors

| Factor | Formula | Notes |
|--------|---------|-------|
| $N_q$ | $e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \tan^2\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | Purely function of $\phi$ |
| $N_c$ | $(N_q - 1) \cdot \cot\phi$ | For $\phi = 0$: $N_c = 5.7$ |
| $N_\gamma$ (Terzaghi) | $\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{K_p}{\cos^2\phi} - 1\right) \tan\phi$ where $K_p = \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)$ | Approximation: $N_\gamma \approx (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$ |

### KTU Formula Sheet — Allowable / Safe Bearing Capacity

| Quantity | Formula | Meaning |
|----------|---------|---------|
| **Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity** | $q_{nu} = q_u - q = q_u - \gamma D_f$ | Removes overburden effect |
| **Net Safe Bearing Capacity** | $q_{ns} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FS}$ | Used in design (settlement of soil from depth already complete) |
| **Gross Safe Bearing Capacity** | $q_{s} = \dfrac{q_u}{FS} = q_{ns} + \gamma D_f$ | Includes overburden |
| **Allowable Bearing Pressure** | $q_{allow} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{q_u}{FS},\, q_{settlement-based}\right)$ | Governing value |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Factor of Safety (IS 6403:1981, KTU Convention):**
> - **FS = 3.0** for *normal* structures on cohesive soils.
> - **FS = 2.5 to 3.0** for structures on cohesionless soils.

## 2.5 Extended Bearing Capacity Equation (Meyerhof / Hansen / Vesic — IS 6403)

The KTU 2024 scheme requires familiarity with the **IS Code General Equation**, which incorporates shape, depth, and inclination factors:

$$q_u = c \cdot N_c \cdot s_c \cdot d_c \cdot i_c + q \cdot N_q \cdot s_q \cdot d_q \cdot i_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B' \cdot N_\gamma \cdot s_\gamma \cdot d_\gamma \cdot i_\gamma$$

Where:
- $s_c, s_q, s_\gamma$ = **Shape factors**
- $d_c, d_q, d_\gamma$ = **Depth factors**
- $i_c, i_q, i_\gamma$ = **Inclination (load) factors**
- $B' = B - 2e$ = **effective width** (for eccentric loading)

### Shape Factors (Meyerhof / Hansen)

| Factor | Formula |
|--------|---------|
| $s_c$ | $1 + \dfrac{N_q}{N_c} \cdot \dfrac{B}{L}$ |
| $s_q$ | $1 + \dfrac{B}{L} \tan\phi$ |
| $s_\gamma$ | $1 - 0.4 \dfrac{B}{L}$ |

### Depth Factors (Hansen)

| Factor | Formula (for $D_f / B \le 1$) |
|--------|-------------------------------|
| $d_c$ | $1 + 0.4 \dfrac{D_f}{B}$ |
| $d_q$ | $1 + 2 \tan\phi (1 - \sin\phi)^2 \dfrac{D_f}{B}$ |
| $d_\gamma$ | $1.0$ |

## 2.6 Eccentric Loading on Spread Footings (Meyerhof's Effective Area Concept)

When a column transmits a moment $M$ in addition to vertical load $P$, an eccentricity $e = M/P$ develops. The footing is then designed for:

$$B' = B - 2e, \quad L' = L \text{ (if no eccentricity about the other axis)}$$

The *kern limit* (no tension at base) requires:
- $e \le \dfrac{B}{6}$ for rectangular footing (triangular stress block, no tension)
- $e > \dfrac{B}{6}$ ⇒ part of footing lifts off, contact area shrinks, contact pressure must be redistributed

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Spread footings are the **most economical foundation** when soil conditions near the surface (within 2–3 m) provide adequate bearing capacity. They are used in:

- Low-to-medium-rise residential and commercial buildings.
- Bridge abutments (continuous wall footings).
- Industrial warehouses with isolated column loads.
- Light-machinery foundations.

When soil near surface is weak, **deep foundations** (piles, piers, caissons) are preferred — this is the boundary condition for switching from spread to deep.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Equation — General Shear Failure (Strip Footing)

### Assumptions of Terzaghi's Theory

1. The footing is **strip** (length $L \gg$ width $B$), representing plane-strain conditions.
2. The soil is **homogeneous, isotropic**, and extends to a great depth.
3. Failure occurs as **general shear** — soil wedges displace along well-defined slip surfaces.
4. The footing base is **rough** (no sliding between footing and soil).
5. The surcharge $q = \gamma D_f$ acts at the level of the footing base, replaced by an equivalent surcharge load.

### Geometry of the Failure Surface

At ultimate load, three distinct zones form beneath the footing:

| Zone | Shape | Function |
|------|-------|----------|
| **Zone I** (under footing) | Triangular wedge, angle $\phi$ with horizontal at base edges | Active wedge — moves downward with footing |
| **Zone II** | Logarithmic spiral / radial shear zone | Transition — radial shear |
| **Zone III** | Passive Rankine wedge, inclined at $(45^\circ - \phi/2)$ with horizontal | Passive zone — pushed up and outward |

### Equilibrium of Zone I (Wedge Under Footing)

The triangular wedge directly beneath the footing has:
- Base length = $B/2$ (half-width per side)
- Inclination of sides with horizontal = $\phi$
- Weight of wedge per unit length = $\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \gamma \cdot D_f \cdot \dfrac{B}{2 \tan\phi}$ (approximation; standard result taken as $\dfrac{\gamma B^2}{8\tan\phi}$ for symmetric wedge considering surcharge)

Forces acting on the wedge (per unit length of strip):
- Vertical load at base from footing = $q_u \cdot (B/2)$ per side
- Surcharge on top = $q \cdot (B/2\tan\phi)$
- Cohesive resistance = $c \cdot (B/2 \cdot \sec\phi)$ along two inclined surfaces
- Weight of wedge = $W$

Summing vertical forces and equating to passive resistance from Zone II & III, then substituting the Rankine passive coefficient $K_p = \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)$, after extensive algebraic manipulation (rigorously performed in Terzaghi's 1943 paper), the final closed-form result is:

$$q_u = c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$$

This is Terzaghi's **General Bearing Capacity Equation** for a strip footing.

### Derivation of $N_c$ and $N_q$ (from Rankine Theory)

Consider the surcharge and cohesion components separately. The passive resistance at angle $(45^\circ - \phi/2)$ gives:

$$N_q = \frac{e^{2(45^\circ + \phi/2)\tan\phi}}{2\cos^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{(intermediate terms)}}$$

After simplification (standard textbook result):

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \tan^2\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$$

For $\phi = 0$ (purely cohesive soil, $\phi$-u = 0 analysis):

$$N_c = \lim_{\phi \to 0}(N_q - 1)\cot\phi = 5.7 \quad \text{(Skempton's value)}$$

$$N_q = 1.0$$

$$N_\gamma = 0$$

### Numerical Verification Table (Standard KTU Reference Values)

| $\phi^\circ$ | $N_c$ | $N_q$ | $N_\gamma$ (Terzaghi) |
|--------------|-------|-------|------------------------|
| 0 | 5.7 | 1.0 | 0.0 |
| 5 | 7.3 | 1.6 | 0.5 |
| 10 | 9.6 | 2.7 | 1.2 |
| 15 | 12.9 | 4.4 | 2.5 |
| 20 | 17.7 | 7.4 | 5.0 |
| 25 | 25.1 | 12.7 | 9.7 |
| 30 | 37.2 | 22.5 | 19.7 |
| 35 | 57.8 | 41.4 | 42.4 |
| 40 | 95.7 | 81.3 | 100.4 |
| 45 | 172.3 | 173.3 | 297.5 |

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Required Footing Area for Given Load

This is the most common KTU Part-B problem. The derivation framework:

### Step 1: Compute Total Service Load at Footing Base

$$P_{total} = P_{column} + W_{footing} + W_{soil\_above\_footing}$$

$$P_{total} = P + \gamma_c \cdot A \cdot t + \gamma \cdot A \cdot (D_f - t)$$

Where $t$ = thickness of footing, $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³, $\gamma \approx 18$–$20$ kN/m³.

### Step 2: Apply Bearing Pressure Criterion

$$\frac{P_{total}}{A} \le q_{s} \text{ (gross safe bearing capacity)}$$

Iterating for an assumed footing thickness (typically $t \approx B/2$ to $B$ for isolated footings), solve for $A$:

$$A \ge \frac{P_{column}}{q_s - \bar{\gamma} \cdot D_f}$$

Where $\bar{\gamma} = $ weighted average of concrete and soil unit weights above footing base.

### Step 3: Choose Footing Geometry

For a *square* footing: $A = B^2 \Rightarrow B = \sqrt{A}$.
For a *rectangular* footing with aspect ratio $\alpha = L/B$: $A = \alpha B^2 \Rightarrow B = \sqrt{A/\alpha}$.

### Step 4: Check Eccentricity (if applicable)

$$e_x = \frac{M_x}{P_{total}}, \quad e_y = \frac{M_y}{P_{total}}$$

Both must satisfy $|e_x| \le L/6$ and $|e_y| \le B/6$ (no-tension condition).

### Step 5: Design for Bending (One-Way & Two-Way Shear)

Bending moment at critical section (face of column):

$$M_{ux} = \frac{q_{u,design}}{L} \cdot \frac{(B - b_c)^2}{8}$$

Where $b_c$ = column dimension perpendicular to direction considered, and $q_{u,design} = 1.5 \cdot q_{service}$ (limit-state design per IS 456).

Required steel area:

$$A_{st} = \frac{M_{ux}}{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot d}$$

Where $d = \text{effective depth}$, $f_y = 415$ MPa (Fe415) or 500 MPa (Fe500).

## 3.3 Analytical Solution to a Canonical KTU-Style Problem

**Problem Statement:** Design an isolated square footing for a column of size $400\text{ mm} \times 400\text{ mm}$ carrying a service load of **1000 kN**. The soil has $\phi = 30^\circ$, $c = 0$, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, $D_f = 1.5$ m. Use FS = 3.

### Step 1: Bearing Capacity Factors (from table for $\phi = 30^\circ$)

$$N_c = 37.2, \quad N_q = 22.5, \quad N_\gamma = 19.7$$

### Step 2: Ultimate Bearing Capacity (Square Footing, Terzaghi)

$$q_u = 1.3 \cdot c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.4 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$$

Since $c = 0$ and $q = \gamma \cdot D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27$ kPa:

$$q_u = 0 + 27 \times 22.5 + 0.4 \times 18 \times B \times 19.7$$

$$q_u = 607.5 + 141.84 \cdot B \;\; \text{(kPa)}$$

### Step 3: Net Safe Bearing Capacity

$$q_{nu} = q_u - q = 607.5 + 141.84B - 27 = 580.5 + 141.84B$$

$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{FS} = \frac{580.5 + 141.84B}{3}$$

### Step 4: Required Area (Trial-and-Iterate)

Initial guess: $B = 1.5$ m. Then $A = 2.25$ m².

$$\text{Net upward capacity} = q_{ns} = \frac{580.5 + 141.84 \times 1.5}{3} = \frac{793.26}{3} = 264.42 \text{ kPa}$$

Required area:

$$A_{req} = \frac{P}{q_{ns} - \bar{\gamma} D_f} = \frac{1000}{264.42 - 20 \times 1.5} = \frac{1000}{234.42} = 4.27 \text{ m}^2$$

$B_{req} = \sqrt{4.27} = 2.07$ m. Try $B = 2.1$ m.

### Step 5: Verify with $B = 2.1$ m

$$q_{nu} = 580.5 + 141.84 \times 2.1 = 580.5 + 297.86 = 878.36 \text{ kPa}$$

$$q_{ns} = 878.36 / 3 = 292.79 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\text{Pressure imposed} = \frac{1000}{2.1^2} + 20 \times 1.5 = 226.76 + 30 = 256.76 \text{ kPa} < 292.79 \;\; \checkmark$$

Hence **provide isolated square footing: $2.1 \text{ m} \times 2.1 \text{ m}$**.

### Step 6: Thickness by Two-Way (Punching) Shear

$$d \ge \frac{P_{punch}}{b_o \cdot \tau_c}$$

Where $b_o$ = perimeter of critical section at $d/2$ from column face = $4(b_c + d) = 4(0.4 + d)$. Using $\tau_c = 0.25\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (for $f_{ck} = 20$ MPa, $\tau_c = 0.25\sqrt{20} \times 1000 = 1118$ kPa, but IS 456 gives table values). With assumed $d = 350$ mm, check $V_p \le \tau_c \cdot b_o \cdot d$. This iterative step typically yields $d \approx 350\text{–}500$ mm for column loads of $1000$ kN.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Footing Type Selection

| Condition | Recommended Footing Type |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| Columns spaced $> 3\text{ m}$ apart AND soil $q_s \ge 150$ kPa | **Isolated Footing** |
| Column on property line AND soil $q_s \ge 200$ kPa | **Strap Footing** |
| Two columns close together AND one eccentric | **Combined Footing** |
| Continuous wall load $< 150$ kN/m | **Wall/Strip Footing** |
| Soil $q_s < 75$ kPa OR columns spaced $< 1.5\text{ m}$ apart | **Mat/Raft Footing** |
| Soil $q_s < 50$ kPa in upper 5 m | **Deep Foundation** (Pile) |

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART-A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define a spread footing. List its different types.  [3 Marks]  `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A spread footing is a shallow foundation whose depth is generally less than or equal to its width, used to transmit concentrated loads from columns or walls to the soil by enlarging the contact area so that the induced pressure remains within the safe bearing capacity of the soil.

**Types:**
1. Isolated / Single / Pad Footing
2. Combined Footing
3. Strap / Connecting Footing
4. Continuous / Wall / Strip Footing

*(Full 3 marks awarded for definition + listing all four types.)*

---

### Question 2. What is meant by net safe bearing capacity? How is it different from gross safe bearing capacity?  [3 Marks]  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

The **net safe bearing capacity** ($q_{ns}$) is the net ultimate bearing capacity divided by the factor of safety:

$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_u - q}{FS} = \frac{q_u - \gamma D_f}{FS}$$

The **gross safe bearing capacity** ($q_s$) is the ultimate bearing capacity divided by the factor of safety:

$$q_s = \frac{q_u}{FS}$$

The key difference is that net safe bearing capacity excludes the overburden pressure $q = \gamma D_f$, since the soil's settlement due to overburden is already complete. Net safe bearing capacity is used directly in design to size the footing area; gross safe bearing capacity is used when comparing total imposed pressure at the footing base.

---

## 5.2 PART-B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Isolated Footing Design  [14 Marks]  `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q. A(a)** Define the terms: Ultimate Bearing Capacity, Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity, Allowable Bearing Pressure, and Factor of Safety. How are these related for design of an isolated spread footing?  **[7 Marks]**

**Q. A(b)** Design an isolated square footing for a $450 \text{ mm} \times 450 \text{ mm}$ column carrying a service load of $1500 \text{ kN}$. Soil properties: $\phi = 30^\circ$, $c = 0$, $\gamma = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $D_f = 1.8 \text{ m}$. Use FS = 3. Take $\gamma_c = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3$.  **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

- **Ultimate Bearing Capacity ($q_u$)**: Maximum gross pressure the soil can sustain at the foundation level without shear failure.
- **Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity ($q_{nu}$)**: $q_u - \gamma D_f$ (removes the weight of overburden already in place).
- **Allowable Bearing Pressure ($q_{allow}$)**: Maximum pressure that can be applied with adequate safety against both shear failure and excessive settlement.
- **Factor of Safety (FS)**: Ratio of ultimate to allowable strength; standard IS 6403 value = 3.

**Design Relation:**

$$q_{allow} = \min\left(\frac{q_u}{FS},\ q_{settlement-based}\right)$$

For purely cohesionless soils ($c=0$), settlement criteria often govern design.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1 — Bearing Capacity Factors (from table, $\phi = 30^\circ$):**

$$N_c = 37.2, \quad N_q = 22.5, \quad N_\gamma = 19.7$$

**Step 2 — Ultimate Bearing Capacity (Square Footing):**

$$q_u = 1.3 c N_c + q N_q + 0.4 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

With $c = 0$, $q = 19 \times 1.8 = 34.2$ kPa:

$$q_u = 0 + 34.2 \times 22.5 + 0.4 \times 19 \times B \times 19.7$$

$$q_u = 769.5 + 149.72 B \;\; \text{(kPa)}$$

**Step 3 — Net Safe Bearing Capacity:**

$$q_{ns} = \frac{769.5 + 149.72 B - 34.2}{3} = \frac{735.3 + 149.72 B}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Iteration for $B$:**

Assume $B = 2.5$ m ⇒ $q_{ns} = \frac{735.3 + 374.3}{3} = 369.87$ kPa.

$$A_{req} = \frac{P}{q_{ns} - \gamma_{avg} D_f}$$

Take $\gamma_{avg} = 21$ kN/m³ (weighted): $A_{req} = \frac{1500}{369.87 - 21 \times 1.8} = \frac{1500}{332.07} = 4.52 \text{ m}^2$.

Thus $B_{req} = \sqrt{4.52} = 2.13$ m. **Adopt $B = 2.2$ m.**

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$q_{ns} = \frac{735.3 + 149.72 \times 2.2}{3} = \frac{735.3 + 329.38}{3} = 354.89$ kPa.

Imposed pressure = $\frac{1500}{2.2^2} + 21 \times 1.8 = 309.92 + 37.8 = 347.72 < 354.89$ ✓.

**Provide isolated square footing: $2.2 \text{ m} \times 2.2 \text{ m}$.**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Combined Footing + Eccentricity  [14 Marks]  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q. B(a)** What are the situations that necessitate the use of a combined footing? Sketch a typical combined footing and explain how its centroid is located relative to the resultant of column loads.  **[7 Marks]**

**Q. B(b)** A column of size $500 \text{ mm} \times 500 \text{ mm}$ carries a service load of $1200 \text{ kN}$ at the corner of a property line. The soil has $q_s = 200$ kPa and $D_f = 1.5$ m. Design an appropriate spread footing and check for eccentricity.  **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

A **combined footing** is provided when:
1. Two columns are very close (less than ~3 m apart) so isolated footings would overlap.
2. An exterior column is near the property line and its isolated footing cannot spread laterally without crossing the line.
3. Soil bearing capacity is low, requiring a large bearing area.
4. The footing must resist large moments or uplift.

The **resultant of the two column loads** must coincide with the **centroid of the footing** for uniform pressure distribution. If $P_1$ and $P_2$ are column loads at distances $x_1$ and $x_2$ from an arbitrary reference, the centroid of the footing (measured from the same reference) is:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{P_1 x_1 + P_2 x_2}{P_1 + P_2}$$

The footing is shaped (rectangular or trapezoidal) so that this centroid coincides with the geometric center of the base.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Situation:** Column on property line → **Strap (Connecting) Footing** is the appropriate choice, paired with an interior column footing.

**Step 1 — Footing area for the corner column:**

Required bearing area = $\dfrac{P}{q_s - \gamma_{avg} D_f} = \dfrac{1200}{200 - 20 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{1200}{170} = 7.06 \text{ m}^2$.

Take square footing $B_1 = L_1 = \sqrt{7.06} = 2.66$ m → adopt $2.7 \text{ m} \times 2.7 \text{ m}$.

**Step 2 — Eccentricity check (column at property line):**

If the column is exactly at the property line edge, the available projection beyond the column face is only on one side (interior side). Set eccentricity $e = B/2 - $ projection. For no tension: $e \le B/6$.

Take $e = 0.2$ m (small permissible eccentricity). Effective width $B' = B - 2e = 2.7 - 0.4 = 2.3$ m. Recompute pressure:

$$q = \frac{1200}{2.3 \times 2.7} + 20 \times 1.5 = 193.1 + 30 = 223.1 \text{ kPa} > 200 \text{ kPa} \;\; \text{(unsafe)}$$

**Step 3 — Adopt Strap Footing:**

Provide an interior column footing (assume interior column carries 1500 kN) and connect it via a *rigid strap beam*. The strap beam transfers moment from the eccentric corner column to the interior footing, neutralizing eccentricity. This is the standard engineering solution.

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting self-weight of footing** when computing service pressure. Always add $\gamma_c A t + \gamma A (D_f - t)$ to the column load.
> 2. **Using gross $q_s$ instead of net $q_{ns}$** in the area formula. KTU specifically tests whether students know that $A_{req} = P / (q_{ns} - \bar{\gamma} D_f)$.
> 3. **Wrong bearing capacity equation** for square footings. Remember the coefficients: $1.3 c N_c$, $0.4 \gamma B N_\gamma$ (Terzaghi).
> 4. **Ignoring eccentricity check** for columns on property lines. Always compute $e$ and verify $e \le B/6$.
> 5. **Wrong choice of footing type** — ISOLATED for normal cases, STRAP for property-line columns, COMBINED for two close columns, MAT for very weak soil.
> 6. **No settlement check** — KTU penalizes up to 2 marks if only bearing capacity is verified.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [ ] **Spread Footing** = shallow foundation, $D_f \le B$, spreads load over wide area to keep pressure $\le q_{allow}$.
- [ ] **Four types**: Isolated, Combined, Strap, Continuous (Wall).
- [ ] **Terzaghi's Equation (Strip):** $q_u = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$.
- [ ] **Square Footing Modification:** replace $N_c$ coefficient with $1.3$ and $\gamma B$ coefficient with $0.4$.
- [ ] **Circular Footing:** $1.3 c N_c + q N_q + 0.3 \gamma B N_\gamma$.
- [ ] **Net Safe Bearing Capacity:** $q_{ns} = (q_u - \gamma D_f)/FS$.
- [ ] **Required Area:** $A_{req} = P / (q_{ns} - \bar{\gamma} D_f)$.
- [ ] **FS values:** 3.0 for cohesive, 2.5–3.0 for cohesionless (IS 6403:1981).
- [ ] **Eccentric Loading:** use effective width $B' = B - 2e$; kern limit $e \le B/6$ to avoid tension.
- [ ] **Bearing Capacity Factors** are functions *only* of $\phi$ — recall table for $\phi = 0^\circ, 30^\circ, 45^\circ$.
- [ ] **Two design criteria**: bearing capacity AND settlement — both must be satisfied.
- [ ] **Footing type selection rule**: weak soil → combined/mat/raft; property line → strap; normal isolated columns → isolated square/rectangular.
- [ ] **Strap footing** uses a *rigid connecting beam* to transfer moment from eccentric column to an interior footing.
- [ ] **Meyerhof/Hansen shape factors**: $s_c = 1 + (N_q/N_c)(B/L)$, $s_q = 1 + (B/L)\tan\phi$, $s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4(B/L)$.
- [ ] **Depth factors** for $D_f/B \le 1$: $d_c = 1 + 0.4(D_f/B)$, $d_q = 1 + 2\tan\phi(1-\sin\phi)^2(D_f/B)$.
- [ ] Always **include footing self-weight** ($24 \text{ kN/m}^3 \times$ volume) in total pressure calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safe Bearing Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safe Bearing Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Safe Bearing Pressure (SBP)** is defined as the maximum net intensity of loading that the soil can safely support **without the risk of shear failure** and **without causing intolerable settlement** of the foundation.

Mathematically, it is the smaller of the two values obtained from:
1. **Shear Failure Criterion:** Ultimate Net Bearing Capacity divided by a suitable Factor of Safety (typically **F.S. = 3**).
2. **Settlement Criterion:** The net bearing pressure corresponding to the **permissible (safe) settlement** of the structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction students ALWAYS confuse:**
> - **Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC)** is expressed in terms of **total (gross) load** — it is the *gross* pressure.
> - **Safe Bearing Pressure (SBP)** is generally the **net pressure** (pressure in excess of overburden).
> - The numerical value of **Net SBP = Net SBC / F.S.** (this is what governs design).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are placing a heavy wooden plank on soft, wet clay.
- If you push **too hard (Ultimate Load)**, the clay **shears and the plank sinks suddenly** — this is *shear failure*.
- Even if you don't cause sudden failure, the plank will **gradually sink over days** — this is *settlement*.

Now, "Safe Bearing Pressure" is like the **maximum comfortable weight** you can put on the plank so that:
- It does **not break the clay suddenly**, AND
- It does **not sink uncomfortably over time**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two checks?** Because in sands, settlement controls (sudden failure is rare). In clays, both controls matter. The engineer takes the **minimum** of the two — because the soil's worst weakness decides the safe load.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Factor of Safety (F.S.) against shear failure:** **3.0** (IS 6403:1981 recommendation for cohesive soils).
- **Factor of Safety against settlement:** Typically **2.0 to 3.0**.
- **Permissible Settlement:** **25 mm** for isolated foundations on clays (IS 1904); **40 mm** for rafts (differential settlement ≤ 20 mm).
- **Standard Plate Size in PLT:** **0.30 m × 0.30 m** (square).
- **Unit Weight of soil (γ):** typically **17 to 20 kN/m³** for most soils, **9.81 to 10 kN/m³** for water (γ_w).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The complete evaluation of SBP for any given foundation follows this **decision tree logic**:

**STEP 1 — Establish Ultimate Bearing Capacity (q_u or q_ult):**
- Use Terzaghi's, Meyerhof's, IS code, or any other accepted equation depending on soil type.
- This gives the **Gross Ultimate Bearing Capacity (q_ult,gross)**.

**STEP 2 — Convert to Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity (q_u,net):**

$$q_{u,net} = q_{ult,gross} - \gamma \cdot D_f$$

Where:
- $\gamma$ = unit weight of soil above the foundation base
- $D_f$ = depth of foundation

> [!NOTE]
> The overburden pressure $\gamma D_f$ is **subtracted** because the soil there is already in equilibrium — only the *additional* pressure causes failure.

**STEP 3 — Apply Factor of Safety for Shear Criterion:**

$$q_{s(net),shear} = \dfrac{q_{u,net}}{F.S.}$$

**STEP 4 — Evaluate Settlement-Based SBP (q_s,settlement):**
- For clays: Use **IS 8009 (Part 1)** settlement equations or Skempton-Bjerrum method.
- For sands: Use **IS 8009 (Part 2)** or **Schmertmann's method** or **Burland-Burbidge** method.
- Identify the net pressure producing the **allowable (permissible) settlement** — call this $q_{s,sett}$.

**STEP 5 — Take the minimum (governing) value:**

$$q_{s(net)} = \min\left(\dfrac{q_{u,net}}{F.S.}, \ q_{s,sett}\right)$$

**STEP 6 — Convert back to Gross SBP (if needed for design):**

$$q_{s(gross)} = q_{s(net)} + \gamma \cdot D_f$$

### 2.2 Methods of Determining SBP from Plate Load Test (PLT)

A **Plate Load Test** is conducted in-situ with a standard plate (usually 30 cm × 30 cm). However, the actual foundation is much larger. We need to **scale up** the PLT result.

#### (A) For Cohesionless Soils (Sands)

The net SBP for the actual foundation is:

$$q_{s(net),f} = q_{s(net),p} \times \left(\dfrac{B_f + 0.3}{B_f}\right)^2$$

Where:
- $B_f$ = width of actual foundation (in metres)
- $q_{s(net),p}$ = net SBP from plate load test
- **0.3 m** = standard plate width

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the squared term?** Because bearing capacity in sands is **proportional to B** (since $N_q$ term dominates at depth, and $N_\gamma$ scales with B). The square comes from the empirical scaling based on settlement equivalence.

#### (B) For Cohesive Soils (Clays)

$$q_{s(net),f} = q_{s(net),p}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In clays, bearing capacity is **independent of foundation width B** (the $N_c$ term dominates, and the cohesion term is independent of B). Therefore, the plate result is **directly applicable** without any scaling — but **settlement must be separately checked**.

#### (C) Housel's Method (Perimeter-Shear Concept)

Housel proposed that the ultimate load on a foundation is composed of **base resistance** and **perimeter shear**:

$$P = K_1 \cdot A + K_2 \cdot P_p$$

Where:
- $P$ = total ultimate load
- $A$ = area of foundation
- $P_p$ = perimeter of foundation
- $K_1$ = base resistance per unit area (constant)
- $K_2$ = perimeter shear per unit length (constant)

From two PLTs of different plate sizes, we solve for $K_1$ and $K_2$, then apply to the actual foundation.

#### (D) Teng's Method (Approximate)

$$q_{s(net),f} = \dfrac{q_{s(net),p} \cdot B_p \cdot (B_f + 0.3)}{B_f \cdot (B_p + 0.3)}$$

Where $B_p$ is the plate width and $B_f$ is the foundation width.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Gross SBC | $q_{s(gross)} = q_{u(gross)}/F.S.$ | kN/m² |
| 2 | Net SBC | $q_{s(net)} = q_{u,net}/F.S.$ | kN/m² |
| 3 | Net Ult. BC | $q_{u,net} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f$ | kN/m² |
| 4 | Gross SBP (Sands, PLT) | $q_{s(net),f} = q_{s(net),p} \left(\dfrac{B_f+0.3}{B_f}\right)^2$ | $B_f$ in m |
| 5 | Gross SBP (Clays, PLT) | $q_{s(net),f} = q_{s(net),p}$ | Direct use |
| 6 | Housel's Eq. | $P = K_1 A + K_2 P_p$ | Two unknowns, two tests |
| 7 | Teng's Eq. | $q_{sf} = \dfrac{q_{sp} B_p (B_f+0.3)}{B_f (B_p+0.3)}$ | Empirical |
| 8 | Effect of W.T. (cohesionless) | $q_{s}' = q_{s} \times \dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{D_{w}}{D_{f}}\right)$ | $D_w$ = depth of W.T. |
| 9 | Effect of W.T. (cohesive) | No reduction (cohesion c is independent) | — |
| 10 | Permissible Settlement (Clay) | **25 mm** isolated; **40–65 mm** raft | IS 1904 |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of Shallow Foundations:** SBP is the **direct input** for sizing the foundation (Area = Column Load / SBP).
- **Plate Load Test Interpretation:** Enables the engineer to extrapolate small-scale field test data to full-scale footings.
- **Building Code Compliance:** SBP values are tabulated in **IS 6403:1981** and **IS 8009** for various soil classes.
- **Settlement-Shear Trade-off:** Critical for **tall buildings on soft clays** where settlement, not shear, governs.
- **Retrofitting Decisions:** For an existing structure, the current SBP can be assessed to decide whether additional floors can be added.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why SBP in Sands Scales with $(B_f + 0.3)/B_f)^2$

The classical reasoning comes from the equivalence of **settlement** between a plate and a full foundation. The settlement $S$ of a foundation on sand, by Terzaghi-Peck (1948), is empirically:

$$S = \dfrac{2 \cdot q_{net} \cdot B}{N_{corr}}$$

Where $N_{corr}$ is the corrected SPT-N value (an empirical bearing capacity index).

**Setting equal settlements of plate and foundation:**

$$\dfrac{2 \cdot q_{sp} \cdot B_p}{N_{corr,p}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot q_{sf} \cdot B_f}{N_{corr,f}}$$

Assuming the soil is uniform, the corrected SPT values are similar ($N_{corr,p} \approx N_{corr,f}$):

$$q_{sp} \cdot B_p = q_{sf} \cdot B_f$$

Substituting $B_p = 0.3$ m (standard plate):

$$q_{sf} = q_{sp} \times \dfrac{B_p}{B_f} = q_{sp} \times \dfrac{0.3}{B_f}$$

**But wait — the actual KTU / IS 6403 formula has a squared term.** The square comes from the more refined **Balla / IS 6403** approach, which considers that the stress influence zone depth (and hence the volume of soil mobilised) scales **linearly with B**, and the stress distribution in sands is **non-linear (Boussinesq-type)**, producing a square dependence.

A more rigorous way to arrive at the squared term is via **Schmertmann's** settlement integration, where the elastic settlement of a flexible footing on sand is:

$$S_e = q_{net} \cdot B \cdot \dfrac{1 - \mu^2}{E_s} \cdot I_w \cdot I_s$$

Where $I_w$ and $I_s$ are influence factors. In granular soils, $I_s \propto B^{0.5}$, hence $S_e \propto q \cdot B^{1.5}$. Equalising $S$ for plate and foundation and solving for $q_{sf}$ yields the **square** term seen in IS 6403.

$$\boxed{q_{sf(net)} = q_{sp(net)} \left(\dfrac{B_f + 0.3}{B_f}\right)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "**+0.3**" inside the bracket is a **size-effect correction** empirically introduced so that when $B_f = 0.3$ m (i.e., a plate), the formula returns $q_{sf} = q_{sp}$ — a self-consistency check.

### 3.2 Derivation: Housel's Method (Solving for $K_1$ and $K_2$)

**Housel's perimeter-shear model** assumes the total ultimate load $P$ on a foundation of area $A$ and perimeter $P_p$ is:

$$P = K_1 A + K_2 P_p$$

**Conduct two PLTs with different plate sizes**, e.g., 30 cm × 30 cm and 45 cm × 45 cm.

For plate 1 ($B_{p1}$):

$$P_1 = K_1 \cdot A_1 + K_2 \cdot P_{p1}$$

For plate 2 ($B_{p2}$):

$$P_2 = K_1 \cdot A_2 + K_2 \cdot P_{p2}$$

**Solve simultaneously:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_1 &= \dfrac{P_1 \cdot P_{p2} - P_2 \cdot P_{p1}}{A_1 \cdot P_{p2} - A_2 \cdot P_{p1}} \\[8pt]
K_2 &= \dfrac{A_1 \cdot P_2 - A_2 \cdot P_1}{A_1 \cdot P_{p2} - A_2 \cdot P_{p1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**For actual foundation (area $A_f$, perimeter $P_{pf}$):**

$$P_f = K_1 \cdot A_f + K_2 \cdot P_{pf}$$

$$\boxed{q_{s(net),f} = \dfrac{P_f}{A_f} = K_1 + K_2 \cdot \dfrac{P_{pf}}{A_f}}$$

This gives the **average safe bearing pressure** that incorporates both base and perimeter contributions.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: PLT Result Interpretation

| Observation from PLT | What it means for Design |
|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Load-Settlement curve is **steep, with a clear peak** | Shear failure governs → Use F.S. on $q_{u,net}$ |
| Curve is **gradual, no peak** (sands) | Settlement governs → Use $q$ at permissible $S$ |
| Settlement is **proportional to load** (linear) | Soil in elastic range; no failure yet |
| Curve **flattens sharply** at higher loads | Soil has yielded; near ultimate |
| Plate settles **≥ 25 mm** before peak | Failure load is reached; use $q_u$ directly |

### 3.4 Worked Example (Plug-and-Chug, KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 30 cm square plate load test in sand gave a net ultimate bearing capacity of **600 kN/m²** and a net SBP (at 12.5 mm settlement) of **250 kN/m²**. Determine the net SBP for a **2.0 m × 2.0 m** square footing on the same sand.

**Solution (Step-by-step):**

For **cohesionless soil**, we use the size-scaling formula:

$$q_{s(net),f} = q_{s(net),p} \left(\dfrac{B_f + 0.3}{B_f}\right)^2$$

Substituting $B_f = 2.0$ m and $q_{s(net),p} = 250$ kN/m²:

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{s(net),f} &= 250 \times \left(\dfrac{2.0 + 0.3}{2.0}\right)^2 \\[6pt]
&= 250 \times \left(\dfrac{2.3}{2.0}\right)^2 \\[6pt]
&= 250 \times (1.15)^2 \\[6pt]
&= 250 \times 1.3225 \\[6pt]
&= 330.6 \ \text{kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{q_{s(net),f} \approx 331 \ \text{kN/m}^2}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The SBP **increases** with foundation size in sands because larger footings mobilise a greater soil mass and per unit area are more efficient. This is the **opposite** of the common student assumption that "larger = more settlement → lower SBP."

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Flow: SBP Determination Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Foundation Geometry B, D_f, Soil Type] --> B{Determine Ultimate Bearing Capacity q_u}
    B --> C[Apply Terzaghi / Meyerhof / IS 6403 Equations]
    C --> D[Compute Net Ultimate BC: q_u_net = q_u - gamma D_f]
    D --> E[Criterion 1: Shear Failure Check]
    E --> F[Apply F.S. = 3 to get q_s_net_shear]
    D --> G[Criterion 2: Settlement Check]
    G --> H[Use PLT or IS 8009 to find q_s_net_settlement]
    F --> I{Take the Minimum}
    H --> I
    I --> J[Governing Net SBP]
    J --> K[Convert to Gross SBP: q_s_gross = q_s_net + gamma D_f]
    K --> L[End: Use SBP for Foundation Sizing]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style L fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: PLT Scaling Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLT[PLATE LOAD TEST IN-SITU]
        P1[Plate 30cm x 30cm] --> S1[Net SBP from PLT: q_sp_net]
        S1 --> SCALE{Soil Type}
    end

    subgraph SCALING[Scaling to Actual Footing]
        SCALE -->|Cohesionless Sand| SQ[Square: q_sf = q_sp x B_p + 0.3 / B_f squared]
        SCALE -->|Cohesive Clay| DIR[Direct: q_sf = q_sp]
        SCALE -->|Housel Method| HOU[K1 A + K2 P_p Model]
        SCALE -->|Teng Method| TNG[Linear Extrapolation]
    end

    SQ --> OUT[Final Net SBP for Footing]
    DIR --> OUT
    HOU --> OUT
    TNG --> OUT

    style PLT fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#8B4513
    style SCALING fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B
    style OUT fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400
```

### 4.3 Comparative Schematic: Sand vs Clay SBP Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Apply Foundation Load on Soil] --> CHECK{Soil Type?}

    CHECK -->|SAND / Cohesionless| SAND[Behaviour: Gradual settlement, no sudden shear peak]
    SAND --> SD1[SBP increases with B: Scaled by B_f+0.3 / B_f squared]
    SD1 --> SD2[Governed by SETTLEMENT Criterion]

    CHECK -->|CLAY / Cohesive| CLAY[Behaviour: Sudden shear failure OR consolidation settlement]
    CLAY --> CL1[SBP independent of B: Direct PLT value]
    CL1 --> CL2[Governed by BOTH Shear and Settlement]

    SD2 --> RESULT[Design SBP = MIN of both criteria]
    CL2 --> RESULT

    style SAND fill:#F4A460,stroke:#8B4513
    style CLAY fill:#4682B4,stroke:#00008B
    style RESULT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
```

### 4.4 Component Interaction: Shear vs Settlement Dominance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHEAR[Shear Failure Domain]
        SF1[Loose Sand: Low q_u]
        SF2[Soft Clay: Low c, low q_u]
        SF3[Sudden catastrophic failure]
    end

    subgraph SETTLE[Settlement Domain]
        ST1[Dense Sand: Low compressibility]
        ST2[Stiff Clay: Long-term consolidation]
        ST3[Gradual differential movement]
    end

    Q[Load Applied] --> CHECK{Is F.S. adequate AND settlement within limits?}
    CHECK -->|No - Shear| SF3
    CHECK -->|No - Settlement| ST3
    CHECK -->|Yes| SAFE[Foundation SAFE]

    style SHEAR fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style SETTLE fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B
    style SAFE fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish clearly between Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC) and Safe Bearing Pressure (SBP).**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC) | Safe Bearing Pressure (SBP) |
|---------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| Definition | Maximum gross pressure soil can support with adequate F.S. | Maximum net pressure soil can support with adequate F.S. and within permissible settlement |
| Includes overburden? | Yes (gross) | No (net) |
| Typical F.S. | 3 (IS 6403) | 3 (shear), 2–3 (settlement) |
| Application | Direct field usage | Used in design (since overburden is balanced) |
| Relation | $SBC_{gross} = SBP_{net} + \gamma D_f$ | $SBP_{net} = SBC_{gross} - \gamma D_f$ |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is the Safe Bearing Pressure of a footing in sand greater than that obtained from a 30 cm plate load test on the same sand?**

**Model Answer (3 key points):**
1. In **cohesionless (sandy) soils**, bearing capacity increases with the width of the foundation $B$ because the soil mass mobilised in shear and the stress influence zone both grow with $B$.
2. The PLT uses a 30 cm plate; the actual foundation is larger, so the soil is more efficient at distributing the load per unit area.
3. The IS code / KTU formula $q_{s(net),f} = q_{s(net),p} \left(\dfrac{B_f + 0.3}{B_f}\right)^2$ mathematically gives a value **greater** than $q_{s(net),p}$ for any $B_f > 0.3$ m.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Define Safe Bearing Pressure. With a neat sketch, explain the plate load test setup. List its **limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plate load test was carried out on a sandy soil using a **30 cm square plate**. The net SBP at 12.5 mm settlement was found to be **180 kN/m²**. Determine the net SBP for a **square footing of size 1.5 m × 1.5 m** on the same soil. Use IS 6403 recommendations. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A**

**(a) Definition and PLT Setup:**

**Definition:** Safe Bearing Pressure is the maximum net intensity of loading that the soil can support safely **without shear failure** and **without exceeding the permissible settlement limit**.

**Plate Load Test Setup (Sketch Description):**

```
   Load from Jack
         |
         v
    ┌──────────┐         Pressure Gauge
    │  Plate   │ <───── |──────|
    │ 30x30 cm │         
    └────┬─────┘         Settlement Dial Gauge (x2)
         │                      │
   ──────┴─────── Ground Level ─┴───
         │
   [Soil under test]
```

- A **30 cm × 30 cm** steel plate is loaded through a **hydraulic jack**.
- Reaction is provided by a **truss/beam** anchored with soil/ballast.
- **Settlement** is measured using **2 dial gauges** on either side.
- Load is applied in **increments** (typically 1/5th of expected ultimate load) and settlement recorded at each stage.

**Limitations of PLT:**
1. **Depth limitation** – test is done at ground level or in a pit; results valid for shallow foundations only.
2. **Size effect** – plate is small (30 cm); scaling required for large footings.
3. **Time effect** – in clays, only **immediate (elastic) settlement** is measured; **consolidation settlement** is not captured.
4. **Soil disturbance** – pit excavation disturbs the soil.
5. **Scale of stress bulb** – the influence zone under the plate is smaller than under the actual footing.
6. **Effect of water table** fluctuations cannot be modelled.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Plate width, $B_p = 0.30$ m
- Footing width, $B_f = 1.5$ m
- Net SBP from PLT, $q_{s(net),p} = 180$ kN/m²
- Soil type: **Sand** (cohesionless)

**Formula (IS 6403 / KTU for sand):**

$$q_{s(net),f} = q_{s(net),p} \times \left(\dfrac{B_f + 0.3}{B_f}\right)^2$$

**Substitution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{s(net),f} &= 180 \times \left(\dfrac{1.5 + 0.3}{1.5}\right)^2 \\[6pt]
&= 180 \times \left(\dfrac{1.8}{1.5}\right)^2 \\[6pt]
&= 180 \times (1.2)^2 \\[6pt]
&= 180 \times 1.44 \\[6pt]
&= 259.2 \ \text{kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{q_{s(net),f} = 259.2 \ \text{kN/m}^2}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain **Housel's method** for determining Safe Bearing Pressure from a plate load test. What are its **assumptions** and **limitations**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two plate load tests were conducted on a clayey soil:
- **Plate 1 (30 cm × 30 cm):** Ultimate load = **120 kN**
- **Plate 2 (60 cm × 60 cm):** Ultimate load = **360 kN**

Using Housel's method, determine the **Safe Bearing Pressure** of a **1.5 m × 1.5 m** square footing. Take F.S. = 3. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B**

**(a) Housel's Method – Explanation:**

Housel (1929) proposed that the **ultimate load** carried by a foundation consists of two components:
1. **Base resistance** proportional to the **area** of the foundation.
2. **Perimeter shear** proportional to the **perimeter** of the foundation.

$$P = K_1 A + K_2 P_p$$

**Procedure:**
1. Conduct **two PLTs** with **different plate sizes** (e.g., 30 cm and 45 cm).
2. From each test, record the **ultimate load** $P_1$ and $P_2$, compute **areas** $A_1, A_2$ and **perimeters** $P_{p1}, P_{p2}$.
3. Solve the two simultaneous equations for $K_1$ (kN/m²) and $K_2$ (kN/m).
4. For actual foundation, compute $A_f$ and $P_{pf}$, find ultimate load $P_f$, divide by F.S. to get SBP.

**Assumptions:**
- Failure occurs by a combination of base compression and perimeter shear.
- $K_1$ and $K_2$ are **constants** for the given soil.
- Soil is **homogeneous** within the stressed zone.

**Limitations:**
- Two PLTs required — time-consuming and expensive.
- Accurate perimeter measurement is difficult in stratified soils.
- Method is more reliable in **cohesive soils** than in sands.
- Does not account for **settlement** — only shear failure.
- Empirical constants are site-specific.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**

| Item | Plate 1 | Plate 2 | Footing |
|------|---------|---------|---------|
| Side (m) | 0.30 | 0.60 | 1.50 |
| Area (m²) | 0.09 | 0.36 | 2.25 |
| Perimeter (m) | 1.20 | 2.40 | 6.00 |
| Ult. Load (kN) | 120 | 360 | ? |

**Step 1 — Write Housel's equations:**

For Plate 1: $120 = K_1 (0.09) + K_2 (1.20)$ … (i)
For Plate 2: $360 = K_1 (0.36) + K_2 (2.40)$ … (ii)

**Step 2 — Solve simultaneously:**

Multiply (i) by 4 to align $K_2$:

$$480 = 0.36 K_1 + 4.80 K_2 \quad \text{...(i')}$$

Subtract (ii) from (i'):

$$\begin{aligned}
480 - 360 &= (0.36 - 0.36)K_1 + (4.80 - 2.40)K_2 \\
120 &= 0 + 2.40 \cdot K_2 \\
K_2 &= 50 \ \text{kN/m}
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute $K_2 = 50$ in (i):

$$\begin{aligned}
120 &= 0.09 K_1 + 1.20 \times 50 \\
120 &= 0.09 K_1 + 60 \\
0.09 K_1 &= 60 \\
K_1 &= 666.67 \ \text{kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply to actual footing:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_f &= K_1 A_f + K_2 P_{pf} \\
&= 666.67 \times 2.25 + 50 \times 6.00 \\
&= 1500 + 300 \\
&= 1800 \ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply F.S. = 3:**

$$P_{safe} = \dfrac{1800}{3} = 600 \ \text{kN}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Safe Bearing Pressure:**

$$q_s = \dfrac{P_{safe}}{A_f} = \dfrac{600}{2.25} = 266.67 \ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Result:** $\boxed{q_s \approx 266.67 \ \text{kN/m}^2}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting F.S. = 3** — students often give ultimate load divided by area. The question demands **SBP** = Ultimate / F.S.
> 2. **Mixing gross vs net** — for clay with PLT data, since the soil is excavated to plate level, the **overburden is already removed**; the PLT result is **net**. Do not subtract $\gamma D_f$ again.
> 3. **Wrong perimeter for square footing** — $P = 4B$, not $\pi B$. Students often use circle formulae.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in $K_2$ — must be in kN/m, not kN. $K_1$ in kN/m².
> 5. **For clayey soils, do NOT apply the $(B_f+0.3/B_f)^2$ correction** — this is exclusively for sands.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **SBP = MIN (Shear Criterion, Settlement Criterion)** — always take the lower value.
- [ ] **F.S. = 3** for shear (IS 6403); **F.S. = 2–3** for settlement-based design.
- [ ] **Permissible settlement** = **25 mm** for isolated footings on clay (IS 1904).
- [ ] **Sand** → SBP scales up with footing size (use **squared** formula).
- [ ] **Clay** → SBP from PLT is **directly used** (no size scaling).
- [ ] **Housel's method** → uses **two PLTs of different sizes**, gives $K_1$ and $K_2$.
- [ ] **Teng's method** → linear extrapolation, used for cohesionless soils.
- [ ] **Net SBP = Net Ultimate BC / F.S.** (numerically equal to Net SBC / F.S.).
- [ ] **Gross SBP = Net SBP + γ D_f** (for design of total contact pressure).
- [ ] **Water table effect** — reduces SBP in **sands** (factor depends on $D_w/D_f$); **no effect in clays** (c is independent of water).
- [ ] **Standard PLT plate size = 30 cm × 30 cm** (square).
- [ ] **Ultimate load** in PLT is when settlement = **1/5 to 1/10** of plate width OR when failure is observed.
- [ ] **Settlement governs in sands** (no clear shear peak); **shear governs in clays**.
- [ ] **PLT limitations:** depth, size, time, soil disturbance, water table effects.
- [ ] **IS 6403:1981** → SBP from shear. **IS 8009** → SBP from settlement. **IS 1904** → Code of practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Memory Trick — "SCSS":**
> **S**ettlement governs in **C**ohesionless (S**a**nds)
> **S**hear governs in **C**ohesive (Clays)
> **S**quare the size factor only for **S**ands

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Settlement of Footings; Pile Foundations: Types of Piles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Settlement of Footings; Pile Foundations: Types of Piles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Settlement of Footings – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Settlement of a footing** is the vertical downward displacement of the foundation caused by the consolidation and elastic deformation of the underlying soil mass under the applied structural loads. It is a critical **Serviceability Limit State (SLS)** parameter in foundation design, distinct from the **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** of bearing capacity failure.

In the framework of **KTU 2024 Scheme** (NEP 2020 aligned), settlement analysis is governed by **Course Outcome CO3**: *"Estimate the settlement and bearing capacity of foundations in cohesive and cohesionless soils."*

### Components of Total Settlement ($S_t$)

The total settlement of a footing resting on a soil profile is the **summation of three independent physical mechanisms**:

$$S_t = S_i + S_c + S_s$$

Where:
- $S_i$ = **Immediate (Elastic) Settlement** – occurs instantaneously upon load application
- $S_c$ = **Consolidation Settlement** – time-dependent in saturated clays due to expulsion of pore water
- $S_s$ = **Secondary (Creep) Settlement** – long-term plastic readjustment of soil grains under constant effective stress

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU board examinations, the typical problem is restricted to **immediate settlement in sands** (using **Schmertmann's method** or **Elastic theory**) and **consolidation settlement in clays** (using **Terzaghi's 1-D Consolidation theory**). Secondary settlement is generally treated as a conceptual question (2-3 marks).

---

## 1.2 Pile Foundations – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Pile Foundation** is a deep foundation system consisting of slender, columnar structural members (typically **concrete, steel, or timber**) that are driven, drilled, or otherwise installed into the ground to transfer superstructural loads through weak/ compressible surface soils to deeper, stronger, and less compressible strata or rock.

### Conceptual Analogy – The "Floating Stake in a Marsh"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine trying to pitch a tent on a **marshy swamp**. A flat board placed on the surface would sink and tilt. However, if you take a **long wooden stake (pile)** and push it down through the soft mud until it rests on the firm clay/hard rock beneath, the tent (your building) stands firm. The stake bypasses the weak marsh soil and transfers the load to competent strata. This is precisely the function of a **point-bearing pile**. If the stake merely "floats" within the marsh but develops friction along its length (like a chopstick in thick honey), it functions as a **friction pile**.

### Why Pile Foundations? – The Engineering Necessity

| Soil/Loading Condition | Reason for Selecting Piles |
|------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Weak surface soils** (soft clays, loose sands) | Insufficient bearing capacity at shallow depth |
| **Heavy structural loads** (high-rise, bridges) | Spread footings become impractically large |
| **Scour-prone riverbeds / offshore structures** | Risk of undermining of shallow foundations |
| **Uplift / lateral loads** (towers, transmission lines) | Tension piles or battered piles |
| **Expansive / collapsible soils** | To bypass the problematic active zone |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Classification Metrics:** Pile **diameter ($D$)** typically ranges from **300 mm to 600 mm** for bored cast-in-situ piles, and up to **1500 mm** for large bridge foundations. Pile **length ($L$)** can extend from **5 m** to over **100 m** for offshore platforms.

---

## 1.3 Course Outcome Mapping (KTU 2024 NEP Alignment)

| Topic Element | Mapped CO | Bloom's Level (Revised) |
|---------------|-----------|--------------------------|
| Settlement calculations | CO3 | Apply / Analyze |
| Types of piles (classification) | CO4 | Understand / Apply |
| Load transfer mechanism | CO4 | Analyze |
| KTU Examiner Focus | CO3 + CO4 | Apply (60%), Understand (30%), Remember (10%) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Settlement

### 2.1.1 Immediate (Elastic) Settlement – $S_i$

The elastic settlement of a **flexible footing** resting on an **elastic half-space** (Boussinesq's solution) is given by the classical **Schleicher's formula**:

$$S_i = \frac{q \cdot B \cdot (1 - \mu^2)}{E_s} \cdot I_w \cdot I_s$$

Where the parameters are physically defined as follows:

- $q$ = **Net contact pressure** at the base of the footing, in **kN/m²**
- $B$ = **Width of the footing**, in **m**
- $\mu$ = **Poisson's ratio** of the soil (dimensionless)
  - For sands: $\mu \approx 0.20$ to $0.30$
  - For clays (undrained): $\mu \approx 0.50$
- $E_s$ = **Modulus of Elasticity** of the soil, in **kN/m²**
- $I_w$ = **Influence factor for depth of foundation** (depends on $D_f/B$)
- $I_s$ = **Shape and rigidity factor** of the footing

#### Influence Factors – KTU Board Standard

| Footing Shape | Rigidity | $I_s$ (Shape Factor) | $I_w$ (Depth Factor, typical) |
|---------------|----------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Square** | Flexible | 1.12 | 0.95 (at $D_f/B = 1$) |
| **Square** | Rigid | 0.82 | 0.95 |
| **Circular** | Rigid | 0.85 | 0.95 |
| **Rectangular** ($L/B = 2$) | Flexible | 1.53 | 0.95 |
| **Rectangular** ($L/B = 5$) | Flexible | 2.10 | 0.95 |

### 2.1.2 Schmertmann's Improved Method for Sands

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:** Schmertmann's method (1970) is a semi-empirical improvement over the Boussinesq elastic method. It uses a **2:1 strain influence factor distribution** beneath the footing.

$$S_i = C_1 \cdot C_2 \cdot (q - q') \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{I_{zi} \cdot \Delta z_i}{E_{si}}$$

Where:
- $C_1$ = Depth correction factor = $1 - 0.5 \cdot (q'/q)$
- $C_2$ = Creep correction factor = $1 + 0.2 \cdot \log_{10}(t/0.1)$
- $q'$ = Effective overburden pressure at foundation level
- $I_{zi}$ = Strain influence factor at the mid-depth of layer $i$
- $\Delta z_i$ = Thickness of sub-layer $i$
- $E_{si}$ = Elastic modulus of sub-layer $i$

### 2.1.3 Consolidation Settlement – $S_c$ (Clays)

For **normally consolidated clays**, Terzaghi's 1-D Consolidation theory gives:

$$S_c = \frac{C_c \cdot H}{1 + e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma}{\sigma'_0}\right)$$

For **overconsolidated clays** (when $\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma \leq \sigma'_c$):

$$S_c = \frac{C_s \cdot H}{1 + e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma}{\sigma'_0}\right)$$

For **overconsolidated clays** (when $\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma > \sigma'_c$):

$$S_c = \frac{C_s \cdot H}{1 + e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma'_c}{\sigma'_0}\right) + \frac{C_c \cdot H}{1 + e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma}{\sigma'_c}\right)$$

Where:
- $C_c$ = **Compression index** (dimensionless, typically 0.1 to 1.0)
- $C_s$ = **Swelling index** (typically $C_s \approx C_c / 5$ to $C_c / 10$)
- $H$ = Thickness of the compressible clay layer, in **m**
- $e_0$ = Initial void ratio
- $\sigma'_0$ = Initial effective overburden pressure
- $\sigma'_c$ = Preconsolidation pressure
- $\Delta\sigma$ = Increase in vertical stress due to footing load

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework of Pile Classification

Piles can be classified based on **five independent criteria** – each with a distinct engineering rationale. KTU questions frequently test these classifications as short-answer or 5-mark questions.

### Classification Axis 1: By Function / Load Transfer

| Pile Type | Mechanism | Governing Equation (Ultimate Capacity) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------------------------------|
| **End-Bearing Pile** | Transfers load to firm stratum at tip | $Q_u = Q_b$ (tip only) |
| **Friction (Skin Friction) Pile** | Transfers load via shaft friction | $Q_u = Q_s$ (shaft only) |
| **Combined End-Bearing & Friction Pile** | Both mechanisms act simultaneously | $Q_u = Q_b + Q_s$ |

### Classification Axis 2: By Material

| Material | Typical Length | Typical Load Capacity | Use Case |
|----------|----------------|----------------------|----------|
| **Concrete** (cast-in-situ / precast) | 5 m – 60 m | 300 kN – 3000 kN | Most common in Kerala |
| **Steel** (H-piles, pipe piles) | 15 m – 100 m | 400 kN – 5000 kN | Heavy industrial, offshore |
| **Timber** | 5 m – 20 m | 100 kN – 500 kN | Temporary works, Kerala backwaters |
| **Composite** (concrete + steel) | 20 m – 60 m | 500 kN – 4000 kN | Marine environments |

### Classification Axis 3: By Method of Installation

| Installation Method | Soil Disturbance | Noise / Vibration | Suitability |
|--------------------|------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Driven (Displacement) Piles** | High | High | Dense sands, gravels |
| **Bored (Replacement) Piles** | Low / None | Low | Urban areas, soft clays |
| **Screw / Helical Piles** | Moderate | Low | Loose sands, soft clays |
| **Jacked Piles** | Moderate | Very Low | Sensitive adjacent structures |

### Classification Axis 4: By Direction of Load

| Direction | Pile Type | Application |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Vertical compression | **Bearing pile** | Most building columns |
| Vertical tension (uplift) | **Tension / Anchor pile** | Basement heave, transmission towers |
| Horizontal (lateral) | **Laterally loaded pile** | Bridge piers, retaining walls |
| Inclined | **Batter / Raker pile** | Combined vertical + horizontal loads |

### Classification Axis 5: By Cross-Section

- **Solid section** (concrete, timber)
- **Hollow section** (pipe piles, hollow precast concrete)
- **H-section** (steel H-piles)
- **Box section** (composite)

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Application | Units | Exam Frequency |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | $S_i = qB(1-\mu^2)I_wI_s / E_s$ | Elastic settlement of footings | m | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $S_i = C_1 C_2 (q-q') \sum I_{zi} \Delta z_i / E_{si}$ | Schmertmann's method (sands) | m | ★★★★ |
| 3 | $S_c = C_c H \log(\sigma'_f/\sigma'_0)/(1+e_0)$ | Consolidation settlement (NC clay) | m | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | $S_c = C_s H \log(\sigma'_f/\sigma'_0)/(1+e_0)$ | Consolidation settlement (OC clay) | m | ★★★ |
| 5 | $\Delta\sigma = q \cdot I$ (Boussinesq) | Stress at depth under footing | kN/m² | ★★★★ |
| 6 | $Q_u = Q_b + Q_s$ | Ultimate pile capacity | kN | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | $Q_b = A_b \cdot q_b = A_b (cN_c^* + \sigma'_v N_q^*)$ | End bearing (IS 2911) | kN | ★★★★★ |
| 8 | $Q_s = \sum (A_{si} \cdot f_{si}) = \sum (A_{si} \cdot \alpha \bar{c}_i)$ | Skin friction in clays | kN | ★★★★★ |
| 9 | $Q_s = \sum (A_{si} \cdot K \sigma'_v \tan\delta)$ | Skin friction in sands | kN | ★★★★ |
| 10 | $Q_{allow} = Q_u / F_S$ | Safe pile load (F_S = 2.5 to 3.0) | kN | ★★★★★ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Utility:** Settlement calculations directly govern the **architectural finishes, door alignment, glass façade integrity, and elevator functionality** of high-rise buildings. For example, KTU-aligned projects around Kochi Metro stations used **bored cast-in-situ piles** ranging from 25 m to 40 m to bypass the soft marine clay and reach the dense lateritic strata.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Consolidation Settlement in Normally Consolidated Clay

We derive the fundamental expression starting from the definition of **void ratio change** under a 1-D compression test.

### Step 1: Definition of Compression Index

The compression index $C_c$ is defined as the slope of the **virgin compression line** on the $e$ vs. $\log \sigma'$ plot:

$$C_c = -\frac{\Delta e}{\log_{10}(\sigma'_f / \sigma'_0)}$$

Where $\sigma'_0$ is the initial effective stress and $\sigma'_f = \sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma$ is the final effective stress.

### Step 2: Relating Change in Thickness to Void Ratio

For a soil layer of thickness $H$ undergoing a change in void ratio from $e_0$ to $e_f$, the change in thickness (settlement) is:

$$\Delta H = H \cdot \frac{e_0 - e_f}{1 + e_0} = -H \cdot \frac{\Delta e}{1 + e_0}$$

### Step 3: Substituting Compression Index

Substituting the expression for $\Delta e$ from Step 1 into Step 2:

$$\Delta H = -H \cdot \frac{1}{1 + e_0} \cdot \left( -C_c \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma'_f}{\sigma'_0}\right) \right)$$

### Step 4: Final Formulation

$$\boxed{S_c = \frac{C_c \cdot H}{1 + e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma}{\sigma'_0}\right)}$$

This equation is the **cornerstone of all clay settlement problems** in the KTU syllabus.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Numerical Solution: Immediate Settlement (Schmertmann's Method)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**
> A **3 m × 3 m square footing** is placed at a depth of 1.5 m in a sandy soil profile. The net contact pressure $q = 150$ kN/m². The soil is divided into **3 sub-layers** beneath the footing, with the following properties:

| Layer | Depth below footing (m) | Thickness $\Delta z_i$ (m) | $E_{si}$ (kN/m²) | $I_{zi}$ |
|-------|--------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------|----------|
| 1 | 0.75 | 1.5 | 15000 | 0.85 |
| 2 | 2.75 | 1.5 | 22000 | 0.55 |
| 3 | 5.50 | 2.0 | 35000 | 0.10 |

Estimate the **immediate settlement** at time $t = 5$ years. Effective overburden at footing level $q' = 27$ kN/m².

### Solution Steps:

**Step 1: Depth Correction Factor ($C_1$)**

$$C_1 = 1 - 0.5 \cdot \frac{q'}{q} = 1 - 0.5 \cdot \frac{27}{150} = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91$$

**Step 2: Creep Correction Factor ($C_2$)**

$$C_2 = 1 + 0.2 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{t}{0.1}\right) = 1 + 0.2 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{5}{0.1}\right)$$

$$\log_{10}(50) = 1.699$$

$$C_2 = 1 + 0.2 \cdot 1.699 = 1 + 0.3398 = 1.3398$$

**Step 3: Compute the Summation Term $\sum I_{zi} \Delta z_i / E_{si}$**

For Layer 1:

$$\frac{I_{z1} \cdot \Delta z_1}{E_{s1}} = \frac{0.85 \cdot 1.5}{15000} = \frac{1.275}{15000} = 0.0000850 \text{ m}^3/\text{kN}$$

For Layer 2:

$$\frac{I_{z2} \cdot \Delta z_2}{E_{s2}} = \frac{0.55 \cdot 1.5}{22000} = \frac{0.825}{22000} = 0.0000375 \text{ m}^3/\text{kN}$$

For Layer 3:

$$\frac{I_{z3} \cdot \Delta z_3}{E_{s3}} = \frac{0.10 \cdot 2.0}{35000} = \frac{0.20}{35000} = 0.00000571 \text{ m}^3/\text{kN}$$

Summing all three:

$$\sum = 0.0000850 + 0.0000375 + 0.00000571 = 0.00012821 \text{ m}^3/\text{kN}$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the Master Equation**

$$S_i = C_1 \cdot C_2 \cdot (q - q') \cdot \sum \frac{I_{zi} \cdot \Delta z_i}{E_{si}}$$

$$S_i = 0.91 \cdot 1.3398 \cdot (150 - 27) \cdot 0.00012821$$

$$S_i = 0.91 \cdot 1.3398 \cdot 123 \cdot 0.00012821$$

$$S_i = 1.21922 \cdot 123 \cdot 0.00012821 = 19.224 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{S_i \approx 19.22 \text{ mm} \approx 0.0192 \text{ m}}$$

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Numerical Solution: Pile Ultimate Capacity (IS 2911 Method)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
> A **bored cast-in-situ concrete pile** of **0.5 m diameter** and **15 m length** is driven through a **soft clay layer** of thickness 15 m. The undrained cohesion $c = 40$ kN/m², and the bulk unit weight $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. The adhesion factor $\alpha = 0.7$. The pile rests on **rock** at its tip. Determine the **ultimate load capacity** of the pile.

### Solution Steps:

**Step 1: Pile Geometry**

Diameter $D = 0.5$ m; Length $L = 15$ m.

Base area:

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.5)^2 = 0.1963 \text{ m}^2$$

Surface area (shaft):
