$$A_s = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \cdot 0.5 \cdot 15 = 23.562 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: End-Bearing Capacity ($Q_b$)**

Since the pile rests on rock, the end-bearing capacity is computed using IS 2911 with $N_c^* = 9$ (standard value for piles on rock, conservative):

$$Q_b = A_b \cdot c \cdot N_c^* = 0.1963 \cdot 40 \cdot 9 = 70.69 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Skin Friction Capacity ($Q_s$) in Clay**

For cohesive soils, the unit skin friction is:

$$f_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} = 0.7 \cdot 40 = 28 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Therefore:

$$Q_s = A_s \cdot f_s = 23.562 \cdot 28 = 659.74 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Total Ultimate Pile Capacity**

$$Q_u = Q_b + Q_s = 70.69 + 659.74 = 730.43 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Allowable Load Capacity (Factor of Safety = 2.5)**

$$Q_{allow} = \frac{Q_u}{F_S} = \frac{730.43}{2.5} = 292.17 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{Q_u \approx 730.43 \text{ kN}, \quad Q_{allow} \approx 292.17 \text{ kN}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix – Selecting Pile Type

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For 14-mark design questions, students are often asked to "**recommend a suitable type of pile**". The decision matrix below must be memorized.

| Site Condition | Recommended Pile Type | Justification |
|----------------|----------------------|---------------|
| Soft clay overlying rock | **End-bearing bored pile** | Minimal driving vibration, high end-bearing on rock |
| Loose sand extending deep | **Friction driven pile** | High skin friction, displacement densifies sand |
| Urban area with adjacent buildings | **Bored pile** | Zero vibration, no risk of structural damage |
| Offshore (Kochi backwaters) | **Steel pipe pile** | Corrosion resistance, prefabrication, length up to 60 m |
| High uplift loads (basement) | **Tension pile with under-reamed bulb** | Under-ream resists pull-out |
| Lateral loads (bridge piers) | **Battered pile group** | Inclined piles resist horizontal thrust |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Settlement Components – Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **physical flow of settlement mechanisms** and the **decision logic** an engineer follows when computing total settlement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply Structural Load to Footing] --> B{Soil Type Identification}
    B --> C[Clay - Saturated, Low Permeability]
    B --> D[Sand - Granular, High Permeability]
    B --> E[Mix of Clay and Sand]
    C --> F[Immediate Settlement Si]
    C --> G[Consolidation Settlement Sc]
    C --> H[Secondary Settlement Ss]
    D --> F
    D --> I[Immediate Settlement Only]
    E --> F
    E --> G
    F --> J[Compute using Elastic Theory or Schmertmann]
    G --> K[Compute using Terzaghi 1-D Theory]
    H --> L[Estimate using Creep Index Ca]
    I --> M[Use SPT or CPT N-values]
    J --> N[Total Settlement St]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O{Compare with Allowable Settlement}
    O --> P[Within Limits - SAFE Design]
    O --> Q[Exceeds Limits - REVISE Footing Size]
```

## 4.2 Pile Classification Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[PILE FOUNDATIONS] --> F1[By Function]
    Root --> F2[By Material]
    Root --> F3[By Installation]
    Root --> F4[By Load Direction]
    F1 --> A1[End-Bearing Pile]
    F1 --> A2[Friction Pile]
    F1 --> A3[Combined Pile]
    F2 --> B1[Concrete Pile]
    B1 --> B1a[Precast]
    B1 --> B1b[Cast-in-situ Bored]
    F2 --> B2[Steel Pile]
    B2 --> B2a[H-Section]
    B2 --> B2b[Pipe Section]
    F2 --> B3[Timber Pile]
    F2 --> B4[Composite Pile]
    F3 --> C1[Driven Pile]
    F3 --> C2[Bored Pile]
    F3 --> C3[Screw Pile]
    F3 --> C4[Jacked Pile]
    F4 --> D1[Vertical Compression]
    F4 --> D2[Vertical Tension Anchor]
    F4 --> D3[Lateral Load]
    F4 --> D4[Batter Inclined]
```

## 4.3 Load Transfer Mechanism – Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Structural Load Q] --> P[Pile Head]
    P --> S1[Shaft Friction Qs]
    P --> S2[End Bearing Qb]
    S1 --> Soil[Soil Surrounding Pile]
    S2 --> TipStratum[Tip Stratum Rock or Hard Layer]
    Soil --> Eq[Equilibrium Sum Qs plus Qb equals Q]
    TipStratum --> Eq
    Eq --> Safe[Safe Pile Capacity Qallow equals Qu by FS]
```

## 4.4 Schmertmann's Strain Influence Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Foot[Footing Base] --> L1[Layer 1 Depth 0 to 1.5B Iz Peak]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2 Depth 1.5B to 3B Iz Linear Decay]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Depth greater than 3B Iz Approaching Zero]
    L1 --> V1[Maximum Strain Influence Factor Iz1]
    L2 --> V2[Reduced Strain Influence Factor Iz2]
    L3 --> V3[Negligible Strain Influence Factor Iz3]
    V1 --> Sum[Sum of Iz times Delta z divided by Es]
    V2 --> Sum
    V3 --> Sum
    Sum --> Out[Multiply by C1 C2 and q minus qprime for Final Si]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between immediate settlement and consolidation settlement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks – distribution shown):**

| Feature | Immediate Settlement ($S_i$) | Consolidation Settlement ($S_c$) |
|---------|------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Time of occurrence** | Occurs immediately on load application (1 mark) | Time-dependent, occurs over years (1 mark) |
| **Mechanism** | Elastic distortion of soil grains (0.5 mark) | Expulsion of pore water from saturated clay (0.5 mark) |
| **Soil type** | Predominant in sands and stiff clays (0.5 mark) | Occurs only in saturated clays (0.5 mark) |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three classifications of piles based on (a) function and (b) material.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a) By Function (1.5 marks):**
1. **End-Bearing Pile** – transfers load to a firm stratum at the tip
2. **Friction Pile** – transfers load via skin friction along the shaft
3. **Combined Pile** – both end-bearing and friction contribute

**(b) By Material (1.5 marks):**
1. **Concrete Piles** – precast or cast-in-situ
2. **Steel Piles** – H-section or pipe section
3. **Timber Piles** – treated wood, used for temporary works

---

## Part B Question – Internal Choice (14 Marks Total)

### Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**Question:** A rectangular footing **3 m × 4 m** transmits a net contact pressure of **180 kN/m²** at a depth of **1.5 m**. The subsoil is loose sand extending to a great depth. Standard Penetration Test (SPT) results (corrected N-values) at depths 1.5 m, 3.0 m, 4.5 m, 6.0 m, 7.5 m, 9.0 m are 8, 10, 12, 15, 18, 20 respectively. The water table is at 4.5 m depth. Estimate the **immediate settlement** using the **Schmertmann's method**. Take Poisson's ratio $\mu = 0.3$.

#### Part (a) – Sub-division into Layers and Influence Factor Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Layer Division**

Width $B = 3$ m; $B/2 = 1.5$ m; $B = 3$ m; $2B = 6$ m.

The soil is divided into **3 layers** beneath the footing based on the 2B strain influence zone.

| Layer | Depth (m) | Thickness $\Delta z$ (m) | Depth to mid-layer (m) | $I_{zi}$ (Linear Decay Model) |
|-------|-----------|--------------------------|-------------------------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | 0 to $B/2$ | 1.5 | 0.75 | **1.0** (peak) |
| 2 | $B/2$ to $B$ | 1.5 | 2.25 | **0.55** |
| 3 | $B$ to $2B$ | 3.0 | 4.50 | **0.10** |

**Step 2: Compute $E_s$ for each layer from corrected N-values**

Using Bowles' correlation: $E_s \text{ (kN/m²)} = 500 (N + 15)$ for sand.

Layer 1 (avg N at 0.75 m and 1.5 m depth, use N = 9):

$$E_{s1} = 500 \cdot (9 + 15) = 500 \cdot 24 = 12000 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Layer 2 (avg N at 3.0 m and 4.5 m, use N = 11):

$$E_{s2} = 500 \cdot (11 + 15) = 500 \cdot 26 = 13000 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Layer 3 (avg N at 6.0 m and 9.0 m, use N = 17.5):

$$E_{s3} = 500 \cdot (17.5 + 15) = 500 \cdot 32.5 = 16250 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the summation term**

Layer 1:

$$\frac{I_{z1} \cdot \Delta z_1}{E_{s1}} = \frac{1.0 \cdot 1.5}{12000} = 0.000125 \text{ m}^3/\text{kN}$$

Layer 2:

$$\frac{I_{z2} \cdot \Delta z_2}{E_{s2}} = \frac{0.55 \cdot 1.5}{13000} = 0.0000635 \text{ m}^3/\text{kN}$$

Layer 3:

$$\frac{I_{z3} \cdot \Delta z_3}{E_{s3}} = \frac{0.10 \cdot 3.0}{16250} = 0.00001846 \text{ m}^3/\text{kN}$$

Summing:

$$\sum = 0.000125 + 0.0000635 + 0.00001846 = 0.00020696 \text{ m}^3/\text{kN}$$

#### Part (b) – Apply Correction Factors and Final Settlement (7 marks)

**Step 4: Effective overburden at footing level**

$$q' = \gamma \cdot D_f = 17 \cdot 1.5 = 25.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

(Assume $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³ for loose sand above water table.)

**Step 5: Compute $C_1$ and $C_2$**

Assuming $t = 5$ years:

$$C_1 = 1 - 0.5 \cdot \frac{q'}{q} = 1 - 0.5 \cdot \frac{25.5}{180} = 1 - 0.0708 = 0.929$$

$$C_2 = 1 + 0.2 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{5}{0.1}\right) = 1 + 0.2 \cdot 1.699 = 1.340$$

**Step 6: Final Immediate Settlement**

$$S_i = C_1 \cdot C_2 \cdot (q - q') \cdot \sum \frac{I_{zi} \cdot \Delta z_i}{E_{si}}$$

$$S_i = 0.929 \cdot 1.340 \cdot (180 - 25.5) \cdot 0.00020696$$

$$S_i = 1.245 \cdot 154.5 \cdot 0.00020696 = 0.0398 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{S_i \approx 39.8 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Alternative]**

**Question:** A **bored cast-in-situ concrete pile** of **0.6 m diameter** and **20 m length** is installed through a **medium stiff clay** layer. The following data are given:
- Undrained cohesion $c = 60$ kN/m²
- Bulk unit weight $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³
- Adhesion factor $\alpha = 0.6$
- Bearing capacity factor at tip $N_c^* = 9$
- Factor of Safety = 3.0

Compute: (a) The **ultimate and allowable load** of the pile. (b) The **group efficiency** if 9 such piles are arranged in a **3 × 3 square pattern** with center-to-center spacing of **3 pile diameters**.

#### Part (a) – Ultimate and Allowable Load (7 marks)

**Step 1: Geometry**

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.6)^2 = 0.2827 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_s = \pi \cdot 0.6 \cdot 20 = 37.699 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: End-Bearing Capacity**

$$Q_b = A_b \cdot c \cdot N_c^* = 0.2827 \cdot 60 \cdot 9 = 152.68 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Skin Friction Capacity**

$$f_s = \alpha \cdot c = 0.6 \cdot 60 = 36 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$Q_s = A_s \cdot f_s = 37.699 \cdot 36 = 1357.16 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Total Ultimate Capacity**

$$Q_u = Q_b + Q_s = 152.68 + 1357.16 = 1509.84 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Allowable Load**

$$Q_{allow} = \frac{Q_u}{F_S} = \frac{1509.84}{3.0} = 503.28 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{Q_u \approx 1510 \text{ kN}; \quad Q_{allow} \approx 503.3 \text{ kN}}$$

#### Part (b) – Group Efficiency (7 marks)

**Step 1: Group Capacity (using Converse-Labarre Equation)**

For a 3 × 3 group: $m = 3$, $n = 3$, $s = 3D = 3 \cdot 0.6 = 1.8$ m, $\theta = \arctan(D/s) = \arctan(0.6/1.8) = \arctan(0.333) = 18.43°$

Converse-Labarre Group Efficiency:

$$E_g = 1 - \theta \cdot \frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{90 \cdot m \cdot n}$$

$$E_g = 1 - 18.43 \cdot \frac{(3-1)(3) + (3-1)(3)}{90 \cdot 3 \cdot 3}$$

$$E_g = 1 - 18.43 \cdot \frac{6 + 6}{810} = 1 - 18.43 \cdot \frac{12}{810}$$

$$E_g = 1 - 18.43 \cdot 0.01481 = 1 - 0.2730 = 0.7270$$

**Step 2: Group Capacity**

$$Q_{g,allow} = E_g \cdot n_{piles} \cdot Q_{allow} = 0.7270 \cdot 9 \cdot 503.28$$

$$Q_{g,allow} = 6.543 \cdot 503.28 = 3293.41 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{E_g \approx 0.727 \text{ or } 72.7\%; \quad Q_{g,allow} \approx 3293.4 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (2 marks lost):** Students often compute $Q_b$ using $N_c^* = 9$ for clay in undrained conditions correctly, but **forget to convert units** (e.g., using $c$ in kPa and $A_b$ in cm²). Always verify $c$ is in **kN/m²** and $A_b$ is in **m²**.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (3 marks lost):** In Converse-Labarre equation, students frequently compute the term $(n-1)m + (m-1)n$ **wrong** (e.g., using $m+n-2$ instead of the correct form). The correct numerator is **$(n-1)m + (m-1)n = 2m + 2n - 2$**.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (1 mark lost):** In Schmertmann's method, the **creep factor $C_2$** is often ignored. Without $C_2$, you under-predict settlement by up to 30% – a major error in design.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Final 5-Minute Scan Before Exam):**

- [x] **Total Settlement:** $S_t = S_i + S_c + S_s$ (memorize the three components)
- [x] **Immediate Settlement Formula:** $S_i = qB(1-\mu^2)I_wI_s / E_s$ for cohesive soils; **Schmertmann** for sands
- [x] **Consolidation Settlement:** $S_c = C_c H \log(\sigma'_f/\sigma'_0)/(1+e_0)$ for NC clay
- [x] **Overconsolidated Clay:** Use $C_s$ if $\sigma'_f \leq \sigma'_c$, else use **two-term formula** with $C_s$ and $C_c$
- [x] **Pile Ultimate Capacity:** $Q_u = Q_b + Q_s$ (always sum both)
- [x] **End-Bearing in Clay:** $Q_b = A_b \cdot c \cdot N_c^*$ with $N_c^* = 9$ (IS 2911)
- [x] **Skin Friction in Clay:** $Q_s = A_s \cdot \alpha \cdot c$
- [x] **Skin Friction in Sand:** $Q_s = A_s \cdot K \cdot \sigma'_v \cdot \tan\delta$
- [x] **Allowable Load:** $Q_{allow} = Q_u / F_S$ with $F_S$ between **2.5 to 3.0**
- [x] **Pile Classifications:** Five axes – Function, Material, Installation, Load Direction, Cross-Section
- [x] **Schmertmann Factors:** $C_1$ = depth correction; $C_2$ = creep correction; never omit $C_2$
- [x] **Group Efficiency (Converse-Labarre):** $E_g = 1 - \theta \cdot \frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{90mn}$
- [x] **Standard SPT Correlation for $E_s$:** $E_s = 500(N+15)$ kN/m² (Bowles)
- [x] **Poisson's Ratio:** 0.3 to 0.4 for sands; 0.5 for undrained clays
- [x] **Allowable Settlement Limits (IS 8009 / IS 1904):** 25 mm for isolated footings on sand, 40 mm on clay, 65 mm for rafts

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pile Driving
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pile Driving

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Pile Driving** is the process of installing prefabricated piles into the ground by applying a series of dynamic impact blows (or continuous vibratory forces) using a pile-driving hammer, in order to transfer superstructural loads through weak/compressible soil strata to deeper, stronger bearing strata or dense granular layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Lock:** *"Pile driving is a method of pile installation wherein a pile is forced into the ground by repeated blows of a hammer or by continuous vibration, while the dynamic resistance offered by the soil is monitored to determine the static load-bearing capacity of the pile."*

## 1.2 Constituent Components of the Pile Driving System

| Component | Role in the System |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Pile (Helmet/Ram)** | Receives the hammer blow and transfers energy to the soil via its base/tip. |
| **Hammer (Drop/Steam/Diesel/Hydraulic)** | The prime mover that delivers impact energy. |
| **Pile Cushion** | Placed between the hammer and pile cap to protect the pile head. |
| **Helmet / Driving Cap** | Guides the hammer and holds the cushion. |
| **Followers** | An extension used when the pile head is below the hammer's reach (e.g., underwater). |
| **Soil Surroundings** | Provides dynamic and static resistance via shaft friction ($f_s$) and end bearing ($q_p$). |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> **The Nailing Analogy:** Imagine hammering a long iron nail into a wooden block. The harder the wood, the more difficult it is to push the nail in, and the more hammer blows you need. The depth of nail penetration per blow is called the **"set"** (denoted $S$). 
>
> Now replace the nail with a steel/concrete pile, the wooden block with soil, and the hammer with a much heavier pile-driving hammer. The resistance you feel is the soil's **bearing capacity**. By counting the blows needed to drive the pile a known distance (the set), engineers can mathematically back-calculate the soil's resistance and predict the pile's load-bearing capacity.
>
> **The big idea:** A pile that is hard to drive has a high bearing capacity, and a pile that drives easily has low bearing capacity.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Gravitational Acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (used for ram weight conversions).
> - **Standard Set (S):** Usually the penetration per blow for the **last few centimeters** of driving, in **mm/blow**.
> - **Standard Hammer Drop Height:** $h = 1 \text{ m}$ (for drop hammer rating per Hiley).
> - **Coefficient of Restitution ($e$):** A dimensionless factor (0 to 1) — $1.0$ for perfectly elastic, $0.0$ for perfectly plastic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Major Categories of Pile Installation

1. **Dynamic Installation (Pile Driving):** Uses impact or vibration — covered in this module.
2. **Static Installation (Bored / Cast-in-situ):** Uses drilling and concrete pouring (covered in the bored piles sub-module).

## 2.2 Types of Pile Hammers (Equipment Classification)

### A. Drop Hammers
- **Mechanism:** A heavy ram is lifted and freely dropped onto the pile head.
- **Hammer Energy:** $E = W \times h$, where $W$ = ram weight, $h$ = drop height.
- **Pros:** Simple, cheap, easy to control.
- **Cons:** Slow, low energy efficiency ($\eta \approx 0.7$ to $0.9$), causes high stress spikes.

### B. Single-Acting Hammers (Steam/Air)
- **Mechanism:** A heavy ram is lifted by steam/air pressure and then released to fall by gravity only (gravity drop).
- **Efficiency:** $\eta \approx 0.75$ to $0.85$.
- **Use:** Heavy, large piles, marine works.

### C. Double-Acting Hammers (Steam/Air)
- **Mechanism:** Steam/air pressure both **lifts** the ram and **accelerates** it downward.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta \approx 0.85$ to $0.95$ (high).
- **Use:** High driving rate in cohesionless soils.

### D. Diesel Hammers
- **Mechanism:** A two-stroke internal combustion engine; the explosion drives the ram down.
- **Energy:** $E = \eta \times (W \times h)$ (with $\eta$ up to $1.0$ for closed-end type).
- **Pros:** Self-contained, high mobility.
- **Cons:** Cannot start on very soft ground; noisy.

### E. Hydraulic / Vibratory Hammers
- **Mechanism:** Counter-rotating eccentric weights create vertical vibrations.
- **Use:** Excellent for non-displacement driving, sheet piles, and granular soils.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** Always know whether a hammer is **single-acting** (gravity drop) or **double-acting** (powered drop) — it directly affects the **rated energy (E)** value used in pile driving formulae.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Governing Equation | Variables | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Energy of Hammer** | $E = W \cdot h$ | $W$ = ram weight, $h$ = drop height | Energy delivered per blow |
| 2 | **Engineering News (ENR) Formula** | $R_u = \dfrac{W \cdot H}{S + C}$ | $C = 2.54$ cm (for SI: $C = 0.254$ m) | Quick/conservative estimate |
| 3 | **Hiley's Formula (Full)** | $R_u = \dfrac{\eta \cdot W \cdot h}{S + \dfrac{C}{2}} \cdot \dfrac{W + e^2 \cdot P}{W + P}$ | $e$ = coefficient of restitution, $P$ = pile weight, $C$ = total temporary elastic compression | Most widely used (KTU preferred) |
| 4 | **Modified Hiley's (in kN)** | $R_u = \dfrac{\eta \cdot W \cdot h}{S + \dfrac{C}{2}} \cdot \dfrac{W + n^2 \cdot P}{W + P}$ | $n = e$ (in some notations) | Numerical problems |
| 5 | **Janbu's Formula** | $R_u = \dfrac{\eta \cdot W \cdot h}{K_u \cdot S}$ | $K_u$ = 0.40 to 0.60 (depends on soil type) | Cohesionless soils |
| 6 | **Bearing Capacity via Set** | $R_u = \dfrac{W \cdot H}{S \cdot F_s}$ | $F_s$ = factor of safety (typically 3 to 6) | Ultimate load on pile |
| 7 | **Set per Blow** | $S = \dfrac{25 \text{ mm}}{\text{Number of blows}}$ | — | Field monitoring |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Units (KTU Valuation):** In SI units, $W$ and $P$ are in **kN**, $h$ and $S$ are in **metres**, $C$ is in **metres**, and $R_u$ comes out in **kN**. Do not mix mm and m — examiners often deduct marks for unit mismatches.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundation design for high-rise buildings**, bridges, offshore platforms.
- **Load testing on the fly** — when a static pile load test is too expensive, the **set at the end of driving (EOD set)** is used to estimate capacity.
- **Quality control** during construction — ensures piles are driven to the required bearing strata.
- **Wave Equation Analysis (WEAP)** — modern, rigorous method replacing older static pile driving formulae.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Hiley's Pile Driving Formula

Hiley's formula is derived from the **principle of conservation of energy** plus an empirical correction for the **energy losses** that occur during impact.

### Step 1: Energy Delivered by the Hammer
The gross energy of the falling ram is purely gravitational:

$$
E_{gross} = W \cdot h
$$

where $W$ = weight of the ram (in kN) and $h$ = effective height of drop (in m).

### Step 2: Efficiency Factor
Not all of the gross energy reaches the pile head. Mechanical losses (friction, cable, hammer inefficiencies) are absorbed by a factor $\eta$ (between 0 and 1):

$$
E_{delivered} = \eta \cdot W \cdot h
$$

### Step 3: Energy Balance on Impact
At the moment of impact, energy is split into two parts:
1. **Energy used to drive the pile** (work done against soil resistance $R_u$ over a set $S$).
2. **Energy lost as elastic compression** of the pile, cap, cushion, and soil ($C$).

$$
\eta \cdot W \cdot h = R_u \cdot S + R_u \cdot \frac{C}{2}
$$

> **Why $C/2$?** The energy stored in elastic compression is the area under the load-deformation triangle, which is $\tfrac{1}{2} R_u \cdot C$. So when we solve for $R_u$, this becomes $R_u \cdot \tfrac{C}{2}$.

### Step 4: Solving for $R_u$ (Without Restitution)

$$
R_u = \frac{\eta \cdot W \cdot h}{S + \frac{C}{2}}
$$

### Step 5: Energy Transferred to the Pile (Coefficient of Restitution $e$)
The **coefficient of restitution** accounts for the energy lost between the ram and the pile head. The fraction of energy retained is $\dfrac{(1-e^2)}{1}$ — a momentum-based correction that **reduces** the effective ram weight during impact.

The momentum transfer modifies the energy in the impact term to:

$$
E_{impact} = \eta \cdot h \cdot \left( W + e^2 \cdot P \right)
$$

where $P$ = weight of the pile (in kN).

### Step 6: Final Hiley's Formula (Assembled)

$$
R_u = \frac{\eta \cdot W \cdot h}{S + \frac{C}{2}} \cdot \frac{W + e^2 \cdot P}{W + P}
$$

This is the equation to be used for numerical problems in KTU 2024 examinations.

## 3.2 KTU-Standard Numerical Example (Worked-Out)

> **Problem:** A concrete pile of weight **20 kN** and length **12 m** is driven by a hammer of weight **40 kN** falling freely from a height of **0.80 m**. The average set per blow is **5 mm**. The coefficient of restitution is **0.5** and the total elastic compression is **18 mm**. The hammer efficiency is **85%**. Estimate the ultimate bearing capacity using Hiley's formula. (Use $F_s = 3$ for safe load.)

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Given data:**
- $W = 40$ kN
- $P = 20$ kN
- $h = 0.80$ m
- $S = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m
- $C = 18$ mm $= 0.018$ m
- $e = 0.5$
- $\eta = 0.85$

**Step 1: Substitute into Hiley's formula.**

$$
R_u = \frac{\eta \cdot W \cdot h}{S + \frac{C}{2}} \cdot \frac{W + e^2 \cdot P}{W + P}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the numerator (energy term).**

$$
\eta \cdot W \cdot h = 0.85 \times 40 \times 0.80 = 27.2 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator (set + half compression).**

$$
S + \frac{C}{2} = 0.005 + \frac{0.018}{2} = 0.005 + 0.009 = 0.014 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the energy-transfer ratio.**

$$
\frac{W + e^2 \cdot P}{W + P} = \frac{40 + (0.5)^2 \times 20}{40 + 20} = \frac{40 + 5}{60} = \frac{45}{60} = 0.75
$$

**Step 5: Combine to obtain $R_u$.**

$$
R_u = \frac{27.2}{0.014} \times 0.75
$$

$$
R_u = 1942.857 \times 0.75
$$

$$
R_u = 1457.14 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6: Apply Factor of Safety for Safe Load.**

$$
Q_{safe} = \frac{R_u}{F_s} = \frac{1457.14}{3} \approx 485.7 \text{ kN}
$$

### Final Answer Table

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Ultimate Load ($R_u$) | **1457.14 kN** |
| Safe Load ($Q_{safe}$) | **485.71 kN** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Step Logic Check:** A high $R_u$ (1457 kN) is consistent with a **low set** (5 mm/blow) — a pile that barely moves per blow indicates very strong soil resistance. This matches our physical intuition from the nailing analogy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pile Driving System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Source] --> B[Pile Hammer]
    B --> C[Helmet and Pile Cushion]
    C --> D[Pile Head]
    D --> E[Pile Shaft]
    E --> F[Pile Tip]
    F --> G[Soil Bearing Stratum]
    H[Driving Log Recorder] --> I[Set and Blow Count]
    I --> J[Bearing Capacity Estimate]
    K[Wave Equation Analysis WEAP] --> J
    J --> L[Accept or Reject Pile]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart for Hammer Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Project] --> B{Soil Type}
    B -- Granular Sandy --> C[Vibratory or Diesel Hammer]
    B -- Cohesive Clay --> D[Hydraulic Single Acting]
    B -- Mixed Strata --> E[Double Acting Air Steam]
    C --> F{Noise and Pollution Constraint}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Hydraulic Hammer]
    F -- No --> H[Use Diesel Hammer]
    D --> I[Proceed with Driving]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Monitor Set and Blow Count]
    J --> K[Compute Capacity via Hiley]
    K --> L{Acceptable Load}
    L -- Yes --> M[Pile Accepted]
    L -- No --> N[Re drive or Cast Bored Pile]
```

## 4.3 Energy Transfer Model Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Energy E_gross] --> B[Hammer Efficiency Loss]
    B --> C[Net Delivered Energy E_d]
    C --> D[Impact at Pile Head]
    D --> E[Energy Loss via Restitution e]
    E --> F[Effective Driving Energy]
    F --> G[Work Against Soil R_u x S]
    F --> H[Elastic Compression Loss R_u x C over 2]
    G --> I[Pile Penetration Set S]
    H --> J[Soil Cushion Pile Compression]
```

## 4.4 Sequence of Operations During Pile Driving

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Position Pile on Ground] --> A2[Align with Hammer Guide]
    A2 --> A3[Apply First Few Light Blows to Seat Pile]
    A3 --> A4[Monitor Initial Set and Verticality]
    A4 --> A5[Continue Driving with Rated Hammer]
    A5 --> A6[Record Blow Count per Foot of Penetration]
    A6 --> A7{Approaching Design Depth}
    A7 -- No --> A5
    A7 -- Yes --> A8[Record Final Set EOD]
    A8 --> A9[Compute Ultimate Load using Hiley]
    A9 --> A10{Load Sufficient}
    A10 -- Yes --> A11[Cut Off Pile to Required Elevation]
    A10 -- No --> A12[Extend Pile and Re drive]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define "Set" and "Blow Count" in pile driving. Why are they monitored?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Set (S):** The penetration of the pile per hammer blow, usually expressed in **mm/blow**. It is measured over the last few centimeters of driving just before the pile reaches its final depth. (1.5 marks)
- **Blow Count:** The number of hammer blows required to drive the pile through a fixed penetration (commonly **25 mm** or **300 mm**). (1 mark)
- **Why monitored:** They are inverse indicators of soil resistance — a low set or a high blow count implies a high bearing capacity. They are used in pile driving formulae (Hiley, ENR) to estimate the ultimate load capacity of the pile. (0.5 marks)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between a single-acting and a double-acting pile hammer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Single-Acting | Double-Acting |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Lifting mechanism** | Steam/air lifts ram | Steam/air lifts ram |
| **Downward motion** | Gravity drop only | Steam/air also accelerates it down |
| **Blow frequency** | Lower (e.g., 30–60/min) | Higher (e.g., 100–300/min) |
| **Efficiency** | 0.75–0.85 | 0.85–0.95 |
| **Best use** | Heavy piles, dense strata | Granular/cohesionless soils |

(3 marks — full table or equivalent text)

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive **Hiley's pile driving formula**, clearly stating all the terms involved. Why is it preferred over the Engineering News Record (ENR) formula?

**(b) [7 marks]** A pile is driven by a **30 kN** hammer falling from a height of **1.0 m**. The pile weight is **15 kN**, the set is **4 mm/blow**, total elastic compression is **15 mm**, coefficient of restitution is **0.4**, and hammer efficiency is **80%**. Compute the **ultimate and safe load** (use $F_s = 4$).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Hiley's Formula Derivation

- **State the energy balance equation:** $\eta W h = R_u S + \tfrac{1}{2} R_u C$ (2 marks)
- **Solve for $R_u$** without restitution: $R_u = \dfrac{\eta W h}{S + C/2}$ (1.5 marks)
- **Introduce restitution correction** with pile weight: $R_u = \dfrac{\eta W h}{S + C/2} \cdot \dfrac{W + e^2 P}{W + P}$ (1.5 marks)
- **Define each term** ($W$, $h$, $S$, $C$, $e$, $P$, $\eta$). (1 mark)
- **Why preferred over ENR:** Hiley accounts for both **elastic compression losses** AND **coefficient of restitution** (energy lost in impact), giving a more realistic estimate; ENR is over-conservative. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Numerical Computation

**Given:** $W = 30$ kN, $P = 15$ kN, $h = 1.0$ m, $S = 0.004$ m, $C = 0.015$ m, $e = 0.4$, $\eta = 0.80$, $F_s = 4$.

**Step 1: Numerator**

$$
\eta \cdot W \cdot h = 0.80 \times 30 \times 1.0 = 24 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 2: Denominator (set + half compression)**

$$
S + \frac{C}{2} = 0.004 + \frac{0.015}{2} = 0.004 + 0.0075 = 0.0115 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Restitution ratio**

$$
\frac{W + e^2 P}{W + P} = \frac{30 + (0.4)^2 \times 15}{30 + 15} = \frac{30 + 2.4}{45} = \frac{32.4}{45} = 0.72
$$

**Step 4: Ultimate load**

$$
R_u = \frac{24}{0.0115} \times 0.72 = 2086.96 \times 0.72 = 1502.61 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5: Safe load**

$$
Q_{safe} = \frac{R_u}{F_s} = \frac{1502.61}{4} = 375.65 \text{ kN}
$$

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| $R_u$ | **1502.61 kN** |
| $Q_{safe}$ | **375.65 kN** |

(7 marks — 1 mark per step + 2 marks for the final answer)

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the various types of **pile hammers** used in installation, with a sketch of their functional working principles. Compare the relative **hammer efficiency** ranges.

**(b) [7 marks]** During driving, the set for the **last 10 blows** was observed as **25 mm total**. The hammer weight is **25 kN** with a drop of **750 mm**. The pile weight is **10 kN** and the elastic compression is **12 mm**. Take $\eta = 0.75$, $e = 0.45$. Using Hiley's formula, find the **safe load** with $F_s = 3.5$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Types of Pile Hammers

1. **Drop Hammer** – Ram raised by cable and dropped by gravity. $\eta = 0.7$ to $0.9$. (1.5 marks)
2. **Single-Acting Hammer** – Steam/air lifts ram; gravity drop. $\eta = 0.75$ to $0.85$. (1.5 marks)
3. **Double-Acting Hammer** – Steam/air lifts AND pushes ram down. $\eta = 0.85$ to $0.95$. (1.5 marks)
4. **Diesel Hammer** – Two-stroke internal combustion. $\eta$ up to $1.0$ (closed-end). (1 mark)
5. **Hydraulic / Vibratory** – Uses eccentric rotation; ideal for sheet piles and granular soils. $\eta$ depends on soil type. (1 mark)
6. **Comparison summary** in tabular form. (0.5 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Numerical Computation

**Given:** $W = 25$ kN, $P = 10$ kN, $h = 0.75$ m, total set for 10 blows = 25 mm → $S = 2.5$ mm = 0.0025 m, $C = 12$ mm = 0.012 m, $\eta = 0.75$, $e = 0.45$, $F_s = 3.5$.

**Step 1: Numerator**

$$
\eta \cdot W \cdot h = 0.75 \times 25 \times 0.75 = 14.0625 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 2: Denominator**

$$
S + \frac{C}{2} = 0.0025 + \frac{0.012}{2} = 0.0025 + 0.006 = 0.0085 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Restitution ratio**

$$
\frac{W + e^2 P}{W + P} = \frac{25 + (0.45)^2 \times 10}{25 + 10} = \frac{25 + 2.025}{35} = \frac{27.025}{35} = 0.772
$$

**Step 4: Ultimate load**

$$
R_u = \frac{14.0625}{0.0085} \times 0.772 = 1654.41 \times 0.772 = 1277.20 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5: Safe load**

$$
Q_{safe} = \frac{R_u}{F_s} = \frac{1277.20}{3.5} = 364.91 \text{ kN}
$$

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| $R_u$ | **1277.20 kN** |
| $Q_{safe}$ | **364.91 kN** |

(7 marks — 1 mark per step + 2 marks for the final answer)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Pile Driving problems:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Mixing (Most Common — 1 to 2 marks lost):** Set $S$ and compression $C$ are usually given in **mm**, but Hiley's formula requires **metres**. Convert before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the Restitution Term:** Many students solve $R_u = \dfrac{\eta W h}{S + C/2}$ and miss the $\dfrac{W + e^2 P}{W + P}$ factor. This is a **3-mark deduction** in typical valuation.
> 3. **Wrong Sign of $e^2$:** The restitution factor must be **$e^2$** (square of the coefficient of restitution), not $e$.
> 4. **Using the wrong drop height:** $h$ is the **effective drop height** of the ram, not the height of the rig.
> 5. **No Factor of Safety:** The problem asks for the *safe load* — if you only give $R_u$, you lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing ENR constant $C$:** In ENR, $C = 25.4$ mm (or $0.254$ m) is a **fixed empirical value**, not the elastic compression.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Pile driving** = installing a pile by repeated hammer blows (or vibration).
- ✅ **Set (S)** = penetration per blow; **Blow Count** = blows per unit depth. Inversely related to bearing capacity.
- ✅ **Hiley's formula (MUST remember):** $R_u = \dfrac{\eta W h}{S + C/2} \cdot \dfrac{W + e^2 P}{W + P}$
- ✅ **Engineering News Formula (Simpler, more conservative):** $R_u = \dfrac{W H}{S + C}$ with $C = 0.254$ m (SI).
- ✅ **Janbu's Formula:** $R_u = \dfrac{\eta W h}{K_u S}$ — useful for cohesionless soils ($K_u = 0.4$ to $0.6$).
- ✅ **Hammer Types:** Drop → Single-Acting → Double-Acting → Diesel → Hydraulic/Vibratory (in order of increasing efficiency).
- ✅ **Efficiency range:** Drop (0.7–0.9), Single (0.75–0.85), Double (0.85–0.95), Diesel (up to 1.0).
- ✅ **Unit Convention:** $W$, $P$ in kN; $h$, $S$, $C$ in **metres**; $e$ and $\eta$ are dimensionless.
- ✅ **Safe Load** = $R_u / F_s$ (where $F_s$ is **3 to 6**, KTU typically uses **3 to 4**).
- ✅ **Coefficient of Restitution ($e$):** Lies between **0 and 1**; $e^2$ is the energy-loss factor.
- ✅ **Elastic Compression (C):** Always appears in the denominator, **divided by 2**.
- ✅ **Modern alternative:** Wave Equation Analysis (WEAP) is more rigorous but not typically asked in 14-mark KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Carrying Capacity of Piles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Load Carrying Capacity of Piles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
The **Load Carrying Capacity of a Pile** is defined as the maximum axial compressive (or tensile) load that a deep foundation element (pile) can transmit to the surrounding soil mass and the founding stratum without causing structural failure of the pile itself or shear failure of the supporting soil. It is the cumulative resistance offered by two distinct mechanisms: **Point (End) Bearing** at the pile tip and **Skin (Shaft) Friction** along the embedded lateral surface.

Mathematically, the **Ultimate Load Carrying Capacity ($Q_{u}$)** of a single pile is expressed as:

$$Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s}$$

where:
- $Q_{b}$ = Ultimate Point Bearing Resistance (Base Resistance)
- $Q_{s}$ = Ultimate Skin Friction Resistance (Shaft Resistance)

The **Safe (Allowable) Load Capacity ($Q_{a}$)** is then obtained by applying a Factor of Safety (FoS), typically **2.5 to 3.0** for static methods and **1.5 to 2.0** when verified by pile load tests.

$$Q_{a} = \frac{Q_{u}}{FoS}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 4, students must master both **Static Methods** (based on soil properties) and **Dynamic Methods** (based on driving energy/hammer data), along with **Pile Load Test** interpretation and the concept of **Negative Skin Friction** (down-drag).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine trying to push a thick wooden stake into soft ground. The resistance you feel comes from two sources:
1. The **tip** of the stake is trying to push aside a column of soil beneath it (this is **Point Bearing**).
2. The **sides** of the stake are rubbing against the soil all along its length, generating friction that tries to hold it in place (this is **Skin Friction**).

If you drive a longer stake into firmer clay or hit a layer of dense sand at the bottom, both resistances increase. A pile foundation works exactly this way; engineers carefully choose the **length, diameter, and type** of pile so that the combined tip resistance and side friction safely support the heavy superstructure load (column load) above.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metrics to Remember:**
> - Unit weight of concrete pile: $\gamma_c \approx \mathbf{24 \text{ kN/m}^3}$
> - Factor of Safety (Static formula): $\mathbf{2.5 \text{ to } 3.0}$
> - Factor of Safety (Pile Load Test verified): $\mathbf{1.5 \text{ to } 2.0}$
> - Typical pile diameter range: **300 mm to 1500 mm**
> - Typical pile length range: **5 m to 60 m**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Piles and Their Load Transfer Mechanism
Piles are broadly classified based on how they transfer load to the soil:
- **End Bearing Piles:** Almost the entire load is transferred to a firm stratum at the tip (e.g., piles socketed into rock). $Q_b \gg Q_s$.
- **Friction Piles (Floating Piles):** The tip rests on a soft layer; the load is carried almost entirely by skin friction. $Q_s \gg Q_b$.
- **Combined End Bearing and Friction Piles:** Both mechanisms contribute significantly. This is the most common practical case.

### 2.2 Static Method – Bearing Capacity of Piles
The static method estimates pile capacity using the **shear strength parameters** of the soil (cohesion $c$ and angle of internal friction $\phi$). The general equations are:

**Point Bearing Capacity ($Q_b$):**
$$Q_{b} = A_{b} \cdot q_{b} = A_{b} \cdot (c \cdot N_{c}^{*} + \sigma_{v}' \cdot N_{q}^{*})$$

where:
- $A_b$ = Cross-sectional area of pile base
- $N_c^{*}, N_q^{*}$ = Modified bearing capacity factors (function of $\phi$ and pile geometry, e.g., Meyerhof/Vesic)
- $\sigma_v'$ = Effective vertical stress at pile tip level

**Skin Friction Capacity ($Q_s$):**
For a homogeneous soil layer of length $L$ and perimeter $P$:
$$Q_{s} = P \cdot L \cdot f_{s} = P \cdot L \cdot (\alpha \cdot \bar{c} + K \cdot \bar{\sigma}_{v}' \cdot \tan \delta)$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = Adhesion factor (function of pile material and soil type)
- $\bar{c}$ = Average undrained cohesion
- $K$ = Coefficient of lateral earth pressure
- $\delta$ = Angle of wall friction between pile and soil

For **clays** (using $\alpha$-method by Tomlinson):
$$Q_{s} = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot A_{s}$$
For **sands** (using $\beta$-method):
$$Q_{s} = K \cdot \bar{\sigma}_{v}' \cdot \tan \delta \cdot A_{s}$$

### 2.3 Dynamic Methods
Used when soil data is unavailable; based on **Hammer Efficiency** and **Set per Blow**.
- **Engineering News Formula (ENR):** $Q_{a} = \frac{W_{H} \cdot h}{F \cdot (S + C)}$
- **Hiley's Formula:** $Q_{a} = \frac{\eta \cdot W_{H} \cdot h}{F \cdot (S + 0.5 \cdot C)} \cdot \frac{W_{H} + n^2 \cdot W_{P}}{W_{H} + W_{P}}$

### 2.4 Pile Load Test (PLT)
A full-scale test where load is applied incrementally to a test pile; the load-settlement curve gives the true ultimate capacity. The failure load is identified as that corresponding to a settlement of **10% of pile diameter**.

### 2.5 Negative Skin Friction (Down-drag)
When a fill layer settles around the pile, it drags the pile downward, adding load instead of resisting it. The **neutral plane** is the depth where pile settlement equals soil settlement. Below the neutral plane, normal positive skin friction develops.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ultimate Capacity | $Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s}$ | kN or kN/m² × area |
| 2 | Safe Capacity | $Q_{a} = Q_{u} / FoS$ | FoS = **2.5 – 3.0** (static) |
| 3 | Point Bearing (General) | $Q_{b} = A_{b}(cN_c^{*} + \sigma_v'N_q^{*})$ | Cohesive and cohesionless soil |
| 4 | Point Bearing (Sand) | $Q_{b} = A_{b} \cdot \sigma_v' \cdot N_q^{*}$ | Use Vesic / Meyerhof $N_q^{*}$ |
| 5 | Point Bearing (Clay, $\phi = 0$) | $Q_{b} = A_{b} \cdot c \cdot N_{c}^{*} = 9 \cdot A_b \cdot c$ | For piles in clay, $N_c^* \approx 9$ |
| 6 | Skin Friction (Clay) | $Q_{s} = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot A_{s}$ | $\alpha$ = 0.3 to 1.0 (Tomlinson) |
| 7 | Skin Friction (Sand) | $Q_{s} = K \cdot \bar{\sigma}_v' \cdot \tan \delta \cdot A_{s}$ | $\beta = K \tan\delta$ |
| 8 | Coyle & Castello (Sand) | $Q_{b} = A_b \cdot \sigma_v' \cdot N_q^{*} \leq 0.5 \cdot A_b \cdot \sigma_v' \cdot N_q$ | Ultimate limit for piles |
| 9 | ENR Formula (Dynamic) | $Q_{a} = \frac{W_H \cdot h}{F(S + C)}$ | Drop/steam hammer; F = **6** (drop), **3** (steam) |
| 10 | Hiley's Formula (Dynamic) | $Q_{a} = \frac{\eta W_H h}{F(S+C/2)} \cdot \frac{W_H + n^2 W_P}{W_H + W_P}$ | Most accurate dynamic formula |
| 11 | PLT Failure Criterion | Settlement = **10% of pile diameter** | Identifies $Q_u$ from field test |
| 12 | Group Efficiency | $\eta_g = \frac{Q_{g,ult}}{n \cdot Q_{u, single}}$ | For pile groups in clay/sand |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Pile load capacity estimation is critical in the design of bridge piers, high-rise building foundations, offshore wind turbine platforms, and any structure built on soft, compressible, or water-saturated soils where shallow footings would undergo excessive settlement. Software like **PLAXIS 3D** and **FB-Pier** uses these exact static formulas combined with finite element analysis for final verification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Ultimate Bearing Capacity of a Pile in Cohesionless Soil (Coyle and Castello Approach)

The Coyle and Castello method is widely tested in KTU for sand deposits. It empirically correlates the unit point bearing and unit skin friction to the effective overburden pressure and a depth ratio.

**Step 1:** Define the effective vertical stress at any depth $z$ within the soil layer:
$$\sigma_{v}'(z) = \gamma' \cdot z$$

where $\gamma'$ is the submerged unit weight below the water table (typically $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w \approx 8 \text{ to } 11 \text{ kN/m}^3$).

**Step 2:** The unit point bearing resistance at the pile tip is given by:
$$q_{b} = \sigma_{v,tip}' \cdot N_{q}^{*}$$

where $\sigma_{v,tip}'$ is the effective vertical stress at the pile tip and $N_q^*$ is the modified bearing capacity factor (obtained from Coyle-Castello charts, function of $L/B$ and $\phi$).

**Step 3:** The total point bearing capacity is obtained by multiplying by base area $A_b$:
$$Q_{b} = q_{b} \cdot A_{b} = \sigma_{v,tip}' \cdot N_{q}^{*} \cdot A_{b}$$

**Step 4:** The unit skin friction at any depth $z$ (from Coyle and Castello empirical curves) is:
$$f_{s}(z) = K \cdot \sigma_{v}'(z) \cdot \tan \delta = \beta \cdot \sigma_{v}'(z)$$

where $\beta = K \tan \delta$ is the skin friction coefficient. From Coyle-Castello, $\beta$ is a function of $L/B$ and $\phi$.

**Step 5:** Integrate the skin friction over the entire embedded length $L$ and perimeter $P$:
$$Q_{s} = P \cdot \int_{0}^{L} f_{s}(z) \, dz = P \cdot \int_{0}^{L} \beta \cdot \gamma' \cdot z \, dz$$

**Step 6:** Perform the integration assuming $\gamma'$ is constant:
$$Q_{s} = P \cdot \beta \cdot \gamma' \cdot \left[ \frac{z^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{L} = P \cdot \beta \cdot \gamma' \cdot \frac{L^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 7:** The total ultimate capacity is the sum:
$$Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s} = A_{b} \cdot \sigma_{v,tip}' \cdot N_{q}^{*} + P \cdot \beta \cdot \gamma' \cdot \frac{L^{2}}{2}$$

This completes the derivation.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Numerical Application (KTU Standard Problem Style)

**Given Data:**
- Pile type: Bored concrete pile, diameter $B = 0.5$ m, length $L = 15$ m.
- Soil: Dense sand, $\phi = 35^\circ$, $\gamma' = 10$ kN/m³.
- Ground water table at ground level.
- Adhesion factor for sand not applicable (cohesionless).

**Step 1: Calculate Base Area and Perimeter**
$$A_{b} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot B^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.5)^{2} = 0.1963 \text{ m}^{2}$$
$$P = \pi \cdot B = \pi \cdot 0.5 = 1.571 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Determine $N_q^*$ and $\beta$ from Coyle-Castello charts**
For $\phi = 35^\circ$ and $L/B = 15/0.5 = 30$:
- $N_q^{*} \approx 80$ (read from standard chart for $L/B = 30$)
- $\beta \approx 0.40$

**Step 3: Effective Vertical Stress at Pile Tip**
$$\sigma_{v,tip}' = \gamma' \cdot L = 10 \cdot 15 = 150 \text{ kN/m}^{2}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Point Bearing Capacity**
$$Q_{b} = A_{b} \cdot \sigma_{v,tip}' \cdot N_{q}^{*} = 0.1963 \cdot 150 \cdot 80 = 2356 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Skin Friction Capacity**
$$Q_{s} = P \cdot \beta \cdot \gamma' \cdot \frac{L^{2}}{2} = 1.571 \cdot 0.40 \cdot 10 \cdot \frac{(15)^{2}}{2}$$
$$Q_{s} = 1.571 \cdot 0.40 \cdot 10 \cdot 112.5 = 707 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Total Ultimate Capacity**
$$Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s} = 2356 + 707 = 3063 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7: Allowable Capacity using FoS = 2.5**
$$Q_{a} = \frac{3063}{2.5} = 1225.2 \text{ kN} \approx \mathbf{1225 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Insight:** Notice that for a long pile in dense sand, the **point bearing dominates** (76.9% of total), while skin friction contributes only 23.1%. This justifies classifying it as a hybrid pile, but with a strong end-bearing behavior.

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix of Methods

| Method | Best Suited For | Key Parameter Required | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static ($\alpha$-method) | Clay | $c_u$, $\alpha$ | **High** |
| Static ($\beta$-method) | Sand | $\phi$, $K$, $\delta$ | **High** |
| Coyle-Castello | Sand (large piles) | $\phi$, $L/B$ chart | **High** |
| Meyerhof | General cohesionless | $N_q^*$, $\sigma_v'$ | Moderate |
| ENR Formula | Preliminary design | Hammer weight, set | Moderate |
| Hiley's Formula | Refined dynamic design | Hammer efficiency $\eta$ | **High** |
| Pile Load Test | Final verification | Load-Settlement curve | **Very High** |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pile Load Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SUPERSTRUCTURE LOAD"]:::load --> B["PILE CAP"]
    B --> C["INDIVIDUAL PILE SHAFT"]
    C --> D["POINT BEARING Qb AT TIP"]
    C --> E["SKIN FRICTION Qs ALONG SHAFT"]
    D --> F["ULTIMATE CAPACITY Qu = Qb + Qs"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["FACTOR OF SAFETY FoS"]
    G --> H["ALLOWABLE CAPACITY Qa"]
    H --> I["DESIGN VERIFICATION VIA PLT"]
    I --> J["FINAL SAFE LOAD"]

    K["HAMMER BLOW ENERGY"]:::energy --> L["DYNAMIC FORMULA ENR HILEY"]
    L --> J

    M["NEGATIVE SKIN FRICTION DOWNDRAG"]:::warn --> C

    classDef load fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef energy fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef warn fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Detailed Load Transfer Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TopHalf["UPPER ZONE ABOVE NEUTRAL PLANE"]
        P1["Pile settles less than soil"]
        P1 --> S1["Soil drags pile DOWN"]
        S1 --> NSF["Negative Skin Friction ADDS LOAD"]
    end

    subgraph MidHalf["NEUTRAL PLANE"]
        NP["Zero Relative Displacement"]
    end

    subgraph BotHalf["LOWER ZONE BELOW NEUTRAL PLANE"]
        P2["Pile settles more than soil"]
        P2 --> S2["Pile pushes soil DOWN"]
        S2 --> PSF["Positive Skin Friction RESISTS LOAD"]
        S2 --> PB["Point Bearing at Tip"]
    end

    TopHalf --> MidHalf
    MidHalf --> BotHalf
```

### 4.3 Flowchart: Decision Logic for Selecting Pile Capacity Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["START"]) --> Q1{"Soil Type Known?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes Clay" --> M1["Use alpha method or Skempton"]
    Q1 -- "Yes Sand" --> M2["Use beta method or Coyle Castello"]
    Q1 -- "No Soil Data" --> M3["Use Dynamic Formula ENR Hiley"]
    M1 --> V["Apply FoS 2.5 to 3.0"]
    M2 --> V
    M3 --> V
    V --> PLT{"Pile Load Test Conducted?"}
    PLT -- "Yes" --> FoS2["Reduce FoS to 1.5 to 2.0"]
    PLT -- "No" --> Final["Final Qa from Formula"]
    FoS2 --> Final
    Final --> End(["END REPORT Qa"])
```

### 4.4 Schematic of Stress Distribution Along Pile

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0["Ground Surface"] --> L1["Depth 0 to L3 Negative Skin Friction Zone"]
    L1 --> L2["Depth L3 Neutral Plane"]
    L2 --> L3["Depth L3 to L Positive Skin Friction Zone"]
    L3 --> L4["Pile Tip Point Bearing Zone"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Negative Skin Friction in piles. Under what conditions does it develop?*

**Model Answer:**
Negative skin friction (or **down-drag**) is the downward shear force exerted on the pile shaft by the surrounding soil when the soil settles more than the pile itself. It **adds** to the structural load on the pile rather than resisting it.

**Conditions for development:**
- A recently placed fill layer above the pile compresses the underlying compressible stratum (e.g., soft clay).
- Lowering of the ground water table causes consolidation of the soil.
- Under-consolidated clay layers continue to settle under self-weight.
- The drag load must be added to the structural dead load; otherwise, the pile may fail in structural compression even if the soil bearing capacity is adequate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Engineering News Record (ENR) formula for pile capacity. What are its limitations?*

**Model Answer:**
The **ENR Formula** is given by:
$$Q_{a} = \frac{W_{H} \cdot h}{F \cdot (S + C)}$$

where:
- $W_H$ = Weight of hammer
- $h$ = Height of drop
- $F$ = Factor of Safety (**6** for drop hammer, **3** for steam hammer)
- $S$ = Final set (penetration per blow, in mm or cm)
- $C$ = Empirical constant (**2.54 cm** or **10 mm**)

**Limitations:**
1. It is purely empirical and does not account for soil properties.
2. It assumes all hammer energy is transmitted to the pile (ignores losses).
3. It is unreliable for very long piles or piles in very soft soils.
4. It does not differentiate between end-bearing and friction piles.
5. It should be used only for **preliminary design**, not for final verification.

---

### 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** A precast concrete pile of **30 cm diameter** and **12 m length** is driven into a homogeneous clay layer with **$c_u = 80$ kN/m²** and **$\phi = 0$**. The pile is driven from a ground surface where the overburden pressure is **$0$ kN/m²**. Using the static formula, determine:
1. The ultimate point bearing capacity $Q_b$
2. The ultimate skin friction capacity $Q_s$ (use $\alpha = 0.6$ for driven pile in stiff clay)
3. The total ultimate load and safe load (FoS = 2.5)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Base Area and Perimeter**
$$A_{b} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.30)^{2} = 0.0707 \text{ m}^{2}$$
$$P = \pi \cdot 0.30 = 0.9425 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Point Bearing for $\phi = 0$ (Skempton/Meyerhof)**
For piles in clay with $\phi = 0$, $N_c^{*} = 9$:
$$Q_{b} = A_{b} \cdot c_u \cdot N_{c}^{*} = 0.0707 \cdot 80 \cdot 9 = 50.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Skin Friction (Tomlinson $\alpha$-method)**
$$Q_{s} = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot A_{s} = \alpha \cdot c_u \cdot (P \cdot L)$$
$$Q_{s} = 0.6 \cdot 80 \cdot (0.9425 \cdot 12) = 0.6 \cdot 80 \cdot 11.31 = 542.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Total Ultimate Capacity**
$$Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s} = 50.9 + 542.9 = 593.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Safe Load**
$$Q_{a} = \frac{Q_{u}}{FoS} = \frac{593.8}{2.5} = \mathbf{237.5 \text{ kN}}$$

**(b)** What are the advantages and disadvantages of a **Pile Load Test** over a static formula-based design?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Pile Load Test | Static Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Most accurate, in-situ behavior | Empirical, approximate |
| **Soil Disturbance** | Captures real soil-pile interaction | Assumes idealized conditions |
| **Cost** | Very high (equipment, time) | Low (uses existing soil data) |
| **Time** | Slow (days to weeks) | Quick |
| **FOS Applicable** | Reduced FOS (1.5 – 2.0) | Higher FOS (2.5 – 3.0) |
| **Negative Skin Friction** | Directly observed | Must be calculated separately |

**Advantages of PLT:** (i) Direct measurement of true load-settlement behavior; (ii) Verifies design assumptions; (iii) Allows reduced FOS; (iv) Reveals hidden soil heterogeneities.
**Disadvantages of PLT:** (i) Expensive and time-consuming; (ii) Requires space for reaction frame and kentledge; (iii) Cannot be performed on every pile (only on test piles); (iv) Difficult for very large piles.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** A **Hiley hammer** with a weight $W_H = 30$ kN drops from a height $h = 1.0$ m to drive a pile. The pile weight $W_P = 15$ kN, coefficient of restitution $n = 0.5$, hammer efficiency $\eta = 0.85$, final set $S = 8$ mm per blow, and temporary compression $C = 5$ mm. Determine the safe load using **Hiley's formula** with FOS = 3.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Terms**
- $W_H = 30$ kN, $h = 1.0$ m, $F = 3$, $S = 0.008$ m, $C = 0.005$ m
- $W_P = 15$ kN, $n = 0.5$, $\eta = 0.85$

**Step 2: Apply Hiley's Formula**
$$Q_{a} = \frac{\eta \cdot W_{H} \cdot h}{F \cdot \left(S + \frac{C}{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{W_{H} + n^{2} \cdot W_{P}}{W_{H} + W_{P}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Energy Factor**
$$Q_{a} = \frac{0.85 \cdot 30 \cdot 1.0}{3 \cdot (0.008 + 0.0025)} \cdot \frac{30 + (0.5)^{2} \cdot 15}{30 + 15}$$
$$Q_{a} = \frac{25.5}{3 \cdot 0.0105} \cdot \frac{30 + 3.75}{45}$$
$$Q_{a} = \frac{25.5}{0.0315} \cdot \frac{33.75}{45}$$
$$Q_{a} = 809.52 \cdot 0.75 = \mathbf{607.14 \text{ kN}}$$

**(b)** Compare **Hiley's formula** and **ENR formula** in terms of accuracy and applicability.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hiley's Formula | ENR Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Energy losses | Considered through $\eta$ and $n$ | Not considered |
| Pile weight | Included | Neglected |
| Hammer type | Any hammer (efficiency-based) | Drop/steam only |
| Set and compression | Both $S$ and $C$ used | Only $S + C$ term |
| Accuracy | **More accurate** | Less accurate |
| Complexity | Slightly more complex | Very simple |
| Best for | Refined final design | Preliminary design |

Hiley's is **recommended** for final design as it accounts for energy lost in elastic compression, which becomes significant in long or heavy piles.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Using $S$ in **mm** but $C$ in **m** in Hiley's formula. Always convert both to the same unit before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting $\sigma_v'$:** Students often use total stress $\gamma \cdot L$ instead of effective stress $\gamma' \cdot L$ in sand below the water table, leading to over-estimation of capacity.
> 3. **Wrong $N_c$ value:** Using $N_c = 6$ (shallow foundation) instead of $N_c^* = 9$ (deep pile) for $\phi = 0$ clay. This costs **2-3 marks easily**.
> 4. **Sign Error in Negative Skin Friction:** Treating NSF as resistance instead of additional loading. NSF is a **load, not a capacity**.
> 5. **Factor of Safety Confusion:** Using FoS = 6 for static formula (it should be 2.5–3.0; 6 is for ENR drop hammer only).
> 6. **Pile Load Test Failure Criterion:** Stating "any settlement is failure" – the correct criterion is **settlement = 10% of pile diameter**.
> 7. **Mixing $\alpha$ and $\beta$ Methods:** $\alpha$ is for **clay**, $\beta$ is for **sand**. Cross-using yields 0 marks in sub-parts.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 4 Revision – Pile Capacity Cheat Points:**
>
> - **Master Formula:** $Q_u = Q_b + Q_s$ (always split into base and shaft).
> - **Sand Tip Bearing:** $Q_b = A_b \cdot \sigma_v' \cdot N_q^*$ (Vesic $N_q^*$ depends on $\phi$).
> - **Clay Tip Bearing ($\phi=0$):** $Q_b = 9 \cdot A_b \cdot c_u$.
> - **Sand Skin Friction:** $Q_s = P \cdot L \cdot \beta \cdot \bar{\sigma}_v'$ where $\beta = K \tan\delta$.
> - **Clay Skin Friction:** $Q_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P \cdot L$; $\alpha$ from Tomlinson chart.
> - **Coyle-Castello:** Uses $L/B$ and $\phi$ to read $N_q^*$ and $\beta$ from charts.
> - **ENR Formula:** $Q_a = W_H h / [F(S+C)]$; F = 6 (drop), 3 (steam).
> - **Hiley's Formula:** Includes efficiency $\eta$, restitution $n$, pile weight $W_P$.
> - **Pile Load Test:** Failure load at settlement = 10% of pile diameter.
> - **FoS:** 2.5 – 3.0 (static), 1.5 – 2.0 (PLT verified).
> - **Negative Skin Friction:** Develops due to soil settling more than pile; adds **downward load**; identified by **neutral plane** concept.
> - **Group Efficiency ($\eta_g$):** For clay, $\eta_g < 1$ (group < individual sum). For sand, $\eta_g$ can be > 1 due to soil compaction.
> - **Pile types:** End bearing, friction, combined – know examples (e.g., **bored, driven, precast, under-reamed**).
> - **For KTU numericals:** Always show area ($A_b = \pi D^2/4$) and perimeter ($P = \pi D$) calculation steps explicitly.
> - **Conversion:** 1 kN/m² = 1 kPa; 1 MPa = 1000 kPa = 10⁶ N/m². Keep units consistent.
> - **Plaxis/PY Curves:** Not in KTU module, skip for exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Formulae
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Formulae

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dynamic formulae** in geotechnical engineering are a family of empirical and semi-empirical equations used to estimate the **ultimate static bearing capacity of a pile** by correlating it with the **resistance offered during driving** under impact hammer blows. They are derived from the principle of **conservation of energy**, equating the kinetic energy delivered by the hammer to the work done in driving the pile against soil resistance and temporary elastic compressions.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (Module 4 – Pile Foundations), dynamic formulae are categorized under **"Pile Driving and Dynamic Loading"** and are essential for the rapid field estimation of pile load capacity when static load tests are not feasible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Dynamic formulae are impact-based energy balance equations used to predict the static bearing capacity of piles by observing the **set (penetration) per blow** during driving, using the **weight and stroke** of the hammer as known input parameters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are hammering a **long iron nail** into a hard wooden block. The deeper the nail goes, the harder it is to drive. Now, if you knew the **weight of your hammer (W)**, the **height from which you drop it (H)**, and the **distance the nail moves per blow (S)**, could you estimate how much total force the wood is pushing back with?

That "push-back force" is exactly what engineers want to know about a pile buried deep in soil!

- The hammer is a **drop weight** (W) falling through a height (H).
- The energy delivered is $E = W \cdot H$.
- The pile moves only a tiny distance (**set, S**) per blow because soil resists.
- The soil's resistance times the set equals the useful work done.
- Whatever energy is "lost" goes into heat, sound, and temporary squeezing of the pile cap, pile, and soil (called **elastic compression**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Use:** Dynamic formulae are heavily used on construction sites to obtain a **quick, on-the-spot check** of pile capacity during driving, before concrete has been poured or before expensive static load tests can be arranged. They are most reliable for **cohesionless soils (sands and gravels)** and **end-bearing piles driven to refusal on rock**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded Constants & Metrics)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|:------:|:--------|:-------------:|
| W | Weight of the hammer (ram) | kN or N |
| H | Height of fall of the hammer | mm or m |
| S | Final set (penetration per blow) | mm |
| C | Empirical constant (loss factor) | mm |
| $\eta$ | Efficiency of the hammer | dimensionless (0.7 – 0.9) |
| $C_1$ | Elastic compression of cap/packing | mm |
| $C_2$ | Elastic compression of the pile | mm |
| $C_3$ | Elastic compression of the soil (quake) | mm |
| e | Coefficient of restitution | dimensionless (0 – 0.5) |
| $W_p$ | Weight of the pile | kN or N |
| $Q_u$ | Ultimate dynamic resistance | kN |
| $Q_a$ | Allowable load (working load) | kN |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Dynamic Formulae

The derivation of every dynamic formula hinges on one fundamental **energy balance**:

$$ \text{Energy Input} = \text{Useful Work} + \text{Energy Losses} $$

This can be expressed in its most generic form as:

$$ W \cdot H \cdot \eta = Q_u \cdot S + \text{Elastic / Impact Losses} $$

Each formula in the literature differs only in **how it accounts for the elastic/impact losses** and **how it includes the weight of the pile and the coefficient of restitution**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Energy Supplied by the Hammer:** A hammer of weight $W$ falling through height $H$ delivers energy $W \cdot H$. If the hammer is a single-acting or double-acting steam hammer, only a fraction $\eta$ of the rated energy is actually transmitted. Therefore, the **effective input energy** is $W \cdot H \cdot \eta$.

- **Step 2 — Useful Work Done on the Pile:** The pile must overcome the soil resistance $Q_u$ and penetrate a distance $S$ per blow. The useful work is $Q_u \cdot S$.

- **Step 3 — Energy Lost in Elastic Compression:** At the instant of impact, the pile cap, the pile itself, and the soil beneath the pile all undergo **temporary elastic compression**. These deformations absorb energy that does not contribute to permanent penetration. This is quantified using $C_1$, $C_2$, and $C_3$ (collectively called the **quake**).

- **Step 4 — Energy Lost to Impact (Coefficient of Restitution):** Not all momentum is transferred; a portion is **bounced back** by the hammer. The coefficient of restitution $e$ captures this. For perfectly plastic impact (drop hammer on steel cap), $e = 0$; for very elastic impact, $e \to 1$.

- **Step 5 — Solve for $Q_u$:** Rearranging the energy balance gives the dynamic formula. Applying a **Factor of Safety (FS = 6 for ENR, FS = 3 for Hiley)** yields the allowable load $Q_a$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the 2024 scheme, students are explicitly tested on **(a) Engineering News-Record (ENR) formula** and **(b) Hiley's formula** along with the **Significance of Elastic Compression Terms** $C_1$, $C_2$, $C_3$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Factor of Safety | Remarks |
|:-:|:-------------|:---------|:----------------:|:--------|
| 1 | **Engineering News-Record (ENR)** | $Q_u = \dfrac{W \cdot H}{S + C}$ | 6 | $C = 25.4$ mm (drop hammer), $C = 2.54$ mm (steam hammer) |
| 2 | **Hiley's Formula** | $Q_u = \dfrac{W \cdot H \cdot \eta}{S + 0.5(C_1 + C_2 + C_3)} \cdot \dfrac{W + e^2 \cdot W_p}{W + W_p}$ | 3 | Most accurate; includes pile weight and restitution |
| 3 | **Modified Hiley's (Janbu form)** | $Q_u = \dfrac{W \cdot H \cdot \eta}{k \cdot u \cdot s}$ where $u = $ empirical constant | 3 | Used for sandy soils |
| 4 | **Dutch Formula** | $Q_u = \dfrac{W \cdot H}{S + \tfrac{W}{W_p} \cdot C_c}$ | 5 | $C_c$ = temporary compression |
| 5 | **Allowable Load (ENR)** | $Q_a = \dfrac{Q_u}{6} = \dfrac{W \cdot H}{6(S + C)}$ | — | $Q_a$ in same units as $W$ |
| 6 | **Elastic Compression of Pile ($C_2$)** | $C_2 = \dfrac{Q_u \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$ | — | $L$ = pile length, $A$ = area, $E$ = Young's modulus |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Rule for KTU:** When $W$ is in **kN** and $H$, $S$, $C$ are all in the **same units (mm or m)**, then $Q_u$ automatically comes out in the **same unit as W (kN)**. This is the most common dimensional trap in KTU numerical problems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Control on Site:** Engineers verify during driving that the pile achieves the design capacity in real-time.
- **Cost-Effective Screening:** Static load tests cost ₹2–5 lakhs per pile; dynamic formulae are **free** once driving is complete.
- **Determination of Refusal:** If $S \to 0$, the formula signals **refusal** (rock or hard stratum reached).
- **Used in Precast Driven Pile Contracts:** Most IS 2911 Part 1 Section 2 specifications reference ENR or Hiley for proof-driving acceptance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the ENR (Engineering News-Record) Formula

**Assumption 1:** A pile hammer delivers a blow equivalent to a sudden impact.

**Assumption 2:** The pile penetrates a distance $S$ against a constant soil resistance $Q_u$ per blow.

**Assumption 3:** A small empirical constant $C$ (called the "set per blow lost to elastic compression") is added to $S$ to account for unrecoverable deformation and impact losses.

**Step 1 — Energy Input:**

$$ E_{in} = W \cdot H $$

**Step 2 — Useful Work Against Soil:**

$$ E_{useful} = Q_u \cdot S $$

**Step 3 — Empirical Loss Term:** Observing field experiments, ENR engineers found that a **fixed empirical constant $C$** (in inches originally) had to be added to the set $S$ to make the formula match observed static loads.

**Step 4 — Energy Balance:**

$$ W \cdot H = Q_u \cdot (S + C) $$

**Step 5 — Solve for $Q_u$:**

$$ Q_u = \dfrac{W \cdot H}{S + C} $$

**Step 6 — Apply Factor of Safety = 6 (ENR convention):**

$$ Q_a = \dfrac{Q_u}{6} = \dfrac{W \cdot H}{6(S + C)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Value of $C$ (Original ENR Specification):**
> - For **drop hammer** (free fall): $C = 1$ inch $= 25.4$ mm
> - For **single/double-acting steam hammer**: $C = 0.1$ inch $= 2.54$ mm

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Hiley's Formula (Detailed)

Hiley improved the ENR formula by:
1. Including hammer efficiency $\eta$.
2. Separating elastic compressions into $C_1$, $C_2$, $C_3$.
3. Including pile weight $W_p$ and coefficient of restitution $e$.

**Step 1 — Effective Energy Delivered:**

$$ E_{eff} = W \cdot H \cdot \eta $$

**Step 2 — Energy Lost to Elastic Compression:** The compression at impact is recovered elastically. Half of the average compression energy is lost because the pile oscillates back. The total recoverable compression is $C = C_1 + C_2 + C_3$. Energy lost is:

$$ E_{loss} = Q_u \cdot \dfrac{(C_1 + C_2 + C_3)}{2} $$

**Step 3 — Energy to Drive Pile (Permanent Set):**

$$ E_{set} = Q_u \cdot S $$

**Step 4 — Energy Balance (before restitution correction):**

$$ W \cdot H \cdot \eta = Q_u \cdot S + Q_u \cdot \dfrac{(C_1 + C_2 + C_3)}{2} $$

$$ Q_u = \dfrac{W \cdot H \cdot \eta}{S + 0.5(C_1 + C_2 + C_3)} $$

**Step 5 — Effect of Pile Weight and Coefficient of Restitution:** A fraction of the hammer's kinetic energy is reflected by the pile bouncing back. This reflection effect reduces the net energy by the factor:

$$ \dfrac{W + e^2 \cdot W_p}{W + W_p} $$

**Step 6 — Final Hiley's Formula:**

$$ \boxed{\, Q_u = \dfrac{W \cdot H \cdot \eta}{S + 0.5(C_1 + C_2 + C_3)} \cdot \dfrac{W + e^2 \cdot W_p}{W + W_p} \,} $$

**Step 7 — Allowable Load:**

$$ Q_a = \dfrac{Q_u}{3} \quad \text{(FS = 3 for Hiley)} $$

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A precast concrete pile of weight $W_p = 30$ kN and length 12 m is driven by a hammer of weight $W = 40$ kN falling freely from a height $H = 1.0$ m. The final set per blow is $S = 4$ mm. The coefficient of restitution $e = 0.4$ and the total elastic compression $C_1 + C_2 + C_3 = 12$ mm. Hammer efficiency $\eta = 0.8$. Estimate the allowable load using **Hiley's formula**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Substitute values into Hiley's formula:**

$$ Q_u = \dfrac{W \cdot H \cdot \eta}{S + 0.5(C_1 + C_2 + C_3)} \cdot \dfrac{W + e^2 \cdot W_p}{W + W_p} $$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator (energy term):**

$$ W \cdot H \cdot \eta = 40 \times 1.0 \times 0.8 = 32 \text{ kN·m} $$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator (set + half-compression):**

$$ S + 0.5(C_1 + C_2 + C_3) = 4 + 0.5 \times 12 = 4 + 6 = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.010 \text{ m} $$

**Step 4 — Compute first fraction:**

$$ \dfrac{32}{0.010} = 3200 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 5 — Compute restitution correction factor:**

$$ \dfrac{W + e^2 \cdot W_p}{W + W_p} = \dfrac{40 + (0.4)^2 \times 30}{40 + 30} = \dfrac{40 + 4.8}{70} = \dfrac{44.8}{70} = 0.64 $$

**Step 6 — Final Ultimate Capacity:**

$$ Q_u = 3200 \times 0.64 = 2048 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 7 — Allowable Load (FS = 3):**

$$ Q_a = \dfrac{2048}{3} \approx 682.67 \text{ kN} $$

**Final Answer:** $Q_a \approx 683$ kN.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: ENR vs Hiley

| Parameter | ENR Formula | Hiley's Formula |
|:----------|:-----------|:----------------|
| Hammer efficiency $\eta$ | Not included | Included |
| Pile weight $W_p$ | Not included | Included |
| Coefficient of restitution $e$ | Not included | Included |
| Elastic compression | Lump-sum $C$ | Separated $C_1, C_2, C_3$ |
| Factor of Safety | 6 | 3 |
| Accuracy | Low to moderate | High |
| Best suited for | Initial estimates | Final design & QC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Flow Block Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **energy flow architecture** during a single hammer blow on a pile, mapping how the input kinetic energy is partitioned into useful work, recoverable elastic work, and impact losses.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Hammer Drop W times H] --> B[Effective Energy Eeff = W times H times eta]
    B --> C{Energy Partitioning}
    C --> D[Useful Work: Qu times S]
    C --> E[Elastic Compression Loss: Qu times 0.5 times C1+C2+C3]
    C --> F[Restitution Loss Factor: W + e squared times Wp over W + Wp]
    D --> G[Ultimate Pile Capacity Qu]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Factor of Safety]
    H --> I[Allowable Load Qa]
    I --> J[Final Design Verification Output]

    style A fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style C fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    style D fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style F fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,color:#000
    style G fill:#80DEEA,stroke:#004D40,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    style I fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style J fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Process Flow of Hiley's Formula Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Start: Site Data Collection] --> P2[Measure W, H, S, Wp]
    P2 --> P3[Determine eta from Hammer Type]
    P3 --> P4[Compute e from Hammer and Cap Material]
    P4 --> P5[Compute C1, C2, C3 from Field Test]
    P5 --> P6[Substitute into Hiley Equation]
    P6 --> P7[Compute Qu Ultimate Capacity]
    P7 --> P8[Apply FS equals 3]
    P8 --> P9[Output Allowable Load Qa]
    P9 --> P10[Compare with Design Load]
    P10 --> P11{Acceptable?}
    P11 --> P12[Yes: Approve Pile] 
    P11 --> P13[No: Redesign or Retest]

    style P1 fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    style P11 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style P12 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style P13 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Schematic Mapping of Elastic Compression Components

| Component | Location of Compression | Symbol |
|:----------|:------------------------|:------:|
| Cap + Packing | At the top of pile, between hammer and pile | $C_1$ |
| Pile Itself | Along the shaft of the pile | $C_2$ |
| Soil Beneath Pile Tip | At the soil-pile interface at the toe | $C_3$ |

```
        HAMMER  (W, H)
            ↓
       ┌─────────┐
       │ Pile Cap│ ← C1 (cap + packing compresses)
       └────┬────┘
            │
       ┌────┴────┐
       │  PILE   │ ← C2 (pile itself compresses)
       │  (Wp,L) │
       └────┬────┘
            │
       ┌────┴────┐
       │   SOIL  │ ← C3 (soil at tip compresses)
       │  Quake  │
       └─────────┘
            ↓
       Permanent Set S
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "dynamic formula" as applied to pile foundations. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**
A dynamic formula is an empirical equation used to estimate the **ultimate static bearing capacity of a pile** by equating the **kinetic energy of a falling hammer** to the **work done in driving the pile** through a given set per blow, while accounting for energy losses due to elastic compression and impact restitution.

The general energy balance is:

$$ W \cdot H \cdot \eta = Q_u \cdot S + \text{Elastic Losses} $$

Examples: **Engineering News-Record (ENR) formula** and **Hiley's formula**.

---

### Question 2: What is the significance of the coefficient of restitution in Hiley's formula? [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**
The **coefficient of restitution ($e$)** quantifies the **elastic rebound** between the hammer and the pile cap during impact. It represents the ratio of relative velocity of separation to relative velocity of approach after collision.

- For a perfectly plastic impact (e.g., soft wood cap on steel), $e = 0$, meaning full energy transfer.
- For a perfectly elastic impact, $e = 1$, meaning maximum energy bounce-back and minimum transfer to the pile.

In Hiley's formula, $e$ appears squared in the term $\dfrac{W + e^2 \cdot W_p}{W + W_p}$, which **reduces the effective capacity** by accounting for energy that is reflected back rather than driving the pile.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Internal Choice Provided: Choose EITHER Question A OR Question B

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the **Engineering News-Record (ENR) formula** for determining the ultimate bearing capacity of a pile driven by a drop hammer, clearly stating the assumptions and the factor of safety used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A precast concrete pile is driven by a **40 kN hammer** falling from a height of **0.8 m**. The final set per blow is **5 mm**. Using the ENR formula, determine the **allowable load** for:
   (i) Drop hammer
   (ii) Single-acting steam hammer **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of ENR Formula (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Assumption:** The soil resistance $Q_u$ is constant during one blow and the pile behaves as a rigid body undergoing energy transfer.

**Step 2 — Energy Supplied by Hammer:**

$$ E_{in} = W \cdot H $$

**Step 3 — Useful Work in Driving the Pile:**

$$ E_{useful} = Q_u \cdot S $$

**Step 4 — Empirical Loss Term $C$:** To account for unrecoverable deformation, ENR added an empirical constant $C$ to the permanent set $S$.

**Step 5 — Energy Balance:**

$$ W \cdot H = Q_u \cdot (S + C) $$

**Step 6 — Solving for $Q_u$:**

$$ Q_u = \dfrac{W \cdot H}{S + C} $$

**Step 7 — Applying FS = 6:**

$$ Q_a = \dfrac{Q_u}{6} = \dfrac{W \cdot H}{6(S + C)} $$

> $C = 25.4$ mm (drop hammer); $C = 2.54$ mm (steam hammer)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $W = 40$ kN, $H = 0.8$ m $= 800$ mm, $S = 5$ mm.

**(i) For Drop Hammer ($C = 25.4$ mm):**

$$ Q_a = \dfrac{40 \times 0.8}{6(0.005 + 0.0254)} = \dfrac{32}{6 \times 0.0304} = \dfrac{32}{0.1824} \approx 175.44 \text{ kN} $$

**(ii) For Steam Hammer ($C = 2.54$ mm):**

$$ Q_a = \dfrac{40 \times 0.8}{6(0.005 + 0.00254)} = \dfrac{32}{6 \times 0.00754} = \dfrac{32}{0.04524} \approx 707.34 \text{ kN} $$

**Final Answers:**
- Drop hammer: $Q_a \approx 175.4$ kN
- Steam hammer: $Q_a \approx 707.3$ kN

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** $H$ and $S$ must be in the **same units**. KTU students frequently write $H$ in metres and $S$ in mm without converting — leading to massive errors.
> 2. **Forgetting FS = 6 for ENR** and reporting $Q_u$ as $Q_a$ — costs **2 to 3 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing $C$ values:** Writing $C = 1$ (without converting to mm) is a frequent mistake.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the various **components of elastic compression** ($C_1$, $C_2$, $C_3$) considered in Hiley's dynamic formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pile weighing **25 kN** with length **10 m** is driven by a hammer of weight **30 kN** falling from a height of **0.9 m**. The set per blow is **3 mm**, the total elastic compression is **10 mm**, the coefficient of restitution is **0.5**, and the hammer efficiency is **0.85**. Calculate the **allowable load** using Hiley's formula. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Components of Elastic Compression (7 marks):**

The total temporary elastic compression $C = C_1 + C_2 + C_3$ accounts for energy absorbed during impact that is not converted to permanent penetration.

| Term | Location | Cause | Typical Range |
|:----:|:---------|:------|:--------------|
| $C_1$ | Top of pile (helmet, cap, packing) | Cushioning material compresses | 1–5 mm |
| $C_2$ | Along the pile shaft | Elastic shortening of pile material under load | 1–5 mm |
| $C_3$ | At the soil-pile tip interface | Elastic compression of soil beneath the tip (quake) | 3–10 mm |

The energy lost in this elastic compression equals $Q_u \cdot 0.5(C_1 + C_2 + C_3)$ because the average force acts over half the total compression during the rebound.

**Sketch (refer to Section 4.3):** The compression occurs at three distinct locations: cap (top), pile shaft (middle), and soil at tip (bottom).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $W = 30$ kN
- $H = 0.9$ m
- $W_p = 25$ kN
- $S = 3$ mm $= 0.003$ m
