- $C_1 + C_2 + C_3 = 10$ mm $= 0.010$ m
- $e = 0.5$
- $\eta = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Energy Term:**

$$ W \cdot H \cdot \eta = 30 \times 0.9 \times 0.85 = 22.95 \text{ kN·m} $$

**Step 2 — Set + Half Compression:**

$$ S + 0.5(C_1 + C_2 + C_3) = 0.003 + 0.5 \times 0.010 = 0.003 + 0.005 = 0.008 \text{ m} $$

**Step 3 — First Fraction:**

$$ \dfrac{22.95}{0.008} = 2868.75 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 4 — Restitution Factor:**

$$ \dfrac{W + e^2 \cdot W_p}{W + W_p} = \dfrac{30 + (0.5)^2 \times 25}{30 + 25} = \dfrac{30 + 6.25}{55} = \dfrac{36.25}{55} \approx 0.6591 $$

**Step 5 — Ultimate Capacity:**

$$ Q_u = 2868.75 \times 0.6591 \approx 1890.65 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 6 — Allowable Load (FS = 3):**

$$ Q_a = \dfrac{1890.65}{3} \approx 630.22 \text{ kN} $$

**Final Answer:** $Q_a \approx 630$ kN.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Squaring of $e$:** Many students write $e \cdot W_p$ instead of $e^2 \cdot W_p$ — this is a **formula mistake worth 1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting $\eta$:** Omitting the hammer efficiency gives an overestimate of $Q_u$ and loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Mixing FS:** Using FS = 6 (ENR) instead of FS = 3 (Hiley) is a **2-mark error** because each formula has its own convention.
> 4. **Unit conversion of $S$ and $C$ to metres** before dividing into $W \cdot H \cdot \eta$ (which is in kN·m) — missing this leads to wrong $Q_u$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Dynamic Formulae:**

- **Core Principle:** Energy balance — $W \cdot H \cdot \eta = Q_u \cdot S + \text{Losses}$.
- **ENR Formula:** $Q_a = \dfrac{W \cdot H}{6(S + C)}$; very simple; $C = 25.4$ mm (drop) or $2.54$ mm (steam).
- **Hiley's Formula:** Most accurate; includes $\eta$, $W_p$, $e$, and $C_1, C_2, C_3$ separately.
- **Factor of Safety Convention:** ENR uses FS = 6, Hiley uses FS = 3.
- **Units Rule:** $W$ in kN, $H$ in m, $S$ and $C$ in m → $Q_u$ in kN.
- **Coefficient of Restitution $e$:** Always squared in Hiley's formula; typical range 0 to 0.5.
- **Elastic Compression Components:**
   - $C_1$ — cap and packing (top)
   - $C_2$ — pile shaft
   - $C_3$ — soil at pile tip (quake)
- **Total Loss Term:** $Q_u \cdot 0.5(C_1 + C_2 + C_3)$ (half factor due to elastic recovery).
- **Best Soil Suitability:** Dynamic formulae are most reliable for **cohesionless soils** and **end-bearing piles** driven to hard strata.
- **Limitations:** Not reliable for **cohesive soils** (clays), **bored piles**, or **friction piles**; cannot capture long-term consolidation effects.
- **KTU Frequently Asked:** Numerical on Hiley's formula + derivation of ENR + definition of $e$ and $C_1, C_2, C_3$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Formulae
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Static Formulae # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of Soil Mechanics and Foundation Engineering, **Static Formulae** refer to the classical theoretical methods (also called *Static Methods* or *Plasticity-based methods*) used to determine the **ultimate bearing capacity (q_u)** of soil beneath a shallow foundation. These formulae are derived from the theory of plasticity assuming a general shear failure mechanism in homogeneous, semi-infinite soil mass. The most widely used static formulae include those proposed by: - **Terzaghi (1943)** – pioneer of bearing capacity theory - **Meyerhof (1963)** – incorporated shape, depth, and inclination factors - **Hansen (1970)** – extended for various base inclinations and ground slopes - **Vesic (1973)** – refined the N_γ factor - **IS 6403:1981 (Reaffirmed 2002)** – Indian Standard Code method The general static bearing capacity equation is expressed as: $$q_u = c'N_c \, s_c \, d_c \, i_c + qN_q \, s_q \, d_q \, i_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma \, s_\gamma \, d_\gamma \, i_\gamma$$ Where every term is a **load contribution from a distinct failure zone** in the soil. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Static formulae are a **direct 14-mark topic** in KTU ESE. The question typically asks to compute the ultimate/allowable bearing capacity of a strip/square/circular footing using **Terzaghi's** or **IS Code (IS 6403)** method. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you place a **heavy iron table** on soft butter. The butter directly below the table leg will be pushed down, and a wedge-shaped block of butter (an **active wedge**) will sink with the table. As it sinks, the surrounding butter is **displaced sideways and pushed up** on both sides, forming inclined slip surfaces that resemble a **logarithmic spiral** ending in the free ground surface. This three-zone failure mechanism consists of: - **Zone I (Active Wedge):** Triangular zone directly below the footing that moves downward with the foundation. - **Zone II (Radial Shear Zone):** Curved transition zone where soil undergoes radial shearing. - **Zone III (Passive Wedge):** Triangular zone where soil is heaved upward. > [!NOTE] > The bearing capacity factors (N_c, N_q, N_γ) are dimensionless numbers that quantify the soil's resistance to each of these three failure modes. **Larger friction angle φ → larger N values → higher load capacity.** ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $c'$ | Effective Cohesion of Soil | **kN/m²** or **kPa** | | $\phi'$ | Effective Angle of Internal Friction | **degrees (°)** | | $\gamma$ | Unit Weight of Soil | **kN/m³** | | $B$ | Width / Diameter of Footing | **m** | | $L$ | Length of Footing | **m** | | $D_f$ | Depth of Foundation | **m** | | $q$ | Surcharge (Overburden Pressure) = $\gamma D_f$ | **kN/m²** | | $q_u$ | Ultimate Bearing Capacity | **kN/m²** | | $q_{net,u}$ | Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity = $q_u - \gamma D_f$ | **kN/m²** | | $q_a$ | Allowable / Safe Bearing Capacity = $q_u / F$ | **kN/m²** | | $F$ / $FS$ | Factor of Safety (typically **3.0** for q_u; **2.5 to 3.0** for q_net) | Dimensionless | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Equation (The Pioneer Formulation) Terzaghi (1943) was the first to present a comprehensive theoretical theory for evaluating the ultimate bearing capacity for rough shallow foundations. He assumed: - The footing base is **rough** (no slip between base and soil). - The soil is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and **semi-infinite**. - The footing is at a **shallow depth** ($D_f \leq B$). - **General Shear Failure** occurs in soil. ### Terzaghi's Form (3 Cases) **Case 1: Strip Footing (B/L → 0)** $$q_u = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$$ **Case 2: Square Footing (B = L)** $$q_u = 1.3 \, c N_c + q N_q + 0.4 \, \gamma B N_\gamma$$ **Case 3: Circular Footing (Diameter = B)** $$q_u = 1.3 \, c N_c + q N_q + 0.3 \, \gamma B N_\gamma$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why the shape factors 1.3 and 0.4 / 0.3?** Terzaghi treated the inclined sides of the failure wedge under square/circular footings separately. Square/circular footings are **stronger** in cohesion (×1.3) but **weaker** in the surcharge + weight term (×0.4 or ×0.3) compared to a strip footing. ### 2.2 Meyerhof's Bearing Capacity Equation (1963) Meyerhof extended Terzaghi's theory by including **shape, depth, and inclination** factors explicitly. His general equation is identical to the IS Code form and is valid for both square and rectangular footings. $$q_u = c' N_c \, s_c \, d_c \, i_c + q N_q \, s_q \, d_q \, i_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma \, s_\gamma \, d_\gamma \, i_\gamma$$ ### 2.3 Hansen's Bearing Capacity Equation (1970) Hansen further refined Meyerhof's factors and added factors for **ground slope (g)** and **base tilt (t)**. KTU problems typically use the **Meyerhof/Hansen** factor values. ### 2.4 IS Code Method (IS 6403:1981) The Indian Standard recommends the following form for **c-φ soils**: $$q_u = c N_c \, s_c \, d_c + q(N_q - 1) s_q \, d_q + 0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma \, s_\gamma \, d_\gamma$$ Note the difference: the IS Code uses $(N_q - 1)$ instead of $N_q$ for the surcharge term because the overburden on the **sides** of the footing must be subtracted from the total load. This correction makes $q_{net,u}$ directly obtainable from the same equation by simply omitting the q-term. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet ### A. Bearing Capacity Factors (Meyerhof / Hansen / Vesic) | Factor | Formula | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $N_q$ | $e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | Universal | | $N_c$ | $(N_q - 1) \cot\phi$ | Universal (for $\phi > 0$) | | $N_c$ (φ = 0) | $5.14$ | Skempton's value for pure clay | | $N_\gamma$ (Meyerhof) | $(N_q - 1) \tan(1.4\phi)$ | Older | | $N_\gamma$ (Hansen) | $1.5(N_q - 1)\tan\phi$ | Recommended for KTU | | $N_\gamma$ (Vesic) | $2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi$ | Common alternative | | $N_\gamma$ (IS Code 6403) | $2.0(N_q - 1)\tan\phi$ | Used in IS tables | ### B. Shape Factors (Hansen / Meyerhof) | Factor | For $\phi = 0$ | For $\phi > 0$ | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $s_c$ | $1 + \dfrac{B}{L}\dfrac{N_q}{N_c}$ | $1 + \dfrac{B}{L}\dfrac{N_q}{N_c}$ | | $s_q$ | $1.0$ | $1 + \dfrac{B}{L}\tan\phi$ | | $s_\gamma$ | $0.6$ | $1 - 0.4\dfrac{B}{L}$ | For **square footing** B/L = 1, for **strip footing** B/L = 0. ### C. Depth Factors (Hansen) **For $D_f/B \leq 1$:** $$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \dfrac{D_f}{B}, \quad d_q = 1 + 2 \tan\phi (1 - \sin\phi)^2 \dfrac{D_f}{B}, \quad d_\gamma = 1.0$$ **For $D_f/B > 1$:** $$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D_f}{B}\right), \quad d_q = 1 + 2 \tan\phi (1 - \sin\phi)^2 \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D_f}{B}\right)$$ ### D. Inclination Factors (Hansen, for load inclined at angle α with vertical) $$i_c = i_q = \left(1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{90°}\right)^2, \quad i_\gamma = \left(1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\phi}\right)^2 \; (\text{for } \phi > 0)$$ ### E. Special Cases (Important for KTU) | Soil Type | Simplified Equation | | :--- | :--- | | **Pure Clay** (φ = 0, undrained) | $q_u = 5.14 c + \gamma D_f$ (Strip) | | **Pure Clay** (φ = 0, with Skempton) | $q_u = c N_c \, s_c \, d_c + \gamma D_f$ | | **Pure Sand** (c = 0) | $q_u = q(N_q - 1)s_q d_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma$ | | **Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity** | $q_{net,u} = q_u - \gamma D_f$ | | **Allowable Bearing Capacity** | $q_a = \dfrac{q_{net,u}}{F} + \gamma D_f$ or $q_a = \dfrac{q_u}{F}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Convention Note:** The most common KTU formulation uses **Meyerhof's N factors** with **Hansen's shape, depth, and inclination factors** (also adopted by IS 6403). Always specify which N_γ formula is being used in your answer. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Shallow Foundation Design** – Used in design of **isolated column footings, combined footings, and raft foundations** for buildings, bridges, and towers. - **Settlement Considerations** – Static formulae give the ultimate load; safe bearing capacity is then calculated by applying a **Factor of Safety (FS = 2.5 to 3.0)** to limit settlement to permissible values. - **Failure Investigation** – Used in forensic analysis of foundation failures such as the **Transcona Silo (1913) failure** which failed at 3.5× design load due to underestimation of $\phi$. - **Geotechnical Software Validation** – These classical equations form the **core algorithms** of software like **PLAXIS, GeoStudio, and SAFE**. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of N_q and N_c Factors (Meyerhof's Approach) The classical derivation of bearing capacity factors arises from the **theory of plasticity** applied to a footing on weightless soil (c, φ soil, no surcharge) and a footing on a purely frictional soil (φ soil, c = 0). ### Derivation of N_q (Surcharge Contribution) Consider a smooth strip footing of width B at depth $D_f$ on a frictionless soil with cohesion $c = 0$ and friction angle $\phi$. The surcharge is $q = \gamma D_f$. The active wedge below the footing forms an angle of **45° + φ/2** with the horizontal. The pressure acting on the inclined plane at the base of the active wedge equals the **passive earth pressure** developed by the surcharge q. The Rankine passive earth pressure coefficient is: $$K_p = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) = \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}$$ Equating the normal stress on the inclined failure plane: $$\sigma_n = K_p \cdot q$$ The vertical component of this normal stress acting on the footing must support the load: $$q_u = \sigma_n \cdot \sec^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) \cdot \text{(geometric correction)}$$ After rigorous analysis using the principle of virtual work on the failure wedge, the result is: $$N_q = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) \cdot e^{\pi \tan\phi} = K_p \cdot e^{\pi \tan\phi}$$ ### Step-by-Step Algebraic Derivation **Step 1: Express K_p in terms of trigonometric identities** $$K_p = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$$ **Step 2: Use the identity $K_p = \sec^2(45° + \phi/2) = \dfrac{1}{\cos^2(45° + \phi/2)}$** $$N_q = \dfrac{1}{\cos^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)} \cdot e^{\pi \tan\phi}$$ **Step 3: Compute N_q for various values of φ** (Sample table) | φ (°) | $N_q$ | $N_c$ | $N_\gamma$ (Hansen) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 0 | 1.00 | 5.14 | 0.00 | | 10 | 2.47 | 8.34 | 1.22 | | 20 | 6.40 | 14.83 | 5.39 | | 30 | 18.40 | 30.14 | 18.09 | | 40 | 64.20 | 75.32 | 79.54 | | 45 | 134.87 | 133.87 | 178.50 | ### Derivation of N_c (Cohesion Contribution) For a footing on purely cohesive soil (φ = 0) with **no surcharge** (q = 0), the analysis reduces to the classical Prandtl problem. Prandtl's exact solution for a smooth punch indenting a weightless, purely cohesive medium gives: $$q_u = (\pi + 2) \, c = 5.14 \, c$$ Hence, the value of **N_c at φ = 0 is 5.14**. For a general φ > 0, the cohesion term contribution is derived by superposition: $$N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi$$ This is obtained by differentiating $N_q$ with respect to the surcharge parameter, using the relationship between cohesive and frictional components of shear strength. **Verification at φ = 0:** $$\lim_{\phi \to 0} (N_q - 1) \cot\phi = \lim_{\phi \to 0} (e^{\pi \tan\phi} - 1) \cot\phi$$ Applying L'Hôpital's rule: $$= \lim_{\phi \to 0} \pi \sec^2\phi \cdot e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{-\csc^2\phi} = \pi \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = \pi \approx 3.14$$ Total N_c = π + 2 = **5.14** ✓ ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of N_γ (Unit Weight Contribution) N_γ cannot be derived by superposition like N_c and N_q because the unit weight term **interacts non-linearly** with the failure geometry. The classical approaches yield different expressions. ### Hansen's Approach (Recommended for KTU) Hansen (1970) assumed the failure surface beneath a rough strip footing consists of: - A **triangular active wedge** at angle **ψ** with the horizontal, - A **log-spiral** radial shear zone, - A **passive Rankine wedge** at angle **45° – φ/2**. By integrating the normal and shear stresses along the log-spiral arc and applying limit equilibrium, the result is: $$N_\gamma = 1.5(N_q - 1)\tan\phi$$ ### Vesic's Approach Vesic (1973) argued that the log-spiral should extend fully from the base of the footing to the ground surface, giving: $$N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi$$ ### Meyerhof's Approach (Older) Meyerhof suggested an empirical expression based on model tests: $$N_\gamma = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** For the KTU ESE, when not specified, use **Hansen's N_γ = 1.5(N_q − 1) tan φ** as it is the most accepted conservative value. ## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A square footing of size **2.5 m × 2.5 m** is placed at depth **1.5 m** in a soil with **c' = 10 kN/m²**, **φ' = 30°**, and **γ = 18 kN/m³**. Compute the ultimate and safe bearing capacity using **IS 6403** with **FS = 3**. ### Solution **Step 1: Compute the bearing capacity factors** $$N_q = e^{\pi \tan 30°} \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{30°}{2}\right)$$ $$\tan 30° = 0.5774, \quad \pi \tan 30° = 1.8138, \quad e^{1.8138} = 6.133$$ $$\tan(60°) = 1.732, \quad \tan^2(60°) = 3.0$$ $$N_q = 6.133 \times 3.0 = 18.40$$ $$N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi = (18.40 - 1) \cot 30° = 17.40 \times 1.732 = 30.14$$ $$N_\gamma = 1.5(N_q - 1)\tan\phi = 1.5 \times 17.40 \times 0.5774 = 15.07$$ **Step 2: Compute shape factors** (for square, B/L = 1) $$s_c = 1 + \dfrac{B}{L} \cdot \dfrac{N_q}{N_c} = 1 + 1 \times \dfrac{18.40}{30.14} = 1.611$$ $$s_q = 1 + \dfrac{B}{L}\tan\phi = 1 + 1 \times 0.5774 = 1.577$$ $$s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4 \dfrac{B}{L} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$$ **Step 3: Compute depth factors** ($D_f/B = 1.5/2.5 = 0.6 \leq 1$) $$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \times 0.6 = 1.24$$ $$d_q = 1 + 2 \tan 30° (1 - \sin 30°)^2 \times 0.6 = 1 + 2 \times 0.5774 \times 0.25 \times 0.6 = 1.173$$ $$d_\gamma = 1.0$$ **Step 4: Compute the ultimate bearing capacity** (IS 6403) $$q_u = c N_c s_c d_c + q(N_q - 1) s_q d_q + 0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma$$ $$q = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ Cohesion term: $$= 10 \times 30.14 \times 1.611 \times 1.24 = 602.3 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ Surcharge term: $$= 27 \times 17.40 \times 1.577 \times 1.173 = 869.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ Unit weight term: $$= 0.5 \times 2.5 \times 18 \times 15.07 \times 0.6 \times 1.0 = 203.4 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$q_u = 602.3 + 869.0 + 203.4 = 1674.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 5: Compute safe bearing capacity** $$q_{net,u} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1674.7 - 27 = 1647.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$q_{safe,net} = \dfrac{q_{net,u}}{F} = \dfrac{1647.7}{3} = 549.2 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$q_{safe,total} = q_{safe,net} + \gamma D_f = 549.2 + 27 = 576.2 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ > [!NOTE] > **Observations from the calculation:** > - The **surcharge term dominates** in this problem (869 kN/m²) because $\phi$ is high. > - The **cohesion term** is moderate (602 kN/m²). > - The **unit weight term** is the smallest (203 kN/m²) due to the small footing width and $s_\gamma = 0.6$. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Terzaghi's Three-Zone Failure Mechanism (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB Load["Vertical Load Q
from Superstructure"] Footing["Rough Rigid Footing
Width B, Depth Df"] Zone1["ZONE I - Active Wedge
Triangular block of soil
Moves DOWNWARD with footing
Inclined at 45 + phi by 2 with horizontal"] Zone2["ZONE II - Radial Shear Zone
Curved log-spiral surface
Soil undergoes radial shearing
Centered at edge of footing base"] Zone3["ZONE III - Passive Wedge
Triangular heave zone
Soil pushed UPWARD
Inclined at 45 minus phi by 2 with horizontal"] Ground["Original Ground Surface"] Failure["General Shear Failure Surface
Continuous log-spiral from base
to ground surface"] Load --> Footing Footing --> Zone1 Zone1 --> Zone2 Zone2 --> Zone3 Zone2 --> Failure Zone3 --> Ground ``` ## 4.2 Generalized Static Bearing Capacity Factor Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Inputs["GEOMETRIC AND SOIL INPUTS"] A1["Cohesion c
kN per m2"] A2["Friction Angle phi
degrees"] A3["Unit Weight gamma
kN per m3"] A4["Footing Width B
m"] A5["Footing Length L
m"] A6["Depth Df
m"] end subgraph Factors["BEARING CAPACITY FACTORS"] B1["Nq equals exp of pi tan phi times tan squared 45 plus phi by 2"] B2["Nc equals Nq minus 1 times cot phi"] B3["Ngamma equals 1.5 times Nq minus 1 times tan phi"] end subgraph Modifiers["EMPIRICAL MODIFIERS"] C1["Shape Factors sc sq sgamma"] C2["Depth Factors dc dq dgamma"] C3["Inclination Factors ic iq igamma"] end subgraph Output["FINAL OUTPUT"] D1["qu = cNc sc dc ic + qNq sq dq iq + 0.5 gamma B Ngamma sgamma dgamma igamma"] D2["qsafe = qu by F plus gamma Df"] end A1 --> B2 A2 --> B1 A2 --> B2 A2 --> B3 B1 --> B2 B1 --> B3 A1 --> D1 A3 --> D1 A4 --> D1 A6 --> D1 A4 --> C1 A5 --> C1 A4 --> C2 A6 --> C2 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C1 A2 --> C1 A2 --> C2 A2 --> C3 C1 --> D1 C2 --> D1 C3 --> D1 D1 --> D2 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flow for Choosing the Correct Formula ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Given a Foundation Problem"] Q1{"Is the Footing
Shallow?"} Q2{"Soil is
c-phi or
pure c or
pure phi?"} Q3{"Footing Shape:
Strip, Square,
Rectangular, or
Circular?"} Path1["Use Static Formulae
Terzaghi or Meyerhof"] Path2["Use Deep Foundation
Pile or Well formulae"] PureC["For pure clay phi=0:
qu = 5.14c + gamma Df"] PurePhi["For pure sand c=0:
qu = qNqsqdq + 0.5BNgammasgammadgamma"] Cphi["General c-phi equation
with Nc, Nq, Ngamma"] Strip["Strip: B by L = 0
Use Terzaghi form"] Square["Square: B by L = 1
Use shape factors"] Rect["Rectangular: B by L
Use shape factors"] Result["Compute qu, qsafe"] Start --> Q1 Q1 -->|Yes, Df less than B| Path1 Q1 -->|No, deep| Path2 Path1 --> Q2 Q2 -->|phi=0| PureC Q2 -->|c=0| PurePhi Q2 -->|c-phi both| Cphi Cphi --> Q3 PureC --> Q3 PurePhi --> Q3 Q3 -->|Strip| Strip Q3 -->|Square| Square Q3 -->|Rectangular| Rect Strip --> Result Square --> Result Rect --> Result ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The Mermaid diagrams above represent the **logical flow** of the static bearing capacity calculation. The first diagram shows the **physical failure mechanism** in the soil, the second shows the **mathematical architecture** of the formula, and the third shows the **decision matrix** for selecting the correct formula in different soil conditions. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **Q: Distinguish between General Shear Failure and Local Shear Failure in soils. Which failure mode is assumed in the derivation of Terzaghi's bearing capacity equation?** **Model Answer:** **General Shear Failure (GSF):** This is a clearly defined failure mode in dense sand or stiff clay where the failure surfaces reach the ground surface. Distinct heave appears on both sides of the footing. The peak failure load is well-defined and occurs at a relatively small strain. **Local Shear Failure (LSF):** This occurs in loose sand or soft clay where the failure surfaces do NOT reach the ground surface. Significant settlement occurs before failure, and the failure load is not well-defined. The vertical boundaries of the failure zone are not fully developed. | Feature | General Shear Failure | Local Shear Failure | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Soil Type | Dense sand, stiff clay | Loose sand, soft clay | | Failure Surface | Reaches ground surface | Does NOT reach ground surface | | Heave | Sudden and distinct | Gradual and less defined | | Failure Load | Clearly defined peak | Not well defined | **Terzaghi's bearing capacity equation assumes General Shear Failure (GSF)**. For Local Shear Failure, Terzaghi modified the friction angle as $\phi^* = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\tan\phi\right)$ and the cohesion as $c^* = \dfrac{2}{3}c$. ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **Q: Define the three bearing capacity factors $N_c$, $N_q$, and $N_\gamma$. State the formulae for $N_c$ and $N_q$ as per Meyerhof.** **Model Answer:** The bearing capacity factors are **dimensionless coefficients** that depend only on the angle of internal friction $\phi$ and quantify the resistance of the soil to the three failure modes: - **$N_c$ (Cohesion factor):** Represents the contribution of **cohesive strength** of the soil to the bearing capacity. Numerically, $N_c$ is the ratio of bearing capacity of a purely cohesive soil to its undrained shear strength. - **$N_q$ (Surcharge factor):** Represents the contribution of **overburden pressure / surcharge** at the level of the footing base. It is the ratio of bearing capacity due to surcharge alone to the surcharge. - **$N_\gamma$ (Unit weight factor):** Represents the contribution of the **weight of soil** in the failure zone to the bearing capacity. It is the ratio of bearing capacity due to soil weight alone to a stress derived from $\gamma B/2$. **Meyerhof's Formulae:** $$N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$$ $$N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi$$ ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Forgetting to subtract surcharge in the IS Code method** – the term is $q(N_q - 1)$, NOT $q N_q$. This single sign error costs **3-4 marks** out of 14. > - **Using the wrong N_γ formula** – Hansen, Meyerhof, and Vesic give **different numerical values**. Always state which one is being used. If not specified, use Hansen's $N_\gamma = 1.5(N_q - 1) \tan\phi$. > - **Not applying depth factors** when $D_f \geq B$ – KTU often sets $D_f = B$ to test this. > - **Unit inconsistency** – $D_f$, $B$, and $L$ must all be in **metres** and γ in **kN/m³**. > - **Confusing B/L with L/B** – shape factors use the **width-to-length ratio (B/L)**, never the inverse. > - **Forgetting to compute $q_{net,u}$** before applying Factor of Safety – the safe bearing capacity formula requires net ultimate capacity. --- ### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern] **(a) A rectangular footing of size 2 m × 3 m is to be placed at a depth of 1.5 m in a soil with cohesion c' = 20 kN/m², angle of internal friction φ' = 25°, and unit weight γ = 18 kN/m³. Compute the ultimate and safe bearing capacity using the IS Code (IS 6403) method, assuming general shear failure. Take Factor of Safety = 3. (7 marks)** **(b) Discuss the assumptions of Terzaghi's bearing capacity theory. How does Meyerhof's theory improve upon Terzaghi's? (7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Given:** B = 2 m, L = 3 m, $D_f = 1.5$ m, $c' = 20$ kN/m², $\phi' = 25°$, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, FS = 3. **Step 1: Compute bearing capacity factors (Hansen)** $$\tan 25° = 0.4663, \quad \pi \tan 25° = 1.4650, \quad e^{1.4650} = 4.328$$ $$\tan^2(45° + 12.5°) = \tan^2(57.5°) = (1.5697)^2 = 2.464$$ $$N_q = 4.328 \times 2.464 = 10.66$$ $$N_c = (10.66 - 1)\cot 25° = 9.66 \times 2.1445 = 20.72$$ $$N_\gamma = 1.5 \times 9.66 \times 0.4663 = 6.756$$ **Step 2: Compute shape factors** (B/L = 2/3 = 0.667) $$s_c = 1 + \dfrac{B}{L}\dfrac{N_q}{N_c} = 1 + 0.667 \times \dfrac{10.66}{20.72} = 1 + 0.343 = 1.343$$ $$s_q = 1 + \dfrac{B}{L}\tan\phi = 1 + 0.667 \times 0.4663 = 1.311$$ $$s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4 \dfrac{B}{L} = 1 - 0.4 \times 0.667 = 1 - 0.267 = 0.733$$ **Step 3: Compute depth factors** ($D_f/B = 1.5/2 = 0.75 \leq 1$) $$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \times 0.75 = 1.30$$ $$d_q = 1 + 2 \tan 25° (1 - \sin 25°)^2 \times 0.75$$ $$= 1 + 2 \times 0.4663 \times (1 - 0.4226)^2 \times 0.75$$ $$= 1 + 2 \times 0.4663 \times (0.5774)^2 \times 0.75$$ $$= 1 + 2 \times 0.4663 \times 0.3334 \times 0.75 = 1 + 0.233 = 1.233$$ $$d_\gamma = 1.0$$ **Step 4: Compute ultimate bearing capacity (IS Code)** $$q = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Cohesion term:** $c N_c s_c d_c = 20 \times 20.72 \times 1.343 \times 1.30 = 723.4$ kN/m² **Surcharge term:** $q(N_q - 1) s_q d_q = 27 \times 9.66 \times 1.311 \times 1.233 = 420.0$ kN/m² **Unit weight term:** $0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma = 0.5 \times 2 \times 18 \times 6.756 \times 0.733 \times 1.0 = 89.1$ kN/m² $$q_u = 723.4 + 420.0 + 89.1 = 1232.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 5: Compute safe bearing capacity** $$q_{net,u} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1232.5 - 27 = 1205.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$q_{safe,net} = \dfrac{q_{net,u}}{FS} = \dfrac{1205.5}{3} = 401.83 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$q_{safe,total} = q_{safe,net} + \gamma D_f = 401.83 + 27 = \mathbf{428.83 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$ #### Model Solution to (b): **Terzaghi's Assumptions:** 1. The soil is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and **semi-infinite**. 2. The footing is **shallow** ($D_f \leq B$) with a **rough base**. 3. The soil above the footing base and the surcharge can be replaced by an **equivalent surcharge** $q = \gamma D_f$. 4. **General shear failure** occurs in soil. 5. The **shear strength of soil** is governed by the **Mohr-Coulomb criterion**: $\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi$. 6. The failure surface consists of: - Curved portion in the radial shear zone - Straight portion in the active and passive wedges 7. The active wedge angle is **45° + φ/2** with horizontal (for the cohesionless soil assumption). 8. **No heave at the ground surface is considered** in the cohesionless soil model (Terzaghi's original). **Meyerhof's Improvements over Terzaghi:** | Aspect | Terzaghi (1943) | Meyerhof (1963) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Shape factors | Only for strip, square, circular | **General B/L** applicable to all shapes | | Depth factors | NOT considered (shallow footings only) | **Explicitly included** ($d_c, d_q, d_\gamma$) | | Inclination factors | NOT considered | **Included** for inclined loads ($i_c, i_q, i_\gamma$) | | Failure surface | Simplified to two straight wedges | **Log-spiral surface** in radial shear zone | | Footing depth | Restricted to shallow ($D_f \leq B$) | Valid for **both shallow and deep** | | Shear zone | Fitted by a curved surface (empirical) | Mathematical log-spiral (analytical) | | Validity | Less general | **More general and rigorous** | | Overburden | Replaced by surcharge $q = \gamma D_f$ | Treated as a **separate term** with depth factors | | Base roughness | Only rough base | **Both smooth and rough** base handled | **Conclusion:** Meyerhof's theory represents a more rigorous and comprehensive treatment of bearing capacity, accounting for realistic footings with depth, inclined loads, and various shapes. It forms the basis of modern codes like **IS 6403** and is therefore more widely used in engineering practice. --- ### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern] **(a) A strip footing of width 2.5 m rests at a depth of 1.2 m in a soil with c = 15 kN/m², φ = 20°, and γ = 17.5 kN/m³. Using Terzaghi's equation, determine the ultimate bearing capacity. Also, compute the safe bearing capacity with FS = 3. (7 marks)** **(b) Explain the concept of net and gross bearing capacity. A footing is subjected to an inclined load at an angle α with the vertical. How does the inclination affect the bearing capacity? (7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Given:** B = 2.5 m, $D_f = 1.2$ m, c = 15 kN/m², φ = 20°, γ = 17.5 kN/m³, FS = 3, **Strip footing**. **Step 1: Compute Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors** (for φ = 20°) From standard tables: $N_c = 17.69$, $N_q = 7.44$, $N_\gamma = 5.00$ (Terzaghi's value) For φ = 20°: - $N_q = e^{\pi \tan 20°} \tan^2(45° + 10°) = e^{1.145} \times \tan^2(55°) = 3.142 \times 2.040 = 6.41$ (slight variation by source) Using **Terzaghi's chart values** for **φ = 20°**: $N_c = 17.69$, $N_q = 7.44$, $N_\gamma = 5.00$ (commonly used in KTU textbooks). **Step 2: Compute surcharge** $$q = \gamma D_f = 17.5 \times 1.2 = 21 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 3: Compute ultimate bearing capacity** (Terzaghi's strip footing equation) $$q_u = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$$ $$q_u = (15 \times 17.69) + (21 \times 7.44) + (0.5 \times 17.5 \times 2.5 \times 5.00)$$ $$q_u = 265.35 + 156.24 + 109.38 = \mathbf{530.97 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$ **Step 4: Compute safe bearing capacity** $$q_{net,u} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 530.97 - 21 = 509.97 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$q_{safe,net} = \dfrac{q_{net,u}}{FS} = \dfrac{509.97}{3} = 169.99 \approx \mathbf{170 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$ $$q_{safe,total} = q_{safe,net} + \gamma D_f = 170 + 21 = \mathbf{191 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$ #### Model Solution to (b): **Gross vs. Net Bearing Capacity:** **Gross Ultimate Bearing Capacity ($q_u$):** The **total ultimate pressure** that the soil can support at the base of the footing, including the contribution of the overburden pressure and surcharge. It is the **maximum gross pressure** the soil can sustain before failure. $$q_u = c'N_c s_c d_c i_c + q N_q s_q d_q i_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma i_\gamma$$ **Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity ($q_{net,u}$):** The **excess pressure** over and above the existing overburden pressure $q = \gamma D_f$ at the footing level. It represents the **net additional load** that the soil can carry. $$q_{net,u} = q_u - \gamma D_f$$ **Significance:** When a footing is constructed, the soil removed is replaced by the footing and the load. The net additional stress on the soil below the footing is $q_{applied} - \gamma D_f$. Hence, for settlement and failure analysis, we use the **net** bearing capacity, which gives a more accurate picture of the **excess** stress imposed on the soil. **Safe Bearing Capacity from Net Ultimate:** $$q_{safe,net} = \dfrac{q_{net,u}}{F} \quad \text{and} \quad q_{safe,gross} = q_{safe,net} + \gamma D_f$$ **Effect of Inclined Load on Bearing Capacity:** When a footing is subjected to an **inclined load** at an angle α with the vertical (typical for retaining wall footings, bridge abutments, and tower footings subjected to wind/seismic loads), the **vertical component** contributes to bearing failure, while the **horizontal component** causes sliding failure. Both must be checked. **Reduction via Inclination Factors (Hansen):** $$i_c = i_q = \left(1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{90°}\right)^2, \quad i_\gamma = \left(1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\phi}\right)^2 \; (\text{for } \phi > 0)$$ **Observations:** 1. As **α increases** (more inclined load), all inclination factors **decrease**, hence the bearing capacity **decreases**. 2. For **vertical load** (α = 0), all inclination factors = 1, and the equation reduces to the standard form. 3. For **φ = 0** (pure clay), inclination factors for the unit weight term $i_\gamma$ = 0, but the cohesion term still contributes. **Practical Implications:** - For retaining walls, the load from earth pressure is inclined. - For bridge abutments, the load includes both vertical (dead load) and horizontal (braking force, wind) components. - Inclined loads reduce the safe bearing capacity by **20% to 50%** depending on the angle and soil type. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rapid Revision Checklist – Static Formulae** - [x] The **general bearing capacity equation** has THREE terms: cohesion term, surcharge term, and unit weight term. - [x] **N_q = e^(π tan φ) × tan²(45° + φ/2)** is the universal formula. - [x] **N_c = (N_q − 1) cot φ** is derived by superposition. - [x] **N_γ** has three common forms: **Hansen (1.5(N_q − 1) tan φ)**, **Meyerhof ((N_q − 1) tan(1.4φ))**, and **Vesic (2(N_q + 1) tan φ)**. State the one used. - [x] **For φ = 0** (pure clay): $N_c = 5.14$, $N_q = 1.0$, $N_\gamma = 0.0$. - [x] **Shape factors** depend on B/L; for square (B/L = 1): $s_c = 1 + N_q/N_c$, $s_q = 1 + \tan\phi$, $s_\gamma = 0.6$. - [x] **Depth factors** apply only if $D_f \geq B$; for $D_f/B \leq 1$: $d_c = 1 + 0.4(D_f/B)$, $d_q = 1 + 2\tan\phi(1 − \sin\phi)^2 (D_f/B)$. - [x] **IS Code (IS 6403) surcharge term** uses **$(N_q - 1)$**, NOT $N_q$ — this is the most common mistake. - [x] **Net ultimate bearing capacity** = $q_u - \gamma D_f$. - [x] **Allowable bearing capacity** = $q_{net,u} / F + \gamma D_f$ (factor of safety typically **3**). - [x] **Terzaghi's equations** are special cases without shape, depth, and inclination factors. - [x] **Local shear failure** uses modified strength parameters: $c^* = 2c/3$ and $\phi^* = \tan^{-1}(2 \tan\phi / 3)$. - [x] **Skempton's formula** for clay at the surface: $N_c = 5.0$ (D/B = 0), $N_c = 6.0$ (D/B ≥ 2.5). - [x] **Always state assumptions** explicitly: general shear vs. local shear, which N_γ formula, which method (Terzaghi/IS Code). - [x] **Unit consistency** is critical: keep $B, L, D_f$ in **m**, γ in **kN/m³**, and c in **kN/m²**.

topics.title: Pile Load Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Pile Load Tests # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Pile Load Test? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (IS 2911 Part 4 – 1985):** > A *Pile Load Test* is a full-scale, in-situ field test conducted on an actual pile installed at the proposed construction site to determine its **axial load-carrying capacity** (vertical compressive), **settlement behaviour** under working load, and to validate the design assumptions made from sub-soil investigation. It is the **most reliable method** to assess the in-situ performance of a pile, because it directly measures the response of the real pile embedded in the actual ground strata — without depending on empirical correlations or soil parameters. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > The test is *destructive* (it loads the pile to failure or near-failure), hence it is generally performed on **test piles** (also called *preliminary piles*), and the results are extrapolated to *working piles* of the same installation in similar soil conditions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Stress Test" for a Pillar Imagine you are building a heavy marble temple. Before placing the dome, you want to be absolutely sure that one of the **stone pillars** standing in the soil will not sink or crack under the weight. You cannot trust calculations alone because the soil is heterogeneous and unpredictable. So, you take an **identical pillar**, place it next to the actual site, and then **gradually pile bags of sand on top** of it — recording how much it sinks at each load level — until it either fails (sinks rapidly) or reaches a safe limit. **That is exactly what a Pile Load Test is:** - The **test pile** = your experimental stone pillar - The **bags of sand** = hydraulic jack reaction load - The **sinking measurement** = settlement dial gauges - The **failure point** = ultimate load of the pile This empirical "stress test" gives the engineer the **true safe load** a pile can carry at the site. ## 1.3 Types of Pile Load Tests > [!IMPORTANT] > Pile load tests are broadly classified into **three families**: | Sl. No. | Test Type | Loading Nature | KTU Relevance | |:-------:|-----------|----------------|---------------| | **1** | **Static Load Test (SLT)** | Axial compressive load applied slowly | **Most important** for KTU | | **2** | **Dynamic Load Test** | Impact / hammer blow | Covered in separate module | | **3** | **Lateral Load Test** | Horizontal pull | Out of scope for this topic | Within the **Static Load Test**, there are three sub-categories: 1. **Maintained Load Test (MLT)** – Most common; load held constant in stages (IS 2911 Part 4 method). 2. **Constant Rate of Penetration Test (CRP)** – Load increased continuously to maintain a constant penetration rate. 3. **Cyclic Load Test** – Load is cycled repeatedly to study long-term / repeated loading behaviour. ## 1.4 Why Conduct a Pile Load Test? > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Purposes:** > - To determine the **ultimate load capacity (Qᵤ)** of a single pile. > - To estimate the **safe / allowable load (Qₐ)** with a suitable factor of safety. > - To study the **load-settlement (P–S) curve** behaviour. > - To confirm design assumptions based on soil data. > - To assess **end-bearing vs skin friction** contribution through instrumentation. > - Mandatory on **1 in 100 piles** (routine) and **all test piles** as per IS 2911. ## 1.5 Standard Reference Codes - **IS 2911 (Part 4) – 1985** (Reaffirmed 2010): Code of practice for design and construction of pile foundations – *Load test on piles*. - **IS 14593 – 1998**: CRP test method. - **ASTM D1143 / D3689**: Standard test methods for piles. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Note:** For KTU 2024 ESE, always cite **IS 2911 (Part 4) – 1985** when describing the loading sequence — examiners award marks for the correct code reference. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Static Pile Load Test – Apparatus & Setup > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Components of a Static Load Test Setup:** 1. **Hydraulic Jack** (high-capacity, ≥ 200 tonnes) – applies the vertical compressive load concentrically on the pile head. 2. **Reaction System** – resists the upward thrust of the jack. Three common types: - **Kentledge** – dead weights (concrete blocks, sand bags, steel rails) stacked on a platform above the pile. - **Anchor / Tension Piles** – 4 to 6 surrounding piles used as anchors, connected via reaction girders. - **Ground Anchors** – pre-stressed rock/soil anchors drilled around the test pile. 3. **Load Cell / Pressure Gauge** – measures the applied jack pressure (calibrated). 4. **Dial Gauges** (minimum 2, placed diametrically opposite) – measure pile head settlement to an accuracy of **0.01 mm**. 5. **Reference Beam / Datum Bar** – independent, supported firmly on firm ground at least **2 m** away from the test pile and anchor piles. 6. **Loading Platform & Reaction Girder** – transfers the jack force to the kentledge or anchors. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Mnemonic — "J-G-D-R-A":** > **J**ack → **G**auge (load) → **D**ial gauge (settlement) → **R**eaction (kentledge/anchors) → **A**pply stages. ## 2.2 Maintained Load Test (MLT) – Loading Procedure (As per IS 2911 Part 4) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The standard IS 2911 (Part 4) procedure — highest weightage topic in KTU:** ### 2.2.1 Estimated Safe Load The pile is loaded up to **1.5 times the estimated safe load** (Qₐ) or up to **2.0 × design load** in some cases. The maximum test load is decided *before* the test. ### 2.2.2 Load Increments The test load is applied in **stages of 20% of the estimated safe load** (i.e., 5 stages total: 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, 100%, 120%, 140%, 150%). ### 2.2.3 Settlement Observation at Each Stage At each load increment, the load is held **constant** and settlement readings are taken at: - **0, 2, 5, 10, 15, 30 minutes** (first 30 min) - **Then every 30 minutes** thereafter. ### 2.2.4 Criterion for Next Load Increment The next load increment is applied **only when** the rate of settlement reduces to **less than 0.1 mm/hour** (for non-cohesive soils) or **less than 0.05 mm/hour** (for cohesive soils) — and the rate is **decreasing** with time. ### 2.2.5 Unloading After reaching the maximum test load, the pile is **unloaded in stages of 25% of the maximum test load**, with 1-hour observation between each unloading stage. **Final rebound** is measured 24 hours after complete unloading. ## 2.3 Constant Rate of Penetration (CRP) Test > [!NOTE] > **CRP Test – Key Idea (IS 14593):** > The load is **continuously increased** to maintain a **constant rate of penetration** of the pile into the ground (typically 0.5 mm/min to 1.0 mm/min for cohesive soils; faster for granular). The test continues until the pile penetrates a certain depth (e.g., 15% of pile diameter) or failure is reached. **Advantage:** Faster than MLT, gives a well-defined failure load. **Disadvantage:** Not suitable for large-diameter bored piles (high capacity required). ## 2.4 Cyclic Load Test Load is applied in cycles: 0 → 50% → 0 → 75% → 0 → 100% → 0 of design load. - Used to study **repeated loading** effects (bridges, off-shore structures). - Helps determine **long-term settlement** and identify soil degradation. ## 2.5 Interpretation of Test Results – Ultimate Load Criteria > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield — Criteria for Determining Ultimate Load (Qᵤ) from P–S Curve:** The **Load–Settlement (P–S) curve** is plotted from the test data. The **ultimate load (Qᵤ)** is determined as the load corresponding to any **one** of the following criteria (whichever occurs first): | Sl. No. | Criterion | Description | Reference | |:-------:|-----------|-------------|-----------| | **1** | **IS Criterion** | Settlement = **10% of pile diameter** (for uniform diameter piles) | IS 2911 Part 4 | | **2** | **Davisson's Method** | Elastic line offset method — ultimate load at intersection of P–S curve and a line parallel to elastic compression line offset by **Δ = 4 mm + (B/30)** (where B = pile diameter in mm) | Davisson (1972) | | **3** | **Brinch Hansen's Method** | 80% criterion: ultimate load is at **intersection of tangent at failure** with tangent at **0.9 × failure load**, projected; or simply the load at 0.9×Qₓ at corresponding settlement | Hansen (1963) | | **4** | **Tangent Method** | Intersection of two tangents to the initial and final portions of the P–S curve | Classic | | **5** | **Butler & Hoy** | Settlement criterion based on plastic movement = some fraction of B | — | | **6** | **Failure Manifest** | Sudden, continuous, runaway settlement without load increase | Field observation | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** If the question says *"as per IS code"*, use the **10% B criterion**. If it specifies "interpret using an international method", use **Davisson's offset** or **Brinch Hansen's 80%** method. ## 2.6 Safe / Allowable Load (Qₐ) Determination > [!IMPORTANT] > **Safe Load Formula:** $$Q_a = \frac{Q_u}{F}$$ Where **F = Factor of Safety**, typically: | Condition | FOS (F) | |-----------|:------:| | Static load test (Single pile) | **2.0** | | Static load test (Group of piles) | **2.5 – 3.0** | | Dynamic formula / Empirical | **3.0 – 6.0** | | **Routine test pile (in working pile, IS 2911)** | **1.5** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Convention:** For pile load tests, IS 2911 allows a **reduced FOS of 1.5** when *routine load tests* are conducted on working piles (compared to 2.0 on preliminary test piles). This is a high-yield distinction. ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Sl. No. | Parameter | Formula / Criterion | Unit | |:-------:|-----------|---------------------|:----:| | 1 | Settlement criterion (IS) | $S_u = 0.10 \cdot B$ | mm | | 2 | Davisson's offset | $\Delta = 4 + \dfrac{B}{30}$ | mm | | 3 | Brinch Hansen 80% | $Q_u$ = 0.9 × $Q_f$ intersection | kN | | 4 | Safe Load (Test pile) | $Q_a = \dfrac{Q_u}{2.0}$ | kN | | 5 | Safe Load (Working pile) | $Q_a = \dfrac{Q_u}{1.5}$ | kN | | 6 | Max test load (MLT) | $Q_{max} = 1.5 \cdot Q_a$ (or 2.0 × design load) | kN | | 7 | Load increment (MLT) | $\Delta Q = 0.20 \cdot Q_a$ | kN | | 8 | Settlement rate (cohesive) | $\le 0.05$ mm/hr | mm/hr | | 9 | Settlement rate (non-cohesive) | $\le 0.10$ mm/hr | mm/hr | | 10 | CRP rate (cohesive) | $0.5$ – $1.0$ mm/min | mm/min | | 11 | Time to next load | When rate of settlement is **decreasing** & below limit | min | | 12 | Unloading stages | $25\%$ of $Q_{max}$ | kN | | 13 | Final rebound reading | 24 hours after unloading | hr | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Pile load tests are **mandatory** for all major infrastructure — bridges, high-rise buildings, off-shore platforms, nuclear plants — because they are the only way to *prove* the design capacity against actual ground response. A miscalculation here can lead to **catastrophic failure** (e.g., the famous Transcona grain elevator overturning in 1913 due to under-predicted bearing capacity). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Davisson's Offset Line > [!NOTE] > **Why Davisson's Method?** > The P–S curve has an *initial elastic portion* (linear) corresponding to **elastic shortening** of the pile itself. Beyond the elastic zone, additional settlement occurs due to **soil plastic movement**. Davisson's criterion separates the elastic from the plastic settlement. ### 3.1.1 Elastic Compression of a Pile For a free-standing pile under axial load $P$, the elastic shortening is: $$\delta_{elastic} = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$$ Where: - $P$ = applied load - $L$ = pile length - $A$ = cross-sectional area - $E$ = Young's modulus of pile material This is linear with $P$ — defines a straight line through the origin on the P–S plot. ### 3.1.2 Davisson's Definition of "Failure" (Ultimate Load) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Davisson's Criterion (1972):** > Ultimate load is reached when the actual pile head settlement **exceeds** the elastic compression by an amount equal to the **offset**: $$\delta_{offset} = 4 \text{ mm} + \frac{B}{30}$$ Where **B** = pile diameter in mm (or width for square piles). ### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Offset Line Equation The **elastic compression line** passes through the origin with slope $\frac{A \cdot E}{L}$ (load per unit settlement): $$P_{elastic}(S) = \frac{A \cdot E}{L} \cdot S$$ Adding the offset $\Delta = 4 + \frac{B}{30}$ mm gives the **Davisson's offset line** (parallel to elastic line, shifted right by $\Delta$): $$P_{davisson}(S) = \frac{A \cdot E}{L} \cdot (S - \Delta)$$ Where $\Delta$ is in consistent length units (mm). ### 3.1.4 Algorithm to Find $Q_u$ Using Davisson **Step 1:** Compute $\Delta = 4 + \frac{B}{30}$ mm. **Step 2:** Compute the slope $m = \frac{A \cdot E}{L}$ from the linear elastic portion of the P–S curve (early readings). **Step 3:** Draw the offset line: $P = m \cdot (S - \Delta)$. **Step 4:** The **ultimate load $Q_u$** is the value of $P$ at the point where the Davisson's offset line **intersects** the actual P–S curve. **Step 5:** $Q_a = \dfrac{Q_u}{F}$ (F = 2.0 for test pile, 1.5 for working pile). ## 3.2 Worked Example – Davisson's Method > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A 600 mm diameter, 20 m long RCC bored pile is tested. Concrete $E = 2.5 \times 10^4$ MPa. The initial elastic slope of the P–S curve is $m = 200$ kN/mm. Using Davisson's method, determine the ultimate load. ### Solution **Step 1 — Compute the offset:** $$\Delta = 4 + \frac{B}{30} = 4 + \frac{600}{30} = 4 + 20 = 24 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Elastic slope** is given: $m = 200$ kN/mm. **Step 3 — Equation of Davisson's line:** $$P = m \cdot (S - \Delta) = 200 \cdot (S - 24)$$ **Step 4 — Intersection with P–S curve:** The actual P–S curve crosses the line $P = 200(S - 24)$ at, say, settlement $S = 32$ mm. $$Q_u = 200 \cdot (32 - 24) = 200 \cdot 8 = 1600 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5 — Safe load (test pile, F = 2):** $$Q_a = \frac{Q_u}{2.0} = \frac{1600}{2.0} = 800 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 6 — Safe load (working pile, F = 1.5):** $$Q_{a,working} = \frac{1600}{1.5} = 1066.67 \text{ kN}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Safe Load from IS 2911 Criterion > [!NOTE] > **IS Criterion:** Ultimate load = Load corresponding to settlement = 10% of pile diameter. $$S_u = 0.10 \cdot B$$ ### 3.3.1 Worked Example – IS Criterion **Given:** Pile diameter $B = 500$ mm. P–S curve data: - At P = 1000 kN, S = 8 mm - At P = 1500 kN, S = 22 mm - At P = 1800 kN, S = 50 mm (failure) **Find:** Safe load using IS criterion. **Step 1:** $S_u = 0.10 \cdot 500 = 50$ mm. **Step 2:** From the P–S curve, settlement of 50 mm occurs at $P = 1800$ kN. So $Q_u = 1800$ kN. **Step 3:** Apply FOS = 2.0 (test pile): $$Q_a = \frac{1800}{2.0} = 900 \text{ kN}$$ ## 3.4 Settlement Rate Criterion – Mathematical Form > [!IMPORTANT] > The settlement rate criterion for proceeding to the next load increment in MLT is: $$\frac{dS}{dt} \le S_{rate,lim} \quad \text{AND} \quad \frac{d^2 S}{dt^2} \le 0$$ | Soil Type | Settlement Rate Limit | |-----------|:---------------------:| | Cohesive (clay) | 0.05 mm/hr | | Non-cohesive (sand) | 0.10 mm/hr | The **second condition** (rate is *decreasing*) ensures the pile has stabilized. A linearly increasing rate indicates **progressive failure** — the test must be stopped. ## 3.5 Maximum Test Load Calculation (IS 2911) For a pile designed for safe working load $Q_a$: $$Q_{test,max} = 1.5 \times Q_a$$ Number of load stages: $$n_{stages} = \frac{Q_{test,max}}{0.20 \cdot Q_a} = \frac{1.5 \cdot Q_a}{0.20 \cdot Q_a} = 7.5 \approx 8 \text{ stages}$$ So the test has **8 stages** at: 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, 100%, 120%, 140%, 150% of $Q_a$. For unloading (4 stages at 25% of $Q_{max}$): $$Q_{unload,i} = Q_{max} - i \cdot 0.25 \cdot Q_{max} \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3, 4$$ ## 3.6 Summary Table – Comparison of Methods | Method | Failure Definition | Best Suited For | FOS (IS) | |--------|-------------------|-----------------|:--------:| | **IS 10% B** | S = 0.10B | All pile types in IS code | 2.0 / 1.5 | | **Davisson** | Elastic line + offset Δ | Rigid piles, end bearing | 2.0 | | **Brinch Hansen 90%** | 0.9 × Q_f intersection | General use | 2.0 | | **Tangent method** | Intersection of two tangents | H-piles, timber | 2.0 | | **CRP test** | Peak of P–S curve | Friction piles in clay | 2.0 | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In **off-shore oil platforms** (e.g., Mumbai High), Davisson's offset is preferred because it captures the elastic-plastic transition accurately. In **highway bridges on bored piles**, IS 10%B is used. In **temporary works**, CRP gives rapid results for **sheet pile walls**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Pile Load Test Setup – Functional Block Architecture > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block illustrates the functional architecture of a static pile load test, mapping the physical interaction between load application, reaction, and measurement systems. ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["Pile Head
