Each answer is expected in **3–4 sentences** with a labeled diagram if needed. --- ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023] > **Q:** Differentiate between **Maintained Load Test (MLT)** and **Constant Rate of Penetration (CRP) Test** for piles. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Maintained Load Test (MLT) | Constant Rate of Penetration (CRP) | |--------|----------------------------|-----------------------------------| | **Loading** | Load applied in **stages** and held constant | Load increased to maintain **constant penetration rate** | | **Loading rate** | Incremental, time-dependent | Rate controlled (0.5–1 mm/min for clay) | | **Code** | IS 2911 Part 4 | IS 14593 | | **Duration** | Long (days) | Short (hours) | | **Failure observation** | At any load stage | As continuous curve | | **Suitability** | All pile types | Friction piles in clay | | **Result** | Load–Settlement curve in stages | Continuous P–S curve with defined peak | > **Keywords to write:** *Incremental vs continuous*, *load-stage vs penetration-controlled*, *IS 2911 vs IS 14593*. --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] > **Q:** What are the **different methods of providing reaction** in a static pile load test? State the suitability of each. **Model Answer:** 1. **Kentledge Method** – Dead weights (concrete blocks, sand bags, steel) are stacked on a platform over the test pile. Suitable for **low to medium test loads** where space is available and load is not excessive. 2. **Anchor Pile Method** – 4 to 6 surrounding tension piles are used to anchor the reaction girder. Suitable for **high test loads** (> 200 tonnes) on **cohesive soils** with sufficient embedment length for anchors. 3. **Ground Anchor Method** – Pre-stressed rock/soil anchors drilled around the test pile, connected to the reaction girder. Suitable for **rock sites** or dense strata where anchors can develop sufficient pull-out capacity. 4. **Combination** – Kentledge + Anchor piles for very high test loads (> 500 tonnes). > **Keywords to write:** *Kentledge, Anchor piles, Ground anchors, Suitable for medium to high test loads, Site condition dependent.* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part B contains **Module-level Internal Choice**. You must answer **either Question A OR Question B**. Each question is **14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. --- ### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] > **Q (A):** *(a)* Describe in detail the **apparatus, setup, and loading procedure** of a Maintained Load Test (MLT) on a single pile as per **IS 2911 (Part 4) – 1985**. Clearly state the load increment, observation timings, criterion for the next increment, and unloading sequence. *(7 marks)* > > *(b)* A **600 mm diameter, 20 m long** driven RCC pile is subjected to a MLT. The estimated safe load is **800 kN**. Draw the **Load–Settlement (P–S) curve** for the data below and determine the **ultimate load** using the **IS 10%B criterion** and the **Davisson's offset method** (concrete $E = 2.5 \times 10^4$ MPa). Compute the **safe load** for both test pile and working pile. *(7 marks)* | Load (kN) | Settlement (mm) | |:----------:|:---------------:| | 0 | 0.00 | | 200 | 0.80 | | 400 | 1.70 | | 600 | 2.65 | | 800 | 3.70 | | 1000 | 5.20 | | 1200 | 7.50 | | 1400 | 11.20 | | 1600 | 18.50 | | 1800 | 32.00 | | 2000 | 65.00 | --- #### **Model Solution to Q (A) (a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Apparatus & Setup (2.5 marks):** - **Hydraulic Jack** of adequate capacity (≥ 1.5 × design load) placed centrally on the pile head, with load-spreading plates. - **Reaction System:** Kentledge platform (concrete blocks/rails) OR 4–6 anchor piles around the test pile, OR ground anchors — to resist the jack reaction. - **Load Measurement:** Calibrated pressure gauge on the jack OR a load cell, accuracy ± 2% of applied load. - **Settlement Measurement:** Two dial gauges (resolution 0.01 mm) placed diametrically opposite, supported on a **reference beam** (datum bar) anchored at least 2 m from the test pile and at least 1.5 pile diameters from the anchor piles. - **Pile Head Preparation:** Levelled with a thin layer of sand-cement mortar, and a steel plate on top for uniform load distribution. **Step 2 — Loading Procedure as per IS 2911 (Part 4) (2.5 marks):** - The pile is loaded up to **1.5 times the estimated safe load** (Qₐ) in **stages of 20% of Qₐ**. - At each load stage, readings are taken at: **0, 2, 5, 10, 15, 30 minutes**, then every 30 minutes. - **Criterion for the next increment:** The rate of settlement must reduce to: - **0.1 mm/hour** for non-cohesive soils, or - **0.05 mm/hour** for cohesive soils, - AND the rate must be **decreasing** (not increasing) with time. **Step 3 — Unloading Sequence (1.5 marks):** - After reaching 1.5 Qₐ, the pile is held for **24 hours** observation. - Unloaded in **4 stages of 25% of the maximum test load**, holding **1 hour** at each stage. - **Final rebound** reading is taken **24 hours** after complete unloading to assess elastic recovery. - The **P–S curve** is plotted, and the ultimate load is interpreted as per IS 2911 / Davisson's method. **Keywords to write:** *Hydraulic jack, kentledge/anchor pile, dial gauges, 20% Qₐ stages, 0.1 mm/hr or 0.05 mm/hr, decreasing rate, 1.5 Qₐ max, 25% unloading, 24-hr rebound.* --- #### **Model Solution to Q (A) (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — IS 10%B Criterion (2.5 marks):** Compute the limiting settlement: $$S_u = 0.10 \cdot B = 0.10 \cdot 600 \text{ mm} = 60 \text{ mm}$$ From the P–S data table, settlement = 65 mm occurs at **P = 2000 kN**, and at 60 mm (interpolating between 32 mm @ 1800 kN and 65 mm @ 2000 kN): $$S = 60 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow P \approx 1800 + \frac{60 - 32}{65 - 32} \cdot 200 = 1800 + \frac{28}{33} \cdot 200 \approx 1800 + 169.7 \approx 1969.7 \text{ kN}$$ So $Q_u$ (IS) $\approx$ **1970 kN**. **Step 2 — Davisson's Offset Method (3 marks):** Compute the offset: $$\Delta = 4 + \frac{B}{30} = 4 + \frac{600}{30} = 4 + 20 = 24 \text{ mm}$$ Compute the elastic slope from the initial linear portion (using P = 0, 200, 400, 600 kN): $$m = \frac{600 - 0}{2.65 - 0.00} = \frac{600}{2.65} \approx 226.4 \text{ kN/mm}$$ Equation of Davisson's offset line (parallel to elastic, shifted by 24 mm): $$P_{Davisson}(S) = 226.4 \cdot (S - 24)$$ At $S = 30$ mm: $P = 226.4 \times 6 = 1358.4$ kN. At $S = 35$ mm: $P = 226.4 \times 11 = 2490.4$ kN. At $S = 32$ mm: $P = 226.4 \times 8 = 1811.2$ kN. The P–S curve has $S = 32$ mm at $P = 1800$ kN. The Davisson's line at $S = 32$ mm gives $P = 1811$ kN. **Intersection point** is approximately at $(S, P) \approx (32 \text{ mm}, 1810 \text{ kN})$. $$Q_u \text{ (Davisson)} \approx 1810 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3 — Safe Load (1.5 marks):** | Condition | FOS | Safe Load $Q_a$ | |-----------|:---:|:---------------:| | **Test pile** | 2.0 | $Q_a = \dfrac{1810}{2.0} = 905$ kN | | **Working pile** | 1.5 | $Q_a = \dfrac{1810}{1.5} = 1206.7$ kN | **Final Answer:** $Q_u$ (IS) ≈ 1970 kN, $Q_u$ (Davisson) ≈ 1810 kN. Safe load: **905 kN (test) / 1207 kN (working).** --- ### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] > **Q (B):** *(a)* Discuss the various **criteria used to determine the ultimate load** of a pile from the load–settlement curve obtained in a static pile load test. Explain **Davisson's offset method** in detail with a neat sketch. *(7 marks)* > > *(b)* A pile is tested by the **CRP method**. The maximum test load sustained was **2400 kN** at a penetration of **20 mm**. Using **Brinch Hansen's 90% criterion**, determine the ultimate load and the safe load for a working pile (FOS = 1.5). Comment on whether the pile is acceptable if the design load is **1300 kN**. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution to Q (B) (a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Introduction to P–S Curve (1 mark):** The P–S curve is a plot of applied load vs. pile head settlement. It typically shows three regions: initial linear elastic, transition (knee), and plastic (failure). The ultimate load $Q_u$ is the load at which the pile is said to have failed. **Step 2 — Criteria for Ultimate Load (4 marks):** 1. **IS 2911 Criterion:** Load at settlement = **10% of pile diameter** (0.10B). 2. **Davisson's Offset Method:** Load at intersection of P–S curve and a line parallel to the elastic line offset by $\Delta = 4 + \dfrac{B}{30}$ mm. 3. **Brinch Hansen's 90% Criterion:** Ultimate load is the **0.9 × failure load**, read at the corresponding settlement on the P–S curve. 4. **Tangent Method:** Intersection of two tangents to the initial and final (steep) portions of the P–S curve. 5. **Failure Manifest Method:** Continuous, runaway settlement without increase in load. 6. **Butler & Hoy Criterion:** Settlement = (B/30 + 4 mm) at *twice* the failure load; similar in spirit to Davisson's. 7. **Chinese Code (TB 10093):** Settlement = **0.05D** for end-bearing piles in residual soil. **Step 3 — Davisson's Method — Detail (2 marks):** > The elastic shortening of a free-standing pile is $S_e = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$. The actual settlement exceeds this by soil movement. Davisson defined **failure** as occurring when total settlement exceeds $S_e + \Delta$, where $\Delta = 4 + \dfrac{B}{30}$ mm. Thus, on the P–S plot, a line is drawn **parallel** to the elastic line but **offset rightward** by $\Delta$. The intersection of this offset line with the actual P–S curve gives the ultimate load. The offset $\Delta$ accounts for the soil's plastic deformation at failure and is independent of pile length. **Sketch Description (Mermaid):** The plot has: - X-axis: Settlement S (mm) - Y-axis: Load P (kN) - A **straight elastic line** through origin with slope $m = AE/L$. - A **parallel offset line** shifted right by $\Delta$ mm. - The **actual P–S curve** starting at origin, curving up, then bending sharply. - The **intersection point** of offset line and P–S curve = $Q_u$. --- #### **Model Solution to Q (B) (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Brinch Hansen's 90% Criterion (3 marks):** Definition: $Q_u$ = load at which settlement is the **same as** the settlement at **0.9 × $Q_f$**, where $Q_f$ = the failure load (peak of the curve). Given: $Q_f$ = 2400 kN (peak in CRP test). $$0.9 \times Q_f = 0.9 \times 2400 = 2160 \text{ kN}$$ The settlement at $P = 2160$ kN on the P–S curve is **less than 20 mm** (interpolated; assume 16 mm for this problem). Now, the actual ultimate load is at the **same settlement (16 mm) on the P–S curve**. By definition, the settlement at $Q_u$ is the same as at $0.9 Q_f$: $$Q_u = 2160 \text{ kN (by Brinch Hansen's 90% criterion)}$$ > **Note:** This is a *self-consistent* definition. The ultimate load under Brinch Hansen is the load at the same settlement as the 90% failure load. In a *monotonically increasing* P–S curve, this gives $Q_u \approx 0.9 Q_f$. **Step 2 — Safe Load for Working Pile (2 marks):** $$Q_a = \frac{Q_u}{F} = \frac{2160}{1.5} = 1440 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3 — Acceptability Check (2 marks):** - Design load = 1300 kN - Safe load $Q_a$ = 1440 kN - Since $Q_a = 1440$ kN > Design load = 1300 kN → **Pile is ACCEPTABLE** with a margin of $1440 - 1300 = 140$ kN. **Final Verdict:** The pile is **safe and acceptable** for the design load of 1300 kN. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE — Pile Load Tests:** 1. **Wrong FOS (1 mark penalty):** Students often write FOS = 3 for pile load tests. **Correct:** FOS = 2.0 for *test pile*; FOS = 1.5 for *working pile* tested in-situ (IS 2911). Mixing these up loses 1 mark per occurrence. 2. **Confusing 10% B with Davisson's offset (2 marks penalty):** IS criterion is **S = 0.10B**, but Davisson's offset is **4 + B/30 mm**. Do NOT interchange them. 3. **Missing the "decreasing rate" condition (1 mark penalty):** For MLT, the next load stage is allowed only when the rate of settlement is **below the limit AND decreasing**. Students often mention only the magnitude, losing 1 mark. 4. **Unloading sequence error:** Unloading is in **4 stages of 25%** (NOT 20%). Final rebound is read at **24 hours** (not 12 hours or immediately after unloading). 5. **Number of stages:** 1.5 Qₐ / 0.20 Qₐ = **7.5 → 8 stages**. State the exact percentages: 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 150 % of Qₐ. 6. **CRP rate unit error:** Write as **0.5 – 1.0 mm/min** for clay (NOT cm/min). 7. **Citing wrong code:** MLT → **IS 2911 (Part 4) – 1985**; CRP → **IS 14593**. Mixing codes is a common slip-up. 8. **Reaction system confusion:** "Kentledge" is a *system*, not a single weight. Define it correctly as a *platform of dead weights* above the pile. --- ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pile Load Tests:** - ☐ **Static load test** is the most reliable method to determine in-situ pile capacity. - ☐ **MLT** uses staged loading (20% Qₐ increments up to 1.5 Qₐ), per **IS 2911 Part 4**. - ☐ **CRP** uses constant penetration rate (0.5–1 mm/min), per **IS 14593**. - ☐ **Dial gauge accuracy** = 0.01 mm; minimum 2 gauges, diametrically opposite. - ☐ **Reference beam** must be ≥ 2 m from test pile and ≥ 1.5D from anchor piles. - ☐ **Settlement rate limit** = 0.05 mm/hr (clay) / 0.10 mm/hr (sand), AND *decreasing*. - ☐ **Unloading**: 4 stages of 25% of $Q_{max}$; **final rebound** at 24 hours. - ☐ **IS 10%B criterion**: $Q_u$ at $S = 0.10B$. - ☐ **Davisson's offset**: $\Delta = 4 + \dfrac{B}{30}$ mm; $Q_u$ at intersection of offset line and P–S curve. - ☐ **Brinch Hansen 90%**: $Q_u = 0.9 \cdot Q_f$ (or load at same settlement as 0.9 $Q_f$). - ☐ **Safe load (test pile)** = $Q_u / 2.0$; **Safe load (working pile)** = $Q_u / 1.5$. - ☐ **Maximum test load** = 1.5 × $Q_a$ (or 2.0 × design load in some codes). - ☐ **Reaction systems**: Kentledge (dead weight), Anchor piles, Ground anchors. - ☐ **Routine tests** are conducted on **1 in 100 working piles** as per IS 2911. - ☐ **Test piles** are pre-designed trial piles installed *before* the actual foundation work. - ☐ **FOS hierarchy**: Static test (1.5–2) < Dynamic formula (3–6). - ☐ **Destructive test**: Loads the pile to failure; not used on production piles. - ☐ **Applications**: Bridges, high-rise, off-shore platforms, nuclear structures. > **Final KTU Tip:** Always start your MLT answer with **"As per IS 2911 (Part 4) – 1985..."** — this is the code reference that examiners expect. It immediately signals the correct approach and earns you a "presentation" mark.

topics.title: Penetration Tests; Well Foundations: Caissons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Penetration Tests; Well Foundations: Caissons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Penetration Tests — The Foundation of Subsurface Investigation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Penetration tests are *in-situ* geotechnical testing methods used to determine the **engineering properties of soil strata** by measuring the resistance offered by the soil against a penetrating tool (sampler or cone) driven or pushed into the ground.

These tests provide direct field estimates of:
- **Shear strength parameters** (cohesion `c` and friction angle `φ`)
- **Relative density** of cohesionless soils
- **Bearing capacity** of foundations
- **Liquefaction susceptibility**
- **Stratification and layer identification** at depth

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Soil Resistance Probe"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine poking a stick into a cake to test if it is baked. If the stick goes in easily, the cake is soft (loose sand/soft clay). If it resists strongly, the cake is firm/dense (dense sand/stiff clay). Penetration tests are a *scientific version* of this probing — instead of a stick, we use calibrated rods with cones or samplers, and instead of feeling the resistance, we **measure it in kilograms or blows**.

### The Three Principal Penetration Tests (KTU Module 4 Focus)

| Test | Acronym | Driving Mechanism | Key Output |
|------|---------|-------------------|------------|
| **Standard Penetration Test** | SPT | Dynamic (Hammer blows) | **N-value** (blows per 300 mm) |
| **Static Cone Penetration Test** | SCPT / CPT | Static (Hydraulic push) | **qc, fs** (cone & sleeve resistance) |
| **Dynamic Cone Penetration Test** | DCPT | Dynamic (Hammer blows) | **DCPT N-value** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Recalled:**
> - SPT Hammer mass: **63.5 kg** (140 lb)
> - SPT Drop height: **750 mm** (30 in)
> - SPT Sampler O.D.: **51 mm** (2 in)
> - I.D.: **35 mm** (1.375 in)

---

## 1.2 Well Foundations & Caissons — The Deep River-Bed Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> A **well foundation** (also called a *caisson* in modern KTU terminology, though traditionally distinct) is a *deep foundation* used for bridges and heavy structures, constructed by **sinking a hollow prefabricated shell (well or caisson)** through water/soil to a firm bearing stratum, then filling it with concrete.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do we need well foundations?**
> - When foundations must be taken **below a river bed** (scour depth).
> - When the top soil is **highly compressible** and unsuitable for shallow footings.
> - When the loads are **enormous** (e.g., long-span bridges, heavy piers).
> - When **open excavation is impractical** due to groundwater.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Dug into Sand"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Picture an **open steel bucket with no bottom** placed on wet sand. You press it down while scooping out sand from the inside. The bucket sinks deeper with each scoop. Eventually it hits hard ground (rock) and you fill the bucket with concrete. That is exactly how an **open caisson** works — except in real engineering, the "bucket" is called a **well**, the sand-scooping is called **grabbing**, and the final concrete fill is the **load-bearing pier**.

### Types of Caissons / Well Foundations

```
CAISSONS (WELL FOUNDATIONS)
│
├── 1. Open Caisson (Well)
│     ├── Single Well (Dumb-well, Circular, Rectangular, Twin)
│     └── Multiple Well (Dumb-bells, Twin-D-shaped)
│
├── 2. Box Caisson
│     (Closed top, open bottom — used on firm bed above water)
│
└── 3. Pneumatic Caisson
      (Compressed air used to keep water out — for deep underwater work)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Emphasis:**
> The 2024 scheme focuses heavily on **Open Caissons (Wells)** — components, sinking, sand filling, and bearing capacity by **Terzaghi's Theory** and **IS 3955** code provisions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Standard Penetration Test (SPT) — The Gold Standard

### Theoretical Background

The SPT was developed by the **Raymond Pile Company (1902)** and standardized by **Terzaghi & Peck (1948)**. It is a **dynamic penetration test** performed inside a borehole.

### Step-by-Step Procedure (KTU Board Favorite)

1. Drill a borehole to the desired depth (typically **150 mm dia.**).
2. Place the **split-spoon sampler** (Standard Penetration Sampler) at the bottom of the hole.
3. Drive the sampler using a **63.5 kg hammer** dropped freely from **750 mm height**.
4. Record blows for **four 75 mm penetrations** (total 300 mm):
   - First 150 mm is **seating drive** → blows recorded but **discarded**.
   - Next 300 mm is the **test drive** → blows recorded as the **N-value**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Fact:**
> $N_{SPT}$ = Number of blows required for **penetration of 300 mm (second and third 150 mm intervals combined)** — this is the *uncorrected* field N-value.

### Corrections to N-value (Board Exam Essential)

#### (a) Dilatancy Correction (Overburden Pressure Correction) — $N_1$

For cohesionless soils, N is corrected to a standard effective overburden pressure of **$\sigma'_0 = 100$ kPa (1 kg/cm²)**.

$$
N_1 = N_{SPT} \cdot C_N
$$

where,

$$
C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2000}{\sigma'_0}\right) \quad \text{(Peck et al.)}
$$

Or, more commonly used in KTU:

$$
C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1935}{\sigma'_0}\right) \quad \text{[Gibbs & Holtz]}
$$

with $\sigma'_0$ in **kPa**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> Students often forget that the **overburden correction is applied only to granular (cohesionless) soils**. For clays, use the uncorrected N directly in correlation charts.

#### (b) SPT Correction for Dilatancy (Liao & Whitman, 1986)

$$
(N_1)_{60} = N_{SPT} \cdot C_N \cdot C_{ER} \cdot C_{ET} \cdot C_{BD} \cdot C_S
$$

Where:
- $C_N$ = Overburden correction factor
- $C_{ER}$ = Energy ratio correction (for 60% theoretical energy: $C_{ER} = 1.0$)
- $C_{ET}$ = Hammer efficiency (Donut hammer = 0.60)
- $C_{BD}$ = Borehole diameter correction
- $C_S$ = Sampler liner correction

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU boards, typically only the **dilatancy/overburden correction** is asked. The full Liao-Whitman formula is for higher-level competitive exams.

### Correlations from N-value (High-Yield Board Table)

| Parameter | Correlation | Soil Type |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| Relative Density $D_r$ | $D_r \approx \sqrt{N/60}$ (Meyerhof) | Sand |
| Friction angle $\phi$ | $\phi = 25° + 15° \cdot N$ (Peck) | Sand (approx) |
| Cohesion $c$ | $c \approx 4.5 \cdot N$ (kPa) | Clay |
| Unconfined Compressive Strength $q_u$ | $q_u \approx 12.5 \cdot N$ (kPa) | Clay |
| Modulus of Elasticity $E$ | $E \approx 0.5 \cdot N$ to $N$ (MPa) | — |

---

## 2.2 Static Cone Penetration Test (SCPT / CPT)

### Theoretical Background

The SCPT uses a **cone (60° apex, 10 cm² base area, 35.7 mm dia.)** pushed **statically** into the soil at **10–20 mm/s** using hydraulic jacking against the reaction of an anchor or dead weight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Principle:**
> The soil cannot be "dilated" or "drained" instantly during a static push → measures **undrained shear strength** in clays and **tip resistance** in sands.

### Outputs from SCPT

1. **Cone Resistance ($q_c$):** Resistance at the cone tip → in **MPa** or **kN/m²**.
2. **Sleeve Friction ($f_s$):** Friction along the friction sleeve.
3. **Friction Ratio ($R_f$):**

$$
R_f = \frac{f_s}{q_c} \times 100\% \quad [\text{typically } 1\% \text{ to } 10\%]
$$

### Correlations from SCPT

For **cohesive soil** (undrained):

$$
q_c = N_k \cdot c_u + \sigma_0
$$

Where,
- $N_k$ = Cone factor (typically **15 to 20**; KTU assumes **N_k = 20** in problems)
- $c_u$ = Undrained cohesion
- $\sigma_0$ = Total overburden pressure

For **cohesionless soil**:

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(0.1 \cdot q_c + 32.7\right) \quad \text{[approx.]}
$$

---

## 2.3 Dynamic Cone Penetration Test (DCPT)

### Theoretical Background

Used for **lightweight exploration** up to shallow depths (typically **< 8 m**). A **63.5 kg hammer (or 8 kg hammer for light DCPT)** drops through a guide rod to drive a cone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Variants Recognized in KTU:**
> 1. **Heavy DCPT:** 63.5 kg hammer, 750 mm drop — same as SPT hammer. Result = DCPT N-value per 300 mm.
> 2. **Light DCPT (LDCPT):** 8 kg hammer, 575 mm drop — used for road subgrade investigations.

### Cone Types

- **Shoe-1:** 60° apex, 5.04 cm dia., base area = 20 cm² (Indian Standard).
- **Shoe-2:** 60° apex, 3.57 cm dia., base area = 10 cm².

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Penetration Tests

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $N_1 = N \cdot C_N$ | Dilatancy-corrected SPT | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}(1935/\sigma'_0)$ | Gibbs-Holtz CN | kPa |
| 3 | $c_u = (q_c - \sigma_0)/N_k$ | SCPT cohesion formula | kPa |
| 4 | $R_f = (f_s/q_c) \times 100$ | Friction ratio | % |
| 5 | $D_r = \sqrt{N/60}$ | Relative density (Meyerhof) | fraction |
| 6 | $\phi = 25° + 15° \cdot N$ | Friction angle (Peck) | degrees |
| 7 | $q_u = 12.5 \cdot N$ | UCS of clay (Terzaghi) | kPa |

---

## 2.5 Well Foundations / Caissons — Theoretical Foundation

### Components of an Open Well (Caisson)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation:**
> A labelled cross-section of a well foundation with **all 5 components** is a guaranteed 7-mark question.

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Cutting Edge** | Sharpened steel edge to penetrate soil during sinking |
| **Well Curb** | Tapered lower portion housing the cutting edge |
| **Steining** | Vertical wall of the well (brick, concrete, RCC) |
| **Well Cap / Top Plug** | RCC slab sealing the top of the well |
| **Bottom Plug** | Concrete plug at bottom after sinking (transfers load to soil) |

### Shape of Wells

| Shape | Use Case |
|-------|----------|
| **Circular** | Most common; easiest sinking (uniform stress) |
| **Rectangular / Square** | Required when circular shape incompatible with pier geometry |
| **Dumb-bell (Twin Circular)** | Used when larger rectangular dimensions needed but sinking of single large rectangle difficult |
| **Twin D** | Alternative to dumb-bells |

---

## 2.6 Bearing Capacity of Well Foundations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Most-Asked Topic:**
> Bearing capacity by **Terzaghi's Theory** (extended for deep foundations) is a board favourite.

### Ultimate Bearing Capacity (Terzaghi)

For a well foundation (deep), treating it as a **shallow footing at depth $D_f$**:

$$
q_u = 1.3 \, c \, N_c + q \, (N_q - 1) + 0.4 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma
$$

Where:
- $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors (function of $\phi$)
- $B$ = Well diameter (or width)
- $D_f$ = Depth below scour level
- $q = \gamma \cdot D_f$ = Surcharge at the foundation level

> [!WARNING]
> **For $\phi = 0$ (clay)**, the formula reduces to:
> $q_u = 1.3 \, c \, N_c + q$, where $N_c = 5.7$ (for circular) or $5.14$ (for square).

### Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$
q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma \cdot D_f
$$

### Safe Bearing Capacity (Using KTU Standard)

$$
q_{safe} = \frac{q_u}{F}
$$

Where $F$ = Factor of Safety = **3.0** (for bridge foundations per IS 3955).

---

## 2.7 Tilts and Shifts (Important KTU Concept)

During sinking, wells can deviate from vertical and horizontal position. These must be limited:

| Deviation | Permissible Limit (IS 3955) |
|-----------|----------------------------|
| **Tilt** | 1 in 80 (horizontal:vertical) |
| **Shift** | Less than the radius of the well (lateral) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tilt** = angular deviation from vertical.
> **Shift** = horizontal displacement of the centre at the base.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — Well Foundations

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $q_u = 1.3 c N_c + q(N_q - 1) + 0.4 \gamma B N_\gamma$ | Ultimate BC (Terzaghi) |
| 2 | $q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f$ | Net ultimate BC |
| 3 | $q_s = q_u / F$ | Safe BC (F=3, IS 3955) |
| 4 | $Q_{ult} = q_u \cdot A_b$ | Total ultimate load (kN) |
| 5 | $V = \pi B^2 \cdot D_f / 4$ | Volume of well (circular) |
| 6 | Grip length = max(2.5 m, $D_f$/3) | Minimum embedment into bearing stratum |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bearing Capacity Factor $N_q$ (Reisnher / Terzaghi)

### Starting Assumption

For a strip footing on a weightless soil with surcharge $q$, the failure surface is approximated by a **logarithmic spiral** under the footing.

### Step 1: Plastic Equilibrium Equations

Consider a vertical line at the edge of the footing. Rankine's active wedge makes angle $\alpha = 45° + \phi/2$ with the horizontal.

The passive zone on the opposite side makes angle $\beta = 45° - \phi/2$.

### Step 2: Equilibrium of Forces on the Heel of the Logarithmic Spiral

By taking moments about the center of the log-spiral, Terzaghi derived:

$$
N_q = \frac{a^2}{2 \cos^2(45° + \phi/2)}
$$

where $a$ is a function of $\phi$ evaluated from the geometry.

### Step 3: Final Closed-Form Expression (Reisnher-Meyerhof form)

$$
\boxed{N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)}
$$

### Step 4: Example Calculation

For $\phi = 30°$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi &= \tan 30° = 0.5774 \\
\pi \tan\phi &= \pi \times 0.5774 = 1.8138 \\
e^{1.8138} &= 6.13 \\
\left(45° + \phi/2\right) &= 45° + 15° = 60° \\
\tan 60° &= 1.732 \\
\tan^2 60° &= 3.0 \\
\therefore N_q &= 6.13 \times 3.0 = 18.4
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $N_q = 18.4$ matches the **Terzaghi bearing capacity table** for $\phi = 30°$.

---

## 3.2 Full Numerical Worked Example — SPT Correction

**Problem (KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern):**

A Standard Penetration Test was conducted at a depth of 9 m below ground level in a saturated sand deposit with water table at 4 m. The recorded SPT N-value was 22. The unit weights are: $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³ (above WT), $\gamma_{bulk} = 18$ kN/m³ (below WT). Determine the corrected N-value for dilatancy.

### Step 1: Effective Overburden Pressure

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma'_0 &= \gamma_{bulk} \times 4 + (\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w) \times 5 \\
\sigma'_0 &= 18 \times 4 + (20 - 9.81) \times 5 \\
\sigma'_0 &= 72 + (10.19)(5) \\
\sigma'_0 &= 72 + 50.95 \\
\sigma'_0 &= 122.95 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate Overburden Correction Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_N &= 0.77 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1935}{\sigma'_0}\right) \\
C_N &= 0.77 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1935}{122.95}\right) \\
C_N &= 0.77 \log_{10}(15.738) \\
C_N &= 0.77 \times 1.197 \\
C_N &= 0.922
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Corrected N-value

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 &= N_{SPT} \times C_N \\
N_1 &= 22 \times 0.922 \\
N_1 &\approx 20.3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $N_1 \approx 20$ (rounded to nearest integer for design).

---

## 3.3 Full Numerical Worked Example — Well Foundation Bearing Capacity

**Problem (KTU University Exam — Model Question):**

A circular well foundation of **8 m diameter** is to be sunk through a clay layer to a bearing stratum. The clay has $c_u = 80$ kPa, $\phi_u = 0$, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. The depth of well below scour level = **18 m**. Calculate the **safe bearing capacity** using Terzaghi's theory. Take FOS = 3.

### Step 1: Identify Bearing Capacity Factors (for $\phi = 0$, circular)

For Terzaghi circular footing:

$$
N_c = 5.7, \quad N_q = 1.0, \quad N_\gamma = 0
$$

### Step 2: Calculate Surcharge at Founding Level

$$
q = \gamma \cdot D_f = 18 \times 18 = 324 \text{ kPa}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Terzaghi's Formula (Circular Footing, Clay)

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= 1.3 \, c \, N_c + q \, (N_q - 1) + 0.4 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma \\
q_u &= 1.3 \times 80 \times 5.7 + 324 \times (1.0 - 1.0) + 0 \\
q_u &= 592.8 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate Safe Bearing Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_s &= \frac{q_u}{F} = \frac{592.8}{3} \\
q_s &= 197.6 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Total Safe Load Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_b &= \frac{\pi B^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 8^2}{4} = 50.27 \text{ m}^2 \\
Q_{safe} &= q_s \times A_b = 197.6 \times 50.27 \\
Q_{safe} &\approx 9{,}933 \text{ kN} \approx 9.93 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table for Penetration Tests

| Scenario | Recommended Test | Reason |
|----------|------------------|--------|
| Soft clay, undrained strength needed | SCPT (CPT) | Static, undrained mode |
| Dense sand, liquefaction check | SPT | Standard $N_1$ correlations |
| Road subgrade (shallow < 5 m) | LDCPT | Quick, economical |
| Deep offshore investigation | CPT (with pore pressure — CPTu) | Continuous profile |
| Borehole already drilled | SPT | Uses existing borehole |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Well Foundation Components

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Ground Surface] --> A2[Well Cap - RCC Slab Top]
    A2 --> A3[Sand / Concrete Fill Inside Well]
    A3 --> A4[Steining - Vertical Wall of Well]
    A4 --> A5[Well Curb - Tapered Lower Section]
    A5 --> A6[Cutting Edge - Sharpened Steel Edge]
    A6 --> A7[Bottom Plug - Concrete Plug]
    A7 --> A8[Founding Stratum - Bearing Rock or Hard Stratum]
    A8 --> A9[Below Scour Level Line]
    A1 -.Scour Level.-> A9
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Standard Penetration Test Procedure

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Drill Borehole to Test Depth] --> stepB[Lower Split-Spoon Sampler on Drill Rods]
    stepB --> stepC[Place 63.5 kg Hammer in Guide]
    stepC --> stepD[Drive Hammer 750 mm Drop Height]
    stepD --> stepE{Record Blows per 150 mm}
    stepE --> stepF[First 150 mm - Seating Drive - DISCARD]
    stepF --> stepG[Second 150 mm - Record N1]
    stepG --> stepH[Third 150 mm - Record N2]
    stepH --> stepI[Combine N1 plus N2 equals N value]
    stepI --> stepJ[Apply Overburden Correction CN]
    stepJ --> stepK[Compute Corrected N1 Value]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Types of Caissons

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[CAISSON TYPES]
    root --> open[OPEN CAISSON]
    root --> box[BOX CAISSON]
    root --> pneum[PNEUMATIC CAISSON]
    open --> openA[Single - Circular]
    open --> openB[Single - Rectangular]
    open --> openC[Multiple - Dumbbell]
    open --> openD[Multiple - Twin D]
    box --> boxA[Open bottom - Top Closed]
    box --> boxB[Used above water on firm bed]
    pneum --> pneumA[Compressed Air to Depress Water]
    pneum --> pneumB[Used for Deep Underwater - Workers Inside]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Architecture Diagram — Well Sinking Operation Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    p1[Stage 1: Lay Well Curb at Bed] --> p2[Stage 2: Build Steining in Lifts]
    p2 --> p3[Stage 3: Remove Soil from Inside by Grabbing]
    p3 --> p4[Stage 4: Well Sinks under Self Weight plus KENTLEDGE]
    p4 --> p5[Stage 5: Correct Tilts and Shifts by Jetting or Pulling]
    p5 --> p6[Stage 6: Reach Founding Stratum]
    p6 --> p7[Stage 7: Cast Bottom Plug underwater]
    p7 --> p8[Stage 8: Dewater Inside if Possible]
    p8 --> p9[Stage 9: Fill Well with Sand or Concrete]
    p9 --> p10[Stage 10: Cast Well Cap on Top]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Comparison Diagram — SPT vs SCPT vs DCPT

```mermaid
graph LR
    spt[SPT - Dynamic - 63.5 kg Hammer - 750 mm Drop - Splitspoon Sampler] -->|Measures N-value| out1[Blows per 300 mm]
    scpt[SCPT or CPT - Static - Hydraulic Push - 60 degree Cone 10 cm sq] -->|Measures qc, fs| out2[MPa or kN per m sq]
    dcpt[DCPT - Dynamic - 8 kg or 63.5 kg Hammer - 575 mm or 750 mm Drop - Cone Tip] -->|Measures DCPT N-value| out3[Blows per 300 mm]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short Answer)

### Question 1 — Define SPT. Write the standard hammer mass and drop height used. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Standard Penetration Test (SPT):** An *in-situ* dynamic penetration test conducted inside a borehole by driving a **standard split-spoon sampler** (51 mm O.D., 35 mm I.D.) using a **63.5 kg (140 lb) hammer** dropped freely from **750 mm (30 in)** height. The number of blows required for the **last 300 mm** of penetration (after 150 mm seating drive) is the **N-value**.

> [!NOTE]
> SPT is the **most widely used** in-situ test globally due to its simplicity and availability of extensive empirical correlations.

---

### Question 2 — Differentiate between Open Caisson and Pneumatic Caisson. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Open Caisson | Pneumatic Caisson |
|--------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Closure** | Open at top and bottom | Closed at top, working chamber with compressed air |
| **Water Entry** | Free water ingress | Air pressure prevents water entry |
| **Working Condition** | Workers do *not* enter | Workers enter air-locked chamber |
| **Depth Limit** | No air pressure limit | Limited to ~30 m (human physiological limit) |
| **Use** | Most river bridge piers | Deep underwater, e.g., large bridge piers in rivers with high velocity |

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Q4, Module 4 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Standard Penetration Test (SPT)** — its procedure, apparatus, corrections to N-value, and any two correlations with soil properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular **well foundation** of **6 m diameter** is to be founded in a **clay stratum** with undrained cohesion $c_u = 60$ kPa, $\phi = 0°$, and unit weight $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³. The well is to be embedded **15 m below scour level**. Using Terzaghi's theory and FOS = 3, determine the **safe bearing capacity** and the **safe load** the well can carry. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**(i) Procedure (3 marks):**

1. Borehole drilled to test depth using rotary or auger method (typically 150 mm dia.).
2. Lower the standard split-spoon sampler attached to drill rods.
3. Position the 63.5 kg hammer on the anvil.
4. Drive the sampler through four 75 mm increments (total 300 mm test drive).
5. Record number of blows for each 75 mm increment.
6. **Discard** the first 150 mm (seating) blows; sum of next 300 mm blows = **N-value**.

**(ii) Apparatus (1.5 marks):**
- Split-spoon sampler (51 mm O.D., 35 mm I.D., 600 mm long)
- Drill rods (AW or BW)
- Trip hammer (donut/ safety/ automatic) — 63.5 kg
- Guide for free fall — 750 mm
- Anvil
- Counting device

**(iii) Corrections (1.5 marks):**

**Overburden Correction (Dilatancy):**

$$
C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1935}{\sigma'_0}\right), \qquad N_1 = N \cdot C_N
$$

> Applied only to **cohesionless (granular) soils** to standardize to a reference effective overburden of 100 kPa.

**(iv) Correlations (1 mark):**

1. **Relative Density (Meyerhof, 1957):** $\quad D_r \approx \sqrt{N/60}$
2. **Friction Angle (Peck):** $\quad \phi \approx 25° + 15° N$ (for sands)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Diameter $B = 6$ m, $c_u = 60$ kPa, $\phi = 0$, $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³
- Depth below scour $D_f = 15$ m, FOS = 3

**Step 1: Surcharge at Founding Level**

$$
q = \gamma D_f = 17 \times 15 = 255 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2: Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Factors (Circular footing, $\phi = 0$)**

$$
N_c = 5.7, \quad N_q = 1.0, \quad N_\gamma = 0
$$

**Step 3: Ultimate Bearing Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= 1.3 \, c \, N_c + q(N_q - 1) + 0.4 \gamma B N_\gamma \\
q_u &= 1.3 \times 60 \times 5.7 + 255 \times (1 - 1) + 0 \\
q_u &= 444.6 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Safe Bearing Capacity**

$$
q_s = \frac{q_u}{F} = \frac{444.6}{3} = 148.2 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 5: Safe Load Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_b &= \frac{\pi B^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 6^2}{4} = 28.27 \text{ m}^2 \\
Q_{safe} &= q_s \times A_b = 148.2 \times 28.27 \\
Q_{safe} &\approx 4{,}189.6 \text{ kN} \approx 4.19 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $q_s = 148.2$ kPa; $Q_{safe} \approx 4.19$ MN.

---

### **QUESTION B** — `[KTU University Exam — Alternate Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **Static Cone Penetration Test (SCPT)** with neat sketches. Discuss its advantages and limitations over SPT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the **components of an open well foundation**? Explain any **three sinking methods** used in practice. Also state the **permissible limits of tilt and shift** as per IS 3955. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**(i) Apparatus (2 marks):**
- 60° apex cone with **10 cm² base area** (35.7 mm dia.)
- Friction sleeve (150 cm² area) above cone
- Hydraulic jacking system (10–20 tonnes capacity)
- Anchor / dead load for reaction
- Push rods (1 m length)
- Measuring instruments (load cell, LVDT)

**(ii) Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Push cone vertically at **10–20 mm/s** (quasi-static).
2. Continuous recording of cone resistance $q_c$ and sleeve friction $f_s$.
3. Records taken every 100–200 mm depth.

**(iii) Advantages over SPT (1.5 marks):**
1. **Continuous profile** (vs discrete 1.5 m intervals in SPT).
2. **Stratification** and thin layer detection — superior.
3. Operator-independent — more reliable.
4. Measures both $q_c$ and $f_s$ → soil classification via $R_f$.

**(iv) Limitations (1.5 marks):**
1. Cannot penetrate dense gravel / boulder strata.
2. Requires heavy reaction force (>10 tonnes).
3. Difficult above groundwater in granular soils.
4. Less empirical correlations compared to SPT.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Components of Open Well (3 marks):**
1. **Cutting Edge** — Hardened steel blade at bottom.
2. **Well Curb** — Tapered lower portion (1–2 m height).
3. **Steining** — Main vertical wall (brick masonry / RCC).
4. **Bottom Plug** — Tremie concrete at bottom.
5. **Well Cap (Top Plug)** — RCC slab at top.
6. **Sand/Concrete Fill** — Inside the well.

**(ii) Three Sinking Methods (3 marks):**

**Method 1: Open Sinking with Grabbing**
- Soil removed from inside well by **grab bucket** (clamshell).
- Well sinks under **self-weight + kentledge** (additional load on top).
- Used when soil is soft to medium.

**Method 2: Sinking with Jetting**
- Water jets directed at cutting edge to **lubricate** and **loosen** soil.
- Reduces skin friction along the outer wall.
- Particularly useful in cohesionless soils.

**Method 3: Pneumatic Sinking (Compressed Air Method)**
- Used when boulders or hard strata encountered.
- Reduces effective weight of soil by air injection.

**Other Methods:** Chiseling (for boulders), Dewatering (to reduce buoyancy).

**(iii) Permissible Limits (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Limit (IS 3955) |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Tilt | 1 in 80 |
| Shift | < Radius of well at base |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> - Writing $N_q = 1$ for $\phi = 0$ without remembering that the formula requires $q(N_q - 1)$, which **always gives zero**, and students often wrongly apply $qN_q$.
> - Forgetting the **1.3 factor** for circular footings in Terzaghi's $c N_c$ term (rectangular uses $1.0 c N_c$).
> - Confusing **N-value** (penetration per 300 mm) with blows per 100 mm or 150 mm.
> - Applying dilatancy correction to **clays** instead of only sands.
> - Using $\gamma$ instead of $\gamma'$ (effective) below water table.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Penetration Tests — Quick Revision Checklist:**
> - SPT: 63.5 kg / 750 mm hammer; **N = blows for last 300 mm**.
> - $C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}(1935/\sigma'_0)$ for **cohesionless soils only**.
> - SCPT: continuous profile; $q_c$ in MPa; **$N_k = 20$ (KTU assumption)**.
> - DCPT: 8 kg (LDCPT) or 63.5 kg (HDCPT) hammer; shallow depths.
> - $D_r = \sqrt{N/60}$ (Meyerhof).
> - $\phi = 25° + 15° N$ (Peck — coarse estimate).
> - $q_u = 12.5 N$ kPa for clay (approximate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Well Foundations / Caissons — Quick Revision Checklist:**
> - 5 components: **Cutting Edge, Well Curb, Steining, Bottom Plug, Well Cap**.
> - Open caisson most common for bridges over rivers.
> - Pneumatic caisson used when water ingress must be prevented.
> - Tilt limit = **1 in 80** (IS 3955).
> - Shift limit = **less than the radius** of well base.
> - Bearing capacity by Terzaghi: $q_u = 1.3 c N_c + q(N_q - 1) + 0.4 \gamma B N_\gamma$.
> - For circular footing, factor **1.3** with $c N_c$.
> - FOS = **3.0** for bridge well foundations (IS 3955).
> - Grip length into bearing stratum ≥ max(2.5 m, $D_f/3$).
> - Most common shape: **circular** (easiest sinking due to uniform stress).
> - Sinking methods: Grabbing + kentledge, Jetting, Pneumatic, Chiseling.
> - Bottom plug by **tremie concrete** (underwater placement).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shape of Wells and Component Parts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT402 - SOIL MECHANICS AND FOUNDATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shape of Wells and Component Parts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **Well Foundation** (also called a **Caisson** in general terminology) is a type of deep foundation that is a large hollow cylindrical or rectangular structure constructed in-situ and sunk into the ground or water to serve as a foundation for heavy structures, most commonly **bridge piers and abutments**. It is built by open excavation from the inside, with the structure sinking under its own weight (and added kentledge) as the soil is dredged out from within.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** The term **"Well"** in Indian practice (and KTU syllabus) specifically refers to the open well/caisson type used in bridge engineering, while the broader engineering term is **"Caisson"** which also includes box caissons and pneumatic caissons. For KTU examinations, both terms are often used interchangeably but "well foundation" is the more common KTU terminology.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to dig a deep pit in sandy soil near a river to build a bridge pillar. The moment you dig, the surrounding sand collapses into the pit. To prevent this, you build a **hollow, open-bottomed bucket** (this is the well) and place it on the ground. Then, you scoop out the soil from the **inside** of the bucket through the open top. The bucket sinks deeper as you scoop. As it sinks, you keep adding more rings (like stacking Lego blocks) on top of the bucket so that its rim stays above ground/water level. Eventually, when the bucket reaches a firm soil layer, you seal the bottom with concrete, fill the inside with sand/concrete, and cap it with a slab. The hollow bucket has now become a solid foundation pillar!

**Key physical concepts:**
- The well is essentially a **mould** sunk into the ground.
- The internal excavation is called **dredging**.
- The added weight to make the well sink is called **kentledge**.
- Standard depth of wells can range from **5 m** to over **50 m** below water level.

## 1.3 When Are Well Foundations Used?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examination Highlight:** This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

Wells are preferred when:
1. The top soil is too soft/loose for shallow foundations.
2. The subsoil contains **scourable strata** (river beds) where other foundations would be undermined.
3. Loads are very heavy (e.g., major bridge piers, heavy building columns).
4. The depth of firm bearing strata is **between 5 m and 50 m**.
5. Construction over or within water bodies is required.
6. The structure is subjected to large lateral loads (from wind, water current, vessel impact, seismic forces).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Wells by Shape

The shape of a well is selected based on the **dimensional requirements of the superstructure** (bridge pier), the **direction of flow of water**, and the **economics of construction**. Each shape has a specific aspect ratio (length-to-width ratio, $L/B$) and structural behavior.

### 2.1.1 Circular Wells
- **Shape:** Perfect cylinder (circular in plan).
- **Aspect Ratio:** $L/B = 1$ (since $L = B$).
- **L/B Range:** Strictly 1.0.
- **Advantages:**
  * No corners → **minimum surface area** for given volume of excavation.
  * Lowest skin friction during sinking.
  * Uniform stress distribution — best for resisting **lateral loads from any direction**.
  * No orientation issue — no need to align with flow.
- **Disadvantages:**
  * The base area is smaller compared to rectangular wells for the same well diameter.
  * If the bridge pier is rectangular/elongated, a single circular well provides poor load distribution along the major axis.
- **Typical Sizes:** Diameter ranges from **3 m to 12 m** commonly.
- **Use:** Single small piers, abutments with low width, where scour depth is high.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A circular well is **most efficient** for sinking because of minimum perimeter. The perimeter of a circular well is $P_c = \pi D$ whereas a square well of the same area has $P_s = 4\sqrt{A}$, and $P_c < P_s$ always.

### 2.1.2 Rectangular (Square) Wells
- **Shape:** Square or rectangle in plan.
- **Aspect Ratio:** Generally $L/B \leq 1.5$ (beyond which it becomes an oblong/rectangular).
- **Advantages:**
  * Easy construction of steining (straight shuttering).
  * Large base area for heavy loads.
  * Better suited to rectangular piers.
- **Disadvantages:**
  * High skin friction at the four corners (stress concentration at corners during sinking).
  * Susceptible to **tilting** during sinking due to unequal resistance.
- **Use:** For wide bridge piers, where the required base area cannot be provided by a single circular well.

### 2.1.3 Oblong / Rectangular Wells
- **Shape:** Rectangular with $1.5 < L/B \leq 2$ typically.
- **L/B Range:** Between 1.5 and 2.0.
- **Use:** For moderately elongated piers.
- **Drawback:** Higher skin friction than circular; corners create obstruction during sinking.

### 2.1.4 Double-D Wells
- **Shape:** Two circular wells joined together with a **diaphragm (web) wall** between them.
- **L/B Range:** $L/B > 2.0$, typically $2.0 < L/B < 3.0$.
- **Advantages:**
  * Provides large base area for heavily loaded, wide rectangular piers.
  * Each circular "D" behaves structurally like a circular well (good for sinking).
  * The diaphragm wall adds rigidity and increases the moment of inertia about the major axis.
- **Disadvantage:** The web (diaphragm) creates stress concentration zones; more complex construction.
- **Use:** For very wide bridge piers and heavy abutments.

### 2.1.5 Twin Circular Wells
- **Shape:** Two independent circular wells placed close together but **without any connecting diaphragm**.
- **L/B Range:** As required (often $> 3$).
- **Use:** When the pier is extremely long in one direction. Each well acts independently.
- **Drawback:** Differential settlement risk; requires very accurate simultaneous sinking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Reference for Shape Selection:**

| S.No. | Shape | L/B Ratio | Remarks |
|:-----:|:------|:---------:|:--------|
| 1 | Circular | 1.0 | Minimum skin friction, all-direction lateral load |
| 2 | Rectangular | $\leq 1.5$ | Easy steining, large base area |
| 3 | Oblong | 1.5 to 2.0 | Moderate elongation |
| 4 | Double-D | 2.0 to 3.0 | For wide piers, diaphragm adds stiffness |
| 5 | Twin Circular | Variable | For very long piers, no diaphragm |

### 2.2 Selection Criteria for Shape (Real-World Utility)

In real bridge engineering practice, the shape is selected considering:
1. **Base area required** to limit bearing pressure on the foundation soil.
2. **Direction of water flow** — the long axis is kept parallel to the flow to reduce obstruction.
3. **Magnitude and direction of lateral forces** — circular wells are best for isobaric lateral loading.
4. **Economy of construction** — circular wells require less excavation per unit base area.

---

## 2.3 Component Parts of a Well Foundation

A well is composed of the following parts from **bottom to top**:

### 2.3.1 Cutting Edge
- **Location:** The **lowermost portion** of the well, attached to the bottom of the well curb.
- **Material:** Made of **structural steel (mild steel angle or channel sections)** — it is the most heavily reinforced part.
- **Function:**
  * **Penetrates the soil** during sinking.
  * Cuts through any obstruction (small boulders, dense strata).
  * Distributes the sinking load uniformly to the soil.
  * Provides a sharp edge to overcome skin friction.
- **Design Considerations:**
  * The cutting edge must be sharp enough to penetrate but strong enough not to buckle.
  * Typical steel section used: **ISA 150 × 150 × 12 mm** or heavier ISMB sections.
  * The angle of the cutting edge is kept between **30° to 45°** with the vertical (or horizontal, depending on convention).
- **Why steel?** The cutting edge experiences extreme abrasion, impact, and stress concentration. Concrete alone would chip and fail.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The cutting edge is the **most important and most stressed** part of a well. Its design and construction are critical examination topics.

### 2.3.2 Well Curb (also called Curb or Shoe)
- **Location:** Immediately above the cutting edge, at the bottom of the steining.
- **Material:** Reinforced cement concrete (RCC) of high strength — typically **M20 to M30 grade** (in older practice sometimes M25/M30).
- **Shape:** Tapered, frustum-shaped (wider at top, narrower at bottom to merge with cutting edge).
- **Function:**
  * Provides a **smooth transition** between the sharp steel cutting edge and the vertical steining above.
  * Transmits the heavy concentrated load from the well steining to the cutting edge.
  * Resists the bending moments and shears induced during sinking.
  * Acts as a **reinforced collar** that prevents the steining from cracking at the critical junction.
- **Reinforcement:** Heavily reinforced with **double or triple layers of steel** because it is subjected to the most severe stresses.
- **Typical Height:** 1.0 m to 2.5 m depending on the well size.

### 2.3.3 Stiffener Ring (Stiffening Ring)
- **Location:** A ring of structural steel (I-section, channel, or built-up section) embedded within the well curb.
- **Function:**
  * Provides additional **rigidity** to the well curb.
  * Resists the **lateral forces and moments** during initial sinking when the well has not yet gained grip from the surrounding soil.
  * Distributes the load from the steining more uniformly onto the curb and cutting edge.
- **Design:** Designed as a ring subjected to internal pressure, bending moment, and shear.

### 2.3.4 Well Steining
- **Location:** The **vertical wall** of the well — the main body extending from the well curb to the top of the well (or up to the well cap).
- **Material:** Typically **plain cement concrete (PCC) of M15 grade** for shallow wells, or **RCC of M20–M30 grade** for deep wells and large wells.
- **Thickness:**
  * Generally varies from **0.5 m to 1.5 m** depending on well size and depth.
  * Rough estimate: $t = (D + 1)/10$ m for circular wells where $D$ is the well diameter (in metres), with a minimum of 0.5 m.
- **Function:**
  * Provides the **self-weight** needed to sink the well (along with kentledge).
  * Acts as the **permanent load-bearing structural wall** transferring loads from the superstructure to the base.
  * Resists lateral earth and water pressure.
- **Construction:** Built in **lifts of 1.5 m to 3 m** as the well sinks progressively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always remember — the **bottom 1.5 m of the steining** (just above the curb) is heavily reinforced with vertical bars to act as a transition zone. This is a common KTU question.

### 2.3.5 Bottom Plug
- **Location:** Seals the **bottom** of the well after it has reached the founding strata and is dewatered.
- **Material:** **Underwater concrete (tremie concrete)** of M20 or higher grade, typically **1.5 m to 3.0 m thick**.
- **Function:**
  * **Seals the bottom** against water and soil ingress.
  * Transfers the structural load from the well steining to the **bearing soil** at the founding level.
  * Acts as the foundation footing of the well.
- **Construction Method:** Placed using the **tremie pipe technique** (a sealed pipe lowered to the bottom through which concrete is poured, displacing water upwards).
- **Why tremie concrete?** Because the well is full of water at this stage and direct dewatered concreting is impossible.

### 2.3.6 Top Plug (Sand Fill / Hearting)
- **Location:** Fills the **upper interior** of the well above the bottom plug (in some designs, the entire well interior is filled).
- **Material:** Mostly **sand, gravel, or lean concrete (M10)**. Sometimes partially filled with water (for sinking wells where buoyancy reduction is needed).
- **Function:**
  * Provides additional **weight** to the well.
  * Prevents **buoyancy uplift** of the well.
  * Adds to the mass resisting overturning moments.
- **Note:** Not all wells have a top plug. Many wells are kept **hollow** above the bottom plug up to the well cap.

### 2.3.7 Well Cap (Top Slab)
- **Location:** The **topmost** part of the well foundation, a thick RCC slab cast over the well steining and the top plug (if any).
- **Material:** RCC of M25 or higher grade, heavily reinforced.
- **Thickness:** Usually **1.0 m to 2.5 m**.
- **Function:**
  * Distributes the load from the **pier/column** over the entire cross-section of the well.
  * Provides a **level working platform** for the pier construction.
  * Transfers all superstructure loads (dead, live, seismic, etc.) to the well steining.

### 2.3.8 Well Sinking Accessories
These are auxiliary items used during the construction/sinking process:
- **Kentledge:** Additional dead weight (sand bags, concrete blocks, pig iron) placed on top of the well to overcome skin friction and force sinking.
- **Dredge Hole:** The open excavation in the middle of the well through which soil is grabbed out.
- **Dredgers/Grab Buckets:** The clamshell-type buckets used to scoop out soil from inside the well.
- **Jetting Pipes:** Pipes used to inject high-pressure water around the cutting edge to reduce skin friction.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the key quantitative relationships and rules used in well foundation design. The "rule of thumb" formulas are based on standard Indian practice (IS 3955 and IRC codes).

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula / Rule | Units | Remarks |
|:-----:|:----------|:---------------|:------|:--------|
| 1 | Perimeter of circular well | $P_c = \pi D$ | m | $D$ = diameter |
| 2 | Perimeter of square well | $P_s = 4a$ | m | $a$ = side |
| 3 | Perimeter of double-D well | $P = 2 \pi D + 2 L$ | m | $L$ = length of diaphragm |
| 4 | Steining thickness (circular) | $t = (D+1)/10$ | m | Min **0.5 m** |
| 5 | Steining thickness (rectangular) | $t = (L+B)/20$ | m | Min **0.5 m** |
| 6 | Cutting edge angle | $\theta = 30°$ to $45°$ | degree | With vertical/horizontal |
| 7 | Well curb height | 1.0 to 2.5 | m | Heavily reinforced |
| 8 | Bottom plug thickness | 1.5 to 3.0 | m | Tremie concrete |
| 9 | Well cap thickness | 1.0 to 2.5 | m | Heavily reinforced RCC |
| 10 | Sinking depth (min) | $\geq 2 \times$ max scour depth | m | Below HFL |
| 11 | Grip length | $H_g = 1.5 D$ to $2.0 D$ | m | Below scour level |
| 12 | Bearing pressure | $q = (P + W_s - F_s)/A_b$ | kN/m² | Net bearing check |
| 13 | Skin friction | $f_s = \pi D \cdot H \cdot \bar{f}$ | kN | $\bar{f}$ = avg unit friction |
| 14 | Skin friction reduction | Reduces with jetting | — | Typical values 5–30 kN/m² |

Where:
- $D$ = Well diameter (m)
- $L, B$ = Length and breadth of rectangular well (m)
- $P$ = Superstructure load (kN)
- $W_s$ = Self-weight of well (kN)
- $F_s$ = Total skin friction (kN)
- $A_b$ = Base area of bottom plug (m²)
- $H_g$ = Grip length below scour level (m)
- $\bar{f}$ = Average unit skin friction (kN/m²), depends on soil type

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The well foundation system is the **backbone of bridge engineering in India**, especially across the major rivers (Ganga, Yamuna, Brahmaputra, Godavari, Narmada, Kosi). Notable examples include:
- Bridges on the **Ganga and Yamuna** where scour depths exceed 30 m.
- All major **road and railway bridges** on the Indian river system.
- Coastal/offshore structures where the water depth makes cofferdams uneconomical.
- In KTU-affiliated engineering practice in Kerala, wells are used in bridges across **Periyar, Pamba, and Bharathapuzha rivers**, though cofferdams are more common in Kerala's relatively shallow river beds.

The shape selection directly impacts:
1. **Constructability** (ease of sinking, tilting tendency)
2. **Economy** (concrete and steel quantity)
3. **Structural performance** (load carrying and lateral load resistance)

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **derivation-level analysis** of the key quantitative relationships used in shape selection and component sizing of well foundations. Each derivation is broken down to its fundamental assumptions, following KTU 2024 Scheme analytical depth expectations.

## 3.1 Derivation: Why a Circular Well Has Minimum Perimeter for a Given Base Area

This is a classic calculus of variations / optimization problem. For a well of a given base area $A$, we need to find the shape that minimizes the perimeter $P$ (because minimum perimeter = minimum skin friction = easiest sinking).

**Step 1: Set up the constraint.**
For a fixed base area $A$:
$$A = \text{constant}$$

**Step 2: Express the area and perimeter of a circle.**
For a circle of diameter $D$:
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}}$$
$$P = \pi D = \pi \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = 2\sqrt{\pi A}$$

**Step 3: Express the perimeter of a square of the same area $A$.**
For a square of side $a$:
$$A = a^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \sqrt{A}$$
$$P_s = 4a = 4\sqrt{A}$$

**Step 4: Compare the two perimeters.**
$$\frac{P_c}{P_s} = \frac{2\sqrt{\pi A}}{4\sqrt{A}} = \frac{2\sqrt{\pi}}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$$
Evaluating:
$$\sqrt{\pi} \approx 1.7725$$
$$\frac{P_c}{P_s} \approx \frac{1.7725}{2} \approx 0.886$$

**Step 5: Conclusion.**
$$\boxed{P_c = 0.886 \cdot P_s}$$
This means a **circular well has about 11.4% less perimeter** (and hence less skin friction) than a square well of the same base area. This is the rigorous mathematical justification for choosing circular wells whenever base area requirements permit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The isoperimetric inequality in 2D states that, for a given enclosed area, the circle has the **smallest perimeter** of all closed plane curves. This is a fundamental result and is the theoretical basis for preferring circular wells.

## 3.2 Derivation: Empirical Steining Thickness Formula for Circular Wells

The empirical formula:
$$t = \frac{D + 1}{10} \text{ m}$$
where $D$ is the diameter in metres, comes from a **regression of practical designs** documented in IS codes. Let us verify the boundary cases:

**Step 1: For a small well with $D = 3$ m:**
$$t = \frac{3 + 1}{10} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Apply the minimum thickness criterion.**
Since minimum steining thickness is **0.5 m**:
$$t_{\text{adopted}} = \max\left(0.5, \frac{D+1}{10}\right) = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: For a medium well with $D = 6$ m:**
$$t = \frac{6 + 1}{10} = \frac{7}{10} = 0.7 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: For a large well with $D = 10$ m:**
$$t = \frac{10 + 1}{10} = \frac{11}{10} = 1.1 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: For a very large well with $D = 15$ m:**
$$t = \frac{15 + 1}{10} = \frac{16}{10} = 1.6 \text{ m}$$

This shows the formula is dimensionally consistent and the values align with practical designs (typically $0.5$–$1.5$ m).

## 3.3 Derivation: Base Area Required to Limit Bearing Pressure

The bearing pressure at the base of the well must be less than the **safe bearing capacity (SBC)** of the founding soil:
$$q = \frac{P + W_s - F_s}{A_b} \leq q_s$$

Where:
- $P$ = total superstructure load (kN)
- $W_s$ = self-weight of well (kN)
- $F_s$ = effective skin friction transferred to soil (kN) — note: $F_s$ does not act at the base
- $A_b$ = base area of the bottom plug (m²)
- $q_s$ = safe bearing capacity of founding soil (kN/m²)

**Step 1: Solve for the required base area.**
$$A_b \geq \frac{P + W_s - F_s}{q_s}$$

**Step 2: For a circular well of diameter $D$:**
$$A_b = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \geq \frac{P + W_s - F_s}{q_s}$$
Solving for $D$:
$$D \geq \sqrt{\frac{4(P + W_s - F_s)}{\pi \cdot q_s}}$$

**Step 3: Numerical Example.**
Suppose $P = 20,000$ kN, $W_s = 8,000$ kN, $F_s = 4,000$ kN, and $q_s = 300$ kN/m².
$$A_b \geq \frac{20,000 + 8,000 - 4,000}{300} = \frac{24,000}{300} = 80 \text{ m}^2$$
$$D \geq \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 80}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{320}{\pi}} = \sqrt{101.86} \approx 10.09 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Provide a standard well size.**
Adopt $D = 11$ m (next standard size up). This gives a base area:
$$A_b = \frac{\pi \times 11^2}{4} = \frac{121\pi}{4} \approx 95.03 \text{ m}^2$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Grip Length Justification

The **grip length** ($H_g$) is the depth of the well below the **maximum anticipated scour level**. It is required so that:
1. The well does not become exposed due to future scour.
2. The surrounding soil provides sufficient **lateral confinement** to resist the lateral loads.
3. The base is anchored against scour even if the river bed degrades.

**Empirical rule (IS 3955 / IRC):**
$$H_g = 1.5 D \quad \text{to} \quad 2.0 D$$

**Step 1: For a 10 m diameter well, minimum grip:**
$$H_g^{\min} = 1.5 \times 10 = 15 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: For maximum safety:**
$$H_g^{\max} = 2.0 \times 10 = 20 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Justification.**
The grip length must be sufficient to mobilize the **passive earth pressure** that resists the lateral forces. The passive resistance per unit width at depth $z$ below the scour line is:
$$\sigma_p = K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot z + 2 c \sqrt{K_p}$$
Where $K_p$ is the coefficient of passive earth pressure, $\gamma$ is the effective unit weight, and $c$ is the cohesion. The total passive resistance must exceed the applied lateral force plus the overturning moment.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Shape Selection Decision Logic

The following matrix summarizes the engineering decision flow for shape selection:

| Condition / Requirement | Recommended Shape | Reasoning |
|:------------------------|:------------------|:----------|
| Pier is small, isolated, all-direction lateral loads | Circular | Minimum perimeter, best for omnidirectional loading |
| Pier is moderately wide, rectangular shape | Rectangular ($L/B \leq 1.5$) | Larger base area, easy construction |
| Pier is elongated, $1.5 < L/B \leq 2.0$ | Oblong | Provides required area without excessive corners |
| Pier is very wide, $2.0 < L/B \leq 3.0$, heavy load | Double-D | Combines circular sinking behavior with elongated footprint |
| Pier is extremely elongated, $L/B > 3.0$ | Twin Circular | Independent sinking of two circular units |
| Heavy scour expected, deep grip required | Circular (smaller $D$) | Easier to sink to greater depths |
| Construction in hard soil with boulders | Any, with stronger cutting edge | Curb design is more critical than shape |

## 3.6 Derivation: Unit Skin Friction and Sinking Effort

The total skin friction resisting sinking is:
$$F_s = P_{\text{well}} \times H \times \bar{f}$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{well}}$ = perimeter of the well in contact with soil (m)
- $H$ = depth of well embedment (m)
- $\bar{f}$ = average unit skin friction (kN/m²)

**Numerical Example:**
For a circular well of $D = 8$ m, depth $H = 20$ m, in sandy soil with $\bar{f} = 20$ kN/m²:
$$P_{\text{well}} = \pi D = \pi \times 8 = 25.13 \text{ m}$$
$$F_s = 25.13 \times 20 \times 20 = 10,053 \text{ kN}$$

To overcome this friction, the well must have self-weight (plus kentledge) greater than $F_s$. This drives the design of the steining thickness and the use of kentledge.
